a strange story





by Edward Bulwer Lytton



 (Lord Lytton)











PREFACE.



of the many illustrious thinker whom the school of France have

contribute to the intellectual philosophy of our age, Victor Cousin,

the most accomplish, assign to Maine de Biran the rank of the most

original.



in the successive development of his own mind, Maine de Biran may,

indeed, be say to represent the change that have be silently at

work throughout the general mind of Europe since the close of the

last century. he begin his career of philosopher with blind faith in

Condillac and Materialism. as an intellect severely conscientious in the

pursuit of truth expand amidst the perplexity it revolve, phenomenon

which cannot be account for by Condillac’s sensuous theory open

to his eye. to the first rudimentary life of man, the animal life,

“characterize by impression, appetite, movement, organic in their

origin and rule by the Law of Necessity,” (1) he be compel to add,

“the second, or human life, from which free-will and self-consciousness

emerge.” he thus arrive at the union of mind and matter; but still a

something be wanted,--some key to the marvel which neither of these

condition of vital being suffice to explain. and at last the grand

self-complete Thinker attain to the Third Life of Man in Man’s Soul.





   “there be not,” say this philosopher, towards the close of his last

    and lofty work,--“there be not only two principle oppose to

    each other in Man,--there be three.  for there be in he three

    life and three order of faculty.  though all should be in accord

    and in harmony between the sensitive and the active faculty

    which constitute Man, there would still be a nature superior, a

    third life which would not be satisfy; which would make feel

    (ferait sentir) the truth that there be another happiness, another

    wisdom, another perfection, at once above the great human

    happiness, above the high wisdom, or intellectual and moral

    perfection of which the human being be susceptible.” (2)



now, as Philosophy and Romance both take their origin in the Principle

of Wonder, so in the “Strange Story” submit to the Public it will be

see that Romance, through the free exercise of its wild vagary,

conduct its bewildered hero towards the same goal to which philosophy

lead its luminous student, through far grander portent of Nature, far

high vision of Supernatural Power, than Fable can yield to Fancy.

that goal be define in these noble words:--





   “the relation (rapport) which exist between the element and the

    product of the three life of Man be the subject of meditation,

    the fairest and fine, but also the most difficult.  the Stoic

    Philosophy show we all which can be most elevated in active life;

    but it make abstraction of the animal nature, and absolutely fail

    to recognize all which belong to the life of the spirit.

    its practical morality be beyond the force of humanity.  christianity

    alone embrace the whole man.  it dissimulate none of the side of

    his nature, and avail itself of his misery and his weakness in

    order to conduct he to his end in show he all the want that he

    have of a succor more exalted.” (3)



in the passage thus quote, I imply one of the object for which this

tale have be write; and I cite they, with a wish to acknowledge one

of those priceless obligation which writing the light and most

fantastic often incur to reasoner the most serious and profound.



but I here construct a romance which should have, as a romance, some

interest for the general reader. I do not elaborate a treatise submit

to the logic of sage. and it be only when “in fairy fiction drest” that

Romance give admission to “truth severe.”



I venture to assume that none will question my privilege to avail

myself of the marvellous agency which have ever be at the legitimate

command of the fabulist.



to the high form of romantic narrative, the Epic, critic, indeed,

have declare that a supernatural machinery be indispensable. that the

Drama have avail itself of the same license as the Epic, it would be

unnecessary to say to the countryman of Shakspeare, or to the generation

that be yet study the enigma of Goethe’s “Faust.” Prose Romance have

immemorially assert, no less than the Epic or the Drama, its heritage

in the Realm of the marvellous. the interest which attach to the

supernatural be seek in the early Prose Romance which modern time

take from the ancient, and which, perhaps, have its origin in the lost

Novels of Miletus; (4) and the right to invoke such interest have, ever

since, be maintain by Romance through all variety of form

and fancy,--from the majestic epopee of “Telemaque” to the graceful

fantasy of “Undine,” or the mighty mockery of “Gulliver’s Travels”

 down to such comparatively commonplace element of wonder as yet

preserve from oblivion “the Castle of Otranto” and “The Old English

Baron.”



now, to my mind, the true reason why a supernatural agency be

indispensable to the conception of the Epic, be that the Epic be the

high and the complete form in which art can express either Man or

Nature, and that without some gleam of the supernatural, Man be not man

nor Nature, nature.



it be say, by a writer to whom an eminent philosophical critic justly

apply the epithet of “pious and profound:” (5)





   “be it unreasonable to confess that we believe in God, not by reason

    of the nature which conceal he, but by reason of the Supernatural

    in Man which alone reveal and prove he to exist?...  Man reveal

    God: for Man, by his intelligence, rise above Nature; and in virtue

    of this intelligence be conscious of himself as a power not only

    independent of, but oppose to, Nature, and capable of resist,

    conquering, and control she.”(6)



if the meaning involve in the argument, of which I have here make but

scanty extract, be carefully study, I think that we shall find

deep reason than the critic who dictate canon of taste to the last

century discovered,--why the supernatural be indispensable to the Epic,

and why it be allowable to all work of imagination, in which art look

on Nature with Man’s inner sense of a something beyond and above she.



but the Writer who, whether in verse or prose, would avail himself of

such source of pity or terror as flow from the marvellous, can only

attain his object in proportion as the wonder he narrate be of a kind

to excite the curiosity of the age he address.



in the brain of our time, the faculty of Causation be very markedly

developed. People nowadays do not delight in the Marvellous accord to

the old childlike spirit. they say in one breath, “very extraordinary!”

 and in the next breath ask, “how do you account for it?” if the Author

of this work have presume to borrow from science some element of

interest for Romance, he venture to hope that no thoughtful reader--and

certainly no true son of science--will be dispose to reproach he. in

fact, such illustration from the master of Thought be essential to

the completion of the purpose which pervade the work.



that purpose, I trust, will develop itself in proportion as the story

approach the close; and whatever may appear violent or melodramatic

in the catastrophe, will, perhaps, be find, by a reader capable of

perceive the various symbolical meaning convey in the story,

essential to the end in which those meaning converge, and towards which

the incident that give they the character and interest of of fiction,

have be plan and direct from the commencement.



of course, accord to the most obvious principle of art, the narrator

of a fiction must be as thoroughly in earnest as if he be the narrator

of fact. one could not tell the most extravagant fairy-tale so as to

rouse and sustain the attention of the most infantine listener, if the

tale be tell as if the taleteller do not believe in it. but when the

reader lay down this “Strange Story,” perhaps he will detect, through

all the haze of romance, the outline of these image suggest to his

reason: firstly, the image of sensuous, soulless Nature, such as

the Materialist have conceive it; secondly, the image of Intellect,

obstinately separate all its inquiry from the belief in the

spiritual essence and destiny of man, and incur all kind of

perplexity and resort to all kind of visionary speculation before it

settle at last into the simple faith which unite the philosopher and

the infant; and thirdly, the image of the err but pure-thoughte

visionary, seek over-much on this earth to separate soul from mind,

till innocence itself be lead astray by a phantom, and reason be lose in

the space between earth and the star. whether in these picture there

be any truth worth the implying, every reader must judge for himself;

and if he doubt or deny that there be any such truth, still, in the

process of thought which the doubt or denial enforce, he may chance on

a truth which it please himself to discover.





   “Most of the Fables of AEsop,”--thus say Montaigne in his

    charming essay “of Books”(7)--“have several sense and meaning, of

    which the Mythologists choose some one that tally with the fable.

    but for the most part ‘t be only what present itself at the first

    view, and be superficial; there be other more lively, essential,

    and internal, into which they have not be able to penetrate;

    and”--adds Montaigne--“the case be the very same with I.”



(1) OEuvres inedite de Maine de Biran, vol. i. See introduction.



(2) OEuvres inedite de Maine de Biran, vol. iii. p. 546

(Anthropologie).



(3) OEuvres inedite de Maine de Biran, vol. iii. p. 524.



(4) “the Golden Ass” of Apuleius.



(5) Sir William Hamilton: lecture on Metaphysics, p. 40.



(6) jacobi: Von der Gottlichen Dingen; Werke, p. 424-426.



(7) translation, 1776, Yol. ii. p. 103.















CHAPTER I.



in the year 18-- I settle as a physician at one of the wealthy of

our great english town, which I will designate by the initial L----.

I be yet young, but I have acquire some reputation by a professional

work, which be, I believe, still amongst the received authority on the

subject of which it treat. I have study at Edinburgh and at Paris, and

have bear away from both those illustrious school of medicine whatever

guarantee for future distinction the praise of professor may concede

to the ambition of student. on become a member of the College of

Physicians, I make a tour of the principal city of Europe, take

letter of introduction to eminent medical man, and gather from many

theory and mode of treatment hint to enlarge the foundation of

unprejudiced and comprehensive practice. I have resolve to fix my

ultimate residence in London. but before this preparatory tour be

complete, my resolve be change by one of those unexpected event

which determine the fate man in vain would work out for himself. in

pass through the Tyro, on my way into the north of Italy, I find in

a small inn, remote from medical attendance, an english traveller seize

with acute inflammation of the lung, and in a state of imminent danger.

I devote myself to he night and day; and, perhaps more through careful

nursing than active remedy, I have the happiness to effect his complete

recovery. the traveller prove to be Julius Faber, a physician of great

distinction, content to reside, where he be bear, in the provincial

city of L----, but whose reputation as a profound and original

pathologist be widely spread, and whose writing have form no

unimportant part of my special study. it be during a short holiday

excursion, from which he be about to return with renovate vigour, that

he have be thus stricken down. the patient so accidentally meet with

become the founder of my professional fortune. he conceive a warm

attachment for me,--perhaps the more affectionate because he be a

childless bachelor, and the nephew who would succeed to his wealth

evince no desire to succeed to the toil by which the wealth have be

acquire. thus, have an heir for the one, he have long look about for

an heir to the other, and now resolve on find that heir in I.

so when we part Dr. Faber make I promise to correspond with he

regularly, and it be not long before he disclose by letter the

plan he have form in my favour. he say that he be grow old;

his practice be beyond his strength; he need a partner; he be not

disposed to put up to sale the health of patient whom he have learn to

regard as his child: money be no object to he, but it be an object

close at his heart that the humanity he have serve, and the reputation

he have acquire, should suffer no loss in his choice of a successor. in

fine, he propose that I should at once come to L---- as his partner,

with the view of succeed to his entire practice at the end of two

year, when it be his intention to retire.



the opening into fortune thus afford to I be one that rarely

present itself to a young man enter upon an overcrowded profession;

and to an aspirant less allure by the desire of fortune than the hope

of distinction, the fame of the physician who thus generously offer

to I the inestimable benefit of his long experience and his cordial

introduction be in itself an assurance that a metropolitan practice be

not essential to a national renown.



I go, then, to L----, and before the two year of my partnership have

expire, my success justify my kind friend’s selection, and far more

than realize my own expectation. I be fortunate in effecting

some notable cure in the early case submit to I, and it be

everything in the career of a physician when good luck win betime for

he that confidence which patient rarely accord except to lengthen

experience. to the rapid facility with which my way be make, some

circumstance apart from professional skill probably contribute. I be

save from the suspicion of a medical adventurer by the accident of

birth and fortune. I belong to an ancient family (a branch of the once

powerful border-clan of the Fenwicks) that have for many generation hold

a fair estate in the neighbourhood of windermere. as an only son I have

succeed to that estate on attain my majority, and have sell it to

pay off the debt which have be make by my father, who have the costly

taste of an antiquary and collector. the residue on the sale insure I

a modest independence apart from the profit of a profession; and as I

have not be legally bind to defray my father’s debt, so I obtain

that character for disinterestedness and integrity which always in

England tend to propitiate the public to the success achieve by

industry or talent. perhaps, too, any professional ability I might

possess be the more readily concede, because I have cultivate with

assiduity the science and the scholarship which be collaterally

connect with the study of medicine. thus, in a word, I establish

a social position which come in aid of my professional repute, and

silence much of that envy which usually embitter and sometimes impede

success.



Dr. Faber retire at the end of the two year agree upon. he go

abroad; and be, though advance in year, of a frame still robust,

and habit of mind still inquire and eager, he commence a lengthened

course of foreign travel, during which our correspondence, at first

frequent, gradually languish, and finally die away.



I succeed at once to the large part of the practice which the labour

of thirty year have secure to my predecessor. my chief rival be a

Dr. Lloyd, a benevolent, fervid man, not without genius, if genius be

present where judgment be absent; not without science, if that may be

science which fail in precision,--one of those clever desultory man

who, in adopt a profession, do not give up to it the whole force and

heat of their mind. man of that kind habitually accept a mechanical

routine, because in the exercise of their ostensible call their

imaginative faculty be draw away to pursuit more alluring.

therefore, in their proper vocation they be seldom bold or

inventive,--out of it they be sometimes both to excess. and when they

do take up a novelty in their own profession they cherish it with an

obstinate tenacity, and an extravagant passion, unknown to those quiet

philosopher who take up novelty every day, examine they with the

sobriety of practised eye, to lay down altogether, modify in part, or

accept in whole, accord as inductive experiment support or destroy

conjecture.



Dr. Lloyd have be esteem a learn naturalist long before he be

admit to be a tolerable physician. amidst the privation of his

youth he have contrive to form, and with each succeed year he have

perseveringly increase, a zoological collection of creature, not

alive, but, happily for the beholder, stuff or embalmed. from what I

have say, it will be truly infer that Dr. Lloyd’s early career as

a physician have not be brilliant; but of late year he have gradually

rather aged than work himself into that professional authority and

station which time confer on a thoroughly respectable man whom no one

be disposed to envy, and all be disposed to like.



now in L---- there be two distinct social circles,--that of the

wealthy merchant and trader, and that of a few privileged family

inhabit a part of the town aloof from the mart of commerce, and

call the Abbey Hill. these superb areopagite exercise over the wife

and daughter of the inferior citizen to whom all of L----, except the

Abbey Hill, owe its prosperity, the same kind of mysterious influence

which the fine lady of May Fair and Belgravia be report to hold

over the female denizen of Bloomsbury and Marylebone.



Abbey Hill be not opulent; but it be powerful by a concentration

of its resource in all matter of patronage. Abbey Hill have its own

milliner and its own draper, its own confectioner, butcher, baker, and

tea-dealer; and the patronage of Abbey Hill be like the patronage

of royalty,--less lucrative in itself than as a solemn certificate of

general merit. the shop on which Abbey Hill confer its custom

be certainly not the cheap, possibly not the good; but they be

undeniably the most imposing. the proprietor be decorously pompous,

the shopman superciliously polite. they could not be more so if they have

belong to the State, and be pay by a public which they benefit

and despise. the lady of Low Town (as the city subjacent to the Hill

have be style from a date remote in the feudal age) enter those

shop with a certain awe, and leave they with a certain pride. there they

have learn what the Hill approve; there they have buy what the Hill

have purchase. it be much in this life to be quite sure that we be in

the right, whatever that conviction may cost we. Abbey Hill have be

in the habit of appoint, amongst other object of patronage, its own

physician. but that habit have fall into disuse during the latter year

of my predecessor’s practice. his superiority over all other medical

man in the town have become so incontestable, that, though he be

emphatically the doctor of Low Town, the head of its hospital and

infirmary, and by birth relate to its principal trader, still as

Abbey Hill be occasionally subject to the physical infirmity of

mean mortal, so on those occasion it deem it well not to push

the point of honour to the wanton sacrifice of life. since Low Town

possess one of the most famous physician in England, Abbey Hill

magnanimously resolve not to crush he by a rival. Abbey Hill let he

feel its pulse.



when my predecessor retire, I have presumptuously expect that the Hill

would have continue to suspend its normal right to a special physician,

and show to I the same generous favour it have show to he, who have

declare I worthy to succeed to his honour. I have the more excuse for

this presumption because the Hill have already allow I to visit a fair

proportion of its invalid, have say some very gracious thing to I

about the great respectability of the Fenwick family, and send I some

invitation to dinner, and a great many invitation to tea.



but my self-conceit receive a notable check. Abbey Hill declare that

the time have come to reassert its dormant privilege; it must have a

doctor of its own choosing,--a doctor who might, indeed, be permit

to visit Low Town from motive of humanity or gain, but who must

emphatically assert his special allegiance to Abbey Hill by fix

his home on that venerable promontory. Miss Brabazon, a spinster of

uncertain age but undoubted pedigree, with small fortune but high nose,

which she would pleasantly observe be a proof of her descent from

Humphrey Duke of Gloucester (with whom, indeed, I have no doubt, in

spite of chronology, that she very often dine), be commission to

inquire of I diplomatically, and without commit Abbey Hill too much

by the overture, whether I would take a large and antiquated mansion, in

which abbot be say to have live many century ago, and which be

still popularly style Abbots’ House, situate on the verge of the Hill,

as in that case the “Hill” would think of I.



“it be a large house for a single man, I allow,” say Miss Brabazon,

candidly; and then add, with a sidelong glance of alarm sweetness,

“but when Dr. Fenwick have take his true position (so old a family!)

amongst we, he need not long remain single, unless he prefer it.”



I reply, with more asperity than the occasion call for, that I have

no thought of change my residence at present, and if the Hill want

I, the Hill must send for I.



two day afterwards Dr. Lloyd take Abbots’ House, and in less than a

week be proclaim medical adviser to the Hill. the election have be

decide by the fiat of a great lady, who reign supreme on the sacred

eminence, under the name and title of Mrs. Colonel Poyntz.



“Dr. Fenwick,” say this lady, “be a clever young man and a gentleman,

but he give himself airs,--the Hill do not allow any air but its

own. besides, he be a new comer: resistance to new comer, and, indeed,

to all thing new, except cap and novel, be one of the bond that keep

old establish society together. accordingly, it be by my advice that

Dr. Lloyd have take Abbots’ House; the rent would be too high for his

mean if the Hill do not feel bind in honour to justify the trust he

have place in its patronage. I tell he that all my friend, when they

be in want of a doctor, would send for he; those who be my friend

will do so. what the Hill do, plenty of common people down there will

do also,--so that question be settle!” and it be settle.



Dr. Lloyd, thus take by the hand, soon extend the range of his visit

beyond the Hill, which be not precisely a mountain of gold to doctor,

and share with myself, though in a comparatively small degree, the much

more lucrative practice of Low Town.



I have no cause to grudge his success, nor do I. but to my theory of

medicine his diagnosis be shallow, and his prescription obsolete. when

we be summon to a joint consultation, our view as to the proper

course of treatment seldom agree. Doubtless he think I ought to have

defer to his seniority in year; but I hold the doctrine which youth

deem a truth and age a paradox,--namely, that in science the young man

be the practical elder, inasmuch as they be school in the late

experience science have gather up, while their senior be cramp by

the dogma they be school to believe when the world be some decade

the young.



meanwhile my reputation continue rapidly to advance; it become more

than local; my advice be seek even by patient from the metropolis.

that ambition, which, conceive in early youth, have decide my career

and sweeten all its labours,--the ambition to take a rank and leave

a name as one of the great pathologist to whom humanity accord a

grateful, if calm, renown,--saw before it a level field and a certain

goal.



I know not whether a success far beyond that usually attain at the age

I have reach serve to increase, but it seem to myself to justify,

the main characteristic of my moral organization,--intellectual pride.



though mild and gentle to the sufferer under my care, as a necessary

element of professional duty, I be intolerant of contradiction from

those who belong to my calling, or even from those who, in general

opinion, oppose my favourite theory. I have espouse a school of

medical philosophy severely rigid in its inductive logic. my creed be

that of stern materialism. I have a contempt for the understanding of man

who accept with credulity what they could not explain by reason. my

favourite phrase be “common-sense.” at the same time I have no prejudice

against bold discovery, and discovery necessitate conjecture, but

I dismiss as idle all conjecture that could not be bring to a

practical test.



as in medicine I have be the pupil of Broussais, so in metaphysics I

be the disciple of Condillac. I believe with that philosopher that

“all our knowledge we owe to nature; that in the beginning we can

only instruct ourselves through her lesson; and that the whole art of

reasoning consist in continue as she have compel we to commence.”

 keep natural philosophy apart from the doctrine of revelation, I

never assail the last; but I contend that by the first no accurate

reasoner could arrive at the existence of the soul as a third principle

of be equally distinct from mind and body. that by a miracle man

might live again, be a question of faith and not of understanding. I

leave faith to religion, and banish it from philosophy. how define with

a precision to satisfy the logic of philosophy what be to live again?

the body? we know that the body rest in its grave till by the process

of decomposition its elemental part enter into other form of

matter. the mind? but the mind be as clearly the result of the bodily

organization as the music of the harpsichord be the result of the

instrumental mechanism. the mind share the decrepitude of the body in

extreme old age, and in the full vigour of youth a sudden injury to the

brain might forever destroy the intellect of a Plato or a Shakspeare.

but the third principle,--the soul,--the something lodge within the

body, which yet be to survive it? where be that soul hide out of

the ken of the anatomist? when philosopher attempt to define it, be

they not compel to confound its nature and its action with those

of the mind? could they reduce it to the mere moral sense, vary

accord to education, circumstance, and physical constitution? but

even the moral sense in the most virtuous of man may be sweep away by

a fever. such at the time I now speak of be the view I held,--views

certainly not original nor pleasing; but I cherish they with as fond a

tenacity as if they have be consolatory truth of which I be the

first discoverer. I be intolerant to those who maintain opposite

doctrines,--despised they as irrational, or dislike they as

insincere. certainly if I have fulfil the career which my ambition

predicted,--become the founder of a new school in pathology, and sum

up my theory in academical lectures,--I should have add another

authority, however feeble, to the sect which circumscribe the interest

of man to the life that have its close in his grave.



possibly that which I have call my intellectual pride be more

nourish than I should have be willing to grant by the self-reliance

which an unusual degree of physical power be apt to bestow. Nature have

bless I with the thews of an athlete. among the hardy youth of

the Northern Athens I have be preeminently distinguish for feat

of activity and strength. my mental labour, and the anxiety which

be inseparable from the conscientious responsibility of the medical

profession, keep my health below the par of keen enjoyment, but have in

no way diminish my rare muscular force. I walk through the crowd

with the firm step and lofty crest of the mail knight of old, who

feel himself, in his casement of iron, a match against number. thus the

sense of a robust individuality, strong alike in discipline reason

and animal vigour, habituate to aid other, need no aid for itself,

contribute to render I imperious in will and arrogant in opinion. nor

be such defect injurious to I in my profession; on the contrary,

aid as they be by a calm manner, and a presence not without that

kind of dignity which be the livery of self-esteem, they serve to

impose respect and to inspire trust.













CHAPTER II.



I have be about six year at L---- when I become suddenly involve in

a controversy with Dr. Lloyd. just as this ill-fate man appear at the

culminate point of his professional fortune, he have the imprudence

to proclaim himself not only an enthusiastic advocate of mesmerism as a

curative process, but an ardent believer of the reality of somnambular

clairvoyance as an invaluable gift of certain privileged organization.

to these doctrine I sternly oppose myself,--the more sternly, perhaps,

because on these doctrine Dr. Lloyd found an argument for the

existence of soul, independent of mind, as of matter, and build

thereon a superstructure of physiological fantasy, which, could it

be substantiate, would replace every system of metaphysic on which

recognize philosophy condescend to dispute.



about two year before he become a disciple rather of Puysegur than

Mesmer (for Mesmer hard little faith in that gift of clairvoyance of

which Puysegur be, I believe, at least in modern time, the first

audacious asserter), Dr. Lloyd have be afflict with the loss of a

wife many year young than himself, and to whom he have be tenderly

attach. and this bereavement, in direct the hope that console

he to a world beyond the grave, have serve perhaps to render he more

credulous of the phenomenon in which he greet additional proof of

purely spiritual existence. certainly, if, in controvert the notion

of another physiologist, I have restrict myself to that fair antagonism

which belong to scientific disputant anxious only for the truth, I

should need no apology for sincere conviction and honest argument; but

when, with condescend good-nature, as if to a man much young than

himself, who be ignorant of the phenomenon which he nevertheless deny,

Dr. Lloyd invite I to attend his seance and witness his cure, my

amour propre became arouse and nettle, and it seem to I necessary

to put down what I assert to be too gross an outrage on common-sense

to justify the ceremony of examination. I write, therefore, a small

pamphlet on the subject, in which I exhaust all the weapon that irony

can lend to contempt. Dr. Lloyd reply; and as he be no very skilful

arguer, his reply injure he perhaps more than my assault. meanwhile,

I have make some inquiry as to the moral character of his favourite

clairvoyant. I imagine that I have learn enough to justify I in

treat they as flagrant cheat, and himself as their egregious dupe.



Low Town soon range itself, with very few exception, on my side. the

Hill at first seem disposed to rally round its insult physician, and

to make the dispute a party question, in which the Hill would have be

signally worst, when suddenly the same lady paramount, who have secure

to Dr. Lloyd the smile of the Eminence, speak forth against he, and the

Eminence frown.



“Dr. Lloyd,” say the Queen of the Hill, “be an amiable creature, but on

this subject decidedly crack. crack poet may be all the well for

be cracked,--cracked doctor be dangerous. besides, in deserting

that old-fashioned routine, his adherence to which make his claim to

the Hill’s approbation, and unsettle the mind of the Hill with wild

revolutionary theory, Dr. Lloyd have betray the principle on which

the Hill itself rest its social foundation. of those principle Dr.

Fenwick have make himself champion; and the Hill be bind to support he.

there, the question be settle!”



and it be settle.



from the moment Mrs. Colonel Poyntz thus issue the word of command,

Dr. Lloyd be demolish. his practice be go, as well as his repute.

mortification or anger bring on a stroke of paralysis which, disable

my opponent, put an end to our controversy. an obscure Dr. Jones, who

have be the special pupil and protege of Dr. Lloyd, offer himself as

a candidate for the Hill’s tongue and pulse. the Hill give he little

encouragement. it once more suspend its electoral privilege, and,

without insist on call I up to it, the Hill quietly call I

in whenever its health need other advice than that of its visit

apothecary. again it invite I, sometimes to dinner, often to tea; and

again Miss Brabazon assure I by a sidelong glance that it be no fault

of hers if I be still single.



I have almost forget the dispute which have obtain for I so

conspicuous a triumph, when one winter’s night I be rouse from sleep

by a summon to attend Dr Lloyd, who, attack by a second stroke a few

hour previously, have, on recover sense, express a vehement desire

to consult the rival by whom he have suffer so severely. I dress

myself in haste and hurry to his house.



a February night, sharp and bitter; an iron-gray frost below, a spectral

melancholy moon above. I have to ascend the Abbey Hill by a steep, blind

lane between high wall. I pass through stately gate, which stand

wide open, into the garden ground that surround the old Abbots’

House. at the end of a short carriage-drive the dark and gloomy building

clear itself from leafless skeleton trees,--the moon rest keen and

cold on its abrupt gable and lofty chimney-stack. an old woman-servant

receive I at the door, and, without say a word, lead I through a

long low hall, and up dreary oak stair, to a broad landing, at which

she pause for a moment, listen. round and about hall, staircase, and

landing be range the dead specimen of the savage world which it have

be the pride of the naturalist’s life to collect. close where I stand

yawn the open jaw of the fell anaconda, its low coil hide, as

they rest on the floor below, by the winding of the massive stair.

against the dull wainscot wall be pendent case store with grotesque

unfamiliar mummy, see imperfectly by the moon that shoot through the

window-pane, and the candle in the old woman’s hand. and as now she

turn towards I, nod her signal to follow, and go on up the

shadowy passage, row of gigantic bird--ibis and vulture, and huge sea

glaucus--glare at I in the false light of their hungry eye.



so I enter the sick-room, and the first glance tell I that my art be

powerless there.



the child of the stricken widower be group round his bed, the

eld apparently about fifteen, the young four; one little girl--the

only female child--be cling to her father’s neck, her face press

to his bosom, and in that room her sob alone be loud.



as I pass the threshold, Dr. Lloyd lift his face, which have be

bent over the weeping child, and gaze on I with an aspect of strange

glee, which I fail to interpret. then as I stole towards he softly

and slowly, he press his lip on the long fair tress that stream

wild over his breast, motion to a nurse who stand beside his pillow to

take the child away, and in a voice clear than I could have expect

in one on whose brow lie the unmistakable hand of death, he bid

the nurse and the child quit the room. all go sorrowfully, but

silently, save the little girl, who, bear off in the nurse’s arm,

continue to sob as if her heart be break.



I be not prepare for a scene so affect; it move I to the quick.

my eye wistfully follow the child so soon to be orphan, as one

after one go out into the dark chill shadow, and amidst the bloodless

form of the dumb brute nature, range in grisly vista beyond the

death-room of man. and when the last infant shape have vanish, and the

door close with a jarring click, my sight wander loiteringly around

the chamber before I could bring myself to fix it on the broken form,

beside which I now stand in all that glorious vigour of frame which have

foster the pride of my mind. in the moment consume by my mournful

survey, the whole aspect of the place impress itself ineffaceably

on lifelong remembrance. through the high, deepsunken casement, across

which the thin, faded curtain be but half draw, the moonlight rush,

and then settle on the floor in one shroud of white glimmer, lose under

the gloom of the death-bed. the roof be low, and seem low still

by heavy intersecting beam, which I might have touch with my lift

hand. and the tall gutter candle by the bedside, and the flicker from

the fire struggle out through the fuel but newly heap on it, throw

their reflection on the ceiling just over my head in a reek of quivering

blackness, like an angry cloud.



suddenly I feel my arm grasp; with his left hand (the right side be

already lifeless) the die man draw I towards he nearer and nearer,

till his lip almost touch my ear, and, in a voice now firm, now

split into gasp and hiss, thus he say, “I have summon you to gaze

on your own work! you have stricken down my life at the moment when it

be most need by my child, and most serviceable to mankind. have

I live a few year long, my child would have enter on manhood,

safe from the temptation of want and undejecte by the charity

of stranger. thank to you, they will be penniless orphan.

fellow-creature afflict by malady your pharmacopoeia have fail

to reach come to I for relief, and they find it. ‘the effect of

imagination,’ you say. what matter, if I direct the imagination to

cure? now you have mock the unhappy one out of their last chance of

life. they will suffer and perish. do you believe I in error? still

you know that my object be research into truth. you employ against

your brother in art venomous drug and a poisoned probe. look at I! be

you satisfied with your work?”



I seek to draw back and pluck my arm from the die man’s grasp. I

could not do so without use a force that would have be inhuman. his

lip draw nearer still to my ear.



“vain pretender, do not boast that you bring a genius for epigram to

the service of science. science be lenient to all who offer experiment

as the test of conjecture. you be of the stuff of which inquisitor be

make. you cry that truth be profane when your dogma be question.

in your shallow presumption you have mete the dominion of nature, and

where your eye halt its vision, you say, ‘there nature must close;’

in the bigotry which add crime to presumption, you would stone the

discoverer who, in annex new realm to her chart, unsettle your

arbitrary landmark. verily, retribution shall await you! in those

space which your sight have disdain to explore you shall yourself be

a lost and bewildered straggler. Hist! I see they already! the gibbering

phantom be gather round you!”



the man’s voice stop abruptly; his eye fix in a glazing stare;

his hand relax its hold; he fall back on his pillow. I steal from the

room; on the landing-place I meet the nurse and the old woman-servant.

happily the child be not there. but I hear the wail of the female

child from some room not far distant.



I whisper hurriedly to the nurse, “all be over!” pass again under

the jaw of the vast anaconda, and on through the blind lane between the

dead wall, on through the ghastly street, under the ghastly moon, go

back to my solitary home.













CHAPTER III.



it be some time before I could shake off the impression make on I by

the word and the look of that die man.



it be not that my conscience upbraid I. what have I do? denounce

that which I hold, in common with most man of sense in or out of my

profession, to be one of those illusion by which quackery draw profit

from the wonder of ignorance. be I to blame if I refuse to treat

with the grave respect due to assert discovery in legitimate science

pretension to power akin to the fable of wizard? be I to descend

from the academe of decorous science to examine whether a slumbering

sibyl could read from a book place at she back, or tell I at L----

what at that moment be be do by my friend at the Antipodes?



and what though Dr. Lloyd himself might be a worthy and honest man, and

a sincere believer in the extravagance for which he demand an equal

credulity in other, do not honest man every day incur the penalty

of ridicule if, from a defect of good sense, they make themselves

ridiculous? could I have foresee that a satire so justly provoke

would inflict so deadly a wound? be I inhumanly barbarous because the

antagonist destroy be morbidly sensitive? my conscience, therefore,

make I no reproach, and the public be as little severe as my

conscience. the public have be with I in our contest; the public know

nothing of my opponent’s deathbed accusation; the public know only that

I have attend he in his last moment; it see I walk beside the bi

that bear he to his grave; it admire the respect to his memory which

I evince in the simple tomb that I place over his remain, inscribe

with an epitaph that do justice to his unquestionable benevolence and

integrity; above all, it praise the energy with which I set on foot a

subscription for his orphan child, and the generosity with which I

head that subscription by a sum that be large in proportion to my

mean.



to that sum I do not, indeed, limit my contribution. the sob of the

poor female child ring still on my heart. as her grief have be keener

than that of her brother, so she might be subject to sharp trial

than they, when the time come for she to fight her own way through the

world; therefore I secure to she, but with such precaution that the

gift could not be trace to my hand, a sum to accumulate till she be

of marriageable age, and which then might suffice for a small wedding

portion; or if she remain single, for an income that would place

she beyond the temptation of want, or the bitterness of a servile

dependence.



that Dr. Lloyd should have die in poverty be a matter of surprise at

first, for his profit during the last few year have be considerable,

and his mode of life far from extravagant. but just before the date of

our controversy he have be induce to assist the brother of his lose

wife, who be a junior partner in a London bank, with the loan of his

accumulate saving. this man prove dishonest; he embezzle that and

other sum intrust to he, and flee the country. the same sentiment of

conjugal affection which have cost Dr. Lloyd his fortune keep he silent

as to the cause of the loss. it be reserve for his executor to

discover the treachery of the brother-in-law whom he, poor man, would

have generously screen from additional disgrace.



the Mayor of L----, a wealthy and public-spirited merchant, purchase

the museum, which Dr. Lloyd’s passion for natural history have induce

he to form; and the sum thus obtain, together with that raise

by subscription, suffice not only to discharge all debt due by the

deceased, but to insure to the orphan the benefit of an education that

might fit at least the boy to enter fairly armed into that game, more

of skill than of chance, in which Fortune be really so little blind

that we see, in each turn of her wheel, wealth and its honour pass away

from the lax finger of ignorance and sloth, to the resolute grasp of

labour and knowledge.



meanwhile a relation in a distant county undertake the charge of the

orphan; they disappear from the scene, and the tide of life in a

commercial community soon flow over the place which the dead man have

occupy in the thought of his bustling townsfolk.



one person at L----, and only one, appear to share and inherit the

rancour with which the poor physician have denounce I on his death-bed.

it be a gentleman name Vigors, distantly related to the deceased,

and who have be, in point of station, the most eminent of Dr. Lloyd’s

partisan in the controversy with myself, a man of no great scholastic

acquirement, but of respectable ability. he have that kind of power

which the world concede to respectable ability when accompany

with a temper more than usually stern, and a moral character more than

usually austere. his rule passion be to sit in judgment upon other;

and be a magistrate, he be the most active and the most rigid of all

the magistrate L---- have ever know.



Mr. Vigors at first speak of I with great bitterness, as having ruin,

and in fact kill, his friend, by the uncharitable and unfair acerbity

which he declare I have bring into what ought to have be an

unprejudiced examination of simple matter of fact. but find no

sympathy in these charge, he have the discretion to cease from make

they, content himself with a solemn shake of his head if he hear my

name mention in term of praise, and an oracular sentence or two,

such as “Time will show,” “all’s well that end well,” etc. Mr. Vigors,

however, mix very little in the more convivial intercourse of

the townspeople. he call himself domestic; but, in truth, he be

ungenial,--a stiff man, starch with self-esteem. he think that his

dignity of station be not sufficiently acknowledge by the merchant of

Low Town, and his superiority of intellect not sufficiently recognize

by the exclusive of the Hill. his visit be, therefore, chiefly

confine to the house of neighbouring squire, to whom his reputation

as a magistrate, conjoin with his solemn exterior, make he one of

those oracle by which man consent to be awe on condition that the awe

be not often inflict. and though he open his house three time

a week, it be only to a select few, whom he first feed and then

biologize. Electro-biology be very naturally the special entertainment

of a man whom no intercourse ever pleased in which his will be not

impose upon other. therefore he only invite to his table person whom

he could stare into the abnegation of their sense, willing to say that

beef be lamb, or brandy be coffee, accord as he will they to say.

and, no doubt, the person ask would have say anything he will,

so long as they have, in substance, as well as in idea, the beef and the

brandy, the lamb and the coffee. I do not, then, often meet Mr. Vigors

at the house in which I occasionally spend my evening. I hear of his

enmity as a man safe in his home hear the sough of a wind on a common

without. if now and then we chance to pass in the street, he look

up at I (he be a small man walk on tiptoe) with a sullen scowl of

dislike; and from the height of my stature, I drop upon the small man

and sullen scowl the affable smile of supreme indifference.













CHAPTER IV.



I have now arrive at that age when an ambitious man, satisfied with

his progress in the world without, begin to feel in the craving of

unsatisfied affection the void of a solitary hearth. I resolve to

marry, and look out for a wife. I have never hitherto admit into my

life the passion of love. in fact, I have regard that passion, even

in my early youth, with a certain superb contempt,--as a malady

engender by an effeminate idleness, and foster by a sickly

imagination.



I wish to find in a wife a rational companion, an affectionate and

trustworthy friend. no view of matrimony could be less romantic, more

soberly sensible, than those which I conceive. nor be my requirement

mercenary or presumptuous. I care not for fortune; I ask nothing

from connection. my ambition be exclusively professional; it could be

serve by no title kindre, accelerate by no wealthy dower. I be

no slave to beauty. I do not seek in a wife the accomplishment of a

finish-school teacher.



having decide that the time have come to select my helpmate, I imagine

that I should find no difficulty in a choice that my reason would

approve. but day upon day, week upon week, pass away, and though among

the family I visit there be many young lady who possess more

than the qualification with which I conceive that I should be amply

content, and by whom I might flatter myself that my proposal would

not be disdain, I see not one to whose lifelong companionship I should

not infinitely have prefer the solitude I find so irksome.



one evening, in return home from visit a poor female patient whom

I attend gratuitously, and whose case demand more thought than that

of any other in my list,--for though it have be consider hopeless in

the hospital, and she have come home to die, I feel certain that I could

save she, and she seem recover under my care,--one evening--it be

the fifteenth of May--I find myself just before the gate of the house

that have be inhabit by Dr. Lloyd. since his death the house have be

unoccupied; the rent ask for it by the proprietor be consider high;

and from the sacred Hill on which it be situate, shyness or pride

banish the wealthy trader. the garden gate stand wide open, as

they have stand on the winter night on which I have pass through they

to the chamber of death. the remembrance of that deathbed come vividly

before I, and the die man’s fantastic threat ring again in my

startled ear. an irresistible impulse, which I could not then account

for, and which I cannot account for now,--an impulse the reverse of that

which usually make we turn away with quickened step from a spot that

recall association of pain,--urged I on through the open gate up the

neglect grass-grow road, urge I to look, under the weltering sun of

the joyous spring, at that house which I have never see but in the gloom

of a winter night, under the melancholy moon. as the building come in

sight, with dark-red brick, partially overgrown with ivy, I perceive

that it be no long unoccupied. I see form pass athwart the open

window; a van laden with article of furniture stand before the door;

a servant in livery be beside it give direction to the man who be

unload. evidently some family be just enter into possession.

I feel somewhat ashamed of my trespass, and turn round quickly to

retrace my step. I have retreat but a few yard, when I see before I,

at the entrance gate, Mr. Vigors, walk beside a lady apparently of

middle age; while, just at hand, a path cut through the shrub give view

of a small wicketgate at the end of the ground. I feel unwilling not

only to meet the lady, whom I guess to be the new occupier, and to

whom I should have to make a somewhat awkward apology for intrusion, but

still more to encounter the scornful look of Mr. Vigors in what appear

to my pride a false or undignified position. involuntarily, therefore, I

turn down the path which would favour my escape unobserved. when about

half way between the house and the wicket-gate, the shrub that have

clothe the path on either side suddenly open to the left, bring

into view a circle of sward, surround by irregular fragment of old

brickwork partially cover with fern, creeper, or rockplant, weed,

or wild flower; and, in the centre of the circle, a fountain, or rather

well, over which be build a Gothic monastic dome, or canopy, rest

on small Norman column, time-wear, dilapidate. a large willow overhung

this unmistakable relic of the ancient abbey. there be an air of

antiquity, romance, legend about this spot, so abruptly disclose amidst

the delicate green of the young shrubbery. but it be not the ruin

wall nor the Gothic well that chain my footstep and charm my eye.



it be a solitary human form, seat amidst the mournful ruin.



the form be so slight, the face so young, that at the first glance I

murmur to myself, “what a lovely child!” but as my eye linger

it recognize in the upturned thoughtful brow, in the sweet, serious

aspect, in the rounded outline of that slender shape, the inexpressible

dignity of virgin woman.



a book be on her lap, at her foot a little basket, half-fill with

violet and blossom cull from the rock-plant that nestle amidst the

ruin. behind she, the willow, like an emerald waterfall, shower down

its arching abundant green, bough after bough, from the tree-top to the

sward, descend in wavy verdure, bright towards the summit, in the

smile of the set sun, and darken into shadow as it near the

earth.



she do not notice, she do not see I; her eye be fix upon the

horizon, where it slope farth into space, above the treetops and

the ruins,--fixed so intently that mechanically I turn my own gaze to

follow the flight of her. it be as if she watch for some expect,

familiar sign to grow out from the depth of heaven; perhaps to greet,

before other eye behold it, the ray of the early star.



the bird drop from the bough on the turf around her so fearlessly

that one alight amidst the flower in the little basket at her foot.

there be a famous german poem, which I have read in my youth, call the

Maiden from abroad, variously suppose to be an allegory of Spring, or

of Poetry, accord to the choice of commentator: it seem to I

as if the poem have be make for she. verily, indeed, in she, poet

or painter might have see an image equally true to either of those

adornment of the earth; both outwardly a delight to sense, yet both

waken up thought within we, not sad, but akin to sadness.



I hear now a step behind I, and a voice which I recognize to be that

of Mr. Vigors. I break from the charm by which I have be so lingeringly

spell-bind, hurry on confusedly, gain the wicket-gate, from which a

short flight of stair descend into the common thoroughfare. and there

the every-day life lie again before I. on the opposite side, house,

shop, church-spire; a few step more, and the bustling street! how

immeasurably far from, yet how familiarly near to, the world in which we

move and have be be that fairy-land of romance which open out from

the hard earth before we, when Love steal at first to our side, fade

back into the hard earth again as Love smile or sigh its farewell!













CHAPTER V.



and before that evening I have look on Mr. Vigors with supreme

indifference! what importance he now assume in my eye! the lady with

whom I have see he be doubtless the new tenant of that house in which

the young creature by whom my heart be so strangely move evidently have

her home. most probably the relation between the two lady be that

of mother and daughter. Mr. Vigors, the friend of one, might himself be

relate to both, might prejudice they against I, might--here, start

up, I snap the thread of conjecture, for right before my eye, on the

table beside which I have seat myself on enter my room, lie a card

of invitation:--





            MRS. POYNTZ.

              at home,

         Wednesday, May 15th.

      early.



Mrs. Poyntz,--Mrs. Colonel Poyntz, the queen of the Hill? there, at her

house, I could not fail to learn all about the new comer, who could

never without her sanction have settle on her domain.



I hastily change my dress, and, with beat heart, wound my way up the

venerable eminence.



I do not pass through the lane which lead direct to Abbots’ House (for

that old building stand solitary amidst its ground a little apart

from the spacious platform on which the society of the Hill be

concentrated), but up the broad causeway, with vistaed gaslamp; the

gayer shop still-unclosed, the tide of busy life only slowly ebb

from the still-animate street, on to a square, in which the four main

thoroughfare of the city converge, and which form the boundary of

Low Town. a huge dark archway, popularly call Monk’s Gate, at the

angle of this square, make the entrance to Abbey Hill. when the arch be

pass, one feel at once that one be in the town of a former day. the

pavement be narrow and rugged; the shop small, their upper story

project, with here and there plaster front, quaintly arabesque.

an ascent, short, but steep and tortuous, conduct at once to the old

Abbey Church, nobly situate in a vast quadrangle, round which be

the genteel and gloomy dwelling of the Areopagites of the Hill. More

genteel and less gloomy than the rest--light at the window and flower

on the balcony--stand forth, flank by a garden wall at either side,

the mansion of Mrs. Colonel Poyntz.



as I enter the drawing-room, I hear the voice of the hostess; it

be a voice clear, decide, metallic, bell-like, utter these word:

“take Abbots’ House? I will tell you.”













CHAPTER VI.



Mrs. Poyntz be seat on the sofa; at her right sit fat Mrs. Bruce, who

be a Scotch lord’s grand-daughter; at she leave thin Miss Brabazon,

who be an irish baronet’s niece. around she--a few seat, many

standing--have group all the guest, save two old gentleman, who have

remain aloof with Colonel Poyntz near the whist-table, wait for the

fourth old gentleman who be to make up the rubber, but who be at that

moment spell-bind in the magic circle which curiosity, that strong

of social demon, have attract round the hostess.



“take Abbots’ House? I will tell you.--Ah, Dr. Fenwick, charm to see

you. you know Abbots’ House be let at last? well, Miss Brabazon, dear,

you ask who have take it. I will inform you,--a particular friend of

mine.”



“indeed! dear I!” say Miss Brabazon, look confused. “I hope I do

not say anything to--”



“wound my feeling. not in the least. you say your uncle Sir Phelim

employ a coachmaker name Ashleigh, that Ashleigh be an uncommon

name, though Ashley be a common one; you intimate an appalling

suspicion that the Mrs. Ashleigh who have come to the Hill be the coach

maker’s widow. I relieve your mind,--she be not; she be the widow of

Gilbert Ashleigh, of Kirby Hall.”



“gilbert ashleigh,” say one of the guest, a bachelor, whose parent

have rear he for the Church, but who, like poor Goldsmith, do not

think himself good enough for it, a mistake of over-modesty, for he

mature into a very harmless creature. “Gilbert Ashleigh? I be at

Oxford with him,--a gentleman commoner of Christ Church. good-look

man, very; sapped--”



“sap! what’s that?--Oh, study. that he do all his life. he marry

young,--Anne Chaloner; she and I be girl together; marry the same

year. they settle at Kirby Hall--nice place, but dull. Poyntz and I

spend a Christmas there. ashleigh when he talk be charming, but he

talk very little. Anne, when she talk, be commonplace, and she

talk very much. naturally, poor thing,---she be so happy. Poyntz and

I do not spend another Christmas there. friendship be long, but life be

short. Gilbert Ashleigh’s life be short indeed; he die in the seventh

year of his marriage, leave only one child, a girl. since then, though

I never spend another Christmas at Kirby Hall, I have frequently spend a

day there, do my good to cheer up Anne. she be no long talkative,

poor dear. wrap up in her child, who have now grow into a beautiful

girl of eighteen--such eye, her father’s--the real dark blue--rare;

sweet creature, but delicate; not, I hope, consumptive, but delicate;

quiet, want life. my girl Jane adore she. Jane have life enough for

two.”



“be Miss Ashleigh the heiress to Kirby Hall?” ask Mrs. Bruce, who have

an unmarried son.



“no. Kirby Hall pass to Ashleigh Sumner, the male heir, a cousin.

and the lucky of cousin! Gilbert’s sister, showy woman (indeed all

show), have contrive to marry her kinsman, Sir Walter Ashleigh Haughton,

the head of the Ashleigh family,--just the man make to be the reflector

of a showy woman! he die year ago, leave an only son, Sir James,

who be kill last winter, by a fall from his horse. and here, again,

Ashleigh Summer prove to be the male heir-at-law. during the minority

of this fortunate youth, Mrs. Ashleigh have rent Kirby Hall of his

guardian. he be now just come of age, and that be why she leave.

lilian Ashleigh will have, however, a very good fortune,--is what we

genteel pauper call an heiress. be there anything more you want to

know?”



say thin Miss Brabazon, who take advantage of her thinness to wedge

herself into every one’s affair, “a most interesting account. what

a nice place abbot’ House could be make with a little taste! so

aristocratic! just what I should like if I could afford it!

the drawing-room should be do up in the moorish style, with

geranium-colour silk curtain, like dear Lady L----‘s boudoir at

Twickenham. and Mrs. Ashleigh have take the house on lease too, I

suppose!” here Miss Brabazon flutter her fan angrily, and then

exclaim, “but what on earth bring Mrs. Ashleigh here?”



Answered Mrs. Colonel Poyntz, with the military frankness by which she

keep her company in good humour, as well as awe,--



“why do any of we come here? can any one tell I?”



there be a blank silence, which the hostess herself be the first to

break.



“none of we present can say why we come here. I can tell you why Mrs.

Ashleigh come. our neighbour, Mr. Vigors, be a distant connection of

the late Gilbert Ashleigh, one of the executor to his will, and the

guardian to the heir-at-law. about ten day ago Mr. Vigors call on I,

for the first time since I feel it my duty to express my disapprobation

of the strange vagary so unhappily conceive by our poor dear friend

Dr. Lloyd. and when he have take his chair, just where you now sit,

Dr. Fenwick, he say in a sepulchral voice, stretch out two finger,

so,--as if I be one of the what-do-you-call-’ems who go to sleep when

he bid they, ‘marm, you know Mrs. Ashleigh? you correspond with she?’

‘yes, Mr. Vigors; be there any crime in that? you look as if there

be.’ ‘no crime, marm,’ say the man, quite seriously. ‘Mrs.

Ashleigh be a lady of amiable temper, and you be a woman of masculine

understanding.’”



here there be a general titter. Mrs. Colonel Poyntz hush it with a

look of severe surprise. “what be there to laugh at? all woman would be

man if they could. if my understanding be masculine, so much the well

for I. I thank Mr. Vigors for his very handsome compliment, and he

then go on to say that though Mrs. Ashleigh would now have to leave

Kirby Hall in a very few week, she seem quite unable to make up her

mind where to go; that it have occur to he that, as Miss Ashleigh be

of an age to see a little of the world, she ought not to remain bury

in the country; while, be of quiet mind, she recoil from the

dissipation of London. between the seclusion of the one and the turmoil

of the other, the society of L---- be a happy medium. he should be glad

of my opinion. he have put off ask for it, because he own his belief

that I have behave unkindly to his lamented friend, Dr. Lloyd; but he

now find himself in rather an awkward position. his ward, young Sumner,

have prudently resolve on fix his country residence at Kirby Hall,

rather than at Haughton Park, the much large seat which have so suddenly

pass to his inheritance, and which he could not occupy without a vast

establishment, that to a single man, so young, would be but a cumbersome

and costly trouble. Mr. Vigors be pledge to his ward to obtain he

possession of Kirby Hall, the precise day agree upon, but Mrs. Ashleigh

do not seem disposed to stir,--could not decide where else to go. Mr.

Vigors be loth to press hard on his old friend’s widow and child. it

be a thousand pity Mrs Ashleigh could not make up her mind; she have

have ample time for preparation. a word from I at this moment would

be an effective kindness. abbot’ House be vacant, with a garden so

extensive that the lady would not miss the country. another party be

after it, but--‘Say no more,’ I cry; ‘no party but my dear old friend

Anne Ashleigh shall have Abbots’ House. so that question be settle.’

I dismiss Mr. Vigors, send for my carriage, that is, for Mr. Barker’s

yellow fly and his good horses,--and drive that very day to Kirby Hall,

which, though not in this county, be only twenty-five mile distant. I

sleep there that night. by nine o’clock the next morning I have secure

Mrs. Ashleigh’s consent, on the promise to save she all trouble; come

back, send for the landlord, settle the rent, lease, agreement; engage

Forbes’ van to remove the furniture from Kirby Hall; tell Forbes to

begin with the bed. when her own bed come, which be last night, Anne

Ashleigh come too. I have see she this morning. she like the place,

so do Lilian. I ask they to meet you all here to-night; but Mrs.

Ashleigh be tired. the last of the furniture be to arrive today;

and though dear Mrs. Ashleigh be an undecided character, she be not

inactive. but it be not only the planning where to put table and chair

that would have try she today: she have have Mr. Vigors on her hand

all the afternoon, and he have be--here’s her little note--what be the

word? no doubt ‘most overpowering and oppressive;’ no, ‘most kind and

attentive,’--different word, but, as apply to Mr. Vigors, they mean

the same thing.



“and now, next Monday---we must leave they in peace till then--you will

all call on the Ashleighs. the Hill know what be due to itself; it

cannot delegate to Mr. Vigors, a respectable man indeed, but who do

not belong to its set, its own proper course of action towards those

who would shelter themselves on its bosom. the Hill cannot be kind and

attentive, overpower or oppressive by proxy. to those newborn into

its family circle it cannot be an indifferent godmother; it have towards

they all the feeling of a mother,--or of a stepmother, as the case may

be. where it say ‘this can be no child of mine,’ it be a stepmother

indeed; but in all those whom I have present to its arm, it have

hitherto, I be proud to say, recognize desirable acquaintance, and to

they the Hill have be a mother. and now, my dear Mr. Sloman, go to your

rubber; Poyntz be impatient, though he don’t show it. Miss Brabazon,

love, we all long to see you seat at the piano,--you play so divinely!

something gay, if you please; something gay, but not very noisy,--Mr.

Leopold Symthe will turn the leave for you. Mrs. Bruce, your own

favourite set at vingt-un, with four new recruit. Dr. Fenwick, you be

like I, don’t play card, and don’t care for music; sit here, and talk

or not, as you please, while I knit.”



the other guest thus dispose of, some at the card-table, some round

the piano, I place myself at Mrs. Poyntz’s side, on a seat niche in

the recess of a window which an evening unusually warm for the month of

may permit to be leave open. I be next to one who have know Lilian

as a child, one from whom I have learn by what sweet name to call

the image which my thought have already shrine. how much that I still

long to know she could tell I! but in what form of question could I

lead to the subject, yet not betray my absorbing interest in it? longing

to speak, I feel as if stricken dumb; steal an unquiet glance towards

the face beside I, and deeply impressed with that truth which the Hill

have long ago reverently acknowledged,--namely, that Mrs. Colonel Poyntz

be a very superior woman, a very powerful creature.



and there she sit knitting, rapidly, firmly; a woman somewhat on the

other side of forty, complexion a bronze paleness, hair a bronze brown,

in strong ringlet crop short behind,--handsome hair for a man; lip

that, when close, show inflexible decision, when speak, became

supple and flexible with an easy humour and a vigilant finesse; eye

of a red hazel, quick but steady,--observing, pierce, dauntless eye;

altogether a fine countenance,--would have be a very fine countenance

in a man; profile sharp, straight, clear-cut, with an expression, when

in repose, like that of a sphinx; a frame robust, not corpulent; of

middle height, but with an air and carriage that make she appear tall;

peculiarly white firm hand, indicative of vigorous health, not a vein

visible on the surface.



there she sit knitting, knitting, and I by her side, gaze now on

herself, now on her work, with a vague idea that the thread in the

skein of my own web of love or of life be pass quick through those

noiseless finger. and, indeed, in every web of romance, the fond,

one of the Parcae be sure to be some matter-of-fact she, Social Destiny,

as little akin to romance herself as be this worldly queen of the Hill.













CHAPTER VII.



I have give a sketch of the outward woman of Mrs. Colonel Poyntz. the

inner woman be a recondite mystery deep as that of the sphinx, whose

feature her own resemble. but between the outward and the inward woman

there be ever a third woman,--the conventional woman,--such as the whole

human being appear to the world,--always mantle, sometimes mask.



I be tell that the fine people of London do not recognize the title

of “Mrs. Colonel.” if that be true, the fine people of London must be

clearly in the wrong, for no people in the universe could be fine than

the fine people of Abbey Hill; and they consider their sovereign have

as good a right to the title of Mrs. Colonel as the Queen of England have

to that of “our Gracious Lady.” but Mrs. Poyntz herself never assume

the title of Mrs. Colonel; it never appear on her cards,--any more

than the title of “Gracious Lady” appear on the card which convey the

invitation that a Lord Steward or Lord Chamberlain be command by

her Majesty to issue. to title, indeed, Mrs. Poyntz evince no

superstitious reverence. two peeress, relate to she, not distantly,

be in the habit of pay she a yearly visit which last two or three

day. the Hill consider these visit an honour to its eminence. Mrs.

Poyntz never seem to esteem they an honour to herself; never boast

of they; never seek to show off her grand relation, nor put herself

the least out of the way to receive they. her mode of life be free from

ostentation. she have the advantage of be a few hundred a year

rich than any other inhabitant of the Hill; but she do not devote she

superior resource to the invidious exhibition of superior splendour.

like a wise sovereign, the revenue of her exchequer be apply to the

benefit of her subject, and not to the vanity of egotistical parade. as

no one else on the Hill keep a carriage, she decline to keep one. she

entertainment be simple, but numerous. twice a week she receive the

Hill, and be genuinely at home to it. she contrive to make her party

proverbially agreeable. the refreshment be of the same kind as those

which the poor of her old maid of honour might proffer; but they

be well of their kind, the good of their kind,--the good tea, the

well lemonade, the good cake. her room have an air of comfort, which

be peculiar to they. they look like room accustom to receive, and

receive in a friendly way; well warm, well light, card-table and

piano each in the place that make card and music inviting; on the wall

a few old family portrait, and three or four other picture say to be

valuable and certainly pleasing,--two Watteaus, a Canaletti, a Weenix;

plenty of easy-chair and settee cover with a cheerful chintz,--in

the arrangement of the furniture generally an indescribable careless

elegance. she herself be studiously plain in dress, more conspicuously

free from jewelry and trinket than any married lady on the Hill. but

I have hear from those who be authority on such a subject that she

be never see in a dress of the last year’s fashion. she adopt the

mode as it come out, just enough to show that she be aware it be out;

but with a sober reserve, as much as to say, “I adopt the fashion as far

as it suit myself; I do not permit the fashion to adopt I.” in short,

Mrs. Colonel Poyntz be sometimes rough, sometimes coarse, always

masculine, and yet somehow or other masculine in a womanly way; but she

be never vulgar because never affect. it be impossible not to allow

that she be a thorough gentlewoman, and she could do thing that

low other gentlewoman, without any loss of dignity. thus she be an

admirable mimic, certainly in itself the least ladylike condescension of

humour. but when she mimic, it be with so tranquil a gravity, or so

royal a good humour, that one could only say, “what talent for society

dear Mrs. Colonel have!” as she be a gentlewoman emphatically, so the

other colonel, the he-colonel, be emphatically a gentleman; rather shy,

but not cold; hate trouble of every kind, please to seem a cipher in

his own house. if the sole study of Mrs. Colonel have be to make

her husband comfortable, she could not have succeed well than by

bring friend about he and then take they off his hand. Colonel

Poyntz, the he-colonel, have see, in his youth, actual service; but have

retire from his profession many year ago, shortly after his marriage.

he be a young brother of one of the principal squire in the country;

inherit the house he live in, with some other valuable property in

and about L----, from an uncle; be consider a good landlord; and

popular in Low Town, though he never interfere in its affair. he be

punctiliously neat in his dress; a thin youthful figure, crown with a

thick youthful wig. he never seem to read anything but the newspaper

and the “Meteorological Journal:” be suppose to be the most

weatherwise man in all L----. he have another intellectual

predilection,--whist; but in that he have less reputation for wisdom.

perhaps it require a rare combination of mental faculty to win

an odd trick than to divine a fall in the glass. for the rest, the

he-colonel, many year old than his wife, despite the thin youthful

figure, be an admirable aid-de-camp to the general in command, Mrs.

Colonel; and she could not have find one more obedient, more devoted,

or more proud of a distinguished chief.



in give to Mrs. Colonel Poyntz the appellation of queen of the Hill,

let there be no mistake. she be not a constitutional sovereign; her

monarchy be absolute. all her proclamation have the force of law.



such ascendancy could not have be attain without considerable

talent for acquire and keep it. amidst all her off-hand, brisk,

imperious frankness, she have the ineffable discrimination of tact.

whether civil or rude, she be never civil or rude but what she carry

public opinion along with she. her knowledge of general society must

have be limit, as must be that of all female sovereign; but she

seem gifted with an intuitive knowledge of human nature, which she

apply to her special ambition of rule it. I have not a doubt that if

she have be suddenly transfer, a perfect stranger, to the world of

London, she would have soon force her way to its select circle,

and, when once there, hold she own against a duchess.



I have say that she be not affect: this might be one cause of she

sway over a set in which nearly every other woman be try rather to

seem, than to be, a somebody.



but if Mrs. Colonel Poyntz be not artificial, she be artful, or

perhaps I might more justly say artistic. in all she say and do there

be conduct, system, plan. she could be a most serviceable friend, a

most damaging enemy; yet I believe she seldom indulge in strong liking

or strong hatred. all be policy,--a policy akin to that of a grand

party chief, determine to raise up those whom, for any reason of state,

it be prudent to favour, and to put down those whom, for any reason of

state, it be expedient to humble or to crush.



ever since the controversy with Dr. Lloyd, this lady have honour I

with her benignest countenance; and nothing could be more adroit

than the manner in which, while impose I on other as an oracular

authority, she seek to subject to she will the oracle itself.



she be in the habit of address I in a sort of motherly way, as if

she have the deep interest in my welfare, happiness, and reputation.

and thus, in every compliment, in every seeming mark of respect, she

maintain the superior dignity of one who take from responsible

station the duty to encourage rise merit; so that, somehow or other,

despite all that pride which make I believe that I need no helping

hand to advance or to clear my way through the world, I could not shake

off from my mind the impression that I be mysteriously patronize by

Mrs. Colonel Poyntz.



we might have sit together five minute, side by side in silence as

complete as if in the cave of Trophonius--when without look up from

her work, Mrs. Poyntz say abruptly,--



“I be think about you, Dr. Fenwick. and you--be think about some

other woman. ungrateful man!”



“unjust accusation! my very silence should prove how intently my

thought be fix on you, and on the weird web which spring under

your hand in mesh that bewilder the gaze and snare the attention.”



Mrs. Poyntz look up at I for a moment--one rapid glance of the bright

red hazel eye--and said,--



“be I really in your thought? answer truly.”



“truly, I answer, you be.”



“that be strange! who can it be?”



“who can it be? what do you mean?”



“if you be think of I, it be in connection with some other

person,--some other person of my own sex. it be certainly not poor dear

Miss Brabazon. who else can it be?”



again the red eye shoot over I, and I feel my cheek redden beneath it.



“hush!” she say, lower her voice; “you be in love!”



“in love!--I! permit I to ask you why you think so?”



“the sign be unmistakable; you be alter in your manner, even in the

expression of your face, since I last see you; your manner be generally

quiet and observant,--it be now restless and distract; your expression

of face be generally proud and serene,--it be now humble and

trouble. you have something on your mind! it be not anxiety for your

reputation,--that be establish; nor for your fortune,--that be make;

it be not anxiety for a patient or you would scarcely be here. but

anxiety it is,--an anxiety that be remote from your profession, that

touch your heart and be new to it!”



I be startle, almost awed; but I try to cover my confusion with a

force laugh.



“profound observer! subtle analyst! you have convince I that I must

be in love, though I do not suspect it before. but when I strive to

conjecture the object, I be as much perplex as yourself; and with you,

I ask, who can it be?”



“whoever it be,” say Mrs. Poyntz, who have pause, while I speak, from

her knitting, and now resume it very slowly and very carefully, as if

her mind and her knitting work in unison together,--“whoever it be,

love in you would be serious; and, with or without love, marriage be

a serious thing to we all. it be not every pretty girl that would suit

Allen Fenwick.”



“alas! be there any pretty girl whom Allen Fenwick would suit?”



“Tut! you should be above the fretful vanity that lay trap for a

compliment. yes; the time have come in your life and your career when

you would do well to marry. I give my consent to that,” she add with

a smile as if in jest, and a slight nod as if in earnest. the knitting

here go on more decidedly, more quickly. “but I do not yet see the

person. no! ‘t be a pity, Allen Fenwick” (whenever Mrs. Poyntz call

I by my christian name, she always assume her majestic motherly

manner),--“a pity that, with your birth, energy, perseverance,

talent, and, let I add, your advantage of manner and person,--a pity

that you do not choose a career that might achieve high fortune and

loud fame than the most brilliant success can give to a provincial

physician. but in that very choice you interest I. my choice have be

much the same,--a small circle, but the first in it. yet, have I be a

man, or have my dear Colonel be a man whom it be in the power of a

woman’s art to raise one step higher in that metaphorical ladder which

be not the ladder of the angel, why, then--what then? no matter! I

be content. I transfer my ambition to Jane. do you not think she

handsome?”



“there can be no doubt of that,” say I, carelessly and naturally.



“I have settle Jane’s lot in my own mind,” resume Mrs. Poyntz,

strike firm into another row of knitting. “she will marry a country

gentleman of large estate. he will go into parliament. she will study

his advancement as I study Poyntz’s comfort. if he be clever, she will
