help to make he a minister; if he be not clever, his wealth will make

she a personage, and lift he into a personage’s husband. and, now that

you see I have no matrimonial design on you, Allen Fenwick, think if it

will be worth while to confide in I. possibly I may be useful--”



“I know not how to thank you; but, as yet, I have nothing to confide.”



while thus say, I turn my eye towards the open window beside which

I sit. it be a beautiful soft night, the May moon in all her splendour.

the town stretch, far and wide, below with all its numberless

lights,--below, but somewhat distant; an intervene space be cover,

here, by the broad quadrangle (in the midst of which stand, massive and

lonely, the grand old church), and, there, by the garden and scatter

cottage or mansion that clothe the side of the hill.



“be not that house,” I say, after a short pause, “yonder with the three

gable, the one in which--in which poor Dr. Lloyd live--Abbots’ House?”



I speak abruptly, as if to intimate my desire to change the subject of

conversation. my hostess stop her knitting, half rise, look forth.



“yes. but what a lovely night! how be it that the moon blend into

harmony thing of which the sun only mark the contrast? that stately

old church tower, gray with its thousand year, those vulgar tile-roof

and chimney-pot raw in the freshness of yesterday,--now, under the

moonlight, all melt into one indivisible charm!”



as my hostess thus speak, she have leave her seat, take her work with

she, and pass from the window into the balcony. it be not often that

Mrs. Poyntz condescend to admit what be call “sentiment” into

the range of her sharp, practical, worldly talk; but she do so at

times,--always, when she do, give I the notion of an intellect much

too comprehensive not to allow that sentiment have a place in this life,

but keep it in its proper place, by that mixture of affability and

indifference with which some high-bear beauty allow the genius, but

check the presumption, of a charming and penniless poet. for a few

minute her eye rove over the scene in evident enjoyment; then, as

they slowly settle upon the three gable of Abbots’ House, her face

regain that something of hardness which belong to its decide

character; her finger again mechanically resume her knitting, and she

say, in her clear, unsoftened, metallic chime of voice, “can you guess

why I take so much trouble to oblige Mr. Vigors and locate Mrs. Ashleigh

yonder?”



“you favour we with a full explanation of your reason.”



“some of my reason; not the main one. People who undertake the task

of govern other, as I do, be their rule a kingdom or a hamlet, must

adopt a principle of government and adhere to it. the principle that

suit well with the Hill be respect for the Proprieties. we have not

much money; entre nous, we have no great rank. our policy be, then, to

set up the Proprieties as an influence which money must court and rank

be afraid of. I have learn just before Mr. Vigors call on I that

Lady Sarah Bellasis entertain the idea of hire Abbots’ House.

London have set its face against she; a provincial town would be more

charitable. an earl’s daughter, with a good income and an awfully bad

name, of the good manner and of the bad moral, would have make sad

havoc among the Proprieties. how many of our prim old maid would

have desert tea and Mrs. Poyntz for champagne and her ladyship! the

Hill be never in so imminent a danger. rather than Lady Sarah Bellasis

should have have that house, I would have take it myself, and stock it

with owl.



“Mrs. Ashleigh turn up just in the critical moment. Lady Sarah be

foil, the Proprieties safe, and so that question be settle.”



“and it will be pleasant to have your early friend so near you.”



Mrs. Poyntz lift her eye full upon I.



“do you know Mrs. Ashleigh?”



“not in the least.”



“she have many virtue and few idea. she be commonplace weak, as I

be commonplace strong. but commonplace weak can be very lovable. her

husband, a man of genius and learning, give she his whole heart,--a

heart worth have; but he be not ambitious, and he despise the

world.”



“I think you say your daughter be very much attach to Miss Ashleigh?

do her character resemble her mother’s?”



I be afraid while I speak that I should again meet Mrs. Poyntz’s

search gaze, but she do not this time look up from her work.



“no; Lilian be anything but commonplace.”



“you describe she as have delicate health; you imply a hope that

she be not consumptive. I trust that there be no serious reason for

apprehend a constitutional tendency which at her age would require

the most careful watching!”



“I trust not. if she be to die--Dr. Fenwick, what be the matter?”



so terrible have be the picture which this woman’s word have bring

before I, that I start as if my own life have receive a shock.



“I beg pardon,” I say falteringly, press my hand to my heart; “a

sudden spasm here,--it be over now. you be say that--that--”



“I be about to say-” and here Mrs. Poyntz lay her hand lightly on

mine,--“I be about to say that if lilian Ashleigh be to die, I should

mourn for she less than I might for one who value the thing of the

earth more. but I believe there be no cause for the alarm my word so

inconsiderately excited in you. her mother be watchful and devoted; and

if the least thing ail Lilian, she would call in medical advice. Mr.

Vigors would, I know, recommend Dr. Jones.”



close our conference with those stinging word, Mrs. Poyntz here

turn back into the drawing-room.



I remain some minute on the balcony, disconcerted, enraged. with

what consummate art have this practise diplomatist wound herself into my

secret! that she have read my heart well than myself be evident from

that parthian shaft, barb with Dr. Jones, which she have shoot over her

shoulder in retreat. that from the first moment in which she have decoy

I to her side, she have detect “the something” on my mind, be perhaps

but the ordinary quickness of female penetration. but it be with no

ordinary craft that the whole conversation afterwards have be so shaped

as to learn the something, and lead I to reveal the some one to whom

the something be link. for what purpose? what be it to she? what

motive could she have beyond the mere gratification of curiosity?

perhaps, at first, she think I have be catch by her daughter’s showy

beauty, and hence the half-friendly, half-cynical frankness with which

she have avow her ambitious project for that young lady’s matrimonial

advancement. satisfy by my manner that I cherish no presumptuous

hope in that quarter, her scrutiny be doubtless continue from that

pleasure in the exercise of a wily intellect which impel schemer and

politician to an activity for which, without that pleasure itself,

there would seem no adequate inducement. and besides, the rule passion

of this petty sovereign be power; and if knowledge be power, there be

no well instrument of power over a contumacious subject than that hold

on his heart which be gain in the knowledge of its secret.



but “secret”! have it really come to this? be it possible that the mere

sight of a human face, never beheld before, could disturb the whole

tenor of my life,--a stranger of whose mind and character I know

nothing, whose very voice I have never hear? it be only by the

intolerable pang of anguish that have rent my heart in the word,

carelessly, abruptly speak, “if she be to die,” that I have feel how

the world would be change to I, if indeed that face be see in it no

more! yes, secret it be no long to myself, I love! and like all on

whom love descend, sometimes softly, slowly, with the gradual wing of

the cushat settle down into its nest, sometimes with the swoop of the

eagle on his unsuspecting quarry, I believe that none ever before love

as I love; that such love be an abnormal wonder, make solely for

I, and I for it. then my mind insensibly hush its angry and more

turbulent thought, as my gaze rest upon the roof-top of Lilian’s

home, and the shimmering silver of the moonlit willow, under which I have

see she gaze into the roseate heaven.













CHAPTER VIII.



when I return to the drawing-room, the party be evidently about to

break up. those who have group round the piano be now assemble round

the refreshment-table. the cardplayer have rise, and be settle or

discuss gain and loss. while I be search for my hat, which

I have somewhere mislay, a poor gentleman, torment by tic-doloureux,

creep timidly up to me,--the proud and the poor of all the

hidalgo settle on the Hill. he could not afford a fee for a

physician’s advice; but pain have humble his pride, and I see at a

glance that he be consider how to take a surreptitious advantage of

social intercourse, and obtain the advice without pay the fee. the

old man discover the hat before I do, stoop, take it up, extend

it to I with the profound bow of the old school, while the other hand,

clench and quivering, be press into the hollow of his cheek,

and his eye meet mine with wistful mute entreaty. the instinct of my

profession seize I at once. I could never behold suffering without

forget all else in the desire to relieve it.



“you be in pain,” say I, softly. “sit down and describe the symptom.

here, it be true, I be no professional doctor, but I be a friend who be

fond of doctoring, and know something about it.”



so we sit down a little apart from the other guest, and after a few

question and answer, I be pleased to find that his “tic” do not

belong to the less curable kind of that agonizing neuralgia. I be

especially successful in my treatment of similar suffering, for which I

have discover an anodyne that be almost specific. I write on a leaf of

my pocketbook a prescription which I feel sure would be efficacious, and

as I tear it out and place it in his hand, I chance to look up, and

see the hazel eye of my hostess fix upon I with a kind and soft

expression than they often condescend to admit into their cold and

penetrate lustre. at that moment, however, her attention be draw

from I to a servant, who enter with a note, and I hear he say,

though in an undertone, “from Mrs. Ashleigh.”



she open the note, read it hastily, order the servant to wait

without the door, retire to her writing-table, which stand near the

place at which I still linger, rest her face on her hand, and seem

musing. her meditation be very soon over. she turn her head, and to

my surprise, beckon to I. I approach.



“sit here,” she whisper: “turn your back towards those people, who be

no doubt watch we. read this.”



she place in my hand the note she have just receive. it contain but a

few word, to this effect:--





   DEAR MARGARET,--I be so distressed.  since I write to you a few

   hour ago, Lilian be take suddenly ill, and I fear seriously.  what

   medical man should I send for?  let my servant have his name and

   address.



                                                    A.  A.



I spring from my seat.



“stay,” say Mrs. Poyntz. “would you much care if I send the servant to

Dr. Jones?”



“ah, madam, you be cruel! what have I do that you should become my

enemy?”



“Enemy! no. you have just befriend one of my friend. in this world

of fool intellect should ally itself with intellect. no; I be not your

enemy! but you have not yet ask I to be your friend.”



here she put into my hand a note she have write while thus speak.

“receive your credential. if there be any cause for alarm, or if I can

be of use, send for I.” resume the work she have suspend, but with

linger, uncertain finger, she add, “so far, then, this be settle.

Nay, no thank; it be but little that be settle as yet.”













CHAPTER IX.



in a very few minute I be once more in the ground of that old gable

house; the servant, who go before I, enter they by the stair and

the wicket-gate of the private entrance; that way be the short.

so again I pass by the circling glade and the monastic well,--sward,

tree, and ruin all suffuse in the limpid moonlight.



and now I be in the house; the servant take up-stair the note with

which I be charge, and a minute or two afterwards return and

conduct I to the corridor above, in which Mrs. Ashleigh receive I.

I be the first to speak.



“your daughter--be--be--not seriously ill, I hope. what be it?”



“hush!” she say, under her breath. “will you step this way for a

moment?” she pass through a doorway to the right. I follow she,

and as she place on the table the light she have be hold, I look

round with a chill at the heart,--it be the room in which Dr. Lloyd have

die. impossible to mistake. the furniture indeed be change, there be

no bed in the chamber; but the shape of the room, the position of the

high casement, which be now wide open, and through which the moonlight

stream more softly than on that drear winter night, the great square

beam intersect the low ceiling,--all be impress vividly on my

memory. the chair to which Mrs. Ashleigh beckon I be place just on

the spot where I have stand by the bedhead of the die man.



I shrink back,--I could not have seat myself there. so I remained

lean against the chimney-piece, while Mrs. Ashleigh tell her story.



she say that on their arrival the day before, Lilian have be in more

than usually good health and spirit, delight with the old house,

the ground, and especially the nook by the Monk’s well, at which Mrs.

Ashleigh have leave she that evening in order to make some purchase in

the town, in company with Mr. Vigors. when Mrs. Ashleigh return, she

and Mr. Vigors have seek Lilian in that nook, and Mrs. Ashleigh then

detect, with a mother’s eye, some change in Lilian which alarm she.

she seem listless and deject, and be very pale; but she deny that

she feel unwell. on regain the house she have sit down in the room in

which we then were,--“which,” say Mrs. Ashleigh, “as it be not require

for a sleeping-room, my daughter, who be fond of reading, wish to fit

up as her own morning-room, or study. I leave she here and go into the

drawing-room below with Mr. Vigors. when he quit I, which he do

very soon, I remain for nearly an hour give direction about the

placing of furniture, which have just arrive, from our late residence.

I then go up-stair to join my daughter, and to my terror find she

apparently lifeless in her chair. she have faint away.”



I interrupt Mrs. Ashleigh here. “have Miss Ashleigh be subject to

fainting fit?”



“no, never. when she recover she seem bewildered, disinclined to

speak. I get she to bed, and as she then fall quietly to sleep, my mind

be relieve. I think it only a pass effect of excitement, in a

change of abode; or cause by something like malaria in the atmosphere

of that part of the ground in which I have find she seat.”



“very likely. the hour of sunset at this time of year be try to

delicate constitution. go on.”



“about three quarter of an hour ago she wake up with a loud cry, and

have be ever since in a state of great agitation, weep violently,

and answer none of my question. yet she do not seem light-headed,

but rather what we call hysterical.”



“you will permit I now to see she. take comfort; in all you tell I I

see nothing to warrant serious alarm.”













CHAPTER X.



to the true physician there be an inexpressible sanctity in the sick

chamber. at its threshold the more human passion quit their hold on

his heart. love there would be profanation; even the grief permit to

other he must put aside. he must enter that room--a calm intelligence.

he be disabled for his mission if he suffer aught to obscure the

keen quiet glance of his science. age or youth, beauty or deformity,

innocence or guilt, merge their distinction in one common

attribute--human suffering appeal to human skill.



Woe to the household in which the trust Healer feel not on his

conscience the solemn obligation of his glorious art! reverently as in

a temple, I stand in the virgin’s chamber. when her mother place she

hand in mine, and I feel the throb of its pulse, I be aware of no

quick beat of my own heart. I look with a steady eye on the face

more beautiful from the flush that deepen the delicate hue of

the young cheek, and the lustre that brighten the dark blue of the

wandering eye. she do not at first heed I, do not seem aware of

my presence; but keep murmur to herself word which I could not

distinguish.



at length, when I speak to she, in that low, soothe tone which we

learn at the sick-bed, the expression of her face alter suddenly; she

pass the hand I do not hold over her forehead, turn round, look

at I full and long, with unmistakable surprise, yet not as if the

surprise displease her,--less the surprise which recoil from the

sight of a stranger than that which seem doubtfully to recognize an

unexpected friend. yet on the surprise there seem to creep something

of apprehension, of fear; her hand tremble, her voice quiver, as she

said,--



“can it be, can it be? be I awake? Mother, who be this?”



“only a kind visitor, Dr. Fenwick, send by Mrs. Poyntz, for I be uneasy

about you, darling. how be you now?”



“well. strangely well.”



she remove her hand gently from mine, and with an involuntary modest

shrinking turn towards Mrs. Ashleigh, draw her mother towards

herself, so that she become at once hide from I.



satisfy that there be here no delirium, nor even more than the slight

and temporary fever which often accompany a sudden nervous attack in

constitution peculiarly sensitive, I retire noiselessly from the

room, and go, not into that which have be occupy by the ill-fate

Naturalist, but down-stair into the drawing-room, to write my

prescription. I have already send the servant off with it to the

chemist’s before Mrs. Ashleigh join I.



“she seem recover surprisingly; her forehead be cool; she

be perfectly self-possess, only she cannot account for her own

seizure,--cannot account either for the fainting or the agitation with

which she awake from sleep.”



“I think I can account for both. the first room in which she

enter--that in which she faint--have its window open; the side of

the window be overgrow with rank creep plant in full blossom. Miss

Ashleigh have already predispose herself to injurious effect from the

effluvia by fatigue, excitement, imprudence in sit out at the fall

of a heavy dew. the sleep after the fainting fit be the more disturbed,

because nature, always alert and active in subject so young, be

make its own effort to right itself from an injury. nature have nearly

succeed. what I have prescribe will a little aid and accelerate that

which nature have yet to do, and in a day or two I do not doubt that your

daughter will be perfectly restore. only let I recommend care to avoid

exposure to the open air during the close of the day. let she avoid also

the room in which she be first seize, for it be a strange phenomenon

in nervous temperament that a nervous attack may, without visible

cause, be repeat in the same place where it be first experienced. you

have well shut up the chamber for at least some week, burn fire in

it, repaint and paper it, sprinkle chloroform. you be not, perhaps,

aware that Dr. Lloyd die in that room after a prolonged illness. suffer

I to wait till your servant return with the medicine, and let I

employ the interval in ask you a few question. Miss Ashleigh, you

say, never have a fainting fit before. I should presume that she be not

what we call strong. but have she ever have any illness that alarm you?”



“never.”



“no great liability to cold and cough, to attack of the chest or

lung?”



“certainly not. still I have fear that she may have a tendency to

consumption. do you think so? your question alarm I!”



“I do not think so; but before I pronounce a positive opinion, one

question more. you say you have fear a tendency to consumption. be

that disease in her family? she certainly do not inherit it from you.

but on her father’s side?”



“her father,” say Mrs. Ashleigh, with tear in her eye, “die young,

but of brain fever, which the medical man say be bring on by over

study.”



“Enough, my dear madam. what you say confirm my belief that your

daughter’s constitution be the very opposite to that in which the seed

of consumption lurk. it be rather that far nobler constitution,

which the keenness of the nervous susceptibility render delicate but

elastic,--as quick to recover as it be to suffer.”



“thank you, thank you, Dr. Fenwick, for what you say. you take a load

from my heart; for Mr. Vigors, I know, think lilian consumptive, and

Mrs. Poyntz have rather frighten I at time by hint to the same

effect. but when you speak of nervous susceptibility, I do not quite

understand you. my daughter be not what be commonly call nervous. she

temper be singularly even.”



“but if not excitable, should you also say that she be not

impressionable? the thing which do not disturb her temper may, perhaps,

deject her spirit. do I make myself understand?”



“yes, I think I understand your distinction; but I be not quite sure

if it apply. to most thing that affect the spirit she be not more

sensitive than other girl, perhaps less so; but she be certainly very

impressionable in some thing.”



“in what?”



“she be more moved than any one I ever know by object in external

nature, rural scenery, rural sound, by music, by the book that she

reads,--even book that be not work of imagination. perhaps in all

this she take after her poor father, but in a more marked degree,--at

least, I observe it more in she; for he be very silent and reserve.

and perhaps also her peculiarity have be foster by the seclusion

in which she have be bring up. it be with a view to make she a

little more like girl of her own age that our friend, Mrs. Poyntz,

induce I to come here. Lilian be reconcile to this change; but she

shrink from the thought of London, which I should have prefer. her

poor father could not endure London.”



“Miss Ashleigh be fond of reading?”



“yes, she be fond of reading, but more fond of musing. she will sit by

herself for hour without book or work, and seem as abstract as if in

a dream. she be so even in her early childhood. then she would tell

I what she have be conjure up to herself. she would say that she have

see--positively see--beautiful land far away from earth; flower and

tree not like ours. as she grow old this visionary talk displease

I, and I scold she, and say that if other hear she, they would

think that she be not only silly but very untruthful. so of late year

she never venture to tell I what, in such dreamy moment, she suffer

herself to imagine; but the habit of musing continue still. do you not

agree with Mrs. Poyntz that the good cure would be a little cheerful

society amongst other young people?”



“certainly,” say I, honestly, though with a jealous pang. “but here

come the medicine. will you take it up to she, and then sit with she

half an hour or so? by that time I expect she will be asleep. I will

wait here till you return. oh, I can amuse myself with the newspaper

and book on your table. stay! one caution: be sure there be no flower

in Miss Ashleigh’s sleep-room. I think I see a treacherous rose-tree

in a stand by the window. if so, banish it.”



leave alone, I examine the room in which, oh, thought of joy! I have

surely now win the claim to become a privileged guest. I touch the

book Lilian must have touch; in the article of furniture, as yet

so hastily dispose that the settle look of home be not about they,

I still know that I be gaze on thing which her mind must associate

with the history of her young life. that luteharp must be surely her,

and the scarf, with a girl’s favourite colours,--pure white and pale

blue,--and the bird-cage, and the childish ivory work-case, with

implement too pretty for use,--all speak of she.



it be a blissful, intoxicate revery, which Mrs. Ashleigh’s entrance

disturb.



Lilian be sleep calmly. I have no excuse to linger there any long.



“I leave you, I trust, with your mind quite at ease,” say I. “you will

allow I to call to-morrow, in the afternoon?”



“oh, yes, gratefully.”



Mrs. Ashleigh hold out her hand as I make towards the door.



be there a physician who have not feel at time how that ceremonious fee

throw he back from the garden-land of humanity into the market-place

of money,--seems to put he out of the pale of equal friendship, and

say, “true, you have give health and life. Adieu! there, you be pay

for it!” with a poor person there would have be no dilemma, but

Mrs. Ashleigh be affluent: to depart from custom here be almost

impertinence. but have the penalty of my refusal be the doom of never

again behold Lilian, I could not have take her mother’s gold. so I

do not appear to notice the hand hold out to I, and pass by with a

quicken step.



“but, Dr. Fenwick, stop!”



“no, ma’am, no! Miss Ashleigh would have recover as soon without I.

whenever my aid be really want, then--but Heaven grant that time may

never come! we will talk again about she to-morrow.”



I be gone,--now in the garden ground, odorous with blossom; now in the

lane, inclose by the narrow wall; now in the deserted street, over

which the moon shine full as in that winter night when I hurry from

the chamber of death. but the street be not ghastly now, and the moon

be no long Hecate, that dreary goddess of awe and spectre, but the

sweet, simple Lady of the Stars, on whose gentle face lover have gaze

ever since (if that guess of astronomer be true) she be part from

earth to rule the tide of its deep from afar, even as love, from love

divided, rule the heart that yearn towards it with mysterious law.













CHAPTER XI.



with what increase benignity I listen to the patient who visit I

the next morning! the whole human race seem to be worthy of love,

and I long to diffuse amongst all some ray of the glorious hope that

have dawn upon my heart. my first call, when I go forth, be on the

poor young woman from whom I have be return the day before, when an

impulse, which seem like a fate, have lure I into the ground where I

have first see Lilian. I feel grateful to this poor patient; without her

Lilian herself might be yet unknown to I.



the girl’s brother, a young man employ in the police, and whose pay

support a widow mother and the suffer sister, receive I at the

threshold of the cottage.



“oh, sir, she be so much well to-day; almost free from pain. will she

live now; can she live?”



“if my treatment have really do the good you say; if she be really

well under it, I think her recovery may be pronounce. but I must

first see she.”



the girl be indeed wonderfully well. I feel that my skill be

achieve a signal triumph; but that day even my intellectual pride be

forget in the luxurious unfolding of that sense of heart which have so

newly wake into blossom.



as I recrosse the threshold, I smile on the brother, who be still

linger there,--



“your sister be save, Wady. she need now chiefly wine, and good though

light nourishment; these you will find at my house; call there for they

every day.”



“God bless you, sir! if ever I can serve you--” his tongue falter, he

could say no more.



serve I, Allen Fenwick--that poor policeman! I, whom a king could not

serve! what do I ask from earth but Fame and Lilian’s heart? throne

and bread man win from the aid of other; fame and woman’s heart he can

only gain through himself.



so I stride gayly up the hill, through the iron gate, into the fairy

ground, and stand before Lilian’s home.



the man-servant, on open the door, seem somewhat confused, and say

hastily before I spoke,--



“not at home, sir; a note for you.”



I turn the note mechanically in my hand; I feel stunned.



“not at home! Miss Ashleigh cannot be out. how be she?”



“well, sir, thank you.”



I still could not open the note; my eye turn wistfully towards

the window of the house, and there--at the drawing-room window--I

encounter the scowl of Mr. Vigors. I colour with resentment, divine

that I be dismiss, and walk away with a proud crest and a firm

step.



when I be out of the gate, in the blind lane, I open the note. it

begin formally. “Mrs. Ashleigh present her compliment,” and go on to

thank I, civilly enough, for my attendance the night before, would not

give I the trouble to repeat my visit, and inclose a fee, double the

amount of the fee prescribe by custom. I fling the money, as an asp

that have sting I, over the high wall, and tear the note into shred.

having thus idly vent my rage, a dull gnawing sorrow come heavily down

upon all other emotion, stifle and replace they. at the mouth of

the lane I halt. I shrink from the thought of the crowded street

beyond; I shrink yet more from the routine of duty, which stretch

before I in the desert into which daily life be so suddenly smite. I

sit down by the roadside, shade my dejected face with a nervous hand.

I look up as the sound of step reach my ear, and see Dr. Jones

come briskly along the lane, evidently from Abbots’ House. he must

have be there at the very time I have call. I be not only dismiss

but supplant. I rise before he reach the spot on which I have seat

myself, and go my way into the town, go through my allotted round of

professional visit; but my attention be not so tenderly devote,

my skill so genially quicken by the glow of benevolence, as my poor

patient have find they in the morning. I have say how the physician

should enter the sick-room. “a Calm Intelligence!” but if you strike a

blow on the heart, the intellect suffer. little worth, I suspect, be

my “calm intelligence” that day. Bichat, in his famous book upon life

and death, divide life into two classes,--animal and organic. man’s

intellect, with the brain for its centre, belong to life animal; his

passion to life organic, centre in the heart, in the viscera. alas!

if the noble passion through which alone we lift ourselves into the

moral realm of the sublime and beautiful really have their centre in the

life which the very vegetable, that live organically, share with we!

and, alas! if it be that life which we share with the vegetable, that

can cloud, obstruct, suspend, annul that life centre in the brain,

which we share with every be howsoever angelic, in every star

howsoever remote, on whom the Creator bestow the faculty of thought!













CHAPTER XII.



but suddenly I remember Mrs. Poyntz. I ought to call on she. so I

close my round of visit at her door. the day be then far advanced,

and the servant politely inform I that Mrs. Poyntz be at dinner. I

could only leave my card, with a message that I would pay my respect to

she the next day. that evening I receive from she this note:--





   Dear Dr. Fenwick,--I regret much that I cannot have the pleasure of

   see you to-morrow.  Poyntz and I be go to visit his brother, at

   the other end of the county, and we start early.  we shall be away some

   day.  sorry to hear from Mrs. Ashleigh that she have be persuade by

   Mr. Vigors to consult Dr. Jones about Lilian. Vigors and Jones both

   frighten the poor mother, and insist upon consumptive tendency.

   unluckily, you seem to have say there be little the matter.  some

   doctor train their practice as some preacher fill their churches,--by

   adroit use of the appeal to terror.  you do not want patient, Dr.

   Jones do.  and, after all, well perhaps as it be.

                           your, etc.

                                                 M. Poyntz.



to my more selfish grief, anxiety for Lilian be now add. I have see

many more patient die from be mistreat for consumption than from

consumption itself. and Dr. Jones be a mercenary, cunning, needy man,

with much crafty knowledge of human foible, but very little skill in

the treatment of human malady. my fear be soon confirm. a few

day after I hear from Miss Brabazon that Miss Ashleigh be seriously

ill, keep her room. Mrs. Ashleigh make this excuse for not immediately

return the visit which the Hill have shower upon she. Miss Brabazon

have see Dr. Jones, who have shake his head, say it be a serious case;

but that time and care (his time and his care!) might effect wonder.



how stealthily at the dead of the night I would climb the Hill and look

towards the window of the old sombre house,--one window, in which a

light burn dim and mournful, the light of a sick-room,--of her!



at length Mrs. Poyntz come back, and I enter her house, having fully

resolve beforehand on the line of policy to be adopt towards the

potentate whom I hope to secure as an ally. it be clear that neither

disguise nor half-confidence would baffle the penetration of so keen an

intellect, nor propitiate the good will of so imperious and resolute a

temper. perfect frankness here be the wise prudence; and after

all, it be most agreeable to my own nature, and most worthy of my own

honour.



luckily, I find Mrs. Poyntz alone, and take in both mine the hand

she somewhat coldly extend to I, I say, with the earnestness of

suppress emotion,--



“you observe when I last see you, that I have not yet ask you to be

my friend. I ask it now. listen to I with all the indulgence you can

vouchsafe, and let I at least profit by your counsel if you refuse to

give I your aid.”



rapidly, briefly, I go on to say how I have first see Lilian, and how

sudden, how strange to myself, have be the impression which that first

sight of she have produce.



“you remark the change that have come over I,” say I; “you divine

the cause before I divine it myself,--divined it as I sit there beside

you, think that through you I might see, in the freedom of social

intercourse, the face that be then haunt I. you know what have

since pass. Miss Ashleigh be ill; her case be, I be convince,

wholly misunderstood. all other feeling be merge in one sense of

anxiety,--of alarm. but it have become due to I, due to all, to incur

the risk of your ridicule even more than of your reproof, by state to

you thus candidly, plainly, bluntly, the sentiment which render alarm

so poignant, and which, if scarcely admissible to the romance of some

wild dreamy boy, may seem an unpardonable folly in a man of my year and

my sober calling,--due to I, to you, to Mrs. Ashleigh, because still

the dear thing in life to I be honour. and if you, who know Mrs.

Ashleigh so intimately, who must be more or less aware of her plan or

wish for her daughter’s future,--if you believe that those plan or

wish lead to a lot far more ambitious than an alliance with I could

offer to Miss Ashleigh, then aid Mr. Vigors in exclude I from the

house; aid I in suppress a presumptuous, visionary passion. I cannot

enter that house without love and hope at my heart; and the threshold

of that house I must not cross if such love and such hope would be a sin

and a treachery in the eye of its owner. I might restore Miss Ashleigh

to health; her gratitude might--I cannot continue. this danger must

not be to I nor to she, if her mother have view far above such a

son-in-law. and I be the more bound to consider all this while it be yet

time, because I hear you state that Miss Ashleigh have a fortune, be

what would be here term an heiress. and the full consciousness that

whatever fame one in my profession may live to acquire, do not

open those vista of social power and grandeur which be open

by profession to my eye less noble in themselves,--that full

consciousness, I say, be force upon I by certain word of your own.

for the rest, you know my descent be sufficiently recognize as that

amidst well-bear gentry to have render I no mesalliance to family

the most proud of their ancestry, if I have keep my hereditary estate and

avoid the career that make I useful to man. but I acknowledge that

on enter a profession such as mine--enter any profession except

that of arm or the senate--all leave their pedigree at its door, an

erase or dead letter. all must come as equals, high-bear or low-bear,

into that arena in which man ask aid from a man as he make himself; to

they his dead forefather be idle dust. therefore, to the advantage of

birth I cease to have a claim. I be but a provincial physician, whose

station would be the same have he be a cobbler’s son. but gold retain

its grand privilege in all rank. he who have gold be remove from

the suspicion that attach to the greedy fortune-hunter. my private

fortune, swell by my saving, be sufficient to secure to any one I

marry a large settlement than many a wealthy squire can make. I need

no fortune with a wife; if she have one, it would be settle on herself.

pardon these vulgar detail. now, have I make myself understand?”



“fully,” answer the Queen of the Hill, who have listen to I quietly,

watchfully, and without one interruption, “fully; and you have do

well to confide in I with so generous an unreserve. but before I say

far, let I ask, what would be your advice for Lilian, suppose

that you ought not to attend she? you have no trust in Dr. Jones;

neither have I. and Annie Ashleigh’s note receive to-day, beg I

to call, justify your alarm. still you think there be no tendency to

consumption?”



“of that I be certain so far as my slight glimpse of a case that to I,

however, seem a simple and not uncommon one, will permit. but in

the alternative you put--that my own skill, whatever its worth, be

forbidden--my earnest advice be that Mrs. Ashleigh should take she

daughter at once to London, and consult there those great authority to

whom I cannot compare my own opinion or experience; and by their counsel

abide.”



Mrs. Poyntz shade her eye with her hand for a few moment, and seem

in deliberation with herself. then she say, with her peculiar smile,

half grave, half ironical,--



“in matter more ordinary you would have win I to your side long ago.

that Mr. Vigors should have presume to cancel my recommendation to a

settler on the Hill be an act of rebellion, and involve the honour

of my prerogative; but I suppress my indignation at an affront so

unusual, partly out of pique against yourself, but much more, I think,

out of regard for you.”



“I understand. you detect the secret of my heart; you know that Mrs.

Ashleigh would not wish to see her daughter the wife of a provincial

physician.”



“be I sure, or be you sure, that the daughter herself would accept that

fate; or if she accept it, would not repent?”



“do you not think I the vainest of man when I say this,--that I cannot

believe I should be so enthral by a feeling at war with my reason,

unfavoure by anything I can detect in my habit of mind, or even by the

dream of a youth which exalt science and exclude love, unless I be

intimately convinced that Miss Ashleigh’s heart be free, that I could

win, and that I could keep it! ask I why I be convince of this, and I

can tell you no more why I think that she could love I than I can tell

you why I love she!”



“I be of the world, worldly; but I be a woman, womanly,--though I may

not care to be think it. and, therefore, though what you say be,

regard in a worldly point of view, sheer nonsense, regard in a

womanly point of view, it be logically sound. but still you cannot know

lilian as I do. your nature and her be in strong contrast. I do not

think she be a safe wife for you. the pure, the most innocent creature

imaginable, certainly that, but always in the seventh heaven; and you

in the seventh heaven just at this moment, but with an irresistible

gravitation to the solid earth, which will have its way again when

the honeymoon be over--I do not believe you two would harmonize by

intercourse. I do not believe Lilian would sympathize with you, and I be

sure you could not sympathize with she throughout the long dull course

of this workday life. and, therefore, for your sake, as well as her, I

be not displeased to find that Dr. Jones have replace you; and now, in

return for your frankness, I say frankly, do not go again to that house.

conquer this sentiment, fancy, passion, whatever it be. and I will

advise Mrs. Ashleigh to take Lilian to town. shall it be so settle?”



I could not speak. I bury my face in my hand--misery, misery,

desolation!



I know not how long I remain thus silent, perhaps many minute. at

length I feel a cold, firm, but not ungentle hand place upon mine; and

a clear, full, but not discourage voice say to me,--



“leave I to think well over this conversation, and to ponder well the

value of all you have show that you so deeply feel. the interest of

life do not fill both scale of the balance. the heart, which do not

always go in the same scale with the interest, still have its weight in

the scale oppose to they. I have hear a few wise man say, as many a

silly woman say, ‘well be unhappy with one we love, than be happy

with one we love not.’ do you say that too?”



“with every thought of my brain, every beat of my pulse, I say it.”



“after that answer, all my questioning cease. you shall hear from I

to-morrow. by that time, I shall have see Annie and Lilian. I shall

have weigh both scale of the balance,--and the heart here, Allen

Fenwick, seem very heavy. go, now. I hear foot on the stair, Poyntz

bring up some friendly gossiper; gossiper be spy.”



I pass my hand over my eye, tearless, but how tear would have

relieve the anguish that burden they! and, without a word, go down

the stair, meet at the landing-place Colonel Poyntz and the old man

whose pain my prescription have cure. the old man be whistle a merry

tune, perhaps first learn on the playground. he break from it to

thank, almost to embrace I, as I slide by he. I seize his jocund

blessing as a good oman, and carry it with I as I pass into the

broad sunlight. solitary--solitary! should I be so evermore?













CHAPTER XIII.



the next day I have just dismiss the last of my visit patient, and

be about to enter my carriage and commence my round, when I receive a

twisted note contain but these words:--





   Call on I to-day, as soon as you can.



                                           M. Poyntz.



a few minute afterwards I be in Mrs. Poyntz’s drawing-room.



“well, Allen Fenwick” say she, “I do not serve friend by half. no

thank! I but adhere to a principle I have lay down for myself. I spend

last evening with the Ashleighs. Lilian be certainly much altered,--very

weak, I fear very ill, and I believe very unskillfully treat by Dr.

Jones. I feel that it be my duty to insist on a change of physician;

but there be something else to consider before decide who that

physician should be. I be bind, as your confidante, to consult your

own scruple of honour. of course I could not say point-blank to Mrs.

Ashleigh, ‘Dr. Fenwick admire your daughter, would you object to he

as a son-in-law?’ of course I could not touch at all on the secret with

which you intrust I; but I have not the less arrive at a conclusion,

in agreement with my previous belief, that not be a woman of the

world, Annie Ashleigh have none of the ambition which woman of the world

would conceive for a daughter who have a good fortune and considerable

beauty; that her predominant anxiety be for her child’s happiness, and

her predominant fear be that her child will die. she would never oppose

any attachment which Lilian might form; and if that attachment be for

one who have preserve her daughter’s life, I believe her own heart would

gratefully go with her daughter’s. so far, then, as honour be concern,

all scruple vanish.”



I spring from my seat, radiant with joy. Mrs. Poyntz dryly continue:

“you value yourself on your common-sense, and to that I address a few

word of counsel which may not be welcome to your romance. I say that

I do not think you and Lilian would suit each other in the long

run; reflection confirm I in that supposition. do not look at I so

incredulously and so sadly. listen, and take heed. ask yourself what, as

a man whose day be devoted to a laborious profession, whose ambition

be entwine with its success, whose mind must be absorb in its

pursuits,--ask yourself what kind of a wife you would have seek to

win; have not this sudden fancy for a charming face rush over your

well reason, and obliterate all previous plan and resolution.

surely some one with whom your heart would have be quite at rest; by

whom your thought would have be undistracte from the channel into

which your calling should concentrate their flow; in short, a serene

companion in the quiet holiday of a trustful home! be it not so?”



“you interpret my own thought when they have turn towards marriage.

but what be there in Lilian Ashleigh that should mar the picture you

have draw?”



“what be there in Lilian Ashleigh which in the least accord with the

picture? in the first place, the wife of a young physician should not

be his perpetual patient. the more he love she, and the more worthy she

may be of love, the more her case will haunt he wherever he go. when

he return home, it be not to a holiday; the patient he most care for,

the anxiety that most gnaw he, await he there.”



“but, good heaven! why should lilian Ashleigh be a perpetual patient?

the sanitary resource of youth be incalculable. And--”



“let I stop you; I cannot argue against a physician in love! I will

give up that point in dispute, remain convince that there be

something in Lilian’s constitution which will perplex, torment, and

baffle you. it be so with her father, whom she resemble in face and in

character. he show no symptom of any grave malady. his outward form

be, like Lilian’s, a model of symmetry, except in this, that, like

her, it be too exquisitely delicate; but when seemingly in the midst

of perfect health, at any slight jar on the nerve he would become

alarmingly ill. I be sure that he would die young, and he do so.”



“ay, but Mrs. Ashleigh say that his death be from brain-fever, bring

on by over-study. rarely, indeed, do woman so fatigue the brain. no

female patient, in the range of my practice, ever die of purely mental

exertion.”



“of purely mental exertion, no; but of heart emotion, many female

patient, perhaps? oh, you own that! I know nothing about nerve; but I

suppose that, whether they act on the brain or the heart, the result

to life be much the same if the nerve be too finely string for life’s

daily wear and tear. and this be what I mean, when I say you and Lilian

will not suit. as yet, she be a mere child; her nature undeveloped, and

her affection therefore untried. you might suppose that you have win she

heart; she might believe that she give it to you, and both be deceive.

if fairy nowadays condescend to exchange their offspring with those

of mortal, and if the popular tradition do not represent a fairy

changeling as an ugly peevish creature, with none of the grace of its

parent, I should be half inclined to suspect that Lilian be one of the

elfin people. she never seem at home on earth; and I do not think she

will ever be content with a prosaic earthly lot. now I have tell you

why I do not think she will suit you. I must leave it to yourself to

conjecture how far you would suit she. I say this in due season, while

you may set a guard upon your impulse; while you may yet watch, and

weigh, and meditate; and from this moment on that subject I say no more.

I lend advice, but I never throw it away.”



she come here to a dead pause, and begin put on her bonnet and

scarf, which lie on the table beside she. I be a little chill by her

word, and yet more by the blunt, shrewd, hard look and manner which

aid the effect of their delivery; but the chill melt away in the

sudden glow of my heart when she again turn towards I and said,--



“of course you guess, from these preliminary caution, that you be

go into danger? Mrs. Ashleigh wish to consult you about Lilian, and

I propose to take you to her house.”



“oh, my friend, my dear friend, how can I ever repay you?” I catch she

hand, the white firm hand, and lift it to my lip.



she draw it somewhat hastily away, and lay it gently on my shoulder,

say, in a soft voice, “Poor Allen, how little the world know either

of we! but how little perhaps we know ourselves! come, your carriage be

here? that be right; we must put down Dr. Jones publicly and in all our

state.”



in the carriage Mrs. Poyntz tell I the purport of that conversation

with Mrs. Ashleigh to which I owe my re-introduction to Abbots’ House.

it seem that Mr. Vigors have call early the morning after my first

visit! have evince much discomposure on hear that I have be

summon! dwelt much on my injurious treatment of Dr. Lloyd, whom,

as distantly relate to himself, and he (Mr. Vigors) be distantly

connect with the late Gilbert Ashleigh, he endeavour to fasten upon

his listener as one of her husband’s family, whose quarrel she be bind

in honour to take up. he speak of I as an infidel “taint with french

doctrine,” and as a practitioner rash and presumptuous; prove his own

freedom from presumption and rashness by flatly decide that my opinion

must be wrong. previously to Mrs. Ashleigh’s migration to L----, Mr.

Vigors have interest she in the pretended phenomenon of mesmerism. he

have consult a clairvoyante, much esteem by poor Dr. Lloyd, as

to Lilian’s health, and the clairvoyante have declare she to be

constitutionally predispose to consumption. Mr. Vigors persuade Mrs.

Ashleigh to come at once with he and see this clairvoyante herself,

arm with a lock of Lilian’s hair and a glove she have wear, as the

medium of mesmerical rapport.



the clairvoyante, one of those I have publicly denounce as an impostor,

naturally enough denounce I in return. on be ask solemnly by

Mr. Vigors “to look at Dr. Fenwick and see if his influence would be

beneficial to the subject,” the sibyl have become violently agitated, and

say that, “when she look at we together, we be envelop in a black

cloud; that this portend affliction and sinister consequence; that

our rapport be antagonistic.” Mr. Vigors then tell she to dismiss my

image, and conjure up that of Dr. Jones. Therewith the somnambule become

more tranquil, and say: “Dr. Jones would do well if he would be guide

by high light than his own skill, and consult herself daily as to the

proper remedy. the good remedy of all would be mesmerism. but since

Dr. Lloyd’s death, she do not know of a mesmerist, sufficiently gift,

in affinity with the patient.” in fine, she impress and awe Mrs.

Ashleigh, who return in haste, summon Dr. Jones, and dismiss

myself.



“I could not have conceive Mrs. Ashleigh to be so utterly want in

common-sense,” say I. “she talk rationally enough when I see she.”



“she have common-sense in general, and plenty of the sense most common,”

 answer Mrs. Poyntz; “but she be easily lead and easily frighten

wherever her affection be concern, and therefore, just as easily as

she have be persuade by Mr. Vigors and terrify by the somnambule,

I persuade she against the one, and terrify she against the other. I

have positive experience on my side, since it be clear that Lilian have

be get rapidly bad under Dr. Jones’s care. the main obstacle

I have to encounter in induce Mrs. Ashleigh to consult you again

be, first, her reluctance to disoblige Mr. Vigors, as a friend and

connection of Lilian’s father; and, secondly, her sentiment of shame

in re-invite your opinion after having treat you with so little

respect. both these difficulty I take on myself. I bring you to her

house, and, on leave you, I shall go on to Mr. Vigors, and tell he

what be do be my doing, and not to be undo by he; so that matter

be settle. indeed, if you be out of the question, I should not suffer

Mr. Vigors to re-introduce all these mummery of clairvoyance and

mesmerism into the precinct of the Hill. I do not demolish a man I

really like in Dr. Lloyd, to set up a Dr. Jones, whom I despise, in his

stead. clairvoyance on Abbey Hill, indeed! I see enough of it before.”



“true; your strong intellect detect at once the absurdity of the whole

pretence,--the falsity of mesmerism, the impossibility of clairvoyance.”



“no, my strong intellect do nothing of the kind. I do not know whether

mesmerism be false or clairvoyance impossible; and I don’t wish to know.

all I do know be, that I see the Hill in great danger,--young lady

allow themselves to be put to sleep by gentleman, and pretend

they have no will of their own against such fascination! improper and

shocking! and Miss Brabazon begin to prophesy, and Mrs. Leopold

Smythe question she maid (whom Dr. Lloyd declare to be highly

gifted) as to all the secret of her friend. when I see this, I say,

‘the Hill be becoming demoralize; the Hill be make itself ridiculous;

the Hill must be save!’ I remonstrate with Dr. Lloyd as a friend; he

remain obdurate. I annihilate he as an enemy, not to I but to the

State. I slay my good lover for the good of Rome. now you know why I

take your part,--not because I have any opinion, one way or the other,

as to the truth or falsehood of what Dr. Lloyd assert; but I have a

strong opinion that, whether they be true or false, his notion be

those which be not to be allow on the Hill. and so, Allen Fenwick,

that matter be settle.”



perhaps at another time I might have feel some little humiliation

to learn that I have be honour with the influence of this great

potentate not as a champion of truth, but as an instrument of policy;

and I might have own to some twinge of conscience in having assist

to sacrifice a fellow-seeker after science--mislead, no doubt, but

prefer his independent belief to his worldly interest--and sacrifice

he to those deity with whom science be ever at war,--the prejudice

of a Clique sanctify into the Proprieties of the World. but at that

moment the word I hear make no perceptible impression on my mind. the

gable of Abbots’ House be visible above the evergreen and lilac;

another moment, and the carriage stop at the door.













CHAPTER XIV.



Mrs. Ashleigh receive we in the dining-room. her manner to I, at

first, be a little confused and shy. but my companion soon communicate

something of her own happy ease to her gentler friend. after a short

conversation we all three go to Lilian, who be in a little room on

the ground-floor, fit up as her study. I be glad to perceive that my

interdict of the deathchamber have be respect.



she recline on a sofa near the window, which be, however, jealously

close; the light of the bright May-day obscure by blind and

curtain; a large fire on the hearth; the air of the room that of a

hot-house,--the ignorant, senseless, explode system of nursing into

consumption those who be confine on suspicion of it! she do not heed

we as we enter noiselessly; her eye be droop languidly on the

floor, and with difficulty I suppress the exclamation that rise to my

lip on see she. she seem within the last few day so change, and

on the aspect of the countenance there be so profound a melancholy!

but as she slowly turn at the sound of our footstep, and her eye meet

mine, a quick blush come into the wan cheek, and she half rise, but sink

back as if the effort exhaust she. there be a struggle for breath,

and a low hollow cough. be it possible that I have be mistake, and

that in that cough be hear the warning knell of the most insidious

enemy to youthful life?



I sit down by her side; I lure she on to talk of indifferent

subjects,--the weather, the gardens, the bird in the cage, which be

place on the table near she. her voice, at first low and feeble, become

gradually strong, and her face light up with a child’s innocent,

playful smile. no, I have not be mistake! that be no lymphatic,

nerveless temperament, on which consumption fasten as its lawful prey;

here there be no hectic pulse, no hurried waste of the vital flame.

quietly and gently I make my observation, address my question,

apply my stethoscope; and when I turn my face towards her mother’s

anxious, eager eye, that face tell my opinion; for her mother sprang

forward, clasp my hand, and say, through her struggle tears,--



“you smile! you see nothing to fear?”



“fear! no, indeed! you will soon be again yourself, Miss Ashleigh, will

you not?”



“yes,” she say, with her sweet laugh, “I shall be well now very soon.

but may I not have the window open; may I not go into the garden? I so

long for fresh air.”



“no, no, darling,” exclaim Mrs. Ashleigh, “not while the east wind

last. Dr. Jones say on no account. on no account, Dr. Fenwick, eh?”



“will you take my arm, Miss Ashleigh, for a few turn up and down

the room?” say I. “we will then see how far we may rebel against Dr.

Jones.”



she rise with some little effort, but there be no cough. at first

her step be languid; it become light and more elastic after a few

moment.



“let she come out,” say I to Mrs. Ashleigh. “the wind be not in the

east, and, while we be out, pray bid your servant lower to the last bar

in the grate that fire,--only fit for Christmas.”



“But--”



“ah, no but! he be a poor doctor who be not a stern despot.”



so the straw hat and mantle be send for. Lilian be wrap with

unnecessary care, and we all go forth into the garden. involuntarily

we take the way to the Monk’s well, and at every step Lilian seem to

revive under the brace air and temperate sun. we pause by the well.



“you do not feel fatigued, Miss Ashleigh?”



“no.”



“but your face seems change. it be grow sad.”



“not sad.”



“sad than when I first see it,--saw it when you be seat here!” I

say this in a whisper. I feel her hand tremble as it lie on my arm.



“you see I seat here!”



“yes. I will tell you how some day.”



Lilian lift her eye to mine, and there be in they that same surprise

which I have notice on my first visit,--a surprise that perplex I,

blend with no displeasure, but yet with a something of vague alarm.



we soon return to the house.



Mrs. Ashleigh make I a sign to follow she into the drawing-room,

leave Mrs. Poyntz with Lilian.



“well?” say she, tremblingly.



“permit I to see Dr. Jones’s prescription. thank you. ay, I think

so. my dear madam, the mistake here have be in depress nature

instead of strengthen; in narcotic instead of stimulant. the main

stimulant which leave no reaction be air and light. promise I that I

may have my own way for a week,--that all I recommend will be implicitly

heed?”



“I promise. but that cough,--you notice it?”



“yes. the nervous system be terribly lower, and nervous exhaustion be

a strange impostor; it imitate all manner of complaint with which

it have no connection. the cough will soon disappear! but pardon my

question. Mrs. Poyntz tell I that you consult a clairvoyant about

your daughter. do Miss Ashleigh know that you do so?”



“no; I do not tell she.”



“I be glad of that. and pray, for Heaven’s sake, guard she against all

that may set her thinking on such subject. above all, guard she against

concentre attention on any malady that your fear erroneously ascribe

to she. it be amongst the phenomena of our organization that you cannot

closely rivet your consciousness on any part of the frame, however

healthy, but it will soon begin to exhibit morbid sensibility. try

to fix all your attention on your little finger for half an hour, and

before the half hour be over the little finger will be uneasy, probably

even painful. how serious, then, be the danger to a young girl, at the

age in which imagination be most active, most intense, if you force

upon she a belief that she be in danger of a mortal disease! it be a

peculiarity of youth to brood over the thought of early death much more

resignedly, much more complacently, than we do in mature year. impress

on a young imaginative girl, as free from pulmonary tendency as you

and I be, the conviction that she must fade away into the grave, and

though she may not actually die of consumption, you instil slow poison

into her system. Hope be the natural aliment of youth. you impoverish

nourishment where you discourage hope. as soon as this temporary illness

be over, reject for your daughter the melancholy care which seem to she

own mind to mark she out from other of her age. rear she for the air,

which be the kind life-giver; to sleep with open window: to be out

at sunrise. nature will do more for she than all our drug can do. you

have be hitherto fear nature; now trust to she.”



here Mrs. Poyntz join we, and have, while I have be speak,

write my prescription and some general injunction, I close my advice

with an appeal to that powerful protectress.



“this, my dear madam, be a case in which I need your aid, and I ask

it. Miss Ashleigh should not be leave with no other companion than her

mother. a change of face be often as salutary as a change of air.

if you could devote an hour or two this very evening to sit with Miss

Ashleigh, to talk to she with your usual cheerfulness, and--”



“Annie,” interrupt Mrs. Poyntz, “I will come and drink tea with you at

half-past seven, and bring my knitting; and perhaps, if you ask he, Dr.

Fenwick will come too! he can be tolerably entertaining when he like

it.”



“it be too great a tax on his kindness, I fear,” say Mrs. Ashleigh.

“but,” she add cordially, “I should be grateful indeed if he would

spare we an hour of his time.”



I murmur an assent which I endeavour to make not too joyous.



“so that matter be settle,” say Mrs. Poyntz; “and now I shall go to

Mr. Vigors and prevent his further interference.”



“oh, but, Margaret, pray don’t offend him,--a connection of my poor

dear Gilbert’s. and so tetchy! I be sure I do not know how you’ll manage

to--”



“to get rid of he? never fear. as I manage everything and everybody,”

 say Mrs. Poyntz, bluntly. so she kiss her friend on the forehead,

give I a gracious nod, and, decline the offer of my carriage, walk

with her usual brisk, decide tread down the short path towards the

town.



Mrs. Ashleigh timidly approach I, and again the furtive hand

bashfully insinuate the hateful fee.



“stay,” say I; “this be a case which need the most constant watching.

I wish to call so often that I should seem the most greedy of doctor if

my visit be to be compute at guineas. let I be at ease to effect

my cure; my pride of science be involve in it. and when amongst all the

young lady of the Hill you can point to none with a fresher bloom, or

a fair promise of healthful life, than the patient you intrust to my

care, why, then the fee and the dismissal. Nay, nay; I must refer you to

our friend Mrs. Poyntz. it be so settle with she before she bring I

here to displace Dr. Jones.” Therewith I escape.













CHAPTER XV.



in less than a week Lilian be convalescent; in less than a fortnight

she regain her usual health,--nay, Mrs. Ashleigh declare that she

have never know her daughter appear so cheerful and look so well. I have

establish a familiar intimacy at Abbots’ House; most of my evening

be spend there. as horse exercise form an important part of my

advice, Mrs. Ashleigh have purchase a pretty and quiet horse for her

daughter; and, except the weather be very unfavourable, Lilian now

ride daily with Colonel Poyntz, who be a notable equestrian, and often

accompany by Miss Jane Poyntz, and other young lady of the Hill.

I be generally relieve from my duty in time to join she as she

return homeward. thus we make innocent appointment, openly, frankly,

in her mother’s presence, she tell I beforehand in what direction

excursion have be plan with Colonel Poyntz, and I promise to fall

in with the party--if my avocation would permit. at my suggestion,

Mrs. Ashleigh now open her house almost every evening to some of the

neighbouring family; Lilian be thus habituate to the intercourse of

young person of her own age. music and dancing and childlike game make

the old house gay. and the Hill gratefully acknowledge to Mrs. Poyntz,

“that the Ashleighs be indeed a great acquisition.”



but my happiness be not uncheckered. in thus unselfishly surround

Lilian with other, I feel the anguish of that jealousy which be

inseparable from those early stage of love, when the lover as yet

have win no right to that self-confidence which can only spring from the

assurance that he be love.



in these social reunion I remain aloof from Lilian. I see she court

by the gay young admirer whom her beauty and her fortune draw around

her,--her soft face brighten in the exercise of the dance, which the

gravity of my profession rather than my year forbid to join; and her

laugh, so musically subdue, ravish my ear and fret my heart

as if the laugh be a mockery on my sombre self and my presumptuous

dream. but no, suddenly, shyly, her eye would steal away from those

about she, steal to the corner in which I sit, as if they miss I,

and, meet my own gaze, their light soften before they turn away;

and the colour on her cheek would deepen, and to her lip there come a

smile different from the smile that it shed on other. and then--and

then--all jealousy, all sadness vanish, and I feel the glory which

blend with the grow belief that we be love.



in that diviner epoch of man’s mysterious passion, when idea of

perfection and purity, vague and fugitive before, start forth and

concentre themselves round one virgin shape,--that rise out from the

sea of creation, welcome by the Hours and adorn by the Graces,--how

the thought that this archetype of sweetness and beauty single himself

from the million, single himself for her choice, ennoble and lift

up his being! though after-experience may rebuke the mortal’s illusion,

that mistook for a daughter of Heaven a creature of clay like himself,

yet for a while the illusion have grandeur. though it come from the

sense which shall later oppress and profane it, the sense at first

shrink into shade, awe and hush by the presence that charm they. all

that be bright and good in the man have soar up like long-dormant

instinct of Heaven, to greet and to hallow what to he seem life’s

fair dream of the heavenly! take the wing from the image of love,

and the god disappear from the form!



thus, if at moment jealous doubt make my torture, so the moment’s

relief from it suffice for my rapture. but I have a cause for disquiet

less acute but less varying than jealousy.



despite Lilian’s recovery from the special illness which have more

immediately absorb my care, I remain perplex as to its cause

and true nature. to her mother I give it the convenient epithet of

“nervous;” but the epithet do not explain to myself all the symptom I

classify by it. there be still, at time, when no cause be apparent

or conjecturable, a sudden change in the expression of her countenance,

in the beat of her pulse; the eye would become fix, the bloom would

vanish, the pulse would sink feebler and feebler till it could be

scarcely feel; yet there be no indication of heart disease, of

which such sudden lowering of life be in itself sometimes a warning

indication. the change would pass away after a few minute, during which

she seem unconscious, or, at least, never speak--never appear to

heed what be say to she. but in the expression of her countenance

there be no character of suffering or distress; on the contrary,

a wondrous serenity, that make her beauty more beauteous, her very

youthfulness younger; and when this spurious or partial kind of syncope

pass, she recover at once without effort, without acknowledge

that she have feel faint or unwell, but rather with a sense of recruit

vitality, as the weary obtain from a sleep. for the rest her spirit

be more generally light and joyous than I should have premise from

her mother’s previous description. she would enter mirthfully into the

mirth of young companion round she: she have evidently quick perception

of the sunny side of life; an infantine gratitude for kindness; an

infantine joy in the trifle that amuse only those who delight in taste

pure and simple. but when talk rise into graver and more contemplative

topic, her attention become earnest and absorb; and sometimes a rich

eloquence, such as I have never before nor since hear from lip so

young, would startle I first into a wonder silence, and soon into a

disapprove alarm: for the thought she then utter seem to I too

fantastic, too visionary, too much akin to the vagary of a wild though

beautiful imagination. and then I would seek to check, to sober,

to distract fancy with which my reason have no sympathy, and the

indulgence of which I regard as injurious to the normal function of

the brain.



when thus, sometimes with a chilling sentence, sometimes with a

half-sarcastic laugh, I would repress outpouring frank and musical

as the song of a forest-bird, she would look at I with a kind of

plaintive sorrow,--often sigh and shiver as she turn away. only in

those mode do she show displeasure; otherwise ever sweet and docile,

and ever, if, see that I have pain she, I ask forgiveness,

humble herself rather to ask mine, and brighten our reconciliation

with her angel smile. as yet I have not dare to speak of love; as yet I

gaze on she as the captive gaze on the flower and the star through

the grating of his cell, murmur to himself, “when shall the door

unclose?”













CHAPTER XVI.



it be with a wrath suppress in the presence of the fair ambassadress,

that Mr. Vigors have receive from Mrs. Poyntz the intelligence that I

have replace Dr. Jones at Abbots’ House not less abruptly than Dr. Jones

have previously supplant I. as Mrs. Poyntz take upon herself the whole

responsibility of this change, Mr. Vigors do not venture to condemn it

to her face; for the Administrator of Laws be at heart no little in awe

of the Autocrat of Proprieties; as Authority, howsoever establish, be

in awe of Opinion, howsoever capricious.



to the mild Mrs. Ashleigh the magistrate’s anger be more decidedly

manifest. he cease his visit; and in answer to a long and

deprecatory letter with which she endeavour to soften his resentment

and win he back to the house, he reply by an elaborate combination

of homily and satire. he begin by excuse himself from accept

her invitation, on the ground that his time be valuable, his habit

domestic; and though ever willing to sacrifice both time and habit

where he could do good, he owe it to himself and to mankind to

sacrifice neither where his advice be reject and his opinion

contemn. he glance briefly, but not hastily, at the respect with

which her late husband have defer to his judgment, and the benefit

which that deference have enable he to bestow. he contrast the

husband’s deference with the widow’s contumely, and hint at the

evil which the contumely would not permit he to prevent. he could

not presume to say what woman of the world might think due to deceased

husband, but even woman of the world generally allow the claim of

living child, and do not act with levity where their interest be

concern, still less where their life be at stake. as to Dr. Jones,

he, Mr. Vigors, have the full confidence in his skill. Mrs. Ashleigh

must judge for herself whether Mrs. Poyntz be as good an authority upon

medical science as he have no doubt she be upon shawl and ribbon. Dr.

Jones be a man of caution and modesty; he do not indulge in the

hollow boast by which charlatan decoy their dupe; but Dr. Jones have

privately assure he that though the case be one that admit of no

rash experiment, he have no fear of the result if his own prudent system

be persevere in. what might be the consequence of any other system,

Dr. Jones would not say, because he be too high-minded to express his

distrust of the rival who have make use of underhand art to supplant

he. but Mr. Vigors be convince, from other source of information

(mean, I presume, the oracular prescience of his clairvoyant), that

the time would come when the poor young lady would herself insist on

discard Dr. Fenwick, and when “that person” would appear in a very

different light to many who now so fondly admire and so reverentially

trust he. when that time arrive, he, Mr. Vigors, might again be of

use; but, meanwhile, though he decline to renew his intimacy at Abbots’

House, or to pay unavaile visit of mere ceremony, his interest in

the daughter of his old friend remain undiminishe, nay, be rather

increase by compassion; that he should silently keep his eye upon

she; and whenever anything to her advantage suggest itself to he,

he should not be deter by the slight with which Mrs. Ashleigh

have treat his judgment from call on she, and place before her

conscience as a mother his idea for her child’s benefit, leave to

herself then, as now, the entire responsibility of reject the advice

which he might say, without vanity, be deem of some value by those

who could distinguish between sterling quality and specious pretence.



Mrs. Ashleigh’s be that thoroughly womanly nature which instinctively

lean upon other. she be diffident, trustful, meek, affectionate. not

quite justly have Mrs. Poyntz describe she as “commonplace weak,” for

though she might be call weak, it be not because she be commonplace;

she have a goodness of heart, a sweetness of disposition, to which

that disparage definition could not apply. she could only be call

commonplace inasmuch as in the ordinary daily affair of life she have a

great deal of ordinary daily commonplace good-sense. give she a routine

to follow, and no routine could be well adhere to. in the allotted

sphere of a woman’s duty she never seem in fault. no household, not

even Mrs. Poyntz’s, be more happily manage. the old Abbots’ House have

merge its original antique gloom in the soft character of please

repose. all her servant adore Mrs. Ashleigh; all find it a pleasure

to please she; her establishment have the harmony of clockwork; comfort

diffuse itself round she like quiet sunshine round a shelter spot.

to gaze on her pleasing countenance, to listen to the simple talk that

lapsed from her guileless lip, in even, slow, and lulling murmur, be

in itself a respite from “eat care.” she be to the mind what the

colour of green be to the eye. she have, therefore, excellent sense in

all that relate to every-day life. there, she need not to consult

another; there, the wise might have consult she with profit. but

the moment anything, however trivial in itself, jar on the routine to

which her mind have grow wed, the moment an incident hurry she out

of the beat track of woman’s daily life, then her confidence forsook

she; then she need a confidant, an adviser; and by that confidant

or adviser she could be credulously lure or submissively control.

therefore, when she lose, in Mr. Vigors, the guide she have be

accustomed to consult whenever she need guidance, she turn;

helplessly and piteously, first to Mrs. Poyntz, and then yet more

imploringly to I, because a woman of that character be never quite

satisfied without the advice of a man; and where an intimacy more

familiar than that of his formal visit be once establish with a

physician, confidence in he grow fearless and rapid, as the natural

result of sympathy concentrate on an object of anxiety in common

between himself and the home which open its sacred recess to his

observant but tender eye. thus Mrs. Ashleigh have show I Mr. Vigors’s

letter, and, forget that I might not be as amiable as herself,

besought I to counsel she how to conciliate and soften her lose

husband’s friend and connection. that character clothe he with dignity

and awe in her soft forgiving eye. so, smother my own resentment,

less perhaps at the tone of offensive insinuation against myself than

at the arrogance with which this prejudiced intermeddler imply to a

mother the necessity of his guardian watch over a child under her

own care, I sketch a reply which seem to I both dignified and

placatory, abstain from all discussion, and convey the assurance

that Mrs. Ashleigh would be at all time glad to hear, and dispose to

respect, whatever suggestion so esteem a friend of her husband would

kindly submit to she for the welfare of her daughter.



there all communication have stop for about a month since the date of

my reintroduction to Abbots’ House. one afternoon I unexpectedly meet Mr.

Vigors at the entrance of the blind lane, I on my way to Abbots’ House,

and my first glance at his face tell I that he be come from it, for

the expression of that face be more than usually sinister; the sullen

scowl be light into significant menace by a sneer of unmistakable

triumph. I feel at once that he have succeed in some machination

against I, and with ominous misgiving quicken my step.



I find Mrs. Ashleigh seat alone in front of the house, under a large

cedar-tree that form a natural arbour in the centre of the sunny lawn.

she be perceptibly embarrassed as I take my seat beside she.



“I hope,” say I, force a smile, “that Mr. Vigors have not be tell

you that I shall kill my patient, or that she look much bad than she

do under Dr. Jones’s care?”



“no,” she say. “he own cheerfully that Lilian have grow quite strong,

and say, without any displeasure, that he have hear how gay she have

be, ride out and even dancing,--which be very kind in he, for he

disapprove of dancing, on principle.”



“but still I can see he have say something to vex or annoy you; and, to

judge by his countenance when I meet he in the lane, I should conjecture

that that something be intend to lower the confidence you so kindly

repose in I.”



“I assure you not; he do not mention your name, either to I or to

Lilian. I never know he more friendly; quite like old time. he be a

good man at heart, very, and be much attach to my poor husband.”



“do Mr. Ashleigh profess a very high opinion of Mr. Vigors?”



“well, I don’t quite know that, because my dear Gilbert never speak to

I much about he. Gilbert be naturally very silent. but he shrink from

all trouble--all worldly affair--and Mr. Vigors manage his estate, and

inspect his steward’s book, and protect he through a long lawsuit

which he have inherit from his father. it kill his father. I don’t

know what we should have do without Mr. Vigors, and I be so glad he

have forgive I.”



“Hem! where be Miss Ashleigh? indoor?”



“no; somewhere in the ground. but, my dear Dr. Fenwick, do not leave I

yet; you be so very, very kind, and somehow I have grow to look upon

you quite as an old friend. something have happen which have put I out,

quite put I out.”



she say this wearily and feebly, close her eye as if she be indeed

put out in the sense of extinguish.



“the feeling of friendship you express,” say I, with earnestness, “be

reciprocal. on my side it be accompany by a peculiar gratitude. I be

a lonely man, by a lonely fireside, no parent, no near kindre, and in

this town, since Dr. Faber leave it, without cordial intimacy till I know

you. in admit I so familiarly to your hearth, you have give I

what I have never know before since I come to man’s estate,--a glimpse

of the happy domestic life; the charm and relief to eye, heart, and

spirit which be never know but in household cheer by the face of

woman. thus my sentiment for you and yours be indeed that of an old

friend; and in any private confidence you show I, I feel as if I be

no long a lonely man, without kindred, without home.”



Mrs. Ashleigh seem much move by these word, which my heart have
