force from my lip; and, after reply to I with simple unaffected

warmth of kindness, she rise, take my arm, and continue thus as we

walk slowly to and fro the lawn: “you know, perhaps, that my poor

husband leave a sister, now a widow like myself, Lady Haughton.”



“I remember that Mrs. Poyntz say you have such a sister-in-law, but I

never hear you mention Lady Haughton till now. well!”



“well, Mr. Vigors have bring I a letter from she, and it be that which

have put I out. I dare say you have not hear I speak before of Lady

Haughton, for I be ashamed to say I have almost forget her existence.

she be many year old than my husband be; of a very different

character. only come once to see he after our marriage. hurt I by

ridicule he as a bookworm; offend he by look a little down on

I, as a nobody without spirit and fashion, which be quite true. and,

except by a cold and unfeele letter of formal condolence after I lose

my dear Gilbert, I have never hear from she since I have be a widow,

till to-day. but, after all, she be my poor husband’s sister, and his

eld sister, and Lilian’s aunt; and, as Mr. Vigors say, ‘duty be

duty.’”



have Mrs. Ashleigh say “duty be torture,” she could not have utter the

maxim with more mournful and despondent resignation.



“and what do this lady require of you, which Mr. Vigors deem it your

duty to comply with?”



“dear I! what penetration! you have guess the exact truth. but I

think you will agree with Mr. Vigors. certainly I have no option; yes, I

must do it.”



“my penetration be in fault now. do what? pray explain.”



“Poor Lady Haughton, six month ago, lose she only son, Sir James. Mr.

Vigors say he be a very fine young man, of whom any mother would have

be proud. I have hear he be wild; Mr. Vigors say, however, that he

be just go to reform, and marry a young lady whom his mother choose

for he, when, unluckily, he would ride a steeplechase, not be

quite sober at the time, and break his neck. Lady Haughton have be, of

course, in great grief. she have retire to Brighton; and she write to I

from thence, and Mr. Vigors bring the letter. he will go back to she

to-day.”



“will go back to Lady Haughton? what! have he be to she? be he, then,

as intimate with Lady Haughton as he be with her brother?”



“no; but there have be a long and constant correspondence. she have a

settlement on the Kirby Estate,--a sum which be not pay off during

Gilbert’s life; and a very small part of the property go to Sir James,

which part Mr. Ashleigh Sumner, the heir-at-law to the rest of the

estate, wish Mr. Vigors, as his guardian, to buy during his minority,

and as it be mix up with Lady Haughton’s settlement her consent be

necessary as well as Sir James’s. so there be much negotiation, and,

since then, Ashleigh Sumner have come into the Haughton property, on poor

Sir James’s decease; so that complicate all affair between Mr. Vigors

and Lady Haughton, and he have just be to Brighton to see she. and

poor Lady Haughton, in short, want I and Lilian to go and visit she.

I don’t like it at all. but you say the other day you think sea air

might be good for Lilian during the heat of the summer, and she seem

well enough now for the change. what do you think?”



“she be well enough, certainly. but Brighton be not the place I would

recommend for the summer; it want shade, and be much hot than L----”



“yes; but unluckily Lady Haughton foresee that objection, and she have a

jointure-house some mile from Brighton, and near the sea. she say the

ground be well woode, and the place be proverbially cool and healthy,

not far from St. Leonard’s Forest. and, in short, I have write to say

we will come. so we must, unless, indeed, you positively forbid it.”



“when do you think of go?”



“Next Monday. Mr. Vigors would make I fix the day. if you know how I

dislike move when I be once settle; and I do so dread Lady Haughton,

she be so fine, and so satirical! but Mr. Vigors say she be very much

alter, poor thing! I should like to show you her letter, but I have

just send it to Margaret--Mrs. Poyntz--a minute or two before you come.

she know something of Lady Haughton. Margaret know everybody. and we

shall have to go in mourning for poor Sir James, I suppose; and Margaret

will choose it, for I be sure I cann’t guess to what extent we should

be suppose to mourn. I ought to have go in mourning before--poor

Gilbert’s nephew--but I be so stupid, and I have never see he. and--but

oh, this be kind! Margaret herself,--my dear Margaret!”



we have just turn away from the house, in our up-and-down walk; and

Mrs. Poyntz stand immediately front we. “so, Anne, you have actually

accept this invitation--and for Monday next?”



“yes. do I do wrong?”



“what do Dr. Fenwick say? can Lilian go with safety?”



I could not honestly say she might not go with safety, but my heart sink

like lead as I answered,--



“Miss Ashleigh do not now need merely medical care; but more than half

her cure have depend on keep her spirit free from depression. she

may miss the cheerful companionship of your daughter, and other young

lady of her own age. a very melancholy house, sadden by a recent

bereavement, without other guest; a hostess to whom she be a stranger,

and whom Mrs. Ashleigh herself appear to deem formidable,--certainly

these do not make that change of scene which a physician would

recommend. when I speak of sea air be good for Miss Ashleigh, I

think of our own northern coast at a later time of the year, when

I could escape myself for a few week and attend she. the journey to a

northern watering-place would be also short and less fatiguing; the

air there more invigorating.”



“no doubt that would be well,” say Mrs. Poyntz, dryly; “but so far

as your objection to visit Lady Haughton have be state, they

be groundless. her house will not be melancholy; she will have other

guest, and Lilian will find companion, young like herself,--young

lady--and young gentleman too!”



there be something ominous, something compassionate, in the look which

Mrs. Poyntz cast upon I, in conclude her speech, which in itself be

calculate to rouse the fear of a lover. lilian away from I, in

the house of a worldly-fine lady--such as I judge Lady Haughton to

be--surround by young gentleman, as well as young lady, by admirer,

no doubt, of a high rank and more brilliant fashion than she have yet

know! I close my eye, and with strong effort suppress a groan.



“my dear Annie, let I satisfy myself that Dr. Fenwick really do

consent to this journey. he will say to I what he may not to you.

pardon I, then, if I take he aside for a few minute. let I find you

here again under this cedar-tree.”



place her arm in mine, and without wait for Mrs. Ashleigh’s answer,

Mrs. Poyntz draw I into the more sequester walk that belt the lawn;

and when we be out of Mrs. Ashleigh’s sight and hearing, said,--



“from what you have now see of lilian ashleigh, do you still desire to

gain she as your wife?”



“still? oh, with an intensity proportion to the fear with which I now

dread that she be about to pass away from my eye--from my life!”



“do your judgment confirm the choice of your heart? reflect before you

answer.”



“such selfish judgment as I have before I know she would not confirm

but oppose it. the nobler judgment that now expand all my reasoning,

approve and second my heart. no, no; do not smile so sarcastically.

this be not the voice of a blind and egotistical passion. let I

explain myself if I can. I concede to you that Lilian’s character be

undeveloped; I concede to you, that amidst the childlike freshness and

innocence of her nature, there be at time a strangeness, a mystery,

which I have not yet trace to its cause. but I be certain that the

intellect be organically as sound as the heart, and that intellect and

heart will ultimately--if under happy auspex--blend in that felicitous

union which constitute the perfection of woman. but it be because

she do, and may for year, may perhaps always, need a more devoted,

thoughtful care than nature less tremulously sensitive, that my

judgment sanction my choice; for whatever be good for she be good for

I. and who would watch over she as I should?”



“you have never yet speak to Lilian as lover speak?”



“oh, no, indeed.”



“and, nevertheless, you believe that your affection would not be

unreturne?”



“I think so once; I doubt now,--yet, in doubt, hope. but why do you

alarm I with these question? you, too, forebode that in this visit I

may lose she forever?”



“if you fear that, tell she so, and perhaps her answer may dispel your

fear.”



“what! now, already, when she have scarcely know I a month. might I not

risk all if too premature?”



“there be no almanac for love. with many woman love be bear the moment

they know they be belove. all wisdom tell we that a moment once go

be irrevocable. be I in your place, I should feel that I approach a

moment that I must not lose. I have say enough; now I shall rejoin Mrs.

Ashleigh.”



“Stay--tell I first what Lady Haughton’s letter really contain to

prompt the advice with which you so transport, and yet so daunt, I when

you proffer it.”



“not now; later, perhaps,--not now. if you wish to see Lilian alone, she

be by the Old Monk’s well; I see she seat there as I pass that way

to the house.”



“one word more,--only one. answer this question frankly, for it be one

of honour. do you still believe that my suit to her daughter would not

be disapprove of by Mrs. Ashleigh?”



“at this moment I be sure it would not; a week hence I might not give

you the same answer.”



so she pass on with her quick but measured tread, back through the

shady walk, on to the open lawn, till the last glimpse of her pale gray

robe disappear under the bough of the cedar-tree. then, with a

start, I break the irresolute, tremulous suspense in which I have vainly

endeavour to analyze my own mind, solve my own doubt, concentrate my

own will, and go the opposite way, skirt the circle of that haunt

ground,--as now, on one side its lofty terrace, the house of the

neighbouring city come full and close into view, divide from my

fairy-land of life but by the trodden murmurous thoroughfare wind low

beneath the ivied parapet; and as now, again, the world of man abruptly

vanish behind the screening foliage of luxuriant June.



at last the enchant glade open out from the verdure, its border

fragrant with syringa and rise and woodbine; and there, by the gray

memorial of the go gothic age, my eye seem to close their unquiet

wandering, rest spell-bind on that image which have become to I the

incarnation of earth’s bloom and youth.



she stand amidst the past, back by the fragment of wall which man

have raise to seclude he from human passion, lock, under those lid

so downcast, the secret of the only knowledge I ask from the boundless

Future.



ah! what mockery there be in that grand word, the world’s fierce

war-cry,--Freedom! who have not know one period of life, and that so

solemn that its shadow may rest over all life hereafter, when one

human creature have over he a sovereignty more supreme and absolute than

Orient servitude adore in the symbol of diadem and sceptre? what crest

so haughty that have not bow before a hand which could exalt or humble!

what heart so dauntless that have not tremble to call forth the voice

at whose sound open the gate of rapture or despair! that life alone be

free which rule, and suffice for itself. that life we forfeit when we

love!













CHAPTER XVII.



how do I utter it? by what word do my heart make itself know? I

remember not. all be as a dream that fall upon a restless, feverish

night, and fade away as the eye unclose on the peace of a cloudless

heaven, on the bliss of a golden sun. a new morrow seem indeed upon

the earth when I wake from a life-long yesterday,--her dear hand in

mine, her sweet face bow upon my breast.



and then there be that melodious silence in which there be no sound

audible from without; yet within we there be hear a lull celestial

music, as if our whole being, grow harmonious with the universe, join

from its happy deep in the hymn that unite the star.



in that silence our two heart seem to make each other understand, to

be draw near and near, blend by mysterious concord into the

completeness of a solemn union, never henceforth to be rent asunder.



at length I say softly: “and it be here on this spot that I first see

you,--here that I for the first time know what power to change our world

and to rule our future go forth from the charm of a human face!”



then Lilian ask I timidly, and without lift her eye, how I have

so see she, remind I that I promise to tell she, and have never yet

do so.



and then I tell she of the strange impulse that have lead I into the

ground, and by what chance my step have be divert down the path

that wound to the glade; how suddenly her form have shine upon my

eye, gather round itself the rose hue of the set sun, and how

wistfully those eye have follow her own silent gaze into the distant

heaven.



as I speak, her hand press mine eagerly, convulsively, and, raise

her face from my breast, she look at I with an intent, anxious

earnestness. that look!--twice before it have thrill and perplex I.



“what be there in that look, oh, my Lilian, which tell I that there

be something that startle you,--something you wish to confide, and yet

shrink from explain? see how, already, I study the fair book from

which the seal have be lift! but as yet you must aid I to construe

its language.”



“if I shrink from explain, it be only because I fear that I cannot

explain so as to be understand or believe. but you have a right to know

the secret of a life which you would link to your own. turn your face

aside from I; a reprove look, an incredulous smile, chill--oh, you

cannot guess how they chill I, when I would approach that which to I

be so serious and so solemnly strange.”



I turn my face away, and her voice grow firm as, after a brief

pause, she resumed,--



“as far back as I can remember in my infancy, there have be moment

when there seem to fall a soft hazy veil between my sight and the

thing around it, thickening and deepening till it have the likeness

of one of those white fleecy cloud which gather on the verge of the

horizon when the air be yet still, but the wind be about to rise; and

then this vapour or veil will suddenly open, as cloud open, and let in

the blue sky.”



“go on,” I say gently, for here she come to a stop. she continue,

speak somewhat more hurriedly,--



“then, in that opening, strange appearance present they self to

I, as in a vision. in my childhood these be chiefly landscape of

wonderful beauty. I could but faintly describe they then; I could not

attempt to describe they now, for they be almost go from my memory.

my dear mother chid I for tell she what I see, so I do not impress

it on my mind by repeat it. as I grow up, this kind of vision--if

I may so call it--become much less frequent, or much less distinct; I

still see the soft veil fall, the pale cloud form and open, but often

what may then have appear be entirely forget when I recover

myself, wake as from a sleep. sometimes, however, the recollection

would be vivid and complete; sometimes I see the face of my lost father;

sometimes I hear his very voice, as I have see and hear he in my

early childhood, when he would let I rest for hour beside he as he

muse or study, happy to be so quietly near he, for I love he, oh,

so dearly! and I remember he so distinctly, though I be only in my

sixth year when he die. much more recently--indeed, within the last few

month--the image of thing to come be reflect on the space that

I gaze into as clearly as in a glass. thus, for week before I come

hither, or know that such a place exist, I see distinctly the old

House, yon tree, this sward, this moss-grow Gothic fount; and, with

the sight, an impression be convey to I that in the scene before I

my old childlike life would pass into some solemn change. so that when I

come here, and recognize the picture in my vision, I take an affection

for the spot,--an affection not without awe, a powerful, perplexing

interest, as one who feel under the influence of a fate of which a

prophetic glimpse have be vouchsafe. and in that evening, when you

first see I, seat here--”



“yes, Lilian, on that evening--”



“I see you also, but in my vision--yonder, far in the deep of

space,--and--and my heart be stir as it have never be before; and

near where your image grow out from the cloud I see my father’s face,

and I hear his voice, not in my ear, but as in my heart, whispering--”



“yes, Lilian--whispering--what?”



“these words,--only these,--‘Ye will need one another.’ but then,

suddenly, between my upward eye and the two form they have behold,

there rise from the earth, obscure the sky, a vague, dusky vapour,

undulous, and coil like a vast serpent,--nothing, indeed, of its

shape and figure definite, but of its face one abrupt glare; a flash

from two dread luminous eye, and a young head, like the Medusa’s,

change, more rapidly than I could have draw breath, into a grinning

skull. then my terror make I bow my head, and when I raise it again,

all that I have see be vanish. but the terror still remain, even

when I feel my mother’s arm round I and hear her voice. and then, when

I enter the house, and sit down again alone, the recollection of what

I have see--those eye, that face, that skull--grow on I strong and

strong till I faint, and remember no more, until my eye, opening,

see you by my side, and in my wonder there be not terror. no, a sense

of joy, protection, hope, yet still shadow by a kind of fear or awe,

in recognize the countenance which have gleam on I from the sky

before the dark vapour have rise, and while my father’s voice have

murmur, ‘ye will need one another.’ and now--and now--will you love

I less that you know a secret in my being which I have tell to no

other,--cannot construe to myself? only--only, at least, do not mock I;

do not disbelieve I! Nay, turn from I no long now: now I ask to meet

your eye. now, before our hand can join again, tell I that you do not

despise I as untruthful, do not pity I as insane.”



“hush, hush!” I say, draw she to my breast. “of all you tell I

we will talk hereafter. the scale of our science have no weight fine

enough for the gossamer thread of a maiden’s pure fancy. enough for

I--for we both--if out from all such illusion start one truth, tell

to you, lovely child, from the heaven; tell to I, ruder man, on the

earth; repeat by each pulse of this heart that woo you to hear and

to trust,--now and henceforth through life unto death, ‘each have need of

the other,’--I of you, I of you! my Lilian! my Lilian!”













CHAPTER XVIII.



in spite of the previous assurance of Mrs. Poyntz, it be not without an

uneasy apprehension that I approach the cedar-tree, under which Mrs.

Ashleigh still sit, her friend beside she. I look on the fair creature

whose arm be link in mine. so young, so singularly lovely, and with

all the gift of birth and fortune which bend avarice and ambition the

more submissively to youth and beauty, I feel as if I have wrong what a

parent might justly deem her natural lot.



“oh, if your mother should disapprove!” say I, falteringly. Lilian

lean on my arm less lightly. “if I have think so,” she say with her

soft blush, “should I be thus by your side?”



so we pass under the bough of the dark tree, and Lilian leave I and

kiss Mrs. Ashleigh’s cheek; then, seat herself on the turf, lay

her head on her mother’s lap. I look on the queen of the Hill, whose

keen eye shoot over I. I think there be a momentary expression of

pain or displeasure on her countenance; but it pass. still

there seem to I something of irony, as well as of triumph or

congratulation, in the half-smile with which she quit her seat, and

in the tone with which she whisper, as she glide by I to the open

sward, “so, then, it be settle.”



she walk lightly and quickly down the lawn. when she be out of sight

I breathe more freely. I take the seat which she have leave, by Mrs.

Ashleigh’s side, and say, “a little while ago I speak of myself as a

man without kindred, without home, and now I come to you and ask for

both.”



Mrs. Ashleigh look at I benignly, then raise her daughter’s face

from her lap, and whisper, “lilian;” and Lilian’s lip move, but I

do not hear her answer. her mother do. she take Lilian’s hand, simply

place it in mine, and say, “as she choose, I choose; whom she love,

I love.”













CHAPTER XIX.



from that evening till the day Mrs. Ashleigh and Lilian go on the

dread visit, I be always at their house, when my avocation allow

I to steal to it; and during those few day, the happy I have ever

know, it seem to I that year could not have more deepen my

intimacy with Lilian’s exquisite nature, make I more reverential of its

purity, or more enamoured of its sweetness. I could detect in she but

one fault, and I rebuke myself for believe that it be a fault.

we see many who neglect the minor duty of life, who lack watchful

forethought and considerate care for other, and we recognize the

cause of this fail in levity or egotism. certainly, neither of those

tendency of character could be ascribe to Lilian. yet still in daily

trifle there be something of that neglect, some lack of that care and

forethought. she love her mother with fondness and devotion, yet it

never occur to she to aid in those petty household care in which her

mother centre so much of habitual interest. she be full of tenderness

and pity to all want and suffering, yet many a young lady on the Hill

be more actively beneficent,--visiting the poor in their sickness, or

instruct their child in the Infant Schools. I be persuade

that her love for I be deep and truthful; it be clearly void of all

ambition; doubtless she would have bear, unflinche and content,

whatever the world consider to be a sacrifice and privation,--yet I

should never have expect she to take her share in the trouble

of ordinary life. I could never have apply to she the homely but

significant name of helpmate. I reproach myself while I write for

notice such defect--if defect it be--in what may be call the

practical routine of our positive, trivial, human existence. no doubt it

be this that have cause Mrs. Poyntz’s harsh judgment against the wisdom

of my choice. but such chiller shade upon Lilian’s charming nature be

reflect from no inert, unamiable self-love. it be but the consequence

of that self-absorption which the habit of revery have foster. I

cautiously abstain from all allusion to those visionary deception,

which she have confide to I as the truthful impression of spirit, if

not of sense. to I any approach to what I term “superstition” be

displease; any indulgence of fantasy not within the measured

and beat track of healthful imagination more than displease I in

her,--it alarm. I would not by a word encourage she in persuasion

which I feel it would be at present premature to reason against, and

cruel indeed to ridicule. I be convince that of themselves these

mist round her native intelligence, engender by a solitary and musing

childhood, would subside in the full daylight of wed life. she

seem pained when she see how resolutely I shun a subject dear to

her thought. she make one or two timid attempt to renew it, but

my grave look suffice to check she. once or twice indeed, on such

occasion, she would turn away and leave I, but she soon come back;

that gentle heart could not bear one unkindli shade between itself

and what it love. it be agree that our engagement should be, for the

present, confide only to Mrs. Poyntz. when Mrs. Ashleigh and Lilian

return, which would be in a few week at furth, it should be

proclaim; and our marriage could take place in the autumn, when I

should be most free for a brief holiday from professional toil.



so we part-as lover part. I feel none of those jealous fear

which, before we be affiance, have make I tremble at the thought of

separation, and have conjure up irresistible rival. but it be with a

settle, heavy gloom that I see her depart. from earth be go a glory;

from life a blessing.













CHAPTER XX.



during the busy year of my professional career, I have snatch leisure

for some professional treatise, which have make more or less sensation,

and one of they, entitle “the Vital Principle; its Waste and Supply,”

 have gain a wide circulation among the general public. this last

treatise contain the result of certain experiment, then new in

chemistry, which be adduce in support of a theory I entertain as to

the re-invigoration of the human system by principle similar to

those which Liebig have apply to the replenishment of an exhaust

soil,--namely, the give back to the frame those essential to its

nutrition, which it have lose by the action or accident of time; or

supply that special pabulum or energy in which the individual

organism be constitutionally deficient; and neutralize or

counterbalance that in which it super-abounds,--a theory upon which

some eminent physician have more recently improve with signal success.

but on these essay, slight and suggestive, rather than dogmatic, I set

no value. I have be for the last two year engage on a work of much

wide range, endear to I by a far bold ambition,--a work upon which

I fondly hope to found an endure reputation as a severe and

original physiologist. it be an Inquiry into Organic Life, similar in

comprehensiveness of survey to that by which the illustrious Muller, of

Berlin, have enrich the science of our age; however inferior, alas! to

that august combination of thought and learn in the judgment which

check presumption, and the genius which adorn speculation. but at that

day I be carry away by the ardour of composition, and I admire my

performance because I love my labour. this work have be entirely lay

aside for the last agitated month; now that Lilian be go, I resume

it earnestly, as the sole occupation that have power and charm enough to

rouse I from the aching sense of void and loss.



the very night of the day she go, I reopen my manuscript. I have leave

off at the commencement of a chapter upon Knowledge as derive from our

sense. as my conviction on this head be found on the well-know

argument of Locke and Condillac against innate idea, and on the

reasoning by which Hume have resolve the combination of sensation

into a general idea to an impulse arise merely out of habit, so I set

myself to oppose, as a dangerous concession to the sentimentality or

mysticism of a pseudo-philosophy, the doctrine favour by most of our

recent physiologist, and of which some of the most eminent of german

metaphysician have accept the substance, though refine into a

subtlety its positive form,--I mean the doctrine which Muller himself

have express in these words:--





   “that innate idea may exist cannot in the slight degree be deny:

    it be, indeed, a fact.  all the idea of animal, which be induce by

    instinct, be innate and immediate: something present to the mind, a

    desire to attain which be at the same time give.  the new-bear lamb

    and foal have such innate idea, which lead they to follow their

    mother and suck the teat.  be it not in some measure the same with

    the intellectual idea of man?” (1)



to this question I answer with an indignant “no!” a “yes” would have

shake my creed of materialism to the dust. I write on rapidly, warmly.

I define the property and mete the limit of natural law, which I

would not admit that a deity himself could alter. I clamp and solder

dogma to dogma in the link of my tinker logic, till out from my page,

to my own complacent eye, grow Intellectual Man, as the pure formation

of his material sense; mind, or what be call soul, bear from and

nurture by they alone; through they to act, and to perish with the

machine they move. strange, that at the very time my love for Lilian

might have teach I that there be mystery in the core of the

feeling which my analysis of idea could not solve, I should so

stubbornly have oppose as unreal all that could be refer to the

spiritual! strange, that at the very time when the thought that I might

lose from this life the being I have know scarce a month have just before

so appal I, I should thus complacently sit down to prove that,

accord to the law of the nature which my passion obey, I must

lose for eternity the blessing I now hope I have win to my life! but how

distinctly dissimilar be man in his conduct from man in his system!

see the poet recline under forest bough, con ode to his mistress;

follow he out into the world; no mistress ever live for he there!(2)

see the hard man of science, so austere in his passionless problem;

follow he now where the brain rest from its toil, where the heart

find its Sabbath--what child be so tender, so yielding, and soft?



but I have prove to my own satisfaction that poet and sage be dust,

and no more, when the pulse cease to beat. and on that consolatory

conclusion my pen stop.



suddenly, beside I I distinctly hear a sigh,--a compassionate,

mournful sigh. the sound be unmistakable. I start from my seat,

look round, amazed to discover no one,--no living thing! the window

be close, the night be still. that sigh be not the wail of the

wind. but there, in the dark angle of the room, what be that? a

silvery whiteness, vaguely shape as a human form, recede, fade,

go! why, I know not--for no face be visible, no form, if form it

be, more distinct than the colourless outline,--why, I know not, but

I cry aloud, “Lilian! Lilian!” my voice come strangely back to my own

ear; I pause, then smile and blush at my folly. “so I, too, have

learn what be superstition,” I mutter to myself. “and here be an

anecdote at my own expense (as Muller frankly tell we anecdote of the

illusion which would haunt his eye, shut or open),--an anecdote I may

quote when I come to my chapter on the Cheats of the Senses and Spectral

Phantasms.” I go on with my book, and write till the light wane in

the gray of the dawn. and I say then, in the triumph of my pride, as

I lay myself down to rest, “I have write that which allot with

precision man’s place in the region of nature; write that which

will find a school, form disciple; and race after race of those

who cultivate truth through pure reason shall accept my basis if they

enlarge my building.” and again I hear the sigh, but this time it

cause no surprise. “certainly,” I murmur, “a very strange thing be

the nervous system!” so I turn on my pillow, and, weary out, fall

asleep.



(1) Muller’s “Elements of Physiology,” vol. ii. p. 134. translate by

Dr. Baley.



(2) Cowley, who wrote so elaborate a series of amatory poem, be say

“never to have be in love but once, and then he never have resolution

to tell his passion.”--Johnson’s “life of the Poets:” COWLEY.













CHAPTER XXI.



the next day, the last of the visit patient to whom my forenoon

be devoted have just quit I, when I be summon in haste to attend

the steward of a Sir Philip Derval not reside at his family seat,

which be about five mile from L----. it be rarely indeed that person

so far from the town, when of no high rank than this applicant, ask

my service.



but it be my principle to go wherever I be summon; my profession

be not gain, it be healing, to which gain be the incident, not

the essential. this case the messenger report as urgent. I go on

horseback, and ride fast; but swiftly as I canter through the village

that skirt the approach to Sir Philip Derval’s park, the evident care

bestow on the accommodation of the cottager forcibly strike I. I

feel that I be on the land of a rich, intelligent, and beneficent

proprietor. enter the park, and pass before the manor-house, the

contrast between the neglect and the decay of the absentee’s stately

Hall and the smile home of his villager be disconsolately mournful.



an impose pile, build apparently by Vanbrugh, with decorate

pilaster, pompous portico, and grand perron (or double flight of stair

to the entrance), enrich with urn and statue, but discolour,

mildew, chip, half-hide with unpruned creeper and ivy. Most of

the window be close with shutter, decay for want of paint; in

some of the casement the pane be break; the peacock perch on the

shatter balustrade, that fence a garden overgrow with weed. the

sun glare hotly on the place, and make its ruinous condition still more

painfully apparent. I be glad when a winding in the park-road shut

the house from my sight. suddenly I emerge through a copse of ancient

yew-tree, and before I there gleam, in abrupt whiteness, a building

evidently design for the family mausoleum, classical in its outline,

with the blind iron door niche into stone wall of massive thickness,

and surround by a funereal garden of rose and evergreen, fence with

an iron rail, party-gilt.



the suddenness with which this House of the Dead come upon I heighten

almost into pain, if not into awe, the dismal impression which the

aspect of the deserted home in its neighbourhood have make. I spur my

horse, and soon arrive at the door of my patient, who live in a fair

brick house at the other extremity of the park.



I find my patient, a man somewhat advanced in year, but of a robust

conformation, in bed: he have be seize with a fit, which be suppose

to be apoplectic, a few hour before; but be already sensible, and out

of immediate danger. after I have prescribe a few simple remedy, I

take aside the patient’s wife, and go with she to the parlour below

stair, to make some inquiry about her husband’s ordinary regimen and

habit of life. these seem sufficiently regular; I could discover no

apparent cause for the attack, which present symptom not familiar to

my experience. “have your husband ever have such fit before?”



“never!”



“have he experience any sudden emotion? have he hear any unexpected

news; or have anything happen to put he out?”



the woman look much disturb at these inquiry. I press they more

urgently. at last she burst into tear, and clasp my hand, say, “oh,

doctor, I ought to tell you--I send for you on purpose--yet I fear you

will not believe I: my good man have see a ghost!”



“a ghost!” say I, repress a smile. “well, tell I all, that I may

prevent the ghost come again.”



the woman’s story be prolix. its substance be this. her husband,

habitually an early riser, have leave his bed that morning still early

than usual, to give direction about some cattle that be to be send

for sale to a neighbouring fair. an hour afterwards he have be find by

a shepherd, near the mausoleum, apparently lifeless. on be remove to

his own house, he have recover speech, and bid all except his wife

leave the room, he then tell she that on walk across the park towards

the cattle-shed, he have see what appear to he at first a pale light

by the iron door of the mausoleum. on approach nearer, this light

change into the distinct and visible form of his master, Sir Philip

Derval, who be then abroad,--supposed to be in the East, where he have

reside for many year. the impression on the steward’s mind be so

strong, that he call out, “oh, Sir Philip!” when look still

more intently, he perceive that the face be that of a corpse. as he

continue to gaze, the apparition seem gradually to recede, as

if vanish into the sepulchre itself. he know no more; he become

unconscious. it be the excess of the poor woman’s alarm, on hear

this strange tale, that make her resolve to send for I instead of the

parish apothecary. she fancy so astounding a cause for her husband’s

seizure could only be properly deal with by some medical man repute

to have more than ordinary learning; and the steward himself object to

the apothecary in the immediate neighbourhood, as more likely to annoy

he by gossip than a physician from a comparative distance.



I take care not to lose the confidence of the good wife by parade too

quickly my disbelief in the phantom her husband declare that he have

see; but as the story itself seem at once to decide the nature of the

fit to be epileptic, I begin to tell she of similar delusion which, in

my experience, have occur to those subject to epilepsy, and

finally soothe she into the conviction that the apparition be clearly

reducible to natural cause. afterwards, I lead she on to talk about

Sir Philip Derval, less from any curiosity I feel about the absent

proprietor than from a desire to re-familiarize her own mind to his

image as a live man. the steward have be in the service of Sir

Philip’s father, and have know Sir Philip himself from a child. he be

warmly attach to his master, whom the old woman describe as a man of

rare benevolence and great eccentricity, which last she impute to his

studious habit. he have succeed to the title and estate as a minor.

for the first few year after attain his majority, he have mix much

in the world. when at Derval Court his house have be fill with gay

companion, and be the scene of lavish hospitality; but the estate

be not in proportion to the grandeur of the mansion, still less to the

expenditure of the owner. he have become greatly embarrassed; and some

love disappointment (so it be rumour) occur simultaneously with

his pecuniary difficulty, he have suddenly change his way of life,

shut himself up from his old friend, live in seclusion, take to

book and scientific pursuit, and as the old woman say vaguely and

expressively, “to odd way.” he have gradually by an economy that,

towards himself, be penurious, but which do not preclude much

judicious generosity to other, clear off his debt; and, once more

rich, he have suddenly quit the country, and take to a life of

travel. he be now about forty-eight year old, and have be eighteen

year abroad. he write frequently to his steward, give he minute and

thoughtful instruction in regard to the employment, comfort, and home

of the peasantry, but peremptorily order he to spend no money on the

ground and mansion, state as a reason why the latter might be allow

to fall into decay, his intention to pull it down whenever he return

to England.



I stay some time long than my engagement well warrant at my

patient’s house, not leave till the sufferer, after a quiet sleep, have

remove from his bed to his armchair, take food, and seem perfectly

recover from his attack.



ride homeward, I muse on the difference that education make, even

pathologically, between man and man. here be a brawny inhabitant of

rural field, lead the healthy of life, not conscious of the

faculty we call imagination, stricken down almost to death’s door by

his fright at an optical illusion, explicable, if examine, by the same

simple cause which have impress I the night before with a moment’s

belief in a sound and a spectre,--me who, thank to sublime education,

go so quietly to sleep a few minute after, convince that no phantom,

the ghostly that ear ever hear or eye ever see, can be anything else

but a nervous phenomenon.













CHAPTER XXII.



that evening I go to Mrs. Poyntz’s; it be one of her ordinary

“reception night,” and I feel that she would naturally expect my

attendance as “a proper attention.”



I join a group engage in general conversation, of which Mrs. Poyntz

herself make the centre, knit as usual,--rapidly while she talk,

slowly when she listen.



without mention the visit I have pay that morning, I turn the

conversation on the different country place in the neighbourhood, and

then incidentally ask, “what sort of a man be Sir Philip Derval? be it

not strange that he should suffer so fine a place to fall into decay?”

 the answer I receive add little to the information I have already

obtain. Mrs. Poyntz know nothing of Sir Philip Derval, except as a man

of large estate, whose rental have be greatly increase by a rise in

the value of property he possess in the town of L----, and which lay

contiguous to that of her husband. two or three of the old inhabitant

of the Hill have remember Sir Philip in his early day, when he be

gay, high-spirit, hospitable, lavish. one observe that the only

person in L---- whom he have admit to his subsequent seclusion be

Dr. Lloyd, who be then without practice, and whom he have employ as an

assistant in certain chemical experiment.





here a gentleman strike into the conversation. he be a stranger to

I and to L----, a visitor to one of the dweller on the Hill, who have

ask leave to present he to its queen as a great traveller and an

accomplish antiquary.



say this gentleman: “Sir Philip Derval? I know he. I meet he in the

East. he be then still, I believe, very fond of chemical science; a

clever, odd, philanthropical man; have study medicine, or at least

practise it; be say to have make many marvellous cure. I become

acquainted with he in Aleppo. he have come to that town, not much

frequent by English traveller, in order to inquire into the murder of

two man, of whom one be his friend and the other his countryman.”



“this be interesting,” say Mrs. Poyntz, dryly. “we who live on this

innocent Hill all love story of crime; murder be the pleasant

subject you could have hit on. pray give we the detail.”



“so encouraged,” say the traveller, good-humouredly, “I will not

hesitate to communicate the little I know. in Aleppo there have live for

some year a man who be hold by the native in great reverence. he have

the reputation of extraordinary wisdom, but be difficult of access;

the lively imagination of the Orientals invest his character with

the fascination of fable,--in short, Haroun of Aleppo be popularly

consider a magician. wild story be tell of his power, of his

preternatural age, of his hoard treasure. apart from such disputable

title to homage, there seem no question, from all I hear, that his

learning be considerable, his charity extensive, his manner of life

irreproachably ascetic. he appear to have resemble those arabian sage

of the Gothic age to whom modern science be largely indebted,--a mystic

enthusiast, but an earnest scholar. a wealthy and singular Englishman,

long resident in another part of the East, afflict by some languish

disease, take a journey to Aleppo to consult this sage, who, among

his other acquirement, be hold to have discover rare secret in

medicine,--his countryman say in ‘charm.’ one morning, not long after

the Englishman’s arrival, Haroun be find dead in his bed, apparently

strangle, and the Englishman, who lodge in another part of the town,

have disappear; but some of his clothe, and a crutch on which he

habitually support himself, be find a few mile distant from

Aleppo, near the roadside. there appear no doubt that he, too, have

be murder, but his corpse could not be discover. Sir Philip Derval

have be a love disciple of this sage of Aleppo, to whom he assure I

he owe not only that knowledge of medicine which, by report, Sir

Philip possess, but the insight into various truth of nature, on the

promulgation of which, it be evident, Sir Philip cherish the ambition

to find a philosophical celebrity for himself.”



“of what description be those truth of nature?” I ask, somewhat

sarcastically.



“Sir, I be unable to tell you, for Sir Philip do not inform I, nor

do I much care to ask; for what may be revere as truth in Asia be

usually despise as dream in Europe. to return to my story: Sir

Philip have be in Aleppo a little time before the murder; have leave the

Englishman under the care of Haroun. he return to Aleppo on hear

the tragic event I have relate, and be busy in collect such

evidence as could be glean, and institute inquiry after our

missing countryman at the time I myself chance to arrive in the city.

I assist in his research, but without avail. the assassin remain

undiscovered. I do not myself doubt that they be mere vulgar robber.

Sir Philip have a dark suspicion of which he make no secret to I; but

as I confess that I think the suspicion groundless, you will pardon

I if I do not repeat it. whether since I leave the East the Englishman’s

remain have be discover, I know not. very probably; for I

understand that his heir have get hold of what fortune he left,--less

than be generally suppose. but it be report that he have bury

great treasure, a rumour, however absurd, not altogether inconsistent

with his character.”



“what be his character?” ask Mrs. Poyntz.



“one of evil and sinister repute. he be regard with terror by the

attendant who have accompany he to Aleppo. but he have live in a

very remote part of the East, little know to Europeans, and, from all I

could learn, have there establish an extraordinary power, strengthen

by superstitious awe. he be say to have study deeply that knowledge

which the philosopher of old call ‘occult,’ not, like the sage

of Aleppo, for benevolent, but for malignant end. he be accuse of

confer with evil spirit, and fill his barbaric court (for

he live in a kind of savage royalty) with charmer and sorcerer. I

suspect, after all, that he be only, like myself, an ardent antiquary,

and cunningly make use of the fear he inspire in order to secure his

authority, and prosecute in safety research into ancient sepulchre or

temple. his great passion be, indeed, in excavate such remain, in

his neighbourhood; with what result I know not, never having penetrate

so far into region infest by robber and pestiferous with malaria. he

wear the eastern dress, and always carry jewel about he. I come

to the conclusion that for the sake of these jewel he be murder,

perhaps by some of his own servant (and, indeed, two at least of his

suite be miss), who then at once bury his body, and keep their

own secret. he be old, very infirm; could never have get far from the

town without assistance.”



“you have not yet tell we his name,” say Mrs. Poyntz.



“his name be Grayle.”



“Grayle!” exclaim Mrs. Poyntz, drop her work. “Louis Grayle?”



“yes; Louis Grayle. you could not have know he?”



“know he! no; but I have often hear my father speak of he. such,

then, be the tragic end of that strong dark creature, for whom, as a

young girl in the nursery, I use to feel a kind of fearful admiring

interest?”



“it be your turn to narrate now,” say the traveller.



and we all draw close round our hostess, who remain silent some

moment, her brow thoughtful, her work suspend.



“well,” say she at last, look round we with a lofty air, which

seem half defy, “force and courage be always fascinating, even

when they be quite in the wrong. I go with the world, because the world

go with I; if it do not--” here she stop for a moment, clench

the firm white hand, and then scornfully wave it, leave the sentence

unfinishe, and break into another.



“go with the world, of course we must march over those who stand

against it. but when one man stand single-handed against our march, we

do not despise he; it be enough to crush. I be very glad I do not see

Louis Grayle when I be a girl of sixteen.” again she pause a moment,

and resume: “Louis Grayle be the only son of a usurer, infamous for

the rapacity with which he have acquire enormous wealth. Old Grayle

desire to rear his heir as a gentleman; send he to Eton. boy be

always aristocratic; his birth be soon throw in his tooth; he be

fierce; he strike boy big than himself,--fought till he be

half kill. my father be at school with he; describe he as

a tiger-whelp. one day he--still a fag--strike a sixth-form boy.

sixth-form boy do not fight fag; they punish they. Louis Grayle be

order to hold out his hand to the cane; he receive the blow, draw

forth his schoolboy knife, and stab the punisher. after that, he leave

Eton. I don’t think he be publicly expel--too mere a child for that

honour--but he be take or send away; educate with great care under

the first master at home. when he be of age to enter the University,

old Grayle be dead. Louis be send by his guardian to Cambridge, with

acquirement far exceed the average of young man, and with unlimited

command of money. my father be at the same college, and describe he

again,--haughty, quarrelsome, reckless, handsome, aspiring, brave.

do that kind of creature interest you, my dear?” (appeal to the

lady).



“La!” say Miss Brabazon; “a horrid usurer’s son!”



“ay, true; the vulgar proverb say it be good to be bear with a silver

spoon in one’s mouth: so it be when one have one’s own family crest on

it; but when it be a spoon on which people recognize their family

crest, and cry out, ‘steal from our plate chest,’ it be a heritage that

outlaw a babe in his cradle. however, young man at college who want

money be less scrupulous about descent than boy at Eton be. Louis

Grayle find, while at college, plenty of wellborn acquaintance willing

to recover from he some of the plunder his father have extort from

theirs. he be too wild to distinguish himself by academical honour,

but my father say that the tutor of the college declare there be

not six undergraduate in the University who know as much hard and dry

science as wild Louis Grayle. he go into the world, no doubt, hope

to shine; but his father’s name be too notorious to admit the son into

good society. the Polite World, it be true, do not examine a scutcheon

with the nice eye of a herald, nor look upon rich with the stately

contempt of a stoic; still the Polite World have its family pride and

its moral sentiment. it do not like to be cheated,--I mean, in

money matter; and when the son of a man who have empty its purse and

foreclose on its acre ride by its club-window, hand on haunch, and

head in the air, no lion have a scowl more awful, no hyena a laugh more

dread, than that same easy, good-temper, tolerant, polite, well-breed

World which be so pleasant an acquaintance, so languid a friend, and--so

remorseless an--enemy. in short, Louis Grayle claim the right to be

courted,--he be shun; to be admired,--he be loathe. even his old

college acquaintance be shame out of know he. perhaps he

could have live through all this have he seek to glide quietly into

position; but he want the tact of the well-breed, and strove to storm

his way, not to steal it. reduce for companion to needy parasite,

he brave and he shock all decorous opinion by that ostentation of

excess, which make Richelieus and Lauzuns the rage. but then Richelieus

and Lauzuns be duke! he now very naturally take the Polite World into

hate,--gave it scorn for scorn. he would ally himself with Democracy;

his wealth could not get he into a club, but it would buy he into

parliament; he could not be a Lauzun, nor, perhaps, a Mirabeau, but he

might be a Danton. he have plenty of knowledge and audacity, and with

knowledge and audacity a good hater be sure to be eloquent. possibly,

then, this poor Louis Grayle might have make a great figure, leave his

mark on his age and his name in history; but in contest the borough,

which he be sure to carry, he have to face an opponent in a real fine

gentleman whom his father have ruin, cool and highbred, with a tongue

like a rapier, a sneer like an adder. a quarrel of course; Louis Grayle

send a challenge. the fine gentleman, know to be no coward (fine

gentleman never be), be at first dispose to refuse with contempt. but

Grayle have make himself the idol of the mob; and at a word from Grayle,

the fine gentleman might have be duck at a pump, or toss in

a blanket,--that would have make he ridiculous; to be shoot at be a

trifle, to be laugh at be serious. he therefore condescend to accept

the challenge, and my father be his second.



“it be settle, of course, accord to english custom, that both

combatant should fire at the same time, and by signal. the antagonist

fire at the right moment; his ball graze Louis Grayle’s temple. Louis

Grayle have not fire. he now seem to the second to take slow and

deliberate aim. they call out to he not to fire; they be rush to

prevent he, when the trigger be pull, and his opponent fall dead on

the field. the fight be, therefore, consider unfair; Louis Grayle be

try for his life: he do not stand the trial in person.(1) he escape

to the continent; hurry on to some distant uncivilized land; could

not be trace; reappear in England no more. the lawyer who conduct

his defence plead skilfully. he argue that the delay in firing be

not intentional, therefore not criminal,--the effect of the stun which

the wound in the temple have occasion. the judge be a gentleman, and

sum up the evidence so as to direct the jury to a verdict against

the low wretch who have murder a gentleman; but the juror be not

gentleman, and Grayle’s advocate have of course excite their sympathy

for a son of the people, whom a gentleman have wantonly insult. the

verdict be manslaughter; but the sentence emphatically mark the

aggravate nature of the homicide,--three year’ imprisonment. Grayle

elude the prison, but he be a man disgrace and an exile,--his

ambition blast, his career an outlaw’s, and his age not yet

twenty-three. my father say that he be suppose to have change his

name; none know what have become of he. and so this creature, brilliant

and daring, whom if bear under well auspex we might now be all

fawn on, cringe to,--after live to old age, no one know

how,--dies murder at Aleppo, no one, you say, know by whom.”



“I see some account of his death in the paper about three year ago,”

 say one of the party; “but the name be misspell, and I have no idea

that it be the same man who have fight the duel which Mrs. Colonel

Poyntz have so graphically describe. I have a very vague recollection of

the trial; it take place when I be a boy, more than forty year since.

the affair make a stir at the time, but be soon forget.”



“soon forget,” say Mrs. Poyntz; “ay, what be not? leave your place

in the world for ten minute, and when you come back somebody else have

take it; but when you leave the world for good, who remember that you

have ever a place even in the parish register?”



“nevertheless,” say I, “a great poet have say, finely and truly,





   “‘the sun of Homer shine upon we still.’”



“but it do not shine upon Homer; and learn folk tell I that we

know no more who and what Homer be, if there be ever a single homer

at all, or rather, a whole herd of Homers, than we know about the man in

the moon,--if there be one man there, or million of man. now, my dear

Miss Brabazon, it will be very kind in you to divert our thought

into channel less gloomy. some pretty french air--Dr. Fenwick, I have

something to say to you.” she draw I towards the window. “so Annie

Ashleigh write I word that I be not to mention your engagement. do you

think it quite prudent to keep it a secret?”



“I do not see how prudence be concern in keep it secret one way or

the other,--it be a mere matter of feeling. Most people wish to abridge,

as far as they can, the time in which their private arrangement be the

topic of public gossip.”



“public gossip be sometimes the good security for the due completion of

private arrangement. as long as a girl be not know to be engage,

her betrothed must be prepare for rival. announce the engagement, and

rival be warn off.”



“I fear no rival.”



“do you not? bold man! I suppose you will write to Lilian?”



“certainly.”



“do so, and constantly. by-the-way, Mrs. Ashleigh, before she go,

ask I to send she back Lady Haughton’s letter of invitation. what

for,--to show to you?”



“very likely. have you the letter still? may I see it?”



“not just at present. when lilian or Mrs. Ashleigh write to you, come

and tell I how they like their visit, and what other guest form the

party.”



Therewith she turn away and converse apart with the traveller.



her word disquiet I, and I feel that they be mean to do so,

wherefore I could not guess. but there be no language on earth which have

more word with a double meaning than that speak by the Clever Woman,

who be never so guard as when she appear to be frank.



as I walk home thoughtfully, I be accost by a young man, the son

of one of the wealthy merchant in the town. I have attend he with

success some month before, in a rheumatic fever: he and his family be

much attach to I.



“ah, my dear Fenwick, I be so glad to see you; I owe you an

obligation of which you be not aware,--an exceedingly pleasant

travel-companion. I come with he to-day from London, where I have

be sight-seeing and holidaymake for the last fortnight.”



“I suppose you mean that you kindly bring I a patient?”



“no, only an admirer. I be stay at Fenton’s Hotel. it so happen

one day that I have leave in the coffee-room your last work on the Vital

Principle, which, by the by, the bookseller assure I be sell

immensely among reader as non-professional as myself. come into the

coffee-room again, I find a gentleman read the book. I claim it

politely; he as politely tender his excuse for take it. we make

acquaintance on the spot. the next day we be intimate. he express

great interest and curiosity about your theory and your experiment. I

tell he I know you. you may guess if I describe you as less clever in

your practice than you be in your writing; and, in short, he come

with I to L----, partly to see our flourish town, principally on my

promise to introduce he to you. my mother, you know, have what she call

a dejeuner tomorrow,--dejeuner and dance. you will be there?”



“thank you for remind I of her invitation. I will avail myself of

it if I can. your new friend will be present? who and what be he,--a

medical student?”



“no, a mere gentleman at ease, but seem to have a good deal of general

information. very young, apparently very rich, wonderfully good-look.

I be sure you will like he; everybody must.”



“it be quite enough to prepare I to like he that he be a friend of

your.” and so we shake hand and part.



(1) Mrs. Poyntz here make a mistake in law which, though very evident,

her listener do not seem to have notice. her mistake will be refer

to later.













CHAPTER XXIII.



it be late in the afternoon of the follow day before I be able to

join the party assemble at the merchant’s house; it be a villa about

two mile out of the town, pleasantly situate amidst flower-garden

celebrate in the neighbourhood for their beauty. the breakfast have be

long over; the company be scatter over the lawn,--some form into

a dance on the smooth lawn; some seat under shady awning; other

glide amidst parterre, in which all the glow of colour take a glory

yet more vivid under the flush of a brilliant sunshine; and the ripple

of a soft western breeze. music, loud and lively, mingle with the

laughter of happy child, who form much the large number of the

party.



stand at the entrance of an arched trelli, that lead from the hardier

flower of the lawn to a rare collection of tropical plant under a

lofty glass dome (connect, as it be, the familiar vegetation of the

North with that of the remotest East), be a form that instantaneously

catch and fix my gaze. the entrance of the arcade be cover with

parasite creeper, in prodigal luxuriance, of variegate gorgeous

tints,--scarlet, golden, purple; and the form, an idealized picture of

man’s youth fresh from the hand of Nature, stand literally in a frame of

bloom.



never have I see human face so radiant as that young man’s. there be

in the aspect an indescribable something that literally dazzle. as one

continue to gaze, it be with surprise; one be force to acknowledge

that in the feature themselves there be no faultless regularity; nor

be the young man’s stature imposing, about the middle height. but the

effect of the whole be not less transcendent. large eye, unspeakably

lustrous; a most harmonious colouring; an expression of contagious

animation and joyousness; and the form itself so critically fine, that

the weld strength of its sinew be well show in the lightness and

grace of its movement.



he be rest one hand carelessly on the golden lock of a child that

have nestle itself against his knee, look up to his face in that

silent love wonder with which child regard something too strangely

beautiful for noisy admiration; he himself be converse with the

host, an old gray-haired, gouty man, prop on his crutched stick, and

listen with a look of mournful envy. to the wealth of the old man all

the flower in that garden owe their renew delight in the summer

air and sun. oh, that his wealth could renew to himself one hour of the

youth whose incarnation stand beside he, Lord, indeed, of Creation; its

splendour weave into his crown of beauty, its enjoyment subject to his

sceptre of hope and gladness.



I be startle by the hearty voice of the merchant’s son. “ah, my dear

Fenwick, I be afraid you would not come,--you be late. there be

the new friend of whom I speak to you last night; let I now make you

acquaint with he.” he draw my arm in his, and lead I up to the

young man, where he stand under the arch flower, and whom he then

introduce to I by the name of Margrave.



nothing could be more frankly cordial than Mr. Margrave’s manner. in a

few minute I find myself converse with he familiarly, as if we

have be rear in the same home, and sport together in the same

playground. his vein of talk be peculiar, off-hand, careless, shift

from topic to topic with a bright rapidity.



he say that he like the place; propose to stay in it some week;

ask my address, which I give to he; promise to call soon at an

early hour, while my time be yet free from professional visit. I

endeavour, when I go away, to analyze to myself the fascination

which this young stranger so notably exercise over all who approach

he; and it seem to I, ever seek to find material cause for all

moral effect, that it rise from the contagious vitality of that rare

of all rare gift in highly-civilized circles,--perfect health; that

health which be in itself the most exquisite luxury; which, find

happiness in the mere sense of existence, diffuse round it, like an

atmosphere, the harmless hilarity of its bright animal being. health,

to the utmost perfection, be seldom know after childhood; health to the

utmost cannot be enjoy by those who overwork the brain, or admit the

sure wear and tear of the passion. the creature I have just see give

I the notion of youth in the golden age of the poets,--the youth of the

careless Arcadian, before nymph or shepherdess have vex his heart with

a sigh.













CHAPTER XXIV.



the house I occupy at L---- be a quaint, old-fashioned building, a

corner-house. one side, in which be the front entrance, look upon

a street which, as there be no shop in it, and it be no direct

thoroughfare to the busy centre of the town, be always quiet, and

at some hour of the day almost deserted. the other side of the house

front a lane; opposite to it be the long and high wall of the garden

to a Young Ladies’ Boarding-school. my stable adjoin the house,

abut on a row of small building, with little garden before they,

chiefly occupy by mercantile clerk and retired tradesman. by the lane

there be a short and ready access both to the high turnpike-road, and

to some pleasant walk through green meadow and along the bank of a

river.



this house I have inhabit since my arrival at L----, and it have to

I so many attraction, in a situation sufficiently central to be

convenient for patient, and yet free from noise, and favourable to

ready outlet into the country for such foot or horse exercise as my

professional avocation would allow I to carve for myself out of what

the latin poet call the “solid day,” that I have refuse to change it

for one well suited to my increase income; but it be not a house

which Mrs. Ashleigh would have like for Lilian. the main objection to

it in the eye of the “genteel” be, that it have formerly belong to a

member of the heal profession who unite the shop of an apothecary

to the diploma of a surgeon; but that shop have give the house a special

attraction to I; for it have be build out on the side of the house

which front the lane, occupy the great portion of a small gravel

court, fence from the road by a low iron palisade, and separate

from the body of the house itself by a short and narrow corridor that

communicate with the entrance-hall. this shop I turn into a rude

study for scientific experiment, in which I generally spend some early

hour of the morning, before my visit patient begin to arrive. I

enjoy the stillness of its separation from the rest of the house; I

enjoy the glimpse of the great chestnut-tree, which overtop the

wall of the school-garden; I enjoy the ease with which, by open the

glaze sash-door, I could get out, if dispose for a short walk, into

the pleasant field; and so completely have I make this sanctuary my own,

that not only my man-servant know that I be never to be disturb when

in it, except by the summon of a patient, but even the housemaid

be forbid to enter it with broom or duster, except upon special

invitation. the last thing at night, before retire to rest, it be the

man-servant’s business to see that the sash-window be close, and the

gate to the iron palisade lock; but during the daytime I so often go

out of the house by that private way that the gate be then very seldom

locked, nor the sash-door bolt from within. in the town of L----

there be little apprehension of house-robberies,--especially in the

daylight,--and certainly in this room, cut off from the main

building, there be nothing to attract a vulgar cupidity. a few of the

apothecary’s shelf and case still remain on the wall, with, here

and there, a bottle of some chemical preparation for experiment; two or

three worm-eat, wooden chair; two or three shabby old table; an

old walnut-tree bureau without a lock, into which odd and end be

confusedly thrust, and sundry ugly-look invention of mechanical

science, be, assuredly, not the article which a timid proprietor

would guard with jealous care from the chance of robbery. it will be

see later why I have be thus prolix in description. the morning after

I have meet the young stranger by whom I have be so favourably impressed,

I be up as usual, a little before the sun, and long before any of my

servant be astir. I go first into the room I have mention, and

which I shall henceforth designate as my study, open the window,

unlock the gate, and saunter for some minute up and down the silent

lane skirt the opposite wall, and overhang by the chestnut-tree

rich in the garniture of a glorious summer; then, refresh for work,

I re-enter my study, and be soon absorb in the examination of

that now well-know machine, which be then, to I at least, a

novelty,--invented, if I remember right, by Dubois-Reymond, so

distinguish by his research into the mystery of organic

electricity. it be a wooden cylinder fix against the edge of a table;

on the table two vessel fill with salt and water be so place that,

as you close your hand on the cylinder, the forefinger of each hand

can drop into the water; each of the vessel have a metallic plate,

and communicate by wire with a galvanometer with its needle. now the

theory be, that if you clutch the cylinder firmly with the right hand,

leave the left perfectly passive, the needle in the galvanometer will

move from west to south; if, in like manner, you exert the left arm,

leave the right arm passive, the needle will deflect from west to

north. hence, it be argue that the electric current be induce through

the agency of the nervous system, and that, as human will produce the

muscular contraction requisite, so be it human Will that cause

the deflection of the needle. I imagine that if this theory be

substantiate by experiment, the discovery might lead to some sublime

and unconjectured secret of science. for human will, thus actively

effective on the electric current, and all matter, animate or inanimate,

have more or less of electricity, a vast field became open to

conjecture. by what series of patient experimental deduction might not

science arrive at the solution of problem which the newtonian law of

gravitation do not suffice to solve; and--but here I halt. at the

date which my story have reach, my mind never lose itself long in the

Cloudland of Guess.



I be dissatisfied with my experiment. the needle stir, indeed,

but erratically, and not in direction which, accord to the theory,

should correspond to my movement. I be about to dismiss the trial with

some uncharitable contempt of the foreign philosopher’s dogma, when

I hear a loud ring at my street-door. while I pause to conjecture

whether my servant be yet up to attend to the door, and which of my

patient be the most likely to summon I at so unseasonable an hour,

a shadow darken my window. I look up, and to my astonishment beheld

the brilliant face of Mr. Margrave. the sash to the door be already

partially open; he raise it higher, and walk into the room. “be it

you who ring at the street-door, and at this hour?” say I.



“yes; and observe, after I have rung, that all the shutter be still

closed, I feel ashamed of my own rash action, and make off rather than

brave the reproachful face of some injure housemaid, rob of she

morning dream. I turn down that pretty lane,--lured by the green

of the chestnut-trees,--caught sight of you through the window, take

courage, and here I be! you forgive I?” while thus speak, he

continue to move along the littered floor of the dingy room, with the

undulating restlessness of some wild animal in the confine of its den,

and he now go on, in short fragmentary sentence, very slightly link

together, but smooth, as it be, into harmony by a voice musical and

fresh as a sky lark’s warble. “morning dream, indeed! dream that waste

the life of such a morning. rosy magnificence of a summer dawn! do you

not pity the fool who prefer to lie a bed, and to dream rather than to

live? what! and you, strong man, with those noble limb, in this den! do

you not long for a rush through the green of the field, a bath in the

blue of the river?”



here he come to a pause, standing, still in the gray light of the

grow day, with eye whose joyous lustre forestall the sun’s, and

lip which seem to laugh even in repose.



but presently those eye, as quick as they be bright, glance over the

wall, the floor, the shelf, the phial, the mechanical invention,

and then rest full on my cylinder fix to the table. he approach,

examine it curiously, ask what it be. I explain. to gratify he

I sit down and renew my experiment, with equally ill success. the

needle, which should have move from west to south, describe an angle

of from thirty degree to forty or even fifty degree, only make a few

troubled, undecided oscillation.



“tut,” cry the young man, “I see what it be; you have a wound in your

right hand.”



that be true; I have burn my band a few day before in a chemical

experiment, and the sore have not heal.



“well,” say I, “and what do that matter?”



“everything; the least scratch in the skin of the hand produce chemical

action on the electric current, independently of your will. let I

try.”



he take my place, and in a moment the needle in the galvanometer

respond to his grasp on the cylinder, exactly as the inventive

philosopher have state to be the due result of the experiment.



I be startle.



“but how come you, Mr. Margrave, to be so well acquainted with a

scientific process little know, and but recently discover?”



“I well acquaint! not so. but I be fond of all experiment that relate

to animal life. electricity, especially, be full of interest.”



on that I draw he out (as I think), and he talk volubly. I be

amazed to find this young man, in whose brain I have conceive thought

keep one careless holiday, be evidently familiar with the physical

science, and especially with chemistry, which be my own study by

predilection. but never have I meet with a student in whom a knowledge so

extensive be mix up with notion so obsolete or so crotchety. in one

sentence he show that he have master some late discovery by Faraday

or Liebig; in the next sentence he be talk the wild fallacy

of Cardan or Van Helmont. I burst out laugh at some paradox about

sympathetic powder, which he enounce as if it be a recognize truth.



“pray tell I,” say I, “who be your master in physics; for a cleverer

pupil never have a more crack-brain teacher.”



“no,” he answer, with his merry laugh, “it be not the teacher’s fault.

I be a mere parrot; just cry out a few scrap of learning pick up here

and there. but, however, I be fond of all research into nature; all

guess at her riddle. to tell you the truth, one reason why I have

take to you so heartily be not only that your publish work catch my

fancy in the dip which I take into its content (pardon I if I say dip,

I never do more than dip into any book), but also because young ----

tell I that which all whom I have meet in this town confirm; namely,

that you be one of those few practical chemist who be at once

exceedingly cautious and exceedingly bold,--willing to try every new

experiment, but submit experiment to rigid test. well, I have an

experiment run wild in this giddy head of mine, and I want you,

some day when at leisure, to catch it, fix it as you have fix that

cylinder, make something of it. I be sure you can.”



“what be it?”



“something akin to the theory in your work. you would replenish or

preserve to each special constitution the special substance that may

fail to the equilibrium of its health. but you own that in a large

proportion of case the good cure of disease be less to deal with the

disease itself than to support and stimulate the whole system, so as to

enable Nature to cure the disease and restore the impair equilibrium

by her own agency. thus, if you find that in certain case of nervous

debility a substance like nitric acid be efficacious, it be because

the nitric acid have a virtue in lock up, as it be, the nervous

energy,--that be, prevent all undue waste. again, in some case of

what be commonly call feverish cold, stimulant like ammonia assist

nature itself to get rid of the disorder that oppress its normal

action; and, on the same principle, I apprehend, it be contend that

a large average of human life be save in those hospital which

have adopt the support system of ample nourishment and alcoholic

stimulant.”



“your medical learning surprise I,” say I, smile; “and without

pause to notice where it deal somewhat superficially with disputable

point in general, and my own theory in particular, I ask you for the

deduction you draw from your premise.”



“it be simply this: that to all animate body, however various, there

must be one principle in common,--the vital principle itself. what if

there be one certain mean of recruit that principle; and what if

that secret can be discover?”



“Pshaw! the old illusion of the mediaeval empiric.”



“not so. but the mediaeval empiric be great discoverer. you sneer at

Van Helmont, who seek, in water, the principle of all thing; but Van

Helmont discover in his search those invisible body call gas.

now the principle of life must be certainly ascribe to a gas.(1) and

what ever be a gas chemistry should not despair of produce! but I can

argue no long now,--never can argue long at a stretch; we be waste

the morning; and, joy! the sun be up! see! out! come out! out! and greet

the great Lifegiver face to face.”



I could not resist the young man’s invitation. in a few minute we

be in the quiet lane under the glinting chestnut-tree. Margrave be

chant, low, a wild tune,--words in a strange language.



“what word be those,--no european language, I think; for I know a

little of most of the language which be speak in our quarter of the

globe, at least by its more civilized race.”



“civilized race! what be civilization? those word be utter by man

who found empire when Europe itself be not civilized! hush, be it

not a grand old air?” and lift his eye towards the sun, he give vent

to a voice clear and deep as a mighty bell! the air be grand; the word

have a sonorous swell that suit it, and they seem to I jubilant and

yet solemn. he stop abruptly as a path from the lane have lead we into

the field, already half-bathe in sunlight, dew glitter on the

hedgerow.



“your song,” say I, “would go well with the clash of cymbal or the

peal of the organ. I be no judge of melody, but this strike I as that

of a religious hymn.”



“I compliment you on the guess. it be a persian fire-worshipper’s hymn

to the sun. the dialect be very different from modern Persian. Cyrus the

Great might have chant it on his march upon Babylon.”



“and where do you learn it?”



“in Persia itself.”



“you have travel much, learn much,--and be so young and so fresh.

be it an impertinent question if I ask whether your parent be yet

live, or be you wholly lord of yourself?”



“thank you for the question,--pray make my answer know in the town.

parent I have not,--never have.”



“never have parent!”



“well, I ought rather to say that no parent ever own I. I be a

natural son, a vagabond, a nobody. when I come of age I receive an

anonymous letter, inform I that a sum--I need not say what, but

more than enough for all I need--be lodge at an english banker’s in

my name; that my mother have die in my infancy; that my father be also

dead--but recently; that as I be a child of love, and he be unwilling

that the secret of my birth should ever be trace, he have provide for

I, not by will, but in his life, by a sum consign to the trust of the

friend who now write to I; I need give myself no trouble to learn more.

faith, I never do! I be young, healthy, rich,--yes, rich! now you know

all, and you have well tell it, that I may win no man’s courtesy and no

maiden’s love upon false pretence. I have not even a right, you see, to

the name I bear. Hist! let I catch that squirrel.”



with what a panther-like bind he spring! the squirrel elude his grasp,

and be up the oak-tree; in a moment he be up the oak-tree too. in

amazement I see he rise from bough to bough; see his bright eye and

glitter tooth through the green leave. presently I hear the sharp

piteous cry of the squirrel, echo by the youth’s merry laugh; and

down, through that maze of green, Hargrave come, drop on the grass

and bound up, as Mercury might have bound with his wing at his

heel.



“I have catch he. what pretty brown eye!”



suddenly the gay expression of his face change to that of a savage; the

squirrel have wrench itself half-loose, and bite he. the poor brute!

in an instant its neck be wrung, its body dash on the ground;

and that fair young creature, every feature quiver with rage, be

stamp his foot on his victim again and again! it be horrible. I

catch he by the arm indignantly. he turn round on I like a wild

beast disturb from its prey,--his tooth set, his hand lift, his eye

like ball of fire.



“Shame!” say I, calmly; “shame on you!”



he continue to gaze on I a moment or so, his eye glaring, his breath

panting; and then, as if master himself with an involuntary effort,

his arm drop to his side, and he say quite humbly, “I beg your

pardon; indeed I do. I be beside myself for a moment; I cannot bear

pain;” and he look in deep compassion for himself at his wound hand.
