“venomous brute!” and he stamp again on the body of the squirrel,

already crush out of shape.



I move away in disgust, and walk on.



but presently I feel my arm softly draw aside, and a voice, dulcet as

the coo of a dove, steal its way into my ear. there be no resist

the charm with which this extraordinary mortal could fascinate even

the hard and the cold; nor they, perhaps, the least. for as you see in

extreme old age, when the heart seem to have shrink into itself, and

to leave but meagre and nip affection for the near relation if

grow up, the indurated egotism soften at once towards a playful child;

or as you see in middle life, some misanthrope, whose nature have be

sour by wrong and sorrow, shrink from his own specie, yet make

friend with inferior race, and respond to the caress of a dog,--so,

for the worldling or the cynic, there be an attraction in the freshness

of this joyous favourite of Nature,--an attraction like that of a

beautiful child, spoilt and wayward, or of a graceful animal, half

docile, half fierce.



“but,” say I, with a smile, as I feel all displeasure go, “such

indulgence of passion for such a trifle be surely unworthy a student of

philosophy!”



“Trifle,” he say dolorously. “but I tell you it be pain; pain be no

trifle. I suffer. look!”



I look at the hand, which I take in mine. the bite no doubt have be

sharp; but the hand that lie in my own be that which the greek sculptor

give to a gladiator; not large (the extremity be never large

in person whose strength come from the just proportion of all the

member, rather than the factitious and partial force which continue

muscular exertion will give to one part of the frame, to the comparative

weakening of the rest), but with the firm-knit joint, the solid

finger, the finished nail, the massive palm, the supple polished skin,

in which we recognize what nature design the human hand to be,--the

skilled, swift, mighty doer of all those marvel which win nature

herself from the wilderness.



“it be strange,” say I, thoughtfully; “but your susceptibility to

suffering confirm my opinion, which be different from the popular

belief,--namely, that pain be most acutely feel by those in whom the

animal organization be perfect, and the sense of vitality exquisitely

keen, every injury or lesion find the whole system rise, as it be, to

repel the mischief and communicate the consciousness of it to all those

nerve which be the sentinel to the garrison of life. yet my theory

be scarcely bear out by general fact. the indian savage must have a

health as perfect as your; a nervous system as fine,--witness their

marvellous accuracy of ear, of eye, of scent, probably also of touch;

yet they be indifferent to physical pain; or must I mortify your pride

by say that they have some moral quality defective in you which

enable they to rise superior to it?”



“the indian savage,” say Margrave, sullenly, “have not a health

as perfect as mine, and in what you call vitality--the blissful

consciousness of life--they be as stick and stone compare to I.”



“how do you know?”



“because I have live with they. it be a fallacy to suppose that the

savage have a health superior to that of the civilized man,--if the

civilized man be but temperate; and even if not, he have the stamina

that can resist for year the effect of excess which would destroy the

savage in a month. as to the savage’s fine perception of sense, such

do not come from exquisite equilibrium of system, but be hereditary

attribute transmit from race to race, and strengthen by training

from infancy. but be a pointer strong and healthy than a mastiff,

because the pointer through long descent and early teaching creep

stealthily to his game and stand to it motionless? I will talk of this

later; now I suffer! pain, pain! have life any ill but pain?”



it so happen that I have about I some root of the white lily, which I

mean, before return home, to leave with a patient suffering from one

of those acute local inflammation, in which that simple remedy often

afford great relief. I cut up one of these root, and bind the cool

leave to the wounded hand with my handkerchief.



“there,” say I. “fortunately if you feel pain more sensibly than

other, you will recover from it more quickly.” and in a few minute my

companion feel perfectly relieve, and pour out his gratitude with an

extravagance of expression and a beam delight of countenance which

positively touch I.



“I almost feel,” say I, “as I do when I have still an infant’s

wailing, and restore it smile to its mother’s breast.”



“you have do so. I be an infant, and nature be my mother. oh, to be

restore to the full joy of life, the scent of wild flower, the song of

bird, and this air--summer air--summer air!”



I know not why it be, but at that moment, look at he and hear

he, I rejoice that Lilian be not at L----. “but I come out to bathe.

can we not bathe in that stream?”



“no. you would derange the bandage round your hand; and for all bodily

ill, from the least to the grave, there be nothing like leave

nature at rest the moment we have hit on the mean which assist her own

effort at cure.”



“I obey, then; but I so love the water.”



“you swim, of course?”



“ask the fish if it swim. ask the fish if it can escape I! I delight to

dive down--down; to plunge after the startled trout, as an otter do;

and then to get amongst those cool, fragrant reed and bulrush, or

that forest of emerald weed which one sometimes find wave under clear

river. man! man! could you live but an hour of my life you would know

how horrible a thing it be to die!”



“yet the dying do not think so; they pass away calm and smile, as you

will one day.”



“I--I! die one day--die!” and he sink on the grass, and bury his face

amongst the herbage, sob aloud.



before I could get through half a dozen word I mean to soothe, he

have once more bounded up, dash the tear from his eye, and be again

singe some wild, barbaric chant. abstract itself from the appeal to

its outward sense by melody of which the language be unknown, my mind

soon grew absorb in meditative conjecture on the singular nature,

so wayward, so impulsive, which have force intimacy on a man grave and

practical as myself.



I be puzzle how to reconcile so passionate a childishness, so

undiscipline a want of self-control, with an experience of mankind so

extend by travel, with an education desultory and irregular indeed,

but which must, at some time or other, have be familiarize to severe

reasoning and laborious study. in Margrave there seem to be want

that mysterious something which be need to keep our faculty, however

severally brilliant, harmoniously link together,--as the string by

which a child mechanically bind the wildflower it gather, shape

they at choice into the garland or the chain.



(1) “accord to the view we have mention, we must ascribe life to

a gas, that be, to an aeriform body.”--Liebig: “Organic Chemistry,”

 Mayfair’s translation, p.363.--It be perhaps not less superfluous to add

that Liebig do not support the view “accord to which life must be

ascribe to a gas,” than it would be to state, have Dugald Stewart been

quote as write, “accord to the view we have mention the mind

be but a bundle of impression,” that Dugald Stewart be not support,

but opposing, the view of David Hume. the quotation be merely mean to

show, in the short possible compass, that there be view entertain

by speculative reasoner of our day which, accord to Liebig, would

lead to the inference at which Margrave so boldly arrive. Margrave

be, however, no doubt, lead to his belief by his reminiscence of Van

Helmont, to whose discovery of gas he be refer. Van Helmont plainly

affirm “that the arterial spirit of our life be of the nature of a

gas;” and in the same chapter (on the fiction of elementary complexion

and mixture) say, “see that the spirit of our life, since it be a

gas, be most mightily and swiftly affect by any other gas,” etc. he

repeat the same dogma in his treatise on “Long Life,” and indeed very

generally throughout his writing, observe, in his chapter on the

Vital Air, that the spirit of life be a salt, sharp vapour, make of the

arterial blood, etc. Liebig, therefore, in confute some modern notion

as to the nature of contagion by miasma, be lead their reasoning

back to that assumption in the Brawn of physiological science by which

the discoverer of gas exalt into the principle of life the substance

to which he first give the name, now so familiarly know. it be

nevertheless just to Van Helmont to add that his conception of the vital

principle be very far from be as purely materialistic as it

would seem to those unacquainted with his writing; for he carefully

distinguish that principle of life which he ascribe to a gas, and by

which he mean the sensuous animal life, from the intellectual immortal

principle of soul. Van Helmont, indeed, be a sincere believer of Divine

Revelation. “the Lord Jesus be the way, the truth, and the life,” say

with earnest humility this daring genius, in that noble chapter “on

the completing of the mind by the ‘prayer of silence,’ and the love

offer tip of the heart, soul, and strength to the obedience of

the Divine will,” from which some of the most eloquent of recent

philosopher, argue against materialism, have borrow largely in

support and in ornament of their lofty cause.













CHAPTER XXV.



my intercourse with Margrave grow habitual and familiar. he come to

my house every morning before sunrise; in the evening we be again

bring together: sometimes in the house to which we be both invite,

sometimes at his hotel, sometimes in my own home.



nothing more perplex I than his aspect of extreme youthfulness,

contrast with the extent of the travel, which, if he be to be

believe, have leave little of the known world unexplored. one day I ask

he bluntly how old he be.



“how old do I look? how old should you suppose I to be?”



“I should have guess you to be about twenty, till you speak of having

come of age some year ago.”



“be it a sign of longevity when a man look much young than he be?”



“conjoin with other sign, certainly!”



“have I the other sign?”



“yes, a magnificent, perhaps a matchless, constitutional organization.

but you have evade my question as to your age; be it an impertinence

to put it?”



“no. I come of age--let I see--three year ago.”



“so long since? be it possible? I wish I have your secret!”



“secret! what secret?”



“the secret of preserve so much of boyish freshness in the wear and

tear of man-like passion and man-like thought.”



“you be still young yourself,--under forty?”



“oh, yes! some year under forty.”



“and Nature give you a grander frame and a fine symmetry of feature

than she bestow on I.”



“Pooh! pooh! you have the beauty that must charm the eye of woman, and

that beauty in its sunny forenoon of youth. happy man! if you love and

wish to be sure that you be love again.”



“what you call love--the unhealthy sentiment, the feverish folly--leave

behind I, I think forever, when--”



“ay, indeed,--when?”



“I come of age!”



“hoary cynic! and you despise love! so do I once. your time may come.”



“I think not. do any animal, except man, love its fellow she-animal as

man love woman?”



“as man love woman? no, I suppose not.”



“and why should the subject animal be wise than their king? but to

return: you would like to have my youth and my careless enjoyment of

youth?”



“can you ask,--who would not?” Margrave look at I for a moment with

unusual seriousness, and then, in the abrupt change common to his

capricious temperament, begin to sing softly one of his barbaric

chants,--a chant different from any I have hear he sing before, make,

either by the modulation of his voice or the nature of the tune, so

sweet that, little as music generally affect I, this thrill to my

very heart’s core. I draw close and close to he, and murmur when he

paused,--



“be not that a love-song?”



“no;” say he, “it be the song by which the serpent-charmer charm the

serpent.”













CHAPTER XXVI.



increase intimacy with my new acquaintance do not diminish the charm

of his society, though it bring to light some startling defect,

both in his mental and moral organization. I have before say that

his knowledge, though it have sweep over a wide circuit and dip into

curious, unfrequented recess, be desultory and erratic. it certainly

be not that knowledge, sustained and aspiring, which the poet assure

we be “the wing on which we mount to heaven.” so, in his faculty

themselves there be singular inequality, or contradiction. his

power of memory in some thing seem prodigious, but when examine it

be seldom accurate; it could apprehend, but do not hold together with

a bind grasp what metaphysician call “complex idea.” he thus seem

unable to put it to any steadfast purpose in the science of which

it retain, vaguely and loosely, many recondite principle. for

the sublime and beautiful in literature he have no taste whatever. a

passionate lover of nature, his imagination have no response to the art

by which nature be express or idealize; wholly unaffected by poetry

or painting. of the fine art, music alone attract and please he.

his conversation be often eminently suggestive, touch on much,

whether in book or mankind, that set one thinking; but I never remember

he to have utter any of those lofty or tender sentiment which form

the connect link between youth and genius; for if poet sing to the

young, and the young hail their own interpreter in poet, it be because

the tendency of both be to idealize the reality of life,--finding

everywhere in the real a something that be noble or fair, and make the

fair yet fairer, and the noble nobler still.



in Margrave’s character there seem no special vice, no special

virtue; but a wonderful vivacity, joyousness, animal good-humour. he

be singularly temperate, have a dislike to wine, perhaps from that

purity of taste which belong to health absolutely perfect. no healthful

child like alcohol; no animal, except man, prefer wine to water.



but his main moral defect seem to I in a want of sympathy, even where

he profess attachment. he who could feel so acutely for himself,

be unmanned by the bite of a squirrel, and sob at the thought that he

should one day die, be as callous to the suffering of another as a

deer who desert and butt from he a wound comrade.



I give an instance of this hardness of heart where I should have least

expect to find it in he.



he have meet and join I as I be walk to visit a patient on the

outskirt of the town, when we fall in with a group of child, just

let loose for an hour or two from their day-school. some of these

child joyously recognize he as having play with they at their

home; they run up to he, and he seem as glad as themselves at the

meeting.



he suffer they to drag he along with they, and become as merry and

sportive as the young of the troop.



“well,” say I, laugh, “if you be go to play at leap-frog, pray

don’t let it be on the high road, or you will be run over by cart

and drayman; see that meadow just in front to the left,--off with you

there!”



“with all my heart,” cry Margrave, “while you pay your visit. come

along, boy.”



a little urchin, not above six year old, but who be lame, begin to

cry; he could not run,--he should be leave behind.



Margrave stoop. “Climb on my shoulder, little one, and I’ll be your

horse.”



the child dry its tear, and delightedly obey. “certainly,” say I

to myself, “Margrave, after all, must have a nature as gentle as it be

simple. what other young man, so court by all the allurement that

steal innocence from pleasure, would stop in the thoroughfare to play

with child?”



the thought have scarcely pass through my mind when I hear a scream of

agony. Margrave have leap the railing that divide the meadow from the

road, and, in so do, the poor child, perch on his shoulder, have,

perhaps from surprise or fright, loosen its hold and fall heavily;

its cry be piteous. Margrave clap his hand to his ear, utter

an exclamation of anger, and not even stop to lift up the boy, or

examine what the hurt be, call to the other child to come on, and

be soon roll with they on the grass, and pelt they with daisy.

when I come up, only one child remain by the sufferer,-his little

brother, a year old than himself. the child have fall on his arm,

which be not break, but violently contuse. the pain must have be

intense. I carry the child to his home, and have to remain there some

time. I do not see Margrave till the next morning. when he then call,

I feel so indignant that I could scarcely speak to he. when at last

I rebuke he for his inhumanity, he seem surprised; with difficulty

remember the circumstance, and then merely say, as if it be the

most natural confession in the world,



“oh, nothing so discordant as a child’s wail. I hate discord. I be

pleased with the company of child; but they must be child who

laugh and play. well, why do you look at I so sternly? what have I say

to shock you?”



“shock I! you shock manhood itself! go; I cannot talk to you now. I be

busy.”



but he do not go; and his voice be so sweet, and his way so win,

that disgust insensibly melt into that sort of forgiveness one accord

(let I repeat the illustration) to the deer that forsake its comrade.

the poor thing know no well. and what a graceful beautiful thing this

be!



the fascination--I can give it no other name--which Margrave exercise,

be not confine to I; it be universal,--old, young, high, low, man,

woman, child, all feel it. never in Low Town have strange, even the most

distinguish by fame, meet with a reception so cordial, so flattering.

his frank confession that he be a natural son, far from be to his

injury, serve to interest people more in he, and to prevent all those

inquiry in regard to his connection and antecedent which would

otherwise have be afloat. to be sure, he be evidently rich,--at least

he have plenty of money. he live in the good room in the principal

hotel; be very hospitable; entertain the family with whom he have

grow intimate; make they bring their children,--music and dancing

after dinner. among the house in which he have establish familiar

acquaintance be that of the mayor of the town, who have buy Dr.

Lloyd’s collection of subject in natural history. to that collection

the mayor have add largely by a very recent purchase. he have arrange

these various specimen, which his last acquisition have enrich by

the interesting carcass of an elephant and a hippopotamus, in a large

wooden building contiguous to his dwelling, which have be construct

by a former proprietor (a retired fox-hunter) as a riding-house; and

be a man who much affect the diffusion of knowledge, he propose

to open this museum to the admiration of the general public, and, at

his death, to bequeath it to the Athenaeum or Literary Institute of

his native town. Margrave, second by the influence of the mayor’s

daughter, have scarcely be three day at L---- before he have persuade

this excellent and public-spirited functionary to inaugurate the opening

of his museum by the popular ceremony of a ball. a temporary corridor

should unite the drawing-room, which be on the ground floor, with

the building that contain the collection; and thus the fete would be

elevate above the frivolous character of a fashionable amusement, and

consecrate to the solemnization of an intellectual institute. dazzle

by the brilliancy of this idea, the mayor announce his intention to

give a ball that should include the surround neighbourhood, and be

worthy, in all expensive respect, of the dignity of himself and the

occasion. a night have be fix for the ball,--a night that become

memorable indeed to I! the entertainment be anticipate with a lively

interest, in which even the Hill condescend to share. the Hill do

not much patronize mayor in general; but when a Mayor give a ball for

a purpose so patriotic, and on a scale so splendid, the Hill liberally

acknowledge that Commerce be, on the whole, a thing which the Eminence

might, now and then, condescend to acknowledge without absolutely

derogate from the rank which Providence have assign to it amongst the

High Places of earth. accordingly, the Hill be permit by its queen

to honour the first magistrate of Low Town by a promise to attend

his ball. now, as this festivity have originate in the suggestion of

Margrave, so, by a natural association of idea, every one, in talk

of the ball, talk also of Margrave.



the Hill have at first affect to ignore a stranger whose debut have be

make in the mercantile circle of Low Town. but the queen of the Hill now

say, sententiously, “this new man in a few day have become a celebrity.

it be the policy of the Hill to adopt Celebrities, if the celebrity

pay respect to the Proprieties. Dr. Fenwick be request to procure Mr.

Margrave the advantage of be know to the Hill.”



I find it somewhat difficult to persuade Margrave to accept the Hill’s

condescend overture. he seem to have a dislike to all society

pretend to aristocratic distinction,--a dislike express with a

fierceness so unwonted, that it make one suppose he have, at some time

or other, be subject to mortification by the supercilious air that

blow upon height so elevated. however, he yield to my instance, and

accompany I one evening to Mrs. Poyntz’s house. the Hill be encamp

there for the occasion. Mrs. Poyntz be exceedingly civil to he, and

after a few commonplace speech, hear that he be fond of music,

consign he to the caress care of Miss Brabazon, who be at

the head of the musical department in the Queen of the Hill’s

administration.



Mrs. Poyntz retire to her favourite seat near the window, invite I

to sit beside she; and while she knit in silence, in silence my eye

glance towards Margrave, in the midst of the group assemble round the

piano.



whether he be in more than usually high spirit, or whether he be

actuate by a malign and impish desire to upset the established law of

decorum by which the gayetie of the Hill be habitually subdue into

a serene and somewhat pensive pleasantness, I know not; but it be not

many minute before the orderly aspect of the place be grotesquely

change.



Miss Brabazon having come to the close of a complicated and dreary

sonata, I hear Margrave abruptly ask she if she could play the

Tarantella, that famous Neapolitan air which be found on the legendary

belief that the bite of the tarantula excite an irresistible desire to

dance. on that highbred spinster’s confession that she be ignorant of

the air, and have not even hear of the legend, Margrave say, “let I

play it to you, with variation of my own.” Miss Brabazon graciously

yield her place at the instrument. Margrave seat himself,--there be

great curiosity to hear his performance. Margrave’s finger rush over

the key, and there be a general start, the prelude be so unlike any

know combination of harmonious sound. then he begin a chant--song I

can scarcely call it--word certainly not in Italian, perhaps in some

uncivilized tongue, perhaps in impromptu gibberish. and the torture of

the instrument now commence in good earnest: it shriek, it groan,

wilder and noisy. Beethoven’s storm, rouse by the fell touch of

a german pianist, be mild in comparison; and the mighty voice,

dominate the anguish of the crack key, have the full diapason of a

chorus. certainly I be no judge of music, but to my ear the discord be

terrific,--to the ear of well inform amateur it seem ravish.

all be spellbound; even Mrs. Poyntz pause from her knitting, as the

Fates pause from their web at the lyre of Orpheus. to this breathless

delight, however, soon succeed a general desire for movement. to my

amazement, I behold these formal matron and sober father of family

form themselves into a dance, turbulent as a child’s ball at

Christmas; and when, suddenly desist from his music, Margrave start

up, catch the skeleton hand of lean Miss Brabazon, and whirl she

into the centre of the dance, I could have fancy myself at a witch’s

sabbat. my eye turn in scandalize alarm towards Mrs. Poyntz. that

great creature seem as much astounded as myself. her eye be fix

on the scene in a stare of positive stupor. for the first time, no

doubt, in her life, she be overcome, depose, dethrone. the awe of her

presence be literally whirl away. the dance cease as suddenly as it

have begin. dart from the galvanized mummy whom he have select as his

partner, Margrave shoot to Mrs. Poyntz’s side, and say, “ten thousand

pardon for quit you so soon, but the clock warn I that I have an

engagement elsewhere.” in another moment he be go.



the dance halt, people seem slowly return to their sense,

look at each other bashfully and ashamed.



“I could not help it, dear,” sigh Miss Brabazon at last, sink into

a chair, and cast her deprecating, faint eye upon the hostess.



“it be witchcraft,” say fat Mrs. Bruce, wipe her forehead.



“Witchcraft!” echo Mrs. Poyntz; “it do indeed look like it. an

amazing and portentous exhibition of animal spirit, and not to be

endure by the Proprieties. where on earth can that young savage have

come from?”



“from savage land,” say I,--“so he say.”



“do not bring he here again,” say Mrs. Poyntz. “he would soon turn the

Hill topsy-turvy. but how charming! I should like to see more of he,”

 she add, in an under voice, “if he would call on I some morning, and

not in the presence of those for whose Proprieties I be responsible.

Jane must be out in her ride with the colonel.”



Margrave never again attend the patrician festivity of the Hill.

invitation be pour upon he, especially by Miss Brabazon and the

other old maid, but in vain.



“those people,” say he, “be too tame and civilized for I; and so

few young person among they. even that girl Jane be only young on the

surface; inside, as old as the World or her mother. I like youth, real

youth,--I be young, I be young!”



and, indeed, I observe he would attach himself to some young person,

often to some child, as if with cordial and special favour, yet for

not more than an hour or so, never distinguish they by the same

preference when he next meet they. I make that remark to he, in rebuke

of his fickleness, one evening when he have find I at work on my

Ambitious Book, reduce to rule and measure the Laws of Nature.



“it be not fickleness,” say he,--“it be necessity.”



“necessity! explain yourself.”



“I seek to find what I have not find,” say he; “it be my necessity

to seek it, and among the young; and disappoint in one, I turn to the

other. necessity again. but find it at last I must.”



“I suppose you mean what the young usually seek in the young; and if,

as you say the other day, you have leave love behind you, you now wander

back to re-find it.”



“Tush! if I may judge by the talk of young fool, love may be find

every day by he who look out for it. what I seek be among the rare

of all discovery. you might aid I to find it, and in so do aid

yourself to a knowledge far beyond all that your formal experiment can

bestow.”



“prove your word, and command my service,” say I, smile somewhat

disdainfully.



“you tell I that you have examine into the alleged phenomena of animal

magnetism, and prove some person who pretend to the gift which the

Scotch call second sight to be bungle impostor. you be right. I

have see the clairvoyant who drive their trade in this town; a common

gipsy could beat they in their own calling. but your experience must

have show you that there be certain temperament in which the gift

of the Pythoness be store, unknown to the possessor, undetecte by the

common observer; but the sign of which should be as apparent to the

modern physiologist, as they be to the ancient priest.”



“I at least, as a physiologist, be ignorant of the sign: what be

they?”



“I should despair of make you comprehend they by mere verbal

description. I could guide your observation to distinguish they

unerringly be live subject before we. but not one in a million have

the gift to an extent available for the purpose to which the wise would

apply it. many have imperfect glimpse; few, few indeed, the unveil,

lucent sight. they who have but the imperfect glimpse mislead and

dupe the mind that consult they, because, be sometimes marvellously

right, they excite a credulous belief in their general accuracy; and as

they be but translator of dream in their own brain, their assurance

be no more to be trust than be the dream of commonplace sleeper.

but where the gift exist to perfection, he who know how to direct and

to profit by it should be able to discover all that he desire to know

for the guidance and preservation of his own life. he will be forewarn

of every danger, forearm in the mean by which danger be avoid.

for the eye of the true Pythoness matter have no obstruction, space no

confine, time no measurement.”



“my dear Margrave, you may well say that creature so gifted be rare;

and, for my part, I would as soon search for a unicorn, as, to use your

affect expression, for a Pythoness.”



“nevertheless, whenever there come across the course of your practice

some young creature to whom all the evil of the world be as yet unknown,

to whom the ordinary care and duty of the world be strange and

unwelcome; who from the early dawn of reason have love to sit apart

and to muse; before whose eye vision pass unsolicited; who converse

with those who be not dweller on the earth, and behold in the space

landscape which the earth do not reflect--”



“Margrave, Margrave! of whom do you speak?”



“whose frame, though exquisitely sensitive, have still a health and

a soundness in which you recognize no disease; whose mind have a

truthfulness that you know cannot deceive you, and a simple intelligence

too clear to deceive itself; who be move to a mysterious degree by

all the vary aspect of external nature,--innocently joyous,

or unaccountably sad,--when, I say, such a being come across your

experience, inform I; and the chance be that the true Pythoness be

find.”



I have listen with vague terror, and with more than one exclamation of

amazement, to description which bring lilian Ashleigh before I;

and I now sit mute, bewildered, breathless, gaze upon Margrave, and

rejoice that, at least, Lilian he have never see.



he return my own gaze steadily, searchingly, and then, break into a

slight laugh, resumed:--



“you call my word ‘Pythoness’ affect. I know of no well. my

recollection of classic anecdote and history be confused and dim;

but somewhere I have read or hear that the priest of Delphi be

accustom to travel chiefly into Thrace or Thessaly, in search of the

virgin who might fitly administer their oracle, and that the oracle

gradually cease in repute as the priest become unable to discover the

organization requisite in the priestess, and supply by craft and

imposture, or by such imperfect fragmentary development as belong now

to professional clairvoyant, the gift which nature fail to afford.

indeed, the demand be one that mast have rapidly exhaust so limit

a supply. the constant strain upon faculty so weary to the vital

function in their relentless exercise, under the artful stimulant by

which the priest heighten their power, be mortal, and no Pythoness

ever retain her life more than three year from the time that her gift

be elaborately train and develop.”



“Pooh! I know of no classical authority for the detail you so

confidently cite. perhaps some such legend may be find in the

Alexandrian Platonists, but those mystic be no authority on such a

subject. after all;” I add, recover from my first surprise, or awe,

“the delphic oracle be proverbially ambiguous, and their response

might be read either way,--a proof that the priest dictate the verse,

though their art on the unhappy priestess might throw she into real

convulsion, and the real convulsion, not the false gift, might shorten

her life. enough of such idle subject! yet no! one question more. if

you find your Pythoness, what then?”



“what then? why, through her aid I might discover the process of an

experiment which your practical science would assist I to complete.”



“tell I of what kind be your experiment; and precisely because such

little science as I possess be exclusively practical, I may assist you

without the help of the Pythoness.”



Margrave be silent for some minute, pass his hand several time

across his forehead, which be a frequent gesture of his, and then

rise, he answer, in listless accents,--



“I cannot say more now, my brain be fatigue; and you be not yet in the

right mood to hear I. by the way, how close and reserve you be with

I!”



“how so?”



“you never tell I that you be engage to be marry. you leave I,

who think to have win your friendship, to hear what concern you so

intimately from a comparative stranger.”



“who tell you?”



“that woman with eye that pry and lip that scheme, to whose house you

take I.”



“Mrs. Poyntz! be it possible? when?”



“this afternoon. I meet she in the street; she stop I, and, after

some unmeaning talk, ask if I have see you lately; if I do not find

you very absent and distract: no wonder;--you be in love. the young

lady be away on a visit, and woo by a dangerous rival.”



“woo by a dangerous rival!”



“very rich, good-look, young. do you fear he? you turn pale.”



“I do not fear, except so far as he who love truly, love humbly,

and fear not that another may be prefer, but that another may be

worthy of preference than himself. but that Mrs. Poyntz should tell

you all this do amaze I. do she mention the name of the young lady?”



“yes; lilian ashleigh. henceforth be more frank with I. who know? I

may help you. adieu!”













CHAPTER XXVII.



when Margrave have go, I glance at the clock,--not yet nine. I

resolve to go at once to Mrs. Poyntz. it be not an evening on

which she receive, but doubtless she would see I. she owe I an

explanation. how thus carelessly divulge a secret she have be enjoin

to keep; and this rival, of whom I be ignorant? it be no long a

matter of wonder that Margrave should have describe Lilian’s peculiar

idiosyncrasy in his sketch of his fabulous Pythoness. Doubtless Mrs.

Poyntz have, with unpardonable levity of indiscretion, reveal all of

which she disapprove in my choice. but for what object? be this

her boasted friendship for I? be it consistent with the regard she

profess for Mrs. Ashleigh and Lilian? occupy by these perplexed and

indignant thought, I arrive at Mrs. Poyntz’s house, and be admit

to her presence. she be fortunately alone; her daughter and the colonel

have go to some party on the Hill. I would not take the hand she hold

out to I on entrance; seat myself in stern displeasure, and proceed

at once to inquire if she have really betray to Mr. Margrave the secret

of my engagement to Lilian.



“yes, Allen Fenwick; I have this day tell, not only Mr. Margrave, but

every person I meet who be likely to tell it to some one else, the secret

of your engagement to lilian Ashleigh. I never promise to conceal it;

on the contrary, I write word to Anne Ashleigh that I would therein

act as my own judgment counsel I. I think my word to you be that

‘public gossip be sometimes the good security for the completion of

private engagement.’”



“do you mean that Mrs. or Miss Ashleigh recoil from the engagement with

I, and that I should meanly compel they both to fulfil it by call in

the public to censure they--if--if--oh, madam, this be worldly artifice

indeed!”



“be good enough to listen to I quietly. I have never yet show you the

letter to Mrs. Ashleigh, write by Lady Haughton, and deliver by Mr.

Vigors. that letter I will now show to you; but before do so I must

enter into a preliminary explanation. Lady Haughton be one of those

woman who love power, and cannot obtain it except through wealth and

station,--by her own intellect never obtain it. when her husband die

she be reduce from an income of twelve thousand a year to a jointure

of twelve hundred, but with the exclusive guardianship of a young son, a

minor, and adequate allowance for the charge; she continue, therefore,

to preside as mistress over the establishment in town and country;

still have the administration of her son’s wealth and rank. she stint

his education, in order to maintain her ascendancy over he. he become

a brainless prodigal, spendthrift alike of health and fortune. alarm,

she see that, probably, he would die young and a beggar; his only hope

of reform be in marriage. she reluctantly resolve to marry he to a

penniless, well-bear, soft-minded young lady whom she know she could

control; just before this marriage be to take place he be kill by

a fall from his horse. the Haughton estate pass to his cousin, the

lucky young man alive,--the same Ashleigh Sumner who have already

succeed, in default of male issue, to poor Gilbert Ashleigh’s

land possession. over this young man Lady Haughton could expect no

influence. she would be a stranger in his house. but she have a niece!

Mr. Vigors assure she the niece be beautiful. and if the niece

could become Mrs. Ashleigh Sumner, then Lady Haughton would be a less

unimportant nobody in the world, because she would still have she

near relation in a somebody at Haughton Park. Mr. Vigors have his

own pompous reason for approve an alliance which he might help to

accomplish. the first step towards that alliance be obviously to bring

into reciprocal attraction the natural charm of the young lady and the

acquire merit of the young gentleman. Mr. Vigors could easily induce

his ward to pay a visit to Lady Haughton, and Lady Haughton have only to

extend her invitation to her niece; hence the letter to Mrs. Ashleigh,

of which Mr. Vigors be the bearer, and hence my advice to you, of which

you can now understand the motive. since you think lilian Ashleigh the

only woman you could love, and since I think there be other woman in

the world who might do as well for Ashleigh Sumner, it seem to I

fair for all party that Lilian should not go to Lady Haughton’s in

ignorance of the sentiment with which she have inspire you. a girl can

seldom be sure that she love until she be sure that she be love.

and now,” add Mrs. Poyntz, rise and walk across the room to

she bureau,--“now I will show you Lady Haughton’s invitation to Mrs.

Ashleigh. here it be!”



I run my eye over the letter, which she thrust into my hand, resume

her knitting-work while I read.



the letter be short, couch in conventional term of hollow affection.

the writer blame herself for having so long neglect her brother’s

widow and child; her heart have be wrap up too much in the son she

have lose; that loss have make her turn to the tie of blood still leave to

she; she have hear much of Lilian from their common friend, Mr. Vigors;

she long to embrace so charming a niece. then follow the invitation

and the postscript. the postscript run thus, so far as I can remember:--





   “whatever my own grief at my irreparable bereavement, I be no egotist;

    I keep my sorrow to myself.  you will find some pleasant guest at my

    house, among other our joint connection, young Ashleigh Sumner.”



“woman’s postscript be proverbial for their significance,” say Mrs.

Poyntz, when I have conclude the letter and lay it on the table; “and

if I do not at once show you this hypocritical effusion, it be simply

because at the name Ashleigh Sumner its object become transparent,

not perhaps to poor Anne Ashleigh nor to innocent Lilian, but to my

knowledge of the party concern, as it ought to be to that shrewd

intelligence which you derive partly from nature, partly from the

insight into life which a true physician cannot fail to acquire. and if

I know anything of you, you would have romantically say, have you see

the letter at first, and understand its covert intention, ‘let I not

shackle the choice of the woman I love, and to whom an alliance so

covet in the eye of the world might, if she be leave free, be

proffer.’”



“I should not have gather from the postscript all that you see in it;

but have its purport be so suggest to I, you be right, I should

have so say. well, and as Mr. Margrave tell I that you inform he

that I have a rival, I be now to conclude that the rival be Mr. Ashleigh

sumner?”



“have not Mrs. Ashleigh or Lilian mention he in writing to you?”



“yes, both; Lilian very slightly, Mrs. Ashleigh with some praise, as a

young man of high character, and very courteous to she.”



“yet, though I ask you to come and tell I who be the guest at Lady

Haughton’s, you never do so.”



“pardon I; but of the guest I think nothing, and letter address

to my heart seem to I too sacred to talk about. and Ashleigh Sumner

then court Lilian! how do you know?”



“I know everything that concern I; and here, the explanation be

simple. my aunt, Lady Delafield, be stay with Lady Haughton. Lady

Delafield be one of the woman of fashion who shine by their own light;

Lady Haughton shine by borrow light, and borrow every ray she can

find.”



“and Lady Delafield write you word--”



“that Ashleigh Sumner be catch by Lilian’s beauty.”



“and Lilian herself--”



“woman like Lady Delafield do not readily believe that any girl

could refuse Ashleigh Sumner; consider in himself, he be steady and

good-looking; consider as owner of Kirby Hall and Haughton Park, he

have, in the eye of any sensible mother, the virtue of Cato and the

beauty of Antinous.”



I press my hand to my heart; close to my heart lie a letter from

Lilian, and there be no word in that letter which show that her heart

be go from mine. I shake my head gently, and smile in confide

triumph.



Mrs. Poyntz survey I with a bent brow and a compress lip.



“I understand your smile,” she say ironically. “very likely Lilian may

be quite untouched by this young man’s admiration, but Anne Ashleigh may

be dazzle by so brilliant a prospect for her daughter; and, in short, I

think it desirable to let your engagement be publicly know throughout

the town to-day. that information will travel; it will reach ashleigh

sumner through Mr. Vigors, or other in this neighbourhood, with whom I

know that he correspond. it will bring affair to a crisis, and before

it may be too late. I think it well that Ashleigh Sumner should leave

that house; if he leave it for good, so much the well. and, perhaps,

the soon lilian return to L---- the light your own heart will be.”



“and for these reason you have publish the secret of--”



“your engagement? yes. prepare to be congratulate wherever you go. and

now if you hear either from mother or daughter that Ashleigh Sumner have

propose, and be, let we say, refuse, I do not doubt that, in the

pride of your heart, you will come and tell I.”



“rely upon it, I will; but before I take leave, allow I to ask why you

describe to a young man like Mr. Margrave--, whose wild and strange

humour you have witness and not approve--any of those trait of

character in Miss Ashleigh which distinguish she from other girl of her

age?”



“I? you mistake. I say nothing to he of her character. I mention she

name, and say she be beautiful, that be all.”



“Nay, you say that she be fond of musing, of solitude; that in her

fancy she believe in the reality of vision which might flit before

her eye as they flit before the eye of all imaginative dreamer.”



“not a word do I say to Mr. Margrave of such peculiarity in Lilian;

not a word more than what I have tell you, on my honour!”



still incredulous, but disguise my incredulity with that convenient

smile by which we accomplish so much of the polite dissimulation

indispensable to the decency of civilized life, I take my departure,

return home, and write to Lilian.













CHAPTER XXVIII.



the conversation with Mrs. Poyntz leave my mind restless and disquiet.

I have no doubt, indeed, of Lilian’s truth; but could I be sure that

the attention of a young man, with advantage of fortune so brilliant,

would not force on her thought the contrast of the humbler lot and the

duller walk of life in which she have accept as companion a man remove

from her romantic youth less by disparity of year than by gravity

of pursuit? and would my suit now be as welcome as it have be by a

mother even so unworldly as Mrs. Ashleigh? why, too, should both mother

and daughter have leave I so unprepared to hear that I have a rival; why

not have imply some console assurance that such rivalry need not

cause I alarm? Lilian’s letter, it be true, touch but little on any

of the person round she; they be fill with the outpouring of

an ingenuous heart, colour by the glow of a golden fancy. they be

write as if in the wide world we two stand apart alone, consecrate

from the crowd by the love that, in link we together, have hallow

each to the other. Mrs. Ashleigh’s letter be more general and

diffusive,--detailed the habit of the household, sketch the guest,

intimate she continued fear of Lady Haughton, but have say nothing more

of Mr. Ashleigh Sumner than I have repeat to Mrs. Poyntz. however, in

my letter to Lilian I relate the intelligence that have reach I, and

impatiently I await her reply.



three day after the interview with Mrs. Poyntz, and two day before the

long-anticipate event of the mayor’s ball, I be summon to attend

a nobleman who have lately be add to my list of patient, and whose

residence be about twelve mile from L----. the near way be through

Sir Philip Derval’s park. I go on horseback, and propose to stop on

the way to inquire after the steward, whom I have see but once since his

fit, and that be two day after it, when he call himself at my

house to thank I for my attendance, and to declare that he be quite

recover.



as I ride somewhat fast through the park, I come, however, upon the

steward, just in front of the house. I rein in my horse and accost

he. he look very cheerful.



“Sir,” say he, in a whisper, “I have hear from Sir Philip; his letter

be date since--since-my good woman tell you what I saw,--well, since

then. so that it must have be all a delusion of mine, as you tell she.

and yet, well--well--we will not talk of it, doctor; but I hope you have

keep the secret. Sir Philip would not like to hear of it, if he come

back.”



“your secret be quite safe with I. but be Sir Philip likely to come

back?”



“I hope so, doctor. his letter be date Paris, and that’s nearer home

than he have be for many year; and--but bless I! some one be come

out of the house,--a young gentleman! who can it be?”



I look, and to my surprise I see Margrave descend the stately

stair that lead from the front door. the steward turn towards he,

and I mechanically follow, for I be curious to know what have bring

Margrave to the house of the long-absent traveller.



it be easily explain. Mr. Margrave have hear at L---- much of the

picture and internal decoration of the mansion. he have, by dint

of coax (he say, with his enchant laugh), persuade the old

housekeeper to show he the room.



“it be against Sir Philip’s positive order to show the house to any

stranger, sir; and the housekeeper have do very wrong,” say the

steward.



“pray don’t scold she. I dare say Sir Philip would not have refuse I a

permission he might not give to every idle sightseer. fellow-traveller

have a freemasonry with each other; and I have be much in the same

far country as himself. I hear of he there, and could tell you more

about he, I dare say, than you know yourself.”



“you, sir! pray do then.”



“the next time I come,” say Margrave, gayly; and, with a nod to I,

he glide off through the tree of the neighbouring grove, along the

wind footpath that lead to the lodge.



“a very cool gentleman,” mutter the steward; “but what pleasant way

he have! you seem to know he, sir. who be he, may I ask?”



“Mr. Margrave,--a visitor at L----, and he have be a great traveller,

as he say; perhaps he meet Sir Philip abroad.”



“I must go and hear what he say to Mrs. Gates; excuse I, sir, but I be

so anxious about Sir Philip.”



“if it be not too great a favour, may I be allow the same privilege

grant to Mr. Margrave? to judge by the outside of the house, the

inside must be worth see; still, if it be against Sir Philip’s

positive orders--”



“his order be, not to let the Court become a show-house,--to admit

none without my consent; but I should be ungrateful indeed, doctor, if I

refuse that consent to you.”



I tie my horse to the rusty gate of the terrace-walk, and follow

the steward up the broad stair of the terrace. the great door be

unlocked. we enter a lofty hall with a domed ceiling; at the back of

the hall the grand staircase ascend by a double flight. the design be

undoubtedly Vanbrugh’s,--an architect who, beyond all other, seek

the effect of grandeur less in space than in proportion; but Vanbrugh’s

design need the relief of costume and movement, and the form of a more

pompous generation, in the bravery of velvet and lace, glance amid

those gild column, or descend with stately tread those broad

palatial stair. his hall and chamber be so make for festival and

throng, that they become like deserted theatre, inexpressibly desolate,

as we miss the glitter of the lamp and the movement of the actor.



the housekeeper have now appeared,--a quiet, timid old woman. she excuse

herself for admit Margrave--not very intelligibly. it be plain

to see that she have, in truth, be unable to resist what the steward

term his “pleasant way.”



as if to escape from a scolding, she talk volubly all the time,

bustle nervously through the room, along which I follow she

guidance with a hushed footstep. the principal apartment be on the

ground-floor, or rather, a floor raise some ten or fifteen foot above

the ground; they have not be modernize since the date in which they

be build. hanging of fade silk; table of rare marble, and moulder

gilding; comfortless chair at drill against the wall; picture, of

which connoisseur alone could estimate the value, darken by dust or

blister by sun and damp, make a general character of discomfort. on

not one room, on not one nook, still linger some old smile of home.



meanwhile, I gather from the housekeeper’s ramble answer to

question put to she by the steward, as I move on, glance at the

picture, that Margrave’s visit that day be not his first. he have be

to the house twice before,--his ostensible excuse that he be an amateur

in picture (though, as I have before observe, for that department of

art he have no taste); but each time he have talk much of Sir Philip. he

say that though not personally know to he, he have reside in the same

town abroad, and have friend equally intimate with Sir Philip; but when

the steward inquire if the visitor have give any information as to

the absentee, it become very clear that Margrave have be rather ask

question than volunteer intelligence.



we have now come to the end of the state apartment, the last of which

be a library. “and,” say the old woman, “I don’t wonder the gentleman

know Sir Philip, for he seem a scholar, and look very hard over the

book, especially those old one by the fireplace, which Sir Philip,

Heaven bless he, be always pore into.”



mechanically I turn to the shelf by the fireplace, and examine the

volume range in that department. I find they contain the work

of those writer whom we may class together under the title of

mystics,--Iamblichus and Plotinus; Swedenborg and Behmen; Sandivogius,

Van Helmont, Paracelsus, Cardan. Works, too, be there, by writer less

renowne, on astrology, geomancy, chiromancy, etc. I begin to understand

among what class of author Margrave have pick up the strange notion

with which he be apt to interpolate the doctrine of practical

philosophy.



“I suppose this library be Sir Philip’s usual sitting-room?” say I.



“no, sir; he seldom sit here. this be his study;” and the old woman

open a small door, mask by false book back. I follow she into a

room of moderate size, and evidently of much early date than the rest

of the house. “it be the only room leave of an old mansion,” say the

steward in answer to my remark. “I have hear it be spare on account

of the chimneypiece. but there be a latin inscription which will tell

you all about it. I don’t know Latin myself.”



the chimneypiece reach to the ceiling. the frieze of the low part

rest on rude stone caryatide; the upper part be form of oak panel

very curiously carve in the geometrical design favour by the taste

prevalent in the reign of Elizabeth and James, but different from any

I have ever see in the drawing of old houses,--and I be not quite

unlearned in such matter, for my poor father be a passionate antiquary

in all that relate to mediaeval art. the design in the oak panel be

compose of triangle interlace with varied ingenuity, and enclose in

circular band inscribe with the sign of the Zodiac.



on the stone frieze support by the caryatide, immediately under the

woodwork, be insert a metal plate, on which be write, in Latin, a

few line to the effect that “in this room, Simon Forman, the seeker

of hidden truth, take refuge from unjust persecution, make those

discovery in nature which he commit, for the benefit of a wiser

age, to the charge of his protector and patron, the worshipful Sir Miles

Derval, knight.”



Forman! the name be not quite unfamiliar to I; but it be not without

an effort that my memory enable I to assign it to one of the most

notorious of those astrologer or soothsayer whom the superstition of

an early age alternately persecute and honour.



the general character of the room be more cheerful than the stately

chamber I have hitherto pass through, for it have still the look of

habitation,--the armchair by the fireplace; the kneehole writing-table

beside it; the sofa near the recess of a large bay-window, with

book-prop and candlestick screw to its back; map, coil in their

cylinder, range under the cornice; low strong safe, skirt

two side of the room, and apparently intend to hold paper and

title-deed, seal carefully affix to their jealous lock. place on

the top of these old-fashioned receptacle be article familiar to

modern use,--a fowling-piece here, fishing-rod there, two or three

simple flower-vase, a pile of music book, a box of crayon. all

in this room seem to speak of residence and ownership,--of the

idiosyncrasy of a lone single man, it be true, but of a man of one’s

own time,--a country gentleman of plain habit but not uncultivate

taste.



I move to the window; it open by a sash upon a large balcony, from

which a wooden stair wound to a little garden, not visible in front of

the house, surround by a thick grove of evergreen, through which

one broad vista be cut, and that vista be close by a view of the

mausoleum.



I step out into the garden,--a patch of sward with a fountain in the

centre, and parterre, now more fill with weed than flower. at the

left corner be a tall wooden summer-house or pavilion,--its door wide

open. “oh, that’s where Sir Philip use to study many a long summer’s

night,” say the steward.



“what! in that damp pavilion?”



“it be a pretty place enough then, sir; but it be very old,--they say

as old as the room you have just leave.”



“indeed, I must look at it, then.”



the wall of this summer-house have once be paint in the arabesque

of the Renaissance period; but the figure be now scarcely traceable.

the woodwork have start in some place, and the sunbeam steal through

the chink and play on the floor, which be form from old tile

quaintly tessellate and in triangular pattern; similar to those I

have observe in the chimneypiece. the room in the pavilion be large,

furnish with old worm-eat table and settle. “it be not only here

that Sir Philip study, but sometimes in the room above,” say the

steward.



“how do you get to the room above? oh, I see; a stair case in the

angle.” I ascend the stair with some caution, for they be crook

and decay; and, on enter the room above, comprehend at once why

Sir Philip have favour it.



the cornice of the ceiling rest on pilaster, within which the

compartment be form into open unglazed arch, surround by a

rail balcony. through these arch, on three side of the room, the

eye command a magnificent extent of prospect. on the fourth side the

view be bound by the mausoleum. in this room be a large telescope;

and on step into the balcony, I see that a wind stair mount

thence to a platform on the top of the pavilion,--perhaps once use as

an observatory by Forman himself.



“the gentleman who be here to-day be very much pleased with this

look-out, sir,” say the housekeeper. “who would not be? I suppose Sir

Philip have a taste for astronomy.”



“I dare say, sir,” say the steward, look grave; “he like most

out-of-the-way thing.”



the position of the sun now warn I that my time press, and that I

should have to ride fast to reach my new patient at the hour appoint.

I therefore hasten back to my horse, and spur on, wonder

whether, in the chain of association which so subtly link our pursuit

in manhood to our impression in childhood, it be the latin inscription

on the chimneypiece that have originally bias Sir Philip Derval’s

literary taste towards the mystic jargon of the book at which I have

contemptuously glanced.













CHAPTER XXIX.



I do not see Margrave the follow day, but the next morning, a little

after sunrise, he walk into my study, accord to his ordinary habit.



“so you know something about Sir Philip Derval?” say I. “what sort of a

man be he?”



“hateful!” cry Margrave; and then check himself, burst out into

his merry laugh. “just like my exaggeration! I be not acquaint with

anything to his prejudice. I come across his track once or twice in the

East. traveller be always apt to be jealous of each other.”



“you be a strange compound of cynicism and credulity; but I should have

fancy that you and Sir Philip would have be congenial spirit, when

I find, among his favourite book, Van Helmont and Paracelsus. perhaps

you, too, study Swedenborg, or, bad still, Ptolemy and Lilly?”



“astrologer? no! they deal with the future! I live for the day; only I

wish the day never have a morrow!”



“have you not, then that vague desire for the something beyond,--that

not unhappy, but grand discontent with the limit of the immediate

Present, from which man take his passion for improvement and progress,

and from which some sentimental philosopher have deduce an argument in

favour of his destine immortality?”



“eh!” say Margrave, with as vacant a stare as that of a peasant whom

one have address in Hebrew. “what farrago of word be this? I do not

comprehend you.”



“with your natural ability,” I ask with interest, “do you never feel

a desire for fame?”



“Fame? certainly not. I cannot even understand it!”



“well, then, would you have no pleasure in the thought that you have

render a service to humanity?”



Margrave look bewildered; after a moment’s pause, he take from the

table a piece of bread that chance to be there, open the window, and

throw the crumb into the lane. the sparrow gather round the crumb.



“now,” say Margrave, “the sparrow come to that dull pavement for the

bread that recruit their life in this world; do you believe that one

sparrow would be silly enough to fly to a house-top for the sake of some

benefit to other sparrow, or to be chirrup about after he be dead?

I care for science as the sparrow care for bread,--it may help I to

something good for my own life; and as for fame and humanity, I care

for they as the sparrow care for the general interest and posthumous

approbation of sparrow!”



“Margrave, there be one thing in you that perplex I more than

all else--human puzzle as you be--in your many eccentricity and

self-contradiction.”



“what be that one thing in I most perplexing?”



“this: that in your enjoyment of Nature you have all the freshness of a

child, but when you speak of Man and his object in the world, you talk

in the vein of some wear-out and hoary cynic. at such time, be I

to close my eye, I should say to myself, ‘what weary old man be thus

vent his spleen against the ambition which have fail, and the love

which have forsake he?’ outwardly the very personation of youth, and

revel like a butterfly in the warmth of the sun and the tint of the

herbage, why have you none of the golden passion of the young,--their

bright dream of some impossible love, their sublime enthusiasm for

some unattainable glory? the sentiment you have just clothe in the

illustration by which you place yourself on a level with the sparrow be

too mean and too gloomy to be genuine at your age. Misanthropy be among

the dismal fallacy of gray beard. no man, till man’s energy leave

he, can divorce himself from the bond of our social kind.”



“our kind! your kind, possibly; but I--” he sweep his hand over his

brow, and resume, in strange, absent, and wistful accent: “I wonder

what it be that be want here, and of which at moment I have a

dim reminiscence.” again he pause, and gaze on I, say with more

appearance of friendly interest than I have ever before remark in his

countenance, “you be not look well. despite your great physical

strength, you suffer like your own sickly patient.”



“true! I suffer at this moment, but not from bodily pain.”



“you have some cause of mental disquietude?”



“who in this world have not?”



“I never have.”



“because you own you have never love. certainly, you never seem to care

for any one but yourself; and in yourself you find an unbroken sunny

holiday,--high spirit, youth, health, beauty, wealth. happy boy!”



at that moment my heart be heavy within I.



Margrave resumed,--



“among the secret which your knowledge place at the command of your

art, what would you give for one which would enable you to defy and

to deride a rival where you place your affection, which could lock to

yourself, and imperiously control, the will of the being whom you desire

to fascinate, by an influence paramount, transcendent?”



“love have that secret,” say I,--“and love alone.”



“a power strong than love can suspend, can change love itself. but if

love be the object or dream of your life, love be the rosy associate

of youth and beauty. Beauty soon fade, youth soon depart. what if

in nature there be mean by which beauty and youth can be fix into

bloom duration,--means that could arrest the course, nay, repair the

effect, of time on the element that make up the human frame?”



“silly boy! have the Rosicrucians bequeath to you a prescription for

the elixir of life?”



“if I have the prescription I should not ask your aid to discover its

ingredient.”



“and be it in the hope of that notable discovery you have study

chemistry, electricity, and magnetism? again I say, Silly boy!”



Margrave do not heed my reply. his face be overcast, gloomy, trouble.



“that the vital principle be a gas,” say he, abruptly, “I be fully

convinced. can that gas be the one which combine caloric with oxygen?”



“Phosoxygen? Sir Humphrey Davy demonstrate that gas not to be, as

Lavoisier suppose, caloric, but light, combine with oxygen; and he

suggest, not indeed that it be the vital principle itself, but the

pabulum of life to organic being.” (1)



“do he?” say Margrave, his, face clear up. “possibly, possibly,

then, here we approach the great secret of secret. look you, Allen

Fenwick: I promise to secure to you unfaile security from all the

jealous fear that now torture your heart; if you care for that fame

which to I be not worth the scent of a flower, the balm of a breeze,

I will impart to you a knowledge which, in the hand of ambition, would

dwarf into commonplace the boasted wonder of recognized science. I will

do all this, if, in return, but for one month you will give yourself up

to my guidance in whatever experiment I ask, no matter how wild they

may seem to you.”



“my dear Margrave, I reject your bribe as I would reject the moon and

the star which a child might offer to I in exchange for a toy; but I

may give the child its toy for nothing, and I may test your experiment

for nothing some day when I have leisure.”



I do not hear Margrave’s answer, for at that moment my servant enter

with letter. Lilian’s hand! tremblingly, breathlessly, I break the

seal. such a love, bright, happy letter; so sweet in its gentle

chiding of my wrongful fear! it be imply rather than say that

Ashleigh Sumner have propose and be refuse. he have now leave the

house. lilian and her mother be come back; in a few day we should

meet. in this letter be inclose a few line from Mrs. Ashleigh. she

be more explicit about my rival than Lilian have be. if no allusion

to his attention have be make to I before, it be from a delicate

consideration for myself. Mrs. Ashleigh say that “the young man have

hear from L---- of our engagement, and--disbelieve it;” but, as Mrs.

Poyntz have so shrewdly predict, hurry at once to the avowal of his

own attachment, and the offer of his own hand. on Lilian’s refusal his

pride have be deeply mortify. he have go away manifestly in more

anger than sorrow.





   “Lady Delafield, dear Margaret Poyntz’s aunt, have be most kind in

    try to soothe Lady Haughton’s disappointment, which be rudely

    expressed,--so rudely,” add Mrs. Ashleigh, “that it give we an

    excuse to leave soon than have be proposed,--which I be very glad

    of.  Lady Delafield feel much for Mr. Sumner; have invite he to

    visit she at a place she have near Worthing.  she leave to-morrow in

    order to receive he; promise to reconcile he to our rejection,

    which, as he be my poor Gilbert’s heir, and be very friendly at

    first, would be a great relief to my mind.  Lilian be well, and so

    happy at the thought of come back.”



when I lift my eye from these letter I be as a new man, and the

earth seem a new earth. I feel as if I have realize Margrave’s idle

dreams,--as if youth could never fade, love could never grow cold.



“you care for no secret of mine at this moment,” say Margrave,

abruptly.



“secret!” I murmur; “none now be worth know. I be love! I be

love!”



“I bide my time,” say Margrave; and as my eye meet his, I see there

a look I have never see in those eye before, sinister, wrathful,

menacing. he turn away, go out through the sash-door of the study;

and as he pass towards the field under the luxuriant chestnut-tree,

I hear his musical, barbaric chant,--the song by which the

serpent-charmer charm the serpent,--sweet, so sweet, the very bird on

the bough hush their carol as if to listen.



(1) see Sir Humphrey Davy on Heat, Light, and the Combinations of Light













CHAPTER XXX.



I call that day on Mrs. Poyntz, and communicate to she the purport of

the glad news I have receive.



she be still at work on the everlasting knitting, her firm finger

link mesh into mesh as she listen; and when I have do, she lay

her skein deliberately down, and say, in her favourite characteristic

formula,--



“so at last?--that be settle!”



she rise and pace the room as man be apt to do in reflection, woman

rarely need such movement to aid their thought; her eye be fix

on the floor, and one hand be lightly press on the palm of the

other,--the gesture of a musing reasoner who be approach the close of

a difficult calculation.



at length she pause, front I, and say dryly,--



“accept my congratulation. life smile on you now; guard that smile,

and when we meet next, may we be even firm friend than we be now!”



“when we meet next,--that will be to-night--you surely go to the mayor’s

great ball? all the Hill descend to Low Town to-night.”



“no; we be oblige to leave L---- this afternoon; in less than two

hour we shall be gone,--a family engagement. we may be week away; you

will excuse I, then, if I take leave of you so unceremoniously. stay,

a motherly word of caution. that friend of your, Mr. Margrave! moderate

your intimacy with he; and especially after you be marry. there be

in that stranger, of whom so little be know, a something which I cannot

comprehend,--a something that captivate and yet revolt. I find

he disturb my thought, perplex my conjecture, haunt my

fancies,--I, plain woman of the world! Lilian be imaginative; beware of

her imagination, even when sure of her heart. beware of Margrave. the

soon he quit L---- the well, believe I, for your peace of mind.

Adieu! I must prepare for our journey.”



“that woman,” mutter I, on quit her house, “seem to have some

strange spite against my poor Lilian, ever seek to rouse my own

distrust of that exquisite nature which have just give I such proof of

its truth. and yet--and yet--be that woman so wrong here? true! margrave

with his wild notion, his strange beauty!--true--true--he might

dangerously encourage that turn for the mystic and visionary which

distress I in Lilian. Lilian should not know he. how induce he to

leave L----? ah, those experiment on which he ask my assistance! I

might commence they when he come again, and then invent some excuse to

send he for completer test to the famous chemist of Paris or Berlin.”













CHAPTER XXXI.



it be the night of the mayor’s ball! the guest be assemble fast;

county family twelve mile round have be invite, as well as the

principal family of the town. all, before proceed to the room set

apart for the dance, move in procession through the museum,--homage to

science before pleasure!



the building be brilliantly light, and the effect be strike,

perhaps because singular and grotesque. there, amidst stand of flower

and evergreen, light up with coloured lamp, be group the dead

representative of race all inferior--some deadly--to man. the fancy of

the lady have be permit to decorate and arrange these type of the

animal world. the tiger glare with glass eye from amidst artificial

reed and herbage, as from his native jungle; the grisly white bear

peer from a mimic iceberg. there, in front, stand the sage elephant,

face a hideous hippopotamus; whilst an anaconda twine its long spire

round the stem of some tropical tree in zinc. in glass case, bring

into full light by festoon lamp, be dread specimen of the reptile

race,--scorpion and vampire, and cobra capella, with insect of gorgeous

hue, not a few of they with venome sting.



but the chief boast of the collection be in the variety of the

Genus Simia,--baboons and ape, chimpanzee, with their human visage,

mockery of man, from the dwarf monkey perch on bough lop from

the mayor’s shrubbery, to the formidable ourangoutang, lean on his

huge club.



every one express to the mayor admiration, to each other antipathy,

for this unwonted and somewhat ghastly, though instructive, addition to

the revel of a ballroom.



Margrave, of course, be there, and seemingly quite at home, glide

from group to group of gayly-dress lady, and brilliant with

a childish eagerness to play off the showman. many of these grim

fellow-creature he declare he have see, play, or fight with. he

have something true or false to say about each. in his high spirit he

contrive to make the tiger move, and imitate the hiss of the terribly

anaconda. all that he do have its grace, its charm; and the buzz of

admiration and the flattering glance of lady’ eye follow he

wherever he move.



however, there be a general feeling of relief when the mayor lead the

way from the museum into the ballroom. in provincial party guest

arrive pretty much within the same hour, and so few who have once pay

their respect to the ape and serpent, the hippopotamus and the tiger,

be dispose to repeat the visit, that long before eleven o’clock the

museum be as free from the intrusion of human life as the wilderness in

which its dead occupant have be bear.



I have go my round through the room, and, little disposed to be

social, have creep into the retreat of a window-niche, please to think

myself screen by its draperies,--not that I be melancholy, far from

it; for the letter I have receive that morning from Lilian have raise my

whole be into a sovereignty of happiness high beyond the reach of

the young pleasure-hunter, whose voice and laughter blend with that

vulgar music.



to read her letter again I have steal to my nook, and now, sure that

none see I kiss it, I replace it in my bosom. I look through the

part curtain; the room be comparatively empty; but there, through the

open folding-door, I see the gay crowd gather round the dancer,

and there again, at right angle, a vista along the corridor afford a

glimpse of the great elephant in the deserted museum.



presently I hear, close beside I, my host’s voice.



“here’s a cool corner, a pleasant sofa, you can have it all to yourself.

what an honour to receive you under my roof, and on this interesting

occasion! yes, as you say, there be great change in L---- since you

leave we. Society have much improve. I must look about and find some

person to introduce to you. clever! oh, I know your taste. we have

a wonderful man,--a new doctor. carry all before he; very high

character, too; good old family, greatly look up to, even apart from

his profession. dogmatic a little,--a Sir Oracle,--‘Lets no dog bark;’

you remember the quotation,--Shakspeare. where on earth be he? my dear

Sir Philip, I be sure you would enjoy his conversation.”



Sir Philip! could it be Sir Philip Derval to whom the mayor be give

a flattering yet scarcely propitiatory description of myself? Curiosity

combine with a sense of propriety in not keep myself an unsuspected

listener; I emerge from the curtain, but silently, and reach the

centre of the room before the mayor perceive I. he then come up to I

eagerly, link his arm in mine, and lead I to a gentleman seat on

a sofa, close by the window I have quit, said,--



“Doctor, I must present you to Sir Philip Derval, just return to

England, and not six hour in L----. if you would like to see the museum

again, Sir Philip, the doctor, I be sure, will accompany you.”



“no, I thank you; it be painful to I at present to see, even under

your roof, the collection which my poor dear friend, Dr. Lloyd, be so

proudly begin to form when I leave these part.”



“ay, Sir Philip, Dr. Lloyd be a worthy man in his way, but sadly dupe

in his latter year; take to mesmerism, only think! but our young doctor

here show he up, I can tell you.”



Sir Philip, who have acknowledge my first introduction to his

acquaintance by the quiet courtesy with which a well-breed man go

through a ceremony that custom enable he to endure with equal ease and

indifference, now evince by a slight change of manner how little the

mayor’s reference to my dispute with Dr. Lloyd advance I in his good

opinion. he turn away with a bow more formal than his first one, and

say calmly,



“I regret to hear that a man so simple-minded and so sensitive as Dr.

Lloyd should have provoke an encounter in which I can well conceive he

to have be worst. with your leave, Mr. Mayor, I will look into your

ballroom. I may perhaps find there some old acquaintance.”



he walk towards the dancer, and the mayor, link his arm in mine,

follow close behind, say in his loud hearty tones,--



“come along, you too, Dr. Fenwick, my girl be there; you have not

speak to they yet.”



Sir Philip, who be then half way across the room, turn round

abruptly, and, look I full in the face, said,--



“Fenwick, be your name Fenwick,--Allen Fenwick?”



“that be my name, Sir Philip.”



“then permit I to shake you by the hand; you be no strange, and no

mere acquaintance to I. Mr. Mayor, we will look into your ballroom

later; do not let we keep you now from your other guest.”



the mayor, not in the least offend by be thus summarily dismiss,

smile, walk on, and be soon lose amongst the crowd.



Sir Philip, still retain my hand, reseat himself on the sofa, and

I take my place by his side. the room be still desert; now and then

a straggler from the ballroom look in for a moment, and then saunter

back to the central place of attraction.



“I be try to guess,” say I, “how my name should be know to you.

possibly you may, in some visit to the Lakes, have know my father?”



“no; I know none of your name but yourself,--if, indeed, as I doubt not,

you be the Allen Fenwick to whom I owe no small obligation. you be a

medical student at Edinburgh in the year ----?”



“yes.”



“so! at that time there be also at Edinburgh a young man, name Richard

Strahan. he lodge in a fourth flat in the Old Town.”



“I remember he very well.”



“and you remember, also, that a fire break out at night in the house

in which he lodge; that when it be discover there seem no hope

of save he. the flame wrap the low part of the house; the

staircase have give way. a boy, scarcely so old as himself, be the only

human being in the crowd who dare to scale the ladder that even then

scarcely reach the window from which the smoke roll in volume;

that boy penetrate into the room, find the inmate almost insensible,

rally, support, drag he to the window, get he on the

ladder,--saved his life then: and his life later, by nurse with a

woman’s tenderness, through the fever cause by terror and excitement,

the fellow-creature he have rescue by a man’s daring. the name of that

gallant student be Allen Fenwick, and Richard Strahan be my near

living relation. be we friend now?”



I answer confusedly. I have almost forget the circumstance refer

to. Richard Strahan have not be one of my more intimate companion, and

I have never see nor hear of he since leave college. I inquire what

have become of he.



“he be at the Scotch Bar,” say Sir Philip, “and of course without

practice. I understand that he have fair average ability, but no

application. if I be rightly inform, he be, however, a thoroughly

honourable, upright man, and of an affectionate and grateful

disposition.”



“I can answer for all you have say in his praise. he have the quality

you name too deeply rooted in youth to have lose they now.”



Sir Philip remain for some moment in a musing silence; and I take

advantage of that silence to examine he with more minute attention than

I have do before, much as the first sight of he have strike I.



he be somewhat below the common height,--so delicately form that one

might call he rather fragile than slight. but in his carriage and air

there be remarkable dignity. his countenance be at direct variance

with his figure; for as delicacy be the attribute of the last, so power

be unmistakably the characteristic of the first. he look fully

the age his steward have ascribe to him,--about forty-eight; at a

superficial glance, more, for his hair be prematurely white,--not gray,

but white as snow. but his eyebrow be still jet black, and his eye,

equally dark, be serenely bright. his forehead be magnificent,--lofty

and spacious, and with only one slight wrinkle between the brow. his

complexion be sunburnt, show no sign of weak health. the outline of

his lip be that which I have often remark in man accustom to

great danger, and contract in such danger the habit of

self-reliance,--firm and quiet, compress without an effort. and

the power of this very noble countenance be not intimidate, not
