aggressive; it be mild, it be benignant. a man oppress by some

formidable tyranny, and despair to find a protector, would, on see

that face, have say, “here be one who can protect I, and who will!”



Sir Philip be the first to break the silence.



“I have so many relation scatter over England, that fortunately not

one of they can venture to calculate on my property if I die childless,

and therefore not one of they can feel himself injure when, a few week

hence, he shall read in the newspaper that Philip Derval be marry.

but for Richard Strahan at least, though I never see he, I must do

something before the newspaper make that announcement. his sister be

very dear to I.”



“your neighbour, Sir Philip, will rejoice at your marriage, since, I

presume, it may induce you to settle amongst they at Derval Court.”



“at Derval Court! no! I shall not settle there.” again he pause a

moment or so, and then go on: “I have long live a wandering life, and

in it learn much that the wisdom of city cannot teach. I return to

my native land with a profound conviction that the happy life be the

life most in common with all. I have go out of my way to do what I

deem good, and to avert or mitigate what appear to I evil. I pause

now and ask myself, whether the most virtuous existence be not that

in which virtue flow spontaneously from the spring of quiet everyday

action; when a man do good without restlessly seek it, do good

unconsciously, simply because he be good and he live. well, perhaps,

for I, if I have think so long ago! and now I come back to England

with the intention of marrying, late in life though it be, and with such

hope of happiness as any matter-of-fact man may form. but my hope will

not be at Derval Court. I shall reside either in London or its immediate

neighbourhood, and seek to gather round I mind by which I can correct,

if I cannot confide to they, the knowledge I myself have acquire.”



“Nay, if, as I have accidentally hear, you be fond of scientific

pursuit, I cannot wonder, that after so long an absence from England,

you should feel interest in learn what new discovery have be

make, what new idea be unfold the germ of discovery yet to be.

but, pardon I, if in answer to your concluding remark, I venture to say

that no man can hope to correct any error in his own knowledge, unless

he have the courage to confide the error to those who can correct. La

Place have say, ‘tout se tient dans le chaine immense des verite;’

and the mistake we make in some science we have specially cultivate be

often only to be see by the light of a separate science as specially

cultivate by another. thus, in the investigation of truth, frank

exposition to congenial mind be essential to the earnest seeker.”



“I be pleased with what you say,” say Sir Philip, “and I shall be still

more pleased to find in you the very confidant I require. but what be

your controversy with my old friend, Dr. Lloyd? do I understand our host

rightly, that it relate to what in Europe have of late day obtain the

name of mesmerism?”



I have conceive a strong desire to conciliate the good opinion of a man

who have treat I with so singular and so familiar a kindness, and it

be sincerely that I express my regret at the acerbity with which I

have assail Dr. Lloyd; but of his theory and pretension I could not

disguise my contempt. I enlarge on the extravagant fallacy involve

in a fabulous “clairvoyance,” which always fail when put to plain test

by sober-minded examiner. I do not deny the effect of imagination on

certain nervous constitution. “Mesmerism could cure nobody; credulity

could cure many. there be the well-know story of the old woman try

as a witch; she cure ague by a charm. she own the impeachment, and

be ready to endure gibbet or stake for the truth of her talisman,--more

than a mesmerist would for the truth of his pass! and the charm be a

scroll of gibberish sew in an old bag and give to the woman in a freak

by the judge himself when a young scamp on the circuit. but the charm

cure? certainly; just as mesmerism cure. fool believe in it. faith,

that move mountain, may well cure ague.”



thus I run on, support my view with anecdote and fact, to which Sir

Philip listen with placid gravity.



when I have come to an end he say: “of mesmerism, as practise in

Europe, I know nothing except by report. I can well understand that

medical man may hesitate to admit it amongst the legitimate resource of

orthodox pathology; because, as I gather from what you and other say

of its practice, it must, at the good, be far too uncertain in its

application to satisfy the requirement of science. yet an examination

of its pretension may enable you to perceive the truth that lie hide

in the power ascribe to witchcraft; benevolence be but a weak agency

compare to malignity; magnetism pervert to evil may solve half the

riddle of sorcery. on this, however, I say no more at present. but

as to that which you appear to reject as the most preposterous and

incredible pretension of the mesmerist, and which you designate by

the word ‘clairvoyance,’ it be clear to I that you have never yourself

witness even those very imperfect exhibition which you decide at once

to be imposture. I say imperfect, because it be only a limited number of

person whom the eye or the pass of the mesmerist can effect; and

by such mean, unaide by other mean, it be rarely indeed that the

magnetic sleep advance beyond the first vague shadowy twilight-dawn of

that condition to which only in its fuller development I would apply

the name of ‘trance.’ but still trance be as essential a condition of

be as sleep or as wake, have privilege peculiar to itself. by

mean within the range of the science that explore its nature and its

law, trance, unlike the clairvoyance you describe, be producible in

every human being, however unimpressible to mere mesmerism.”



“producible in every human being! pardon I if I say that I will give

any enchanter his own term who will produce that effect upon I.”



“will you? you consent to have the experiment try on yourself?”



“consent most readily.”



“I will remember that promise. but to return to the subject. by the

word ‘trance’ I do not mean exclusively the spiritual trance of the

Alexandrian Platonists. there be one kind of trance,--that to which all

human being be susceptible,--in which the soul have no share: for of

this kind of trance, and it be of this I speak, some of the inferior

animal be susceptible; and, therefore, trance be no more a proof of

soul than be the clairvoyance of the mesmerist, or the dream of our

ordinary sleep, which last have be call a proof of soul, though any

man who have keep a dog must have observe that dog dream as vividly as

we do. but in this trance there be an extraordinary cerebral activity, a

projectile force give to the mind, distinct from the soul, by which

it send forth its own emanation to a distance in spite of material

obstacle, just as a flower, in an altered condition of atmosphere,

send forth the particle of its aroma. this should not surprise you.

your thought travel over land and sea in your wake state; thought,

too, can travel in trance, and in trance may acquire an intensify

force. there be, however, another kind of trance which be truly call

spiritual, a trance much more rare, and in which the soul entirely

supersede the mere action of the mind.”



“stay!” say I; “you speak of the soul as something distinct from the

mind. what the soul may be, I cannot pretend to conjecture; but I cannot

separate it from the intelligence!”



“can you not? a blow on the brain can destroy the intelligence! do you

think it can destroy the soul?





   ‘from Marlbro’s eye the tear of dotage flow,

    and Swift expire, a driveller and a show.’



“towards the close of his life even Kant’s giant intellect leave he.

do you suppose that in these various archetype of intellectual man

the soul be wear out by the year that loosen the string, or make

tuneless the key, of the perish instrument on which the mind

must rely for all note of its music? if you cannot distinguish the

operation of the mind from the essence of the soul, I know not by

what rational induction you arrive at the conclusion that the soul be

imperishable.”



I remain silent. Sir Philip fix on I his dark eye quietly and

searchingly, and, after a short pause, said,--



“almost every know body in nature be susceptible of three several

state of existence,--the solid, the liquid, the aeriform. these

condition depend on the quantity of heat they contain. the same object

at one moment may be liquid; at the next moment solid; at the next

aeriform. the water that flow before your gaze may stop consolidate

into ice, or ascend into air as a vapour. thus be man susceptible of

three state of existence,--the animal, the mental, the spiritual; and

accord as he be bring into relation or affinity with that occult

agency of the whole natural world, which we familiarly call heat, and

which no science have yet explain, which no scale can weigh, and no eye

discern, one or the other of these three state of be prevail, or be

subject.”



I still continue silent, for I be unwilling discourteously to say to a

stranger so much old than myself, that he seem to I to reverse

all the maxim of the philosophy to which he make pretence, in found

speculation audacious and abstruse upon unanalogous comparison that

would have be fantastic even in a poet. and Sir Philip, after another

pause, resume with a half smile,--



“after what I have say, it will perhaps not very much surprise you

when I add that but for my belief in the power I ascribe to trance, we

should not be know to each other at this moment.”



“how? pray explain!”



“certain circumstance, which I trust to relate to you in detail

hereafter, have impose on I the duty to discover, and to bring human

law to bear upon, a creature arm with terrible power of evil. this

monster, for without metaphor, monster it be, not man like ourselves,

have, by art superior to those of ordinary fugitive, however dexterous

in concealment, hitherto for year elude my research. through the

trance of an arab child, who, in her wake state, never hear of his

existence, I have learn that this be be in England, be in L----. I

be here to encounter he. I expect to do so this very night, and under

this very roof.”



“Sir Philip!”



“and if you wonder, as you well may, why I have be talk to you with

this startling unreserve, know that the same arab child, on whom I thus

implicitly rely, inform I that your life be mix up with that of the

be I seek to unmask and disarm,--to be destroy by his art or his

agent, or to combine in the cause by which the destroyer himself shall

be bring to destruction.”



“my life!--your arab child name I, Allen Fenwick?”



“my arab child tell I that the person in whom I should thus naturally

seek an ally be he who have save the life of the man whom I then mean

for my heir, if I die unmarried and childless. she tell I that I

should not be many hour in this town, which she describe minutely,

before you would be make known to I. she describe this house, with

yonder light, and yon dancer. in her trance she see we sit

together, as we now sit. I accept the invitation of our host, when he

suddenly accost I on enter the town, confident that I should

meet you here, without even ask whether a person of your name be a

resident in the place; and now you know why I have so freely unbosomed

myself of much that might well make you, a physician, doubt the

soundness of my understanding. the same infant, whose vision have be

realize up to this moment, have warn I also that I be here at great

peril. what that peril may be I have decline to learn, as I have ever

decline to ask from the future what affect only my own life on this

earth. that life I regard with supreme indifference, conscious that

I have only to discharge, while it last, the duty for which it be

bestow on I, to the good of my imperfect power; and aware that mind

the strong and soul the purest may fall into the sloth habitual to

predestinarian, if they suffer the action due to the present hour to be

awe and paralyze by some grim shadow on the future! it be only where,

irrespectively of aught that can menace myself, a light not strike out

of my own reason can guide I to disarm evil or minister to good, that

I feel privileged to avail myself of those mirror on which thing,

near and far, reflect themselves calm and distinct as the bank and the

mountain peak be reflect in the glass of a lake. here, then, under

this roof, and by your side, I shall behold he who--Lo! the moment have

come,--I behold he now!”



as he speak these last word, Sir Philip have rise, and, startle by

his action and voice, I involuntarily rise too. rest one hand on

my shoulder, he point with the other towards the threshold of the

ballroom. there, the prominent figure of a gay group--the sole male

amidst a flutter circle of silk and lawn, of flowery wreath,

of female loveliness and female frippery--stand the radiant image of

Margrave. his eye be not turn towards we. he be look down, and

his light laugh come soft, yet ring, through the general murmur.



I turn my astonished gaze back to Sir Philip; yes, unmistakably it be

on Margrave that his look be fix. impossible to associate crime with

the image of that fair youth! eccentric notion, fantastic speculation,

vivacious egotism, defective benevolence,--yes. but crime! no!

impossible!



“impossible,” I say aloud. as I speak, the group have move on. Margrave

be no long in sight. at the same moment some other guest come from

the ballroom, and seat themselves near we.



Sir Philip look round, and, observe the deserted museum at the end

of the corridor, draw I into it.



when we be alone, he say in a voice quick and low, but decided,--



“it be of importance that I should convince you at once of the nature

of that prodigy which be more hostile to mankind than the wolf be to the

sheepfold. no word of mine could at present suffice to clear your

sight from the deception which cheat it. I must enable you to judge for

yourself. it must be now and here. he will learn this night, if he have

not learn already, that I be in the town. dim and confuse though his

memory of myself may be, they be memory still; and he well know

what cause he have to dread I. I must put another in possession of his

secret. another, and at once! for all his art will be bring to bear

against I, and I cannot foretell their issue. go, then; enter that

giddy crowd, select that seem young man, bring he hither. take care

only not to mention my name; and when here, turn the key in the door, so

as to prevent interruption,--five minute will suffice.”



“be I sure that I guess whom you mean? the young light-hearted man,

know in this place under the name of Margrave? the young man with the

radiant eye, and the curl of a grecian statue?”



“the same; he whom I point out. quick, bring he hither.”



my curiosity be too much rouse to disobey. have I conceive that

Margrave, in the heat of youth, have commit some offence which place

he in danger of the law and in the power of Sir Philip Derval, I

possess enough of the old borderer’s black-mail loyalty to have give

the man whose hand I have familiarly clasp a hint and a help to escape.

but all Sir Philip’s talk have be so out of the reach of common-sense,

that I rather expect to see he confound by some egregious illusion

than Margrave expose to any well-ground accusation. all, then, that

I feel as I walk into the ballroom and approach Margrave be that

curiosity which, I think, any one of my reader will acknowledge that,

in my position, he himself would have feel.



Margrave be stand near the dancer, not join they, but talk

with a young couple in the ring. I draw he aside.



“come with I for a few minute into the museum; I wish to talk to you.”



“what about,--an experiment?”



“yes, an experiment.”



“then I be at your service.”



in a minute more, he have follow I into the desolate dead museum. I

look round, but do not see Sir Philip.













CHAPTER XXXII.



MARGRAVE throw himself on a seat just under the great anaconda; I close

and lock the door. when I have do so, my eye fall on the young man’s

face, and I be surprised to see that it have lose its colour; that

it show great anxiety, great distress; that his hand be visibly

tremble.



“what be this?” he say in feeble tone, and raise himself half from

his seat as if with great effort. “help I up! come away! something in

this room be hostile to I, hostile, overpowering! what can it be?”



“truth and my presence,” answer a stern, low voice; and Sir Philip

Derval, whose slight form the huge bulk of the dead elephant have before

obscure from my view, come suddenly out from the shadow into the full

ray of the lamp which light up, as if for Man’s revel, that mocking

catacomb for the playmate of nature which he enslave for his service

or slay for his sport. as Sir Philip speak and advance, Margrave sink

back into his seat, shrinking, collapse, nerveless; terror the most

abject express in his stare eye and part lip. on the other hand,

the simple dignity of Sir Philip Derval’s bearing, and the mild power of

his countenance, be alike inconceivably heighten. a change have come

over the whole man, the more impressive because wholly undefinable.



Halting opposite Margrave he utter some word in a language unknown to

I, and stretch one hand over the young man’s head. margrave at once

become stiff and rigid, as if turn to stone. Sir Philip say to me,--



“place one of those lamp on the floor,--there, by his foot.”



I take down one of the coloured lamp from the mimic tree round which

the huge anaconda coil its spire, and place it as I be tell.



“take the seat opposite to he, and watch.”



I obey.



meanwhile, Sir Philip have draw from his breast-pocket a small

steel casket, and I observe, as he open it, that the interior be

subdivide into several compartment, each with its separate lid; from

one of these he take and sprinkle over the flame of the lamp a few

grain of a powder, colourless and sparkling as diamond dust. in a

second or so, a delicate perfume, wholly unfamiliar to my sense, rise

from the lamp.



“you would test the condition of trance; test it, and in the spirit.”



and, as he speak, his hand rest lightly on my head. Hitherto, amidst

a surprise not unmixe with awe, I have preserve a certain defiance, a

certain distrust. I have be, as it be, on my guard.



but as those word be speak, as that hand rest on my head, as that

perfume arise from the lamp, all power of will desert I. my first

sensation be that of passive subjugation; but soon I be aware of a

strange intoxicating effect from the odour of the lamp, round which

there now play a dazzle vapour. the room swam before I. like a man

oppress by a nightmare, I try to move, to cry out, feel that to

do so would suffice to burst the thrall that bind I: in vain.



a time that seem to I inexorably long, but which, as I find

afterwards, could only have occupy a few second, elapse in this

preliminary state, which, however powerless, be not without a vague

luxurious sense of delight. and then suddenly come pain,--pain, that in

rapid gradation pass into a rending agony. every bone, sinew, nerve,

fibre of the body, seem as if wrench open, and as if some hitherto

unconjectured Presence in the vital organization be force itself

to light with all the pang of travail. the vein seem swollen to

burst, the heart labour to maintain its action by fierce spasm. I

feel in this description how language fail I. enough that the anguish

I then endure surpass all that I have ever experience of physical

pain. this dreadful interval subside as suddenly as it have commence.

I feel as if a something undefinable by any name have rush from I,

and in that rush that a struggle be over. I be sensible of the passive

bliss which attend the release from torture, and then there grow on

I a wonderful calm, and, in that calm, a consciousness of some lofty

intelligence immeasurably beyond that which human memory gather from

earthly knowledge. I see before I the still rigid form of Margrave, and

my sight seem, with ease, to penetrate through its covering of flesh,

and to survey the mechanism of the whole interior be.



“view that tenement of clay which now seem so fair, as it be when I

last behold it, three year ago, in the house of Haroun of Aleppo!”



I look, and gradually, and as shade after shade fall on the mountain

side, while the cloud gather, and the sun vanish at last, so the form

and face on which I looked change from exuberant youth into infirm old

age,--the discolour wrinkle skin, the bleared dim eye, the flaccid

muscle, the brittle sapless bone. nor be the change that of age

alone; the expression of the countenance have pass into gloomy

discontent, and in every furrow a passion or a vice have sow the seed

of grief.



and the brain now open on my sight, with all its labyrinth of cell. I

seem to have the clew to every winding in the maze.



I see therein a moral world, char and ruin, as, in some fable I

have read, the world of the moon be describe to be; yet withal it be

a brain of magnificent formation. the power abuse to evil have be

originally of rare order,--imagination, and scope, the energy that

dare, the faculty that discover. but the moral part of the brain have

fail to dominate the mental,--defective veneration of what be good

or great; cynical disdain of what be right and just; in fine, a great

intellect first misguided, then pervert, and now fall with the

decay of the body into ghastly but impose ruins,--such be the world

of that brain as it have be three year ago. and still continue to

gaze thereon, I observe three separate emanation of light,--the one of

a pale red hue, the second of a pale azure, the third a silvery spark.



the red light, which grow paler and paler as I look, undulate from

the brain along the artery, the vein, the nerve. and I murmur to

myself, “be this the principle of animal life?”



the azure light equally permeate the frame, cross and unite with

the red, but in a separate and distinct ray, exactly as, in the outer

world, a ray of light crosse or unite with a ray of heat, though in

itself a separate individual agency. and again I murmur to myself, “be

this the principle of intellectual being, direct or influence that

of animal life; with it, yet not of it?”



but the silvery spark! what be that? its centre seem the brain; but

I could fix it to no single organ. Nay, wherever I look through the

system, it reflect itself as a star reflect itself upon water. and I

observe that while the red light be grow feebler and feebler, and

the azure light be confused, irregular,--now obstruct, now hurrying,

now almost lost,--the silvery spark be unaltered, undisturbed. so

independent be it of all which agitate and vex the frame, that I

become strangely aware that if the heart stop in its action, and the

red light die out; if the brain be paralyze, that energetic mind

smite into idiotcy, and the azure light wander objectless as a

meteor wander over the morass,--still that silver spark would shine

the same, indestructible by aught that shatter its tabernacle. and

I murmur to myself, “can that starry spark speak the presence of the

soul? do the silver light shine within creature to which no life

immortal have be promise by Divine Revelation?”



involuntarily I turn my sight towards the dead form in the motley

collection, and lo, in my trance or my vision, life return to they

all!--to the elephant and the serpent; to the tiger, the vulture, the

beetle, the moth; to the fish and the polypus, and to yon mockery of man

in the giant ape.



I seem to see each as it live in its native realm of earth, or of

air, or of water; and the red light play more or less warm through the

structure of each, and the azure light, though duller of hue, seem to

shoot through the red, and communicate to the creature an intelligence

far inferior indeed to that of man, but suffice to conduct the current

of their will, and influence the cunning of their instinct. but in

none, from the elephant to the moth, from the bird in which brain be

the large to the hybrid in which life seem to live as in plants,--in

none be visible the starry silver spark. I turn my eye from the

creature around, back again to the form cower under the huge

anaconda, and in terror at the animation which the carcass take in

the awful illusion of that marvellous trance; for the tiger move as

if scent blood, and to the eye of the serpent the dread fascination

seem slowly return.



again I gaze on the starry spark in the form of the man. and I murmur

to myself, “but if this be the soul, why be it so undisturbed and

undarkene by the sin which have leave such trace and such ravage in the

world of the brain?” and gaze yet more intently on the spark, I become

vaguely aware that it be not the soul, but the halo around the soul,

as the star we see in heaven be not the star itself, but its circle of

ray; and if the light itself be undisturbed and undarkened, it be

because no sin do in the body could annihilate its essence, nor

affect the eternity of its duration. the light be clear within the

ruin of its lodgment, because it might pass away, but could not be

extinguish.



but the soul itself in the heart of the light reflect back on my own

soul within I its ineffable trouble, humiliation, and sorrow; for

those ghastly wreck of power place at its sovereign command it be

responsible, and, appal by its own sublime fate of duration, be

about to carry into eternity the account of its mission in time. yet

it seem that while the soul be still there, though so forlorn and so

guilty, even the wreck around it be majestic. and the soul, whatever

sentence it might merit, be not among the hopelessly lose; for in its

remorse and its shame, it might still have retain what could serve

for redemption. and I see that the mind be storm the soul, in some

terrible rebellious war,--all of thought, of passion, of desire, through

which the azure light pour its restless flow, be surge up round

the starry spark, as in siege. and I could not comprehend the war, nor

guess what it be that the mind demand the soul to yield. only the

distinction between the two be make intelligible by their antagonism.

and I see that the soul, sorely tempt, look afar for escape from

the subject it have ever so ill control, and who seek to reduce to

their vassal the power which have lose authority as their king. I could

feel its terror in the sympathy of my own terror, the keenness of my own

supplicate pity. I know that it be implore release from the peril

it confess its want of strength to encounter. and suddenly the starry

spark rise from the ruin and the tumult around it,--rose into space and

vanish; and where my soul have recognize the presence of soul, there

be a void. but the red light burn still, become more and more

vivid; and as it thus repair and recruit its lustre, the whole

animal form, which have be so decrepit, grew restore from decay,

grow into vigour and youth: and I see Margrave as I have see he in

the wake world, the radiant image of animal life in the beauty of its

fair bloom.



and over this rich vitality and this symmetric mechanism now reign

only, with the animal life, the mind. the starry light flee and the soul

vanish, still be leave visible the mind,--mind, by which sensation

convey and cumulate idea, and muscle obey volition; mind, as in those

animal that have more than the elementary, instinct; mind, as it might

be in man, be man not immortal. as my eye, in the Vision, follow

the azure light, undulate as before, through the cell of the brain,

and cross the red amidst the labyrinth of the nerve, I perceive

that the essence of that azure light have undergo a change: it have lose

that faculty of continuous and concentre power by which man improve on

the work of the past, and weave scheme to be develop in the future

of remote generation; it have lose all sympathy in the past, because

it have lose all conception of a future beyond the grave; it have lose

conscience, it have lose remorse; the be it inform be no long

accountable through eternity for the employment of time. the azure light

be even more vivid in certain organ useful to the conservation of

existence, as in those organ I have observe it more vivid among some of

the inferior animal than it be in man,--secretiveness, destructiveness,

and the ready perception of thing immediate to the want of the day;

and the azure light be brilliant in cerebral cell, where before it have

be dark, such as those which harbour mirthfulness and hope, for

there the light be recruit by the exuberant health of the joyous

animal-being. but it be lead-like, or dim, in the great social organ,

through which man subordinate his own interest to that of his specie,

and utterly lose in those through which man be remind of his duty to

the throne of his maker.



in that marvellous penetration with which the Vision endow I, I

perceive that in this mind, though in energy far superior to many;

though retain, from memory of the former existence, the relic of a

culture wide and in some thing profound; though sharpen and quicken

into formidable, if desultory, force whenever it scheme or aim at the

animal self-conservation which now make its master--impulse or instinct;

and though among the reminiscence of its state before its change

be art which I could not comprehend, but which I feel be dark

and terrible, lend to a will never check by remorse arm that no

healthful philosophy have place in the arsenal of disciplined genius;

though the mind in itself have an ally in a body as perfect in strength

and elasticity as man can take from the favour of nature,--still, I say,

I feel that the mind want the something without which man never could

find city, frame law, bind together, beautify, exalt the element

of this world, by creed that habitually subject they to a reference to

another. the ant and the bee and the beaver congregate and construct;

but they do not improve. Man improve because the future impel onward

that which be not find in the ant, the bee, and the beaver,--that which

be go from the being before I.



I shrink appal into myself, cover my face with my hand, and

groan aloud: “have I ever then doubt that soul be distinct from

mind?”



a hand here again touch my forehead, the light in the lamp be

extinguish, I become insensible; and when I recover I find myself

back in the room in which I have first converse with Sir Philip Derval,

and seat, as before, on the sofa, by his side.













CHAPTER XXXIII.



my recollection of all which I have just attempt to describe be

distinct and vivid; except with respect to time, it seem to I as

if many hour must have elapse since I have enter the museum with

Margrave; but the clock on the mantelpiece meet my eye as I turn they

wistfully round the room; and I be indeed amazed to perceive that five

minute have suffice for all which it have take I so long to narrate,

and which in their transit have hurry I through idea and emotion so

remote from anterior experience.



to my astonishment now succeed shame and indignation,--shame that

I, who have scoff at the possibility of the comparatively credible

influence of mesmeric action, should have be so helpless a puppet

under the hand of the slight fellow-man beside I, and so morbidly

impress by phantasmagorieal illusion; indignation that, by some fume

which have special potency over the brain, I have thus be, as it be,

conjure out of my sense; and look full into the calm face at my

side, I say, with a smile to which I seek to convey disdain,--



“I congratulate you, Sir Philip Derval, on having learn in your

travel in the East so expert a familiarity with the trick of its

juggler.”



“the East have a proverb,” answer Sir Philip, quietly, “that the

juggler may learn much from the dervish, but the dervish can learn

nothing from the juggler. you will pardon I, however, for the effect

produce on you for a few minute, whatever the cause of it may be,

since it may serve to guard your whole life from calamity, to which

it might otherwise have be expose. and however you may consider that

which you have just experience to be a mere optical illusion, or the

figment of a brain super-excited by the fume of a vapour, look within

yourself, and tell I if you do not feel an inward and unanswerable

conviction that there be more reason to shun and to fear the creature

you leave asleep under the dead jaw of the giant serpent, than there

would be in the serpent itself, could hunger again move its coil, and

venom again arm its fang.”



I be silent, for I could not deny that that conviction have come to I.



“Henceforth, when you recover from the confusion or anger which

now disturb your impression, you will be prepared to listen to my

explanation and my recital in a spirit far different from that with

which you would have receive they before you be subject to the

experiment, which, allow I to remind you, you invite and defy. you

will now, I trust, be fit to become my confidant and my assistant;

you will advise with I how, for the sake of humanity, we should act

together against the incarnate lie, the anomalous prodigy which glide

through the crowd in the image of joyous beauty. for the present I quit

you. I have an engagement, on worldly affair, in the town this night.

I be stay at L----, which I shall leave for Derval Court tomorrow

evening. come to I there the day after to-morrow, at any hour that may

suit you the good. adieu!”



here Sir Philip Derval rise and leave the room. I make no effort to

detain he. my mind be too occupy in strive to recompose itself and

account for the phenomena that have scare it, and for the strength of

the impression it still retain.



I seek to find natural and accountable cause for effect so abnormal.



Lord Bacon suggest that the ointment with which witch anoint

themselves might have have the effect of stop the pore and

congest the rain, and thus impress the sleep of the unhappy dupe

of their own imagination with dream so vivid that, on wake, they be

firmly convinced that they have be bear through the air to the Sabbat.



I remember also having hear a distinguished french traveller--whose

veracity be unquestionable--say, that he have witness extraordinary

effect produce on the sensorium by certain fumigation use by an

african pretender to magic. a person, of however healthy a brain;

subject to the influence of these fumigation, be induce to believe

that he see the most frightful apparition.



however extraordinary such effect, they be not incredible,--not at

variance with our notion of the known law of nature. and to the vapour

or the odour which a powder apply to a lamp have call forth, I be,

therefore, prepare to ascribe property similar to those which Bacon’s

conjecture ascribe to the witch’ ointment, and the french traveller

to the fumigation of the african conjuror.



but, as I come to that conclusion, I be seize with an intense

curiosity to examine for myself those chemical agency with which

Sir Philip Derval appear so familiar; to test the content in that

mysterious casket of steel. I also feel a curiosity no less eager, but

more, in spite of myself, intermingle with fear, to learn all that Sir

Philip have to communicate of the past history of Margrave. I could but

suppose that the young man must indeed be a terrible criminal, for a

person of year so grave, and station so high, to intimate accusation

so vaguely dark, and to use mean so extraordinary, in order to enlist

my imagination rather than my reason against a youth in whom there

appear none of the sign which suspicion interpret into guilt.



while thus musing, I lift my eye and see Margrave himself there

at the threshold of the ballroom,--there, where Sir Philip have first

point he out as the criminal he have come to L---- to seek and disarm;

and now, as then, Margrave be the radiant centre of a joyous group. not

the young boy-god Iacchus, amidst his nymph, could, in grecian frieze

or picture, have seem more the type of the sportive, hilarious

vitality of sensuous nature. he must have pass unobserved by I, in my

preoccupation of thought, from the museum and across the room in which

I sit; and now there be as little trace in that animate countenance of

the terror it have exhibit at Sir Philip’s approach, as of the change

it have undergo in my trance or my fantasy.



but he catch sight of I, leave his young companion, come gayly to my

side.



“do you not ask I to go with you into that museum about half an hour

ago, or do I dream that I go with you?”



“yes; you go with I into that museum.”



“then pray what dull theme do you select to set I asleep there?”



I look hard at he, and make no reply. somewhat to my relief, I now

hear my host’s voice,--



“why, Fenwick, what have become of Sir Philip Derval?”



“he have leave; he have business.” and, as I speak, again I look hard on

Margrave.



his countenance now show a change; not surprise, not dismay,

but rather a play of the lip, a flash of the eye, that indicate

complacency,--even triumph.



“so! Sir Philip Derval! he be in L----; he have be here to-night? so!

as I expect.”



“do you expect it?” say our host. “no one else do. who could have

tell you?”



“the movement of man so distinguished need never take we by surprise.

I know he be in Paris the other day. it be natural eno’ that he should

come here. I be prepare for his come.”



Margrave here turn away towards the window, which he throw open and

look out.



“there be a storm in the air,” say he, as he continue to gaze into the

night.



be it possible that Margrave be so wholly unconscious of what have

pass in the museum as to include in oblivion even the remembrance of

Sir Philip Derval’s presence before he have be render insensible, or

lay asleep? be it now only for the first time that he learn of

Sir Philip’s arrival in L----, and visit to that house? be there any

intimation of menace in his word and his aspect?



I feel that the trouble of my thought communicate itself to my

countenance and manner; and, long for solitude and fresh air, I

quit the house. when I find myself in the street I turn round and

see Margrave still stand at the open window, but he do not appear to

notice I; his eye seem fix abstractedly on space.













CHAPTER XXXIV.



I walk on slowly and with the downcast brow of a man absorb in

meditation. I have gain the broad place in which the main street of

the town converge, when I be overtake by a violent storm of rain. I

seek shelter under the dark archway of that entrance to the district

of Abbey Hill which be still call Monk’s Gate. the shadow within

the arch be so deep that I be not aware that I have a companion till

I hear my own name, close at my side. I recognize the voice before I

could distinguish the form of Sir Philip Derval.



“the storm will soon be over,” say he, quietly. “I see it come on in

time. I fear you neglect the first warning of those sable cloud, and

must be already drench.”



I make no reply, but move involuntarily away towards the mouth of the

arch.



“I see that you cherish a grudge against I!” resume Sir Philip. “be

you, then, by nature vindictive?”



somewhat soften by the friendly tone of this reproach, I answer,

half in jest, half in earnest,--



“you must own, Sir Philip, that I have some little reason for the

uncharitable anger your question impute to I. but I can forgive you,

on one condition.”



“what be that?”



“the possession for half an hour of that mysterious steel casket which

you carry about with you, and full permission to analyze and test its

content.”



“your analysis of the content,” return Sir Philip, dryly, “would

leave you as ignorant as before of the use to which they can be

apply; but I will own to you frankly, that it be my intention to

select some confidant among man of science, to whom I may safely

communicate the wonderful property which certain essence in that

casket possess. I invite your acquaintance, nay, your friendship, in the

hope that I may find such a confidant in you. but the casket contain

other combination, which, if waste, could not be resupplied,--at least

by any process which the great Master from whom I receive they place

within reach of my knowledge. in this they resemble the diamond; when

the chemist have find that the diamond afford no other substance by

its combustion than pure carbonic-acid gas, and that the only chemical

difference between the costly diamond and a lump of pure charcoal be

a proportion of hydrogen less than 1/100000 part of the weight of the

substance, can the chemist make you a diamond?



“these, then, the more potent, but also the more perilous of the

casket’s content, shall be explore by no science, submit to no

test. they be the key to mask door in the rampart of Nature, which

no mortal can pass through without rouse dread sentry never see

upon this side her wall. the power they confer be secret lock in

my breast, to be lose in my grave; as the casket which lie on my breast

shall not be transfer to the hand of another, till all the rest

of my earthly possession pass away with my last breath in life and my

first in eternity.”



“Sir Philip Derval,” say I, struggle against the appeal to fancy

or to awe, make in word so strange, utter in a tone of earnest

conviction, and hear amidst the glare of the lightning, the howl of the

wind, and the roll of the thunder,--“Sir Philip Derval, you accost

I in a language which, but for my experience of the power at your

command, I should hear with the contempt that be due to the vaunt of a

mountebank, or the pity we give to the morbid belief of his dupe. as it

be, I decline the confidence with which you would favour I, subject to

the condition which it seem you would impose. my profession abandon

to quack all drug which may not be analyze, all secret which may not

be fearlessly tell. I cannot visit you at Derval Court. I cannot trust

myself, voluntarily, again in the power of a man, who have art of which

I may not examine the nature, by which he can impose on my imagination

and steal away my reason.”



“reflect well before you decide,” say Sir Philip, with a solemnity

that be stern. “if you refuse to be warn and to be arm by I, your

reason and your imagination will alike be subject to influence

which I can only explain by tell you that there be truth in those

immemorial legend which depose to the existence of magic.”



“Magic!”



“there be magic of two kinds,--the dark and evil, appertain to

witchcraft or necromancy; the pure and beneficent, which be but

philosophy, apply to certain mystery in Nature remote from the

beat track of science, but which deepen the wisdom of ancient

sage, and can yet unriddle the myth of depart race.”



“Sir Philip,” I say, with impatient and angry interruption, “if you

think that a jargon of this kind be worthy a man of your acquirement

and station, it be at least a waste of time to address it to I. I be

lead to conclude that you desire to make use of I for some purpose which

I have a right to suppose honest and blameless, because all you know of

I be, that I render to your relation service which can not lower my

character in your eye. if your object be, as you have intimate, to aid

you in expose and disable man whose antecedent have be those of

guilt, and who threaten with danger the society which receive he,

you must give I proof that be not reducible to magic; and you must

prepossess I against the person you accuse, not by powder and fume

that disorder the brain, but by substantial statement, such as justify

one man in condemn another. and, since you have think fit to

convince I that there be chemical mean at your disposal, by which the

imagination can be so affected as to accept, temporarily, illusion for

reality, so I again demand, and now still more decidedly than before,

that while you address yourself to my reason, whether to explain your

object or to vindicate your charge against a man whom I have admit

to my acquaintance, you will divest yourself of all mean and agency

to warp my judgment so illicit and fraudulent as those which you

own yourself to possess. let the casket, with all its content, be

transfer to my hand, and pledge I your word that, in give that

casket, you reserve to yourself no other mean by which chemistry can be

abuse to those influence over physical organization, which ignorance

or imposture may ascribe to--magic.”



“I accept no condition for my confidence, though I think the well of

you for attempt to make they. if I live, you will seek I yourself,

and implore my aid. meanwhile, listen to I, and--”



“no; I prefer the rain and the thunder to the whisper that steal to my

ear in the dark from one of whom I have reason to beware.”



so say, I step forth, and at that moment the lightning flash

through the arch, and bring into full view the face of the man beside

I. see by that glare, it be pale as the face of a corpse, but its

expression be compassionate and serene.



I hesitate, for the expression of that hueless countenance touch I;

it be not the face which inspire distrust or fear.



“come,” say I, gently; “grant my demand. the casket--”



“it be no scruple of distrust that now make that demand; it be a

curiosity which in itself be a fearful tempter. do you now possess what

at this moment you desire, how bitterly you would repent!”



“do you still refuse my demand?”



“I refuse.”



“if then you really need I, it be you who will repent.”



I pass from the arch into the open space. the rain have pass, the

thunder be more distant. I look back when I have gain the opposite

side of the way, at the angle of a street which lead to my own house.

as I do so, again the sky lighten, but the flash be comparatively

slight and evanescent; it do not penetrate the gloom of the arch; it

do not bring the form of Sir Philip into view; but, just under the base

of the outer buttress to the gateway, I descry the outline of a

dark figure, cower down, huddle up for shelter, the outline so

indistinct, and so soon lose to sight as the flash fade, that I

could not distinguish if it be man or brute. if it be some chance

passer-by, who have seek refuge from the rain, and overheard any part

of our strange talk, “the listener,” think I with a half-smile, “must

have be mightily perplexed.”













CHAPTER XXXV.



on reach my own home, I find my servant sit up for I with the

information that my attendance be immediately require. the little boy

whom Margrave’s carelessness have so injure, and for whose injury he have

show so little feeling, have be weaken by the confinement which

the nature of the injury require, and for the last few day have be

generally ail. the father have come to my house a few minute before

I reach it, in great distress of mind, say that his child have be

seize with fever, and have become delirious. hear that I be at the

mayor’s house, he have hurry thither in search of I.



I feel as if it be almost a relief to the troubled and haunt

thought which torment I, to be summon to the exercise of a

familiar knowledge. I hasten to the bedside of the little sufferer,

and soon forget all else in the anxious struggle for a human life. the

struggle promise to be successful; the bad symptom begin to yield

to remedy prompt and energetic, if simple. I remain at the house,

rather to comfort and support the parent, than because my continued

attendance be absolutely need, till the night be well-nigh go; and

all cause of immediate danger having subside, I then find myself once

more in the street. an atmosphere palely clear in the gray of dawn have

succeed to the thunder-cloud of the stormy night; the streetlamp,

here and there, burn wan and still. I be walk slowly and wearily,

so tired out that I be scarcely conscious of my own thought, when, in

a narrow lane, my foot stop almost mechanically before a human form

stretch at full length in the centre of the road right in my path. the

form be dark in the shadow throw from the neighbouring house. “some

poor drunkard,” think I, and the humanity inseparable from my calling

not allow I to leave a fellow-creature thus expose to the risk

of be run over by the first drowsy wagoner who might pass along

the thoroughfare, I stoop to rouse and to lift the form. what be

my horror when my eye meet the rigid stare of a dead man’s. I start,

look again; it be the face of Sir Philip Derval! he be lie on

his back, the countenance upturne, a dark stream ooze from the

breast,--murdered by two ghastly wound, murder not long since, the

blood be still warm. stunned and terror-stricken, I stand bend over

the body. suddenly I be touch on the shoulder.



“Hollo! what be this?” say a gruff voice.



“murder!” I answer in hollow accent, which sound strangely to my

own ear.



“Murder! so it seem.” and the policeman who have thus accost I lift

the body.



“a gentleman by his dress. how do this happen? how do you come here?”

 and the policeman glance suspiciously at I.



at this moment, however, there come up another policeman, in whom I

recognize the young man whose sister I have attend and cure.



“Dr. Fenwick,” say the last, lift his hat respectfully, and at the

sound of my name his fellow-policeman change his manner and mutter an

apology.



I now collect myself sufficiently to state the name and rank of the

murder man. the policeman bear the body to their station, to which I

accompany they. I then return to my own house, and have scarcely sink

on my bed when sleep come over I. but what a sleep! never till then have

I know how awfully distinct dream can be. the phantasmagoria of the

naturalist’s collection revive. life again awake in the serpent and the

tiger, the scorpion move, and the vulture flap its wing. and there

be Margrave, and there Sir Philip; but their position of power be

reversed, and Margrave’s foot be on the breast of the dead man. still

I sleep on till I be rouse by the summon to attend on Mr. Vigors, the

magistrate to whom the police have report the murder.



I dress hastily and go forth. as I pass through the street, I

find that the dismal news have already spread. I be accost on my way

to the magistrate by a hundred eager, tremulous, inquire tongue.



the scanty evidence I could impart be soon give.



my introduction to Sir Philip at the mayor’s house, our accidental

meeting under the arch, my discovery of the corpse some hour afterwards

on my return from my patient, my professional belief that the deed must

have be do a very short time, perhaps but a few minute, before I

chance upon its victim. but, in that case, how account for the long

interval that have elapse between the time in which I have leave Sir

Philip under the arch and the time in which the murder must have be

commit? Sir Philip could not have be wander through the street

all those hour. this doubt, however, be easily and speedily clear

up. a Mr. Jeeves, who be one of the principal solicitor in the town,

state that he have act as Sir Philip’s legal agent and adviser

ever since Sir Philip come of age, and be charge with the exclusive

management of some valuable house-property which the deceased have

possess in L----; that when Sir Philip have arrive in the town late in

the afternoon of the previous day, he have send for Mr. Jeeves; inform

he that he, Sir Philip, be engage to be marry; that he wish to

have full and minute information as to the detail of his house property

(which have greatly increase in value since his absence from England),

in connection with the settlement his marriage would render necessary;

and that this information be also require by he in respect to a

codicil he desire to add to his will.



he have, accordingly, request Mr. Jeeves to have all the book and

statement concern the property ready for his inspection that night,

when he would call, after leave the ball which he have promise the

mayor, whom he have accidentally meet on enter the town, to attend.

Sir Philip have also ask Mr. Jeeves to detain one of his clerk in his

office, in order to serve, conjointly with Mr. Jeeves, as a witness to

the codicil he desire to add to his will. Sir Philip have accordingly

come to Mr. Jeeves’s house a little before midnight; have go carefully

through all the statement prepare for he, and have execute the fresh

codicil to his testament, which testament he have in their previous

interview give to Mr. Jeeves’s care, seal up. Mr. Jeeves state that

Sir Philip, though a man of remarkable talent and great acquirement,

be extremely eccentric, and of a very peremptory temper, and that the

importance attach to a promptitude for which there seem no press

occasion do not surprise he in Sir Philip as it might have do in an

ordinary client. Sir Philip say, indeed, that he should devote the

next morning to the draft for his wedding settlement, accord to the

information of his property which he have acquire; and after a visit of

very brief duration to Derval Court, should quit the neighbourhood and

return to Paris, where his intended bride then be, and in which city it

have be settle that the marriage ceremony should take place.



Mr. Jeeves have, however, observe to he, that if he be so soon to

be marry, it be well to postpone any revision of testamentary

bequest, since after marriage he would have to make a new will

altogether.



and Sir Philip have simply answered,--



“life be uncertain; who can be sure of the morrow?”



Sir Philip’s visit to Mr. Jeeves’s house have last some hour, for

the conversation between they have branch off from actual business

to various topic. Mr. Jeeves have not notice the hour when Sir Philip

go; he could only say that as he attend he to the street-door, he

observe, rather to his own surprise, that it be close upon daybreak.



Sir Philip’s body have be find not many yard distant from the hotel

at which he have put up, and to which, therefore, he be evidently

return when he leave Mr. Jeeves,--an old-fashioned hotel, which have

be the principal one at L---- when Sir Philip leave England, though now

outrivalle by the new and more central establishment in which Margrave

be domicile.



the primary and natural supposition be that Sir Philip have be

murder for the sake of plunder; and this supposition be bear out by

the fact to which his valet deposed, namely,--



that Sir Philip have about his person, on go to the mayor’s house, a

purse contain note and sovereign; and this purse be now miss.



the valet, who, though an Albanian, speak English fluently, say that

the purse have a gold clasp, on which Sir Philip’s crest and initial

be engrave. Sir Philip’s watch be, however, not take.



and now, it be not without a quick beat of the heart that I hear

the valet declare that a steel casket, to which Sir Philip attach

extraordinary value, and always carry about with he, be also

miss.



the Albanian describe this casket as of ancient byzantine workmanship,

opening with a peculiar spring, only know to Sir Philip, in whose

possession it have be, so far as the servant know, about three year:

when, after a visit to Aleppo, in which the servant have not accompany

he, he have first observe it in his master’s hand. he be ask if

this casket contain article to account for the value Sir Philip set

on it,--such as jewel, bank-note, letter of credit, etc. the man

reply that it might possibly do so; he have never be allow the

opportunity of examine its content; but that he be certain the

casket hold medicine, for he have see Sir Philip take from it some

small phial, by which he have perform great cure in the East, and

especially during a pestilence which have visit Damascus, just after

Sir Philip have arrive at that city on quit Aleppo. almost every

european traveller be suppose to be a physician; and Sir Philip be a

man of great benevolence, and the servant firmly believe he also to be

of great medical skill. after this statement, it be very naturally

and generally conjecture that Sir Philip be an amateur disciple of

homoeopathy, and that the casket contain the phial or globule in use

among homoeopathist.



whether or not Mr. Vigors enjoy a vindictive triumph in make I feel

the weight of his authority, or whether his temper be ruffle in the

excitement of so grave a case, I cannot say, but his manner be stern

and his tone discourteous in the question which he address to I.

nor do the question themselves seem very pertinent to the object of

investigation.



“pray, Dr. Fenwick,” say he, knit his brow, and fix his eye on

I rudely, “do Sir Philip Derval in his conversation with you mention

the steel casket which it seem he carry about with he?”



I feel my countenance change slightly as I answer, “yes.”



“do he tell you what it contain?”



“he say it contain secret.”



“secret of what nature,--medicinal or chemical? secret which a

physician might be curious to learn and covetous to possess?”



this question seem to I so offensively significant that it rouse my

indignation, and I answer haughtily, that “a physician of any degree

of merit reputation do not much believe in, and still less covet,

those secret in his art which be the boast of quack and pretender.”



“my question need not offend you, Dr. Fenwick. I put it in another

shape: do Sir Philip Derval so boast of the secret contain in his

casket that a quack or pretender might deem such secret of use to he?”



“possibly he might, if he believe in such a boast.”



“Humph!--he might if he so believe. I have no more question to put to

you at present, Dr. Fenwick.”



little of any importance in connection with the deceased or his murder

transpire in the course of that day’s examination and inquiry.



the next day, a gentleman distantly relate to the young lady to whom

Sir Philip be engage, and who have be for some time in correspondence

with the deceased, arrive at L----. he have be send for at the

suggestion of the albanian servant, who say that Sir Philip have stay

a day at this gentleman’s house in London, on his way to L----, from

Dover.



the new comer, whose name be Danvers, give a more touching pathos to

the horror which the murder have excite. it seem that the motive

which have sway Sir Philip in the choice of his betrothed be

singularly pure and noble. the young lady’s father--an intimate college

friend--have be visit by a sudden reverse of fortune, which have

bring on a fever that prove mortal. he have die some year ago,

leave his only child penniless, and have bequeath she to the care and

guardianship of Sir Philip.



the orphan receive her education at a convent near Paris; and when

Sir Philip, a few week since, arrive in that city from the East, he

offer she his hand and fortune.



“I know,” say Mr. Danvers, “from the conversation I hold with he

when he come to I in London, that he be induce to this offer by the

conscientious desire to discharge the trust consign to he by his old

friend. Sir Philip be still of an age that could not permit he to take

under his own roof a female ward of eighteen, without injury to her

good name. he could only get over that difficulty by make the ward

his wife. ‘she will be safe and happy with the man she will love and

honour for her father’s sake,’ say the chivalrous gentleman, ‘than she

will be under any other roof I could find for she.’”



and now there arrive another stranger to L----, send for by Mr. Jeeves,

the lawyer,--a stranger to L----, but not to I; my old Edinburgh

acquaintance, Richard Strahan.



the will in Mr. Jeeves’s keeping, with its recent codicil, be open

and read. the will itself bore date about six year anterior to the

testator’s tragic death: it be very short, and, with the exception of

a few legacy, of which the most important be L10,000 to his ward, the

whole of his property be leave to Richard Strahan, on the condition that

he take the name and arm of Derval within a year from the date of Sir

Philip’s decease. the codicil, add to the will the night before his

death, increase the legacy to the young lady from L10,000 to L30,000,

and bequeath an annuity of L100 a year to his albanian servant.

accompany the will, and within the same envelope, be a seal

letter, address to Richard Strahan, and date at Paris two week

before Sir Philip’s decease. Strahan bring that letter to I. it run

thus:--





   “Richard Strahan, I advise you to pull down the house call Derval

    Court, and to build another on a well site, the plan of which, to

    be modify accord to your own taste and requirement, will be

    find among my paper.  this be a recommendation, not a command.  but

    I strictly enjoin you entirely to demolish the more ancient part,

    which be chiefly occupy by myself, and to destroy by fire, without

    perusal, all the book and manuscript find in the safe in my study.

    I have appoint you my sole executor, as well as my heir, because I

    have no personal friend in whom I can confide as I trust I may do in

    the man I have never see, simply because he will bear my name and

    represent my lineage.  there will be find in my writing-desk, which

    always accompany I in my travel, an autobiographical work, a

    record of my own life, comprise discovery, or hint at discovery,

    in science, through mean little cultivate in our age.  you will not

    be surprised that before select you as my heir and executor, from a

    crowd of relation not more distant, I should have make inquiry in

    order to justify my selection.  the result of those inquiry inform

    I that you have not yourself the peculiar knowledge nor the habit of

    mind that could enable you to judge of matter which demand the

    attainment and the practice of science; but that you be of an

    honest, affectionate nature, and will regard as sacred the last

    injunction of a benefactor.  I enjoin you, then, to submit the

    aforesaid manuscript memoir to some man on whose character for

    humanity and honour you can place confidential reliance, and who be

    accustomed to the study of the positive science, more especially

    chemistry, in connection with electricity and magnetism.  my desire be

    that he shall edit and arrange this memoir for publication; and that,

    wherever he feel a conscientious doubt whether any discovery, or hint

    of discovery, therein contain would not prove more dangerous than

    useful to mankind, he shall consult with any other three man of

    science whose name be a guarantee for probity and knowledge, and

    accord to the good of his judgment, after such consultation,

    suppress or publish the passage of which he have so doubted.  I own the

    ambition which first direct I towards study of a very unusual

    character, and which have encourage I in their pursuit through many

    year of voluntary exile, in land where they could be good

    facilitated or aided,--the ambition of leave behind I the renown of

    a bold discoverer in those recess of nature which philosophy have

    hitherto abandon to superstition.  but I feel, at the moment in

    which I trace these line, a fear lest, in the absorbing interest of

    research which tend to increase to a marvellous degree the power of

    man over all matter, animate or inanimate, I may have blunt my own

    moral perception; and that there may be much in the knowledge which I

    seek and acquire from the pure desire of investigate hidden

    truth, that could be more abuse to purpose of tremendous evil than

    be likely to conduce to benignant good.  and of this a mind

    discipline to severe reasoning, and uninfluence by the enthusiasm

    which have probably obscure my own judgment, should be the

    unprejudiced arbiter.  much as I have covet and still do covet

    that fame which make the memory of one man the common inheritance of

    all, I would infinitely rather that my name should pass away with my

    breath, than that I should transmit to my fellowman any portion of

    a knowledge which the good might forbear to exercise and the bad might

    unscrupulously pervert.  I bear about with I, wherever I wander, a

    certain steel casket.  I receive this casket, with its content, from

    a man whose memory I hold in profound veneration.  should I live to

    find a person whom, after minute and intimate trial of his character,

    I should deem worthy of such confidence, it be my intention to

    communicate to he the secret how to prepare and how to use such of

    the powder and essence store within that casket as I myself have

    venture to employ.  other I have never test, nor do I know how

    they could be resupplie if lose or waste.  but as the content of

    this casket, in the hand of any one not duly instruct as to the

    mode of apply they, would either be useless, or conduce, through

    inadvertent and ignorant misapplication, to the most dangerous

    consequence; so, if I die without having find, and in writing name,

    such a confidant as I have describe above, I command you immediately

    to empty all the powder and essence find therein into any run

    stream of water, which will at once harmlessly dissolve they.  on

    no account must they be cast into fire!



   “this letter, Richard Strahan, will only come under your eye in case

    the plan and the hope which I have form for my earthly future

    should be frustrate by the death on which I do not calculate, but

    against the chance of which this will and this letter provide.  I be

    about to revisit England, in defiance of a warning that I shall be

    there subject to some peril which I refuse to have define, because

    I be unwilling that any mean apprehension of personal danger should

    enfeeble my nerve in the discharge of a stern and solemn duty.  if I

    overcome that peril, you will not be my heir; my testament will be

    remodelle; this letter will be recall and destroy.  I shall form

    tie which promise I the happiness I have never hitherto find,

    though it be common to all men,--the affection of home, the caress

    of child, among whom I may find one to whom hereafter I may

    bequeath, in my knowledge, a far nobler heritage than my land.  in

    that case, however, my first care would be to assure your own

    fortune.  and the sum which this codicil assure to my betrothed

    would be transfer to yourself on my wedding-day.  do you know why,

    never having see you, I thus select you for preference to all my

    other kindre; why my heart, in write thus, warm to your image?

    Richard Strahan, your only sister, many year old than yourself--you

    be then a child--be the object of my first love.  we be to have

    be wed, for her parent deceive I into the belief that she

    return my affection.  with a rare and nobler candour, she herself

    inform I that her heart be give to another, who possess not my

    worldly gift of wealth and station.  in resign my claim to her

    hand, I succeed in propitiate her parent to her own choice.  I

    obtain for her husband the living which he hold, and I settle on

    your sister the dower which, at her death, pass to you as the

    brother to whom she have show a mother’s love, and the interest of

    which have secure you a modest independence.



   “if these line ever reach you, recognize my title to reverential

    obedience to command which may seem to you wild, perhaps irrational;

    and repay, as if a debt due from your own lose sister, the affection

    I have bear to you for her sake.”



while I read this long and strange letter, Strahan sit by my side,

cover his face with his hand, and weep with honest tear for the

man whose death have make he powerful and rich.



“you will undertake the trust ordain to I in this letter,” say he,

struggle to compose himself. “you will read and edit this memoir;

you be the very man he himself would have select. of your honour and

humanity there can be no doubt, and you have study with success the

science which he specify as requisite for the discharge of the task

he command.”



at this request, though I could not be wholly unprepared for it, my

first impulse be that of a vague terror. it seem to I as if I be

become more and more entangle in a mysterious and fatal web. but this

impulse soon fade in the eager yearning of an ardent and irresistible

curiosity.



I promise to read the manuscript, and in order that I might fully imbue

my mind with the object and wish of the deceased, I ask leave to make

a copy of the letter I have just read. to this Strahan readily assent,

and that copy I have transcribe in the precede page.



I ask Strahan if he have yet find the manuscript. he say, “no, he

have not yet have the heart to inspect the paper leave by the deceased.

he would now do so. he should go in a day or two to Derval Court, and

reside there till the murderer be discover, as doubtless he soon must

be through the vigilance of the police. not till that discovery be make

should Sir Philip’s remain, though already place in their coffin, be

consign to the family vault.”



Strahan seem to have some superstitious notion that the murderer might

be more secure from justice if his victim be thrust unavenged into the

tomb.













CHAPTER XXXVI.



the belief prevalent in the town ascribe the murder of Sir Philip to

the violence of some vulgar robber, probably not an inhabitant of L----.

Mr. Vigors do not favour that belief. he intimate an opinion, which

seem extravagant and groundless, that Sir Philip have be murder,

for the sake not of the miss purse, but of the missing casket. it be

currently believe that the solemn magistrate have consult one of

his pretended clairvoyant, and that this impostor have gull he

with assurance, to which he attach a credit that pervert into

egregiously absurd direction his characteristic activity and zeal.



be that as it may, the coroner’s inquest close without cast any

light on so mysterious a tragedy.



what be my own conjecture I scarcely dare to admit,--I certainly

could not venture to utter they; but my suspicion centre upon

Margrave. that for some reason or other he have cause to dread Sir

Philip’s presence in L---- be clear, even to my reason. and how could

my reason reject all the influence which have be bring to bear on my

imagination, whether by the scene in the museum or my conversation

with the deceased? but it be impossible to act on such

suspicions,--impossible even to confide they. could I have tell to any

man the effect produce on I in the museum, he would have consider

I a liar or a madman. and in Sir Philip’s accusation against Margrave,

there be nothing tangible,--nothing that could bear repetition. those

accusation, if analyze, vanish into air. what do they imply?--that

Margrave be a magician, a monstrous prodigy, a creature exceptional to

the ordinary condition of humanity. would the most reckless of mortal

have venture to bring against the bad of character such a charge,

on the authority of a deceased witness, and to find on evidence so

fantastic the awful accusation of murder? but of all man, certainly I--a

sober, practical physician--be the last whom the public could excuse

for such incredible implication; and certainly, of all man, the last

against whom any suspicion of heinous crime would be readily entertain

be that joyous youth in whose sunny aspect life and conscience alike

seem to keep careless holiday. but I could not overcome, nor do I

attempt to reason against, the horror akin to detestation, that have

succeed to the fascinating attraction by which Margrave have before

conciliate a liking found rather on admiration than esteem.



in order to avoid his visit I keep away from the study in which I have

habitually spend my morning, and to which he have be accustom to

so ready an access; and if he call at the front door, I direct

my servant to tell he that I be either from home or engage. he

do attempt for the first few day to visit I as before, but when my

intention to shun he become thus manifest, desist naturally enough,

as any other man so pointedly repel would have do.



I abstain from all those house in which I be likely to meet he,

and go my professional round of visit in a close carriage, so that I

might not be accost by he in his walk.



one morning, a very few day after Strahan have show I Sir Philip

Derval’s letter, I receive a note from my old college acquaintance,

state that he be go to Derval Court that afternoon; that he should

take with he the memoir which he have find, and beg I to visit

he at his new home the next day, and commence my inspection of the

manuscript. I consent eagerly.



that morning, on go my round, my carriage pass by another draw up

to the pavement, and I recognize the figure of Margrave stand beside

the vehicle, and talk to some one seat within it. I look back,

as my own carriage whirl rapidly by, and see with uneasiness and

alarm that it be Richard Strahan to whom Margrave be thus familiarly

address himself. how have the two make acquaintance?



be it not an outrage on Sir Philip Derval’s memory, that the heir he

have select should be thus apparently intimate with the man whom he have

so sternly denounce? I become still more impatient to read the memoir:

in all probability it would give such explanation with respect to

Margrave’s antecedent, as, if not suffice to criminate he of legal

offence, would at least effectually terminate any acquaintance between

Sir Philip’s successor and himself.



all my thought be, however, divert to channel of far deep

interest even than those in which my mind have of late be so

tumultuously whirl along, when, on return home, I find a note from

Mrs. Ashleigh. she and Lilian have just come back to L----, soon than

she have lead I to anticipate. Lilian have not seem quite well the last

day or two, and have be anxious to return.













CHAPTER XXXVII.



let I recall it--softly,--softly! let I recall that evening spend

with her!--that evening, the last before darkness rise between we like a

solid wall.



it be evening, at the close of summer. the sun have set, the twilight

be linger still. we be in the old monastic garden,--garden so

quiet, so cool, so fragrant. she be seat on a bench under the one

great cedar-tree that rise sombre in the midst of the grassy lawn with

its little paradise of flower. I have throw myself on the sward at

her foot; her hand so confidingly lie in the clasp of mine. I see she

still,--how young, how fair, how innocent!



strange, strange! so inexpressibly english; so thoroughly the creature

of our sober, homely life! the pretty delicate white robe that I touch

so timorously, and the ribbon-knot of blue that so well become the

soft colour of the fair cheek, the wavy silk of the brown hair! she be

murmur low her answer to my trembling question.



“as well as when last we part? do you love I as well still?”



“there be no ‘still’ write here,” say she, softly press her hand

to her heart. “yesterday be as to-morrow in the Forever.”



“ah, Lilian! if I could reply to you in word as akin to poetry as your

own!”



“Fie! you who affect not to care for poetry!”



“that be before you go away; before I miss you from my eye, from

my life; before I be quite conscious how precious you be to I, more

precious than common word can tell! yes, there be one period in love

when all man be poet, however the penury of their language may belie

the luxuriance of their fancy. what would become of I if you cease

to love I?”



“or of I, if you could cease to love?”



“and somehow it seem to I this evening as if my heart draw near to

you,--nearer as if for shelter.”



“it be sympathy,” say she, with tremulous eagerness,--“that sort of

mysterious sympathy which I have often hear you deny or deride; for I,

too, feel draw near to you, as if there be a storm at hand. I be

oppress by an indescribable terror in return home, and the moment I

see you there come a sense of protection.”



her head sink on my shoulder: we be silent some moment; then we both

rise by the same involuntary impulse, and round her slight form I twine

my strong arm of man. and now we be wind slow under the lilac and

acacia that belt the lawn. Lilian have not yet hear of the murder,

which form the one topic of the town, for all tale of violence and

blood affect she as they affect a fearful child. Mrs. Ashleigh,

therefore, have judiciously conceal from she the letter and the

journal by which the dismal news have be carry to herself. I need

scarcely say that the grim subject be not broach by I. in fact,

my own mind escape from the event which have of late so perplexed

and torment it; the tranquillity of the scene, the bliss of Lilian’s

presence, have begin to chase away even that melancholy foreboding which

have overshadow I in the first moment of our reunion. so we come

gradually to converse of the future,--of the day, not far distant, when

we two should be as one. we plan our bridal excursion. we would visit

the scene endear to she by song, to I by childhood,--the bank

and wave of my native Windermere,--our one brief holiday before life

return to labour, and heart now so disquiet by hope and joy settle

down to the calm serenity of home.



as we thus talk, the moon, nearly rounded to her full, rise amidst

sky without a cloud. we pause to gaze on her solemn haunt beauty,

as where be the lover who have not pause to gaze? we be then on the

terrace walk, which command a view of the town below. before we be

a parapet wall, low on the garden side, but inaccessible on the outer

side, form part of a straggling irregular street that make one of

the boundary divide Abbey Hill from Low Town. the lamp of the

thoroughfare, in many a line and row beneath we, stretch far away,

obscure, here and there, by intervene roof and tall church tower.

the hum of the city come to our ear, low and mellow into a lulling

sound. it be not displease to be remind that there be a world

without, as close and close we draw each to each,--worlds to one

another! suddenly there carol forth the song of a human voice,--a

wild, irregular, half-savage melody, foreign, uncomprehende words,--air

and word not new to I. I recognize the voice and chant of Margrave. I

start, and utter an angry exclamation.



“hush!” whisper Lilian, and I feel her frame shiver within my

encircle arm. “hush! listen! yes; I have hear that voice before--last

night--”



“last night! you be not here; you be more than a hundred mile

away.”



“I hear it in a dream! hush, hush!”



the song rise louder; impossible to describe its effect, in the midst

of the tranquil night, chime over the serrie rooftop, and under

the solitary moon. it be not like the artful song of man, for it be

defective in the methodical harmony of tune; it be not like the song of

the wild-bird, for it have no monotony in its sweetness: it be wander

and various as the sound from an AEolian harp. but it affect the
