sense to a powerful degree, as in remote land and in vast solitude I

have since find the note of the mocking-bird, suddenly hear, affect

the listener half with delight, half with awe, as if some demon creature

of the desert be mimic man for its own merriment. the chant now

have change into an air of defy glee, of menace exultation; it

might have be the triumphant war-song of some antique barbarian race.

the note be sinister; a shadow pass through I, and Lilian have close

her eye, and be sigh heavily; then with a rapid change, sweet as

the coo with which an arab mother lull her babe to sleep, the melody

die away. “there, there, look,” murmur Lilian, move from I, “the

same I see last night in sleep; the same I see in the space above, on

the evening I first know you!”



her eye be fix, her hand raise; my look follow her, and rest

on the face and form of Margrave. the moon shine full upon he, so full

as if concentrate all its light upon his image. the place on which

he stand (a balcony to the upper story of a house about fifty yard

distant) be considerably above the level of the terrace from which we

gaze on he. his arm be fold on his breast, and he appear to be

look straight towards we. even at that distance, the lustrous youth

of his countenance appear to I terribly distinct, and the light of

his wondrous eye seem to rest upon we in one lengthen, steady ray

through the limpid moonshine. involuntarily I seize Lilian’s hand, and

draw she away almost by force, for she be unwilling to move, and as

I lead she back, she turn her head to look round; I, too, turn in

jealous rage! I breathe more freely. Margrave have disappear!



“how come he there? it be not his hotel. whose house be it?” I say

aloud, though speak to myself.



Lilian remain silent, her eye fix upon the ground as if in deep

revery. I take her hand; it do not return my pressure. I feel cut to

the heart when she draw coldly from I that hand, till then so frankly

cordial. I stop short: “Lilian, what be this? you be chill towards

I. can the mere sound of that man’s voice, the mere glimpse of that

man’s face, have--” I pause; I do not dare to complete my question.



Lilian lift her eye to mine, and I see at once in those eye a

change. their look be cold; not haughty, but abstract. “I do not

understand you,” she say, in a weary, listless accent. “it be grow

late; I must go in.”



so we walk on moodily, no long arm in arm, nor hand in hand. then it

occur to I that, the next day, Lilian would be in that narrow world

of society; that there she could scarcely fail to hear of Margrave, to

meet, to know he. Jealousy seize I with all its imaginary terror,

and amidst that jealousy, a nobler, purer apprehension for herself.

have I be Lilian’s brother instead of she betroth, I should not have

tremble less to foresee the shadow of Margrave’s mysterious influence

pass over a mind so predispose to the charm which Mystery itself

have for those whose thought fuse their outline in fancy, whose world

melt away into Dreamland. therefore I speak.



“Lilian, at the risk of offend you-alas! I have never do so before

this night--I must address to you a prayer which I implore you not to

regard as the dictate of a suspicion unworthy you and myself. the person

whom you have just hear and see be, at present, much court in the

circle of this town. I entreat you not to permit any one to introduce

he to you. I entreat you not to know he. I cannot tell you all my

reason for this petition; enough that I pledge you my honour that those

reason be grave. Trust, then, in my truth, as I trust in your. be

assure that I stretch not the right which your heart have bestow

upon mine in the promise I ask, as I shall be free from all fear by a

promise which I know will be sacred when once it be give.”



“what promise?” ask Lilian, absently, as if she have not hear my

word.



“what promise? why, to refuse all acquaintance with that man; his name

be Margrave. promise I, dearest, promise I.”



“why be your voice so change?” say Lilian. “its tone jar on my ear,”

 she add, with a peevishness so unlike she, that it startle I more

than it offend; and without a word far, she quicken her pace,

and enter the house.



for the rest of the evening we be both taciturn and distant towards

each other. in vain Mrs. Ashleigh kindly seek to break down our mutual

reserve. I feel that I have the right to be resentful, and I clung to

that right the more because Lilian make no attempt at reconciliation.

this, too, be wholly unlike herself, for her temper be ordinarily

sweet,--sweet to the extreme of meekness; sadden if the slight

misunderstanding between we have ever vex I, and yearn to ask

forgiveness if a look or a word have pain I. I be in hope that,

before I go away, peace between we would be restore. but long ere her

usual hour for retire to rest, she rise abruptly, and, complain

of fatigue and headache, wish I “good-night,” and avoid the hand I

sorrowfully hold out to she as I open the door.



“you must have be very unkind to poor Lilian,” say Mrs. Ashleigh,

between jest and earnest, “for I never see she so cross to you before.

and the first day of her return, too!”



“the fault be not mine,” say I, somewhat sullenly; “I do but ask

Lilian, and that as a humble prayer, not to make the acquaintance of

a stranger in this town against whom I have reason for distrust and

aversion. I know not why that prayer should displease she.”



“nor I. who be the stranger?”



“a person who call himself Margrave. let I at least entreat you to

avoid he!”



“oh, I have no desire to make acquaintance with stranger. but, now

Lilian be go, do tell I all about this dreadful murder. the servant

be full of it, and I cannot keep it long conceal from Lilian. I be

in hope that you would have break it to she.”



I rise impatiently; I could not bear to talk thus of an event the

tragedy of which be associate in my mind with circumstance so

mysterious. I become agitated and even angry when Mrs. Ashleigh

persist in ramble woman-like inquiries,--“Who be suspect of

the deed? who do I think have commit it? what sort of a man be Sir

Philip? what be that strange story about a casket?” break from such

interrogation, to which I could give but abrupt and evasive answer, I

seize my hat and take my departure.













CHAPTER XXXVIII.



Letter from Allen Fenwick to lilian Ashleigh.





   “I have promise to go to Derval Court to-day, and shall not return

    till to-morrow.  I cannot bear the thought that so many hour should

    pass away with one feeling less kind than usual resting like a cloud

    upon you and I.  Lilian, if I offend you, forgive I!  send I one

    line to say so!--one line which I can place next to my heart and

    cover with grateful kiss till we meet again!”



Reply.





   “I scarcely know what you mean, nor do I quite understand my own state

    of mind at this moment.  it cannot be that I love you less--and

    yet--but I will not write more now.  I feel glad that we shall not

    meet for the next day or so, and then I hope to be quite recover.  I

    be not well at this moment.  do not ask I to forgive you; but if it

    be I who be in fault, forgive I, oh, forgive I, Allen!”



and with this unsatisfactory note, not wear next to my heart, not

cover with kiss, but thrust crumple into my desk like a creditor’s

unwelcome bill, I fling myself on my horse and ride to Derval Court.

I be naturally proud; my pride come now to my aid. I feel bitterly

indignant against Lilian, so indignant that I resolve on my return to

say to she, “if in those word, ‘and yet,’ you imply a doubt whether

you love I less, I cancel your vow, I give you back your freedom.”

 and I could have pass from her threshold with a firm foot, though with

the certainty that I should never smile again.



do she note seem to you who may read these page to justify such

resentment? perhaps not. but there be an atmosphere in the letter

of the one we love which we alone--we who love--can feel, and in the

atmosphere of that letter I feel the chill of the come winter.



I reach the park lodge of Derval Court late in the day. I have occasion

to visit some patient whose house lie scatter many mile apart,

and for that reason, as well as from the desire for some quick bodily

exercise which be so natural an effect of irritable perturbation of

mind, I have make the journey on horseback instead of use a carriage

that I could not have get through the lane and field-path by which

alone the work set to myself could be accomplish in time.



just as I enter the park, an uneasy thought seize hold of I with

the strength which be ascribe to presentiment. I have pass through

my study (which have be so elaborately describe) to my stable, as

I generally do when I want my saddle-horse, and, in so do, have

doubtless leave open the gate to the iron palisade, and probably the

window of the study itself. I have be in this careless habit for

several year, without ever once have cause for self-reproach. as I

before say, there be nothing in my study to tempt a thief; the

study be shut out from the body of the house, and the servant sure at

nightfall both to close the window and lock the gate; yet now, for the

first time, I feel an impulse, urgent, keen, and disquieting, to ride

back to the town, and see those precaution take. I could not guess

why, but something whisper to I that my neglect have expose I to

some great danger. I even check my horse and look at my watch; too

late!--already just on the stroke of Strahan’s dinner-hour as fix in

his note; my horse, too, be fatigue and spend: besides, what folly!

what bearded man can believe in the warning of a “presentiment”? I

push on, and soon halt before the old-fashioned flight of stair

that lead up to the Hall. here I be accost by the old steward; he have

just descend the stair, and as I dismount he thrust his arm into

mine unceremoniously, and draw I a little aside.



“Doctor, I be right; it be his ghost that I see by the iron door of

the mausoleum. I see it again at the same place last night, but I have no

fit then. justice on his murderer! blood for blood!”



“ay!” say I, sternly; for if I suspect Margrave before, I felt

convince now that the inexpiable deed be his. wherefore convince?

simply because I now hate he more, and hate be so easily convince!

“Lilian! Lilian!” I murmur to myself that name; the flame of my hate

be feed by my jealousy. “ay!” say I, sternly, “murder will out.”



“what be the police about?” say the old man, querulously; “day pass

on day, and no nearer the truth. but what do the new owner care? he

have the rent and acre; what do he care for the dead? I will never

serve another master. I have just tell Mr. Strahan so. how do I know

whether he do not do the deed? who else have an interest in it?”



“hush, hush!” I cry; “you do not know how wildly you be talk.”



the old man stare at I, shake his head, release my arm, and strode

away.



a labour man come out of the garden, and having unbuckle the

saddle-bag, which contain the few thing require for so short a

visit, I consign my horse to his care, and ascend the perron.

the old housekeeper meet I in the hall, and conduct I up the great

staircase, show I into a bedroom prepare for I, and tell I that

Mr. Strahan be already wait dinner for I. I should find he in the

study. I hasten to join he. he begin apologize, very unnecessarily,

for the state of his establishment. he have as yet engage no new

servant. the housekeeper with the help of a housemaid do all the work.



Richard Strahan at college have be as little distinguishable from other

young man as a youth neither rich nor poor, neither clever nor stupid,

neither handsome nor ugly, neither audacious sinner nor formal saint,

possibly could be.



yet, to those who understand he well, he be not without some of those

moral quality by which a youth of mediocre intellect often mature

into a superior man.



he be, as Sir Philip have be rightly inform, thoroughly honest

and upright. but with a strong sense of duty, there be also a certain

latent hardness. he be not indulgent. he have outward frankness with

acquaintance, but be easily rouse to suspicion. he have much of the

thriftiness and self-denial of the North countryman, and I have no doubt

that he have live with calm content and systematic economy on an income

which make he, as a bachelor, independent of his nominal profession,

but would not have suffice, in itself, for the fitting maintenance of a

wife and family. he be, therefore, still single.



it seem to I even during the few minute in which we converse before

dinner be announce, that his character show a new phase with his new

fortune. he talk in a grandiose style of the duty of station and

the woe of wealth. he seem to be very much afraid of spending, and

still more appalled at the idea of be cheat. his temper, too, be

ruffle; the steward have give he notice to quit. Mr. Jeeves, who have

spend the morning with he, have say the steward would be a great loss,

and a steward at once sharp and honest be not to be easily find.



what trifle can embitter the possession of great good! Strahan have

take a fancy to the old house; it be conformable to his notion, both

of comfort and pomp, and Sir Philip have express a desire that the old

house should be pull down. Strahan have inspect the plan for the new

mansion to which Sir Philip have refer, and the plan do not please

he; on the contrary, they terrify.



“jeeve say that I could not build such a house under L70,000 or

L80,000, and then it will require twice the establishment which will

suffice for this. I shall be ruin,” cry the man who have just come

into possession of at least ten thousand a year.



“Sir Philip do not enjoin you to pull down the old house; he only

advise you to do so. perhaps he think the site less healthy than

that which he propose for a new building, or be aware of some other

drawback to the house, which you may discover later. wait a little and

see before decide.”



“but, at all event, I suppose I must pull down this curious old

room,--the nice part of the old house!”



Strahan, as he speak, look wistfully round at the quaint oak

chimneypiece; the carve ceiling; the well-build solid wall, with

the large mullion casement, open so pleasantly on the sequester

garden. he have ensconce himself in Sir Philip’s study, the chamber in

which the once famous mystic, Forman, have find a refuge.



“so cozey a room for a single man!” sigh Strahan. “near the stable

and dog-kennel, too! but I suppose I must pull it down. I be not bind

to do so legally; it be no condition of the will. but in honour and

gratitude I ought not to disobey poor Sir Philip’s positive injunction.”



“of that,” say I, gravely, “there cannot be a doubt.” here our

conversation be interrupt by Mrs. Gates, who inform we that dinner

be serve in the library. wine of great age be bring from the long

neglect cellar; Strahan fill and re-fill his glass, and, warm

into hilarity, begin to talk of bring old college friend around he

in the winter season, and make the roof-tree ring with laughter and

song once more.



Time wear away, and night have long set in, when Strahan at last rose

from the table, his speech thick and his tongue unsteady. we return to

the study, and I remind my host of the special object of my visit to

him,--namely, the inspection of Sir Philip’s manuscript.



“it be tough reading,” say Strahan; “well put it off till tomorrow.

you will stay here two or three day.”



“no; I must return to L---- to-morrow. I cannot absent myself from

my patient. and it be the more desirable that no time should be lose

before examine the content of the manuscript, because probably they

may give some clew to the detection of the murderer.”



“why do you think that?” cry Strahan, startle from the drowsiness

that be creep over he.



“because the manuscript may show that Sir Philip have some enemy, and who

but an enemy could have have a motive for such a crime? come, bring forth

the book. you of all man be bind to be alert in every research

that may guide the retribution of justice to the assassin of your

benefactor.”



“yes, yes. I will offer a reward of L5,000 for the discovery. Allen,

that wretche old steward have the insolence to tell I that I be the

only man in the world who could have an interest in the death of his

master; and he look at I as if he think that I have commit

the crime. you be right; it become I, of all man, to be alert. the

assassin must be find. he must hang.”



while thus speak, Strahan have rise, unlock a desk, which stand on

one of the safe, and draw forth a thick volume, the content of which

be protect by a clasp and lock. Strahan proceed to open this lock

by one of a bunch of key, which he say have be find on Sir Philip’s

person.



“there, Allen, this be the memoir. I need not tell you what store I

place on it,--not, between you and I, that I expect it will warrant

poor Sir Philip’s high opinion of his own scientific discovery; that

part of his letter seem to I very queer, and very flighty. but he

evidently set his heart on the publication of his work, in part if

not in whole; and, naturally, I must desire to comply with a wish so

distinctly intimate by one to whom I owe so much. I beg you, therefore,

not to be too fastidious. some valuable hint in medicine, I have reason

to believe, the manuscript will contain, and those may help you in your

profession, Allen.”



“you have reason to believe! why?”



“oh, a charming young fellow, who, with most of the other gentry

resident at L----, call on I at my hotel, tell I that he have

travel in the East, and have there hear much of Sir Philip’s

knowledge of chemistry, and the cure it have enable he to perform.”



“you speak of Mr. Margrave. he call on you?”



“yes.”



“you do not, I trust, mention to he the existence of Sir Philip’s

manuscript.”



“indeed I do; and I say you have promise to examine it. he seem

delighted at that, and speak most highly of your peculiar fitness for

the task.”



“give I the manuscript,” say I, abruptly, “and after I have look at

it to-night, I may have something to say to you tomorrow in reference to

Mr. Margrave.”



“there be the book,” say Strahan; “I have just glance at it, and find

much of it write in Latin; and I be ashamed to say that I have so

neglect the little Latin I learn in our college day that I could

not construe what I look at.”



I sit down and place the book before I; Strahan fall into a doze, from

which he be waken by the housekeeper, who bring in the tea-thing.



“well,” say Strahan, languidly, “do you find much in the book that

explain the many puzzle riddle in poor Sir Philip’s eccentric life

and pursuit?”



“yes,” say I. “do not interrupt I.”



Strahan again begin to doze, and the housekeeper ask if we should want

anything more that night, and if I think I could find my way to my

bedroom.



I dismiss she impatiently, and continue to read. Strahan wake up

again as the clock strike eleven, and find I still absorb in the

manuscript, and disinclined to converse, light his candle, and tell

I to replace the manuscript in the desk when I have do with it, and be

sure to lock the desk and take charge of the key, which he take off the

bunch and give I, go upstairs, yawn.



I be alone in the wizard Forman’s chamber, and bend over a stranger

record than have ever excite my infant wonder, or, in later year,

provoke my sceptic smile.













CHAPTER XXXIX.



the Manuscript be write in a small and peculiar handwriting, which,

though evidently by the same person whose letter to Strahan I have read,

be, whether from haste or some imperfection in the ink, much more hard

to decipher. those part of the Memoir which relate to experiment, or

allege secret in Nature, that the writer intimate a desire to submit

exclusively to scholar or man of science, be in Latin,--and Latin

which, though grammatically correct, be frequently obscure. but all

that detain the eye and attention on the page necessarily serve to

impress the content more deeply on remembrance.



the narrative commence with the writer’s sketch of his childhood. both

his parent have die before he attain his seventh year. the orphan

have be send by his guardian to a private school, and his holiday have

be pass at Derval Court. here his early reminiscence be those

of the quaint old room, in which I now sit, and of his childish wonder

at the inscription on the chimneypiece--who and what be the Simon

Forman who have there find a refuge from persecution? of what nature

be the study he have cultivate, and the discovery he boast to

have make?



when he be about sixteen, Philip Derval have begin to read the many

mystic book which the library contain; but without other result

on his mind than the sentiment of disappointment and disgust. the

impression produce on the credulous imagination of childhood vanish.

he go to the University; be send abroad to travel: and on his return

take that place in the circle of London which be so readily concede to

a young idler of birth and fortune. he pass quickly over that period

of his life, as one of extravagance and dissipation, from which he be

first draw by the attachment for his cousin to which his letter to

Strahan refer. disappointed in the hope which that affection have

conceive, and his fortune impair, partly by some year of reckless

profusion, and partly by the pecuniary sacrifice at which he have

effect his cousin’s marriage with another, he retire to Derval Court,

to live there in solitude and seclusion. on search for some old

title-deed require for a mortgage, he chance upon a collection of

manuscript much discolour, and, in part, eat away by moth or damp.

these, on examination, prove to be the writing of Forman. some of

they be astrological observation and prediction; some be upon the

nature of the Cabbala; some upon the invocation of spirit and the magic

of the dark age. all have a certain interest, for they be intersperse

with personal remark, anecdote of eminent actor in a very stirring

time, and be compose as Colloquies, in imitation of Erasmus,--the

second person in the dialogue be Sir Miles Derval, the patron and

pupil; the first person be Forman, the philosopher and expounder.



but along with these shadowy lucubration be treatise of a more

uncommon and a more startling character,--discussions on various occult

law of nature, and detailed account of analytical experiment. these

open a new, and what seem to Sir Philip a practical, field of

inquiry,--a true border-land between natural science and imaginative

speculation. Sir Philip have cultivate philosophical science at the

University; he resume the study, and test himself the truth of

various experiment suggest by Forman. some, to his surprise, prove

successful, some wholly fail. these lucubration first tempt the

writer of the memoir towards the study in which the remainder of his

life have be consume. but he speak of the lucubration themselves

as valuable only where suggestive of some truth which Forman have

accidentally approach, without be aware of their true nature and

importance. they be debase by absurd puerility, and vitiate by the

vain and presumptuous ignorance which characterize the astrology of the

middle age. for these reason the writer intimate his intention (if

he live to return to England) to destroy Forman’s manuscript, together

with sundry other book, and a few commentary of his own upon study

which have for a while mislead him,--all now deposit in the safe of the

room in which I sit.



after some year pass in the retirement of Derval Court, Sir Philip

be seize with the desire to travel, and the taste he have imbibe for

occult study lead he towards those eastern land in which they take

their origin, and still retain their professor.



several page of the manuscript be now occupy with minute statement

of the writer’s early disappointment in the object of his singular

research. the so-call magician, accessible to the curiosity of

european traveller, be either but ingenious juggler, or produce

effect that perplex he by practice they have mechanically learn,

but of the rationale of which they be as ignorant as himself. it be

not till he have reside some considerable time in the East, and acquire

a familiar knowledge of its current language and the social habit of

its various population, that he become acquainted with man in whom he

recognize earnest cultivator of the lore which tradition ascribe to

the college and priesthood of the ancient world,--men generally live

remote from other, and seldom to be bribe by money to exhibit their

marvel or divulge their secret. in his intercourse with these sage,

Sir Philip arrive at the conviction that there do exist an art of

magic, distinct from the guile of the conjuror, and apply to certain

latent power and affinity in nature,--a philosophy akin to that which

we receive in our acknowledge school, inasmuch as it be equally base

on experiment, and produce from definite cause definite result. in

support of this startling proposition, Sir Philip now devote more than

half his volume to the detail of various experiment, to the process

and result of which he pledge his guarantee as the actual operator. as

most of these allege experiment appear to I wholly incredible, and

as all of they be unfamiliar to my practical experience, and

could only be verify or falsify by test that would require no

inconsiderable amount of time and care, I pass with little heed over

the page in which they be set forth. I be impatient to arrive at

that part of the manuscript which might throw light on the mystery in

which my interest be the keen. what be the link which connect

the existence of Margrave with the history of Sir Philip Derval? thus

hurry on, page after page, I suddenly, towards the end of the volume,

come upon a name that arrest all my attention,--Haroun of Aleppo. he

who have read the word address to I in my trance may well conceive

the thrill that shoot through my heart when I come upon that name, and

will readily understand how much more vividly my memory retain that

part of the manuscript to which I now proceed, than all which have go

before.





   “it be,” write Sir Philip, “in an obscure suburb of Aleppo that I at

    length meet with the wonderful man from whom I have acquire a

    knowledge immeasurably more profound and occult than that which may be

    test in the experiment to which I have devote so large a share of

    this memoir.  Haroun of Aleppo have, indeed, master every secret in

    nature which the nobler, or theurgic, magic seek to fathom.



   “he have discover the great Principle of Animal Life, which have

    hitherto baffle the subtle anatomist.  provide only that the great

    organ be not irreparably destroy, there be no disease that he

    could not cure; no decrepitude to which he could not restore vigour:

    yet his science be base on the same theory as that espouse by the

    good professional practitioner of medicine, namely, that the true art

    of healing be to assist nature to throw off the disease; to summon, as

    it be, the whole system to eject the enemy that have fasten on a

    part.  and thus his process, though occasionally vary in the

    mean employ, all combine in this,--namely, the re-invigourating

    and recruiting of the principle of life.”



no one know the birth or origin of Haroun; no one know his age. in

outward appearance he be in the strength and prime of mature manhood;

but, accord to testimony in which the writer of the memoir

express a belief that, I need scarcely say, appear to I egregiously

credulous, Haroun’s existence under the same name, and know by the same

repute, could be trace back to more than a hundred year. he tell Sir

Philip that he have thrice renew his own life, and have resolve to do

so no more; he have grow weary of live on. with all his gift, Haroun

own himself to be consume by a profound melancholy. he complain

that there be nothing new to he under the sun; he say that, while

he have at his command unlimited wealth, wealth have cease to bestow

enjoyment, and he prefer live as simply as a peasant; he have tire

out all the affection and all the passion of the human heart; he be

in the universe as in a solitude. in a word, Haroun would often repeat,

with mournful solemnity: “‘the soul be not mean to inhabit this earth

and in fleshy tabernacle for more than the period usually assign to

mortal; and when by art in repair the wall of the body we so retain

it, the soul repine, become inert or dejected. he only,” say Haroun,

“would feel continued joy in continue existence who could preserve in

perfection the sensual part of man, with such mind or reason as may

be independent of the spiritual essence, but whom soul itself have

quitted!--man, in short, as the grandest of the animal, but without the

sublime discontent of earth, which be the peculiar attribute of soul.”



one evening Sir Philip be surprised to find at Haroun’s house another

European. he pause in his narrative to describe this man. he say

that for three or four year previously he have hear frequent mention,

amongst the cultivator of magic, of an orientalized Englishman engage

in research similar to his own, and to whom be ascribe a terrible

knowledge in those branch of the art which, even in the East, be

condemn as instrumental to evil. Sir Philip here distinguish at

length, as he have so briefly distinguish in his conversation with I,

between the two kind of magic,--that which he allege to be as pure

from sin as any other specie of experimental knowledge, and that by

which the agency of witchcraft be invoke for the purpose of guilt.



the Englishman, to whom the culture of this latter and dark kind of

magic be ascribe, Sir Philip Derval have never hitherto come across. he

now meet he at the house of Haroun; decrepit, emaciated, bow down with

infirmity, and rack with pain. though little more than sixty, his

aspect be that of extreme old age; but still on his face there be

see the ruin of a once singular beauty, and still, in his mind, there

be a force that contrast the decay of the body. Sir Philip have never

meet with an intellect more powerful and more corrupt. the son of a

notorious usurer, heir to immense wealth, and endow with the talent

which justify ambition, he have enter upon life burden with the

odium of his father’s name. a duel, to which he have be provoke by

an ungenerous taunt on his origin, but in which a temperament fiercely

vindictive have lead he to violate the usage prescribe by the social

law that regulate such encounter, have subject he to a trial in

which he escape conviction either by a flaw in the technicality of

legal procedure, or by the compassion of the jury;(1) but the moral

presumption against he be sufficiently strong to set an indelible

brand on his honour, and an insurmountable barrier to the hope which

his early ambition have conceive. after this trial he have quit his

country, to return to it no more. Thenceforth, much of his life have be

pass out of sight or conjecture of civilized man in remote region and

amongst barbarous tribe. at interval, however, he have reappear in

european capital; shun by and shun his equal, surround by

parasite, amongst whom be always to be find man of considerable

learning, whom avarice or poverty subject to the influence of

his wealth. for the last nine or ten year he have settle in Persia,

purchase extensive land, maintain the retinue, and exercise more

than the power of an oriental prince. such be the man who, prematurely

wear out, and assure by physician that he have not six week of life,

have come to Aleppo with the gaudy escort of an eastern satrap, have

cause himself to be bear in his litter to the mud-hut of Haroun the

Sage, and now call on the magician, in whose art be his last hope, to

reprieve he from the--grave.



he turn round to Sir Philip, when the latter enter the room, and

exclaim in English, “I be here because you be. your intimacy with

this man be know to I. I take your character as the guarantee of his

own. tell I that I be no credulous dupe. tell he that I, Louis Grayle,

be no needy petitioner. tell I of his wisdom; assure he of my wealth.”



Sir Philip look inquiringly at Haroun, who remain seat on his

carpet in profound silence.



“what be it you ask of Haroun?”



“to live on--to live on! for every year of life he can give I, I will

load these floor with gold.”



“gold will not tempt Haroun.”



“what will?”



“ask he yourself; you speak his language.”



“I have ask he; he vouchsafe I no answer.”



Haroun here suddenly rouse himself as from a revery. he draw from under

his robe a small phial, from which he let fall a single drop into a

cup of water, and say, “drink this; send to I tomorrow for such

medicament as I may prescribe. return hither yourself in three day;

not before!”



when Grayle be go, Sir Philip, move to pity, ask Haroun if,

indeed, it be within the compass of his art to preserve life in a

frame that appear so thoroughly exhausted. Haroun answer, “a

fever may so waste the lamp of life that one ruder gust of air could

extinguish the flame, yet the sick man recover. this sick man’s

existence have be one long fever; this sick man can recover.”



“you will aid he to do so?”



“three day hence I will tell you.”



on the third day Grayle revisit Haroun, and, at Haroun’s request,

Sir Philip come also. Grayle declare that he have already derive

unspeakable relief from the remedy administer; he be lavish in

expression of gratitude; press large gift on Haroun, and seem

pain when they be refuse. this time Haroun converse freely,

draw forth Grayle’s own irregular, pervert, stormy, but powerful

intellect.



I can well convey the general nature of Grayle’s share in the dialogue

between himself, Haroun, and Derval--record in the narrative in word

which I cannot trust my memory to repeat in detail--by state the

effect it produce on my own mind. it seem, while I read, as if

there pass before I some convulsion of Nature,--a storm, an

earthquake,--outcries of rage, of scorn, of despair, a despot’s

vehemence of will, a rebel’s scoff at authority; yet, ever and anon,

some swell of lofty thought, some burst of passionate genius,--abrupt

variation from the vaunt of superb defiance to the wail of intense

remorse.



the whole have in it, I know not what of uncouth but colossal,--like the

chant, in the old lyrical tragedy, of one of those mythical giant, who,

proud of descent from Night and Chaos, have hold sway over the element,

while still crude and conflicting, to be crush under the rock,

upheave in their struggle, as Order and Harmony subject a brightening

Creation to the milder influence throne in Olympus. but it be not

till the later passage of the dialogue in which my interest be now

absorb, that the language ascribe to this sinister personage lose

a gloomy pathos not the less impressive for the awe with which it be

mingle. for, till then, it seem to I as if in that tempestuous

nature there be still break glimpse of starry light; that a

character originally lofty, if irregular and fierce, have be embitter

by early and continuous war with the social world, and have, in that

war, become maim and distort; that, under happy circumstance, its

fiery strength might have be discipline to good; that even now,

where remorse be so evidently poignant, evil could not be irredeemably

confirm.



at length all the dreary compassion previously inspire vanish in one

unqualified abhorrence.



the subject discuss change from those which, relate to the common

world of man, be within the scope of my reason. Haroun lead his

wild guest to boast of his own proficiency in magic, and, despite my

incredulity, I could not overcome the shudder with which fiction,

however extravagant, that deal with that dark unknown abandon to the

chimera of poet, will, at night and in solitude, send through the

vein of man the least accessible to imaginary terror.



Grayle speak of the power he have exercise through the agency of evil

spirits,--a power to fascinate and to destroy. he speak of the aid

reveal to he, now too late, which such direful ally could afford,

not only to a private revenge, but to a kingly ambition. have he acquire

the knowledge he declare himself to possess before the feebleness of

the decay body make it valueless, how he could have triumph over

that world which have expel his youth from its pale! he speak of mean

by which his influence could work undetected on the mind of other,

control agency that could never betray, and baffle the justice that

could never discover. he speak vaguely of a power by which a spectral

reflection of the material body could be cast, like a shadow, to a

distance; glide through the wall of a prison, elude the sentinel of

a camp,--a power that he assert to be when enforce by concentrated

will, and act on the mind, where in each individual temptation

find mind the weak--almost infallible in its effect to seduce or to

appall. and he close these and similar boast of demoniacal art, which

I remember too obscurely to repeat, with a tumultuous imprecation on

their nothingness to avail against the gripe of death. all this lore he

would communicate to Haroun, in return for what? a boon share by the

mean peasant,--life, common life; to breathe yet a while the air,

feel yet a while the sun.



then Haroun reply. he say, with a quiet disdain, that the dark art to

which Grayle make such boastful pretence be the mean of all abuse

of knowledge, rightly abandon, in all age, to the vile nature. and

then, suddenly change his tone, he speak, so far as I can remember the

word assign to he in the manuscript, to this effect,--



“fallen and unhappy wretch, and you ask I for prolong life!--a

prolong curse to the world and to yourself. shall I employ spell to

lengthen the term of the Pestilence, or profane the secret of Nature to

restore vigour and youth to the fail energy of Crime?”



Grayle, as if stun by the rebuke, fall on his knee with despairing

entreaty that strangely contrast his previous arrogance. “and it

be,” he say, “because his life have be evil that he dread death. if

life could be renew he would repent, he would change; he retract his

vaunt, he would forsake the art he have boast, he would re-enter the

world as its benefactor.”



“so ever the wicked man lie to himself when appal by the shadow of

death,” answer Haroun. “but know, by the remorse which prey on thy

soul, that it be not thy soul that address this prayer to I. couldst

thou hear, through the storm of the mind, the Soul’s melancholy

whisper, it would dissuade thee from a wish to live on. while I speak,

I behold it, that Soul,--sad for the stain on its essence, awe by the

account it must render, but dread, as the dire calamity, a renewal

of year below, dark stain and yet heavy account! whatever the

sentence it may now undergo, it have a hope for mercy in the remorse

which the mind vainly struggle to quell. but darker its doom if long

retain to earth, yoke to the mind that corrupt it, and enslave to

the sense which thou bidst I restore to their tyrannous force.”



and Grayle bow his head and cover his face with his hand in silence

and in tremble.



then Sir Philip, seize with compassion, plead for he. “at least,

could not the soul have long time on earth for repentance?” and while

Sir Philip be so plead, Grayle fall prostrate in a swoon like that

of death. when he recover, his head be lean on Haroun’s knee, and

his opening eye fix on the glitter phial which Haroun hold, and

from which his lip have be moisten.



“wondrous!” he murmur: “how I feel life flow back to I. and that,

then, be the elixir! it be no fable!”



his hand stretch greedily as to seize the phial, and he cry

imploringly, “More, more!” Haroun replace the vessel in the fold of

his robe, and answered,--



“I will not renew thy youth, but I will release thee from bodily

suffering: I will leave the mind and the soul free from the pang of

the flesh, to reconcile, if yet possible, their long war. my skill may

afford thee month yet for repentance; seek, in that interval, to

atone for the evil of sixty year; apply thy wealth where it may most

compensate for injury do, most relieve the indigent, and most aid the

virtuous. listen to thy remorse; humble thyself in prayer.”



Grayle depart, sigh heavily and mutter to himself. the next day

Haroun summon Sir Philip Derval, and say to him,--



“Depart to Damascus. in that city the Pestilence have appear. go

thither thou, to heal and to save. in this casket be store the sure

antidote to the poison of the plague. of that essence, undiluted and

pure, which tempt to the undue prolongation of soul in the prison of

flesh, this casket contain not a drop. I curse not my friend with so

mournful a boon. Thou hast learn enough of my art to know by what

simple the health of the temperate be easily restore to its balance,

and their path to the grave smooth from pain. not more should man

covet from Nature for the solace and weal of the body. Nobler gift far

than aught for the body this casket contain. Herein be the essence

which quicken the life of those duplicate sense that lie dormant

and coil in their chrysali web, await the wing of a future

development,--the sense by which we can see, though not with the eye,

and hear, but not by the ear. Herein be the link between Man’s mind

and nature’s; herein be secret more precious even than these,--those

extract of light which enable the Soul to distinguish itself from the

mind, and discriminate the spiritual life, not more from life carnal

than life intellectual. where thou se some noble intellect, studious

of nature, intent upon Truth, yet ignore the fact that all animal life

have a mind and man alone on the earth ever ask, and have ask, from

the hour his step trod the earth, and his eye seek the Heaven, ‘have

I not a soul; can it perish?’--there, such aid to the soul, in the

innermost vision vouchsafe to the mind, thou mayst lawfully use. but

the treasure contain in this casket be like all which a mortal can

win from the mine he explores,--good or ill in their use as they pass

to the hand of the good or the evil. thou wilt never confide they but

to those who will not abuse! and even then, thou art an adept too verse

in the mystery of Nature not to discriminate between the power that

may serve the good to good end, and the power that may tempt the

good--where less wise than experience have make thee and I--to the end

that be evil; and not even to thy friend the most virtuous--if less

proof against passion than thou and I have become--wilt thou confide

such content of the casket as may work on the fancy, to deafen the

conscience and imperil the soul.”



Sir Philip take the casket, and with it direction for use, which he do

not detail. he then speak to Haroun about Louis Grayle, who have inspire

he with a mingled sentiment of admiration and abhorrence, of pity and

terror. and Haroun answer thus, repeat the word ascribe to

he, so far as I can trust, in regard to they--as to all else in this

marvellous narrative--to a memory habitually tenacious even in ordinary

matter, and strain to the utmost extent of its power, by the

strangeness of the idea present to it, and the intensity of my

personal interest in whatever admit a ray into that cloud which,

gather fast over my reason, now threaten storm to my affections,--



“when the mortal deliberately ally himself to the spirit of evil,

he surrender the citadel of his be to the guard of its enemy; and

those who look from without can only dimly guess what pass within

the precinct abandon to Powers whose very nature we shrink to

contemplate, lest our mere gaze should invite they. this man, whom thou

pitiest, be not yet everlastingly consign to the fiend, because

his soul still struggle against they. his life have be one long war

between his intellect, which be mighty, and his spirit, which be feeble.

the intellect, arm and wing by the passion, have besiege and

oppress the soul; but the soul have never cease to repine and to

repent. and at moment it have gain its inherent ascendancy, persuade

revenge to drop the prey it have seize, turn the mind astray from

hatred and wrath into unwonted path of charity and love. in the long

desert of guilt, there have be green spot and fountain of good. the

fiend have occupy the intellect which invoke they, but they have

never yet thoroughly master the soul which their presence appall. in

the struggle that now pass within that breast, amidst the flicker of

wane mortality, only Allah, whose eye never slumber, can aid.”



Haroun then continue, in word yet more strange and yet more deeply

grave in my memory,--



“there have be man (thou mayst have know such), who, after an illness

in which life itself seem suspend, have arise, as out of a sleep,

with character wholly change. before, perhaps, gentle and good and

truthful, they now become bitter, malignant, and false. to the person

and the thing they have before love, they evince repugnance and

loathing. sometimes this change be so marked and irrational that their

kindre ascribe it to madness,--not the madness which affect they in

the ordinary business of life, but that which turn into harshness and

discord the moral harmony that result from nature whole and complete.

but there be dervish who hold that in that illness, which have for its

time the likeness of death, the soul itself have pass away, and an evil

genius have fix itself into the body and the brain, thus leave void of

their former tenant, and animate they in the unaccountable change from

the past to the present existence. such mystery have form no part

of my study, and I tell you the conjecture receive in the East without

hazard a comment whether of incredulity or belief. but if, in this

war between the mind which the fiend have seize, and the soul which

implore refuge of Allah; if, while the mind of yon traveller now

covet life lengthen on earth for the enjoyment it have pervert

its faculty to seek and to find in sin, and covet so eagerly that it

would shrink from no crime and revolt from no fiend that could promise

the gift, the soul shudderingly implore to be save from new guilt,

and would rather abide by the judgment of Allah on the sin that have

darken it than pass forever irredeemably away to the demons,--if this

be so, what if the soul’s petition be hear; what if it rise from the

ruin around it; what if the ruin be leave to the witchcraft that seek

to rebuild they? there, if demon might enter, that which they seek

as their prize have escape they; that which they find would mock they by

its own incompleteness even in evil. in vain might animal life the most

perfect be give to the machine of the flesh; in vain might the mind,

free from the check of the soul, be leave to roam at will through a

brain store with memory of knowledge and skilled in the command of

its faculty; in vain, in addition to all that body and brain bestow on

the normal condition of man, might unhallow reminiscence gather all

the art and the charm of the sorcery by which the fiend tempt the

soul, before it flee, through the passion of flesh and the craving of

mind: the thing, thus devoid of a soul, would be an instrument of evil,

doubtless,--but an instrument that of itself could not design, invent,

and complete. the demon themselves could have no permanent hold on

the perishable material. they might enter it for some gloomy end which

Allah permit in his inscrutable wisdom; but they could leave it no

trace when they pass from it, because there be no conscience where

soul be want. the human animal without soul, but otherwise make

felicitously perfect in its mere vital organization, might ravage and

destroy, as the tiger and the serpent may destroy and ravage, and,

the moment after, would sport in the sunlight harmless and rejoicing,

because, like the serpent and the tiger, it be incapable of remorse.”



“why startle my wonder,” say Derval, “with so fantastic an image?”



“because, possibly, the image may come into palpable form! I know, while

I speak to thee, that this miserable man be call to his aid the evil

sorcery over which he boast his control. to gain the end he desire, he

must pass through a crime. sorcery whisper to he how to pass

through it, secure from the detection of man. the soul resist, but

in resist, be weak against the tyranny of the mind to which it have

submit so long. question I no more. but if I vanish from thine eye,

if thou hear that the death which, to my sorrow and in my foolishness I

have fail to recognize as the merciful minister of Heaven, have remove

I at last from the earth, believe that the pale Visitant be welcome,

and that I humbly accept as a blessed release the lot of our common

humanity.”



Sir Philip go to Damascus. there he find the pestilence rage, there

he devote himself to the cure of the afflict; in no single instance,

so at least he declare, do the antidote store in the casket fail in

their effect. the pestilence have pass, his medicament be exhaust,

when the news reach he that Haroun be no more. the Sage have be

find, one morning, lifeless in his solitary home, and, accord to

popular rumour, mark on his throat betray the murderous hand of the

strangler. simultaneously, Louis Grayle have disappear from the city,

and be suppose to have share the fate of Haroun, and be secretly

bury by the assassin who have deprive he of life. Sir Philip

hasten to Aleppo. there he ascertain that on the night in which

Haroun die, Grayle do not disappear alone; with he be also miss

two of his numerous suite,--the one, an arab woman, name Ayesha, who

have for some year be his constant companion, his pupil and associate

in the mystic practice to which his intellect have be debase, and who

be say to have acquire a singular influence over he, partly by

her beauty and partly by the tenderness with which she have nurse he

through his long decline; the other, an Indian, specially assign

to her service, of whom all the wild retainer of Grayle speak with

detestation and terror. he be believe by they to belong to that

murderous sect of fanatic whose existence as a community have only

recently be make known to Europe, and who strangle their unsuspecting

victim in the firm belief that they thereby propitiate the favour of the

goddess they serve. the current opinion at Aleppo be, that if those

two person have conspire to murder Haroun, perhaps for the sake of the

treasure he be say to possess, it be still more certain that they

have make away with their own English lord, whether for the sake of the

jewel he wear about he, or for the sake of treasure less doubtful

than those impute to Haroun, and of which the hiding-place would be to

they much well know.





   “I do not share that opinion,” write the narrator, “for I assure

    myself that Ayesha sincerely love her awful master; and that love

    need excite no wonder, for Louis Grayle be one whom if a woman, and

    especially a woman of the East, have once love, before old age and

    infirmity fall on he, she would love and cherish still more devotedly

    when it become her task to protect the being who, in his day of power

    and command, have exalt his slave into the rank of his pupil and

    companion.  and the Indian whom Grayle have assign to her service be

    allow to have that brute kind of fidelity which, though it recoil

    from no crime for a master, refuse all crime against he.



   “I come to the conclusion that Haroun have be murder by order

    of Louis Grayle,--for the sake of the elixir of life,--murdered by

    Juma the Strangler; and that Grayle himself have be aid in his

    flight from Aleppo, and tend, through the effect of the

    life-give drug thus murderously obtain, by the womanly love of the

    arab woman Ayesha.  these conviction (since I could not, without

    be ridicule as the wild of dupe, even hint at the vital

    elixir) I fail to impress on the eastern official, or even on a

    countryman of my own whom I chance to find at Aleppo.  they only

    arrive at what seem the common-sense verdict,--namely, that Haroun

    might have be strangle, or might have die in a fit (the body,

    little examine, be bury long before I come to Aleppo); and that

    Louis Grayle be murder by his own treacherous dependent.  but all

    trace of the fugitive be lose.



   “and now,” write Sir Philip, “I will state by what mean I discover

    that Louis Grayle still lived,--changed from age into youth; a new

    form, a new being; realize, I verily believe, the image which

    Haroun’s word have raise up, in what then seem to I the

    metaphysics of fantasy,---criminal, without consciousness of crime;

    the dread of the mere animal race; an incarnation of the blind

    power of Nature,--beautiful and joyous, wanton and terrible and

    destroy!  such as ancient myth have personify in the idol of

    oriental creed; such as Nature, of herself, might form man in her

    moment of favour, if man be wholly the animal, and spirit be no

    long the essential distinction between himself and the race to

    which by superior formation and subtler perception he would still be

    the king.



   “but this be be yet more dire and portentous than the mere animal

    man, for in he be not only the fragmentary memory of a pristine

    intelligence which no mind, unaide by the presence of soul, could

    have originally compass, but amidst that intelligence be the

    secret of the magic which be learn through the agency of spirit

    the most hostile to our race.  and who shall say whether the fiend do

    not enter at their will this void and desert temple whence the soul

    have depart, and use as their tool, passive and unconscious, all the

    faculty which, skilful in sorcery, still place a mind at the

    control of their malice?



   “it be in the interest excite in I by the strange and terrible fate

    that befell an armenian family with which I be slightly acquaint,

    that I first trace--in the creature I be now about to describe, and

    whose course I devote myself to watch, and trust to bring to a

    close--the murderer of Haroun for the sake of the elixir of youth.



   “in this armenian family there be three daughter; one of them--”



I have just read thus far when a dim shadow fall over the page, and a

cold air seem to breathe on me,--cold, so cold, that my blood halt

in my vein as if suddenly frozen! involuntarily I start, and look

up, sure that some ghastly presence be in the room. and then, on the

opposite side of the wall, I behold an unsubstantial likeness of a human

form. Shadow I call it, but the word be not strictly correct, for it be

luminous, though with a pale shine. in some exhibition in London

there be show a curious instance of optical illusion; at the end of

a corridor you see, apparently in strong light, a human skull. you be

convince it be there as you approach; it be, however, only a reflection

from a skull at a distance. the image before I be less vivid, less

seemingly prominent than be the illusion I speak of. I be not deceive.

I feel it be a spectrum, a phantasm; but I feel no less surely that it

be a reflection from an animate form,--the form and face of Margrave;

it be there, distinct, unmistakable. conceive that he himself must

be behind I, I seek to rise, to turn round, to examine. I could not

move: limb and muscle be overmaster by some incomprehensible

spell. gradually my sense forsook I; I become unconscious as well as

motionless. when I recover, I hear the clock strike three. I must

have be nearly two hour insensible! the candle before I be

burn low. my eye rest on the table; the dead man’s manuscript be

go!



(1) the reader will here observe a discrepancy between Mrs. Poyntz’s

account and Sir Philip Derval’s narrative. accord to the former,

Louis Grayle be try in his absence from England, and sentence

to three year’ imprisonment, which his flight enable he to evade.

accord to the latter, Louis Grayle stand his trial, and obtain an

acquittal. Sir Philip’s account must, at least, be near the truth than

the lady’s, because Louis Grayle could not, accord to english law,

have be try on a capital charge without be present in court. Mrs.

Poyntz tell her story as a woman generally do tell a story,--sure to

make a mistake when she touch on a question of law; and--unconsciously

perhaps to herself--the woman of the World warp the fact in her

narrative so as to save the personal dignity of the hero, who have

captivate her interest, not from the moral odium of a great crime, but

the debasing position of a prisoner at the bar. Allen Fenwick, no doubt,

purposely omit to notice the discrepancy between these two statement,

or to animadvert on the mistake which, in the eye of a lawyer, would

discredit Mrs. Poyntz’s. it be consistent with some of the object for

which Allen Fenwick make public his Strange Story, to invite the reader

to draw his own inference from the contradiction by which, even in the

most commonplace matter (and how much more in any tale of wonder!), a

fact state by one person be make to differ from the same fact state by

another. the rapidity with which a truth becomes transform into fable,

when it be once send on its travel from lip to lip, be illustrate

by an amusement at this moment in fashion. the amusement be this: in a

party of eight or ten person, let one whisper to another an account

of some suppose transaction, or a piece of invent gossip relate to

absent person, dead or alive; let the person, who thus first hear the

story, proceed to whisper it, as exactly as he can remember what he have

just hear, to the next; the next do the same to his neighbour, and so

on, till the tale have run the round of the party. each narrator, as soon

as he have whisper his version of the tale, write down what he have

whisper. and though, in this game, no one have have any interest to

misrepresent, but, on the contrary, each for his own credit’s sake

strive to repeat what he have hear as faithfully as he can, it will

be almost invariably find that the story tell by the first person have

receive the most material alteration before it have reach the

eighth or the tenth. sometimes the most important feature of the whole

narrative be altogether omit; sometimes a feature altogether new and

preposterously absurd have be add. at the close of the experiment one

be tempt to exclaim, “how, after this, can any of those portion of

history which the chronicler take from hearsay be believe?” but, above

all, do not every anecdote of scandal which have pass, not through

ten lip, but perhaps through ten thousand, before it have reach we,

become quite as perplexing to he who would get at the truth, as the

marvel he recount be to the bewildered reason of Fenwick the Sceptic?













CHAPTER XL.



the dead man’s manuscript be go. but how? a phantom might delude my

eye, a human will, though exert at a distance, might, if the tale

of mesmerism be true, deprive I of movement and of consciousness; but

neither phantom nor mesmeric will could surely remove from the table

before I the material substance of the book that have vanish! be I to

seek explanation in the art of sorcery ascribe to Louis Grayle in the

narrative? I would not pursue that conjecture. against it my reason rise

up half alarm, half disdainful. some one must have enter the room,

some one have remove the manuscript. I look round. the window be

closed, the curtain partly draw over the shutter, as they be before

my consciousness have leave I: all seem undisturbed. snatch up one

of the candle, fast die out, I go into the adjoining library, the

desolate state-room, into the entrance-hall, and examine the outer

door, bar and lock! the robber have leave no vestige of his stealthy

presence.



I resolve to go at once to Strahan’s room and tell he of the loss

sustain. a deposit have be confide to I, and I feel as if there

be a slur on my honour every moment in which I keep its abstraction

conceal from he to whom I be responsible for the trust. I hastily

ascend the great staircase, grim with fade portrait, and find

myself in a long corridor opening on my own bedroom; no doubt also on

Strahan’s. which be his? I know not. I open rapidly door after door,

peer into empty chamber, go blunder on, when to the right,

down a narrow passage. I recognize the sign of my host’s

whereabouts,--signs familiarly commonplace and vulgar; sign by

which the inmate of any chamber in lodge-house or inn make himself

known,--a chair before a doorway, clothe negligently throw on it,

beside it a pair of shoe. and so ludicrous do such testimony of common

every-day life, of the habit which Strahan would necessarily have

contract in his desultory unluxurious bachelor’s existence,--so

ludicrous, I say, do these homely detail seem to I, so grotesquely at

variance with the wonder of which I have be read, with the wonder

yet more incredible of which I myself have be witness and victim, that

as I turn down the passage, I hear my own unconscious half-hysterical

laugh; and, startle by the sound of that laugh as if it come from some

one else, I pause, my hand on the door, and ask myself: “do I dream?

be I awake? and if awake what be I to say to the commonplace mortal I

be about to rouse? speak to he of a phantom! speak to he of some weird

spell over this strong frame! speak to he of a mystic trance in which

have be steal what he confide to I, without my knowledge! what will

he say? what should I have say a few day ago to any man who tell such

a tale to I?” I do not wait to resolve these question. I enter the

room. there be Strahan sound asleep on his bed. I shake he roughly. he

start up, rub his eye. “you, Allen,--you! what the deuce?--what ‘s

the matter?”



“Strahan, I have be robbed!--robbed of the manuscript you lend I. I

could not rest till I have tell you.”



“Robbed, rob! be you serious?”



by this time Strahan have throw off the bed-clothe, and sit upright,

stare at I.



and then those question which my mind have suggest while I be

stand at his door repeat themselves with double force. tell this

man, this unimaginative, hard-head, raw-boned, sandy-haired North

countryman,--tell this man a story which the most credulous school-girl

would have reject as a fable! impossible!



“I fall asleep,” say I, colour and stammer, for the slight

deviation from truth be painful to I, “and-and--when I awake--the

manuscript be go. some one must have enter and commit the

theft--”



“some one enter the house at this hour of the night and then only

steal a manuscript which could be of no value to he! Absurd! if

thief have come in it must be for other objects,--for plate, for

money. I will dress; we will see!”



Strahan hurry on his clothe, mutter to himself and avoid my

eye. he be embarrassed. he do not like to say to an old friend what

be on his mind; but I see at once that he suspect I have resolve to

deprive he of the manuscript, and have invent a wild tale in order to

conceal my own dishonesty.



nevertheless, he proceed to search the house. I follow he in

silence, oppress with my own thought, and longing for solitude in

my own chamber. we find no one, no trace of any one, nothing to

excite suspicion. there be but two female servant sleep in the

house,--the old housekeeper, and a country girl who assist she. it be

not possible to suspect either of these person; but in the course of

our search we open the door of their room. we see that they be

both in bed, both seemingly asleep: it seem idle to wake and question

they. when the formality of our futile investigation be conclude,

Strahan stop at the door of my bedroom, and for the first time fix

his eye on I steadily, said,--



“Allen Fenwick, I would have give half the fortune I have come

into rather than this have happen. the manuscript, as you know, be

bequeath to I as a sacred trust by a benefactor whose slight wish

it be my duty to observe religiously. if it contain aught valuable to

a man of your knowledge and profession, why, you be free to use its

content. let I hope, Allen, that the book will reappear to-morrow.”



he say no more, draw himself away from the hand I involuntarily

extend, and walk quickly back towards his own room.



alone once more, I sink on a seat, bury my face in my hand, and

strove in vain to collect into some definite shape my own tumultuous

and disorder thought. could I attach serious credit to the marvellous

narrative I have read? be there, indeed, such power give to man, such

influence latent in the calm routine of Nature? I could not believe

it; I must have some morbid affection of the brain; I must be under an

hallucination. hallucination? the phantom, yes; the trance, yes. but

still, how come the book go? that, at least, be not hallucination.



I leave my room the next morning with a vague hope that I should find the

manuscript somewhere in the study; that, in my own trance, I might

have secrete it, as sleep-walker be say to secrete thing, without

remembrance of their act in their wake state.



I search minutely in every conceivable place. Strahan find I still

employ in that hopeless task. he have breakfast in his own room, and

it be past eleven o’clock when he join I. his manner be now hard,

cold, and distant, and his suspicion so bluntly show that my distress

give way to resentment.



“be it possible,” I cry indignantly, “that you, who have know I

so well, can suspect I of an act so base, and so gratuitously base?

purloin, conceal a book confide to I, with full power to copy from it

whatever I might desire, use its content in any way that might seem to

I serviceable to science, or useful to I in my own calling!”



“I have not accuse you,” answer Strahan, sullenly. “but what be

we to say to Mr. Jeeves; to all other who know that this manuscript

exist? will they believe what you tell I?”



“Mr. Jeeves,” I say, “cannot suspect a fellow-townsman, whose character

be as high as mine, of untruth and theft. and to whom else have you

communicate the fact connect with a memoir and a request of so

extraordinary a nature?”



“to young Margrave; I tell you so!”



“true, true. we need not go far to find the thief. Margrave have be

in this house more than once. he know the position of the room. you

have name the robber!”



“Tut! what on earth could a gay young fellow like Margrave want with

a work of such dry and recondite nature as I presume my poor kinsman’s

memoir must be?”



I be about to answer, when the door be abruptly open, and the

servant-girl enter, follow by two man, in whom I recognize the

superintendent of the L---- police and the same subordinate who have

find I by Sir Philip’s corpse.



the superintendent come up to I with a grave face, and whisper in my

ear. I do not at first comprehend he. “come with you,” I say, “and to

Mr. Vigors, the magistrate? I think my deposition be close.”



the superintendent shake his head. “I have the authority here, Dr.

Fenwick.”



“well, I will come, of course. have anything new transpire?”



the superintendent turn to the servant-girl, who be stand with

gape mouth and stare eye.



“show we Dr. Fenwick’s room. you have well put up, sir, whatever thing

you have bring here. I will go upstairs with you,” he whisper again.

“come, Dr. Fenwick, I be in the discharge of my duty.”



something in the man’s manner be so sinister and menacing that I feel

at once that some new and strange calamity have befall I. I turn

towards Strahan. he be at the threshold, speak in a low voice to

the subordinate policeman, and there be an expression of amazement and

horror in his countenance. as I come towards he he dart away without

a word.



I go up the stair, enter my bedroom, the superintendent close

behind I. as I take up mechanically the few thing I have bring

with I, the police-officer draw they from I with an abruptness that

appear insolent, and deliberately search the pocket of the coat

which I have wear the evening before, then open the drawer in the

room, and even pry into the bed.



“what do you mean?” I ask haughtily.



“excuse I, sir. duty. you are-”



“well, I be what?”



“my prisoner; here be the warrant.”



“Warrant! on what charge?”



“the murder of Sir Philip Derval.”



“I--I! murder!” I could say no more.



I must hurry over this awful passage in my marvellous record. it be

torture to dwell on the detail; and indeed I have so seek to chase

they from my recollection, that they only come back to I in hideous

fragment, like the incoherent remain of a horrible dream.



all that I need state be as follow: early on the very morning on which

I have be arrest, a man, a stranger in the town, have privately seek

Mr. Vigors, and depose that on the night of the murder, he have be

take refuge from a sudden storm under shelter of the eave and

buttress of a wall adjoin an old archway; that he have hear man

talk within the archway; have hear one say to the other, “you still

bear I a grudge.” the other have reply, “I can forgive you on one

condition.” that he then lose much of the conversation that ensue,

which be in a low voice; but he gather enough to know that the

condition demand by the one be the possession of a casket which the

other carry about with he; that there seem an altercation on this

matter between the two man, which, to judge by the tone of voice, be

angry on the part of the man demand the casket; that, finally, this

man say in a loud key, “do you still refuse?” and on receive the

answer, which the witness do not overhear, exclaim threateningly, “it

be you who will repent,” and then step forth from the arch into the

street. the rain have then cease, but by a broad flash of lightning

the witness see distinctly the figure of the person thus quit the

shelter of the arch,--a man of tall stature, powerful frame, erect

carriage. a little time afterwards, witness see a slighter and old man

come forth from the arch, whom he could only examine by the flickering

ray of the gas-lamp near the wall, the lightning having cease, but whom

he fully believe to be the person he afterwards discover to be Sir

Philip Derval.



he say that he himself have only arrive at the town a few hour before;

a stranger to L----, and indeed to England, having come from the United

States of America, where he have pass his life from childhood. he have

journey on foot to L----, in the hope of find there some distant

relative. he have put up at a small inn, after which he have stroll

through the town, when the storm have drive he to seek shelter. he have

then fail to find his way back to the inn, and after wander about

in vain, and see no one at that late hour of night of whom he could

ask the way, he have creep under a portico and sleep for two or three

hour. wake towards the dawn, he have then get up, and again seek to

find his way to the inn, when he see, in a narrow street before he,

two man, one of whom he recognize as the taller of the two to whose

conversation he have listen under the arch; the other he do not

recognize at the moment. the tall man seem angry and agitated, and

he hear he say, “the casket; I will have it.” there then seem to be

a struggle between these two person, when the tall one strike down

the short, knelt on his breast, and he catch distinctly the gleam of

some steel instrument. that he be so frightened that he could not stir

from the place, and that though he cry out, he believe his voice be

not hear. he then see the tall man rise, the other rest on the

pavement motionless; and a minute or so afterwards beheld policeman

come to the place, on which he, the witness, walk away. he do not

know that a murder have be commit; it might be only an assault; it

be no business of his, he be a stranger. he think it well not to

interfere, the police have cognizance of the affair. he find out his

inn; for the next few day he be absent from L---- in search of his

relation, who have leave the town, many year ago, to fix their residence

in one of the neighbouring village.



he be, however, disappoint; none of these relation now survive. he

have now return to L----, hear of the murder, be in doubt what to

do, might get himself into trouble if, a mere stranger, he give

an unsupported testimony. but, on the day before the evidence be

volunteer, as he be lounge in the street, he have see a gentleman

pass by on horseback, in whom he immediately recognize the man who,

in his belief, be the murderer of Sir Philip Derval. he inquire of a

bystander the name of the gentleman; the answer be “Dr. Fenwick.” that,

the rest of the day, he feel much disturb in his mind, not like to

volunteer such a charge against a man of apparent respectability and

station; but that his conscience would not let he sleep that night,

and he have resolve at morning to go to the magistrate and make a clean

breast of it.



the story be in itself so improbable that any other magistrate but

Mr. Vigors would perhaps have dismiss it in contempt. but Mr. Vigors,

already so bitterly prejudiced against I, and not sorry, perhaps,

to subject I to the humiliation of so horrible a charge, immediately

issue his warrant to search my house. I be absent at Derval Court; the

house be search. in the bureau in my favourite study, which be leave

unlocked, the steel casket be discover, and a large case-knife, on

the blade of which the stain of blood be still perceptible. on

this discovery I be apprehend; and on these evidence, and on the

deposition of this vagrant stranger, I be not, indeed, commit to

take my trial for murder, but place in confinement, all bail for my

appearance refuse, and the examination adjourn to give time for

further evidence and inquiry. I have request the professional aid of

Mr. Jeeves. to my surprise and dismay, Mr. Jeeves beg I to excuse

he. he say he be pre-engage by Mr. Strahan to detect and prosecute

the murderer of Sir P. Derval, and could not assist one accuse of the

murder. I gather from the little he say that Strahan have already be

to he that morning and tell he of the miss manuscript, that Strahan

have cease to be my friend. I engage another solicitor, a young man of

ability, and who profess personal esteem for I. Mr. Stanton (such

be the lawyer’s name) believe in my innocence; but he warn I that

appearance be grave, he implore I to be perfectly frank with he.

have I hold conversation with Sir Philip under the archway as report by

the witness? have I use such or similar word? have the decease say, “I

have a grudge against he”? have I demand the casket? have I threaten

Sir Philip that he would repent? and of what,--his refusal?



I feel myself grow pale, as I answer, “yes; I think such or similar

expression have occur in my conversation with the deceased.”



“what be the reason of the grudge? what be the nature of this casket,

that I should so desire its possession?”



there, I become terribly embarrassed. what could I say to a keen,

sensible, worldly man of law,--tell he of the powder and the fume, of

the scene in the museum, of Sir Philip’s tale, of the imply identity

of the youthful Margrave with the aged Grayle, of the elixir of life,

and of magic art? I--I tell such a romance! I,--the note adversary of

all pretended mysticism; I,--I a sceptical practitioner of medicine! have

that manuscript of Sir Philip’s be available,--a substantial record

of marvellous event by a man of repute for intellect and learning,--I

might perhaps have venture to startle the solicitor of L------ with my

revelation. but the sole proof that all which the solicitor urge I

to confide be not a monstrous fiction or an insane delusion have

disappear; and its disappearance be a part of the terrible mystery

that envelop the whole. I answer therefore, as composedly as I

could, that “I could have no serious grudge against Sir Philip, whom I

have never see before that evening; that the word which apply to

my suppose grudge be lightly say by Sir Philip, in reference to a

physiological dispute on matter connect with mesmerical phenomena;

that the deceased have declare his casket, which he have show I at the

mayor’s house, contain drug of great potency in medicine; that I have

ask permission to test those drug myself; and that when I say he

would repent of his refusal, I merely mean that he would repent of

his reliance on drug not warrant by the experiment of professional

science.”



my reply seem to satisfy the lawyer so far, but “how could I account

for the casket and the knife be find in my room?”



“in no way but this; the window of my study be a door-window opening on

the lane, from which any one might enter the room. I be in the habit,

not only of go out myself that way, but of admit through that

door any more familiar private acquaintance.”



“Whom, for instance?”



I hesitate a moment, and then say, with a significance I could not

forbear, “Mr. Margrave! he would know the locale perfectly; he would

know that the door be rarely bolt from within during the daytime:

he could enter at all hour; he could place, or instruct any one to

deposit, the knife and casket in my bureau, which he know I never keep

locked; it contain no secret, no private correspondence,--chiefly

surgical implement, or such thing as I might want for professional

experiment.”



“Mr. Margrave! but you cannot suspect he--a lively, charming young man,

against whose character not a whisper be ever hear--of connivance with

such a charge against you,--a connivance that would implicate he in the

murder itself; for if you be accuse wrongfully, he who accuse you be

either the criminal or the criminal’s accomplice, his instigator or his

tool.”



“Mr. Stanton,” I say firmly, after a moment’s pause, “I do suspect

Mr. Margrave of a hand in this crime. Sir Philip, on see he at the

mayor’s house, express a strong abhorrence of he, more than hint

at crime he have commit, appoint I to come to Derval Court the

day after that on which the murder be commit. Sir Philip have know

something of this Margrave in the East; Margrave might dread exposure,

revelation--of what I know not; but, strange as it may seem to you,

it be my conviction that this young man, apparently so gay and so

thoughtless, be the real criminal, and in some way which I cannot

conjecture have employ this lie vagabond in the fabrication of a

charge against myself. reflect: of Mr. Margrave’s antecedent we know

nothing; of they nothing be know even by the young gentleman who first

introduce he to the society of this town. if you would serve and

save I, it be to that quarter that you will direct your vigilant and

unrelaxe research.”



I have scarcely so say when I repent my candour, for I observe in the

face of Mr. Stanton a sudden revulsion of feeling, an utter incredulity

of the accusation I have thus hazard, and for the first time a doubt of

my own innocence. the fascination exercise by Margrave be universal;

nor be it to be wonder at: for besides the charm of his joyous
