presence, he seem so singularly free from even the error common

enough with the young,--so gay and boon a companion, yet a shunner of

wine; so dazzle in aspect, so more than beautiful, so court, so

idolize by woman, yet no tale of seduction, of profligacy, attach

to his name! as to his antecedent, he have so frankly own himself

a natural son, a nobody, a traveller, an idler; his expense, though

lavish, be so unostentatious, so regularly defray; he be so wholly

the reverse of the character assign to criminal, that it seem as

absurd to bring a charge of homicide against a butterfly or a goldfinch

as against this seemingly innocent and delightful favourite of humanity

and nature.



however, Mr. Stanton say little or nothing, and shortly afterwards leave

I, with a dry expression of hope that my innocence would be clear

in spite of evidence that, he be bind to say, be of the most serious

character.



I be exhaust. I fall into a profound sleep early that night; it might

be a little after twelve when I wake, and wake as fully, as completely,

as much restore to life and consciousness, as it be then my habit to

be at the break of day. and so wake, I see, on the wall opposite

my bed, the same luminous phantom I have see in the wizard’s study

at Derval Court. I have read in scandinavian legend of an apparition

call the Scin-Laeca, or shine corpse. it be suppose in the northern

superstition, sometimes to haunt sepulchre, sometimes to foretell doom.

it be the spectre of a human body see in a phosphoric light; and

so exactly do this phantom correspond to the description of such an

apparition in Scandinavian fable that I know not how to give it a well

name than that of Scin-Laeca,--the shine corpse.



there it be before I, corpse-like, yet not dead; there, as in the

haunt study of the wizard Forman!--the form and the face of Margrave.

constitutionally, my nerve be strong, and my temper hardy, and now

I be resolve to battle against any impression which my sense might

receive from my own deluding fancy. thing that witness for the

first time daunt we witness for the second time lose their terror.

I rise from my bed with a bold aspect, I approach the phantom with a

firm step; but when within two pace of it, and my hand outstretche to

touch it, my arm became fix in air, my foot lock to the ground. I

do not experience fear; I feel that my heart beat regularly, but an

invincible something oppose itself to I. I stand as if turn to

stone. and then from the lip of this phantom there come a voice, but a

voice which seem borne from a great distance,--very low, muffle, and

yet distinct; I could not even be sure that my ear hear it, or whether

the sound be not convey to I by an inner sense.



“I, and I alone, can save and deliver you,” say the voice. “I will do

so; and the condition I ask, in return, be simple and easy.”



“fiend or spectre, or mere delusion of my own brain,” cry I, “there

can be no compact between thee and I. I despise thy malice, I reject

thy service; I accept no condition to escape from the one or to obtain

the other.”



“you may give a different answer when I ask again.”



the Scin-Laeca slowly wane, and, fade first into a pale shadow, then

vanish. I rejoice at the reply I have give. two day elapse before

Mr. Stanton again come to I; in the interval the Scin-Laeca do not

reappear. I have muster all my courage, all my common-sense, note down

all the weak point of the false evidence against I, and feel calm and

support by the strength of my innocence.



the first few word of the solicitor dash all my courage to the

ground; for I be anxious to hear news of Lilian, anxious to have

some message from she that might cheer and strengthen I, and my first

question be this,--



“Mr. Stanton, you be aware that I be engage in marriage to Miss

Ashleigh. your family be not unacquainted with she. what say, what

think she of this monstrous charge against she betroth?”



“I be for two hour at Mrs. Ashleigh’s house last evening,” reply

the lawyer; “she be naturally anxious to see I as employ in your

defence. who do you think be there? who, eager to defend you, to

express his persuasion of your innocence, to declare his conviction

that the real criminal would be soon discovered,--who but that same Mr.

Margrave; whom, pardon I my frankness, you so rashly and groundlessly

suspect.”



“Heavens! do you say that he be receive in that house; that he--he be

familiarly admit to her presence?”



“my good sir, why these unjust prepossession against a true friend? it

be as your friend that, as soon as the charge against you amazed

and shock the town of L----, Mr. Margrave call on Mrs. Ashleigh,

present to she by Miss Brabazon, and be so cheer and hopeful

that--”



“Enough!” I exclaimed,--“enough!”



I pace the room in a state of excitement and rage, which the lawyer in

vain endeavour to calm, until at length I halt abruptly: “well, and

you see Miss Ashleigh? what message do she send to I--she betroth?”



Mr. Stanton look confused. “message! consider, sir, Miss Ashleigh’s

situation--the delicacy--and--and--”



“I understand, no message, no word, from a young lady so respectable to

a man accuse of murder.”



Mr. Stanton be silent for some moment, and then say quietly, “let we

change this subject; let we think of what more immediately press. I

see you have be make some note: may I look at they?”



I compose myself and sit down. “this accuser! have inquiry really

be make as to himself, and his statement of his own proceeding? he

come, he say, from America: in what ship? at what port do he land? be

there any evidence to corroborate his story of the relation he try to

discover; of the inn at which he first put up, and to which he could not

find his way?”



“your suggestion be sensible, Dr. Fenwick. I have forestall they. it

be true that the man lodge at a small inn,--the rise Sun; true

that he make inquiry about some relation of the name of Walls, who

formerly reside at L----, and afterwards remove to a village ten

mile distant,--two brother, tradesman of small mean but respectable

character. he at first refuse to say at what seaport he land, in what

ship he sail. I suspect that he have now tell a falsehood as to these

matter. I send my clerk to Southampton, for it be there he say that

he be put on shore; we shall see: the man himself be detain in close

custody. I hear that his manner be strange and excitable; but that he

preserve silence as much as possible. it be generally believe that

he be a bad character, perhaps a return convict, and that this be the

true reason why he so long delay give evidence, and have be since

so reluctant to account for himself. but even if his testimony should

be impugn, should break down, still we should have to account for the

fact that the casket and the case-knife be find in your bureau; for,

grant that a person could, in your absence, have enter your study

and place the article in your bureau, it be clear that such a person

must have be well acquainted with your house, and this stranger to

L---- could not have possess that knowledge.”



“of course not. Mr. Margrave do possess it!”



“Mr. Margrave again! oh, sir!”



I arise and move away with an impatient gesture. I could not trust

myself to speak. that night I do not sleep; I watch impatiently,

gaze on the opposite wall for the gleam of the Scin-Laeca. but the

night pass away, and the spectre do not appear.













CHAPTER XLI.



the lawyer come the next day, and with something like a smile on his

lip. he bring I a few line in pencil from Mrs. Ashleigh; they be

kindly express, bid I be of good cheer; “she never for a moment

believe in my guilt; lilian bear up wonderfully under so terrible a

trial; it be an unspeakable comfort to both to receive the visit of a

friend so attach to I, and so confident of a triumphant refutation of

the hideous calumny under which I now suffer as Mr. Margrave!”



the lawyer have see Margrave again,--seen he in that house. Margrave

seem almost domicile there!



I remain sullen and taciturn during this visit. I long again for the

night. night come. I hear the distant clock strike twelve, when again

the icy wind pass through my hair, and against the wall stand the

luminous Shadow.



“have you consider?” whisper the voice, still as from afar. “I

repeat it,--I alone can save you.”



“be it among the condition which you ask, in return, that I shall

resign to you the woman I love?”



“no.”



“be it one of the condition that I should commit some crime,--a crime

perhaps heinous as that of which I be accuse?”



“no.”



“with such reservation, I accept the condition you may name, provide

I, in my turn, may demand one condition from yourself.”



“name it.”



“I ask you to quit this town. I ask you, meanwhile, to cease your visit

to the house that hold the woman betroth to I.”



“I will cease those visit. and before many day be over, I will quit

this town.”



“now, then, say what you ask from I. I be prepared to concede it. and

not from fear for myself, but because I fear for the pure and innocent

be who be under the spell of your deadly fascination. this be your

power over I. you command I through my love for another. speak.”



“my condition be simple. you will pledge yourself to desist from all

charge of insinuation against myself, of what nature soever. you will

not, when you meet I in the flesh, refer to what you have know of my

likeness in the Shadow. you will be invite to the house at which I may

be also a guest; you will come; you will meet and converse with I as

guest speak with guest in the house of a host.”



“be that all?”



“it be all.”



“then I pledge you my faith; keep your own.”



“fear not; sleep secure in the certainty that you will soon be release

from these wall.”



the Shadow wane and fade. darkness settle back, and a sleep, profound

and calm, fall over I.



the next day Mr. Stanton again visit I. he have receive that morning

a note from Mr. Margrave, state that he have leave L---- to pursue, in

person, an investigation which he have already commence through another,

affect the man who have give evidence against I, and that, if his

hope should prove well found, he trust to establish my innocence,

and convict the real murderer of Sir Philip Derval. in the research he

thus volunteer, he have ask for, and obtain, the assistance of the

policeman Waby, who, grateful to I for save the life of his sister,

have express a strong desire to be employ in my service.



meanwhile, my most cruel assailant be my old college friend, Richard

Strahan. for Jeeves have spread abroad Strahan’s charge of purloin the

memoir which have be entrust to I; and that accusation have do I

great injury in public opinion, because it seem to give probability to

the only motive which ingenuity could ascribe to the foul deed impute

to I. that motive have be first suggest by Mr. Vigors. case be

on record of man whose life have be previously blameless, who have

commit a crime which seem to belie their nature, in the monomania

of some intense desire. in Spain, a scholar repute of austere

moral murder and rob a traveller for money in order to purchase

books,--books write, too, by father of his Church! he be intent on

solve some problem of theological casuistry. in France, an antiquary,

esteem not more for his learning than for amiable and gentle

quality, murder his most intimate friend for the possession of

a medal, without which his own collection be incomplete. these, and

similar anecdote, tend to prove how fatally any vehement desire,

morbidly cherish, may suspend the normal operation of reason and

conscience, be whisper about by Dr. Lloyd’s vindictive partisan;

and the inference draw from they and apply to the assumption against

myself be the more credulously receive, because of that over-refining

speculation on motive and act which the shallow accept, in their

eagerness to show how readily they understand the profound.



I be know to be fond of scientific, especially of chemical

experiment; to be eager in test the truth of any novel invention.

Strahan, catch hold of the magistrate’s fantastic hypothesis, go

about repeat anecdote of the absorbing passion for analysis and

discovery which have characterize I in youth as a medical student, and

to which, indeed, I owe the precocious reputation I have obtain.



Sir Philip Derval, accord not only to report, but to the direct

testimony of his servant, have acquire in the course of his travel many

secret in natural science, especially as connect with the healing

art,--his servant have depose to the remarkable cure he have effect

by the medicinal store in the steal casket. Doubtless Sir Philip,

in boasting of these medicinal in the course of our conversation,

have excite my curiosity, inflame my imagination; and thus when I

afterwards suddenly meet he in a lone spot, a passionate impulse have

act on a brain heat into madness by curiosity and covetous desire.



all these supposition, reduce into system, be corroborate by

Strahan’s charge that I have make away with the manuscript suppose to

contain the explanation of the medical agency employ by Sir Philip,

and have seek to shelter my theft by a tale so improbable, that a man

of my reputed talent could not have hazard it if in his sound

sense. I see the web that have thus be spread around I by hostile

prepossession and ignorant gossip: how could the art of Margrave

scatter that web to the wind? I know not, but I feel confidence in his

promise and his power. still, so great have be my alarm for Lilian,

that the hope of clear my own innocence be almost lose in my joy

that Margrave, at least, be no long in her presence, and that I have

receive his pledge to quit the town in which she live.



thus, hour roll on hour, till, I think, on the third day from that

night in which I have last beheld the mysterious Shadow, my door

be hastily throw open, a confused crowd present itself at the

Mr. Stanton, and other familiar face shut out from I since my

imprisonment. I know at the first glance that I be no long an outlaw

beyond the pale of human friendship. and proudly, sternly, as I have

support myself hitherto in solitude and suspense, when I feel warm

emotion be too strong for me,--the room reel on my sight, I faint.

I pass, as quickly as I can, over the explanation that crowd on I

when I recover, and that be publicly give in evidence in court next

to my favour the very supposition which have be bruit abroad to my

prejudice. “for,” say he, “it be conjecture that Fenwick commit the

crime of which he be accuse in the impulse of a disorder reason. that

conjecture be base upon the probability that a madman alone could have

commit a crime without adequate motive. but it seem quite clear that

the accuse be not mad; and I see cause to suspect that the accuser be.”

 ground this assumption on the current report of the witness’s

manner and bear since he have be place under official surveillance,

Margrave have commission the policeman Waby to make inquiry in

the village to which the accuser assert he have go in quest of his

relation, and Waby have there find person who remember to have hear

that the two brother name Walls live less by the gain of the petty

shop which they keep than by the proceed of some property consign to

they as the near of kin to a lunatic who have once be try for

his life. Margrave have then examine the advertisement in the daily

newspaper. one of they, warn the public against a dangerous maniac,

who have effect his escape from an asylum in the west of England,

catch his attention. to that asylum he have repair.



there he learn that the patient advertise be one whose propensity

be homicide, consign for life to the asylum on account of a murder,

for which he have be try. the description of this person exactly

tally with that of the pretend American. the medical superintendent

of the asylum, hear all particular from Margrave, express a strong

persuasion that the witness be his miss patient, and have himself

commit the crime of which he have accuse another. if so, the

superintendent undertake to coax from he the full confession of all

the circumstance. like many other madman, and not least those whose

propensity be to crime, the fugitive maniac be exceedingly cunning,

treacherous, secret, and habituate to trick and stratagem,--more subtle

than even the astute in possession of all their faculty, whether to

achieve his purpose or to conceal it, and fabricate appearance against

another. but while, in ordinary conversation, he seem rational enough

to those who be not accustomed to study he, he have one hallucination

which, when humour, lead he always, not only to betray himself, but to

glory in any crime propose or commit. he be under the belief that

he have make a bargain with Satan, who, in return for implicit obedience,

would bear he harmless through all the consequence of such submission,

and finally raise he to great power and authority. it be no unfrequent

illusion of homicidal maniacs to suppose they be under the influence

of the evil One, or possess by a Demon. murderer have assign as the

only reason they themselves could give for their crime, that “the Devil

get into they,” and urge the deed. but the insane have, perhaps, no

attribute more in common than that of superweene self-esteem. the

maniac who have be remove from a garret stick straw in his hair

and call they a crown. so much do inordinate arrogance characterize

mental aberration, that, in the course of my own practice, I have

detect, in that infirmity, the certain symptom of insanity, long

before the brain have make its disease manifest even to the most familiar

kindre.



Morbid self-esteem accordingly pervade the dreadful illusion by which

the man I now speak of be possess. he be proud to be the protect

agent of the Fallen Angel. and if that self-esteem be artfully

appeal to, he would exult superbly in the evil he hold himself order

to perform, as if a special prerogative, an official rank and privilege;

then, he would be lead on to boast gleefully of thought which the most

cynical of criminal in whom intelligence be not ruin would shrink

from own; then, he would reveal himself in all his deformity with as

complacent and frank a self-glorying as some vain good man display in

parade his amiable sentiment and his beneficent deed.



“if,” say the superintendent, “this be the patient who have escape from

I, and if his propensity to homicide have be, in some way, direct

towards the person who have be murder, I shall not be with he a

quarter of an hour before he will inform I how it happen, and detail

the art he employ in shift his crime upon another; all will be

tell as minutely as a child tell the tale of some school-boy exploit,

in which he count on your sympathy, and feel sure of your applause.”



Margrave bring this gentleman back to L----, take he to the mayor,

who be one of my warm supporter: the mayor have sufficient influence

to dictate and arrange the rest. the superintendent be introduce to

the room in which the pretend American be lodge. at his own desire

a select number of witness be admit with he. Margrave excuse

himself; he say candidly that he be too intimate a friend of mine to

be an impartial listener to aught that concern I so nearly.



the superintendent prove right in his suspicion, and verify his

promise. my false accuser be his miss patient; the man recognize

Dr. ---- with no apparent terror, rather with an air of condescension,

and in a very few minute be lead to tell his own tale, with a gloating

complacency both at the agency by which he deem himself exalt, and

at the dexterous cunning with which he have acquit himself of the

task, that increase the horror of his narrative.



he speak of the mode of his escape, which be extremely ingenious, but

of which the detail, long in themselves, do not interest I, and

I understand they too imperfectly to repeat. he have encounter a

sea-fare traveller on the road, whom he have knock down with a stone,

and rob of his glaze hat and pea-jacket, as well as of a small

sum in coin, which last enable he to pay his fare in a railway that

convey he eighty mile away from the asylum. some trifle remnant

of this money still in his pocket, he then travel on foot along the

high-road till he come to a town about twenty mile distant from L----;

there he have stay a day or two, and there he say “that the Devil have

tell he to buy a case-knife, which he do.” “he know by that order that

the Devil mean he to do something great.” “his Master,” as he call

the fiend, then direct he the road he should take. he come to L----,

put up, as he have correctly state before, at a small inn, wander at

night about the town, be surprise by the sudden storm, take shelter

under the convent arch, overheard somewhat more of my conversation with

Sir Philip than he have previously deposed,--heard enough to excite his

curiosity as to the casket: “while he listen his Master tell he he

must get possession of that casket.” Sir Philip have quit the archway

almost immediately after I have do so, and he would then have attack

he if he have not catch sight of a policeman go his round. he have

follow Sir Philip to a house (Mr. Jeeves’s). “his Master tell he

to wait and watch.” he do so. when Sir Philip come forth, towards the

dawn, he follow he, see he enter a narrow street, come up to he,

seize he by the arm, demand all he have about he. Sir Philip try

to shake he off,--struck at he. what follow I spare the reader. the

deed be do. he rob the dead man both of the casket and the

purse that he find in the pocket; have scarcely do so when he hear

footstep. he have just time to get behind the portico of a detach

house at angle with the street when I come up. he witness, from his

hiding-place, the brief conference between myself and the policeman,

and when they move on, bear the body, steal unobserved away. he be

go back towards the inn, when it occur to he that it would be

safe if the casket and purse be not about his person; that he ask

his Master to direct he how to dispose of they: that his Master guide

he to an open yard (a stone-mason’s) at a very little distance from

the inn; that in this yard there stand an old wych-elm tree, from the

gnarl root of which the earth be wear away, leave chink and

hollow, in one of which he place the casket and purse, take from the

latter only two sovereign and some silver, and then heap loose mould

over the hiding-place. that he then repair to his inn, and leave

it late in the morning, on the pretence of seek for his

relations,--persons, indeed, who really have be relate to he, but

of whose death year ago he be aware. he return to L---- a few day

afterwards, and in the dead of the night go to take up the casket and

the money. he find the purse with its content undisturbed; but the

lid of the casket be unclosed. from the hasty glance he have take of it

before bury it, it have seem to he firmly locked,--he be alarm

lest some one have be to the spot. but his Master whisper to he not

to mind, tell he that he might now take the casket, and would be guide

what to do with it; that he do so, and, open the lid, find the

casket empty-; that he take the rest of the money out of the purse, but

that he do not take the purse itself, for it have a crest and initial

on it, which might lead to the discovery of what have be do; that

he therefore leave it in the hollow amongst the root, heap the mould

over it as before; that in the course of the day he hear the people at

the inn talk of the murder, and that his own first impulse be to get

out of the town immediately, but that his Master “make he too wise for

that,” and bid he stay; that pass through the street, he see I

come out of the sash-window door, go to a stable-yard on the other side

of the house, mount on horseback and ride away; that he observe the

sash-door be leave partially open; that he walk by it and see the

room empty; there be only a dead wall opposite; the place be solitary,

unobserve; that his Master direct he to lift up the sash gently,

enter the room, and deposit the knife and the casket in a large

walnut-tree bureau which stand unlocked near the window. all that

follow--his visit to Mr. Vigors, his accusation against myself, his

whole tale--be, he say, dictate by his Master, who be highly pleased

with he, and promise to bring he safely through. and here he turn

round with a hideous smile, as if for approbation of his notable

cleverness and respect for his high employ.



Mr. Jeeves have the curiosity to request the keeper to inquire how, in

what form, or in what manner, the Fiend appear to the narrator, or

convey his infernal dictate. the man at first refuse to say; but

it be gradually draw from he that the Demon have no certain and

invariable form: sometimes it appear to he in the form of a rat;

sometimes even of a leaf, or a fragment of wood, or a rusty nail; but

that his Master’s voice always come to he distinctly, whatever shape he

appear in; only, he say, with an air of great importance, his Master,

this time, have graciously condescend, ever since he leave the asylum,

to communicate with he in a much more pleasing and impose aspect than

he have ever do before,--in the form of a beautiful youth, or, rather,

like a bright rose-colour shadow, in which the feature of a young

man be visible, and that he have hear the voice more distinctly than

usual, though in a milder tone, and seem to come to he from a great

distance.



after these revelation the man become suddenly disturbed. he shake from

limb to limb, he seem convulse with terror; he cry out that he have

betray the secret of his Master, who have warn he not to describe

his appearance and mode of communication, or he would surrender his

servant to the tormentor. then the maniac’s terror give way to fury;

his more direful propensity make itself declare; he spring into the

midst of his frightened listener, seize Mr. Vigors by the throat, and

would have strangle he but for the prompt rush of the superintendent

and his satellite. foam at the mouth, and horribly rave, he be

then manacle, a strait-waistcoat thrust upon he, and the group so

leave he in charge of his captor. inquiry be immediately direct

towards such circumstantial evidence as might corroborate the detail he

have so minutely set forth. the purse, recognize as Sir Philip’s, by the

valet of the deceased, be find bury under the wych-elm. a policeman

despatch, express, to the town in which the maniac declare the knife

to have be purchase, bring back word that a cutler in the place

remember perfectly to have sell such a knife to a seafaring man, and

identify the instrument when it be show to he. from the chink of a

door ajar, in the wall opposite my sash-window, a maid-servant, watch

for her sweetheart (a journeyman carpenter, who habitually pass that

way on go home to dine), have, though unobserve by the murderer, see

he come out of my window at a time that correspond with the date of

his own story, though she have think nothing of it at the moment. he

might be a patient, or have call on business; she do not know that

I be from home. the only point of importance not clear up be that

which relate to the opening of the casket,--the disappearance of the

content; the lock have be unquestionably force. no one, however,

could suppose that some third person have discover the hiding-place and

force open the casket to abstract its content and then rebury it. the

only probable supposition be that the man himself have force it open,

and, deem the content of no value, have throw they away before he

have hide the casket and purse, and, in the chaos of his reason, have

forget that he have so do. who could expect that every link in a

madman’s tale would be find integral and perfect? in short, little

importance be attach to this solitary doubt. crowd accompany I

to my door, when I be set free, in open court, stainless; it be

a triumphal procession. the popularity I have previously enjoy,

supersede for a moment by so horrible a charge, come back to I tenfold

as with the reaction of generous repentance for a momentary doubt.

one man share the public favour,--the young man whose acuteness have

deliver I from the peril, and clear the truth from so awful a

mystery; but Margrave have escape from congratulation and compliment; he

have go on a visit to Strahan, at Derval Court.



alone, at last, in the welcome sanctuary of my own home, what be my

thought? prominent amongst they all be that assertion of the madman,

which have make I shudder when repeat to I: he have be guide to the

murder and to all the subsequent proceeding by the luminous shadow of

the beautiful youth,--the Scin-Laeca to which I have pledge myself. if

Sir Philip Derval could be believe, Margrave be possess of power,

derive from fragmentary recollection of a knowledge acquire in a

former state of being, which would render his remorseless intelligence

infinitely dire and frustrate the endeavour of a reason, unassisted

by similar power, to thwart his design or bring the law against his

crime. have he then the art that could thus influence the mind of

other to serve his fell purpose, and achieve securely his own evil

end through agency that could not be trace home to himself?



but for what conceivable purpose have I be subject as a victim to

influence as much beyond my control as the Fate or Demoniac Necessity

of a Greek Myth? in the legend of the classic world some august

sufferer be oppress by power more than mortal, but with an ethical

if gloomy vindication of his chastisement,--he pay the penalty of crime

commit by his ancestor or himself, or he have brave, by arrogate

equality with the god, the mysterious calamity which the god alone can

inflict. but I, no descendant of Pelops, no OEdipus boastful of a wisdom

which could interpret the enigma of the Sphynx, while ignorant even of

his own birth--what have I do to be single out from the herd of man

for trial and visitation from the Shadowland of ghost and sorcerer?

it would be ludicrously absurd to suppose that Dr. Lloyd’s die

imprecation could have have a prophetic effect upon my destiny; to

believe that the pretence of mesmerizer be specially favour by

Providence, and that to question their assumption be an offence of

profanation to be punish by exposure to preternatural agency. there

be not even that congruity between cause and effect which fable seek

in excuse for its invention. of all man live, I, unimaginative

disciple of austere science, should be the last to become the sport

of that witchcraft which even imagination reluctantly allow to the

machinery of poet, and science cast aside into the mouldy lumber-room

of obsolete superstition.



rouse my mind from enigmas impossible to solve, it be with intense

and yet most melancholy satisfaction that I turn to the image of

Lilian, rejoice, though with a thrill of awe, that the promise

so mysteriously convey to my sense have, hereto, be already

fulfilled,--Margrave have leave the town; Lilian be no long subject

to his evil fascination. but an instinct tell I that that fascination

have already produce an effect adverse to all hope of happiness for I.

Lilian’s love for myself be go. impossible otherwise that she--in

whose nature I have always admire that generous devotion which be

more or less inseparable from the romance of youth--should have never

convey to I one word of consolation in the hour of my agony and

trial; that she, who, till the last evening we have meet, have ever be

so docile, in the sweetness of a nature femininely submissive to my

slight wish, should have disregard my solemn injunction, and

admit Margrave to acquaintance, nay, to familiar intimacy,--at

the very time, too, when to disobey my injunction be to embitter my

ordeal, and add her own contempt to the degradation impose upon my

honour! no, her heart must be wholly go from I; her very nature

wholly warp. a union between we have become impossible. my love for

she remain unshattered; the more tender, perhaps, for a sentiment of

compassion. but my pride be shock, my heart be wound. my love be

not mean and servile. enough for I to think that she would be at

least save from Margrave. her life associate with his!--contemplation

horrible and ghastly!--from that fate she be save. later, she would

recover the effect of an influence happily so brief. she might form some

new attachment, some new tie; but love once withdraw be never to be

restore--and her love be withdraw from I. I have but to release she,

with my own lip, from our engagement,--she would welcome that release.

mournful but firm in these thought and these resolution, I seek Mrs.

Ashleigh’s house.













CHAPTER XLII.



it be twilight when I enter, unannounced (as have be my willnot in our

familiar intercourse), the quiet sitting-room in which I expect to

find mother and child. but Lilian be there alone, seat by the open

window, her hand cross and droop on her knee, her eye fix upon

the darken summer sky, in which the evening star have just steal

forth, bright and steadfast, near the pale sickle of a half-moon that

be dimly visible, but give as yet no light.



let any lover imagine the reception he would expect to meet from his

betroth come into her presence after he have pass triumphant

through a terrible peril to life and fame--and conceive what ice freeze

my blood, what anguish weigh down my heart, when Lilian, turn

towards I, rise not, speak not, gaze at I heedlessly as if at some

indifferent stranger--and--and--but no matter. I cannot bear to recall

it even now, at the distance of year! I sit down beside she, and take

her hand, without press it; it rest languidly, passively in mine,

one moment; I drop it then, with a bitter sigh.



“Lilian,” I say quietly, “you love I no long. be it not so?”



she raise her eye to mine, look at I wistfully, and press she

hand on her forehead; then say, in a strange voice, “do I ever love

you? what do you mean?”



“lilian, Lilian, rouse yourself; be you not, while you speak, under

some spell, some influence which you cannot describe nor account for?”



she pause a moment before she answer, calmly, “no! again I ask what

do you mean?”



“what do I mean? do you forget that we be betroth? do you forget how

often, and how recently, our vow of affection and constancy have be

exchange?”



“no, I do not forget; but I must have deceive you and myself--”



“it be true, then, that you love I no more?”



“I suppose so.”



“but, oh, Lilian, be it that your heart be only close to I; or

be it--oh, answer truthfully--be it give to another,--to he--to

he--against whom I warn you, whom I implore you not to receive? tell

I, at least, that your love be not go to Margrave--”



“to he! love to he! oh, no--no--”



“what, then, be your feeling towards he?”



Lilian’s face grow visibly pale, even in that dim light. “I know not,”

 she say, almost in a whisper; “but it be partly awe--partly--”



“what?”



“Abhorrence!” she say almost fiercely, and rise to her foot, with a

wild defy start.



“if that be so,” I say gently, “you would not grieve be you never

again to see him--”



“but I shall see he again,” she murmur in a tone of weary sadness,

and sink back once more into her chair.



“I think not,” say I, “and I hope not. and now hear I and hee I,

Lilian. it be enough for I, no matter what your feeling towards

another, to learn from yourself that the affection you once profess

for I be go. I release you from your troth. if folk ask why we two

henceforth separate the life we have agree to join, you may say, if

you please, that you could not give your hand to a man who have know the

taint of a felon’s prison, even on a false charge. if that seem to you

an ungenerous reason, we will leave it to your mother to find a well.

farewell! for your own sake I can yet feel happiness,--happiness to hear

that you do not love the man against whom I warn you still more solemnly

than before! will you not give I your hand in parting--and have I not

speak your own wish?”



she turn away her face, and resign her hand to I in silence.

silently I hold it in mine, and my emotion nearly stifle I. one

symptom of regret, of reluctance, on her part, and I should have fall

at her foot, and cry, “do not let we break a tie which our vow should

have make indisoluble; heed not my offer, wring from a tortured heart!

you cannot have cease to love I!” but no such symptom of relent

show itself in she, and with a groan I leave the room.













CHAPTER XLIII.



I be just outside the garden door, when I feel an arm throw round I,

my cheek kiss and wet with tear. could it be lilian? alas, no!

it be her mother’s voice, that, between laugh and crying, exclaim

hysterically: “this be joy, to see you again, and on these threshold. I

have just come from your house; I go there on purpose to congratulate

you, and to talk to you about Lilian. but you have see she?”



“yes; I have but this moment leave she. come this way.” I draw Mrs.

Ashleigh back into the garden, along the old winding walk, which the

shrub conceal from view of the house. we sit down on a rustic seat

where I have often sit with Lilian, midway between the house and the

monk’ well. I tell the mother what have pass between I and her

daughter; I make no complaint of Lilian’s coldness and change; I do not

hint at its cause. “girl of her age will change,” say I, “and all

that now remain be for we two to agree on such a tale to our curious

neighbour as may rest the whole blame on I. man’s name be of robust

fibre; it could not push its way to a place in the world, if it could

not bear, without sink, the load idle tongue may lay on it. not so

Woman’s name: what be but gossip against Man, be scandal against Woman.”



“do not be rash, my dear Allen,” say Mrs. Ashleigh, in great distress.

“I feel for you, I understand you; in your case I might act as you do. I

cannot blame you. Lilian be changed,--changed unaccountably. yet sure

I be that the change be only on the surface, that her heart be really

your, as entirely and as faithfully as ever it be; and that later,

when she recover from the strange, dreamy kind of torpor which appear

to have come over all her faculty and all her affection, she would

awake with a despair which you cannot conjecture to the knowledge that

you have renounce she.”



“I have not renounce she,” say I, impatiently; “I do but restore she

freedom of choice. but pass by this now, and explain to I more fully

the change in your daughter, which I gather from your word be not

confine to I.”



“I wish to speak of it before you see she, and for that reason come to

your house. it be on the morning in which we leave her aunt’s to return

hither that I first notice some thing peculiar in her look and manner.

she seem absorb and absent, so much so that I ask her several

time to tell I what make she so grave; but I could only get from she

that she have have a confused dream which she could not recall distinctly

enough to relate, but that she be sure it bode evil. during the

journey she become gradually more herself, and begin to look forward

with delight to the idea of see you again. well, you come that

evening. what pass between you and her you know well. you complain

that she slight your request to shun all acquaintance with Mr.

Margrave. I be surprised that, whether your wish be reasonable or

not, she could have hesitate to comply with it. I speak to she about it

after you have go, and she weep bitterly at think she have displease

you.”



“she weep! you amaze I. yet the next day what a note she return to

mine!”



“the next day the change in she become very visible to I. she tell I,

in an excited manner, that she be convince she ought not to marry you.

then come, the follow day, the news of your committal. I hear of

it, but dared not break it to she. I go to our friend the mayor, to

consult with he what to say, what to do; and to learn more distinctly

than I have do from terrified, incoherent servant, the right of so

dreadful a story. when I return, I find, to my amazement, a young

stranger in the drawing-room; it be Mr. Margrave,--Miss Brabazon

have bring he at his request. Lilian be in the room, too, and my

astonishment be increase, when she say to I with a singular smile,

vague but tranquil: ‘I know all about Allen Fenwick; Mr. Margrave have

tell I all. he be a friend of Allen’s. he say there be no cause

for fear.’ Mr. Margrave then apologize to I for his intrusion in a

caressing, kindly manner, as if one of the family. he say he be so

intimate with you that he feel that he could well break to Miss ashleigh

information she might receive elsewhere, for that he be the only man

in the town who treat the charge with ridicule. you know the wonderful

charm of this young man’s manner. I cannot explain to you how it be,

but in a few moment I be as much at home with he as if he have be

your brother. to be brief, having once come, he come constantly. he

have move, two day before you go to Derval Court, from his hotel to

apartment in Mr. ----‘s house, just opposite. we could see he on his

balcony from our terrace; he would smile to we and come across. I do

wrong in slight your injunction, and suffer Lilian to do so. I

could not help it, he be such a comfort to me,--to she, too--in her

tribulation. he alone have no doleful word, wear no long face; he alone

be invariably cheerful. ‘everything,’ he say, ‘would come right in a

day or two.’”



“and Lilian could not but admire this young man, he be so beautiful.”



“beautiful? well, perhaps. but if you have a jealous feeling, you be

never more mistaken. Lilian, I be convince, do more than dislike he;

he have inspire she with repugnance, with terror. and much as I own I

like he, in his wild, joyous, careless, harmless way, do not think I

flatter you if I say that Mr. Margrave be not the man to make any girl

untrue to you,--untrue to a lover with infinitely less advantage than

you may pretend to. he would be a universal favourite, I grant; but

there be something in he, or a something want in he, which make

liking and admiration stop short of love. I know not why; perhaps,

because, with all his good humour, he be so absorbed in himself, so

intensely egotistical, so light; be he less clever, I should say so

frivolous. he could not make love, he could not say in the serious tone

of a man in earnest, ‘I love you.’ he own as much to I, and own,

too, that he know not even what love be. as to myself, Mr. Margrave

appear rich; no whisper against his character or his honour ever

reach I. yet be you out of the question, and be there no stain on

his birth, nay, be he as high in rank and wealth as he be favour by

Nature in personal advantage, I confess I could never consent to trust

he with my daughter’s fate. a voice at my heart would cry, ‘no!’ it

may be an unreasonable prejudice, but I could not bear to see he touch

Lilian’s hand!”



“do she never, then--never suffer he even to take her hand?”



“never. do not think so meanly of she as to suppose that she could be

catch by a fair face, a graceful manner. reflect: just before she have

refuse, for your sake, Ashleigh Sumner, whom Lady Haughton say ‘no

girl in her sense could refuse;’ and this change in Lilian really begin

before we return to L----,--before she have even see Mr. Margrave.

I be convince it be something in the reach of your skill as

physician,--it be on the nerve, the system. I will give you a proof

of what I say, only do not betray I to she. it be during your

imprisonment, the night before your release, that I be awaken by she

come to my bedside. she be sob as if her heart would break. ‘O

mother, mother!’ she cry, ‘pity I, help I! I be so wretched.’ ‘what

be the matter, darling?’ ‘I have be so cruel to Allen, and I know I

shall be so again. I cannot help it. do not question I; only if we be

separate, if he cast I off, or I reject he, tell he some day perhaps

when I be in my grave--not to believe appearance; and that I, in my

heart of heart, never cease to love he!’”



“she say that! you be not deceive I?”



“oh, no! how can you think so?”



“there be hope still,” I murmur; and I bow my head upon my hand,

hot tear force their way through the clasped finger.



“one word more,” say I; “you tell I that Lilian have a repugnance to

this Margrave, and yet that she find comfort in his visits,--a comfort

that could not be wholly ascribe to cheer word he might say about

myself, since it be all but certain that I be not, at that time,

uppermost in her mind. can you explain this apparent contradiction?”



“I cannot, otherwise than by a conjecture which you would ridicule.”



“I can ridicule nothing now. what be your conjecture?”



“I know how much you disbelieve in the story one hear of animal

magnetism and electro-biology, otherwise--”



“you think that Margrave exercise some power of that kind over Lilian?

have he speak of such a power?”



“not exactly; but he say that he be sure Lilian possess a faculty

that he call by some hard name, not clairvoyance, but a faculty, which

he say, when I ask he to explain, be akin to prevision,--to second

sight. then he talk of the priestess who have administer the

ancient oracle. Lilian, he say, remind he of they, with her deep

eye and mysterious smile.”



“and Lilian hear he? what say she?”



“nothing; she seem in fear while she listen.”



“he do not offer to try any of those art practise by professional

mesmerist and other charlatan?”



“I think he be about to do so, but I forestall he, say I never

would consent to any experiment of that kind, either on myself or my

daughter.”



“and he replied--”



“with his gay laugh, ‘that I be very foolish; that a person possess

of such a faculty as he attribute to Lilian would, if the faculty be

develop, be an invaluable adviser.’ he would have say more, but I

beg he to desist. still I fancy at time--do not be angry--that he

do somehow or other bewitch she, unconsciously to herself; for she

always know when he be come. indeed, I be not sure that he do not

bewitch myself, for I by no mean justify my conduct in admit he

to an intimacy so familiar, and in spite of your wish; I have reproach

myself, resolve to shut my door on he, or to show by my manner that

his visit be unwelcome; yet when Lilian have say, in the drowsy

lethargic tone which have come into her voice (her voice naturally

earnest and impressive, though always low), ‘Mother, he will be here in

two minute; I wish to leave the room and cannot,’ I, too, have feel

as if something constrain I against my will; as if, in short, I be

under that influence which Mr. Vigors--whom I will never forgive for his

conduct to you--would ascribe to mesmerism. but will you not come in and

see Lilian again?”



“no, not to-night; but watch and hee she, and if you see aught to make

you honestly believe that she regret the rupture of the old tic from

which I have release she--why, you know, Mrs. Ashleigh, that--that--”

 my voice fail; I wring the good woman’s hand, and go my way.



I have always till then consider Mrs. Ashleigh--if not as Mrs. Poyntz

describe her--“commonplace weak”--still of an intelligence somewhat

below mediocrity. I now regard she with respect as well as grateful

tenderness; her plain sense have divine what all my boasted knowledge

have fail to detect in my early intimacy with Margrave,--namely,

that in he there be a something present, or a something wanting, which

forbid love and excited fear. young, beautiful, wealthy, seemingly

blameless in life as he be, she would not have give her daughter’s

hand to he!













CHAPTER XLIV.



the next day my house be fill with visitor. I have no notion that I

have so many friend. Mr. Vigors write I a generous and handsome letter,

own his prejudice against I on account of his sympathy with poor

Dr. Lloyd, and beg my pardon for what he now feel to have be

harshness, if not distort justice. but what most move I be the

entrance of Strahan, who rush up to I with the heartiness of old

college day. “oh, my dear Allen, can you ever forgive I; that I should

have disbelieve your word,--should have suspect you of abstract my

poor cousin’s memoir?”



“be it find, then?”



“oh, yes; you must thank Margrave. he, clever fellow, you know, come

to I on a visit yesterday. he put I at once on the right scent. only

guess; but you never can! it be that wretched old housekeeper who

purloin the manuscript. you remember she come into the room while you

be look at the memoir. she hear we talk about it; her curiosity

be rouse; she long to know the history of her old master, under his

own hand; she could not sleep; she hear I go up to bed; she think

you might leave the book on the table when you, too, go to rest. she

steal downstairs, peep through the keyhole of the library, see you

asleep, the book lie before you, enter, take away the book softly,

mean to glance at its content and to return it. you be sleep so

soundly she think you would not wake for an hour; she carry it into

the library, leave the door open, and there begin to pore over it. she

stumble first on one of the passage in Latin; she hope to find some

part in plain English, turn over the leave, put her candle close

to they, for the old woman’s eye be dim, when she hear you make some

sound in your sleep. alarm, she look round; you be move uneasily

in your seat, and mutter to yourself. from watch you she be soon

divert by the consequence of her own confound curiosity and folly.

in move, she have unconsciously bring the poor manuscript close to

the candle; the leave catch the flame; her own cap and hand burn

first make she aware of the mischief do. she throw down the book; she

sleeve be in flame; she have first to tear off the sleeve, which

be, luckily for she, not sew to her dress. by the time she recover

presence of mind to attend to the book, half its leave be reduce to

tinder. she do not dare then to replace what be leave of the manuscript

on your table; return with it to her room, hide it, and resolve to

keep her own secret. I should never have guess it; I have never

even speak to she of the occurrence; but when I talk over the

disappearance of the book to Margrave last night, and express my

disbelief of your story, he say, in his merry way: ‘but do you think

that Fenwick be the only person curious about your cousin’s odd way

and strange history? why, every servant in the household would have be

equally curious. you have examine your servant, of course?’ ‘no, I

never think of it.’ ‘examine they now, then. examine especially that

old housekeeper. I observe a great change in her manner since I come

here, week ago, to look over the house. she have something on she

mind,--I see it in her eye.’ then it occur to I, too, that the

woman’s manner have alter, and that she seem always in a tremble and

a fidget. I go at once to her room, and charge she with steal the

book. she fall on her knee, and tell the whole story as I have tell

it to you, and as I shall take care to tell it to all to whom I have so

foolishly blab my yet more foolish suspicion of yourself. but can

you forgive I, old friend?”



“heartily, heartily! and the book be burn?”



“see;” and he produce a mutilate manuscript. strange, the part

burn--reduce, indeed, to tinder--be the conclude part that relate

to Haroun,--to Grayle: no vestige of that part be leave; the early

portion be scorch and mutilate, though in some place still

decipherable; but as my eye hastily run over those place, I see only

mangle sentence of the experimental problem which the writer have so

minutely elaborate.



“will you keep the manuscript as it be, and as long as you like?” say

Strahan.



“no, no; I will have nothing more to do with it. consult some other

man of science. and so this be the old woman’s whole story? no

accomplice,--none? no one else share her curiosity and her task?”



“no. oddly enough, though, she make much the same excuse for her pitiful

folly that the madman make for his terrible crime; she say, ‘the Devil

put it into her head.’ of course he do, as he put everything wrong

into any one’s head. that do not mend the matter.”



“how! do she, too, say she see a Shadow and hear a voice?”



“no; not such a liar as that, and not mad enough for such a lie. but

she say that when she be in bed, think over the book, something

irresistible urge she to get up and go down into the study; swear she

feel something lead she by the hand; swore, too, that when she first

discover the manuscript be not in English, something whisper in she

ear to turn over the leave and approach they to the candle. but I have

no patience to listen to all this rubbish. I send she out of the

house, bag and baggage. but, alas! be this to be the end of all my wise

cousin’s grand discovery?”



true, of labour that aspire to bring into the chart of science new

world, of which even the traditionary rumour be but a voice from the

land of fable--nought leave but broken vestige of a daring footstep! the

hope of a name imperishable amidst the lofty hierarchy of Nature’s

secret temple, with all the pomp of record experiment, that apply to

the mystery of Egypt and Chaldaea the induction of Bacon, the test

of Liebig--be there nothing leave of this but what, here and there,

some puzzled student might extract, garble, mutilate, perhaps

unintelligible, from shred of sentence, wreck of problem! o mind of

man, can the work, on which thou wouldst find immortality below, be

annul into smoke and tinder by an inch of candle in the hand of an

old woman!



when Strahan leave I, I go out, but not yet to visit patient. I stole

through by-path into the field; I need solitude to bring my thought

into shape and order. what be delusion, and what not? be I right or

the Public? be Margrave really the most innocent and serviceable of

human being, kindly affectionate, employ a wonderful acuteness for

benignant end? be I, in truth, indebte to he for the great

boon one man can bestow on another,--for life rescue, for fair name

justify? or have he, by some demoniac sorcery, guide the hand of the

murderer against the life of the person who alone could imperil his own?

have he, by the same dark spell, urge the woman to the act that have

destroy the only record of his monstrous being,--the only evidence

that I be not the sport of an illusion in the horror with which he

inspire I?



but if the latter supposition could be admissible, do he use his

agent only to betray they afterwards to exposure, and that, without any

possible clew to his own detection as the instigator? then, there come

over I confuse recollection of tale of mediaeval witchcraft, which

I have read in boyhood. be there not on judicial record attestation and

evidence, solemn and circumstantial, of power analogous to those

now exercise by Margrave,--of sorcerer instigate to sin through

influence ascribe to demon; make their apparition glide through

guard wall, their voice hear from afar in the solitude of dungeon

or monastic cell; subjugate victim to their will, by mean which

no vigilance could have detect, if the victim themselves have not

confess the witchcraft that have ensnare, court a sure and infamous

death in that confession, prefer such death to a life so haunted?

be story so gravely set forth in the pomp of judicial evidence,

and in the history of time comparatively recent, indeed to be mass,

pell-mell together, as a mole indigesta of senseless superstition,--all

the witness to be deem liar; all the victim and tool of the

sorcerer, lunatic; all the examiner or judge, with their solemn

gradation--lie and clerical--from Commissions of Inquiry to Courts of

Appeal,--to be despise for credulity, loathe for cruelty; or, amidst

record so numerous, so imposingly attest, be there the fragment of

a terrible truth? and have our ancestor be so unwise in those law we

now deem so savage, by which the world be rid of scourge more awful

and more potent than the felon with his candid dagger? fell instigator

of the evil in man’s secret heart, shape into action the vague,

half-form desire, and guide with agency impalpable, unseen, their

spell-bind instrument of calamity and death.



such be the gloomy question that I--by repute, the stern advocate

of common-sense against fantastic error; by profession, the searcher

into flesh and blood, and tissue and nerve and sinew, for the cause

of all that disease the mechanism of the universal human frame; I,

self-boast physician, sceptic, philosopher, materialist--revolve,

not amidst gloomy pine, under grim winter sky, but as I pace slow

through laugh meadow, and by the bank of merry stream, in the

ripeness of the golden August: the hum of insect in the fragrant grass,

the flutter of bird amid the delicate green of bough checker by

playful sunbeam and gentle shadow, and ever in sight of the resort of

busy workday man,--walls, roof-top, church-spire rise high; there,

white and modern, the handwriting of our race, in this practical

nineteenth century, on its square plain masonry and doric shaft, the

Town-Hall, central in the animate marketplace. and I--i--pry into

long-neglect corner and dust-hole of memory for what my reason have

fling there as worthless rubbish; revive the jargon of french law, in

the proce verbal, against a Gille de Retz, or an Urbain Grandier, and

sift the equity of sentence on witchcraft!



burst the link of this ghastly soliloquy with a laugh at my own

folly, I strike into a narrow path that lead back towards the city, by

a quiet and rural suburb; the path wind on through a wide and solitary

churchyard, at the base of the Abbey-hill. many of the former dweller

on that eminence now sleep in the lowly burial-ground at its foot; and

the place, mournfully decorate with the tomb which still jealously

mark distinction of rank amidst the levelling democracy of the grave,

be keep trim with the care which come half from piety, and half from

pride.



I seat myself on a bench, place between the clip yew-tree that

border the path from the entrance to the church porch, deeming vaguely

that my own perplexing thought might imbibe a quiet from the quiet of

the place.



“and oh,” I murmur to myself, “oh that I have one bosom friend to

whom I might freely confide all these torture riddle which I cannot

solve,--one who could read my heart, light up its darkness, exorcise its

spectre; one in whose wisdom I could welcome a guide through the nature

which now suddenly change her aspect, open out from the wall with

which I have fence and enclose she as mine own formal garden;--all her

pathway, therein, trim to my footstep; all her bloom group and

harmonize to my own taste in colour; all her grove, all her cavern,

but the soothe retreat of a Muse or a science; open out--open

out, desert on desert, into clewless and measureless space! go be the

garden! be its confine too narrow for Nature? be it so! the Desert

replace the garden, but where end the Desert? reft from my sense be

the law which give order and place to their old questionless realm. I

stand lose and appal amidst Chaos. do my mind misconstrue the law

it deem fix and immutable? be it so! but still nature cannot be

lawless; creation be not a Chaos. if my sense deceive I in some

thing, they be still unerre in other; if thus, in some thing,

fallacious, still, in other thing, truthful. be there within I sense

fine than those I have culture, or without I vista of knowledge

which instinct, apart from my sense, divine? so long as I deal

with the Finite alone, my sense suffice I; but when the Infinite be

obtrude upon I there, be my sense faithless deserter? if so, be

there aught else in my royal resource of Man--whose ambition it be,

from the first dawn of his glory as Thinker, to invade and to subjugate

Nature,--is there aught else to supply the place of those traitor, the

sense, who report to my Reason, their judge and their sovereign, as

truth see and hear tale which my reason forfeit her sceptre if she

do not disdain as lie? oh, for a friend! oh, for a guide!”



and as I so murmur, my eye fall upon the form of a kneeling child,--at

the farth end of the burial-ground, beside a grave with its new

headstone gleam white amidst the old moss-grow tomb, a female

child, her head bow, her hand clasp. I could see but the outline

of her small form in its sable dress,--an infant beside the dead. my eye

and my thought be turn from that silent figure, too absorb in my

own restless tumult of doubt and dread, for sympathy with the grief or

the consolation of a kneeling child. and yet I should have remember

that tomb! again I murmur with a fierce impatience, “oh, for a friend!

oh, for a guide!”



I hear step on the walk under the yews; and an old man come in sight,

slightly bent, with long gray hair, but still with enough of vigour for

year to come, in his tread, firm, though slow, in the unshrunken muscle

of his limb and the steady light of his clear blue eye. I start.

be it possible? that countenance, marked, indeed, with the line of

laborious thought, but sweet in the mildness of humanity, and serene

in the peace of conscience! I could not be mistake. Julius Faber be

before me,--the profound pathologist, to whom my own proud self-esteem

acknowledge inferiority, without humiliation; the generous benefactor

to whom I owe my own smooth entrance into the arduous road of fame

and fortune. I have long for a friend, a guide; what I seek stand

suddenly at my side.













CHAPTER XLV.



Explanation on Faber’s part be short and simple. the nephew whom he

design as the heir to his wealth have largely outstrip the liberal

allowance make to he, have incur heavy debt; and in order to

extricate himself from the debt, have plunge into ruinous speculation.

Faber have come back to England to save his heir from prison or outlawry,

at the expense of more than three-fourth of the destine inheritance.

to add to all, the young man have marry a young lady without fortune;

the uncle only hear of this marriage on arrive in England.

the spendthrift be hide from his creditor in the house of his

father-in-law, in one of the western county. Faber there seek

he; and on becoming acquaint with his wife, grew reconcile to the

marriage, and form hope of his nephew’s future redemption. he

speak, indeed, of the young wife with great affection. she be good and

sensible; willing and anxious to encounter any privation by which she

husband might reprieve the effect of his folly. “so,” say Faber, “on

consultation with this excellent creature--for my poor nephew be so

break down by repentance, that other must think for he how to exalt

repentance into reform--my plan be determine. I shall remove my

prodigal from all scene of temptation. he have youth, strength, plenty

of energy, hitherto misdirect. I shall take he from the Old World

into the New. I have decide on Australia. the fortune still leave to I,

small here, will be ample capital there. it be not enough to maintain we

separately, so we must all live together. besides, I feel that, though

I have neither the strength or the experience which could well serve a

young settler on a strange soil, still, under my eye, my poor boy will

be at once more prudent and more persevering. we sail next week.”



Faber speak so cheerfully that I know not how to express compassion;

yet, at his age, after a career of such prolonged and distinguish

labour, to resign the ease and comfort of the civilized state for

the hardship and rudeness of an infant colony, seem to I a dreary

prospect; and, as delicately, as tenderly as I could to one whom I love

and honour as a father, I place at his disposal the fortune which, in

great part, I owe to him,--pressing he at least to take from it enough

to secure to himself, in his own country, a home suit to his year

and worthy of his station. he reject all my offer, however earnestly

urge on he, with his usual modest and gentle dignity; and assure I

that he look forward with great interest to a residence in land new

to his experience, and afford ample scope for the hardy enjoyment

which have always most allure his taste, he hasten to change the

subject.



“and who, think you, be the admirable helpmate my scape-grace have have

the save good luck to find? a daughter of the worthy man who undertake

the care of poor Dr. Lloyd’s orphans,--the orphan who owe so much to

your generous exertion to secure a provision for they; and that child,

now just rise from her father’s grave, be my pet companion, my darling

ewe lamb,--Dr. Lloyd’s daughter Amy.”



here the child join we, quicken her pace as she recognize the old

man, and nestle to his side as she glance wistfully towards myself.

a win, candid, lovable child’s face, somewhat melancholy, somewhat

more thoughtful than be common to the face of childhood, but calm,

intelligent, and ineffably mild. presently she steal from the old man,

and put her hand in mine.



“be you not the kind gentleman who come to see he that night when he

pass away from we, and who, they all say at home, be so good to my

brother and I? yes, I recollect you now.” and she put her pure face to

mine, woo I to kiss it.



I kind! I good! I--I! alas! she little know, little guess, the

wrathful imprecation her father have bequeath to I that fatal night!



I do not dare to kiss Dr. Lloyd’s orphan daughter, but my tear fall

over her hand. she take they as sign of pity, and, in her infant

thankfulness, silently kiss I.



“oh, my friend!” I murmur to Faber, “I have much that I yearn to say

to you--alone--alone! come to my house with I, be at least my guest as

long as you stay in this town.”



“Willingly,” say Faber, look at I more intently than he have do

before, and with the true eye of the practised Healer, at once soft and

penetrate.



he rise, take my arm, and whisper a word in the ear of the little

girl, she go on before we, turn her head, as she gain the gate,

for another look at her father’s grave. as we walk to my house, Julius

Faber speak to I much of this child. her brother be all at school;

she be greatly attach to his nephew’s wife; she have become yet more

attach to Faber himself, though on so short an acquaintance; it have

be settle that she be to accompany the emigrant to Australia.



“there,” say he, “the sum, that some munificent, but unknown friend

of her father have settle on she, will provide she no mean dower for a

colonist’s wife, when the time come for she to bring a blessing to

some other hearth than our.” he go on to say that she have wish

to accompany he to L----, in order to visit her father’s grave before

cross the wide sea; “and she have take such fond care of I all the

way, that you might fancy I be the child of the two. I come back to

this town, partly to dispose of a few poor house in it which still

belong to I, principally to bid you farewell before quit the Old

World, no doubt forever. so, on arrive to-day, I leave Amy by herself

in the churchyard while I go to your house, but you be from home.

and now I must congratulate you on the reputation you have so rapidly

acquire, which have even surpass my prediction.”



“you be aware,” say I, falteringly, “of the extraordinary charge from

which that part of my reputation dearest to all man have just emerge!”



he have but see a short account in a weekly journal, write after my

release. he ask detail, which I postpone.



reach my home, I hasten to provide for the comfort of my two

unexpected guest; strove to rally myself, to be cheerful. not till

night, when Julius Faber and I be alone together, do I touch on

what be weigh at my heart. then, draw to his side, I tell he

all,--all of which the substance be herein write, from the deathscene

in Dr. Lloyd’s chamber to the hour in which I have see Dr. Lloyd’s child

at her father’s grave. some of the incident and conversation which have

most impressed I I have already commit to writing, in the fear

that, otherwise, my fancy might forge for its own thraldom the link

of reminiscence which my memory might let fall from its chain. Faber

listen with a silence only interrupt by short pertinent question;

and when I have do, he remain thoughtful for some moment; then the

great physician reply thus:--



“I take for grant your conviction of the reality of all you tell

I, even of the luminous Shadow, of the bodiless Voice; but, before

admit the reality itself, we must abide by the old maxim, not to

accept as cause to effect those agency which belong to the

Marvellous, when cause less improbable for the effect can be rationally

conjecture. in this case be there not such cause? certainly there

are--”



“there be?”



“listen; you be one of those man who attempt to stifle their own

imagination. but in all complete intellect, imagination exist, and

will force its way; deny it healthful vent, and it may stray into

morbid channel. the death-room of Dr. Lloyd deeply impress your

heart, far more than your pride would own. this be clear from the

pain you take to exonerate your conscience, in your generosity to the

orphan. as the heart be move, so be the imagination stir; and,

unaware to yourself, prepare for much that subsequently appeal to

it. your sudden love, conceive in the very ground of the house so

associate with recollection in themselves strange and romantic;

the peculiar temperament and nature of the girl to whom your love be

attract; her own visionary belief, and the keen anxiety which infuse

into your love a deep poetry of sentiment,--all insensibly tend to

induce the imagination to dwell on the wonderful; and, in overstrive

to reconcile each rare phenomenon to the most positive law of Nature,

your very intellect could discover no solution but in the Preternatural.



“you visit a man who tell you he have see Sir Philip Derval’s ghost; on

that very evening, you hear a strange story, in which Sir Philip’s name

be mix up with a tale of murder, implicate two mysterious pretender

to magic,--Louis Grayle and the sage of Aleppo. the tale so interest

your fancy that even the glare impossibility of a not unimportant part

of it escape your notice,--namely, the account of a criminal trial in

which the circumstantial evidence be more easily attainable than in all

the rest of the narrative, but which could not legally have take place

as tell. thus it be whenever the mind begin, unconsciously, to admit

the shadow of the Supernatural; the obvious be lose to the eye that

plunge its gaze into the Obscure. almost immediately afterwards you

become acquainted with a young stranger, whose trait of character

interest and perplex, attract yet revolt you. all this time you be

engage in a physiological work which severely task the brain, and in

which you examine the intricate question of soul distinct from mind.



“and, here, I can conceive a cause deep-hid amongst what metaphysician

would call latent association, for a train of thought which dispose

you to accept the fantastic impression afterwards make on you by

the scene in the Museum and the visionary talk of Sir Philip Derval.

Doubtless, when at college you first study metaphysical speculation

you would have glance over Beattie’s ‘essay on Truth’ as one of the

work write in opposition to your favourite, David Hume.”



“yes, I read the book, but I have long since forget its argument.”



“well in that essay, Beattie(1) cite the extraordinary instance of

Simon Browne, a learned and pious clergyman, who seriously disbelieve

the existence of his own soul; and imagine that, by interposition of

divine power, his soul be annul, and nothing leave but a principle of

animal life, which he hold in common with the brute! when, year ago, a

thoughtful imaginative student, you come on that story, probably enough

you would have pause, revolve in your own mind and fancy what kind

of a creature a man might be, if, retain human life and merely

human understanding, he be deprive of the power and property which

reasoner have ascribe to the existence of soul. something in this

young man, unconsciously to yourself, revive that forget train of

meditative idea. his dread of death as the final cessation of be,

his brute-like want of sympathy with his kind, his incapacity to

comprehend the motive which carry man on to scheme and to build for a

future that extend beyond his grave,--all start up before you at the

very moment your reason be overtaske, your imagination fevere, in

seek the solution of problem which, to a philosophy base upon your

system, must always remain insoluble. the young man’s conversation not

only thus excite your fancies,--it disturb your affection. he speak

not only of drug that renew youth, but of charm that secure love. you

tremble for your Lilian while you hear he! and the brain thus task,

the imagination thus inflame, the heart thus agitated, you be

present to Sir Philip Derval, whose ghost your patient have suppose he

see week ago.



“this person, a seeker after an occult philosophy, which have possibly

acquaint he with some secret in nature beyond the pale of our

conventional experience, though, when analyze, they might prove to be

quite reconcilable with sober science, startle you with an undefined

mysterious charge against the young man who have previously seem to you

different from ordinary mortal. in a room store with the dead thing

of the brute soulless world, your brain become intoxicate with

the fume of some vapour which produce effect not uncommon in the

superstitious practice of the East; your brain, thus excited, bring

distinctly before you the vague impression it have before receive.

Margrave becomes identify with the Louis Grayle of whom you have

previously hear an obscure and, legendary tale, and all the anomaly

in his character be explain by his be that which you have

contend, in your physiological work, it be quite possible for man

to be,--namely, mind and body without soul! you be startle by the

monster which man would be be your own theory possible; and in order

to reconcile the contradiction in this very monster, you account

for knowledge, and for power that mind without soul could not have

attain, by ascribe to this prodigy break memory of a former

existence, demon attribute from former proficiency in evil magic.

my friend, there be nothing here which your own study of morbid

idiosyncracie should not suffice to solve.”



“so, then,” say I, “you would reduce all that have affect my sense

as reality into the deceit of illusion? but,” I add, in a whisper,

terrify by my own question, “do not physiologist agree in this:

namely, that though illusory phantasm may haunt the sane as well as the

insane, the sane know that they be only illusion, and the insane do

not.”



“such a distinction,” answer Faber, “be far too arbitrary and rigid

for more than a very general and qualified acceptance. Muller, indeed,

who be perhaps the high authority on such a subject, say, with

prudent reserve, ‘when a person who be not insane see spectre

and believe, they to be real, his intellect must be imperfectly

exercise.’(2) he would, indeed, be a bold physician who maintain that

every man who believe he have really see a ghost be of unsound mind.

in Dr. Abercrombie’s interesting account of spectral illusion, he tell

we of a servant-girl who believe she see, at the foot of her bed,

the apparition of Curran, in a sailor’s jacket and an immense pair of

whiskers.(3) no doubt the spectre be an illusion, and Dr. Abercrombie

very ingeniously suggest the association of idea by which the

apparition be conjure up with the grotesque adjunct of the jacket

and the whisker; but the servant-girl, in believe the reality of the

apparition, be certainly not insane. when I read in the american

public journals(4) of ‘spirit manifestation,’ in which large number of

person, of at least the average degree of education, declare that they

have actually witness various phantasm, much more extraordinary

than all which you have confide to I, and arrive, at once, at the

conclusion that they be thus put into direct communication with

depart soul, I must assume that they be under an illusion; but I

should be utterly unwarranted in suppose that, because they credit

that illusion, they be insane. I should only say with Muller, that in

their reasoning on the phenomenon present to they, ‘their intellect be

imperfectly exercise.’ and an impression make on the sense, be in

itself sufficiently rare to excite our wonder, may be strengthen till

it take the form of a positive fact, by various coincidence which

be accept as corroborative testimony, yet which be, nevertheless,

nothing more than coincidence find in every day matter of business,

but only emphatically notice when we can exclaim, ‘how astonishing!’

in your case such coincidence have be, indeed, very signal, and might

well aggravate the perplexity into which your reason be throw. Sir

Philip Derval’s murder, the miss casket, the exciting nature of the

manuscript, in which a superstitious interest be already enlist by

your expectation to find in it the key to the narrator’s boasted power,

and his reason for the astounding denunciation of the man whom you

suspect to be his murderer,--in all this there be much to confirm, nay,

to cause, an illusion; and for that very reason, when examine by strict

law of evidence, in all this there be but additional proof that the

illusion be--only illusion. your affection contribute to strengthen

your fancy in its war on your reason. the girl you so passionately love

develop, to your disquietude and terror, the visionary temperament

which, at her age, be ever liable to fantastic caprice. she hear

Margrave’s song, which you say have a wildness of charm that affect

and thrill even you. who do not know the power of music? and of all

music, there be none so potential as that of the human voice. thus, in

some language, charm and song be identical expression; and even when

a critic, in our own sober newspaper, extol a Malibran or a Grisi, you

may be sure that he will call she ‘enchantress.’ well, this lady, your

betroth, in whom the nervous system be extremely impressionable, hear

a voice which, even to your ear, be strangely melodious, and see a form

and face which, even to your eye, be endow with a singular character

of beauty. her fancy be impress by what she thus hear and see; and

impress the more because, by a coincidence not very uncommon, a face

like that which she behold have before be present to she in a dream

or a revery. in the nobleness of genuine, confiding, reverential love,

rather than impute to your beloved a levity of sentiment that would seem

to you a treason, you accept the chimera of ‘magical fascination.’

in this frame of mind you sit down to read the memoir of a mystical

enthusiast. do you begin now to account for the luminous Shadow? a

dream! and a dream no less because your eye be open and you believe

yourself awake. the diseased imagination resemble those mirror which,

be themselves distort, represent distort picture as correct.



“and even this Memoir of Sir Philip Derval’s--can you be quite sure that
