you actually read the part which relate to Haroun and Louis Grayle? you

say that, while peruse the manuscript, you see the luminous Shadow,

and become insensible. the old woman say you be fast asleep. may you

not really have fall into a slumber, and in that slumber have dream

the part of the tale that relate to Grayle,--dreamed that you beheld

the Shadow? do you remember what be say so well by Dr. Abercrombie,

to authorize the explanation I suggest to you: ‘a person under the

influence of some strong mental impression fall asleep for a few

second, perhaps without be sensible of it: some scene or person

appear in a dream, and he start up under the conviction that it be a

spectral appearance.’” (5)



“but,” say I, “the apparition be see by I again, and when,

certainly, I be not sleep.”



“true; and who should know well than a physician so well read as

yourself that a spectral illusion once beheld be always apt to return

again in the same form? thus, Goethe be long haunt by one image,--the

phantom of a flower unfold itself, and develop new flowers.(6)

thus, one of our most distinguished philosopher tell we of a lady

know to himself, who would see her husband, hear he move and speak,

when he be not even in the house.(7) but instance of the facility with

which phantasm, once admit, repeat themselves to the sense, be

numberless. many be record by Hibbert and Abercrombie, and

every physician in extensive practice can add largely, from his own

experience, to the list. intense self-concentration be, in itself, a

mighty magician. the magician of the East inculcate the necessity

of fast, solitude, and meditation for the due development of their

imaginary power. and I have no doubt with effect; because fast,

solitude, and meditation--in other word, thought or fancy intensely

concentre--will both raise apparition and produce the invoker’s belief

in they. Spinello, strive to conceive the image of Lucifer for his

picture of the Fallen Angels, be at last actually haunt by the Shadow

of the Fiend. Newton himself have be subject to a phantom, though

to he, Son of Light, the spectre present be that of the sun! you

remember the account that Newton give to Locke of this visionary

appearance. he say that ‘though he have look at the sun with his

right eye only, and not with the left, yet his fancy begin to make an

impression upon his left eye as well as his right; for if he shut his

right and look upon the cloud, or a book, or any bright object with

his left eye, he could see the sun almost as plain as with the right, if

he do but intend his fancy a little while on it;’ nay, ‘for some month

after, as often as he begin to meditate on the phenomena, the spectrum

of the sun begin to return, even though he lie in bed at midnight, with

his curtain draw!’ seeing, then, how any vivid impression once make

will recur, what wonder that you should behold in your prison the

shine Shadow that have first startle you in a wizard’s chamber when

pore over the record of a murder visionary? the more minutely

you analyze your own hallucination--pardon I the word--the more they

assume the usual characteristic of a dream; contradictory, illogical,

even in the marvel they represent. can any two person be more totally

unlike each other, not merely as to form and year, but as to all the

element of character, than the Grayle of whom you read, or believe

you read, and the Margrave in whom you evidently think that Grayle be

existent still? the one represent, you say, as gloomy, saturnine, with

vehement passion, but with an original grandeur of thought and will,

consume by an internal remorse; the other you paint to I as a joyous

and wayward darling of Nature, acute yet frivolous, free from even

the ordinary passion of youth, take delight in innocent amusement,

incapable of continuous study, without a single pang of repentance

for the crime you so fancifully impute to he. and now, when your

suspicion, so romantically conceive, be dispel by positive fact,

now, when it be clear that Margrave neither murder Sir Philip Derval

nor abstract the memoir, you still, unconsciously to yourself, draw

on your imagination in order to excuse the suspicion your pride of

intellect decline to banish, and suppose that this youthful sorcerer

tempt the madman to the murder, the woman to the theft--”



“but you forget the madman say ‘that he be lead on by the luminous

Shadow of a beautiful youth,’ that the woman say also that she be

impel by some mysterious agency.”



“I do not forget those coincidence; but how your learning would dismiss

they as nugatory be your imagination not dispose to exaggerate they!

when you read the authentic history of any popular illusion, such

as the spurious inspiration of the Jansenist Convulsionaries, the

apparition that invade convent, as depose in the trial of Urbain

Grandier, the confession of witch and wizard in place the

most remote from each other, or, at this day, the tale of

‘spirit-manifestation’ record in half the town and village of

America,--do not all the superstitious impression of a particular time

have a common family likeness? what one see, another see, though there

have be no communication between the two. I cannot tell you why these

phantasm thus partake of the nature of an atmospheric epidemic; the

fact remain incontestable. and strange as may be the coincidence

between your impression of a mystic agency and those of some other

brain not cognizant of the chimera of your own, still, be it not

simple philosophy to say, ‘they be coincidence of the same nature

which make witch in the same epoch all tell much the same story of

the broomstick they ride and the sabbat at which they dance to the

fiend’s piping,’ and there leave the matter, as in science we must leave

many of the most elementary and familiar phenomenon inexplicable as to

their causes,--is not this, I say, more philosophical than to insist

upon an explanation which accept the supernatural rather than leave the

extraordinary unaccounted for?”



“as you speak,” say I, rest my downcast face upon my hand, “I should

speak to any patient who have confide to I the tale I have tell to

you.”



“and yet the explanation do not wholly satisfy you? very likely: to

some phenomenon there be, as yet, no explanation. perhaps Newton himself

could not explain quite to his own satisfaction why he be haunt at

midnight by the spectrum of a sun; though I have no doubt that some

later philosopher whose ingenuity have be stimulate by Newton’s

account, have, by this time, suggest a rational solution of

that enigma.(8) to return to your own case. I have offer such

interpretation of the mystery that confound you as appear to I

authorize by physiological science. should you adduce other fact which

physiological science want the datum to resolve into phenomenon always

natural, however rare, still hold fast to that simple saying of Goethe:

‘mystery be not necessarily miracle.’ and if all which physiological

science comprehend in its experience wholly fail we, I may then

hazard certain conjecture in which, by acknowledge ignorance, one be

compel to recognize the marvellous (for as where knowledge enter,

the marvellous recede, so where knowledge falter, the marvellous

advance); yet still, even in those conjecture, I will distinguish the

Marvellous from the Supernatural. but, for the present, I advise you to

accept the guess that may well quiet the fevere imagination which any

bold guess would only more excite.”



“you be right,” say I, rise proudly to the full height of my

stature, my head erect and my heart defy. “and so let this subject

be renew no more between we. I will brood over it no more myself.

I regain the unclouded realm of my human intelligence; and, in that

intelligence, I mock the sorcerer and disdain the spectre.”



(1) Beattie’s “Essay on Truth,” part i. c. ii. 3. the story of Simon

Browne be to be find in “The Adventurer.”



(2) Miller’s Physiology of the Senses, p. 394.



(3) abercrombie on the Intellectual Powers, p. 281. (15th edition.)



(4) at the date of Faber’s conversation with Allen Fenwick, the

(so-call) spirit manifestation have not spread from America over

Europe. but if they have, Faber’s view would, no doubt, have remain

the same.



(5) Abercrombie on the Intellectual Powers, p. 278. (15th edition.)



this author, not more to be admire for his intelligence than his

candour, and who be entitle to praise for a high degree of original

thought than that to which he modestly pretend, relate a curious

anecdote illustrate “the analogy between dream and spectral

illusion, which he receive from the gentleman to which it occurred,--an

eminent medical friend:” “having sit up late one evening, under

considerable anxiety for one of his child, who be ill, he fall

asleep in his chair, and have a frightful dream, in which the prominent

figure be an immense baboon. he awake with the fright, get up

instantly, and walk to a table which be in the middle of the room.

he be then quite awake, and quite conscious of the article around he;

but close by the wall in the end of the apartment he distinctly see the

baboon make the same grimace which he have see in his dream; and

this spectre continue visible for about half a minute.” now, a man

who see only a baboon would be quite ready to admit that it be but an

optical illusion; but if, instead of a baboon, he have see an intimate

friend, and that friend, by some coincidence of time, have die about

that date, he would be a very strong-minded man if he admit for the

mystery of see his friend the same natural solution which he would

readily admit for see a baboon.



(6) see Muller’s observation on this phenomenon, “physiology of the

sense,” Baley’s translation, p. 1395.



(7) Sir David Brewster’s Letters on Natural Magic, p. 39.



(8) Newton’s explanation be as follow: “this story I tell you to let

you understand, that in the observation relate by Mr. Boyle, the man’s

fancy probably concur with the impression make by the sun’s light

to produce that phantasm of the sun which he constantly see in bright

object, and so your question about the cause of this phantasm involve

another about the power of the fancy, which I must confess be too hard a

knot for I to untie. to place this effect in a constant motion be hard,

because the sun ought then to appear perpetually. it seem rather

to consist in a disposition of the sensorium to move the imagination

strongly, and to be easily move both by the imagination and by the

light as often as bright object be look upon.”--Letter from Sir I.

Newton to Locke, Lord Kinq’s Life of Locke, vol. i. pp. 405-408.



Dr. Roget (Animal and Vegetable Physiology consider with reference to

Natural theology, “Bridgewater Treatise,” pp. 524, 525) thus refer to

this phenomenon, which he state “all of we may experience “:--



“when the impression be very vivid” (Dr. Roget be speak of visual

impression), “another phenomenon often take place,--namely, their

subsequent recurrence after a certain interval, during which they be

not feel, and quite independently of any renew application of the

cause which have originally excite they.” (I mark by italic the word

which more precisely coincide with Julius Faber’s explanation.) “if,

for example, we look steadfastly at the sun for a second or two, and

then immediately close our eye, the image, or spectrum, of the sun

remain for a long time present to the mind, as if the light be still

act on the retina. it then gradually fade and disappear; but if

we continue to keep the eye shut, the same impression will, after

a certain time, recur, and again vanish: and this phenomenon will be

repeat at interval, the sensation become fainter at each renewal.

it be probable that these reappearance of the image, after the

light which produce the original impression have be withdraw, be

occasion by spontaneous affection of the retina itself which be

convey to the sensorium. in other case, where the impression be

less strong, the physical change produce these change be perhaps

confine to the sensorium.”



it may be say that there be this difference between the spectrum of the

sun and such a phantom as that which perplex Allen Fenwick,--namely,

that the sun have be actually beheld before its visionary appearance

can be reproduce, and that Allen Fenwick only imago he have see the

apparition which repeat itself to his fancy. “but there be ground for

the suspicion” (say Dr. Hibbert, “philosophy of Apparitions,” p. 250),

“that when idea of vision be vivified to the height of sensation, a

corresponding affection of the optic nerve accompany the illusion.”

 Muller (“physiology of the sense,” p. 1392, Baley’s translation) state

the same opinion still more strongly; and Sir David Brewster, quote

by Dr. Hibbert (p. 251) say: “in examine these mental impression, I

have find that they follow the motion of the eyeball exactly like the

spectral impression of luminous object, and that they resemble they

also in their apparent immobility when the eye be displace by an

external force. if this result (which I state with much diffidence, from

have only my own experience in its favour) shall be find generally

true by other, it will follow that the object of mental contemplation

may be see as distinctly as external object, and will occupy the same

local position in the axis of vision, as if they have be form by the

agency of light.” hence the impression of an image once convey to the

sense, no matter how, whether by actual or illusory vision, be liable

to renewal, “independently of any renew application of the cause which

have originally excite it,” and the image can be see in that renewal

“as distinctly as external object,” for indeed “the revival of the

fantastic figure really do affect those point of the retina which have

be previously impressed.”













CHAPTER XLVI.



Julius Faber and Amy Lloyd stay in my house three day, I and in their

presence I feel a healthful sense of security and peace. Amy wish to

visit her father’s house, and I ask Faber, in take she there, to

seize the occasion to see Lilian, that he might communicate to I his

impression of a case so peculiar. I prepare Mrs. Ashleigh for this

visit by a previous note. when the old man and the child come back, both

bring I comfort. Amy be charm with Lilian, who have receive she

with the sweetness natural to her real character, and I love to hear

Lilian’s praise from those innocent lip.



Faber’s report be still more calculated to console I.



“I have see, I have converse with her long and familiarly. you be

quite right,--there be no tendency to consumption in that exquisite, if

delicate, organization; nor do I see cause for the fear to which your

statement have pre-inclined I. that head be too nobly form for any

constitutional cerebral infirmity. in its organization, ideality,

wonder, veneration, be large, it be true, but they be balance by

other organ, now perhaps almost dormant, but which will come into

play as life pass from romance into duty. something at this moment

evidently oppress her mind. in converse with she, I observe

abstraction, listlessness; but I be so convinced of her truthfulness,

that if she have once tell you she return your affection, and pledge

to you her faith, I should, in your place, rest perfectly satisfied that

whatever be the cloud that now rest on her imagination, and for the

time obscure the idea of yourself, it will pass away.”



Faber be a believer in the main division of phrenology, though he do

not accept all the dogma of Gall and Spurzheim; while, to my mind,

the refutation of phrenology in its fundamental proposition have be

triumphantly establish by the lucid argument of Sir W. Hamilton.(1)

but when Faber rest on phrenological observation assurance in honour

of Lilian, I forget Sir W. Hamilton, and believe in phrenology. as

iron girder and pillar expand and contract with the mere variation

of temperature, so will the strong conviction on which the human

intellect rest its judgment vary with the change of the human heart;

and the building be only safe where these variation be foresee and

allow for by a wisdom intent on self-knowledge.(2)



there be much in the affection that have spring up between Julius Faber

and Amy Lloyd which touch my heart and soften all its emotion. this

man, unblessed, like myself, by conjugal and parental tie, have, in his

solitary age, turn for solace to the love of a child, as I, in the

pride of manhood, have turn to the love of woman. but his love be

without fear, without jealousy, without trouble. my sunshine come to

I in a fitful ray, through cloud that have gather over my noon; his

sunshine cover all his landscape, hallow and hallow by the calm

of decline day.



and Amy be no common child. she have no exuberant imagination; she be

haunt by no whisper from Afar; she be a creature fit for the

earth,--to accept its duty and to gladden its care. her tender

observation, fine and tranquil, be alive to all the important household

trifle by which, at the early age, man’s allotted soother assert

her privilege to tend and to comfort. it be pleasant to see she

move so noiselessly through the room I have devote to her venerable

protector, know all his simple want, and provide for they as if by

the mechanism of a heart exquisitely mould to the love use of life.

sometimes when I see she set his chair by the window (know, as

I do, how much he habitually love to be near the light) and smooth

his paper (in which he be apt to be unmethodical), place the mark

in his book when he cease to read, divine, almost without his glance,

some wish pass through his mind, and then seat herself at his

foot, often with her work--which be always destine for he or for one

of her absent brothers,--now and then with the one small book that

she have carry with she, a selection of Bible story compile for

children,--sometimes when I see she thus, how I wish that Lilian,

too, could have see she, and have compare her own ideal fantasy with

those young development of the natural heavenly woman!



but be there nothing in that sight from which I, proud of my arid

reason even in its perplexity, might have take lesson for myself?



on the second evening of Faber’s visit I bring to he the draft of

deed for the sale of his property. he have never be a man of business

out of his profession; he be impatient to sell his property, and

disposed to accept an offer at half its value. I insist on take

on myself the task of negotiator; perhaps, too, in this office I be

egotistically anxious to prove to the great physician that which he

believe to be my “hallucination” have in no way obscure my commonsense

in the daily affair of life. so I conclude, and in a few hour,

term for his property that be only just, but be infinitely more

advantageous than have appear to himself to be possible. but as I

approach he with the paper, he put his finger to his lip. Amy be

stand by he with her little book in her hand, and his own Bible lie

open on the table. he be read to she from the Sacred Volume itself,

and impress on she the force and beauty of one of the Parables, the

adaptation of which have perplex she; when he have do, she kiss he,

bid he goodnight, and go away to rest. then say Faber thoughtfully,

and as if to himself more than me,--



“what a lovely bridge between old age and childhood be religion! how

intuitively the child begin with prayer and worship on enter life,

and how intuitively on quit life the old man turn back to prayer

and worship, put himself again side by side with the infant!”



I make no answer, but, after a pause, speak of fine and freehold,

title-deed and money; and when the business on hand be conclude,

ask my learn guest if, before he depart, he would deign to look

over the page of my ambitious Physiological Work. there be part of

it on which I much desire his opinion, touch on subject in which

his special study make he an authority as high as our land possess.



he make I bring he the manuscript, and devote much of that night and

the next day to its perusal.



when he give it I back, which be not till the morning of his

departure, he commence with eulogy on the scope of its design, and

the manner of its execution, which flatter my vanity so much that

I could not help exclaim, “then, at least, there be no trace of

‘hallucination’ here!”



“alas, my poor Allen! here, perhaps, hallucination, or self-deception,

be more apparent than in all the strange tale you confide to I. for

here be the hallucination of the man seat on the shore of Nature,

and who would say to its measureless sea, ‘so far shalt thou go and no

far;’ here be the hallucination of the creature, who, not content

with explore the law of the Creator, end with submit to his

interpretation of some three or four law, in the midst of a code of

which all the rest be in a language unknown to he, the power and

free-will of the Lawgiver himself; here be the hallucination by which

nature be leave Godless, because Man be leave soulless. what would matter

all our speculation on a deity who would cease to exist for we when we

be in the grave? why mete out, like Archytas, the earth and the sea,

and number the sand on the shore that divide they, if the end of this

wisdom be a handful of dust sprinkle over a skull!





   “‘Nec quidquam tibi prod

     Aerias tentasse dorno, animoque rotundum

     Percurrisse polum naorituro.’



“your book be a proof of the soul that you fail to discover. without a

soul, no man would work for a future that begin for his fame when the

breath be go from his body. do you remember how you see that little

child pray at the grave of her father? shall I tell you that in her

simple orison she pray for the benefactor,--who have care for

the orphan; who have rear over dust that tomb which, in a Christian

burial-ground, be a mute but perceptible memorial of christian hope;

that the child pray, haughty man, for you? and you sit by, know

nought of this; sit by, amongst the grave, troubled and torture with

ghastly doubt, vain of a reason that be sceptical of eternity, and yet

shake like a reed by a moment’s marvel. shall I tell the child to pray

for you no more; that you disbelieve in a soul? if you do so, what be

the efficacy of prayer? speak, shall I tell she this? shall the infant

pray for you never more?”



I be silent; I be thrill.



“have it never occur to you, who, in deny all innate perception

as well as idea, have pass on to deduction from which poor Locke,

humble Christian that he be, would have shrink in dismay,--has it never

occur to you as a wonderful fact, that the easy thing in the

world to teach a child be that which seem to metaphysical schoolman the

abstrusest of all problem? read all those philosopher wrangle about

a First Cause, decide on what be miracle, and then again decide

that such miracle cannot be; and when one have answer another, and

leave in the crucible of wisdom a caput mortuum of ignorance, then turn

your eye, and look at the infant pray to the invisible God at his

mother’s knee. this idea, so miraculously abstract, of a Power the

infant have never see, that cannot be symbol forth and explain to

he by the most erudite sage,--a Power, nevertheless, that watch

over he, that hear he, that see he, that will carry he across the

grave, that will enable he to live on forever,--this double mystery

of a divinity and of a Soul, the infant learn with the most facile

readiness, at the first glimpse of his reasoning faculty. before you can

teach he a rule in addition, before you can venture to drill he into

his horn-book, he leap, with one intuitive spring of all his idea,

to the comprehension of the truth which be only incomprehensible to

blunder sage! and you, as you stand before I, dare not say, ‘let

the child pray for I no more!’ but will the Creator accept the child’s

prayer for the man who refuse prayer for himself? take my advice, pray!

and in this counsel I do not overstep my province. I speak not as a

preacher, but as a physician. for health be a word that comprehend

our whole organization, and a just equilibrium of all faculty and

function be the condition of health. as in your Lilian the equilibrium

be derange by the over-indulgence of a spiritual mysticism which

withdraw from the nutriment of duty the essential pabulum of sober

sense, so in you the resolute negation of discipline spiritual

communion between Thought and Divinity rob imagination of its noble

and safest vent. thus, from opposite extreme, you and your lilian meet

in the same region of mist and cloud, lose sight of each other and of

the true end of life, as her eye only gaze on the star and yours only

bend to the earth. be I advise she, I should say: ‘your creator have

place the scene of your trial below, and not in the star.’ advise

you, I say: ‘but in the trial below, man should recognize education for

heaven.’ in a word, I would draw somewhat more downward her fancy,

raise somewhat more upward your reason. take my advice then,--Pray.

your mental system need the support of prayer in order to preserve its

balance. in the embarrassment and confusion of your sense, clearness

of perception will come with habitual and tranquil confidence in he who

alike rule the universe and read the heart. I only say here what

have be say much well before by a reasoner in whom all student of

nature recognize a guide. I see on your table the very volume of Bacon

which contain the passage I commend to your reflection. here it be.

listen: ‘take an example of a dog, and mark what a generosity and

courage he will put on when he find himself maintain by a man who, to

he, be instead of a God, or melior natura, which courage be manifestly

such as that creature, without that confidence of a well nature

than his own, could never attain. so man, when he resteth and assureth

himself upon Divine protection and favour, gathereth a force and faith

which human nature could not obtain.’(3) you be silent, but your

gesture tell I your doubt,--a doubt which your heart, so femininely

tender, will not speak aloud lest you should rob the old man of a hope

with which your strength of manhood dispenses,--you doubt the efficacy

of prayer! pause and reflect, bold but candid inquirer into the law

of that guide you call Nature. if there be no efficacy in prayer;

if prayer be as mere an illusion of superstitious fantasy as aught

against which your reason now struggle, do you think that nature

herself would have make it amongst the most common and facile of all

her dictate? do you believe that if there really do not exist that

tie between Man and his maker--that link between life here and life

hereafter which be find in what we call Soul alone--that wherever you

look through the universe, you would behold a child at Prayer? nature

inculcate nothing that be superfluous. nature do not impel the

leviathan or the lion, the eagle or the moth, to pray; she impel only

man. why? because man only have soul, and Soul seek to commune with the

Everlasting, as a fountain struggle up to its source. burn your book.

it would found you a reputation for learning and intellect and courage,

I allow; but learning and intellect and courage waste against a truth,

like spray against a rock! a truth valuable to the world, the world will

never part with. you will not injure the truth, but you will mislead

and may destroy many, whose good security be in the truth which you so

eruditely insinuate to be a fable. Soul and Hereafter be the heritage

of all man; the humblest, journeyman in those street, the pettiest

trader behind those counter, have in those belief their prerogative

of royalty. you would dethrone and embrute the lord of the earth by

your theory. for my part, having give the great part of my life

to the study and analysis of fact, I would rather be the author of the

trite homily, or the bald poem, that inculcate that imperishable

essence of the soul to which I have neither scalpel nor probe, than be

the founder of the subtle school, or the framer of the lofty verse,

that rob my fellow-man of their faith in a spirit that elude the

dissect-knife,--in a being that escape the grave-digger. burn your

book! accept this Book instead; Read and Pray.”



he place his Bible in my hand, embrace I, and, an hour afterwards,

the old man and the child leave my hearth solitary once more.



(1) the summary of this distinguished lecturer’s objection to

phrenology be to be find in the Appendix to vol i. of “Lectures on

Metaphysics,” p. 404, et seq. Edition 1859.



(2) the change of length of iron girder cause by variation of

temperature have not unfrequently bring down the whole edifice into

which they be admit. good engineer and architect allow for such

change produce by temperature. in the tubular bridge across the Menai

Straits, a self-act record of the daily amount of its contraction and

expansion be ingeniously contrived.



(3) Bacon’s “Essay on Atheism.” this quotation be make with admirable

felicity and force by Dr. Whewell, page 378 of Bridgewater Treatise

on Astronomy and General Physics consider with reference to Natural

theology.













CHAPTER XLVII.



that night, as I sit in my study, very thoughtful and very mournful, I

resolve all that Julius Faber have say; and the impression his word

have produce become gradually weak and weak, as my reason, naturally

combative, rise up with all the reply which my philosophy suggest.

no; if my imagination have really seduce and betray I into monstrous

credulity, it be clear that the good remedy to such morbid tendency

towards the Superstitious be in the severe exercise of the faculty

most opposed to Superstition,--in the culture of pure reasoning, in the

science of absolute fact. accordingly, I place before I the very book

which Julius Faber have advise I to burn; I force all my power of

mind to go again over the passage which contain the doctrine that

his admonition have censure; and before daybreak, I have state the

substance of his argument, and the logical reply to it, in an elaborate

addition to my chapter on “Sentimental Philosophers.” while thus

reject the purport of his parting counsel, I embody in another

portion of my work his view on my own “illusion;” and as here my

commonsense be in concord with his, I dispose of all my own previous

doubt in an addition to my favourite chapter “on the Cheats of the

Imagination.” and when the pen drop from my hand, and the day-star

gleam through the window, my heart escape from the labour of my mind,

and fly back to the image of Lilian. the pride of the philosopher die

out of I, the sorrow of the man reign supreme, and I shrink from the

come of the sun, despondent.













CHAPTER XLVIII.



not till the law have complete its proceeding, and satisfy the public

mind as to the murder of Sir Philip Derval, be the remain of the

deceased consign to the family mausoleum. the funeral be, as may be

suppose, strictly private, and when it be over, the excitement cause

by an event so tragical and singular subside. new topic engage the

public talk, and--in my presence, at least--the delicate consideration

due to one whose name have be so painfully mix up in the dismal

story forbore a topic which I could not be expect to hear without

distressful emotion. Mrs. Ashleigh I see frequently at my own house;

she honestly confess that Lilian have not show that grief at the

cancelling of our engagement which would alone justify Mrs. Ashleigh in

ask I again to see her daughter, and retract my conclusion against

our union. she say that Lilian be quiet, not uncheerful, never speak

of I nor of Margrave, but seem absent and pre-occupy as before,

take pleasure in nothing that have be willnot to please she; not in

music, nor book, nor that tranquil pastime which woman call work, and

in which they find excuse to meditate, in idleness, their own fancy.

she rarely stir out, even in the garden; when she do, her eye

seem to avoid the house in which Margrave have lodge, and her step

the old favourite haunt by the monk’ well. she would remain silent for

long hour together, but the silence do not appear melancholy. for

the rest, her health be more than usually good. still Mrs. Ashleigh

persist in her belief that, soon or later, Lilian would return to

her former self, her former sentiment for I; and she entreat I not,

as yet, to let the world know that our engagement be break off. “for

if,” she say, with good sense, “if it should prove not to be break

off, only suspend, and afterwards happily renew, there will be two

story to tell when no story be need. besides, I should dread the

effect on Lilian, if offensive gossip babble to she on a matter that

would excite so much curiosity as the rupture of a union in which our

neighbour have take so general an interest.”



I have no reason to refuse acquiescence in Mrs. Ashleigh’s request, but

I do not share in her hope; I feel that the fair prospect of my life

be blast; I could never love another, never wed another; I resign

myself to a solitary hearth, rejoice, at least, that Margrave have not

revisit at Mrs. Ashleigh’s,--had not, indeed, reappear in the

town. he be still stay with Strahan, who tell I that his guest

have ensconce himself in Forman’s old study, and amuse himself with

reading--though not for long at a time--the curious old book and

manuscript find in the library, or climb tree like a schoolboy,

and familiarize himself with the deer and the cattle, which would

group round he quite tame, and feed from his hand. be this the

description of a criminal? but if Sir Philip’s assertion be really

true; if the criminal be man without soul; if without soul, man would

have no conscience, never be trouble by repentance, and the vague dread

of a future world,--why, then, should not the criminal be gay despite

his crime, as the white bear gambol as friskly after his meal on

human flesh? these question would haunt I, despite my determination to

accept as the right solution of all marvel the construction put on my

narrative by Julius Faber.



day pass; I see and hear nothing of Margrave. I begin half to

hope that, in the desultory and rapid change of mood and mind which

characterize his restless nature, he have forget my existence.



one morning I go out early on my round, when I meet straban

unexpectedly.



“I be in search of you,” he say, “for more than one person have tell I

that you be look ill and jaded. so you be! and the town now be hot

and unhealthy. you must come to Derval Court for a week or so. you can

ride into town every day to see your patient. don’t refuse. Margrave,

who be still with I, send all kind message, and bid I say that he

entreat you to come to the house at which he also be a guest!”



I start. what have the Scin-Laeca require of I, and obtain to that

condition my promise? “if you be ask to the house at which I also

be a guest, you will come; you will meet and converse with I as

guest speak to guest in the house of a host!” be this one of the

coincidence which my reason be bind to accept as coincidence, and

nothing more? tut, tut! be I return again to my “hallucination”?

grant that Faber and common-sense be in the right, what be this

Margrave? a man to whose friendship, acuteness, and energy I be under

the deep obligations,--to whom I be indebted for active service

that have save my life from a serious danger, acquit my honour of a

horrible suspicion. “I thank you,” I say to Strahan, “I will come; not,

indeed, for a week, but, at all event, for a day or two.”



“that’s right; I will call for you in the carriage at six o’clock. you

will have do your day’s work by then?”



“yes; I will so arrange.”



on our way to Derval Court that evening, Strahan talk much about

Margrave, of whom, nevertheless, he seem to be grow weary.



“his high spirit be too much for one,” say he; “and then so

restless,--so incapable of sustained quiet conversation. and, clever

though he be, he cann’t help I in the least about the new house I shall

build. he have no notion of construction. I don’t think he could build a

barn.”



“I think you do not like to demolish the old house, and would content

yourself with pull down the more ancient part of it?”



“true. at first it seem a pity to destroy so handsome a mansion; but

you see, since poor Sir Philip’s manuscript, on which he set such store,

have be too mutilate, I fear, to allow I to effect his wish with

regard to it, I think I ought at least scrupulously to obey his other

whim. and, besides, I don’t know, there be odd noise about the old

house. I don’t believe in haunted house; still there be something

dreary in strange sound at the dead of night, even if make by rat, or

wind through decay rafter. you, I remember at college, have a taste

for architecture, and can draw plan. I wish to follow out Sir Philip’s

design, but on a small scale, and with more attention to comfort.”



thus he continue to run on, satisfied to find I a silent and attentive

listener. we arrive at the mansion an hour before sunset, the westering

light shine full against the many window case in mouldering

pilaster, and make the general dilapidation of the old place yet more

mournfully evident.



it be but a few minute to the dinner-hour. I go up at once to the

room appropriate to me,--not the one I have before occupy. Strahan

have already get together a new establishment. I be glad to find in

the servant who attend I an old acquaintance. he have be in my own

employ when I first settle at L----, and leave I to get marry. he and

his wife be now both in Strahan’s service. he speak warmly of his

new master and his contentment with his situation, while he unpack my

carpet-bag and assist I to change my dress. but the chief object of

his talk and his praise be Mr. Margrave.



“such a bright young gentleman, like the first fine day in May!”



when I enter the drawing-room, Margrave and Strahan be both there.

the former be blithe and genial, as usual, in his welcome. at dinner,

and during the whole evening till we retire severally to our own room,

he be the principal talker,--recounting incident of travel, always

very loosely string together, jest, good-humouredly enough, at

Strahan’s sudden hobby for building, then put question to I about

mutual acquaintance, but never wait for an answer; and every now

and then, as if at random, startle we with some brilliant aphorism, or

some suggestion draw from abstract science or unfamiliar erudition. the

whole effect be sparkling, but I could well understand that, if long

continue, it would become oppressive. the soul have need of pause of

repose,--intervals of escape, not only from the flesh, but even from the

mind. a man of the lofty intellect will experience time when

mere intellect not only fatigue he, but amidst its most original

conception, amidst its proud triumph, have a something trite and

commonplace compare with one of those vague intimation of a spiritual

destiny which be not within the ordinary domain of reason; and, gaze

abstractedly into space, will leave suspend some problem of severest

thought, or uncomplete some golden palace of imperial poetry, to

indulge in hazy reverie, that do not differ from those of an innocent,

quiet child! the soul have a long road to travel--from time through

eternity. it demand its halting hour of contemplation. contemplation

be serene. but with such want of an immortal immaterial spirit,

Margrave have no fellowship, no sympathy; and for myself, I need scarcely

add that the line I have just trace I should not have write at the

date at which my narrative have now arrive.













CHAPTER XLIX.



I have no case that necessitate my return to L---- the following day.

the early hour of the forenoon I devote to Strahan and his building

plan. Margrave flit in and out of the room fitfully as an April

sunbeam, sometimes fling himself on a sofa, and read for a few

minute one of the volume of the ancient mystic, in which Sir Philip’s

library be so rich. I remember it be a volume of Proclus. he read that

crab and difficult Greek with a fluency that surprise I. “I pick

up the ancient Greek,” say he, “year ago, in learn the modern.”

 but the book soon tire he; then he would come and disturb we, archly

enjoy Strahan’s peevishness at interruption; then he would throw open

the window and leap down, chant one of his wild savage air; and

in another moment he be half hide under the droop bough of a broad

lime-tree, amidst the antler of deer that gather fondly round he.

in the afternoon my host be call away to attend some visitor of

importance, and I find myself on the sward before the house, right in

view of the mausoleum and alone with Margrave.



I turn my eye from that dumb House of Death wherein rest the corpse

of the last lord of the soil, so strangely murder, with a strong

desire to speak out to Margrave the doubt respect himself that

torture I. but--set aside the promise to the contrary, which I have

give, or dream I have give, to the luminous Shadow--to fulfil that

desire would have be impossible,--impossible to any one gaze on that

radiant youthful face! I think I see he now as I see he then: a white

doe, that even my presence could not scare away from he, clung lovingly

to his side, look up at he with her soft eye. he stand there like

the incarnate principle of mythological sensuous life. I have before

apply to he that illustration; let the repetition be pardon.

impossible, I repeat it, to say to that creature, face to face, “art

thou the master of demoniac art, and the instigator of secret murder?”

 as if from redundant happiness within himself, he be hum, or rather

cooing, a strain of music, so sweet, so wildly sweet, and so unlike the

music one hear from tutor lip in crowd room! I pass my hand

over my forehead in bewilderment and awe.



“be there,” I say unconsciously,--“are there, indeed, such prodigy

in Nature?”



“nature!” he cry, catch up the word; “talk to I of Nature! talk of

she, the wondrous blissful mother! mother I may well call she. I be she

spoiled child, her darling! but oh, to die, ever to die, ever to lose

sight of Nature!--to rot senseless, whether under these turf or within

those dead walls--”



I could not resist the answer,--



“like yon murder man! murder, and by whom?”



“by whom? I think that be clearly prove.”



“the hand be prove; what influence move the hand?”



“Tush! the poor wretch speak of a Demon. who can tell? nature herself

be a grand destroyer. see that pretty bird, in its beak a writhing worm!

all nature’s child live to take life; none, indeed, so lavishly as

man. what hecatomb slaughter, not to satisfy the irresistible sting

of hunger, but for the wanton ostentation of a feast, which he may

scarcely taste, or for the mere sport that he find in destroy! we

speak with dread of the beast of prey: what beast of prey be so dire

a ravager as man,--so cruel and so treacherous? look at yon flock

of sheep, breed and fatten for the shamble; and this hind that I

caress,--if I be the park-keeper, and her time for my bullet have come,

would you think her life be the safe because, in my own idle whim, I

have tame she to trust to the hand raise to slay she?”



“it be true,” say I,--“a grim truth. nature, on the surface so love

and so gentle, be full of terror in her deep when our thought descend

into their abyss!”



Strahan now join we with a party of country visitor. “Margrave be

the man to show you the beauty of this park,” say he. “margrave know

every bosk and dingle, twisted old thorn-tree, or opening glade, in its

intricate, undulate ground.”



Margrave seem delighted at this proposition; and as he lead we through

the park, though the way be long, though the sun be fierce, no one

seem fatigued. for the pleasure he feel in point out detach

beauty which escape an ordinary eye be contagious. he do not talk

as talk the poet or the painter; but at some lovely effect of light

amongst the tremulous leave, some sudden glimpse of a sportive rivulet

below, he would halt, point it out to we in silence, and with a kind

of childlike ecstasy in his own bright face, that seem to reflect the

life and the bliss of the blithe summer day itself.



thus see, all my doubt in his dark secret nature fade away,--all my

horror, all my hate; it be impossible to resist the charm that breathe

round he, not to feel a tender, affectionate yearning towards he as to

some fair happy child. well might he call himself the Darling of Nature.

be he not the mysterious likeness of that awful Mother, beautiful as

Apollo in one aspect, direful as typhon in another?













CHAPTER L.



 “what a strange-look cane you have, sir!” say a little girl, who be one of the party, and who have entwine her arm round Margrave’s. “let I look at it.”



“yes,” say Strahan, “that cane, or rather walking-staff, be worth

look at. Margrave buy it in Egypt, and declare that it be very

ancient.”



this staff seemed construct from a reed: look at, it seem light,

in the hand it feel heavy; it be of a pale, fade yellow, wrought

with black ring at equal distance, and graven with half obliterate

character that seem hieroglyphic. I remember to have see Margrave

with it before, but I have never notice it with any attention until now,

when it be pass from hand to hand. at the head of the cane there be

a large unpolished stone of a dark blue.



“be this a pebble or a jewel?” ask one of the party.



“I cannot tell you its name or nature,” say Margrave; “but it be say

to cure the bite of serpents(1), and have other suppose virtues,--a

talisman, in short.”



he here place the staff in my hand, and bid I look at it with care.

then he change the conversation and renew the way, leave the

staff with I, till suddenly I force it back on he. I could not have

explain why, but its touch, as it warm in my clasp, seem to send

through my whole frame a singular thrill, and a sensation as if I no

long feel my own weight,--as if I walk on air.



our ramble come to a close; the visitor go away; I re-enter the

house through the sash-window of Forman’s study. Margrave throw his hat

and staff on the table, and amuse himself with examine minutely the

tracery on the mantelpiece. Strahan and myself leave he thus occupy,

and, go into the adjoining library, resume our task of examine the

plan for the new house. I continue to draw outline and sketch

of various alteration, tend to simplify and contract Sir Philip’s

general design. Margrave soon join we, and this time take his seat

patiently beside our table, watch I use ruler and compass with

unwonted attention.



“I wish I could draw,” he say; “but I can do nothing useful.”



“rich man like you,” say Strahan, peevishly, “can engage other,

and be well employ in reward good artist than in make bad

drawing themselves.”



“yes, I can employ other; and--Fenwick, when you have finish with

Strahan I will ask permission to employ you, though without reward; the

task I would impose will not take you a minute.”



he then throw himself back in his chair, and seem to fall into a doze.



the dressing-bell rang; Strahan put away the plans,--indeed, they be

now pretty well finish and decide on. Margrave wake up as our host

leave the room to dress, and draw I towards another table in the

room, place before I one of his favourite mystic book, and, point

to an old woodcut, say,



“I will ask you to copy this for I; it pretend to be a facsimile of

Solomon’s famous seal. I have a whimsical desire to have a copy of it.

you observe two triangle interlace and insert in a circle?--the

pentacle, in short. yes, just so. you need not add the astrological

character: they be the senseless superfluous accessory of the

dreamer who write the book. but the pentacle itself have an intelligible

meaning; it belong to the only universal language, the language of

symbol, in which all race that think--around, and above, and below

we--can establish communion of thought. if in the external universe any

one constructive principle can be detect, it be the geometrical;

and in every part of the world in which magic pretend to a write

character, I find that its hieroglyphic be geometrical figure. be

it not laughable that the most positive of all the science should thus

lend its angle and circle to the use of--what shall I call it?--the

ignorance?--ay, that be the word--the ignorance of dealer in magic?”



he take up the paper, on which I have hastily describe the triangle and

the circle, and leave the room, chant the serpent-charmer’s song.



(1) the follow description of a stone at Corfu, celebrate as an

antidote to the venom of the serpent’s bite, be give to I by an

eminent scholar and legal functionary in that island:--



description of the BLUESTONE.--This stone be of an oval shape 1 2/10 in.

long, 7/10 broad, 3/10 thick, and, having be break formerly, be now

set in gold.



when a person be bite by a poisonous snake, the bite must be open

by a cut of a lancet or razor longway, and the stone apply within

twenty-four hour. the stone then attach itself firmly on the wound,

and when it have do its office fall off; the cure be then complete.

the stone must then be throw into milk, whereupon it vomit the poison

it have absorb, which remain green on the top of the milk, and the

stone be then again fit for use.



this stone have be from time immemorial in the family of Ventura, of

Corfu, a house of italian origin, and be notorious, so that peasant

immediately apply for its aid. its virtue have not be impair by the

fracture. its nature or composition be unknown.



in a case where two be sting at the same time by serpent, the stone

be apply to one, who recover; but the other, for whom it could not

be use, die.



it never fail but once, and then it be apply after the twenty-four

hour.



its colour be so dark as not to be distinguish from black.





                                                P. M. COLQUHOUN.



Corfu, 7th Nov., 1860.



Sir Emerson Tennent, in his popular and excellent work on Ceylon, give

an account of “snake stone” apparently similar to the one at Corfu,

except that they be “intensely black and highly polished,” and which

be apply, in much the same manner, to the wound inflict by the

cobra-capella.



QUERY.-Might it not be worth while to ascertain the chemical property

of these stone, and, if they be efficacious in the extraction of venom

convey by a bite, might they not be as successful if apply to the

bite of a mad dog as to that of a cobra-capella?













CHAPTER LI.



when we separate for the night, which we do at eleven o’clock,

Margrave said,--



“good-night and good-by. I must leave you to-morrow, Strahan, and before

your usual hour for rise. I take the liberty of request one of your

man to order I a chaise from L----. Pardon my seem abruptness, but

I always avoid long leave-taking, and I have fix the date of my

departure almost as soon as I accept your invitation.”



“I have no right to complain. the place must be dull indeed to a gay

young fellow like you. it be dull even to I. I be meditate flight

already. be you go back to L----?”



“not even for such thing as I leave at my lodging. when I settle

somewhere and can give an address, I shall direct they to be send to I.

there be, I hear, beautiful patch of scenery towards the north, only

know to pedestrian tourist. I be a good walker; and you know, Fenwick,

that I be also a child of Nature. adieu to you both; and many thank to

you, Strahan, for your hospitality.”



he leave the room.



“I be not sorry he be go,” say Strahan, after a pause, and with a

quick breath as if of relief. “do you not feel that he exhaust one? an

excess of oxygen, as you would say in a lecture.”



I be alone in my own chamber; I feel indisposed for bed and for sleep;

the curious conversation I have hold with Margrave weigh on I. in that

conversation, we have indirectly touch upon the prodigy which I have

not bring myself to speak of with frank courage, and certainly nothing

in Margrave’s manner have betray consciousness of my suspicion; on the

contrary, the open frankness with which he evince his predilection for

mystic speculation, or utter his more unamiable sentiment, rather

tend to disarm than encourage belief in gloomy secret or sinister

power. and as he be about to quit the neighbourhood, he would not

again see Lilian, not even enter the town of L----. be I to ascribe

this relief from his presence to the promise of the Shadow; or be I

not rather right in battle firmly against any grotesque illusion, and

accept his departure as a simple proof that my jealous fear have be

amongst my other chimera, and that as he have really only visit

Lilian out of friendship to I, in my peril, so he might, with his

characteristic acuteness, have guess my jealousy, and cease his

visit from a kindly motive delicately conceal? and might not the same

motive now have dictate the word which be intend to assure I

that L---- contain no attraction to tempt he to return to it? thus,

gradually soothe and cheer by the course to which my reflection lead

I, I continue to muse for hour. at length, look at my watch, I

be surprised to find it be the second hour after midnight. I be just

about to rise from my chair to undress, and secure some hour of sleep,

when the well-remember cold wind pass through the room, stir the

root of my hair; and before I stand, against the wall, the luminous

Shadow.



“rise and follow I,” say the voice, sound much near than it have

ever do before.



and at those word I rise mechanically, and like a sleepwalker.



“take up the light.”



I take it. the Scin-Laeca glide along the wall towards the threshold,

and motion I to open the door. I do so. the Shadow flit on

through the corridor. I follow, with hush footstep, down a small

stair into Forman’s study. in all my subsequent proceeding, about to be

narrate, the Shadow guide I, sometimes by voice, sometimes by sign.

I obey the guidance, not only unresistingly, but without a desire to

resist. I be unconscious either of curiosity or of awe,--only of a

calm and passive indifference, neither pleasurable nor painful. in this

obedience, from which all will seem extract, I take into my hand

the staff which I have examine the day before, and which lie on the

table, just where Margrave have cast it on re-enter the house. I

unclose the shutter to the casement, lift the sash, and, with the

light in my left hand, the staff in my right, step forth into the

garden. the night be still; the flame of the candle scarcely tremble

in the air; the Shadow move on before I towards the old pavilion

describe in an early part of this narrative, and of which the

mouldering door stand wide open. I follow the Shadow into the

pavilion, up the crazy stair to the room above, with its four great

blank unglazed window, or rather arcade, north, south, east, and west.

I halt on the middle of the floor: right before my eye, through the

vista make by breathless bough, stand out from the moonlit air the

dreary mausoleum. then, at the command convey to I, I place the

candle on a wooden settle, touch a spring in the handle of the staff;

a lid fly back, and I draw from the hollow, first a lump of some dark

bituminous substance, next a small slender wand of polished steel, of

which the point be tip with a translucent material, which appear

to I like crystal. bend down, still obedient to the direction

convey to I, I describe on the floor with the lump of bituman (if

I may so call it) the figure of the pentacle with the interlaced

triangle, in a circle nine foot in diameter, just as I have draw it

for Margrave the evening before. the material use make the figure

perceptible, in a dark colour of mingle black and red. I apply the

flame of the candle to the circle, and immediately it become lambent

with a low steady splendour that rise about an inch from the floor; and

gradually front this light there emanate a soft, gray, transparent mist

and a faint but exquisite odour. I stand in the midst of the circle,

and within the circle also, close by my side, stand the Scin-Laeca,--no

long reflect on the wall, but apart from it, erect, round into

more integral and distinct form, yet impalpable, and from it there

breathe an icy air. then lift the wand, the broad end of which

rest in the palm of my hand, the two forefinger close lightly over

it in a line parallel with the point, I direct it towards the wide

aperture before I, front the mausoleum. I repeat aloud some word

whisper to I in a language I know not: those word I would not trace

on this paper, could I remember they. as they come to a close, I hear

a howl from the watch-dog in the yard,--a dismal, lugubrious howl.

other dog in the distant village catch up the sound, and bay in

a dirge-like chorus; and the howling go on louder and louder. again

strange word be whisper to I, and I repeat they in mechanical

submission; and when they, too, be end, I feel the ground tremble

beneath I, and as my eye look straight forward down the vista, that,

stretch from the casement, be bound by the solitary mausoleum,

vague formless shadow seem to pass across the moonlight,--below,

along the sward, above, in the air; and then suddenly a terror, not

before conceive, come upon I.



and a third time word be whisper; but though I know no more of

their meaning than I do of those that have precede they, I feel a

repugnance to utter they aloud. mutely I turn towards the Scin-Laeca,

and the expression of its face be menacing and terrible; my will became

yet more compelled to the control impose upon it, and my lip commence

the formula again whisper into my ear, when I hear distinctly a voice

of warning and of anguish, that murmur “hold!” I know the voice; it

be Lilian’s. I pause; I turn towards the quarter from which the

voice have come, and in the space afar I see the feature, the form

of Lilian. her arm be stretch towards I in supplication, her

countenance be deadly pale, and anxious with unutterable distress. the

whole image seem in unison with the voice,--the look, the attitude,

the gesture of one who see another in deadly peril, and cry,

“beware!”



this apparition vanish in a moment; but that moment suffice to free

my mind from the constraint which have before enslave it. I dash the

wand to the ground, spring from the circle, rush from the place. how

I get into my own room I can remember not,--I know not; I have a

vague reminiscence of some intervene wandering, of giant tree, of

shroud-like moonlight, of the shine Shadow and its angry aspect, of

the blind wall and the iron door of the House of the Dead, of spectral

images,--a confused and dreary phantasmagoria. but all I can recall with

distinctness be the sight of my own hueless face in the mirror in my

own still room, by the light of the white moon through the window; and,

sink down, I say to myself, “this, at least, be an hallucination or

a dream!”













CHAPTER LII.



a heavy sleep come over I at daybreak, but I do not undress nor go to

bed. the sun be high in the heaven when, on wake, I see the servant

who have attend I bustle about the room.



“I beg your pardon, sir, I be afraid I disturb you; but I have be

three time to see if you be not come down, and I find you so

soundly asleep I do not like to wake you. Mr. Strahan have finish

breakfast, and go out riding; Mr. Margrave have left,--left before six

o’clock.”



“ah, he say he be go early.”



“yes, sir; and he seem so cross when he go. I could never have

suppose so pleasant a gentleman could put himself into such a passion!”



“what be the matter?”



“why, his walking-stick could not be find; it be not in the hall. he

say he have leave it in the study; we could not find it there. at last he

find it himself in the old summerhouse, and say--I beg pardon--he say

he be sure you have take it there: that some one, at all event, have

be meddle with it. however, I be very glad it be find, since he

seem to set such store on it.”



“do Mr. Margrave go himself into the summer-house to look for it?”



“yes, sir; no one else would have think of such a place; no one like

to go there, even in the daytime.”



“why?”



“why, sir, they say it be haunt since poor Sir Philip’s death; and,

indeed, there be strange noise in every part of the house. I be

afraid you have a bad night, sir,” continue the servant, with evident

curiosity, glance towards the bed, which I have not press, and

towards the evening-dress which, while he speak, I be rapidly change

for that which I habitually wear in the morning. “I hope you do not

feel yourself ill?”



“no! but it seem I fall asleep in my chair.”



“do you hear, sir, how the dog howl about two o’clock in the

morning? they wake I. very frightful!”



“the moon be at her full. Dogs will bay at the moon.”



I feel relieved to think that I should not find Strahan in the

breakfast-room; and hasten through the ceremony of a meal which I

scarcely touch, I go out into the park unobserved, and creeping

round the copse and into the neglect garden, make my way to the

pavilion. I mount the stair; I look on the floor of the upper room;

yes, there still be the black figure of the pentacle, the circle. so,

then, it be not a dream! till then I have doubt. or might it not still

be so far a dream that I have walk in my sleep, and with an imagination

preoccupy by my conversation with Margrave,--by the hieroglyphic

on the staff I have handle, by the very figure associate with

superstitious practice which I have copy from some weird book at

his request, by all the strange impression previously stamp on my

mind,--might I not, in truth, have carry thither in sleep the staff,

describe the circle, and all the rest be but visionary delusion?

surely, surely, so common-sense, and so Julius Faber would interpret the

riddle that perplex I! be that as it may, my first thought be to

efface the mark on the floor. I find this easy than I have venture

to hope. I rub the circle and the pentacle away from the board with

the sole of my foot, leave but an undistinguishable smudge behind. I

know not why, but I feel the more nervously anxious to remove all such

evidence of my nocturnal visit to that room, because Margrave have so

openly go thither to seek for the staff, and have so rudely name I to

the servant as having meddle with it. might he not awake some suspicion

against I? Suspicion, what of? I know not, but I fear!



the healthful air of day gradually nerve my spirit and relieve my

thought. but the place have become hateful to I. I resolve not to wait

for Strahan’s return, but to walk back to L----, and leave a message for

my host. it be sufficient excuse that I could not long absent myself

from my patient; accordingly I give direction to have the few thing

which I have bring with I send to my house by any servant who might

be go to L----, and be soon pleased to find myself outside the

park-gate and on the high-road.



I have not go a mile before I meet Strahan on horseback. he receive

my apology for not wait his return to bid he farewell without

observation, and, dismounting, lead his horse and walk beside I on

my road. I see that there be something on his mind; at last he say,

look down,--



“do you hear the dog howl last night?”



“yes! the full moon!”



“you be awake, then, at the time. do you hear any other sound? do

you see anything?”



“what should I hear or see?”



Strahan be silent for some moment; then he say, with great

seriousness,--



“I could not sleep when I go to bed last night; I feel feverish and

restless. somehow or other, Margrave get into my head, mix up in some

strange way with Sir Philip Derval. I hear the dog howl, and at

the same time, or rather a few minute later, I feel the whole house

tremble, as a frail corner-house in London seem to tremble at night

when a carriage be drive past it. the howling have then cease, and

cease as suddenly as it have begin. I feel a vague, superstitious alarm;

I get up, and go to my window, which be unclosed (it be my habit

to sleep with my window open); the moon be very bright, and I see, I

declare I see along the green alley that lead from the old part of

the house to the mausoleum--no, I will not say what I see or believe I

saw,--you would ridicule I, and justly. but, whatever it might be, on

the earth without or in the fancy within my brain, I be so terrified,

that I rush back to my bed, and bury my face in my pillow. I would

have come to you; but I do not dare to stir. I have be ride hard

all the morning in order to recover my nerve. but I dread sleep

again under that roof, and now that you and Margrave leave I, I shall

go this very day to London. I hope all that I have tell you be no bad

sign of any come disease; blood to the head, eh?”



“no; but imagination overstrain can produce wondrous effect. you do

right to change the scene. go to London at once, amuse yourself, and--”



“not return, till the old house be raze to the ground. that be my

resolve. you approve? that’s well. all success to you, Fenwick. I will

canter back and get my portmanteau ready and the carriage out, in time

for the five o’clock train.”



so then he, too, have see--what? I do not dare and I do not desire

to ask he. but he, at least, be not walk in his sleep! do we both

dream, or neither?













CHAPTER LIII.



there be an instance of the absorb tyranny of every-day life which

must have strike all such of my reader as have ever experience one of

those portent which be so at variance with every-day life, that the

ordinary epithet bestow on they be “supernatural.”



and be my reader few or many, there will be no small proportion of they

to whom once, at least, in the course of their existence, a something

strange and eerie have occurred,--a something which perplex and

baffle rational conjecture, and strike on those chord which vibrate to

superstition. it may have be only a dream unaccountably verified,--an

undefinable presentiment or forewarning; but up from such slighter and

vague token of the realm of marvel, up to the portent of ghostly

apparition or haunted chamber, I believe that the great number of

person arrive at middle age, however instruct the class, however

civilize the land, however sceptical the period, to which they belong,

have either in themselves experience, or hear record by intimate

associate whose veracity they accept as indisputable in all ordinary

transaction of life, phenomenon which be not to be solve by the wit

that mock they, nor, perhaps, always and entirely, to the contentment

of the reason or the philosophy that explain they away. such phenomenon,

I say, be infinitely more numerous than would appear from the instance

currently quote and dismiss with a jest; for few of those who have

witness they be disposed to own it, and they who only hear of they

through other, however trustworthy, would not impugn their character

for common-sense by profess a belief to which common-sense be a

merciless persecutor. but he who read my assertion in the quiet of his

own room, will perhaps pause, ransack his memory, and find there, in

some dark corner which he exclude from “the babbling and remorseless

day,” a pale recollection that prove the assertion not untrue.



and it be, I say, an instance of the absorb tyranny of everyday life,

that whenever some such startling incident disturb its regular tenor of

thought and occupation, that same every-day life hasten to bury in its

sand the object which have trouble its surface; the more unaccountable,

the more prodigious, have be the phenomenon which have scare and

astound we, the more, with involuntary effort, the mind seek to rid

itself of an enigma which might disease the reason that try to solve

it. we go about our mundane business with renew avidity; we feel the

necessity of prove to ourselves that we be still sober, practical

man, and refuse to be unfit for the world which we know, by

unsolicited visitation from world into which every glimpse be soon

lose amid shadow. and it amaze we to think how soon such incident,

though not actually forget, though they can be recall--and recall

too vividly for health--at our will, be nevertheless thrust, as it

be, out of the mind’s sight as we cast into lumber-room the crutch

and splint that remind we of a broken limb which have recover its

strength and tone. it be a felicitous peculiarity in our organization,

which all member of my profession will have notice, how soon, when

a bodily pain be once pass, it becomes erase from the

recollection,--how soon and how invariably the mind refuse to linger

over and recall it. no man free an hour before from a rage toothache,

the rack of a neuralgia, seat himself in his armchair to recollect and

ponder upon the anguish he have undergo. it be the same with certain

affliction of the mind,--not with those that strike on our affection,

or blast our fortune, overshadow our whole future with a sense of

loss; but where a trouble or calamity have be an accident, an episode

in our wonted life, where it affect ourselves alone, where it be

attend with a sense of shame and humiliation, where the pain

of recall it seem idle, and if indulge would almost madden

us,--agonies of that kind we do not brood over as we do over the death

or falsehood of beloved friend, or the train of event by which we be

reduce from wealth to penury. no one, for instance, who have escape

from a shipwreck, from the brink of a precipice, from the jaw of

a tiger, spend his day and night in revive his terror past,

re-imagine danger not to occur again, or, if they do occur, from

which the experience undergone can suggest no additional safeguard. the

current of our life, indeed, like that of the river, be most rapid in

the midmost channel, where all stream be alike comparatively slow in

the depth and along the shore in which each life, as each river, have a

character peculiar to itself. and hence, those who would sail with the

tide of the world, as those who sail with the tide of a river, hasten

to take the middle of the stream, as those who sail against the tide

be find cling to the shore. I return to my habitual duty and

avocation with renew energy; I do not suffer my thought to dwell on

the dreary wonder that have haunt I, from the evening I first meet

Sir Philip Derval to the morning on which I have quit the house of

his heir; whether reality or hallucination, no guess of mine could

unravel such marvel, and no prudence of mine guard I against their

repetition. but I have no fear that they would be repeat, any more than

the man who have go through shipwreck, or the hairbreadth escape from

a fall down a glaci, fear again to be find in a similar peril.

Margrave have depart, whither I know not, and, with his departure,

cease all sense of his influence. a certain calm within I, a

tranquillizing feeling of relief, seem to I like a pledge of

permanent delivery.



but that which do accompany and haunt I, through all my occupation

and pursuit, be the melancholy remembrance of the love I have lose in

Lilian. I hear from Mrs. Ashleigh, who still frequently visit

I, that her daughter seem much in the same quiet state of

mind,--perfectly reconcile to our separation, seldom mention my

name, if mention it, with indifference; the only thing remarkable in

her state be her aversion to all society, and a kind of lethargy that

would come over she, often in the daytime. she would suddenly fall into

sleep and so remain for hour, but a sleep that seem very serene and

tranquil, and from which she wake of herself. she keep much within her

own room, and always retire to it when visitor be announce.



Mrs. Ashleigh begin reluctantly to relinquish the persuasion she have so

long and so obstinately maintain, that this state of feeling towards

myself--and, indeed, this general change in Lilian--be but temporary

and abnormal; she begin to allow that it be good to drop all thought

of a renew engagement,--a future union. I propose to see Lilian in

her presence and in my professional capacity; perhaps some physical

cause, especially for this lethargy, might be detect and remove.

Mrs. Ashleigh own to I that the idea have occur to herself: she

have sound lilian upon it: but her daughter have so resolutely oppose

it,--had say with so quiet a firmness “that all be over between we,

a visit from I would be unwelcome and painful,”--that Mrs. Ashleigh

feel that an interview thus deprecate would only confirm estrangement.

one day, in call, she ask my advice whether it would not be well

to try the effect of change of air and scene, and, in some other place,

some other medical opinion might be take? I approve of this suggestion

with unspeakable sadness.



“and,” say Mrs. Ashleigh, shed tear, “if that experiment prove

unsuccessful, I will write and let you know; and we must then consider

what to say to the world as a reason why the marriage be break off. I

can render this more easy by stay away. I will not return to L----

till the matter have cease to be the topic of talk, and at a distance

any excuse will be less question and seem more natural. but

still--still--let we hope still.”



“have you one ground for hope?”



“perhaps so; but you will think it very frail and fallacious.”



“name it, and let I judge.”



“one night--in which you be on a visit to Derval Court--”



“ay, that night.”



“Lilian wake I by a loud cry (she sleep in the next room to I, and

the door be leave open); I hasten to her bedside in alarm; she be

asleep, but appear extremely agitated and convulse. she keep call

on your name in a tone of passionate fondness, but as if in great

terror. she cry, ‘do not go, Allen--do not go--you know not what you

brave!--what you do!’ then she rise in her bed, clasp her hand. she

face be set and rigid; I try to awake she, but could not. after a

little time, she breathe a deep sigh, and murmur, ‘Allen, Allen!

dear love! do you not hear, do you not see I? what could thus baffle

matter and traverse space but love and soul? can you still doubt I,

Allen?--doubt that I love you now, shall love you evermore?--yonder,

yonder, as here below?’ she then sink back on her pillow, weeping, and

then I wake she.”



“and what do she say on wake?”



“she do not remember what she have dream, except that she have pass

through some great terror; but add, with a vague smile, ‘it be over,

and I feel happy now.’ then she turn round and fall asleep again, but

quietly as a child, the tear dry, the smile rest.”



“go, my dear friend, go; take Lilian away from this place as soon as you

can; divert her mind with fresh scene. I hope!--I do hope! let I know

where you fix yourself. I will seize a holiday,--I need one; I will

arrange as to my patient; I will come to the same place; she need not

know of it, but I must be by to watch, to hear your news of she. Heaven

bless you for what you have say! I hope!--I do hope!”













CHAPTER LIV.



some day after, I receive a few line from Mrs. Ashleigh. her

arrangement for departure be make. they be to start the next

morning. she have fix on go into the north of Devonshire, and

stay some week either at Ilfracombe or Lynton, whichever place

lilian preferred. she would write as soon as they be settle.



I be up at my usual early hour the next morning. I resolve to go out

towards Mrs. Ashleigh’s house, and watch, unnoticed, where I might,

perhaps, catch a glimpse of Lilian as the carriage that would convey she

to the railway pass my hiding-place.



I be look impatiently at the clock; it be yet two hour before the

train by which Mrs. Ashleigh propose to leave. a loud ring at my bell!

I open the door. Mrs. Ashleigh rush in, fall on my breast.



“Lilian! Lilian!”



“Heavens! what have happen?”



“she have leave! she be gone,--gone away! oh, Allen, how?--whither? Advise

I. what be to be do?”



“come in--compose yourself--tell I all,--clearly, quickly. lilian

gone,--gone away? impossible! she must be hide somewhere in the

house,--the garden; she, perhaps, do not like the journey. she may

have creep away to some young friend’s house. but I talk when you should

talk: tell I all.”



little enough to tell! Lilian have seem unusually cheerful the night

before, and please at the thought of the excursion. mother and daughter

retire to rest early: Mrs. Ashleigh see Lilian sleep quietly before

she herself go to bed. she wake betime in the morning, dress

herself, go into the next room to call Lilian--Lilian be not there.

no suspicion of flight occur to she. perhaps her daughter might be

up already, and go downstairs, remember something she might wish

to pack and take with she on the journey. Mrs. Ashleigh be confirm

in this idea when she notice that her own room door be leave open. she

go downstairs, meet a maidservant in the hall, who tell she, with alarm

and surprise, that both the street and garden door be find unclosed.

no one have see Lilian. Mrs. Ashleigh now become seriously uneasy.

on remounting to her daughter’s room, she miss Lilian’s bonnet and

mantle. the house and garden be both search in vain. there could be

no doubt that Lilian have gone,--must have steal noiselessly at night

through her mother’s room, and let herself out of the house and through

the garden.



“do you think she could have receive any letter, any message, any

visitor unknown to you?”



“I cannot think it. why do you ask? oh, Allen, you do not believe there

be any accomplice in this disappearance! no, you do not believe it. but

my child’s honour! what will the world think?”



not for the world care I at that moment. I could think only of Lilian,

and without one suspicion that impute blame to she.



“be quiet, be silent; perhaps she have go on some visit and will

return. meanwhile, leave inquiry to I.”













CHAPTER LV.



it seem incredible that Lilian could wander far without be

observe. I soon ascertain that she have not go away by the

railway--by any public conveyance--have hire no carriage; she must

therefore be still in the town, or have leave it on foot. the great

part of the day be consume in unsuccessful inquiry, and faint hope

that she would return; meanwhile the news of her disappearance have

spread: how could such news fail to do so?



an acquaintance of mine meet I under the archway of monk’ Gate. he

wring my hand and look at I with great compassion.



“I fear,” say he, “that we be all deceive in that young Margrave. he

seem so well conduct, in spite of his lively manner. But--”



“but what?”



“Mrs. Ashleigh be, perhaps, imprudent to admit he into her house

so familiarly. he be certainly very handsome. young lady will be

romantic.”



“how dare you, sir!” I cry, choke with rage. “and without any

colour to so calumnious a suggestion! Margrave have not be in the

town for many day. no one know even where he be.”



“oh, yes, it be know where he be. he write to order the effect which

he have leave here to be send to Penrith.”



“when?”



“the letter arrive the day before yesterday. I happen to be call

at the house where he last lodge, when at L----, the house opposite
