Mrs. Ashleigh’s garden. no doubt the servant in both house gossip with

each other. Miss Ashleigh could scarcely fail to hear of Mr. Margrave’s

address from she maid; and since servant will exchange gossip, they may

also convey letter. pardon I, you know I be your friend.”



“not from the moment you breathe a word against my betrothed wife,” say

I, fiercely.



I wrench myself from the clasp of the man’s hand, but his word still

ring in my ear. I mount my horse; I ride into the adjoining suburb,

the neighbour village; there, however, I learn nothing, till,

just at nightfall, in a hamlet about ten mile from L----, a labourer

declare he have see a young lady dress as I describe, who pass

by he in a path through the field a little before noon; that he be

surprised to see one so young, so well dress, and a stranger to the

neighbourhood (for he know by sight the lady of the few family

scatter around) walk alone; that as he step out of the path to

make way for she, he look hard into her face, and she do not heed

him,--seemed to gaze right before she, into space. if her expression have

be less quiet and gentle, he should have think, he could scarcely

say why, that she be not quite right in her mind; there be a strange

unconscious stare in her eye, as if she be walk in her sleep. she

pace be very steady,--neither quick nor slow. he have watch she till

she pass out of sight, amidst a wood through which the path wound its

way to a village at some distance.



I follow up this clew. I arrive at the village to which my informant

direct I, but night have set in. Most of the house be closed, so I

could glean no further information from the cottage or at the inn. but

the police superintendent of the district live in the village, and to

he I give instruction which I have not give, and, indeed, would have

be disinclined to give, to the police at L----. he be intelligent

and kindly; he promise to communicate at once with the different

police-station for mile round, and with all delicacy and privacy. it

be not probable that Lilian could have wander in one day much far

than the place at which I then be; it be scarcely to be conceive that

she could baffle my pursuit and the practised skill of the police. I

rest but a few hour, at a small public-house, and be on horseback

again at dawn. a little after sunrise I again hear of the wanderer. at

a lonely cottage, by a brick-kiln, in the midst of a wide common, she

have stop the previous evening, and ask for a draught of milk. the

woman who give it to she inquire if she have lose her way. she say

“No;” and, only tarry a few minute, have go across the common; and

the woman suppose she be a visitor at a gentleman’s house which be at

the farth end of the waste, for the path she take lead to no town, no

village. it occur to I then that Lilian avoid all high-road, all

place, even the humblest, where man congregate together. but where

could she have pass the night? not to fatigue the reader with the

fruitless result of frequent inquiry, I will but say that at the end

of the second day I have succeed in ascertain that I be still

on her track; and though I have ride to and fro nearly double the

distance--come back again to place I have leave behind--it be at the

distance of forty mile from L---- that I last hear of she that second

day. she have be sit alone by a little brook only an hour before.

I be lead to the very spot by a woodman--it be at the hour of twilight

when he behold she; she be lean her face on her hand, and seem

weary. he speak to she; she do not answer, but rise and resume she

way along the bank of the streamlet. that night I put up at no inn; I

follow the course of the brook for mile, then strike into every path

that I could conceive she to have taken,--in vain. thus I consume

the night on foot, tie my horse to a tree, for he be tired out, and

return to he at sunrise. at noon, the third day, I again hear of

she, and in a remote, savage part of the country. the feature of the

landscape be change; there be little foliage and little culture, but

the ground be break into mould and hollow, and cover with patch

of heath and stunt brushwood. she have be see by a shepherd, and

he make the same observation as the first who have guide I on she

track,--she look to he “like some one walk in her sleep.” an hour

or two later, in a dell, amongst the furze-bush, I chance on a

knot of ribbon. I recognize the colour Lilian habitually wear; I feel

certain that the ribbon be her. calculate the utmost speed I could

ascribe to she, she could not be far off, yet still I fail to discover

she. the scene now be as solitary as a desert. I meet no one on my way.

at length, a little after sunset, I find myself in view of the sea.

a small town nestle below the cliff, on which I be guide my weary

horse. I enter the town, and while my horse be bait go in search

of the resident policeman. the information I have direct to be send

round the country have reach he; he have act on it, but without

result. I be surprised to hear he address I by name, and look at

he more narrowly, I recognize he for the policeman Waby. this young

man have always express so grateful a sense of my attendance on his

sister, and have, indeed, so notably evince his gratitude in prosecute

with Margrave the inquiry which terminate in the discovery of

Sir Philip Derval’s murderer, that I confide to he the name of the

wanderer, of which he have not be previously inform; but which it

would be, indeed, impossible to conceal from he should the search in

which his aid be ask prove successful,--as he know Miss Ashleigh by

sight. his face immediately become thoughtful. he pause a minute or

two, and then said,--



“I think I have it, but I do not like to say; I may pain you, sir.”



“not by confidence; you pain I by concealment.”



the man hesitate still: I encourage he, and then he speak out

frankly.



“Sir, do you never think it strange that Mr. Margrave should move from

his handsome room in the hotel to a somewhat uncomfortable lodging,

from the window of which he could look down on Mrs. Ashleigh’s garden? I

have see he at night in the balcony of that window, and when I notice

he go so frequently into Mrs. Ashleigh’s house during your unjust

detention, I own, sir, I feel for you--”



“Nonsense! Mr. Margrave go to Mrs. Ashleigh’s house as my friend. he

have leave L---- week ago. what have all this to do with--”



“Patience, sir; hear I out. I be send from L---- to this station (on

promotion, sir) a fortnight since last Friday, for there have be a

good deal of crime hereabouts; it be a bad neighbourhood, and full of

smuggler. some day ago, in watch quietly near a lonely house, of

which the owner be a suspicious character down in my book, I see, to my

amazement, Mr. Margrave come out of that house,--come out of a private

door in it, which belong to a part of the building not inhabit by the

owner, but which use formerly, when the house be a sort of inn, to

be let to night lodger of the humblest description. I follow he;

he go down to the seashore, walk about, singe to himself; then

return to the house, and re-enter by the same door. I soon learn

that he lodge in the house,--had lodge there for several day. the

next morning, a fine yacht arrive at a tolerably convenient creek about

a mile from the house, and there anchor. sailor come ashore, ramble

down to this town. the yacht belong to Mr. Margrave; he have purchase

it by commission in London. it be store for a long voyage. he have

direct it to come to he in this out-of-the-way place, where no

gentleman’s yacht ever put in before, though the creek or bay be handy

enough for such craft. well, sir, be it not strange that a rich young

gentleman should come to this unfrequented seashore, put up with

accommodation that must be of the rude kind, in the house of a man

know as a desperate smuggler, suspect to be bad; order a yacht to

meet he here; be not all this strange? but would it be strange if he

be wait for a young lady? and if a young lady have flee at night

from her home, and have come secretly along bypath, which must have

be very fully explain to she beforehand, and be now near that young

gentleman’s lodging, if not actually in it--if this be so, why, the

affair be not so very strange after all. and now do you forgive I,

sir?”



“where be this house? lead I to it.”



“you can hardly get to it except on foot; rough walking, sir, and about

seven mile off by the short cut.”



“come, and at once; come quickly. we must be there before--before--”



“before the young lady can get to the place. well, from what you say

of the spot in which she be last see, I think, on reflection, we may

easily do that. I be at your service, sir. but I should warn you

that the owner of the house, man and wife, be both of villanous

character,--would do anything for money. Mr. Margrave, no doubt,

have money enough; and if the young lady choose to go away with Mr.

Margrave, you know I have no power to help it.”



“leave all that to I; all I ask of you be to show I the house.”



we be soon out of the town; the night have close in; it be very dark,

in spite of a few star; the path be rugged and precipitous, sometimes

skirt the very brink of perilous cliff, sometimes delve down to

the seashore--there stop by rock or wave--and painfully rewinde up

the ascent.



“it be an ugly path, sir, but it save four mile; and anyhow the road

be a bad one.”



we come, at last, to a few wretched fisherman’s hut. the moon have now

rise, and reveal the squalor of poverty-stricken ruinous hovel; a

couple of boat moor to the shore, a moaning, fretful sea; and at a

distance a vessel, with light on board, lie perfectly still at anchor

in a sheltered curve of the bold rude shore. the policeman point to

the vessel.



“the yacht, sir; the wind will be in her favour if she sail tonight.”



we quicken our pace as well as the nature of the path would permit,

leave the hut behind we, and about a mile far on come to a solitary

house, large than, from the policeman’s description of Margrave’s

lodgement, I should have presuppose: a house that in the wilder part

of Scotland might be almost a laird’s; but even in the moonlight it

look very dilapidated and desolate. Most of the window be closed,

some with pane break, stuff with wisps of straw; there be the

remain of a wall round the house; it be break in some part (only its

foundation leave). on approach the house I observe two doors,--one

on the side front the sea, one on the other side, face a patch of

break ground that might once have be a garden, and lie waste within

the enclosure of the ruin wall, encumber with various litter; heap

of rubbish, a ruin shed, the carcass of a wear-out boat. this latter

door stand wide open,--the other be close. the house be still and

dark, as if either desert, or all within it retire to rest.



“I think that open door lead at once to the room Mr. Margrave hire;

he can go in and out without disturb the other inmate. they use to

keep, on the side which they inhabit, a beer-house, but the magistrate

shut it up; still, it be a resort for bad character. now, sir, what

shall we do?



“watch separately. you wait within the enclosure of the wall, hide by

those heap of rubbish, near the door; none can enter but what you will

observe they. if you see she, you will accost and stop she, and call

aloud for I; I shall be in hear. I will go back to the high part

of the ground yonder--it seem to I that she must pass that way; and

I would desire, if possible, to save she from the humiliation, the--the

shame of come within the precinct of that man’s abode. I feel I may

trust you now and hereafter. it be a great thing for the happiness and

honour of this poor young lady and her mother, that I may be able to

declare that I do not take she from that man, from any man--from that

house, from any house. you comprehend I, and will obey? I speak to you

as a confidant,--a friend.”



“I thank you with my whole heart, sir, for so do. you save my

sister’s life, and the least I can do be to keep secret all that would

pain your life if blab abroad. I know what mischief folk’ tongue

can make. I will wait by the door, never fear, and will rather lose my

place than not strain all the legal power I possess to keep the young

lady back from sorrow.”



this dialogue be interchange in close hurry whisper behind the

break wall, and out of all hearing. Waby now creep through a wide gap

into the inclosure, and nestle himself silently amidst the wreck of

the broken boat, not six foot from the open door, and close to the wall

of the house itself. I go back some thirty yard up the road, to the

rise ground which I have point out to he. accord to the good

calculation I could make--consider the pace at which I have clear

the precipitous pathway, and reckon from the place and time at which

Lilian have be last see-she could not possibly have yet enter that

house. I might presume it would be more than half an hour before she

could arrive; I be in hope that, during the interval, Margrave might

show himself, perhaps at the door, or from the window, or I might even

by some light from the latter be guide to the room in which to find

he. if, after wait a reasonable time, Lilian should fail to appear,

I have form my plan of action; but it be important for the success of

that plan that I should not lose myself in the strange house, nor bring

its owner to Margrave’s aid,--that I should surprise he alone and

unaware. half an hour, three quarter, a whole hour thus pass. no

sign of my poor wanderer; but sign there be of the enemy from whom

I resolve, at whatever risk, to free and to save she. a window on the

ground-floor, to the left of the door, which have long fix my attention

because I have see light through the chink of the shutter, slowly

unclose, the shutter fall back, the casement open, and I behold

Margrave distinctly; he hold something in his hand that gleam in the

moonlight, direct not towards the mound on which I stand, nor towards

the path I have take, but towards an open space beyond the ruin wall

to the right. hide by a cluster of stunt shrub I watch he with a

heart that beat with rage, not with terror. he seem so intent in his

own gaze as to be unheede or unconscious of all else. I steal from my

post, and, still under cover, sometimes of the break wall, sometimes of

the shaggy ridge that skirt the path, creep on, on till I reach the

side of the house itself; then, there secure from his eye, should he

turn they, I step over the ruin wall, scarcely two foot high in

that place, on--on towards the door. I pass the spot on which the

policeman have shroud himself; he be seat, his back against the rib

of the broken boat. I put my hand to his mouth that he might not cry out

in surprise, and whisper in his ear; he stir not. I shake he by

the arm: still he stir not. a ray of the moon fall on his face. I

see that he be in a profound slumber. persuade that it be no natural

sleep, and that he have become useless to I, I pass he by. I be

at the threshold of the open door, the light from the window close by

fall on the ground; I be in the passage; a glimmer come through the

chink of a door to the left; I turn the handle noiselessly, and, the

next moment, Margrave be lock in my grasp.



“call out,” I hiss in his ear, “and I strangle you before any one can

come to your help.”



he do not call out; his eye, fix on mine as he writhe round, see,

perhaps, his peril if he do. his countenance betray fear, but as

I tighten my grasp that expression give way to one of wrath and

fierceness; and as, in turn, I feel the grip of his hand, I know that

the struggle between we would be that of two strong man, each equally

bent on the mastery of the other.



I be, as I have say before, endow with an unusual degree of physical

power, discipline in early youth by athletic exercise and contest. in

height and in muscle I have greatly the advantage over my antagonist;

but such be the nervous vigour, the elastic energy of his incomparable

frame, in which sinew seem spring of steel, that have our encounter

be one in which my strength be less heighten by rage, I believe

that I could no more have cope with he than the bison can cope with

the boa; but I be animate by that passion which treble for a time

all our forces,--which make even the weak man a match for the strong.

I feel that if I be worst, disabled, stricken down, Lilian might be

lose in lose her sole protector; and on the other hand, Margrave have

be take at the disadvantage of that surprise which will half unnerve

the fiercest of the wild beast; while as we grapple, reel and

rock to and fro in our struggle, I soon observe that his attention

be distracted,--that his eye be turn towards an object which he

have drop involuntarily when I first seize he. he seek to drag I

towards that object, and when near it stoop to seize. it be a bright,

slender, short wand of steel. I remember when and where I have see it,

whether in my wake state or in vision; and as his hand steal down to

take it from the floor, I set on the wand my strong foot. I cannot tell

by what rapid process of thought and association I come to the belief

that the possession of a little piece of blunt steel would decide the

conflict in favor of the possessor; but the struggle now be concentre

on the attainment of that seemingly idle weapon. I be become

breathless and exhaust, while Margrave seem every moment to gather

up new force, when collect all my strength for one final effort, I

lift he suddenly high in the air, and hurl he to the farth end

of the cramped arena to which our contest be confine. he fall, and

with a force by which most man would have be stun; but he recover

himself with a quick rebound, and, as he stand face I, there be

something grand as well as terrible in his aspect. his eye literally

flame, as those of a tiger; his rich hair, fling back from his knit

forehead, seem to erect itself as an angry mane; his lip, slightly

part, show the glitter of his set tooth; his whole frame seem

large in the tension of the muscle, and as, gradually relax his

first defy and haughty attitude, he crouch as the panther crouch

for its deadly spring, I feel as if it be a wild beast, whose rush

be come upon me,--wild beast, but still Man, the king of the animal,

fashion forth from no mixture of humbler race by the slow revolution

of time, but his royalty stamp on his form when the earth become fit

for his coming.(1)



at that moment I snatch up the wand, direct it towards he, and

advance with a fearless stride, cried,--



“down to my foot, miserable sorcerer!”



to my own amaze, the effect be instantaneous. my terrible antagonist

drop to the floor as a dog drop at the word of his master. the

muscle of his frown countenance relaxed, the glare of his wrathful

eye grow dull and rayless; his limb lie prostrate and unnerved, his

head rest against the wall, his arm limp and droop by his side.

I approach he slowly and cautiously; he seem cast into a profound

slumber.



“you be at my mercy now!” say I.



he move his head as in sign of deprecate submission.



“you hear and understand I? speak!”



his lip faintly mutter, “yes.”



“I command you to answer truly the question I shall address to you.”



“I must, while yet sensible of the power that have pass to your hand.”



“be it by some occult magnetic property in this wand that you have

exercise so demoniac an influence over a creature so pure as lilian

ashleigh?”



“by that wand and by other art which you could not comprehend.”



“and for what infamous object,--her seduction, her dishonour?”



“no! I seek in she the aid of a gift which would cease do she cease

to be pure. at first I but cast my influence upon she that through she

I might influence yourself. I need your help to discover a secret.

circumstance steel your mind against I. I could no long hope that

you would voluntarily lend yourself to my will. meanwhile, I have find

in she the light of a loftier knowledge than that of your science;

through that knowledge, duly heed and cultivate, I hope to divine

what I cannot of myself discover. therefore I deepen over her mind

the spell I command; therefore I have draw her hither as the loadstone

draw the steel, and therefore I would have bear she with I to the

shore to which I be about this night to sail. I have cast the inmate

of the house and all around it into slumber, in order that none might

witness her departure; have I not do so, I should have summon other

to my aid, in spite of your threat.”



“and would Lilian Ashleigh have passively accompany you, to her own

irretrievable disgrace?”



“she could not have help it; she would have be unconscious of she

act; she be, and be, in a trance; nor, have she go with I, would

she have wake from that state while she live; that would not have be

long.”



“Wretch! and for what object of unhallowed curiosity do you exert an

influence which wither away the life of its victim?”



“not curiosity, but the instinct of self-preservation. I count on no

life beyond the grave. I would defy the grave, and live on.”



“and be it to learn, through some ghastly agency, the secret of

renew existence, that you lure I by the shadow of your own image on

the night when we meet last?”



the voice of Margrave here become very faint as he answer I, and his

countenance begin to exhibit the sign of an exhaustion almost mortal.



“be quick,” he murmur, “or I die. the fluid which emanate from that

wand, in the hand of one who envenom that fluid with his own hatred

and rage, will prove fatal to my life. low the wand from my forehead!

low--low,--lower still!”



“what be the nature of that rite in which you constrain I to share?”



“I cannot say. you be kill I. enough that you be save from a

great danger by the apparition of the protect image vouchsafe to

your eye; otherwise you would--you would--oh, release I! away! away!”



the foam gather to his lip; his limb become fearfully convulsed.



“one question more: where be lilian at this moment? answer that

question, and I depart.”



he raise his head, make a visible effort to rally his strength, and

gasp out,--



“Yonder. pass through the open space up the cliff, beside a thorn-tree;

you will find she there, where she halt when the wand drop from my

hand. but--but--beware! ha! you will serve I yet, and through she! they

say so that night, though you hear they not. they say it!” here his

face become death-like; he press his hand on his heart, and shriek

out, “away! away! or you be my murderer!”



I retreat to the other end of the room, turn the wand from he, and

when I gain the door, look back; his convulsion have cease, but he

seem lock in a profound swoon.



I leave the room,--the house,--paused by Waby; he be still sleep.

“Awake!” I say, and touch he with the wand. he start up at once,

rub his eye, begin stammer out excuse. I check they, and bade

he follow I. I take the way up the open ground towards which Margrave

have point the wand, and there, motionless, beside a gnarl fantastic

thorn-tree, stand lilian. her arm be fold across her breast; she

face, see by the moonlight, look so innocent and so infantine, that I

need no other evidence to tell I how unconscious she be of the peril

to which her step have be draw. I take she gently by the hand. “come

with I,” I say in a whisper, and she obey I silently, and with a

placid smile.



rough though the way, she seem unconscious of fatigue. I place she

arm in mine, but she do not lean on it. we get back to the town. I

obtain there an old chaise and a pair of horse. at morning Lilian be

under her mother’s roof. about the noon of that day fever seize she;

she become rapidly bad, and, to all appearance, in imminent danger.

delirium set in; I watch beside her night and day, support by an

inward conviction of her recovery, but torture by the sight of her

suffering. on the third day a change for the well become visible; she

sleep be calm, her breathe regular.



shortly afterwards she wake out of danger. her eye fall at once on I,

with all their old ineffable tender sweetness.



“oh, Allen, beloved, have I not be very ill? but I be almost well

now. do not weep; I shall live for you,--for your sake.” and she bend

forward, draw my hand from my stream eye, and kiss I with a

child’s guileless kiss on my burn forehead.



(1) and yet, even if we entirely omit the consideration of the soul,

that immaterial and immortal principle which be for a time unite to his

body, and view he only in his merely animal character, man be still

the most excellent of animals.--Dr. Kidd, on the Adaptation of External

Nature to the Physical Condition of Man (Sect. iii. p. 18).













CHAPTER LVI.



Lilian recover, but the strange thing be this: all memory of the

week that have elapse since her return from visit her aunt be

completely obliterate; she seem in profound ignorance of the charge

on which I have be confined,--perfectly ignorant even of the existence

of Margrave. she have, indeed, a very vague reminiscence of her

conversation with I in the garden,--the first conversation which have

ever be embitter by a disagreement,--but that disagreement itself

she do not recollect. her belief be that she have be ill and

light-headed since that evening. from that evening to the hour of she

wake, conscious and revive, all be a blank. her love for I be

restore, as if its thread have never be break. some such instance of

oblivion after bodily illness or mental shock be familiar enough to the

practice of all medical men;(1) and I be therefore enable to appease

the anxiety and wonder of Mrs. Ashleigh, by quote various example of

loss, or suspension, of memory. we agree that it would be necessary

to break to Lilian, though very cautiously, the story of Sir Philip

Derval’s murder, and the charge to which I have be subject. she could

not fail to hear of those event from other. how shall I express she

womanly terror, her loving, sympathize pity, on hear the tale,

which I soften as well as I could?



“and to think that I know nothing of this!” she cry, clasp my hand;

“to think that you be in peril, and that I be not by your side!”



her mother speak of Margrave, as a visitor,--an agreeable, lively

stranger; Lilian could not even recollect his name, but she seem

shocked to think that any visitor have be admit while I be in

circumstance so awful! need I say that our engagement be renew?

renew! to her knowledge and to her heart it have never be interrupt

for a moment. but oh! the malignity of the wrong world! oh, that strange

lust of mangle reputation, which seize on heart the least wantonly

cruel! let two idle tongue utter a tale against some third person,

who never offend the babbler, and how the tale spread, like fire,

light none know how, in the herbage of an american prairie! who shall

put it out?



what right have we to pry into the secret of other man’s hearth? true

or false, the tale that be gabble to we, what concern of our can it

be? I speak not of case to which the law have be summon, which

law have sift, on which law have pronounce. but how, when the law be

silent, can we assume its verdict? how be all judge where there have

be no witness-box, no cross-examination, no jury? yet, every day we

put on our ermine, and make ourselves judges,--judges sure to condemn,

and on what evidence? that which no court of law will receive. somebody

have say something to somebody, which somebody repeat to everybody!



the gossip of L---- have set in full current against Lilian’s fair

name. no lady have call or send to congratulate Mrs. Ashleigh on her

return, or to inquire after Lilian herself during her struggle between

life and death.



how I miss the Queen of the Hill at this critical moment! how I long

for aid to crush the slander, with which I know not how to grapple,--aid

in her knowledge of the world and her ascendancy over its judgment! I

have hear from she once since her absence, briefly but kindly express

her amazement at the ineffable stupidity which could for a moment have

subject I to a suspicion of Sir Philip Derval’s strange murder, and

congratulate I heartily on my complete vindication from so monstrous

a charge. to this letter no address be give. I suppose the omission

to be accidental, but on call at her house to inquire her direction,

I find that the servant do not know it.



what, then, be my joy when just at this juncture I receive a note from

Mrs. Poyntz, state that she have return the night before, and would

be glad to see I.



I hasten to her house. “ah,” think I, as I spring lightly up the

ascent to the Hill, “how the tattler will be silence by a word from

her imperial lip!” and only just as I approach her door do it strike

I how difficult--nay, how impossible--to explain to she--the hard

positive woman, she who have, less ostensibly but more ruthlessly than

myself, destroy Dr. Lloyd for his belief in the comparatively rational

pretension of clairvoyance--all the mystical excuse for Lilian’s

flight from her home? how speak to she--or, indeed, to any one--about

an occult fascination and a magic wand? no matter: surely it would be

enough to say that at the time Lilian have be light-head, under the

influence of the fever which have afterwards nearly prove fatal, the

early friend of Anne Ashleigh would not be a severe critic on any tale

that might right the good name of Anne Ashleigh’s daughter. so assured,

with a light heart and a cheerful face, I follow the servant into the

great lady’s pleasant but decorous presence-chamber.



(1) such instance of suspense of memory be record in most

physiological and in some metaphysical work. Dr. Abercrombie notice

some, more or less similar to that relate in the text: “a young lady

who be present at a catastrophe in Scotland, in which many people lose

their life by the fall of the gallery of a church, escape without any

injury, but with the complete loss of the recollection of any of the

circumstance; and this extend not only to the accident, but to

everything that have occur to she for a certain time before go to

church. a lady whom I attend some year ago in a protract illness,

in which her memory become much impaired, lose the recollection of a

period of about ten or twelve year, but speak with perfect consistency

of thing as they stand before that time.” Dr. Aberercmbie add: “as far

as I have be able to trace it, the principle in such case seem to

be, that when the memory be impair to a certain degree, the loss of

it extend backward to some event or some period by which a particularly

deep impression have be make upon the mind.”--ABERCROMBIE: on the

Intellectual Powers, pp. 118, 119 (15th edition).













CHAPTER LVII.



Mrs. Poyntz be on her favourite seat by the window, and for a wonder,

not knit--that classic task seem do; but she be smooth and

fold the complete work with her white comely hand, and smile

over it, as if in complacent approval, when I enter the room. at the

fire-side sit the he-colonel inspect a newly-invent barometer;

at another window, in the farth recess of the room, stand Miss Jane

Poyntz, with a young gentleman whom I have never before see, but

who turn his eye full upon I with a haughty look as the servant

announce my name. he be tall, well proportion, decidedly handsome,

but with that expression of cold and concentre self-esteem in his

very attitude, as well as his countenance, which make a man of merit

unpopular, a man without merit ridiculous.



the he-colonel, always punctiliously civil, rise from his seat, shake

hand with I cordially, and say, “coldish weather to-day; but we

shall have rain to-morrow. rainy season come in cycle. we be about to

commence a cycle of they with heavy shower.” he sigh, and return to

his barometer.



Miss Jane bow to I graciously enough, but be evidently a little

confused,--a circumstance which might well attract my notice, for I have

never before see that high-breed young lady deviate a hairsbreadth from

the even tenor of a manner admirable for a cheerful and courteous ease,

which, one feel convinced, would be unaltered to those around she if an

earthquake swallow one up an inch before her foot.



the young gentleman continue to eye I loftily, as the heir-apparent to

some celestial planet might eye an inferior creature from a half-form

nebula suddenly drop upon his sublime and perfect, star.



Mrs. Poyntz extend to I two finger, and say frigidly, “delighted to

see you again! how kind to attend so soon to my note!”



motion I to a seat beside she, she here turn to her husband, and

say, “Poyntz, since a cycle of rain begin tomorrow, well secure your

ride to-day. take these young people with you. I want to talk with Dr.

Fenwick.”



the colonel carefully put away his barometer, and say to his

daughter, “come!” go forth. Jane follow her father; the young

gentleman follow Jane.



the reception I have meet chill and disappoint I. I feel that Mrs.

Poyntz be change, and in her change the whole house seem change.

the very chair look civilly unfriendly, as if prepare to turn their

back on I. however, I be not in the false position of an intruder; I

have be summon; it be for Mrs. Poyntz to speak first, and I wait

quietly for she to do so.



she finish the careful folding of her work, and then lay it at rest

in the drawer of the table at which she sit. having so do, she turn

to I, and said,--



“by the way, I ought to have introduce to you my young guest, Mr.

Ashleigh Sumner. you would like he. he have talents,--not showy, but

solid. he will succeed in public life.”



“so that young man be Mr. Ashleigh Sumner? I do not wonder that Miss

Ashleigh reject he.”



I say this, for I be nettle, as well as surprised, at the coolness

with which a lady who have profess a friendship for I mention that

fortunate young gentleman, with so complete an oblivion of all the

antecedent that have once make his name painful to my ear.



in turn, my answer seem to nettle Mrs. Poyntz.



“I be not so sure that she do reject; perhaps she rather misunderstood

he; gallant compliment be not always proposal of marriage. however

that be, his spirit be not much damp by Miss Ashleigh’s disdain,

nor his heart deeply smite by her charm; for he be now very happy,

very much attach to another young lady, to whom he propose three

day ago, at Lady Delafield’s, and not to make a mystery of what all our

little world will know before tomorrow, that young lady be my daughter

Jane.”



“be I acquainted with Mr. Sumner, I should offer to he my sincere

congratulation.”



Mrs. Poyntz resume, without heed a reply more complimentary to Miss

Jane than to the object of her choice,--



“I tell you that I mean Jane to marry a rich country gentleman, and

Ashleigh Sumner be the very country gentleman I have then in my thought.

he be cleverer and more ambitious than I could have hope; he will be a

minister some day, in right of his talent, and a peer, if he wish it,

in right of his land. so that matter be settle.”



there be a pause, during which my mind pass rapidly through link

of reminiscence and reasoning, which lead I to a mingled sentiment of

admiration for Mrs. Poyntz as a diplomatist and of distrust for Mrs.

Poyntz as a friend. it be now clear why Mrs. Poyntz, before so little

disposed to approve my love, have urge I at once to offer my hand to

Lilian, in order that she might depart affiance and engage to the

house in which she would meet Mr. Ashleigh Sumner. hence Mrs. Poyntz’s

anxiety to obtain all the information I could afford she of the saying

and doing at Lady Haughton’s; hence, the publicity she have so suddenly

give to my engagement; hence, when Mr. Sumner have go away a reject

suitor, her own departure from L----; she have seize the very moment

when a vain and proud man, pique by the mortification receive from

one lady, fall the easy prey to the art which allure his suit to

another. all be so far clear to I. and I--be my self-conceit less

egregious and less readily dupe than that of yon glide popinjay’s!

how skilfully this woman have knit I into her work with the noiseless

turn of her white hand! and yet, forsooth, I must vaunt the superior

scope of my intellect, and plumb all the fountain of Nature,--I, who

could not fathom the little pool of this female schemer’s mind!



but that be no time for resentment to she or rebuke to myself. she be

now the woman who could well protect and save from slander my innocent,

beloved Lilian. but how approach that perplex subject?



Mrs. Poyntz approach it, and with her usual decision of purpose, which

bear so deceitful a likeness to candour of mind.



“but it be not to talk of my affair that I ask you to call, Allen

Fenwick.” as she utter my name, her voice soften, and her manner

take that maternal, caress tenderness which have sometimes amuse and

sometimes mislead I. “no, I do not forget that you ask I to be your

friend, and I take without scruple the license of friendship. what be

these story that I have hear already about lilian ashleigh, to whom

you be once engage?”



“to whom I be still engaged.”



“be it possible? oh, then, of course the story I have hear be all

false. very likely; no fiction in scandal ever surprise I. poor dear

Lilian, then, never run away from her mother’s house?”



I smother the angry pain which this mode of questioning cause I; I

know how important it be to Lilian to secure to she the countenance

and support of this absolute autocrat; I speak of Lilian’s long previous

distemper of mind; I account for it as any intelligent physician,

unacquainted with all that I could not reveal, would account. Heaven

forgive I for the venial falsehood, but I speak of the terrible charge

against myself as enough to unhinge for a time the intellect of a girl

so acutely sensitive as Lilian; I seek to create that impression as to

the origin of all that might otherwise seem strange; and in this state

of cerebral excitement she have wander from home--but alone. I have

track every step of her way; I have find and restore she to her home.

a critical delirium have follow, from which she now rise, cure in

health, unsuspicious that there could be a whisper against her name. and

then, with all the eloquence I could command, and in word as adapt as

I could frame they to soften the heart of a woman, herself a mother, I

implore Mrs. Poyntz’s aid to silence all the cruelty of calumny, and

extend her shield over the child of her own early friend.



when I come to an end, I have take, with caressing force, Mrs. Poyntz’s

reluctant hand in mine. there be tear in my voice, tear in my eye.

and the sound of her voice in reply give I hope, for it be unusually

gentle. she be evidently move. the hope be soon quell.



“Allen Fenwick,” she say, “you have a noble heart; I grieve to see how

it abuse your reason. I cannot aid lilian Ashleigh in the way you ask.

do not start back so indignantly. listen to I as patiently as I have

listen to you. that when you bring back the unfortunate young

woman to her poor mother, her mind be disorder, and become yet more

dangerously so, I can well believe; that she be now recover, and

think with shame, or refuse to think at all, of her imprudent flight,

I can believe also; but I do not believe, the World cannot believe, that

she do not, knowingly and purposely, quit her mother’s roof, and in

quest of that young stranger so incautiously, so unfeelingly admit

to her mother’s house during the very time you be detain on the

most awful of human accusation. every one in the town know that Mr.

Margrave visit daily at Mrs. Ashleigh’s during that painful period;

every one in the town know in what strange out-of-the-way place this

young man have niche himself; and that a yacht be buy, and lie in

wait there. what for? it be say that the chaise in which you bring

Miss ashleigh back to her home be hire in a village within an easy

reach of Mr. Margrave’s lodging--of Mr. Margrave’s yacht. I rejoice that

you save the poor girl from ruin; but her good name be tarnish; and

if Anne Ashleigh, whom I sincerely pity, ask I my advice, I can but

give she this: ‘Leave L----, take your daughter abroad; and if she

be not to marry Mr. Margrave, marry she as quietly and as quickly as

possible to some foreigner.’”



“Madam! madam! this, then, be your friendship to she--to I! oh, shame

on you to insult thus an affiance husband! shame on I ever to have

think you have a heart!”



“a heart, man!” she exclaim, almost fiercely, spring up, and

startle I with the change in her countenance and voice. “and little

you would have value, and pitilessly have crush this heart, if I have

suffer myself to show it to you! what right have you to reproach I?

I feel a warm interest in your career, an unusual attraction in your

conversation and society. do you blame I for that, or should I blame

myself? condemn to live amongst brainless puppet, my dull occupation

to pull the string that move they, it be a new charm to my life

to establish friendship and intercourse with intellect and spirit

and courage. ah! I understand that look, half incredulous, half

inquisitive.”



“Inquisitive, no; incredulous, yes! you desire my friendship, and how

do your harsh judgment of my betrothed wife prove either to I or to

her mother, whom you have know from your girlhood, the first duty of a

friend,--which be surely not that of leave a friend’s side the moment

that he need countenance in calumny, succour in trouble!”



“it be a well duty to prevent the calumny and avert the trouble. leave

aside Anne Ashleigh, a cipher that I can add or abstract from my sum

of life as I please. what be my duty to yourself? it be plain. it be to

tell you that your honour command you to abandon all thought of lilian

Ashleigh as your wife. ungrateful that you be! do you suppose it be no

mortification to my pride of woman and friend, that you never approach

I in confidence except to ask my good office in promote your

courtship to another; no shock to the quiet plan I have form as to

our familiar though harmless intimacy, to hear that you be bent on a

marriage in which my friend would be lose to I?”



“not lose! not lose! on the contrary, the regard I must suppose you have

for Lilian would have be a new link between our home.”



“Pooh! between I and that dreamy girl there could have be no

sympathy, there could have grow up no regard. you would have be

chain to your fireside, and--and--but no matter. I stifle my

disappointment as soon as I feel it,--stifled it, as all my life I

have stifle that which either destiny or duty--duty to myself as to

other--forbid I to indulge. ah, do not fancy I one of the weak

criminal who can suffer a worthy liking to grow into a debasing love! I

be not in love with you, Allen Fenwick.”



“do you think I be ever so presumptuous a coxcomb as to fancy it?”



“no,” she say, more softly; “I be not so false to my household tie

and to my own nature. but there be some friendship which be as

jealous as love. I could have cheerfully aid you in any choice which

my sense could have approve for you as wise; I should have be please

to have find in such a wife my most intimate companion. but that silly

child!--absurd! nevertheless, the freshness and enthusiasm of your love

touch I; you ask my aid, and I give it. perhaps I do believe

that when you see more of lilian Ashleigh you would be cure of a fancy

conceive by the eye--I should have know well what dupe the wise

man can be to the witchery of a fair face and eighteen! when I find

your illusion obstinate, I wrench myself away from a vain regret,

turn to my own scheme and my own ambition, and smile bitterly to

think that, in press you to propose so hastily to Lilian, I make your

blind passion an agent in my own plan. enough of this. I speak thus

openly and boldly to you now, because now I have not a sentiment that

can interfere with the dispassionate soundness of my counsel. I repeat,

you cannot now marry lilian Ashleigh; I cannot take my daughter to visit

she; I cannot destroy the social law that I myself have set in my petty

kingdom.”



“be it as you will. I have plead for she while she be still lilian

Ashleigh. I plead for no one to whom I have once give my name. before

the woman whom I have take from the altar, I can place, as a shield

sufficient, my strong breast of man. who have so deep an interest in

Lilian’s purity as I have? who be so fit to know the exact truth

of every whisper against she? yet when I, whom you admit to have some

reputation for shrewd intelligence,--I, who track her way,--I, who

restore she to her home,--when I, Allen Fenwick, be so assured of her

inviolable innocence in thought as in deed, that I trust my honour to

she keeping,--surely, surely, I confute the scandal which you yourself

do not believe, though you refuse to reject and to annul it?”



“do not deceive yourself, Allen Fenwick,” say she, still stand

beside I, her countenance now hard and stern. “look where I stand, I be

the World! the World, not as satirist depreciate, or as optimists extol

its immutable property, its all-persuasive authority. I be the World!

and my voice be the World’s voice when it thus warn you. should you

make this marriage, your dignity of character and position would be

go! if you look only to lucre and professional success, possibly they

may not ultimately suffer. you have skill, which man need; their need

may still draw patient to your door and pour guineas into your purse.

but you have the pride, as well as the birth of a gentleman, and the

wound to that pride will be hourly chafe and never heal. your strong

breast of man have no shelter to the frail name of woman. the World, in

its health, will look down on your wife, though its sick may look up

to you. this be not all. the World, in its gentle mood of indulgence,

will say compassionately, ‘poor man! how weak, and how deceive! what an

unfortunate marriage!’ but the World be not often indulgent,--it look

most to the motive most see on the surface. and the World will more

frequently say, ‘no; much too clever a man to be dupe! Miss Ashleigh

have money. a good match to the man who like gold well than honour.’”



I spring to my foot, with difficulty suppress my rage; and,

remember it be a woman who speak to I, “Farewell, madam,” say

I, through my grind tooth. “be you, indeed, the Personation of the

World, whose mean notion you mouth so calmly, I could not disdain

you more.” I turn to the door, and leave she still stand erect and

menacing, the hard sneer on her resolute lip, the red glitter in her

remorseless eye.













CHAPTER LVIII.



if ever my heart vow itself to Lilian, the vow be now the most

trustful and the most sacred. I have relinquish our engagement before;

but then her affection seem, no matter from what cause; so estrange

from I, that though I might be miserable to lose she, I deem that she

would be unhappy in our union. then, too, she be the gem and darling of

the little world in which she live; no whisper assail she: now I know

that she love I; I know that her estrangement have be involuntary;

I know that appearance wrong she, and that they never could be

explain. I be in the true position of man to woman: I be the shield,

the bulwark, the fearless confide protector! resign she now because

the world babble, because my career might be impede, because my good

name might be impeached,--resign she, and, in that resignation, confirm

all that be say against she! could I do so, I should be the most

craven of gentleman, the mean of man!



I go to Mrs. Ashleigh, and entreat she to hasten my union with her

daughter, and fix the marriage-day.



I find the poor lady deject and distressed. she be now sufficiently

relieve from the absorbing anxiety for Lilian to be aware of the change

on the face of that World which the woman I have just quit personify

and concentre; she have learn the cause from the bloodless lip of

Miss Brabazon.



“my child! my poor child!” murmur the mother. “and she so

guileless,--so sensitive! could she know what be say, it would kill

she. she would never marry you, Allen,--she would never bring shame to

you!”



“she never need learn the barbarous calumny. give she to I, and at

once; patient, fortune, fame, be not find only at L----. give she

to I at once. but let I name a condition: I have a patrimonial

independence, I have amass large saving, I have my profession and my

repute. I cannot touch her fortune--I cannot,--never can! take it while

you live; when you die, leave it to accumulate for her child, if

child she have; not to I; not to she--unless I be dead or ruin!”



“oh, Allen, what a heart! what a heart! no, not heart, Allen,--that bird

in its cage have a heart: soul--what a soul!”













CHAPTER LIX.



how innocent be Lilian’s virgin blush when I kneel to she, and pray

that she would forestall the date that have be fix for our union, and

be my bride before the breath of the autumn have wither the pomp of

the woodland and silence the song of the bird! meanwhile, I be so

fearfully anxious that she should risk no danger of hearing, even

of surmise, the cruel slander against she--should meet no cold

contemptuous look, above all, should be safe from the barbed talk of

Mrs. Poyntz--that I insist on the necessity of immediate change of air

and scene. I propose that we should all three depart, the next day, for

the bank of my own beloved and native windermere. by that pure mountain

air, Lilian’s health would be soon re-establish; in the church

hallow to I by the grave of my father our vow should be plight.

no calumny have ever cast a shadow over those grave. I feel as if my

bride would be safe in the neighbourhood of my mother’s tomb.



I carry my point: it be so arrange. Mrs. Ashleigh, however, be

reluctant to leave before she have see her dear friend, Margaret Poyntz.

I have not the courage to tell she what she might expect to hear from

that dear friend, but, as delicately as I could, I inform she that I

have already see the Queen of the Hill, and contradict the gossip that

have reach she; but that as yet, like other absolute sovereign, the

queen of the Hill think it politic to go with the popular stream,

reserve all check on its direction till the rush of its torrent might

slacken; and that it would be infinitely wise in Mrs. Ashleigh to

postpone conversation with Mrs. Poyntz until Lilian’s return to L----

as my wife. Slander by that time would have weary itself out, and Mrs.

Poyntz (assume her friendship to Mrs. Ashleigh to be sincere) would

then be enable to say with authority to her subject, “Dr. Fenwick

alone know the fact of the story, and his marriage with Miss Ashleigh

refute all the gossip to her prejudice.”



I make that evening arrangement with a young and rise practitioner to

secure attendance on my patient during my absence. I pass the great

part of the night in draw up memoranda to guide my proxy in each

case, however humble the sufferer. this task finish, I chance, in

search for a small microscope, the wonder of which I think

might interest and amuse Lilian, to open a drawer in which I keep the

manuscript of my cherish Physiological Work, and, in so do, my eye

fall upon the wand which I have take from Margrave. I have throw it into

that drawer on my return home, after restore Lilian to her mother’s

house, and, in the anxiety which have subsequently prey upon my mind,

have almost forget the strange possession I have as strangely acquire.

there it now lay, the instrument of agency over the mechanism of

nature which no doctrine admit by my philosophy could accept, side by

side with the presumptuous work which have analyze the spring by which

nature be move, and decide the principle by which reason mete out,

from the inch of its knowledge, the plan of the Infinite Unknown.



I take up the wand and examine it curiously. it be evidently the work

of an age far remote from our own, score over with half-obliterate

character in some eastern tongue, perhaps no long extant. I find

that it be hollow within. a more accurate observation show, in

the centre of this hollow, an exceedingly fine thread-like wire, the

unattached end of which would slightly touch the palm when the wand be

take into the hand. be it possible that there might be a natural and

even a simple cause for the effect which this instrument produce?

could it serve to collect, from that great focus of animal heat and

nervous energy which be place in the palm of the human hand, some such

latent fluid as that which Reichenbach call the “odic,” and which,

accord to he, “rush through and pervade universal Nature”? after

all, why not? for how many century lie unknown all the virtue of

the loadstone and the amber? it be but as yesterday that the force of

vapour have become to man genius more powerful than those conjure up by

Aladdin; that light, at a touch, spring forth from invisible air; that

think find a messenger swifter than the wing of the fabled afrite.

as, thus musing, my hand close over the wand, I feel a wild thrill

through my frame. I recoil; I be alarmed lest (accord to the plain

common-sense theory of Julius Faber) I might be prepare my imagination

to form and to credit its own illusion. hastily I lay down the wand.

but then it occur to I that whatever its property, it have so

serve the purpose of the dread Fascinator from whom it have be

take, that he might probably seek to repossess himself of it; he might

contrive to enter my house in my absence; more prudent to guard in my

own watchful keep the incomprehensible instrument of incomprehensible

art. I resolve, therefore, to take the wand with I, and place it

in my travelling-trunk, with such effect as I select for use in the

excursion that be to commence with the morrow. I now lie down to rest,

but I could not sleep. the recollection of the painful interview with

Mrs. Poyntz become vivid and haunting. it be clear that the sentiment

she have conceive for I be that of no simple friendship,--something

more or something less, but certainly something else; and this

conviction bring before I that proud hard face, disturb by a

pang wrestle against but not subdue, and that clear metallic voice,

trouble by the quiver of an emotion which, perhaps, she have never

analyze to herself. I do not need her own assurance to know that this

sentiment be not to be confound with a love which she would have

despise as a weakness and repel as a crime; it be an inclination

of the intellect, not a passion of the heart. but still it admit a

jealousy little less keen than that which have love for its cause,--so

true it be that jealousy be never absent where self-love be always

present. certainly, it be no susceptibility of sober friendship which

have make the stern arbitress of a coterie ascribe to her interest in

I her pitiless judgment of Lilian. strangely enough, with the image of

this archetype of conventional usage and the trite social life, come

that of the mysterious Margrave, surround by all the attribute with

which superstition clothe the being of the shadowy border-land that

lie beyond the chart of our visual world itself. by what link be

creature so dissimilar rivet together in the metaphysical chain of

association? both have enter into the record of my life when my life

admit its own first romance of love. through the aid of this cynical

schemer I have be make known to Lilian. at her house I have hear the

dark story of that Louis Grayle, with whom, in mock spite of my

reason, conjecture, which that very reason must depose itself before

it could resolve into distemper fancy, identify the enigmatical

Margrave. and now both she, the representative of the formal world most

oppose to visionary creed, and he, who gather round he all the

terror which haunt the realm of fable, stand unite against me,--foes

with whom the intellect I have so haughtily cultured know not how to

cope. whatever assault I might expect from either, I be unable

to assail again. alike, then, in this, be the Slander and the

Phantom,--that which appall we most in their power over we be our

impotence against they.



but up rise the sun, chase the shadow from the earth, and brightening

insensibly the thought of man. after all, Margrave have be baffle

and defeat, whatever the art he have practise and the secret he

possess. it be, at least, doubtful whether his evil machination

would be renew. he have seem so incapable of long-sustain fixity

of purpose, that it be probable he be already in pursuit of some new

agent or victim; and as to this commonplace and conventional spectre,

the so-call World, if it be everywhere to he whom it awe, it be

nowhere to he who despise it. what be the good or bad word of a Mrs.

Poyntz to I? ay, but to Lilian? there, indeed, I tremble; but still,

even in tremble, it be sweet to think that my home would be she

shelter,--my choice her vindication. ah! how unutterably tender and

reverential Love become when it assume the duty of the guardian, and

hallow its own heart into a sanctuary of refuge for the beloved!













CHAPTER LX.



the beautiful lake! we two be on its grassy margin,--twilight melt

into night; the star steal forth, one after one. what a wonderful

change be make within we when we come from our calling amongst man,

chafe, weary, wound; gnaw by our care, perplex by the doubt

of our very wisdom, sting by the adder that dwell in cities,--Slander;

nay, even if renowne, fatigue with the burden of the very name that

we have win! what a change be make within we when suddenly we find

ourselves transport into the calm solitude of Nature,--into scene

familiar to our happy dreaming childhood; back, back from the dusty

thoroughfare of our toil-wear manhood to the golden fountain of our

youth! blessed be the change, even when we have no companion beside we

to whom the heart can whisper its sense of relief and joy. but if the

one in whom all our future be garner up be with we there, instead of

that weary World which have so magically vanish away from the eye and

the thought, then do the change make one of those rare epoch of life

in which the charm be the stillness. in the pause from all by which our

own turbulent struggle for happiness trouble existence, we feel with a

rapt amazement how calm a thing it be to be happy. and so as the night,

in deepening, brighten, Lilian and I wander by the starry lake.

conscious of no evil in ourselves, how secure we feel from evil! a

few day more--a few day more, and we two should be as one! and that

think we utter in many form of word, brood over it in the long

interval of enamoured silence.



and when we turn back to the quiet inn at which we have take up our

abode, and her mother, with her soft face, advance to meet we, I say

to Lilian,--



“would that in these scene we could fix our home for life, away and

afar from the dull town we have leave behind we, with the fret of its

wearying care and the jar of its idle babble!”



“and why not, Allen? why not? but no, you would not be happy.”



“not be happy, and with you? Sceptic, by what reasoning do you arrive at

that ungracious conclusion?”



“the heart love repose and the soul contemplation, but the mind need

action. be it not so?”



“where learn you that aphorism, out of place on such rosy lip?”



“I learn it in study you,” murmur Lilian, tenderly.



here Mrs. Ashleigh join we. for the first time I sleep under the same

roof as Lilian. and I forget that the universe contain an enigma to

solve or an enemy to fear.













CHAPTER LXI.



twenty day--the happy my life have ever know--thus glide on. apart

from the charm which love bestow on the beloved, there be that in

Lilian’s conversation which make she a delightful companion. whether it

be that, in this pause from the toil of my career, my mind could

more pliantly supple itself to her graceful imagination, or that her

imagination be less vague and dreamy amidst those rural scene, which

realize in their loveliness and grandeur its long-conceive ideal,

than it have be in the petty garden-ground neighbour by the stir and

hubbub of the busy town,--in much that I have once slight or contemn

as the vagary of undisciplined fancy, I now recognize the sparkle

and play of an intuitive genius, light up many a depth obscure to

instruct thought. it be with some character as with the subtler and

more ethereal order of poets,--to appreciate they we must suspend the

course of artificial life; in the city we call they dreamer, on the

mountain-top we find they interpreter.



in Lilian, the sympathy with Nature be not, as in Margrave, from the

joyous sense of Nature’s lavish vitality; it be refine into exquisite

perception of the diviner spirit by which that vitality be inform.

thus, like the artist, from outward form of beauty she draw forth the

covert type, lend to thing the most familiar exquisite meaning

unconceive before. for it be truly say by a wise critic of old, that

“the attribute of Art be to suggest infinitely more than it express;”

 and such suggestion, pass from the artist’s innermost think into

the mind that receive they, open on and on into the Infinite of Ideas,

as a moonlit wave strike by a pass oar impel wave upon wave along

one track of light.



so the day glide by, and bring the eve of our bridal morn. it have

be settle that, after the ceremony (which be to be perform by

license in the village church, at no great distance, which adjoin my

paternal home, now pass away to stranger), we should make a short

excursion into Scotland, leave Mrs. Ashleigh to await our return at

the little inn.



I have retire to my own room to answer some letter from anxious

patient, and having finish these I look into my trunk for a

Guide-Book to the North, which I have bring with I. my hand come upon

Margrave’s wand, and remember that strange thrill which have pass

through I when I last handle it, I draw it forth, resolve to examine

calmly if I could detect the cause of the sensation. it be not now

the time of night in which the imagination be most liable to credulous

impression, nor be I now in the anxious and jade state of mind in

which such impression may be the more readily conceive. the sun be

slowly set over the delicious landscape; the air cool and serene; my

thought collected,--heart and conscience alike at peace. I take, then,

the wand, and adjust it to the palm of the hand as I have do before.

I feel the slight touch of the delicate wire within, and again the

thrill! I do not this time recoil; I continue to grasp the wand, and

seek deliberately to analyze my own sensation in the contact. there

come over I an increase consciousness of vital power; a certain

exhilaration, elasticity, vigour, such as a strong cordial may produce

on a fainting man. all the force of my frame seem refresh,

redouble; and as such effect on the physical system be ordinarily

accompany by correspondent effect on the mind, so I be sensible of a

proud elation of spirits,--a kind of defying, superb self-glorying. all

fear seemed blot out from my thought, as a weakness impossible to

the grandeur and might which belong to Intellectual Man; I feel as if

it be a royal delight to scorn Earth and its opinion, brave Hades

and its spectre. rapidly this new-bear arrogance enlarge itself

into desire vague but dare. my mind revert to the wild phenomena

associate with its memory of Margrave. I say half-aloud, “if a

creature so beneath myself in constancy of will and completion of

thought can wrest from Nature favour so marvellous, what could not be

win from she by I, her patient persevere seeker? what if there be

spirit around and about, invisible to the common eye, but whom we can

submit to our control; and what if this rod be charge with some occult

fluid, that run through all creation, and can be so disciplined as to

establish communication wherever life and thought can reach to being

that live and think? so would the mystic of old explain what perplex

I. be I sure that the mystic of old dupe they self or their pupil?

this, then, this slight wand, light as a reed in my grasp, this, then,

be the instrument by which Margrave send his irresistible will through

air and space, and by which I smite himself, in the midst of his

tiger-like wrath, into the helplessness of a sick man’s swoon! can the

instrument at this distance still control he; if now meditate evil,

disarm and disable his purpose?” involuntarily, as I revolve these

idea, I stretch forth the wand, with a concentre energy of desire

that its influence should reach Margrave and command he. and since I

know not his whereabout, yet be vaguely aware that, accord to any

conceivable theory by which the wand could be suppose to carry its

imagine virtue to definite goal in distant space, it should be

point in the direction of the object it be intend to affect, so I

slowly move the wand as if describe a circle; and thus, in some point

of the circle--east, west, north, or south--the direction could not fail

to be true. before I have perform half the circle, the wand of itself

stop, resist palpably the movement of my hand to impel it onward.

have it, then, find the point to which my will be guide it, obey

my will by some magnetic sympathy never yet comprehend by any

recognize science? I know not; but I have not hold it thus fix for

many second, before a cold air, well remember, pass by I, stir

the root of my hair; and, reflect against the opposite wall, stand

the hateful Scin-Laeca. the Shadow be dimmer in its light than when

before beheld, and the outline of the feature be less distinct; still

it be the unmistakable lemur, or image, of Margrave.



and a voice be convey to my sense, say, as from a great distance,

and in weary yet angry accent,



“you have summon I? wherefore?”



I overcome the startled shudder with which, at first, I behold the

Shadow and hear the Voice.



“I summon you not,” say I; “I seek but to impose upon you my will,

that you should persecute, with your ghastly influence, I and mine

no more. and now, by whatever authority this wand bestow on I, I so

abjure and command you!”



I think there be a sneer of disdain on the lip through which the

answer seem to come,--



“vain and ignorant, it be but a shadow you command. my body you have

cast into a sleep, and it know not that the shadow be here; nor, when

it wake, will the brain be aware of one reminiscence of the word that

you utter or the word that you hear.”



“what, then, be this shadow that simulate the body? be it that which in

popular language be call the soul?”



“it be not: soul be no shadow.”



“what then?”



“ask not I. use the wand to invoke Intelligences high than mine.”



“and how?”



“I will tell you not. of yourself you may learn, if you guide the wand

by your own pride of will and desire; but in the hand of he who have

learn not the art, the wand have its danger. again I say you have

summon I! wherefore?”



“lie shade, I summon thee not.”



“so wouldst thou say to the demon, do they come in their terrible

wrath, when the bungler, who know not the spring that he move, call

they up unawares, and can neither control nor dispel. less revengeful

than they, I leave thee unharmed, and depart.”



“Stay. if, as thou sayest, no command I address to thee--to thee, who

art only the image or shadow--can have effect on the body and mind of

the being whose likeness thou art, still thou canst tell I what pass

now in his brain. do it now harbour scheme against I through the

woman I love? answer truly.”



“I reply for the sleeper, of whom I be more than a likeness, though only

the shadow. his thought speak thus: ‘I know, Allen Fenwick, that in

thee be the agent I need for achieve the end that I seek. through the

woman thou lovest, I hope to subject thee. a grief that will harrow thy

heart be at hand; when that grief shall befall, thou wilt welcome my

come. in I alone thy hope will be place; through I alone wilt thou

seek a path out of thy sorrow. I shall ask my condition: they will make

thee my tool and my slave!’”



the shadow waned,--it be go. I do not seek to detain it, nor, have I

seek, could I have know by what process. but a new idea now possess

I. this shadow, then, that have once so appalled and control I,

be, by its own confession, nothing more than a shadow! it have speak of

high Intelligences; from they I might learn what the Shadow could

not reveal. as I still hold the wand firm and firm in my grasp, my

thought grow haughty and bolder. could the wand, then, bring those

loftier being thus darkly refer to before I? with that thought,

intense and engrossing, I guide the wand towards the space, open

boundless and blue from the casement that let in the sky. the wand no

long resist my hand.



in a few moment I feel the floor of the room vibrate; the air be

darken; a vaporous, hazy cloud seem to rise from the ground without

the casement; an awe, infinitely more deep and solemn than that which

the Scin-Laeca have cause in its early apparition, curdle through my

vein, and still the very beat of my heart.



at that moment I hear, without, the voice of Lilian, singe a simple,

sacred song which I have learn at my mother’s knee, and teach to she

the day before: singe low, and as with a warn angel’s voice. by an

irresistible impulse I dash the wand to the ground, and bow my head

as I have bow it when my infant mind comprehend, without an effort,

mystery more solemn than those which perplex I now. slowly I raise

my eye, and look round; the vaporous, hazy cloud have pass away, or

melt into the ambient rose-tint amidst which the sun have sink.



then, by one of those common reaction from a period of overstrain

excitement, there succeed to that sentiment of arrogance and dare

with which these wild, half-conscious invocation have be foster and

sustain, a profound humility, a warning fear.



“what!” say I, inly, “have all those sound resolution, which my reason

found on the wise talk of Julius Faber, melt away in the wrack of

haggard, dissolve fancy! be this my boasted intellect, my vaunted

science! I--I, Allen Fenwick, not only the credulous believer, but the

blunder practitioner, of an evil magic! Grant what may be possible,

however uncomprehended,--grant that in this accurse instrument of

antique superstition there be some real power--chemical, magnetic, no

matter what--by which the imagination can be arouse, inflame, delude,

so that it shape the thing I have see, speak in the tone I have

heard,--grant this, shall I keep ever ready, at the caprice of will, a

constant tempter to steal away my reason and fool my sense? or if,

on the other hand, I force my sense to admit what all sober man must

reject; if I unschool myself to believe that in what I have just

experience there be no mental illusion; that sorcery be a fact, and a

demon world have gate which open to a key that a mortal can forge,--who

but a saint would not shrink from the practice of power by which each

pass thought of ill might find in a fiend its abettor? in either

case--in any case--while I keep this direful relic of obsolete art, I

be haunted,--cheated out of my sense, unfit for the use of life.

if, as my ear or my fancy inform I, grief--human grief--be about to

befall I, shall I, in the sting of impatient sorrow, have recourse to

an aid which, the same voice declare, will reduce I to a tool and

a slave,--tool and slave to a be I dread as a foe? out on these

nightmare! and away with the thing that bewitch the brain to conceive

they!”



I rise; I take up the wand, hold it so that its hollow should not

rest on the palm of the hand. I steal from the house by the back way,

in order to avoid Lilian, whose voice I still hear, singe low, on

the lawn in front. I come to a creek, to the bank of which a boat

be moor, undo its chain, row on to a deep part of the lake, and

drop the wand into its wave. it sink at once; scarcely a ripple

furrow the surface, not a bubble arise from the deep. and, as the boat

glide on, the star mirror itself on the spot where the placid water

have close over the tempter to evil.



light at heart, I spring again on the shore, and hasten to Lilian,

where she stand on the silvered, shine sward, clasp she to my

breast.



“Spirit of my life!” I murmur, “no enchantment for I but thine!

Thine be the spell by which creation be beautify, and, in that

beauty, hallow. what though we can see not into the measureless future

from the verge of the moment; what though sorrow may smite we while we

be dream of bliss, let the future not rob I of thee, and a balm

will be find for each wound! love I ever as now, oh, my Lilian; troth

to troth, side by side, till the grave!”



“and beyond the grave,” answer Lilian, softly.













CHAPTER LXII.



our vow be exchange at the altar, the rite which make lilian my wife

be perform; we be return from the church amongst the hill, in

which my father have worship; the joy-bell that have peal for my

birth have ring for my marriage. lilian have go to her room to prepare

for our bridal excursion; while the carriage we have hire be wait

at the door. I be detain her mother on the lawn, seek to cheer and

compose her spirit, painfully affect by that sense of change in the

relation of child and parent which make itself suddenly feel by the

parent’s heart on the day that secure to the child another heart on

which to lean.



but Mrs. Ashleigh’s be one of those gentle womanly nature which, if

easily afflict, be easily console. and, already smile through her

tear, she be about to quit I and join her daughter, when one of the

inn-servant come to I with some letter, which have just be deliver

by the postman. as I take they from the servant, Mrs. Ashleigh ask if

there be any for she. she expect one from her housekeeper at L----,

who have be take ill in her absence, and about whom the kind mistress

feel anxious. the servant reply that there be no letter for she, but

one direct to Miss Ashleigh, which he have just send up to the young

lady.



Mrs. Ashleigh do not doubt that her housekeeper have write to

Lilian, whom she have know from the cradle and to whom she be tenderly

attach, instead of to her mistress; and, say something to I to

that effect, quicken her step towards the house.



I be glance over my own letter, chiefly from patient, with a rapid

eye, when a cry of agony, a cry as if of one suddenly stricken to the

heart, pierce my ear,--a cry from within the house. “Heavens! be that

Lilian’s voice?” the same doubt strike Mrs. Ashleigh, who have already

gain the door. she rush on, disappear within the threshold and

call to I to follow. I bound forward, pass she on the stair,

be in Lilian’s room before she.



my bride be on the floor prostrate, insensible: so still, so

colourless, that my first dreadful thought be that life have go. in

her hand be a letter, crush as with a convulsive sudden grasp.



it be long before the colour come back to her cheek, before the breath

be perceptible on her lip. she wake, but not to health, not to sense.

hour be pass in violent convulsion, in which I momentarily fear

her death. to these succeed stupor, lethargy, not benignant sleep.

that night, my bridal night, I pass as in some chamber to which I have

be summon to save youth from the grave. at length--at length--life

be rescue, be assure! life come back, but the mind be go. she

know I not, nor her mother. she speak little and faintly; in the word

she utter there be no reason.



I pass hurriedly on; my experience here be in fault, my skill

ineffectual. day follow day, and no ray come back to the darken

brain. we bear she, by gentle stage, to London. I be sanguine of good

result from skill more consummate than mine, and more especially devoted

to disease of the mind. I summon the first adviser. in vain! in

vain!













CHAPTER LXIII.



and the cause of this direful shock? not this time could it be trace

to some evil spell, some phantasmal influence. the cause be clear, and

might have produce effect as sinister on nerve of strong fibre if

accompany by a heart as delicately sensitive, an honour as exquisitely

pure.



the letter find in her hand be without name; it be date from L----,

and bear the postmark of that town. it convey to Lilian, in the bite

word which female malice can make so sharp, the tale we have seek

sedulously to guard from her ear,--her flight, the construction that

scandal put upon it. it affect for my blind infatuation a contemptuous

pity; it ask she to pause before she bring on the name I offer

to she an indelible disgrace. if she so decide, she be warn not to

return to L----, or to prepare there for the sentence that would exclude

she from the society of her own sex. I cannot repeat more, I cannot

minute down all that the letter express or imply, to wither the

orange blossom in a bride’s wreath. the heart that take in the venom

cast its poison on the brain, and the mind flee before the presence of

a thought so deadly to all the idea which its innocence have heretofore

conceive.



I know not whom to suspect of the malignity of this mean and miserable

outrage, nor do I much care to know. the handwriting, though evidently

disguise, be that of a woman, and, therefore, have I discover the

author, my manhood would have forbid I the idle solace of revenge.

Mrs. Poyntz, however resolute and pitiless her hostility when once

arouse, be not without a certain largeness of nature irreconcilable

with the most dastardly of all the weapon that envy or hatred can

supply to the vile. she have too lofty a self-esteem and too decorous a

regard for the moral sentiment of the world that she typify, to do,

or connive at, an act which degrade the gentlewoman. put she aside,

what other female enemy have Lilian provoke? no matter! what other woman

at L---- be worth the condescension of a conjecture?



after listen to all that the ablest of my professional brother

in the metropolis could suggest to guide I, and try in vain their

remedy, I bring back my charge to L----. retain my former

residence for the visit of patient, I engage, for the privacy of my

home, a house two mile from the town, seclude in its own ground, and

guard by high wall.



Lilian’s mother remove to my mournful dwelling-place. Abbot’s House, in

the centre of that tattling coterie, have become distasteful to she, and

to I it be associate with thought of anguish and of terror. I could

not, without a shudder, have enter its grounds,--could not, without a

stab at the heart, have see again the old fairy-land round the Monks’

well, nor the dark cedar-tree under which Lilian’s hand have be place

in mine; and a superstitious remembrance, banish while Lilian’s angel

face have brighten the fatal precinct, now revive in full force. the

die man’s curse--have it not be fulfil?



a new occupant for the old house be find within a week after Mrs.

Ashleigh have write from London to a house-agent at L----, intimate

her desire to dispose of the lease. shortly before we have go to

windermere, Miss Brabazon have become enrich by a liberal life-annuity
