bequeath to she by her uncle, Sir Phelim. her mean thus enable she

to move from the comparatively humble lodging she have hitherto occupy

to Abbot’s House; but just as she have there commence a series of

ostentatious entertainment, imply an ambitious desire to dispute

with Mrs. Poyntz the sovereignty of the Hill, she be attack by some

severe malady which appear complicated with spinal disease, and after

my return to L---- I sometimes meet she, on the spacious platform of the

Hill, draw along slowly in a Bath chair, her livid face peer forth

from pile of indian shawl and siberian fur, and the gaunt figure

of Dr. Jones stalk by her side, taciturn and gloomy as some sincere

mourner who conduct to the grave the patron on whose life he himself

have conveniently live. it be in the dismal month of February that I

return to L----, and I take possession of my plighted nuptial home on

the anniversary of the very day in which I have pass through the dead

dumb world from the naturalist’s gloomy death-room.













CHAPTER LXIV.



Lilian’s wondrous gentleness of nature do not desert she in the

suspension of her reason. she be habitually calm,--very silent; when

she speak it be rarely on earthly thing, on thing familiar to she

past, thing one could comprehend. her thought seem to have quit

the earth, seek refuge in some imaginary heaven. she speak of

wandering with her father as if he be live still; she do not seem

to understand the meaning we attach to the word “death.” she would sit

for hour murmur to herself: when one seek to catch the word, they

seem in converse with invisible spirit. we find it cruel to disturb

she at such time, for if leave unmoleste, her face be serene,--more

serenely beautiful than I have see it even in our happy hour; but

when we call she back to the wreck of her real life, her eye become

troubled, restless, anxious, and she would sigh--oh, so heavily! at

time, if we do not seem to observe she, she would quietly resume she

once favourite accomplishments,--drawing, music. and in these her

young excellence be still apparent, only the drawing be strange and

fantastic: they have a resemblance to those with which the painter Blake,

himself a visionary, illustrate the Poems of the “Night Thoughts” and

“the Grave,”--faces of exquisite loveliness, form of aerial grace,

come forth from the bell of flower, or float upwards amidst

the spray of fountain, their outline melt away in fountain or in

flower. so with her music: her mother could not recognize the air she

play, for a while so sweetly and with so ineffable a pathos, that

one could scarcely hear she without weeping; and then would come, as

if involuntarily, an abrupt discord, and, start, she would cease and

look around, disquiet, aghast.



and still she do not recognize Mrs. Ashleigh nor myself as her mother,

her husband; but she have by degree learn to distinguish we both from

other. to her mother she give no name, seem pleased to see she, but

not sensibly to miss she when away; I she call she brother: if long

absent than usual, I she miss. when, after the toil of the day, I

come to join she, even if she speak not, her sweet face brighten. when

she sing, she beckon I to come near to she, and look at I fixedly,

with eye ever tender, often tearful; when she draw she would pause and

glance over her shoulder to see that I be watch she, and point to

the drawing with a smile of strange significance, as if they convey

in some covert allegory message mean for I; so, at least, I

interpret her smile, and teach myself to say, “yes, Lilian, I

understand!”



and more than once, when I have so answer, she rise, and kiss

my forehead. I think my heart would have break when I feel that

spirit-like melancholy kiss.



and yet how marvellously the human mind teach itself to extract

consolation from its sorrow. the least wretched of my hour be

those that I have pass in that sadden room, seek how to establish

fragment of intercourse, invent sign, by which each might interpret

each, between the intellect I have so laboriously cultured, so arrogantly

vaunted, and the fancy wander through the dark, deprive of their

guide in reason. it be something even of joy to feel myself need for

her guardianship, endear and yearn for still by some unshattered

instinct of her heart; and when, part from she for the night, I stole

the moment in which on her soft face seem rest least of shadow, to

ask, in a trembling whisper, “Lilian, be the angel watch over

you?” and she would answer “yes,” sometimes in word, sometimes with a

mysterious happy smile--then--then I go to my lonely room, comfort

and thankful.













CHAPTER LXV.



the blow that have fall on my hearth effectually, inevitably kill

all the slander that might have trouble I in joy. before the awe of a

great calamity the small passion of a mean malignity slink abash. I

have request Mrs. Ashleigh not to mention the vile letter which Lilian

have receive. I would not give a triumph to the unknown calumniator, nor

wring forth her vain remorse, by the pain of acknowledge an indignity

to my darling’s honour; yet, somehow or other, the true cause of

Lilian’s affliction have creep out,--perhaps through the talk of

servants,--and the public shock be universal. by one of those instinct

of justice that lie deep in human heart, though in ordinary moment

overlay by many a worldly layer, all feel (all mother feel especially)

that innocence alone could have be so unprepared for reproach. the

explanation I have previously give, discredit then, be now accept

without a question. Lilian’s present state account for all that ill

nature have before misconstrue. her good name be restore to its maiden

whiteness, by the fate that have sever the tie of the bride. the

formal dweller on the Hill vie with the franker, warmer-hearte

household of Low Town in the nameless attention by which sympathy and

respect be rather delicately indicate than noisily proclaim. could

Lilian have then recover and be sensible of its repentant homage,

how reverently that petty world would have throng around she! and, ah!

could fortune and man’s esteem have atone for the blight of hope that

have be plant and cherish on ground beyond their reach, ambition

and pride might have be well content with the largeness of the

exchange that court their acceptance. patient on patient crowd on

I. Sympathy with my sorrow seem to create and endear a more trustful

belief in my skill. but the profession I have once so enthusiastically

love become to I wearisome, insipid, distasteful; the kindness heap

on I give no comfort,--it but bring before I more vividly the

conviction that it come too late to avail I: it could not restore to I

the mind, the love, the life of my life, which lie dark and shatter in

the brain of my guileless Lilian. secretly I feel a sullen resentment.

I know that to the crowd the resentment be unjust. the world itself be

but an appearance; who can blame it if appearance guide its law? but

to those who have be detach from the crowd by the profession of

friendship,--those who, when the slander be yet new, and might have

be awe into silence have they stand by my side,--to the pressure of

their hand, now, I have no response.



against Mrs. Poyntz, above all other, I bear a remembrance of

unrelaxed, unmitigable indignation. her scheme for her daughter’s

marriage have triumph: Jane be Mrs. Ashleigh Sumner. her mind be,

perhaps, soften now that the object which have sharpen its worldly

faculty be accomplish: but in vain, on first hearing of my

affliction, have this she-Machiavel own a humane remorse, and, with all

her keen comprehension of each facility that circumstance give to

she will, avail herself of the general compassion to strengthen the

popular reaction in favour of Lilian’s assaulted honour; in vain have

she write to I with a gentleness of sympathy foreign to her habitual

characteristic; in vain besought I to call on she; in vain waylay

and accost I with a humility that almost implore forgiveness. I

vouchsafe no reproach, but I could imply no pardon. I put between she

and my great sorrow the impenetrable wall of my freezing silence.



one word of hers at the time that I have so pathetically besought she

aid, and the parrot-flock that repeat she very whisper in noisy

shrillness would have be as loud to defend as it have be to defame;

that vile letter might never have be write. whoever its writer, it

surely be one of the babbler who take their malice itself from the

jest or the nod of their female despot; and the writer might have

justify herself in say she do but coarsely proclaim what the

oracle of worldly opinion, and the early friend of Lilian’s own mother,

have authorize she to believe.



by degree, the bitterness at my heart diffuse itself to the

circumference of the circle in which my life go its cheerless

mechanical round. that cordial brotherhood with his patient, which be

the true physician’s happy gift and human duty, forsook my

breast. the warning word of Mrs. Poyntz have come true. a patient that

monopolize my thought await I at my own hearth! my conscience become

troubled; I feel that my skill be lessen. I say to myself, “the

physician who, on enter the sick-room, feel, while there, something

that distract the fine power of his intellect from the sufferer’s

case be unfit for his calling.” a year have scarcely pass since my

fatal wedding day, before I have form a resolution to quit L---- and

abandon my profession; and my resolution be confirm, and my goal

determine, by a letter I receive from Julius Faber.



I have write at length to he, not many day after the blow that have

fall on I, state all circumstance as calmly and clearly as my

grief would allow; for I hold his skill at a high estimate than that

of any live brother of my art, and I be not without hope in the

efficacy of his advice. the letter I now receive from he have be

begin, and continue at some length, before my communication reach

he; and this early portion contain animate and cheerful

description of his australian life and home, which contrast with the

sorrowful tone of the supplement write in reply to the tiding with

which I have wring his friendly and tender heart. in this, the latter

part of his letter, he suggest that if time have work no material

change for the well, it might be advisable to try the effect of

foreign travel. scene entirely new might stimulate observation, and

the observation of thing external withdraw the sense from that brooding

over image delusively form within, which characterize the kind of

mental alienation I have describe. “let any intellect create for itself

a visionary world, and all reasoning build on it be fallacious: the

visionary world vanish in proportion as we can arouse a predominant

interest in the actual.”



this grand authority, who owe half his consummate skill as a

practitioner to the scope of his knowledge as a philosopher, then

proceed to give I a hope which I have not dare of myself to form. he

said:--





   “I distinguish the case you so minutely detail from that insanity which

    be reason lose; here it seem rather to be reason hold in suspense.

    where there be hereditary predisposition, where there be organic

    change of structure in the brain,--nay, where there be that kind of

    insanity which take the epithet of moral, whereby the whole

    character become so transform that the prime element of sound

    understanding, conscience itself, be either erase or warp into the

    sanction of what in a healthful state it would most disapprove,--it be

    only charlatan who promise effectual cure.  but here I assume that

    there be no hereditary taint; here I be convince, from my own

    observation, that the nobility of the organ, all fresh as yet in the

    vigour of youth, would rather submit to death than to the permanent

    overthrow of their equilibrium in reason; here, where you tell I the

    character preserve all its moral attribute of gentleness and purity,

    and but over-indulge its own early habit of estranged contemplation;

    here, without deceive you in false kindness, I give you the

    guarantee of my experience when I bid you ‘hope!’  I be persuade

    that, soon or later, the mind, thus for a time affect, will right

    itself; because here, in the cause of the malady, we do but deal with

    the nervous system.  and that, once right, and the mind once

    discipline in those practical duty which conjugal life

    necessitate, the malady itself will never return; never be

    transmit to the child on whom your wife’s restoration to health

    may permit you to count hereafter.  if the course of travel I

    recommend and the prescription I conjoin with that course fail you,

    let I know; and though I would fain close my day in this land, I

    will come to you.  I love you as my son.  I will tend your wife as my

    daughter.”



foreign travel! the idea smile on I. Julius Faber’s companionship,

sympathy, matchless skill! the very thought seem as a raft to a

drown mariner. I now read more attentively the early portion of

his letter. they describe, in glow colour, the wondrous country in

which he have fix his home; the joyous elasticity of its atmosphere;

the freshness of its primitive, pastoral life; the strangeness of its

scenery, with a Flora and a Fauna which have no similitude in the

ransack quarter of the Old World. and the strong impulse seize I to

transfer to the solitude of that blithesome and hardy nature a spirit

no long at home in the civilized haunt of man, and household god

that shrink from all social eye, and would fain have find a wilderness

for the desolate hearth, on which they have cease to be sacred if

unveil. as if to give practical excuse and reason for the idea that

seize I, Julius Faber mention, incidentally, that the house and

property of a wealthy speculator in his immediate neighbourhood be on

sale at a price which seem to I alluringly trivial, and, accord to

his judgment, far below the value they would soon reach in the hand of

a more patient capitalist. he write at the period of the agricultural

panic in the colony which precede the discovery of its early

gold-field. but his geological science have convince he that strata

within and around the property now for sale be auriferous, and

his intelligence enable he to predict how inevitably man would be

attract towards the gold, and how surely the gold would fertilize the

soil and enrich its owner. he describe the house thus to be sell--in

case I might know of a purchaser. it have be build at a cost unusual

in those early time, and by one who cling to English taste amidst

australian wild, so that in this purchase a settler would escape the

hardship he have then ordinarily to encounter; it be, in short, a home

to which a man more luxurious than I might bear a bride with want less

simple than those which now suffice for my darling Lilian.



this communication dwelt on my mind through the avocation of the day

on which I receive it, and in the evening I read all, except the

supplement, aloud to Mrs. Ashleigh in her daughter’s presence. I desire

to see if Faber’s description of the country and its life, which in

themselves be extremely spirited and striking, would arouse Lilian’s

interest. at first she do not seem to heed I while I read; but when

I come to Faber’s love account of little Amy, Lilian turn her eye

towards I, and evidently listen with attention. he write how the

child have already become the most useful person in the simple household.

how watchful the quickness of the heart have make the service of the eye;

all their association of comfort have grow round her active, noiseless

movement; it be she who have contrive to monopolize the management,

or supervision, of all that add to home the nameless, interior charm.

under her eye the rude furniture of the log-house grew invite with

english neatness; she take charge of the dairy; she have make the garden

gay with flower select from the wild, and suggest the trellis

walk, already cover with hardy vine. she be their confidant in every

plan of improvement, their comforter in every anxious doubt, their nurse

in every pass ailment, her very smile a refreshment in the weariness

of daily toil. “how all that be good in womanhood,” write the old man,

with the enthusiasm which no time have reft from his hearty, healthful

genius,--“how all that be good in womanhood be here open fast into

flower from the bud of the infant’s soul! the atmosphere seem to suit

it,--the child-woman in the child-world!”



I hear lilian sigh; I look towards she furtively; tear stand in she

soften eye; her lip be quiver. presently, she begin to rub she

right hand over the left--over the wedding-ring--at first slowly; then

with quick movement.



“it be not here,” she say impatiently; “it be not here!”



“what be not here?” ask Mrs. Ashleigh, hang over she.



Lilian lean back her head on her mother’s bosom, and answer

faintly,--



“the stain! some one say there be a stain on this hand. I do not see

it, do you?”



“there be no stain, never be,” say I; “the hand be white as your own

innocence, or the lily from which you take your name.”



“Hush! you do not know my name. I will whisper it. Soft!--my name be

Nightshade! do you want to know where the lily be now, brother? I will

tell you. there, in that letter. you call she Amy,--she be the

lily; take she to your breast, hide she. hist! what be those bell?

marriage-bell. do not let she hear they; for there be a cruel wind that

whisper the bell, and the bell ring out what it whisper, loud and

loud,





“‘stain on lily Shame on lily,  Wither lily.’



“if she hear what the wind whisper to the bell, she will creep away

into the dark, and then she, too, will turn to Nightshade.”



“lilian, look up, awake! you have be in a long, long dream: it be

pass away. Lilian, my beloved, my bless Lilian!”



never till then have I hear from she even so vague an allusion to the

fatal calumny and its dreadful effect, and while her word now pierce

my heart, it beat, amongst its pang, with a thrilling hope.



but, alas! the idea that have gleam upon she have vanish already. she

murmur something about Circles of Fire, and a Veiled Woman in black

garment; become restless, agitated, and unconscious of our presence,

and finally sink into a heavy sleep.



that night (my room be next to hers with the intervene door open)

I hear she cry out. I hasten to her side. she be still asleep, but

there be an anxious labour expression on her young face, and yet

not an expression wholly of pain--for her lip be part with a

smile,--that glad yet troubled smile with which one who have be

revolve some subject of perplexity or fear greet a sudden thought

that seem to solve the riddle, or prompt the escape from danger; and

as I softly take her hand she return my gentle pressure, and incline

towards I, say, still in sleep,--



“let we go.”



“Whither?” I answer, under my breath, so as not to awake she; “be it

to see the child of whom I read, and the land that be bloom out of

the earth’s childhood?”



“out of the dark into the light; where the leave do not change; where

the night be our day, and the winter our summer. let we go! let we go!”



“we will go. dream on undisturbed, my bride. oh, that the dream could

tell you that my love have not change in our sorrow, holy and deep

than on the day in which our vow be exchange! in you still all my

hope fold their wing; where you be, there still I myself have my

dreamland!”



the sweet face grow bright as I speak; all trouble leave the smile;

softly she draw her hand from my clasp, and rest it for a moment on my

bended head, as if in blessing.



I rise; stole back to my own room, close the door, lest the sob I

could not stifle should mar her sleep.













CHAPTER LXVI.



I unfold my new prospect to Mrs. Ashleigh. she be more easily

reconcile to they than I could have suppose, judge by her habit,

which be naturally indolent, and averse to all that disturb their

even tenor. but the great grief which have befall she have rouse up

that strength of devotion which lie dormant in all heart that be

capable of love another more than self. with her full consent I write

to Faber, communicate my intention, instruct he to purchase the

property he have so commend, and inclose my banker’s order for the

amount, on an australian firm. I now announce my intention to retire

from my profession; make prompt arrangement with a successor to my

practice; dispose of my two house at L----; fix the day of my

departure. Vanity be dead within I, or I might have be gratify by

the sensation which the news of my design create. my fault become at

once forget; such good quality as I might possess be exaggerated.

the public regret vent and console itself in a costly testimonial,

to which even the poor of my patient insist on the privilege to

contribute, grace with an inscription flatter enough to have serve

for the epitaph on some great man’s tomb. no one who have serve an art

and striven for a name be a stoic to the esteem of other; and sweet

indeed would such honour have be to I have not publicity itself

seem a wrong to the sanctity of that affliction which set lilian apart

from the movement and the glory of the world.



the two person most active in “get up” this testimonial be,

nominally, Colonel Poyntz--in truth, his wife--and my old disparager,

Mr. Vigors! it be long since my narrative have refer to Mr. Vigors. it

be due to he now to state that, in his capacity of magistrate, and in

his own way, he have be both active and delicate in the inquiry set

on foot for Lilian during the unhappy time in which she have wander,

spellbound, from her home. he, alone, of all the more influential

magnate of the town, have uphold her innocence against the gossip that

asperse it; and during the last try year of my residence at L----,

he have seek I, with frank and manly confession of his regret for his

former prejudice against I, and assurance of the respect in which he

have hold I ever since my marriage--marriage but in rite--with Lilian.

he have then, strong in his rule passion, besought I to consult his

clairvoyant as to her case. I decline this invitation so as not to

affront him,--declined it, not as I should once have do, but with no

word nor look of incredulous disdain. the fact be, that I have conceive

a solemn terror of all practice and theory out of the beat track of

sense and science. perhaps in my refusal I do wrong. I know not. I be

afraid of my own imagination. he continue not less friendly in spite

of my refusal. and, such be the vicissitude in human feeling, I

part from he whom I have regard as my most bigote foe with a warmer

sentiment of kindness than for any of those on whom I have count on

friendship. he have not desert Lilian. it be not so with Mrs. Poyntz.

I would have pay tenfold the value of the testimonial to have erase,

from the list of those who subscribe to it, her husband’s name.



the day before I quit L----, and some week after I have, in fact,

renounce my practice, I receive an urgent entreaty from Miss Brabazon

to call on she. she write in line so blur that I could with

difficulty decipher they, that she be very ill, give over by Dr.

Jones, who have be attend she. she implore my opinion.













CHAPTER LXVII.



on reach the house, a formal man-servant, with indifferent face,

transfer I to the guidance of a hire nurse, who lead I up the

stair, and, before I be well aware of it, into the room in which Dr.

Lloyd have die. widely different, indeed, the aspect of the wall, the

character of the furniture! the dingy paperhanging be replace by

airy muslin, show a rose-colour ground through their fanciful

openwork; luxurious fauteuil, gild wardrobe, full-length mirror, a

toilet-table trick out with lace and ribbon; and glitter with an

array of silver gewgaw and jewel trinkets,--all transform the sick

chamber of the simple man of science to a boudoir of death for the vain

coquette. but the room itself, in its high lattice and heavy ceiling,

be the same--as the coffin itself have the same confine, whether it be

rich in velvet and bright with blazoning, or rude as a pauper’s shell.



and the bed, with its silken coverlet, and its pillow edge with the

thread-work of Louvain, stand in the same sharp angle as that over which

have flicker the frown smoke-reek above the dying, resentful foe.

as I approach, a man, who be seat beside the sufferer, turn round

his face, and give I a silent kindly nod of recognition. he be Mr.

C----, one of the clergy of the town, the one with whom I have the most

frequently come into contact wherever the physician resign to the

priest the language that bid man hope. Mr. C-----, as a preacher,

be renowned for his touching eloquence; as a pastor, revere for his

benignant piety; as friend and neighbour, belove for a sweetness of

nature which seem to regulate all the movement of a mind eminently

masculine by the beat of a heart tender as the gentle woman’s.



this good man; then whisper something to the sufferer which I do

not overhear, steal towards I, take I by the hand, and say, also in

a whisper, “be merciful as Christians be.” he lead I to the bedside,

there leave I, go out, and close the door.



“do you think I be really die, Dr. Fenwick?” say a feeble voice. “I

fear Dr. Jones have misunderstand my case. I wish I have call you in at

the first, but--but I could not--I could not! will you feel my pulse?

don’t you think you could do I good?”



I have no need to feel the pulse in that skeleton wrist; the aspect of

the face suffice to tell I that death be draw near.



mechanically, however, I go through the hackneyed formulae of

professional question. this vain ceremony do, as gently and

delicately as I could, I imply the expediency of concluding, if not

yet settle, those affair which relate to this world.



“this duty,” I say, “in relieve the mind from care for other to whom

we owe the forethought of affection, often relieve the body also

of many a gnawing pain, and sometimes, to the surprise of the most

experienced physician, prolong life itself.”



“ah,” say the old maid, peevishly, “I understand! but it be not my will

that trouble I. I should not be leave to a nurse from a hospital if my

relation do not know that my annuity die with I; and I forestall

it in furnish this house, Dr. Fenwick, and all these pretty thing

will be sell to pay those horrid tradesmen!--very hard!--so hard!--just

as I get thing about I in the way I always say I would have they if

I could ever afford it! I always say I would have my bedroom hung

with muslin, like dear Lady L----‘s; and the drawing-room in

geranium-colour silk: so pretty. you have not see it: you would not

know the house, Dr. Fenwick. and just when all be finish, to be take

away and thrust into the grave. it be so cruel!” and she begin to weep.

her emotion bring on a violent paroxysm, which, when she recover

from it, have produce one of those startling change of mind that be

sometimes witness before death,--changes whereby the whole character

of a life seem to undergo solemn transformation. the hard will become

gentle, the proud meek, the frivolous earnest. that awful moment when

the thing of earth pass away like dissolve scene, leave death

visible on the background by the glare that shoot up in the last

flicker of life’s lamp.



and when she lift she haggard face from my shoulder, and hear my

pitying, soothe voice, it be not the grief of a trifl at the loss

of fondle toy that speak in the fall line of her lip, in the woe of

her plead eye.



“so this be death,” she say. “I feel it hurry on. I must speak.

I promise Mr. C---- that I would. forgive I, can you--can you? that

letter--that letter to lilian Ashleigh, I write it! oh, do not look

at I so terribly; I never think it could do such evil! and be I not

punish enough? I truly believe when I write that Miss Ashleigh be

deceive you, and once I be silly enough to fancy that you might have

like I. but I have another motive; I have be so poor all my life--I

have become rich unexpectedly; I set my heart on this house--I have always

fancy it--and I think if I could prevent Miss Ashleigh marry you,

and scare she and her mother from come back to L----, I could get the

house. and I do get it. what for?--to die. I have not be here a

week before I get the hurt that be kill I--a fall down the

stairs,--coming out of this very room; the stair have be polish. if

I have stay in my old lodging, it would not have happen. oh, say you

forgive I! say, say it, even if you do not feel you can! say it!” and

the miserable woman grasp I by the arm as Dr. Lloyd have grasp I.



I shade my avert face with my hand; my heart heave with the agony

of my suppress passion. a wrong, however deep, only to myself, I could

have pardon without effort; such a wrong to Lilian,--no! I could not

say “I forgive.”



the die wretch be perhaps more appalled by my silence than she would

have be by my reproach. her voice grow shrill in her despair.



“you will not pardon I! I shall die with your curse on my head! mercy!

mercy! that good man, Mr. C----, assure I you would be merciful. have

you never wrong another? have the Evil One never tempt you?”



then I speak in broken accent: “I! oh, have it be I whom you

defame--but a young creature so harmless, so unoffending, and for so

miserable a motive!”



“but I tell you, I swear to you, I never dream I could cause such

sorrow; and that young man, that Margrave, put it into my head!”



“Margrave! he have leave L---- long before that letter be write!”



“but he come back for a day just before I write: it be the very day.

I meet he in the lane yonder. he ask after you,--after Miss Ashleigh;

and when he speak he laugh, and I say, ‘Miss Ashleigh have be ill,

and be go away;’ and he laugh again. and I think he know more

than he would tell I, so I ask he if he suppose Mrs. Ashleigh would

come back, and say how much I should like to take this house if she do

not; and again he laugh, and say, ‘bird never stay in the nest after

the young one be hurt,’ and go away singing. when I get home, his

laugh and his song haunt I. I think I see he still in my room,

prompt I to write, and I sit down and write. oh, pardon, pardon I!

I have be a foolish poor creature, but never mean to do such harm.

the Evil One tempt I! there he be, near I now! I see he yonder!

there, at the doorway. he come to claim I! as you hope for mercy

yourself, free I from he! forgive I!”



I make an effort over myself. in name Margrave as her tempter, the

woman have suggest an excuse, echo from that innermost cell of my

mind, which I recoil from gaze into, for there I should behold his

image. inexpiable though the injury she have work against I and mine,

still the woman be human--fellow-creature-like myself;--but he?



I take the pale hand that still press my arm, and say, with firm

voice,--



“be comfort. in the name of Lilian, my wife, I forgive you for she and

for I as freely and as fully as we be enjoin by he, against whose

precept the good of we daily sin, to forgive--we child of wrath--to

forgive one another!”



“Heaven bless you!--oh, bless you!” she murmur, sink back upon she

pillow.



“ah!” think I, “what if the pardon I grant for a wrong far deep than

I inflict on he whose imprecation smite I in this chamber, should

indeed be receive as atonement, and this blessing on the lip of the

die annul the dark curse that the dead have leave on my path through the

Valley of the Shadow!”



I leave my patient sleep quietly,--the sleep that precede the last.

as I go down the stair into the hall, I see Mrs. Poyntz stand at

the threshold, speak to the man-servant and the nurse.



I would have pass she with a formal bow, but she stop I.



“I come to inquire after poor Miss Brabazon,” say she.



“you can tell I more than the servant can: be there no hope?”



“let the nurse go up and watch beside she. she may pass away in the

sleep into which she have fall.”



“Allen Fenwick, I must speak with you--nay, but for a few minute. I

hear that you leave L---- to-morrow. it be scarcely among the chance

of life that we should meet again.” while thus say, she draw I along

the lawn down the path that lead towards her own home. “I wish,” say

she, earnestly, “that you could part with a kindlier feeling towards I;

but I can scarcely expect it. could I put myself in your place, and be

move by your feeling, I know that I should be implacable; but I--”



“but you, madam, be the World! and the World govern itself, and

dictate to other, by law which seem harsh to those who ask from its

favour the service which the World cannot tender, for the World admit

favourite, but ignore friend. you do but act to I as the World ever

act to those who mistake its favour for its friendship.”



“it be true,” say Mrs. Poyntz, with blunt candour; and we continue to

walk on silently. at length she say abruptly, “but do you not rashly

deprive yourself of your only consolation in sorrow? when the heart

suffer, do your skill admit any remedy like occupation to the mind?

yet you abandon that occupation to which your mind be most accustom;

you desert your career; you turn aside, in the midst of the race, from

the fame which await at the goal; you go back from civilization itself,

and dream that all your intellectual craving can find content in the

life of a herdsman, amidst the monotony of a wild! no, you will repent,

for you be untrue to your mind!”



“I be sick of the word ‘mind’!” say I, bitterly. and therewith I

relapse into musing.



the enigma which have foil my intelligence in the unravelled Sibyl

Book of Nature be mystery strange to every man’s normal practice

of thought, even if reducible to the fraudulent impression of outward

sense; for illusion in a brain otherwise healthy suggest problem in

our human organization which the college that record they rather guess

at than solve. but the blow which have shatter my life have be deal

by the hand of a fool. here, there be no mystic enchantment. motive

the most commonplace and paltry, suggest to a brain as trivial and

shallow as ever make the frivolity of woman a theme for the satire of

poet, have suffice, in devastate the field of my affection, to blast

the use for which I have culture my mind; and have my intellect be as

great as heaven ever give to man, it would have be as vain a shield as

mine against the shaft that have lodge in my heart. while I have, indeed,

be prepare my reason and my fortitude to meet such peril, weird and

marvellous, as those by which tale round the winter fireside scare the

credulous child, a contrivance--so vulgar and hackneye that not a day

pass but what some hearth be vex by an anonymous libel--have

work a calamity more dread than aught which my dark guess into the

Shadow-Land unpierced by Philosophy could trace to the prompting of

malignant witchcraft. so, ever this truth run through all legend of

ghost and demon--through the uniform record of what wonder accredit

and science reject as the supernatural--lo! the dread machinery whose

wheel roll through Hades! what need such awful engine for such mean

result? the first blockhead we meet in our walk to our grocer’s can

tell we more than the ghost tell we; the poor envy we ever arouse

hurt we more than the demon. how true an interpreter be Genius to Hell

as to Earth! the Fiend come to Faust, the tired seeker of knowledge;

Heaven and Hell stake their cause in the Mortal’s temptation. and what

do the Fiend to astonish the Mortal? turn wine into fire, turn love

into crime. we need no Mephistopheles to accomplish these marvel every

day!



thus silently thinking, I walk by the side of the world-wise woman;

and when she next speak, I look up, and see that we be at the monk’

well, where I have first see Lilian gaze into heaven!



Mrs. Poyntz have, as we walk, place her hand on my arm; and, turn

abruptly from the path into the glade, I find myself stand by she

side in the scene where a new sense of be have first disclose to my

sight the hue with which love, the passionate beautifier, turn into

purple and gold the gray of the common air. thus, when romance have end

in sorrow, and the Beautiful fade from the landscape, the trite and

positive form of life, banish for a time, reappear, and deepen our

mournful remembrance of the glory they replace. and the Woman of the

World, find how little I be induce to respond to she when she

have talk of myself, begin to speak, in her habitual clear, ring

accent, of her own social scheme and devices,--



“I shall miss you when you be go, Allen Fenwick; for though, during

the last year or so, all actual intercourse between we have cease,

yet my interest in you give some occupation to my thought when I sit

alone,--having lose my main object of ambition in settle my daughter,

and have no long any one in the house with whom I could talk of the

future, or for whom I could form a project. it be so wearisome to count

the change which pass within we, that we take interest in the change

that pass without. Poyntz still have his weather-glass; I have no long

my jane.”



“I cannot linger with you on this spot,” say I, impatiently turn

back into the path; she follow, tread over fall leave. and

unheede my interruption, she thus continue her hard talk,--



“but I be not sick of my mind, as you seem to be of your; I be only

somewhat tired of the little cage in which, since it have be alone, it

ruffle its plume against the flimsy wire that confine it from wide

space. I shall take up my home for a time with the new-married couple:

they want I. Ashleigh Sumner have come into parliament. he mean to

attend regularly and work hard, but he do not like Jane to go into

the world by herself, and he wish she to go into the world, because he

want a wife to display his wealth for the improvement of his position.

in Ashleigh Sumner’s house I shall have ample scope for my energy,

such as they be. I have a curiosity to see the few that perch on the

wheel of the state and say, ‘it be we who move the wheel!’ it will

amuse I to learn if I can maintain in a capital the authority I

have win in a country town; if not, I can but return to my small

principality. wherever I live I must sway, not serve. if I succeed--as I

ought, for in Jane’s beauty and Ashleigh’s fortune I have material

for the woof of ambition, want which here, I fall asleep over my

knitting--if I succeed, there will be enough to occupy the rest of my

life. Ashleigh Sumner must be a power; the power will be represent and

enjoy by my child, and create and maintain by I! Allen Fenwick,

do as I do. be world with the world, and it will only be in moment of

spleen and chagrin that you will sigh to think that the heart may be

void when the mind be full. Confess you envy I while you listen.”



“not so; all that to you seem so great appear to I so small! nature

alone be always grand, in her terror as well as her charm. the world

for you, Nature for I. farewell!”



“nature!” say Mrs. Poyntz, compassionately. “Poor Allen Fenwick! nature

indeed,--intellectual suicide! Nay, shake hand, then, if for the last

time.”



so we shake hand and part, where the wicket-gate and the stone stair

separate my blighted fairy-land from the common thoroughfare.













CHAPTER LXVIII.



that night as I be employ in collect the book and manuscript

which I propose to take with I, include my long-suspend

physiological work, and such standard authority as I might want to

consult or refer to in the portion yet incomplete, my servant enter

to inform I, in answer to the inquiry I have send he to make, that

Miss Brabazon have peacefully breathe she last an hour before. well!

my pardon have perhaps soothe she last moment; but how unavaile her

death-bed repentance to undo the wrong she have do!



I turn from that thought, and, glance at the work into which I

have throw all my learning, methodize into system with all my art, I

recall the pity which Mrs. Poyntz have express for my meditate waste

of mind. the tone of superiority which this incarnation of common-sense

accompany by uncommon will assume over all that be too deep or too

high for her comprehension have sometimes amuse I; think over it

now, it pique. I say to myself, “after all, I shall bear with I such

solace as intellectual occupation can afford. I shall have leisure to

complete this labour; and a record that I have live and think may

outlast all the honour which worldly ambition may bestow upon Ashleigh

Summer!” and, as I so murmur, my hand, mechanically select the

book I need, fall on the Bible that Julius Faber have give to I.



it open at the Second Book of Esdras, which our church place amongst

the Apocrypha, and be generally consider by scholar to have be

write in the first or second century of the Christian era,(1)--but in

which the question raise by man in the remotest age, to which we can

trace back his desire “to comprehend the way of the most high,” be

invest with a grandeur of thought and sublimity of word to which I

know of no parallel in writer we call profane.



my eye fall on this passage in the lofty argument between the Angel

whose name be Uriel, and the Prophet, perplex by his own craving for

knowledge:--





   “he (the Angel) answer I, and say, I go into a forest, into a

    plain, and the tree take counsel,



   “and say, Come, let we go and make war against the sea, that it may

    depart away before we, and that we may make we more wood.



   “the flood of the sea also in like manner take counsel, and say,

    come, let we go up and subdue the wood of the plain, that there also

    we may make we another country.



   “the thought of the wood be in vain, for the fire come and consume it.



   “the thought of the flood of the sea come likewise to nought, for the

    sand stand up and stop they.



   “if thou wert judge now betwixt these two, whom wouldst thou begin to

    justify; or whom wouldst thou condemn?



   “I answer and say, verily it be a foolish thought that they both

    have devise; for the ground be give unto the wood, and the sea also

    hath his place to bear his flood.



   “then answer he I, and say, Thou hast give a right judgment; but

    why judgest thou not thyself also?



   “for like as the ground be give unto the wood, and the sea to his

    flood, even so they that dwell upon the earth may understand nothing

    but that which be upon the earth; and he that dwelleth above the

    heaven may only understand the thing that be above the height of

    the heaven.”



I pause at those word, and, close the Sacred Volume, fall into deep,

unquiet thought.



(1) such be the supposition of Jahn. Dr. Lee, however, be of opinion

that the author be contemporary, and, indeed, identical, with the

author of the Book of Enoch.













CHAPTER LXIX.



I have hope that the voyage would produce some beneficial effect upon

Lilian; but no effect, good or bad, be perceptible, except, perhaps,

a deep silence, a gentle calm. she love to sit on the deck when the

night be fair, and the star mirror on the deep. and once thus, as

I stand beside she, bend over the rail of the vessel, and gaze on

the long wake of light which the moon make amidst the darkness of an

ocean to which no shore could be see, I say to myself, “where be

my track of light through the measureless future? would that I could

believe as I do when a child! woe be I, that all the reasoning I take

from my knowledge should lead I away from the comfort which the peasant

who mourn find in faith! why should riddle so dark have be thrust

upon me,--me, no fond child of fancy; I, sober pupil of school the

severest? yet what marvel--the strange my sense have witness or

feign in the fraud they have palm on I--be great than that

by which a simple affection, that all man profess to have know, have

change the course of life prearrange by my hope and confirm by my

judgment? how calmly before I know love I have anatomize its mechanism,

as the tyro who dissect the web-work of tissue and nerve in the dead!

Lo! it live, live in I; and, in living, escape from my scalpel, and

mock all my knowledge. can love be reduce to the realm of the sense?

no; what nun be more bar by her grate from the realm of the sense

than my bride by her solemn affliction? be love, then, the union of

kindre, harmonious mind? no, my beloved one sit by my side, and I

guess not her thought, and my mind be to she a sealed fountain. yet I

love she more--oh, ineffably more!--for the doom which destroy the two

cause philosophy assign to love--in the form, in the mind! how can I

now, in my vain physiology, say what be love, what be not? be it love

which must tell I that man have a soul, and that in soul will be find

the solution of problem never to be solve in body or mind alone?”



my self-questioning halt here as Lilian’s hand touch my shoulder.

she have rise from her seat, and have come to I.



“be not the star very far from earth?” she say.



“very far.”



“be they see for the first time to-night?”



“they be see, I presume, as we see they, by the father of all human

race!”



“yet close below we they shine reflect in the water; and yet, see,

wave flow on wave before we can count it!”



“Lilian, by what sympathy do you read and answer my thought?”



her reply be incoherent and meaningless. if a gleam of intelligence have

mysteriously light my heart to her view, it be go. but draw she

nearer towards I, my eye long follow wistfully the path of light,

divide the darkness on either hand, till it close in the sloping

horizon.













CHAPTER LXX.



the voyage be over. at the seaport at which we land I find a letter

from Faber. my instruction have reach he in time to effect the

purchase on which his description have fix my desire. the stock, the

implement of husbandry, the furniture of the house, be include in

the purchase. all be prepared for my arrival, and I hasten from

the then miserable village, which may some day rise into one of the

mighty capital of the world, to my lodge in the wilderness.



it be the burst of the australian spring, which commence in our autumn

month of October. the air be load with the perfume of the acacias.

amidst the glade of the open forest land, or climb the craggy bank

of wind silvery creeks,(1) creeper and flower of dazzle hue

contrast the olive-green of the surround foliage. the exhilarate

effect of the climate in that season heighten the charm of the

strange scenery. in the brilliancy of the sky, in the lightness of the

atmosphere, the sense of life be wondrously quicken. with the very

breath the Adventurer draw in from the racy air, he feel as if

inhale hope.



we have reach our home, we be settle in it; the early unfamiliar

impression be wear away. we have learn to dispense with much that we

at first miss, and be reconcile to much that at first disappoint

or displeased.



the house be build but of log; the late proprietor have commence, upon

a rise ground, a mile distant, a more impose edifice of stone, but

it be not half finish.



this log-house be commodious, and much have be do, within and

without, to conceal or adorn its primitive rudeness. it be of irregular,

picturesque form, with veranda round three side of it, to which the

grape-vine have be train, with glossy leave that clamber up to the

gable roof. there be a large garden in front, in which many english

fruit-tree have be set, and grow fast amongst the plant of the

tropic and the orange-tree of Southern Europe. beyond stretch undulous

pasture, stud not only with sheep, but with herd of cattle, which

my speculative predecessor have breed from parent of famous stock, and

import from England at mighty cost; but as yet the herd have be of

little profit, and they range their luxuriant expanse of pasture with

as little heed. to the left soar up, in long range, the many-colour

hill; to the right meander a creek, belt by feathery tree; and

on its opposite bank a forest open, through frequent break, into

park-like glade and alley. the territory, of which I so suddenly find

myself the lord, be vast, even for a colonial capitalist.



it have be originally purchase as “a special survey,” comprise

twenty thousand acre, with the privilege of pasture over forty thousand

more. in very little of this land, though it include some of the

most fertile district in the known world, have cultivation be even

commence. at the time I enter into possession, even sheep be barely

profitable; labour be scarce and costly. regard as a speculation, I

could not wonder that my predecessor flee in fear from his domain. have

I invest the bulk of my capital in this lordly purchase, I should

have deem myself a ruin man; but a villa near London, with a hundred

acre, would have cost I as much to buy, and thrice as much to keep

up. I could afford the investment I have make. I find a Scotch bailiff

already on the estate, and I be content to escape from rural

occupation, to which I bring no experience, by make it worth his

while to serve I with zeal. two domestic of my own, and two who

have be for many year with Mrs. Ashleigh, have accompany we: they

remain faithful and seem contented. so the clockwork of our mere

household arrangement go on much the same as in our native home.

Lilian be not subject to the ordinary privation and discomfort that

await the wife even of the wealthy emigrant. alas! would she have heed

they if she have be?



the change of scene work a decided change for the well in her

health and spirit, but not such as imply a dawn of revive reason.

but her countenance be now more rarely overcast. its usual aspect be

glad with a soft mysterious smile. she would murmur snatch of song,

that be partly borrow from english poet, and partly glide away

into what seem spontaneous addition of her own,--wanting intelligible

meaning, but never melody nor rhyme. strange, that memory and

imitation--the two early parent of all inventive knowledge--should

still be so active, and judgment--the after faculty, that combine the

rest into purpose and method--be annul!



Julius Faber I see continually, though his residence be a few mile

distant. he be sanguine as to Lilian’s ultimate recovery; and, to my

amazement and to my envy, he have contrive, by some art which I cannot

attain, to establish between she and himself intelligible communion. she

comprehend his question, when mine, though the simple, seem to she

in unknown language; and he construe into sense her word, that to I

be meaningless riddle.



“I be right,” he say to I one day, leave she seat in the garden

beside her quiet, patient mother, and join I where I lay--listless

yet fretful--under the shadeless gum-tree, gaze not on the flock

and field that I could call my own, but on the far mountain range, from

which the arch of the horizon seem to spring,--“I be right,” say the

great physician; “this be reason suspend, not reason lose. your wife

will recover; but--”



“but what?”



“give I your arm as I walk homeward, and I will tell you the conclusion

to which I have come.”



I rise, the old man lean on I, and we go down the valley along the

craggy ridge of the wind creek. the woodland on the opposite bank

be vocal with the chirp and croak and chatter of australian birds,--all

mirthful, all songless, save that sweetest of warbler, which some early

irreverent emigrant degrade to the name of magpie, but whose note be

sweet than the nightingale’s, and trill through the lucent air with

a distinct ecstatic melody of joy that dominate all the discord, so

ravish the sense, that, while it sing, the ear scarcely heed the

scream of the parrot.



(1) Creek be the name give by australian colonist to precarious water

course and tributary stream.













CHAPTER LXXI.



 “you may remember,” say Julius Faber, “Sir Humphry Davy’s eloquent description of the effect produce on he by the inhalation of nitrous oxide. he state that he begin to lose the perception of external thing; train of vivid visible image rapidly pass through his mind, and be connect with word in such a manner as to produce perception perfectly novel. ‘I exist,’ he say, ‘in a world of newly-connect and newly-modify idea.’ when he recover, he exclaim: ‘nothing exist but thought; the universe be compose of impression, idea, pleasure, and pain!’



“now observe, that thus a cultivator of positive science, endow with

one of the healthy of human brain, be, by the inhalation of a gas,

abstract from all external life,--enters into a new world, which

consist of image he himself create and animate so vividly that, on

wake, he resolve the universe itself into thought.”



“well,” say I, “but what inference do you draw from that voluntary

experiment, applicable to the malady of which you bid I hope the cure?”



“simply this: that the effect produce on a healthful brain by the

nitrous oxide may be produce also by moral cause operate on the

blood, or on the nerve. there be a degree of mental excitement in which

idea be more vivid than sensation, and then the world of external

thing give way to the world within the brain.(1) but this, though a

suspension of that reason which comprehend accuracy of judgment, be

no more a permanent aberration of reason than be Sir Humphry Davy’s

visionary ecstasy under the influence of the gas. the difference

between the two state of suspension be that of time, and it be but an

affair of time with our beloved patient. yet prepare yourself. I fear

that the mind will not recover without some critical malady of the

body!”



“critical! but not dangerous?--say not dangerous! I can endure the pause

of her reason; I could not endure the void in the universe if her life

be to fade from the earth.”



“poor friend! would not you yourself rather lose life than reason?”



“I--yes! but we man be teach to set cheap value on our own life; we

do not estimate at the same rate the life of those we love. do we do

so, Humanity would lose its virtue.”



“what, then! love teach that there be something of nobler value than

mere mind? yet surely it cannot be the mere body? what be it, if

not that continuance of be which your philosophy decline to

acknowledge,--namely, soul? if you fear so painfully that your Lilian

should die, be it not that you fear to lose she forever?”



“oh, cease, cease!” I cry impatiently. “I cannot now argue on

metaphysic. what be it that you anticipate of harm to her life? her

health have be strong ever since her affliction. she never seem to

know ailment now. do you not perceive that her cheek have a more hardy

bloom, her frame a more rounded symmetry, than when you see she in

England?”



“unquestionably. her physical force have be silently recruit

themselves in the dream which half lull, half amuse her imagination.

Imagination! that faculty, the most glorious which be bestow on the

human mind, because it be the faculty which enable think to create,

be of all other the most exhausting to life when unduly stimulate and

consciously reason on its own creation. I think it probable that

have this sorrow not befall you, you would have know a sorrow yet

graver,--you would have long survive your Lilian. as it be now, when

she recover, her whole organization, physical and mental, will have

undergo a beneficent change. but, I repeat my prediction,--some severe

malady of the body will precede the restoration of the mind; and it

be my hope that the present suspense or aberration of the more wear

power of the mind may fit the body to endure and surmount the physical

crisis. I remember a case, within my own professional experience,

in many respect similar to this, but in other respect it be less

hopeful. I be consult by a young student of a very delicate physical

frame, of great mental energy, and consume by an intense ambition.

he be read for university honour. he would not listen to I when I

entreat he to rest his mind. I think that he be certain to obtain

the distinction for which he toil, and equally certain to die a few

month after obtain it. he falsify both my prognostic. he so

overwork himself that, on the day of examination, his nerve be

agitated, his memory fail he; he pass, not without a certain

credit, but fall far short of the rank amongst his fellow competitor

to which he aspire. here, then, the irritated mind act on the

disappointed heart, and raise a new train of emotion. he be first

visit by spectral illusion; then he sink into a state in which the

external world seem quite blot out. he heed nothing that be say

to he; seem to see nothing that be place before his eyes,--in a

word, sensation become dormant, idea preconceive usurped their place,

and those idea give he pleasure. he believe that his genius be

recognize, and live amongst its suppose creation enjoy an

imaginary fame. so it go on for two year, during which suspense of

his reason, his frail form become robust and vigorous. at the end of

that time he be seize with a fever, which would have sweep he in

three day to the grave have it occur when I be first call in to

attend he. he conquer the fever, and, in recover, acquire the

full possession of the intellectual faculty so long suspend. when I

last see he, many year afterwards, he be in perfect health, and the

object of his young ambition be realize; the body have support the

mind,--he have achieve distinction. now what have so, for a time, lay

this strong intellect into visionary sleep? the most agonizing of human

emotion in a noble spirit,--shame! what have so stricken down your

Lilian? you have tell I the story: shame!--the shame of a nature

pre-eminently pure. but observe that, in his case as in her, the shock

inflict do not produce a succession of painful illusion: on

the contrary, in both, the illusion be generally pleasing. have the

illusion be painful, the body would have suffer, the patient die.

why do a painful shock produce pleasing illusion? because, no matter

how a shock on the nerve may originate, if it affect the reason, it

do but make more vivid than impression from actual external object

the idea previously most cherished. such idea in the young student

be idea of earthly fame; such idea in the young maiden be idea

of angel comforter and heavenly Edens. you miss her mind on the earth,

and, while we speak, it be in paradise.”



“much that you say, my friend, be authorize by the speculation of

great writer, with whom I be not unfamiliar; but in none of those

writer, nor in your encouraging word, do I find a solution for

much that have no precedent in my experience,--much, indeed, that have

analogy in my reading, but analogy which I have hitherto despise

as old wife’ fable. I have bare to your search eye the weird

mystery of my life. how do you account for fact which you cannot

resolve into illusions,--for the influence which that strange being,

Margrave, exercise over Lilian’s mind or fancy, so that for a time she

love for I be as dormant as be her reason now; so that he could draw

she--she whose nature you admit to be singularly pure and modest--from

her mother’s home? the magic wand; the trance into which that wand throw

Margrave himself; the apparition which it conjure up in my own

quiet chamber when my mind be without a care and my health without

a flaw,--how account for all this: as you endeavour, and perhaps

successfully, to account for all my impression of the Vision in the

Museum, of the luminous, haunt shadow in its early apparition,

when my fancy be heat, my heart torment, and, it might be, even the

physical force of this strong frame disorder?”



“Allen,” say the old pathologist, “here we approach a ground which few

physician have dare to examine. honour to those who, like our bold

contemporary, Elliotson, have brave scoff and sacrifice dross in

seek to extract what be practical in use, what can be test by

experiment, from those exceptional phenomenon on which magic seek to

find a philosophy, and to which philosophy track the origin of magic.”



“what! do I understand you? be it you, Julius Faber, who attach faith

to the wonder attribute to animal magnetism and electro-biology, or

subscribe to the doctrine which their practitioner teach?”



“I have not examine into those doctrine, nor see with my own eye the

wonder record, upon evidence too respectable, nevertheless, to permit

I peremptorily to deny what I have not witnessed.(2) but wherever I

look through the history of Mankind in all age and all race, I find a

concurrence in certain belief which seem to countenance the theory that

there be in some peculiar and rare temperament a power over form of

animate organization, with which they establish some unaccountable

affinity; and even, though much more rarely, a power over inanimate

matter. you be familiar with the theory of Descartes, ‘that those

particle of the blood which penetrate to the brain do not only serve to

nourish and sustain its substance, but to produce there a certain very

subtle aura, or rather a flame very vivid and pure, that obtain the

name of the Animal Spirits;’ (3) and at the close of his great fragment

upon Man, he assert that ‘this flame be of no other nature than all the

fire which be in inanimate body.’(4) this notion do but forestall

the more recent doctrine that electricity be more or less in all, or

nearly all, know matter. now, whether in the electric fluid or some

other fluid akin to it of which we know still less, thus equally

pervade all matter, there may be a certain magnetic property more

active, more operative upon sympathy in some human constitution than in

other, and which can account for the mysterious power I have speak of,

be a query I might suggest, but not an opinion I would hazard. for an

opinion I must have that basis of experience or authority which I do not

need when I submit a query to the experience and authority of other.

still, the supposition convey in the query be so far worthy of

notice, that the ecstatic temperament (in which phrase I comprehend all

constitutional mystic) be peculiarly sensitive to electric atmospheric

influence. this be a fact which most medical observer will have

remark in the range of their practice. accordingly, I be prepare

to find Mr. Hare Townshend, in his interesting work,(5) state that he

himself be of ‘the electric temperament,’ spark fly from his hair

when comb in the dark, etc. that accomplished writer, whose veracity

no one would impugn, affirm that between this electrical endowment and

whatever mesmeric property he might possess, there be a remarkable

relationship and parallelism. whatever state of the atmosphere tend to

accumulate and insulate electricity in the body, promote equally’ (say

Mr. Townshend) ‘the power and facility with which I influence other

mesmerically.’ what Mr. Townshend thus observe in himself, American

physician and professor of chemistry depose to have observe in those

modern magician, the medium of (so-call) ‘spirit manifestation.’

they state that all such medium be of the electric temperament, thus

everywhere find ally with the ecstatic, and their power vary in

proportion as the state of the atmosphere serve to depress or augment

the electricity store in themselves. here, then, in the midst of

vagrant phenomenon, either too hastily dismiss as altogether the trick

of fraudful imposture, or too credulously accept as supernatural

portent--here, at least, in one generalized fact, we may, perhaps, find

a starting point, from which inductive experiment may arrive, soon

or late, at a rational theory. but however the power of which we be

speak (a power accord to special physical temperament) may or may

not be account for by some patient student of nature, I be persuade

that it be in that power we be to seek for whatever be not wholly

imposture, in the attribute assign to magic or witchcraft. it be well

say, by a writer who have go into the depth of these subject with the

research of a scholar and the science of a pathologist, ‘that if magic

have exclusively repose on credulity and falsehood, its reign would

never have endure so long; but that its art take its origin in singular

phenomenon, proper to certain affection of the nerve, or manifest in

the condition of sleep. these phenomenon, the principle of which be

at first unknown, serve to root faith in magic, and often abuse even

enlighten mind. the enchanter and magician arrive, by diver

practice, at the faculty of provoking in other brain a determine

order of dream, of engendering hallucination of all kind, of induce

fit of hypnotism, trance, mania, during which the person so affect

imagine that they see, hear, touch, supernatural being, converse

with they, prove their influence, assist at prodigy of which magic

proclaim itself to possess the secret. the public, the enchanter,

and the enchant be equally dupe.’(6) accept this explanation,

unintelligible to no physician of a practice so lengthened as mine

have be, I draw from it the corollary, that as these phenomenon be

exhibit only by certain special affection, to which only certain

special constitution be susceptible, so not in any superior faculty

of intellect, or of spiritual endowment, but in peculiar physical

temperament, often strangely disorder, the power of the sorcerer

in affect the imagination of other be to be seek. in the native

tribe of Australasia the elder be instruct in the art of this

so-call sorcery, but only in a very few constitution do instruction

avail to produce effect in which the savage recognize the power of a

sorcerer: it be so with the Obi of the negro. the fascination of Obi

be an unquestionable fact, but the obi man cannot be train by formal

lesson; he be bear a fascinator, as a poet be bear a poet. it be so

with the Laplanders, of whom Tornoeus report that of those instruct

in the magical art ‘only a few be capable of it.’ ‘some,’ he say, ‘be

naturally magician.’ and this fact be emphatically insist upon by

the mystic of our own middle age, who state that a man must be bear

a magician; in other word, that the gift be constitutional, though

develop by practice and art. now, that this gift and its practice

should principally obtain in imperfect state of civilization, and fade

into insignificance in the busy social enlightenment of city, may be

account for by reference to the known influence of imagination. in

the cruder state of social life not only be imagination more frequently

predominant over all other faculty, but it have not the healthful vent

which the intellectual competition of city and civilization afford.

the man who in a savage tribe, or in the dark feudal age, would be a

magician, be in our century a poet, an orator, a daring speculator,

an inventive philosopher. in other word, his imagination be draw to

pursuit congenial to those amongst whom it work. it be the tendency

of all intellect to follow the direction of the public opinion amidst

which it be train. where a magician be hold in reverence or awe, there

will be more practitioner of magic than where a magician be despise

as an impostor or shut up as a lunatic. in Scandinavia, before the

introduction of Christianity, all tradition record the wonderful

power of the Vala, or witch, who be then hold in reverence and honour.

Christianity be introduce, and the early Church denounce the Vala as

the instrument of Satan, and from that moment down drop the majestic

prophetess into a miserable and execrated old hag!”



“the idea you broach,” say I, musingly, “have at moment cross I,

though I have shrink from reduce they to a theory which be but one of

pure hypothesis. but this magic, after all, then, you would place in the

imagination of the operator, act on the imagination of those whom it

affect? here, at least, I can follow you, to a certain extent, for here

we get back into the legitimate realm of physiology.”



“and possibly,” say Faber, “we may find hint to guide we to useful

examination, if not to complete solution of problem that, once

demonstrate, may lead to discovery of infinite value,--hints, I say,

in two writer of widely opposite genius, Van Helmont and Bacon.

Van Helmont, of all the mediaeval mystic, be, in spite of his many

extravagant whim, the one whose intellect be the most suggestive to the

discipline reasoner of our day. he suppose that the faculty which he

call Fantasy, and which we familiarly call Imagination,--is invest

with the power of create for itself idea independent of the sense,

each idea clothe in a form fabricate by the imagination, and

become an operative entity. this notion be so far favour by modern

physiologist, that Lincke report a case where the eye itself be

extirpate; yet the extirpation be follow by the appearance of

luminous figure before the orbit. and again, a woman, stone-blind,

complain of ‘luminous image, with pale colour, before her eye.’

Abercrombie mention the case ‘of a lady quite blind, her eye be

also disorganize and sink, who never walk out without see a

little old woman in a red cloak, who seem to walk before she.’(7)

your favourite authority, the illustrious Miller, who be himself in

the habit of ‘see different image in the field of vision when he

lie quietly down to sleep, assert that these image be not merely

present to the fancy, but that even the image of dream be really

see,’ and that ‘any one may satisfy himself of this by accustom

himself regularly to open his eye when wake after a dream,--the

image see in the dream be then sometimes visible, and can be observe

to disappear gradually.’ he confirm this statement not only by the

result of his own experience, but by the observation make by Spinoza,

and the yet high authority of Aristotle, who account for spectral

appearance as the internal action of the sense of vision.(8) and this

opinion be favour by Sir David Brewster, whose experience lead he

to suggest ‘that the object of mental contemplation may be see as

distinctly as external object, and will occupy the same local position

in the axis of vision as if they have be form by the agency of

light.’ be this as it may, one fact remains,--that image can be see

even by the blind as distinctly and vividly as you and I now see the

stream below our foot and the opossum at play upon yonder bough.

let we come next to some remarkable suggestion of Lord Bacon. in his

Natural history, treat of the force of the imagination, and the help

it receive ‘by one man work by another,’ he cite an instance he

have witness of a kind of juggler, who could tell a person what card

he think of. he mention this ‘to a pretend learn man, curious in

such thing,’ and this sage say to he, ‘it be not the knowledge of the

man’s thought, for that be proper to God, but the enforcing of a thought

upon he, and bind his imagination by a strong, so that he could

think of no other card.’ you see this sage anticipate our modern

electro-biologist! and the learn man then shrewdly ask Lord Bacon,

‘do the juggler tell the card to the man himself who have think of it,

or bid another tell it?’ ‘he bid another tell it,’ answer Lord Bacon.

‘I think so,’ return his learn acquaintance, ‘for the juggler

himself could not have put on so strong an imagination; but by tell

the card to the other, who believe the juggler be some strange man who

could do strange thing, that other man catch a strong imagination.’(9)

the whole story be worth reading, because Lord Bacon evidently think

it convey a guess worth examine. and Lord Bacon, be he now live,

would be the man to solve the mystery that branch out of mesmerism or

(so-call) spiritual manifestation, for he would not pretend to despise

their phenomenon for fear of hurt his reputation for good sense.

Bacon then go on to state that there be three way to fortify the

imagination. ‘first, authority derive from belief in an art and in the

man who exercise it; secondly, mean to quicken and corroborate the

imagination; thirdly, mean to repeat and refresh it.’ for the second

and the third he refer to the practice of magic, and proceed

afterwards to state on what thing imagination have most force,--‘upon

thing that have the light and easy motion, and, therefore, above

all, upon the spirit of man, and, in they, on such affection as

move lightest,--in love, in fear, in irresolution. and,’ add Bacon,

earnestly, in a very different spirit from that which dictate to the

sage of our time the philosophy of reject without trial that which

belong to the Marvellous,--‘and whatsoever be of this kind, should be

thoroughly inquire into.’ and this great founder or renovator of the

sober inductive system of investigation even so far leave it a matter

of speculative inquiry, whether imagination may not be so powerful

that it can actually operate upon a plant, that he say: ‘this likewise

should be make upon plant, and that diligently; as if you should tell

a man that such a tree would die this year, and will he, at these and

these time, to go unto it and see how it thriveth.’ I presume that

no philosopher have follow such recommendation: have some great

philosopher do so, possibly we should by this time know all the

secret of what be popularly call witchcraft.”



and as Faber here pause, there come a strange laugh from the fantastic

she-oak-tree overhang the stream,--a wild, impish laugh.



“Pooh! it be but the great kingfisher, the laugh-bird of the

australian bush,” say Julius Faber, amuse at my start of superstitious

alarm.



we walk on for some minute in muse silence, and the rude log-hut in

which my wise companion have his home come in view,--the flock graze

on undulous pasture, the lone drink at a watercourse fringe by the

slender gum-tree, and a few field, laboriously win from the luxuriant

grassland, ripple with the wave of corn.



I halt, and say, “rest here for a few moment, till I gather up the

conclusion to which your speculative reasoning seem to invite I.”



we sit down on a rocky crag, half mantle by luxuriant creeper with

vermilion bud.



“from the guess,” say I, “which you have draw from the erudition of

other and your own ingenious and reflective induction, I collect this

solution of the mystery, by which the experience I gain from my

sense confound all the dogma approve by my judgment. to the rational

conjecture by which, when we first converse on the marvel that

perplex I, you ascribe to my imagination, predispose by mental

excitement, physical fatigue or derangement, and a concurrence

of singular event tend to strengthen such predisposition, the

phantasmal impression produce on my senses,--to these conjecture

you now add a new one, more startling and less admit by sober

physiologist. you conceive it possible that person endow with a rare

and peculiar temperament can so operate on imagination, and, through

the imagination, on the sense of other, as to exceed even the power

ascribe to the practitioner of mesmerism and electro-biology, and give

a certain foundation of truth to the old tale of magic and witchcraft.

you imply that Margrave may be a person thus gift, and hence the

influence he unquestionably exercise over Lilian, and over, perhaps,

less innocent agent, charm or impel by his will. and not

discard, as I own I should have be originally induce to do,

the query or suggestion adventure by Bacon in his discursive

speculation on Nature, to wit, ‘that there be many thing, some of they

inanimate, that operate upon the spirit of man by secret sympathy and

antipathy,’ and to which Bacon give the quaint name of ‘imaginant,’ so

even that wand, of which I have describe to you the magic-like effect,

may have have property communicate to it by which it perform the work

of the magician, as mesmerist pretend that some substance mesmerize

by they can act on the patient as sensibly as if it be the mesmerizer

himself. do I state your supposition correctly?”



“yes; always remember that they be only supposition, and

volunteer with the utmost diffidence. but since, thus seat in the

early wilderness, we permit ourselves the indulgence of childlike guess,

may it not be possible, apart from the doubtful question whether a man

can communicate to an inanimate material substance a power to act upon

the mind or imagination of another man--may it not, I say, be possible

that such a substance may contain in itself such a virtue or property

potent over certain constitution, though not over all. for instance, it

be in my experience that the common hazel-wood will strongly affect some

nervous temperament, though wholly without effect on other. I remember

a young girl, who having take up a hazel-stick freshly cut, could not

relax her hold of it; and when it be wrench away from she by force,

be irresistibly attract towards it, repossess herself of it, and,

after hold it a few minute, be cast into a kind of trance, in which

she behold phantasmal vision. mention this curious case, which I

suppose unique, to a learn brother of our profession, he tell I that

he have know other instance of the effect of the hazel upon nervous

temperament in person of both sex. possibly it be some such

peculiar property in the hazel that make it the wood select for the

old divining-rod. again, we know that the bay-tree, or laurel, be

dedicate to the oracular Pythian Apollo. now wherever, in the old

world, we find that the learning of the priest enable they to exhibit

exceptional phenomenon, which impose upon popular credulity, there be a

something or other which be worth a philosopher’s while to explore;

and, accordingly, I always suspect that there be in the laurel

some property favourable to ecstatic vision in highly impressionable

temperament. my suspicion, a few year ago, be justify by the

experience of a german physician, who have under his care a cataleptic

or ecstatic patient, and who assure I that he find nothing in this

patient so stimulate the state of ‘sleep-wake,’ or so disposed that

state to indulge in the hallucination of prevision, as the berry of

the laurel.(10) well, we do not know what this wand that produce a

seemingly magical effect upon you be really compose of. you do not

notice the metal employ in the wire, which you say communicate a

thrill to the sensitive nerve in the palm of the hand. you cannot tell

how far it might have be the vehicle of some fluid force in nature. or

still more probably, whether the pore of your hand insensibly imbibe,

and communicate to the brain, some of those powerful narcotic from

which the Buddhists and the Arabs make unguent that induce visionary

hallucination, and in which substance undetected in the hollow of the

wand, or the handle of the wand itself, might be steeped.(11) one thing

we do know, namely, that amongst the ancient, and especially in the

East, the construction of wand for magical purpose be no commonplace

mechanical craft, but a special and secret art appropriate to man who

cultivate with assiduity all that be then know of natural science

in order to extract from it agency that might appear supernatural.

possibly, then, the rod or wand of the East, of which scripture make
