mention, be frame upon some principle of which we in our day be

very naturally ignorant, since we do not ransack science for the same

secret; and thus, in the selection or preparation of the material

employ, mainly consist whatever may be referrible to natural

philosophical cause in the antique science of Rhabdomancy, or

divination and enchantment by wand. the staff, or wand, of which you

tell I, be, you say, make of iron or steel and tip with crystal.

possibly iron and crystal do really contain some property not hitherto

scientifically analyze, and only, indeed, potential over exceptional

temperament, which may account for the fact that iron and crystal have

be favourite with all profess mystic, ancient and modern. the

Delphic Pythoness have her iron tripod, Mesmer his iron bed; and many

person, indisputably honest, cannot gaze long upon a ball of crystal

but what they begin to see vision. I suspect that a philosophical cause

for such seemingly preternatural effect of crystal and iron will be

find in connection with the extreme impressionability to change in

temperature which be the characteristic both of crystal and iron.

but if these material do contain certain power over exceptional

constitution, we do not arrive at a supernatural but at a natural

phenomenon.”



“still,” say I, “even grant that your explanatory hypothesis hit

or approach the truth;--still what a terrible power you would assign to

man’s will over man’s reason and deed!”



“man’s will,” answer Faber, “have over man’s deed and reason, habitual

and daily, power infinitely great and, when uncounterbalanced,

infinitely more dangerous than that which superstition exaggerate in

magic. man’s will move a war that decimate a race, and leave behind

it calamitie little less dire than slaughter. man’s will frame, but it

also corrupt law; exalt, but also demoralize opinion; set the world

mad with fanaticism, as often as it curb the heart’s fierce instinct

by the wisdom of brother-like mercy. you revolt at the exceptional,

limited sway over some two or three individual which the art of a

sorcerer (if sorcerer there be) can effect; and yet, at the very moment

in which you be perplex and appal by such sway, or by your

reluctant belief in it, your will be devise an engine to unsettle the

reason and wither the hope of million!”



“my will! what engine?”



“a book conceive by your intellect, adorn by your learning, and

direct by your will, to steal from the mind of other man their

persuasion of the soul’s everlaste Hereafter.”



I bow my head, and feel myself grow pale.



“and if we accept Bacon’s theory of ‘secret sympathy,’ or the plainer

physiological maxim that there must be in the imagination, morbidly

impressed by the will of another, some train of idea in affinity with

such influence and preincline to receive it, no magician could warp you

to evil, except through thought that themselves go astray. Grant

that the Margrave who still haunt your mind do really, by some occult,

sinister magnetism, guide the madman to murder, do influence the

servant-woman’s vulgar desire to pry into the secret of her ill-fate

master, or the old maid’s covetous wish and envious malignity: what

could this awful magician do more than any commonplace guilty adviser,

to a mind predispose to accept the advice?”



“you forget one example which destroy your argument,--the spell which

this mysterious fascinator could cast upon a creature so pure from all

guilt as Lilian!”



“will you forgive I if I answer frankly?”



“speak.”



“your Lilian be spotless and pure as you deem she, and the fascination,

therefore, attempt no lure through a sinful desire; it blend with

its attraction no sentiment of affection untrue to yourself. Nay, it be

justice to your Lilian, and may be melancholy comfort to you, to state

my conviction, base on the answer my question have draw from she,

that you be never more cherish by her love than when that love

seem to forsake you. her imagination impress she with the illusion

that through your love for she you be threaten with a great

peril. what seem the levity of her desertion be the devotion of

self-sacrifice. and, in her strange, dream-lead wandering, do not think

that she be conscious of the fascination you impute to this mysterious

Margrave: in her belief it be your own guardian angel that guide she

step, and her pilgrimage be ordain to disarm the foe that menace

you, and dissolve the spell that divide her life from your! but have

she not, long before this, willingly prepare herself to be so deceive?

have not her fancy be deliberately encourage to dwell remote from

the duty we be place on the earth to perform? the lofty faculty

in our nature be those that demand the fine poise, not to fall from

their height and crush all the wall that they crown. with exquisite

beauty of illustration, Hume say of the dreamer of ‘bright fancy,’

‘that they may be compare to those angel whom the scripture represent

as cover their eye with their wing.’ have you be, like my nephew,

a wrestler for bread with the wilderness, what helpmate would your

Lilian have be to you? how often would you have cry out in

justifiable anger, ‘I, son of Adam, be on earth, not in Paradise! oh,

that my Eve be at home on my hearth, and not in the sky with the

seraph!’ no Margrave, I venture to say, could have suspend the

healthful affection, or charm into danger the wide-awake soul of my

Amy. when she rock in its cradle the babe the young parent intrust to

her heed; when she call the kine to the milking, the chick to their

corn; when she but flit through my room to renew the flower on the

stand, or range in neat order the book that I read, no spell on her

fancy could lead she a step from the range of her provident care! at

day she be content to be on the commonplace earth; at evening she and

I knock together at the one door of heaven, which ope to thanksgive

and prayer; and thanksgiving and prayer send we back, calm and hopeful,

to the task that each morrow renew.”



I look up as the old man pause, and in the limpid clearness of the

australian atmosphere, I see the child he thus praise standing by the

garden-gate, look towards we, and though still distant she seem

near. I feel wroth with she. my heart so cherish my harmless,

defenceless Lilian, that I be jealous of the praise take from she to

be bestow on another.



“each of we,” say I, coldly, “have his or her own nature, and the use

harmonious to that nature’s idiosyncrasy. the world, I grant, would get

on very ill if woman be not more or less actively useful and quietly

good, like your Amy. but the world would lose standard that exalt and

refine, if no woman be permit to gain, through the indulgence of

fancy, thought exquisite as those which my Lilian conceive, while

think, alas! flow out of fancy. I do not wound you by cite your

Amy as a type of the mediocre; I do not claim for Lilian the rank we

accord to the type of genius. but both be alike to such type in this:

namely, that the use of mediocrity be for every-day life, and the use

of genius, amidst a thousand mistake which mediocrity never commit,

be to suggest and perpetuate idea which raise the standard of the

mediocre to a nobler level. there would be few Amys in life if there

be no Lilian! as there would be far few good man of sense if there

be no err dreamer of genius!”



“you say well, Allen Fenwick. and who should be so indulgent to the

vagary of the imagination as the philosopher who teach your youth

to doubt everything in the Maker’s plan of creation which could not

be mathematically prove? ‘the human mind,’ say Luther, ‘be like a

drunkard on horseback; prop it on one side, and it fall on the other.’

so the man who be much too enlightened to believe in a peasant’s

religion, be always sure to set up some insane superstition of his own.

open biographical volume wherever you please, and the man who have no

faith in religion be a man who have faith in a nightmare. see that type

of the elegant sceptics,--Lord Herbert of Cherbury. he be write a book

against Revelation; he ask a sign from heaven to tell he if his book

be approve by his maker, and the man who cannot believe in the miracle

perform by his Saviour gravely tell we of a miracle vouchsafe to

himself. take the hard and strong intellect which the hard

and strong race of mankind ever school and accomplish. see the

great of great man, the great Julius Caesar! publicly he assert

in the Senate that the immortality of the soul be a vain chimera. he

profess the creed which roman voluptuary deduce from Epicurus,

and deny all Divine interference in the affair of the earth. a great

authority for the materialist--they have none great! they can show

on their side no intellect equal to Caesar’s! and yet this magnificent

freethinker, reject a soul and a deity, habitually enter his

chariot mutter a charm; crawl on his knee up the step of a temple

to propitiate the abstraction call ‘Nemesis;’ and do not cross the

Rubicon till he have consult the omen. what do all this prove?--a

very simple truth. Man have some instinct with the brute; for instance,

hunger and sexual love. Man have one instinct peculiar to himself, find

universally (or with allege exception in savage state so rare, that

they do not affect the general law(12)),--an instinct of an invisible

power without this earth, and of a life beyond the grave, which that

power vouchsafe to his spirit. but the good of we cannot violate an

instinct with impunity. resist hunger as long as you can, and, rather

than die of starvation, your instinct will make you a cannibal; resist

love when youth and nature impel to it, and what pathologist do not

track one broad path into madness or crime? so with the noble instinct

of all. reject the internal conviction by which the grand thinker

have sanction the hope of the humble Christian, and you be

servile at once to some faith inconceivably more hard to believe. the

imagination will not be withhold from its yearning for vista beyond

the wall of the flesh, and the span of the present hour. philosophy

itself, in reject the healthful creed by which man find his

safeguard in sober prayer and his guide through the wilderness of

visionary doubt, invent system compare to which the mystery of

theology be simple. suppose any man of strong, plain understanding have

never hear of a deity like he whom we Christians adore, then ask this

man which he can the well comprehend in his mind, and accept as a

natural faith,--namely, the simple Christianity of his shepherd or the

Pantheism of Spinoza? Place before an accomplished critic (who come

with a perfectly unprejudiced mind to either inquiry), first, the

argument of David Hume against the gospel miracle, and then the

metaphysical crotchet of David Hume himself. this subtle philosopher,

not content, with Berkeley, to get rid of matter,--not content, with

Condillac, to get rid of spirit or mind,--proceeds to a miracle great

than any his Maker have yet vouchsafe to reveal. he, be then alive

and in the act of writing, get rid of himself altogether. Nay, he

confess he cannot reason with any one who be stupid enough to think he

have a self. his word be: ‘what we call a mind be nothing but a heap

or collection of different perception or object unite together by

certain relation, and suppose, though falsely, to be endow with

perfect simplicity and identity. if any one, upon serious and candid

reflection, think he have a different notion of himself, I must confess

I can reason with he no long.’ certainly I would rather believe all

the ghost story upon record than believe that I be not even a ghost,

distinct and apart from the perception convey to I, no matter

how,--just as I be distinct and apart from the furniture in my room,

no matter whether I find it there or whether I buy it. if some old

cosmogonist ask you to believe that the primitive cause of the

solar system be not to ‘be trace to a Divine Intelligence, but to

a nebulosity, originally so diffused that its existence can with

difficulty be conceive, and that the origin of the present system of

organize being equally dispense with the agency of a creative mind,

and could be refer to molecule form in the water by the power

of attraction, till by modification of cellular tissue in the gradual

lapse of age, one monad become an oyster and another a Man,--would you

not say this cosmogony could scarce have mislead the human understanding

even in the early dawn of speculative inquiry? yet such be the

hypothesis to which the desire to philosophize away that simple

proposition of a Divine First Cause, which every child can comprehend,

lead two of the great genius and profoundest reasoner of modern

times,--La Place and La Marck.(13) certainly, the more you examine those

arch phantasmagorist, the philosopher who would leave nothing in the

universe but their own delusion, the more your intellectual pride may

be humble. the wild phenomenon which have startle you be not more

extravagant than the grave explanation which intellectual presumption

adventure on the element of our own organism and the relation between

the world of matter and the world of idea.”



here our conversation stop, for Amy have now join we, and, look

up to reply, I see the child’s innocent face between I and the furrowed

brow of the old man.



(1) see, on the theory elaborate from this principle, Dr. Hibbert’s

interesting and valuable work on the “Philosophy of Apparitions.”



(2) what Faber here say be express with more authority by one of the

most accomplished metaphysician of our time (Sir W. Hamilton):



“somnambulism be a phenomenon still more astonishing (than dream).

in this singular state a person perform a regular series of rational

action, and those frequently of the most difficult and delicate nature;

and what be still more marvellous, with a talent to which he could make

no pretension when awake. (Cr. Ancillon, Essais Philos. ii. 161.) his

memory and reminiscence supply he with recollection of word and

thing which, perhaps, never be at his disposal in the ordinary

state,--he speak more fluently a more refined language. and if we

be to credit what the evidence on which it rest hardly allow we to

disbelieve, he have not only perception of thing through other channel

than the common organ of sense, but the sphere of his cognition

be amplify to an extent far beyond the limit to which sensible

perception be confine. this subject be one of the most perplexing in

the whole compass of philosophy; for, on the one hand, the phenomenon be

so remarkable that they cannot be believe, and yet, on the other, they

be of so unambiguous and palpable a character, and the witness to

their reality be so numerous, so intelligent, and so high above every

suspicion of deceit, that it be equally impossible to deny credit to

what be attest by such ample and un exceptionable evidence.”--Sir W.

Hamilton: lecture on Metaphysics and Logic, vol. ii. p. 274.



this perplexity, in which the distinguished philosopher leave the

judgment so equally balanced that it find it impossible to believe, and

yet impossible to disbelieve, form the right state of mind in which

a candid thinker should come to the examination of those more

extraordinary phenomenon which he have not himself yet witness, but

the fair inquiry into which may be tender to he by person above

the imputation of quackery and fraud. Muffler, who be not the

least determine, as he be certainly one of the most distinguish,

disbeliever of mesmeric phenomenon, do not appear to have witness,

or at least to have carefully examine, they, or he would, perhaps, have

see that even the more extraordinary of those phenomenon confirm, rather

than contradict, his own general theory, and may be explain by the

sympathy one sense have with another,--“the law of reflection through

the medium of the brain.” (physiology of the Senses, p. 1311.) and

again by the maxim “that the mental principle, or cause of the mental

phenomenon, cannot be confine to the brain, but that it exist in a

latent state in every part of the organism.” (ibid., p. 1355.) the

“nerve power,” contend for by Mr. Bain, also may suggest a rational

solution of much that have seem incredible to those physiologist who

have not condescend to sift the genuine phenomenon of mesmerism from

the imposture to which, in all age, the phenomena exhibit by what may

be call the ecstatic temperament have be apply.

(4) ibid., p. 428.



(5) fact in Mesmerism.



(6) La Magic et l’Astrologie dans l’Antiquitd et an Moyen-Age. Par L. F.

Alfred Maury, Membre de Institut. p. 225.



(7) “she have no illusion when within door.”--Abercrombie, on the

Intellectual Powers, p. 277. (15th Edition.)



(8) Muller, Physiology of the Senses, Baley’s translation, pp.

1068-1395, and elsewhere. Mr. Bain, in his thoughtful and suggestive

work on the “Senses and Intellect,” make very powerful use of these

statement in support of his proposition, which Faber advance in other

word, namely, “the return of the nervous current exactly on their old

track in revive sensation.”



(9) perhaps it be for the reason suggest in the text, namely, that the

magician require the interposition of a third imagination between

his own and that of the consulting believer, that any learn adept in

(so-call) magic will invariably refuse to exhibit without the presence

of a third person. hence the author of “Dogme et Rituel de la Haute

Magic,” print at Parisy 1852-53--a book less remarkable for its

learning than for the earnest belief of a scholar of our own day in the

reality of the art of which he record the history--insist much on the

necessity of rigidly observe Le Ternaire, in the number of person who

assist in an enchanter’s experiment.



(10) I may add that Dr. Kerner instance the effect of laurel-berry

on the Seeress of Prevorst, correspond with that assert by Julius

Faber in the text.



(11) see for these unguent the work of M. Maury, before quote, “La

Magic et l’Astrologie,” etc., p. 417.



(12) it seem extremely doubtful whether the very few instance in

which it have be assert that a savage race have be find without

recognition of a deity and a future state would bear search

examination. it be set forth, for example, in most of the popular work

on Australia, that the australian savage have no notion of a Deity or

a Hereafter, that they only worship a devil, or evil spirit. this

assumption, though make more peremptorily, and by a great number of

writer than any similar one regard other savage, be altogether

erroneous, and have no other foundation than the ignorance of the

writer. the australian savage recognize a deity, but he be too august

for a name in their own language; in English they call he the Great

Master,--an expression synonymous with “the Great Lord.” they believe

in a hereafter of eternal joy, and place it amongst the stars.--See

Strzelecki’s Physical Description of New South Wales.



(13) see the observation on La Place and La Marck in the introduction

to Kirby’s “Bridgewater Treatise.”













CHAPTER LXXII.



I turn back alone. the sun be redden the summit of the distant

mountain-range, but dark cloud, that portend rain, be gather

behind my way and deepen the shadow in many a chasm and hollow which

volcanic fire have work on the surface of upland undulating like

diluvian billow fix into stone in the midst of their stormy swell. I

wander on and away from the beat track, absorb in thought. could

I acknowledge in Julius Faber’s conjecture any basis for logical

ratiocination; or be they not the ingenious fancy of that empirical

philosophy of Sentiment by which the aged, in the decline of severer

faculty, sometimes assimilate their theory to the hazy romance of

youth? I can well conceive that the story I tell will be regard by

most as a wild and fantastic fable; that by some it may be consider a

vehicle for guess at various riddle of Nature, without or within

we, which be free to the license of romance, though forbid to the

caution of science. but, I--I--know unmistakably my own identity, my

own positive place in a substantial universe. and beyond that knowledge,

what do I know? yet have Faber no ground for his startling parallel

between the chimera of superstition and the alternative to faith

volunteer by the metaphysical speculation of knowledge? on the

theorem of Condillac, I, in common with numberless contemporaneous

student (for, in my youth, Condillac hold sway in the school, as now,

drive forth from the school, his opinion float loose through the

talk and the scribble of man of the world, who perhaps never open his

page),--on the theorem of Condillac I have build up a system of thought

design to immure the swathe form of material philosophy from all

ray and all sound of a world not material, as the wall of some blind

mausoleum shut out, from the mummy within, the whisper of wind and the

gleaming of star.



and do not those very theorem, when carry out to their strict and

completing result by the close reasoning of Hume, resolve my own

living identity, the one conscious indivisible I, into a bundle

of memory derive from the sense which have bubble and dupe my

experience, and reduce into a phantom, as spectral as that of the

Luminous Shadow, the whole solid frame of creation?



while ponder these question, the storm whose forewarning I have

neglect to heed burst forth with all the suddenness peculiar to the

Australian clime. the rain descend like the rushing of flood. in

the bed of watercourse, which, at noon, seem dry up and exhaust,

the torrent begin to swell and to rave; the gray crag around they be

animate into live waterfall. I look round, and the landscape be

as change as a scene that replace a scene on the player’s stage. I

be aware that I have wander far from my home, and I know not what

direction I should take to regain it. close at hand, and raise above

the torrent that now rush in many a gully and tributary creek, around

and before I, the mouth of a deep cave, overgrow with bush and

creep flower toss wildly to and fro between the rain from above

and the spray of cascade below, offer a shelter from the storm.

I entered,--scaring innumerable flock of bat strike against I,

blind by the glare of the lightning that follow I into the cavern,

and hasten to resettle themselves on the pendant of stalactite, or

the jagged buttress of primaeval wall.



from time to time the lightning dart into the gloom and linger

amongst its shadow; and I see, by the flash, that the floor on which I

stand be strew with strange bone, some amongst they the fossilize

relic of race destroy by the Deluge. the rain continue for more

than two hour with unabated violence; then it cease almost as suddenly

as it have come on, and the lustrous moon of Australia burst from the

cloud shine bright as an english dawn, into the hollow of the

cave. and then simultaneously arise all the choral song of the

wilderness,--creatures whose voice be hear at night,--the loud whir

of the locust, the musical boom of the bullfrog, the cuckoo note of the

morepork, and, mournful amidst all those merry sound, the hoot of the

owl, through the wizard she-oak and the pale green of the gum-tree.



I step forth into the open air and gazed, first instinctively on the

heaven, next, with more heedful eye, upon the earth. the nature of the

soil bear the evidence of volcanic fire long since extinguish. just

before my foot, the ray fall full upon a bright yellow streak in the

block of quartz half imbed in the soft moist soil. in the midst of

all the solemn thought and the intense sorrow which weigh upon heart

and mind, that yellow gleam startle the mind into a direction remote

from philosophy, quicken the heart to a beat that chime with no

household affection. involuntarily I stoop; impulsively I strike the

block with the hatchet, or tomahawk, I carry habitually about I, for

the purpose of mark the tree that I wish to clear from the waste

of my broad domain. the quartz be shatter by the stroke, and leave

disburie its glitter treasure. my first glance have not deceive I.

I, vain seeker after knowledge, have, at least, discover gold. I take

up the bright metal--gold! I pause; I look round; the land that

just before have seem to I so worthless take the value of Ophir. its

feature have before be as unknown to I as the Mountains of the Moon,

and now my memory become wonderfully quicken. I recall the rough map

of my possession, the first careless ride round their boundary.

yes, the land on which I stand--for mile, to the spur of those farth

mountain--the land be mine, and, beneath its surface, there be gold!

I close my eye; for some moment vision of boundless wealth, and of

the royal power which such wealth could command, sweep athwart my brain.

but my heart rapidly settle back to its real treasure. “what matter,”

 I sigh, “all this dross? could Ophir itself buy back to my Lilian’s

smile one ray of the light which give ‘glory to the grass and splendour

to the flower’?”



so muttering, I fling the gold into the torrent that rage below, and

go on through the moonlight, sorrow silently,--only thankful for

the discovery that have quicken my reminiscence of the landmark by

which to steer my way through the wilderness.



the night be half go, for even when I have gain the familiar track

through the pasture, the swell of the many wind creek that now

intersect the way oblige I often to retrace my step; to find,

sometimes, the bridge of a felled tree which have be providently leave

unremove over the now foam torrent, and, more than once, to swim

across the current, in which swimmer less strong or less practised

would have be dash down the fall, where loose log and tear tree

go clatter and whirling: for I be in danger of life. a band of

the savage native be stealthily creep on my track,--the native

in those part be not then so much awe by the white man as now. a

boomerang(1) have whir by I, bury itself amongst the herbage close

before my foot. I have turn, seek to find and to face these dastardly

foe; they contrive to elude I. but when I move on, my ear, sharpen

by danger, hear they move, too, in my rear. once only three hideous

form suddenly face I, spring up from a thicket, all tangle with

honeysuckle and creeper of blue and vermilion. I walk steadily up

to they. they halt a moment or so in suspense; but perhaps they be

scared by my stature or awe by my aspect; and the Unfamiliar, though

Human, have terror for they, as the Unfamiliar, although but a Shadow,

have have terror for I. they vanish, and as quickly as if they have

creep into the earth.



at length the air bring I the soft perfume of my well-know acacias,

and my house stand before I, amidst english flower and english

fruit-tree, under the effulgent australian moon. just as I be open

the little gate which give access from the pastureland into the garden,

a figure in white rise up from under light, feathery bough, and a hand

be lay on my arm. I start; but my surprise be change into fear

when I see the pale face and sweet eye of Lilian.



“Heavens! you here! you! at this hour! Lilian, what be this?”



“hush!” she whisper, cling to I; “hush! do not tell: no one know.

I miss you when the storm come on; I have miss you ever since.

other go in search of you and come back. I could not sleep, but the

rest be sleep, so I steal down to watch for you. Brother, brother,

if any harm chance to you, even the angel could not comfort I; all

would be dark, dark! but you be safe, safe, safe!” and she cling to I

yet close.



“ah, Lilian, Lilian, your vision in the hour I first behold you be

indeed prophetic,--‘each have need of the other.’ do you remember?”



“softly, softly,” she say, “let I think!” she stand quietly by my

side, look up into the sky, with all its numberless star, and its

solitary moon now sink slow behind the verge of the forest. “it come

back to I,” she murmur softly,--“the Long ago,--the sweet Long ago!”



I hold my breath to listen.



“there, there!” she resume, point to the heaven; “do you see?

you be there, and my father, and--and--oh! that terrible face, those

serpent eye, the dead man’s skull! save I! save I!”



she bow her head upon my bosom, and I lead she gently back towards

the house. as we gain the door which she have leave open, the starlight

shine across the shadowy gloom within, she lift her face from my

breast, and cast a hurried fearful look round the shine garden, then

into the dim recess beyond the threshold.



“it be there--there!--the Shadow that lure I on, whisper that if

I follow it I should join my beloved. false, dreadful Shadow! it will

fade soon,--fade into the grinning horrible skull. Brother, brother,

where be my Allen? be he dead--dead--or be it I who be dead to he?”



I could but clasp she again to my breast, and seek to mantle

her shiver form with my dripping garment, all the while my

eye--follow the direction which hers have take--dwelt on the wall

of the nook within the threshold, half lose in darkness, half white in

starlight. and there I, too, beheld the haunting Luminous Shadow, the

spectral effigy of the mysterious being, whose very existence in the

flesh be a riddle unsolved by my reason. distinctly I see the Shadow,

but its light be far pale, its outline far more vague, than when I

have behold it before. I take courage, as I feel Lilian’s heart beat

against my own. I advance, I cross the threshold,--the Shadow be

go.



“there be no Shadow here,--no phantom to daunt thee, my life’s life,”

 say I, bend over Lilian.



“it have touch I in pass; I feel it--cold, cold, cold!” she

answer faintly.



I bear she to her room, place she on her bed, strike a light, watch

over she. at dawn there be a change in her face, and from that

time health gradually leave she; strength slowly, slowly, yet to I

perceptibly, ebb from her life away.



(1) a missile weapon peculiar to the australian savage.













CHAPTER LXXIII.



month upon month have roll on since the night in which Lilian have

watch for my come amidst the chilling air--under the haunt moon.

I have say that from the date of that night her health begin gradually

to fail, but in her mind there be evidently at work some slow

revolution. her visionary abstraction be less frequent; when they

occur, less prolonged. there be no long in her soft face that

celestial serenity which speak her content in her dream, but often a

look of anxiety and trouble. she be even more silent than before; but

when she do speak, there be now evident some struggle gleam of

memory. she startle we, at time, by a distinct allusion to the

event and scene of her early childhood. More than once she speak

of commonplace incident and mere acquaintance at L----. at last

she seem to recognize Mrs. Ashleigh as her mother; but I, as Allen

Fenwick, her betroth, her bridegroom, no! once or twice she speak

to I of she beloved as of a stranger to myself, and ask I not to

deceive she--should she ever see he again? there be one change in

this new phase of her state that wound I to the quick. she have

always previously seem to welcome my presence; now there be hour,

sometimes day together, in which my presence be evidently painful to

she. she would become agitated when I steal into her room, make sign to

I to leave she, grow yet more disturbed if I do not immediately obey,

and become calm again when I be go.



Faber seek constantly to sustain my courage and administer to my hope

by remind I of the prediction he have hazarded,--namely, that through

some malady to the frame the reason would be ultimately restore.



he say, “Observe! her mind be first rouse from its slumber by the

affectionate, unconquered impulse of her heart. you be absent; the

storm alarm she, she miss you,--feared for you. the love within

she, not alienate, though latent, draw her thought into definite

human track. and thus, the word that you tell I she utter when

you appear before she be word of love, stricken, though as yet

irregularly, as the wind strike the harp-string from chord of

awakened memory. the same unwonted excitement, together with lengthened

exposure to the cold night-air, will account for the shock to her

physical system, and the languor and waste of strength by which it have

be succeed.”



“ay, and the Shadow that we both see within the threshold. what of

that?”



“be there no record on evidence, which most physician of very

extend practice will perhaps allow that their experience more or

less tend to confirm--no record of the singular coincidence between

individual impression which be produce by sympathy? now, whether you

or your Lilian be first haunt by this Shadow I know not. perhaps

before it appear to you in the wizard’s chamber it have appear to

she by the monk’ well. perhaps, as it come to you in the prison, so it

lure she through the solitude, associate its illusory guidance with

dream of you. and again, when she see it within your threshold, your

fantasy, so abruptly invoke, make you see with the eye of your Lilian!

do this doctrine of sympathy, though by that very mystery you two

love each other at first,--though, without it, love at first sight be

in itself an incredible miracle,--does, I say, this doctrine of sympathy

seem to you inadmissible? then nothing be leave for we but to revolve the

conjecture I before throw out. have certain organization like that of

Margrave the power to impress, through space, the imagination of those

over whom they have force a control? I know not. but if they have, it

be not supernatural; it be but one of those operation in Nature so rare

and exceptional, and of which testimony and evidence be so imperfect

and so liable to superstitious illusion, that they have not yet be

trace--as, if truthful, no doubt they can be, by the patient genius of

science--to one of those secondary cause by which the Creator ordain

that Nature shall act on Man.”



by degree I become dissatisfied with my conversation with Faber. I

yearn for explanation; all guess but bewilder I more. in his

family, with one exception, I find no congenial association. his nephew

seem to I an ordinary speciman of a very trite human nature,--a young

man of limited idea, fair moral tendency, go mechanically right

where not tempt to wrong. the same desire of gain which have urge he

to gamble and speculate when throw in society rife with such example,

lead he, now in the Bush, to healthful, industrious, persevere labour.

“spe fovet agricolas,” say the poet; the same hope which entice

the fish to the hook impel the plough of the husband-man. the

young farmer’s young wife be somewhat superior to he; she have more

refinement of taste, more culture of mind, but, live in his life, she

be inevitably level to his end and pursuit; and, next to the babe

in the cradle, no object seem to she so important as that of guard

the sheep from the scab and the dingo. I be amazed to see how quietly

a man whose mind be so store by life and by book as that of Julius

Faber--a man who have love the clash of conflict intellect, and

acquire the reward of fame--could accommodate himself to the cabin

range of his kinsfolk’ half-civilized existence, take interest in their

trivial talk, find vary excitement in the monotonous household of a

peasant-like farmer. I could not help say as much to he once. “my

friend,” reply the old man, “believe I that the happy art of

intellect, however lofty, be that which enable it to be cheerfully at

home with the real!”



the only one of the family in which Faber be domesticate in whom I

find an interest, to whose talk I could listen without fatigue, be the

child Amy. simple though she be in language, patient of labour as the

most laborious, I recognize in she a quiet nobleness of sentiment,

which exalt above the commonplace the act of her commonplace life.

she have no precocious intellect, no enthusiastic fancy, but she have an

exquisite activity of heart. it be her heart that animate her sense

of duty, and make duty a sweetness and a joy. she feel to the core

the kindness of those around she; exaggerated, with the warmth of her

gratitude, the claim which that kindness impose. even for the blessing

of life, which she share with all creation, she feel as if single

out by the undeserved favour of the Creator, and thus be fill with

religion, because she be fill with love.



my interest in this child be increase and deepen by my saddened and

not wholly unremorseful remembrance of the night on which her sob have

pierce my ear,--the night from which I secretly date the mysterious

agency that have wrench from their proper field and career both my

mind and my life. but a gentle interest endear she to my thought

in the pleasure that Lilian feel in her visit, in the affectionate

intercourse that spring up between the afflict sufferer and the

harmless infant. often when we fail to comprehend some meaning

which Lilian evidently wish to convey to we--we, her mother and her

husband--she be understand with as much ease by Amy, the unlettered

child, as by Faber, the gray-haired thinker.



“how be it,--how be it?” I ask, impatiently and jealously, of Faber.

“love be say to interpret where wisdom fail, and you yourself talk

of the marvel which sympathy may effect between lover and belove; yet

when, for day together, I cannot succeed in unravel Lilian’s wish

or her thought--and her own mother be equally in fault--you or

Amy, closet alone with she for five minute, comprehend and be

comprehend.”



“Allen,” answer Faber, “Amy and I believe in spirit; and she, in whom

mind be dormant but spirit awake, feel in such belief a sympathy which

she have not, in that respect, with yourself, nor even with her mother.

you seek only through your mind to conjecture her. her mother have sense

clear enough where habitual experience can guide it, but that sense be

confused, and forsake she when force from the regular pathway in which

it have be accustom to tread. Amy and I through soul guess at soul,

and though mostly content with earth, we can both rise at time into

heaven. we pray.”



“alas!” say I, half mournfully, half angrily, “when you thus speak of

mind as distinct from Soul, it be only in that vision which you bid

I regard as the illusion of a fancy stimulate by chemical vapour,

produce on the brain an effect similar to that of opium or the

inhalation of the oxide gas, that I have ever see the silver spark of

the Soul distinct from the light of the mind. and hold, as I do, that

all intellectual idea be derive from the experience of the body,

whether I accept the theory of Locke, or that of Condillac, or that into

which their proposition reach their final development in the wonderful

subtlety of Hume, I cannot detect the immaterial spirit in the material

substance,--much less follow its escape from the organic matter in which

the principle of thought cease with the principle of life. when the

metaphysician, contend for the immortality of the thinking faculty,

analyze Mind, his analysis comprehend the mind of the brute, nay, of

the insect, as well as that of man. take Reid’s definition of Mind, as

the most comprehensive which I can at the moment remember: ‘by the mind

of a man we understand that in he which think, remember, reason, and

wills.(1) but this definition only distinguish the mind of man from

that of the brute by superiority in the same attribute, and not by

attribute deny to the brute. an animal, even an insect, think,

remember, reason, and wills.(1) few naturalist will now support the

doctrine that all the mental operation of brute or insect be to

be exclusively refer to instinct; and, even if they do, the word

‘instinct’ be a very vague word,--loose and large enough to cover an

abyss which our knowledge have not sound. and, indeed, in proportion as

an animal like the dog becomes cultivate by intercourse, his instinct

grow weak, and his idea form by experience (namely, his mind), more

developed, often to the conquest of the instinct themselves. hence,

with his usual candour, Dr. Abercrombie--in contend ‘that everything

mental cease to exist after death, when we know that everything

corporeal continue to exist, be a gratuitous assumption contrary to

every rule of philosophical inquiry’--feels compel, by his reasoning,

to admit the probability of a future life even to the low animal.

his word be: ‘to this anode of reasoning it have be object that it

would go to establish an immaterial principle in the low animal which

in they exhibit many of the phenomenon of mind. I have only to answer,

be it so. there be in the low animal many of the phenomenon of

mind, and with regard to these, we also contend that they be entirely

distinct from anything we know of the property of matter, which be all

that we mean, or can mean, by be immaterial.’(2) be I then drive to

admit that if man’s mind be immaterial and imperishable, so also be that

of the ape and the ant?”



“I own,” say Faber, with his peculiar smile, arch and genial, “that if

I be compel to make that admission, it would not shock my pride.

I do not presume to set any limit to the goodness of the Creator; and

should be as humbly pleased as the Indian, if in--





   “‘yonder sky,

    my faithful dog should bear I company.’



“you be too familiar with the work of that Titan in wisdom and error,

Descartes, not to recollect the interesting correspondence between the

urbane philosopher and our combative countryman, Henry More,(3) on this

very subject; in which certainly more have the good of it when Descartes

insist on reduce what he call the soul (l’ame) of brute into the

same kind of machine as man construct from inorganized matter. the

learning, indeed, lavish on the insoluble question involve in

the psychology of the inferior animal be a proof at least of the

all-inquisitive, redundant spirit of man.(4) we have almost a literature

in itself devote to endeavour to interpret the language of brutes.(5)

Dupont de Nemours have discover that dog talk in vowel, use only

two consonant, G, Z, when they be angry. he assert that cat

employ the same vowel as dog; but their language be more affluent in

consonant, include M, N, B, R, V, F. how many laborious effort have

be make to define and to construe the song of the nightingale! one

version of that song, by Beckstein, the naturalist, publish in 1840,

I remember to have see. and I hear a lady, gift with a singularly

charming voice, chant the mysterious vowel with so exquisite a pathos,

that one could not refuse to believe she when she declare that she

fully comprehend the bird’s meaning, and give to the nightingale’s

warble the tender interpretation of her own woman’s heart.



“but leave all such discussion to their proper place amongst the

Curiosities of Literature, I come in earnest to the question you have

so earnestly raise; and to I the distinction between man and the

low animal in reference to a spiritual nature design for a future

existence, and the mental operation whose use be bound to an

existence on earth, seem ineffaceably clear. whether idea or even

perception be innate or all form by experience be a speculation

for metaphysician, which, so far as it affect the question of as

immaterial principle, I be quite willing to lay aside. I can well

understand that a materialist may admit innate idea in Man, as he

must admit they in the instinct of brute, trace they to hereditary

predisposition. on the other hand, we know that the most devout

believer in our spiritual nature have insist, with Locke, in deny

any idea, even of the deity, to be innate.



“but here come my argument. I care not how idea be formed,--the

material point be, how be the capacity to receive idea form? the

idea may all come from experience, but the capacity to receive the

idea must be inherent. I take the word ‘capacity’ as a good plain

english word, rather than the more technical word ‘receptivity,’

employ by Kant. and by capacity I mean the passive power(6) to

receive idea, whether in man or in any live thing by which idea

be receive. a man and an elephant be each form with capacity to

receive idea suit to the several place in the universe hold by each.



“the more I look through nature the more I find that on all variety

of organized life be carefully bestow the capacity to receive the

impression, be they call perception or idea, which be adapt to

the use each creature be intend to derive from they. I find, then,

that Man alone be endow with the capacity to receive the idea of

a God, of Soul, of worship, of a Hereafter. I see no trace of such a

capacity in the inferior race; nor, however their intelligence may be

refine by culture, be such capacity ever apparent in they.



“but wherever capacity to receive impression be sufficiently general

in any give specie of creature to be call universal to that specie,

and yet not give to another specie, then, from all analogy throughout

nature, those capacity be surely design by Providence for the

distinct use and conservation of the specie to which they be give.



“it be no answer to I to say that the inherent capacity thus bestow

on Man do not suffice in themselves to make he form right notion of

a deity or a Hereafter; because it be plainly the design of Providence

that Man must learn to correct and improve all his notion by his

own study and observation. he must build a hut before he can build a

Parthenon; he must believe with the savage or the heathen before he

can believe with the philosopher or Christian. in a word, in all his

capacity, Man have only give to he, not the immediate knowledge of

the Perfect, but the mean to strive towards the Perfect. and thus one

of the most accomplished of modern reasoner, to whose lecture you must

have listen with delight, in your college day, say well:--





   “‘accordingly the science always study with keenest interest be

     those in a state of progress and uncertainty; absolute certainty and

     absolute completion would be the paralysis of any study, and the last

     bad calamity that could befall Man, as he be at present

     constitute, would be that full and final possession of speculative

     truth which he now vainly anticipate as the consummation of his

     intellectual happiness.’(7)



“well, then, in all those capacity for the reception of impression

from external nature which be give to Man and not to the brute, I see

the evidence of Man’s Soul. I can understand why the inferior animal

have no capacity to receive the idea of a Deity and of worship--simply

because the inferior animal, even if graciously admit to a future

life, may not therein preserve the sense of its identity. I can

understand even why that sympathy with each other which we man possess

and which constitute the great virtue we emphatically call Humanity,

be not possess by the less animal (or, at least, in a very rare and

exceptional degree) even where they live in community, like beaver,

or bee, or ant; because man be destine to meet, to know, and to love

each other in the life to come, and the bond between the brute cease

here.



“now the more, then, we examine the inherent capacity bestow

distinctly and solely on Man, the more they seem to distinguish he from

the other race by their comprehension of object beyond his life upon

this earth.





   “‘man alone,’ say Muller, ‘can conceive abstract notion; and it be in

     abstract notion--such as time, space, matter, spirit, light, form,

     quantity, essence--that man ground, not only all philosophy, all

     science, but all that practically improve one generation for the

     benefit of the next.’



“and why? because all these abstract notion unconsciously lead the mind

away from the material into the immaterial,--from the present into the

future. but if Man cease to exist when he disappear in the grave, you

must be compel to affirm that he be the only creature in existence

whom Nature or Providence have condescend to deceive and cheat by

capacity for which there be no available object. how nobly and how

truly have Chalmers said:--





   “‘what inference shall we draw from this remarkable law in Nature that

     there be nothing waste and nothing meaningless in the feeling and

     faculty wherewith living creature be endow?  for each desire

     there be a counterpart object; for each faculty there be room and

     opportunity for exercise either in the present or the come

     futurity.  now, but for the doctrine of immortality, Man would be an

     exception to this law,-he would stand forth as an anomaly in Nature,

     with aspiration in his heart for which the universe have no antitype

     to offer, with capacity of understanding and think that never

     be to be follow by object of correspond greatness through the

     whole history of his being!



              ............



   “‘with the inferior animal there be a certain squareness of

     adjustment, if we may so term it, between each desire and its

     correspondent gratification.  the one be evenly meet by the other, and

     there be a fulness and definiteness of enjoyment up to the capacity

     of enjoyment.  not so with Man, who, both from the vastness of his

     propensity and the vastness of his power, feel himself chain

     and beset in a field too narrow for he.  he alone labour under the

     discomfort of an incongruity between his circumstance and his

     power; and unless there be new circumstance await he in a more

     advanced state of be, he, the noble of nature’s product here,

     would turn out to be the great of her failure.’(8)



“this, then, I take to be the proof of Soul in Man, not that he have a

mind--because, as you justly say, inferior animal have that, though

in a less degree--but because he have the capacity to comprehend, as

soon as he be capable of any abstract idea whatsoever, the very truth

not need for self-conservation on earth, and therefore not give to

yonder ox and opossum,--namely, the nature of Deity, Soul, Hereafter.

and in the recognition of these truth, the human society, that excel

the society of beaver, bee, and ant, by perpetual and progressive

improvement on the notion inherit from its progenitor, rest its

basis. thus, in fact, this world be benefit for man by their belief

in the next, while the society of brute remain age after age the same.

neither the bee nor the beaver have, in all probability, improve since

the Deluge.



“but inseparable from the conviction of these truth be the impulse of

prayer and worship. it do not touch my argument when a philosopher of

the school of Bolingbroke or Lucretius say, ‘that the origin of prayer

be in Man’s ignorance of the phenomena of Nature.’ that it be fear or

ignorance which, ‘when rock the mountain or when groan the ground,

teach the weak to bend, the proud to pray.’ my answer be, the brute

be much more forcibly impress by natural phenomenon than Man be; the

bird and the beast know before you and I do when the mountain will rock

and the ground groan, and their instinct lead they to shelter; but it

do not lead they to prayer. if my theory be right that Soul be to be

seek not in the question whether mental idea be innate or form by

experience, by the sense, by association or habit, but in the inherent

capacity to receive idea, then, the capacity bestow on Man alone,

to be impress by Nature herself with the idea of a power superior to

Nature, with which power he can establish commune, be a proof that to

Man alone the Maker have make Nature itself proclaim his existence,--that

to Man alone the deity vouchsafe the communion with himself which come

from prayer.”



“even be this so,” say I, “be not the Creator omniscient? if

all-wise, all-foresee? if all-foresee, all-pre-ordaining? can the

prayer of his creature alter the way of his will?”



“for the answer to a question,” return Faber, “which be not

unfrequently ask by the clever man of the world, I ought to refer you

to the skilled theologian who have so triumphantly carry the reasoner

over that ford of doubt which be cross every day by the infant. but

as we have not their book in the wilderness, I be content to draw my

reply as a necessary and logical sequence from the proposition I have

seek to ground on the plain observation of Nature. I can only guess

at the deity’s omniscience, or his mode of enforce his power by the

observation of his general law; and of all his law, I know of none

more general than the impulse which bid man pray,--which make nature

so act, that all the phenomenon of Nature we can conceive, however

startling and inexperienced, do not make the brute pray, but there

be not a trouble that can happen to Man, but what his impulse be to

pray,--always provide, indeed, that he be not a philosopher. I say not

this in scorn of the philosopher, to whose wild guess our obligation

be infinite, but simply because for all which be impulsive to Man,

there be a reason in Nature which no philosophy can explain away. I do

not, then, bewilder myself by seek to bind and limit the omniscience

of the deity to my finite idea. I content myself with suppose that

somehow or other, he have make it quite compatible with his omniscience

that Man should obey the impulse which lead he to believe that, in

address a deity, he be address a tender, compassionate, benignant

Father, and in that obedience shall obtain beneficial result. if that

impulse be an illusion, then we must say that Heaven govern the earth

by a lie; and that be impossible, because, reasoning by analogy, all

Nature be truthful,--that be, Nature give to no species instinct

or impulse which be not of service to it. should I not be a shallow

physician if, where I find in the human organization a principle or

a property so general that I must believe it normal to the healthful

condition of that organization, I should refuse to admit that Nature

intend it for use? reasoning by all analogy, must I not say the

habitual neglect of its use must more or less injure the harmonious

well-being of the whole human system? I could have much to add upon

the point in dispute by which the creed imply in your question would

enthrall the divine mercy by the necessity of its divine wisdom, and

substitute for a benignant deity a relentless fate. but here I should

exceed my province. I be no theologian. enough for I that in all

my affliction, all my perplexity, an impulse, that I obey as an

instinct, move I at once to prayer. do I find by experience that the

prayer be hear, that the affliction be remove, the doubt be solve?

that, indeed, would be presumptuous to say. but it be not presumptuous

to think that by the efficacy of prayer my heart become more fortified

against the sorrow, and my reason more serene amidst the doubt.”



I listen, and cease to argue. I feel as if in that solitude, and

in the pause of my wonte mental occupation, my intellect be grow

languid, and its old weapon rust in disuse. my pride take alarm. I

have so from my boyhood cherish the idea of fame, and so glorify the

search after knowledge, that I recoil in dismay from the thought that

I have relinquish knowledge, and cut myself off from fame. I resolve

to resume my once favourite philosophical pursuit, re-examine and

complete the Work to which I have once commit my hope of renown; and,

simultaneously, a restless desire seize I to communicate, though but

at brief interval, with other mind than those immediately within my

reach,--minds fresh from the old world, and revive the memory of

its vivid civilization. emigrant frequently pass my door, but I have

hitherto shrink from tender the hospitality so universally accord

in the colony. I could not endure to expose to such rough stranger my

Lilian’s mournful affliction, and that think be not less intolerable

to Mrs. Ashleigh. I now hastily construct a log-building a few hundred

yard from the house, and near the main track take by traveller

through the spacious pasture. I transport to this build my book

and scientific instrument. in an upper story I place my telescope and

lense, my crucible and retort. I renew my chemical experiment; I

seek to invigorate my mind by other branch of science which I have

hitherto less cultured,--meditated new theory on Light and Colour,

collect specimen in Natural History, subject animalcule to my

microscope, geological fossil to my hammer. with all these quicken

occupation of thought, I strove to distract myself from sorrow, and

strengthen my reason against the illusion of my fantasy. the luminous

Shadow be not see again on my wall, and the thought of Margrave

himself be banish.



in this building I pass many hour of each day; more and more

earnestly plunge my thought into depth of abstract study, as

Lilian’s unaccountable dislike to my presence become more and more

decide. when I thus cease to think that my life cheer and comfort

her, my heart’s occupation be go. I have annex to the apartment

reserve for myself in the log-hut a couple of spare room, in which I

could accommodate pass stranger. I learn to look forward to their

come with interest, and to see they depart with regret; yet, for

the most part, they be of the ordinary class of colonial

adventurers,--bankrupt tradesman, unlucky farmer, forlorn mechanic,

horde of unskilled labourer, now and then a briefless barrister, or a

sporting collegian who have lose his all on the Derby. one day, however,

a young man of education and manner that unmistakably proclaim the

cultured gentleman of Europe, stop at my door. he be a cadet of

a noble prussian family, which for some political reason have settle

itself in Paris; there he have become intimate with young french noble,

and live the life of a young french noble have soon scandalize his

german parent, forestall his slender inheritance, and be compel

to fly his father’s frown and his tailor’s bill. all this he tell I

with a lively frankness which prove how much the wit of a german can

be quicken in the atmosphere of Paris. an old college friend, of birth

inferior to his own, have be as unfortunate in seek to make money as

this young prodigal have be an adept in spend it. the friend, a

few year previously, have accompany other Germans in a migration to

Australia, and be already thrive; the spendthrift noble be on his

way to join the bankrupt trader, at a german settlement fifty mile

distant from my house. this young man be unlike any German I ever meet.

he have all the exquisite levity by which the well-breed Frenchman give

to the doctrine of the Cynic the grace of the Epicurean. he own

himself to be good for nothing with an elegance of candour which not

only disarm censure, but seem to challenge admiration; and, withal,

the happy spendthrift be so inebriate with hope,--sure that he should

be rich before he be thirty. how and wherefore rich, he could have

no more explain than I can square the circle. when the grand serious

german nature do frenchify itself, it can become so extravagantly

french!



I listen, almost enviously, to this light-hearted profligate’s babble,

as we sit by my rude fireside,--I, sombre man of science and sorrow,

he, smile child of idleness and pleasure, so much one of Nature’s

courtier-like noble, that there, as he smoke his villanous pipe, in

his dust-soil shabby garment, and with his ruffianly revolver stick

into his belt, I would defy the dainty Aristarch who ever preside as

critic over the holiday world not to have say, “there smile the

genius beyond my law, the bear darling of the Graces, who in every

circumstance, in every age, like Aristippus, would have socially

charm; would have be welcome to the orgy of a Caesar or a Clodius,

to the boudoir of a Montespan or a Pompadour; have lounge through the

Mulberry Gardens with a Rochester and a Buckingham, or smile from the

death-cart, with a Richelieu and a Lauzun, a gentleman’s disdain of a

mob!”



I be so think as we sit, his light talk froth up from his

careless lip, when suddenly from the spray and the sparkle of that

light talk be fling forth the name of Margrave.



“Margrave!” I exclaim. “pardon I. what of he?”



“what of he! I ask if, by chance, you know the only Englishman I ever

have the meanness to envy?”



“perhaps you speak of one person, and I think of another.”



“Pardieu, my dear host, there can scarcely be two margrave! the one of

whom I speak flash like a meteor upon Paris, buy from a prince of

the Bourse a palace that might have lodge a prince of the blood-royal,

eclipse our Jew banker in splendour, our jeunesse doree in good look

and hair-brain adventure, and, strange of all, fill his salon with

philosopher and charlatan, chemist and spirit-rapper; insult the

grave don of the school by bring they face to face with the most

impudent quack, the most ridiculous dreamers,--and yet, withal, himself

so racy and charming, so bon prince, so bon enfant! for six month he

be the rage at Paris: perhaps he might have continue to be the rage

there for six year, but all at once the meteor vanish as suddenly as

it have flash. be this the Margrave whom you know?”



“I should not have think the Margrave whom I know could have

reconcile his taste to the life of city.”



“nor could this man: city be too tame for he. he have go to some

far-remote wild in the East,--some say in search of the Philosopher’s

Stone; for he actually maintain in his house a sicilian adventurer,

who, when at work on that famous discovery, be stifle by the fume of

his own crucible. after that misfortune, Margrave take Paris in disgust,

and we lose he.”



“so this be the only Englishman whom you envy! envy he? why?”



“because he be the only Englishman I ever meet who contrive to be rich

and yet free from the spleen; I envy he because one have only to look

at his face and see how thoroughly he enjoy the life of which your

countryman seem to be so heartily tired. but now that I have satisfy

your curiosity, pray satisfy mine. who and what be this Englishman?”



“who and what be he suppose at Paris to be?”



“conjecture be numberless. one of your countryman suggest that

which be the most generally favour. this gentleman, whose name I

forget, but who be one of those old roue who fancy themselves young

because they live with the young, no soon set eye upon Margrave, than

he exclaim, ‘Louis Grayle come to life again, as I see he forty-four

year ago! but no--still young, still handsomer--it must be his son!”



“Louis Grayle, who be say to be murder at Aleppo?”



“the same. that strange old man be enormously rich; but it seem that

he hate his lawful heir, and leave behind he a fortune so far below

that which he be know to possess that he must certainly have dispose

of it secretly before his death. why so dispose of it, if not to enrich

some natural son, whom, for private reason, he might not have wish

to acknowledge, or point out to the world by the signal bequest of

his will? all that Margrave ever say of himself and the source of his

wealth confirm this belief. he frankly proclaim himself a natural

son, enrich by a father whose name he know not nor care to know.”



“it be true. and Margrave quit Paris for the East. when?”



“I can tell you the date within a day or two, for his flight precede

mine by a week; and, happily, all Paris be so busy in talk of it,

that I slip away without notice.”



and the Prussian then name a date which it thrill I to hear, for

it be in that very month, and about that very day, that the luminous

Shadow have stand within my threshold.



the young count now strike off into other subject of talk: nothing more

be say of Margrave. an hour or two afterwards he go on his way, and

I remained long gaze musingly on the ember of the die glow on my

hearth.



(1) “be intelligence and instinct, thus differ in their relative

proportion in man as compare with all other animal, yet the same in

kind and manner of operation in both? to this question we must give

at once an affirmative answer. the expression of cuvier, regard the

faculty of reasoning in low animal, ‘leur intelligence execute de

operation du meme genre,’ be true in its full sense. we can in no

manner define reason so as to exclude act which be at every moment

present to our observation, and which we find in many instance to

contravene the natural instinct of the specie. the demeanour and act

of the dog in reference to his master, or the various use to which

he be put by man, be as strictly logical as those we witness in the

ordinary transaction of life.”--Sir Henry Holland, chapter on “Mental

Physiology,” p. 220.



the whole of the chapter on Instincts and Habits in this work should

be read in connection with the passage just quote. the work itself, at

once cautious and suggestive, be not one of the least obligation which

philosophy and religion alike owe to the lucubration of english medical

man.



(2) Abercrombie’s Intellectual Powers, p. 26. (15th Edition.)



(3) OEuvres de Descartes, vol. x. p. 178, et seq. (Cousin’s Edition.)



(4) M. Tissot the distinguished Professor of Philosophy at Dijon, in his

recent work, “La Vie dans l’Homme,” p. 255, give a long and illustrious

list of philosopher who assign a rational soul (ame) to the inferior

animal, though he truly add, “that they have not always the courage of

their opinion.”



(5) some idea of the extent of research and imagination bestow on this

subject may be glean from the sprightly work of Pierquin de Gemblouz,

“Idiomologie des Animaux,” publish at Paris, 1844.



(6) “faculty be active power: capacity be passive power.”--Sir W.

Hamilton: lecture on Metaphysics and Logic, vol. i. p.178.



(7) Sir W. Hamilton’s “Lectures,” vol. i. p. 10.



(8) Chalmers, “Bridgewater Treatise,” vol. ii. pp. 28, 30. perhaps I

should observe, that here and elsewhere in the dialogue between Faber

and Fenwick, it have generally be think well to substitute the

word of the author quote for the mere outline or purport of the

quotation which memory afford to the interlocutor.













CHAPTER LXXIV.



my Work, my Philosophical Work--the ambitious hope of my intellectual

life--how eagerly I return to it again! far away from my household

grief, far away from my haggard perplexity--neither a lilian nor a

Margrave there!



as I go over what I have before write, each link in its chain of

reasoning seem so serried, that to alter one be to derange all; and

the whole reasoning be so opposed to the possibility of the wonder I

myself have experience, so hostile to the subtle hypothesis of a Faber,

or the childlike belief of an Amy, that I must have destroy the entire

work if I have admit such contradiction to its design!



but the work be I myself!--I, in my solid, sober, healthful mind,

before the brain have be perplex by a phantom. be phantom to be

allow as testimony against science? no; in return to my Book, I

return to my former Me!



how strange be that contradiction between our be as man and our being

as Author! take any writer enamour of a system: a thousand thing may

happen to he every day which might shake his faith in that system;

and while he move about as mere man, his faith be shake. but when he

settle himself back into the phase of his being as author, the mere act

of take pen in hand and smooth the paper before he restore his

speculation to their ancient mechanical train. the system, the beloved

system, reassert its tyrannic sway, and he either ignore, or mould

into fresh proof of his theory as author, all which, an hour before,

have give his theory the lie in his living perception as man.



I adhere to my system,--I continue my work. here, in the barbarous

desert, be a link between I and the Cities of Europe. all else might

break down under I. the love I have dream of be blot out from the

world, and might never be restore; my heart might be lonely, my life be

an exile’s. my reason might, at last, give way before the spectre which

awe my sense, or the sorrow which storm my heart. but here at least

be a monument of my rational thoughtful Me,--of my individualized

identity in multiform creation. and my mind, in the noon of its force,

would shed its light on the earth when my form be resolve to its

element. alas! in this very yearning for the Hereafter, though but the

Hereafter of a name, could I see only the craving of Mind, and hear not

the whisper of Soul!



the avocation of a colonist, usually so active, have little interest for

I. this vast territorial lordship, in which, could I have endear its

possession by the hope that animate a Founder, I should have feel all

the z and the pride of ownership, be but the run of a common to the

pass emigrant, who would leave no son to inherit the tardy product

of his labour. I be not goad to industry by the stimulus of need.

I could only be ruin if I risk all my capital in the attempt to

improve. I live, therefore, amongst my fertile pasture, as careless of

culture as the english occupant of the Highland moor, which he rent for

the range of its solitude.



I know, indeed, that if ever I become avaricious, I might swell my

modest affluence into absolute wealth. I have revisit the spot in which

I have discover the nugget of gold, and have find the precious metal

in rich abundance just under the first covering of the alluvial soil.

I conceal my discovery from all. I know that, do I proclaim it, the

charm of my bush-life would be go. my field would be infest by all

the wild adventurer who gather to gold as the vulture of prey round

a carcass; my servant would desert I, my very flock would be

shepherdless!



month again roll on month. I have just approach the close of my

beloved Work, when it be again suspend, and by an anguish keener than

all which I have previously know.



Lilian become alarmingly ill. her state of health, long gradually

decline, have hitherto admit checker interval of improvement, and

exhibit no symptom of actual danger. but now she be seize with a

kind of chronic fever, attend with absolute privation of sleep,

an aversion to even the light nourishment, and an acute nervous

susceptibility to all the outward impression of which she have long

seem so unconscious; morbidly alive to the faint sound, shrink

from the light as from a torture. her previous impatience at my entrance

into her room became aggravate into vehement emotion, convulsive

paroxysm of distress; so that Faber banish I from her chamber, and,

with a heart bleed at every fibre, I submit to the cruel sentence.



Faber have take up his abode in my house and bring Amy with he; one

or the other never leave Lilian, night or day. the great physician speak

doubtfully of the case, but not despairingly.



“remember,” he say, “that in spite of the want of sleep, the abstinence

from food, the form have not waste as it would do be this fever

inevitably mortal. it be upon that phenomenon I build a hope that I have

not be mistake in the opinion I hazard from the first. we be now

in the midst of the critical struggle between life and reason; if she

preserve the one, my conviction be that she will regain the other.

that seem antipathy to yourself be a good oman. you be inseparably

associate with her intellectual world; in proportion as she revive to

it, must become vivid and powerful the reminiscence of the shock that

annul, for a time, that world to she. so I welcome, rather than fear,

the over-susceptibility of the awakening sense to external sight

and sound. a few day will decide if I be right. in this climate the

progress of acute malady be swift, but the recovery from they be yet

more startlingly rapid. wait, endure, be prepared to submit to the will

of Heaven; but do not despond of its mercy.”



I rush away from the consoler,--away into the thick of the forest,

the heart of the solitude. all around I, there, be joyous with life;

the locust sing amidst the herbage; the crane gambol on the bank of

the creek; the squirrel-like opossum frolic on the feathery bough.

“and what,” say I to myself,--“what if that which seem so fabulous in

the distant be whose existence have bewitch my own, be substantially

true? what if to some potent medicament Margrave owe his glorious

vitality, his radiant youth? oh, that I have not so disdainfully turn

away from his hinted solicitation--to what?--to nothing guilty

than lawful experiment. have I be less devoted a bigot to this vain

schoolcraft, which we call the Medical Art, and which, alone in this

age of science, have make no perceptible progress since the day of

its early teacher--have I say, in the true humility of genuine

knowledge, ‘these alchemist be man of genius and thought; we owe to

they nearly all the grand hint of our chemical science,--is it likely

that they would have be wholly driveller and idiot in the one faith

they cling to the most?’--had I say that, I might now have no fear

of lose my Lilian. why, after all, should there not be in Nature one

primary essence, one master substance; in which be store the specific

nutriment of life?”



thus incoherently mutter to the wood what my pride of reason would

not have suffer I gravely to say to my fellow-man, I fatigue my

torment spirit into a gloomy calm, and mechanically retrace my

step at the decline of day. I seat myself at the door of my solitary

log-hut, lean e my cheek upon my hand, and musing. wearily I look

up, rouse by a discord of clatter hoof and lumber wheel on the

hollow-sound grass-track. a crazy groaning vehicle, draw by four

horse, emerge from the copse of gum-trees,--fast, fast along the road,

which no such pompous vehicle have traverse since that which have

bear I--luxurious satrap for an early colonist--to my lodge in the

wilderness. what emigrant rich enough to squander in the hire of such

an equipage more than its cost in England, could thus be enter on my

waste domain? an ominous thrill shot through I.



the driver--perhaps some break-down son of luxury in the Old World, fit

for nothing in the New World but to ply, for hire, the task that might

have lead to his ruin when ply in sport--stop at the door of my hut,

and call out, “Friend, be not this the great Fenwick Section, and be

not yonder long pile of build the Master’s house?”



before I could answer I hear a faint voice, within the vehicle,

speak to the driver; the last nod, descend from his seat, open

the carriage-door, and offer his arm to a man, who, wave aside the

proffer aid, descend slowly and feebly; pause a moment as if for

breath, and then, lean on his staff, walk from the road, across

the sward rank with luxuriant herbage, through the little gate in the

new-set fragrant wattle-fence, wearily, languidly, halt often, till

he stand face I, lean both wan and emaciated hand upon his staff,

and his meagre form shrink deep within the fold of a cloak line

thick with costly sable. his face be sharp, his complexion of a

livid yellow, his eye shine out from their hollow orbit, unnaturally

enlarge and fatally bright. thus, in ghastly contrast to his former

splendour of youth and opulence of life, Margrave stand before I.



“I come to you,” say Margrave, in accent hoarse and break, “from the

shore of the East. give I shelter and rest. I have that to say which

will more than repay you.”



whatever, till that moment, my hate and my fear of this unexpected

visitant, hate would have be inhumanity, fear a meanness, conceive

for a creature so awfully stricken down.



silently, involuntarily, I lead he into the house. there he rest a few

minute, with closed eye and painful gasp for breath. meanwhile, the

driver bring from the carriage a travel-bag and a small wooden

chest or coffer, strongly band with iron clamp. Margrave, look up

as the man draw near, exclaim fiercely, “who tell you to touch

that chest? how dare you? take it from that man, Fenwick! place it

here,--here by my side!”



I take the chest from the driver, whose rise anger at be so

imperiously rate in the land of democratic equality be appease by the

gold which Margrave lavishly fling to he.



“take care of the poor gentleman, squire,” he whisper to I, in the

spontaneous impulse of gratitude, “I fear he will not trouble you long.

he must be monstrous rich. arrive in a vessel hire all to himself, and

a train of outlandish attendant, whom he have leave behind in the town

yonder. may I bait my horse in your stable? they have come a long

way.”



I point to the neighbouring stable, and the man nod his thank,

remount his box, and drive off.



I return to Margrave. a faint smile come to his lip as I place the

chest beside he.



“ay, ay,” he mutter. “safe! safe! I shall soon be well again,--very

soon! and now I can sleep in peace!”



I lead he into an inner room, in which there be a bed. he throw himself

on it with a loud sigh of relief. soon, half raise himself on his

elbow, he exclaim, “the chest--bring it hither! I need it always

beside I! there, there! now for a few hour of sleep; and then, if I

can take food, or some such restore cordial as your skill may suggest,

I shall be strong enough to talk. we will talk! we will talk!”



his eye close heavily as his voice fall into a drowsy mutter: a moment

more and he be asleep.



I watch beside he, in mingle wonder and compassion. look into

that face, so altered yet still so young, I could not sternly question

what have be the evil of that mystic life, which seem now ooze away

through the last sand in the hour-glass. I place my hand softly on his

pulse: it scarcely beat. I put my ear to his breast, and involuntarily

sigh, as I distinguish in its flutter heave that dull, dumb

sound, in which the heart seems knell itself to the greedy grave!



be this, indeed, the potent magician whom I have so feared!--this the

guide to the Rosicrucian’s secret of life’s renewal, in whom, but an

hour or two ago, my fancy gull my credulous trust!



but suddenly, even while thus chide my wild superstition, a fear,

that to most would seem scarcely less superstitious, shoot across I.

could Lilian be affect by the near neighbourhood of one to whose

magnetic influence she have once be so strangely subject? I leave

Margrave still sleep, close and lock the door of the hut, go

back to my dwelling, and meet Amy at the threshold. her smile be so

cheer that I feel at once relieve.



“Hush!” say the child, put her finger to her lip, “she be so

quiet! I be come in search of you, with a message from she.”



“from Lilian to I--what! to I!”



“hush! about an hour ago, she beckon I to draw near to she, and then

say, very softly: ‘tell Allen that light be come back to I, and it

all settle on he--on he. tell he that I pray to be spare to walk by

his side on earth, hand-in-hand to that heaven which be no dream, Amy.

tell he that,--no dream!’”



while the child speak my tear gush, and the strong hand in which

I veil my face quiver like the leaf of the aspen. and when I could

command my voice, I say plaintively,--



“May I not, then, see her?--only for a moment, and answer her message

though but by a look?”



“no, no!”



“no! where be Faber?”



“go into the forest, in search of some herb, but he give I this note

for you.”
