I wipe the blind tear from my eye, and read these lines:--



“I have, though with hesitation, permit Amy to tell you the cheering

word, by which our beloved patient confirm my belief that reason

be come back to her,--slowly, labouringly, but if she survive, for

permanent restoration. on no account attempt to precipitate or disturb

the work of nature. as dangerous as a sudden glare of light to eye long

blind and newly regain vision in the friendly and soothe dark would

be the agitation that your presence at this crisis would cause. Confide

in I.”



I remained brood over these line and over Lilian’s message long and

silently, while Amy’s soothe whisper steal into my ear, soft as

the murmur of a rill hear in the gloom of forest. rouse myself at

length, my thought return to Margrave. Doubtless he would soon awake.

I bid Amy bring I such slight nutriment as I think well suit to

his enfeeble state, tell she it be for a sick traveller, rest

himself in my hut. when Amy return, I take from she the little basket

with which she be charge, and have, meanwhile, make a careful

selection from the content of my medicine-chest, go back to the

hut. I have not long resume my place beside Margrave’s pillow before he

awake.



“what o’clock be it?” he ask, with an anxious voice.



“about seven.”



“not later? that be well; my time be precious.”



“compose yourself, and eat.”



I place the food before he, and he partook of it, though sparingly,

and as if with effort. he then doze for a short time, again wake up,

and impatiently demand the cordial, which I have prepare in the mean

while. its effect be great and more immediate than I could have

anticipate, prove, perhaps, how much of youth there be still leave in

his system, however undermined and ravage by disease. colour come back

to his cheek, his voice grow perceptibly strong. and as I light the

lamp on the table near we--for it be grow dark--he gather himself

up, and speak thus,--



“you remember that I once press on you certain experiment. my object

then be to discover the material from which be extract the specific

that enable the organ of life to expel disease and regain vigour. in

that hope I seek your intimacy,--an intimacy you give, but withdraw.”



“dare you complain? who and what be the being from whose intimacy I

shrink appal?”



“ask what question you please,” cry Margrave, impatiently, “later--if

I have strength leave to answer they; but do not interrupt I, while

I husband my force to say what alone be important to I and to you.

disappoint in the hope I have place in you, I resolve to repair

to Paris,--that great furnace of all bold idea. I question learn

formalist; I listen to audacious empiric. the first, with all their

boasted knowledge, be too timid to concede my premise; the second,

with all their speculative daring, too knavish to let I trust to their

conclusion. I find but one man, a Sicilian, who comprehend the

secret that be call occult, and have the courage to meet Nature and

all her agency face to face. he believe, and sincerely, that he be

approach the grand result, at the very moment when he perish from

want of the common precaution which a tyro in chemistry would have

take. at his death the gaudy city become hateful; all its pretend

pleasure only serve to exhaust life the fast. the true joy of youth

be those of the wild bird and wild brute, in the healthful enjoyment

of Nature. in city, youth be but old age with a varnish. I flee to the

East; I pass through the tent of the Arabs; I be guide--no matter

by whom or by what--to the house of a Dervish, who have have for his

teacher the most erudite master of secret occult, whom I know year ago

at Aleppo---why that exclamation?”



“Proceed. what I have to say will come--later.”



“from this Dervish I half force and half purchase the secret I seek

to obtain. I now know from what peculiar substance the so-call elixir

of life be extract; I know also the step of the process through which

that task be accomplish. you smile incredulously. what be your doubt?

state it while I rest for a moment. my breath labour; give I more of

the cordial.”



“need I tell you my doubt? you have, you say, at your command the elixir

of life of which Cagliostro do not leave his disciple the recipe; and

you stretch out your hand for a vulgar cordial which any village chemist

could give you!”



“I can explain this apparent contradiction. the process by which the

elixir be extract from the material which hoard its essence be one

that require a hardihood of courage which few possess. this Dervish,

who have pass through that process once, be deaf to all prayer, and

unmove by all bribe, to attempt it again. he be poor; for the secret

by which metal may be transmute be not, as the old alchemist seem to

imply, identical with that by which the elixir of life be extract. he

have only be enable to discover, in the niggard strata of the land

within range of his travel, a few scanty morsel of the glorious

substance. from these he have extract scarcely enough of the elixir to

fill a third of that little glass which I have just drain. he guard

every drop for himself. who that hold healthful life as the one boon

above all price to the living, would waste upon other what prolong

and recruit his own being? therefore, though he sell I his secret, he

would not sell I his treasure.”



“any quack may sell you the information how to make not only an elixir,

but a sun and a moon, and then scare you from the experiment by tale

of the danger of try it! how do you know that this essence which the

Dervish possess be the elixir of life, since, it seem, you have not

try on yourself what effect its precious drop could produce? poor

wretch, who once seem to I so awfully potent! do you come to the

Antipodes in search of a drug that only exist in the fable by which a

child be amuse?”



“the elixir of life be no fable,” cry Margrave, with a kindling of

eye, a power of voice, a dilatation of form, that startle I in one

just before so feeble. “that elixir be bright in my vein when we last

meet. from that golden draught of the life-spring of joy I take all that

can gladden creation. what sage would not have exchange his wearisome

knowledge for my lusty revel with nature? what monarch would not have

barter his crown, with its brain-ache of care, for the radiance that

circle my brow, flash out from the light that be in I? oh again,

oh again! to enjoy the freedom of air with the bird, and the glow of the

sun with the lizard; to sport through the bloom of the earth, Nature’s

playmate and darling; to face, in the forest and desert, the pard and

the lion,--Nature’s brave and fiercest,--her firstborn, the heir of

her realm, with the rest of her child for slave!”



as these word burst from his lip, there be a wild grandeur in the

aspect of this enigmatical being which I have never behold in the former

time of his affluent, dazzle youth. and, indeed, in his language, and

in the thought it clothe, there be an earnestness, a concentration, a

directness, a purpose, which have seem want to his desultory talk

in the early day I expect that reaction of languor and exhaustion

would follow his vehement outbreak of passion, but, after a short pause,

he go on with steady accent. his will be sustain his strength. he

be determined to force his conviction on I, and the vitality, once so

rich, rally all its linger force to the aid of its intense desire.



“I tell you, then,” he resume, with deliberate calmness, “that, year

ago, I test in my own person that essence which be the sovereign

medicament. in I, as you see I at L----, you behold the proof of its

virtue. feeble and ill as I be now, my state be incalculably more

hopeless when formerly restore by the elixir. he from whom I then

take the sublime restorative die without reveal the secret of its

composition. what I obtain be only just sufficient to recruit the

lamp of my life, then die down--and no drop be leave for renew the

light which waste its own ray in the air that it gild. though the

Dervish would not sell I his treasure, he permit I to see it.

the appearance and odour of this essence be strangely

peculiar,--unmistakable by one who have once behold and partake of

it. in short, I recognize in the hand of the Dervish the bright

life-renewer, as I have bear it away from the corpse of the Sage of

Aleppo.”



“hold! be you then, in truth, the murderer of Haroun, and be your true

name Louis Grayle?”



“I be no murderer, and Louis Grayle do not leave I his name. I again

adjure you to postpone, for this night at least, the question you wish

to address to I.



“see that this obstinate pauper possess that for which the pale

owner of million, at the first touch of palsy or gout, would consent

to be pauper, of course I covet the possession of the essence even

more than the knowledge of the substance from which it be extract. I

have no coward fear of the experiment, which this timid driveller have not

the nerve to renew. but still the experiment might fail. I must traverse

land and sea to find the fit place for it, while, in the rag of the

Dervish, the unfailing result of the experiment be at hand. the Dervish

suspect my design, he dread my power. he flee on the very night

in which I have mean to seize what he refuse to sell I. after all, I

should have do he no great wrong; for I should have leave he wealth

enough to transport himself to any soil in which the material for the

elixir may be most abundant; and the desire of life would have give

his shrink nerve the courage to replenish its ravished store. I

have Arabs in my pay, who obey I as hound their master. I chase the

fugitive. I come on his track, reach a house in a miserable village,

in which, I be tell, he have enter but an hour before. the day be

decline, the light in the room imperfect. I see in a corner what

seem to I the form of the Dervish,--stooped to seize it, and my hand

close on an asp. the artful Dervish have so pile his rag that they

take the shape of the form they have clothe, and he have leave, as a

substitute for the giver of life, the venomous reptile of death.



“the strength of my system enable I to survive the effect of the

poison; but during the torpor that numb I, my Arabs, alarmed, give no

chase to my quarry. at last, though enfeeble and languid, I be again

on my horse. again the pursuit, again the track! I learn--but this

time by a knowledge surer than man’s--that the Dervish have take his

refuge in a hamlet that have spring up over the site of a city once fame

through Assyria. the same voice that inform I of his whereabouts

warn I not to pursue. I reject the warning. in my eager impatience

I spring on to the chase; in my fearless resolve I feel sure of the

prey. I arrive at the hamlet weary out, for my force be no long

the same since the bite of the asp. the Dervish elude I still; he have

leave the floor, on which I sink exhaust, but a few minute before my

horse stop at the door. the carpet, on which he have rest, still

lie on the ground. I dismiss the young and keenest of my troop in

search of the fugitive. sure that this time he would not escape, my eye

close in sleep.



“how long I sleep I know not,--a long dream of solitude, fever, and

anguish. be it the curse of the Dervish’s carpet? be it a taint in

the wall of the house, or of the air, which brood sickly and rank over

place where city lie bury? I know not; but the Pest of the East

have seize I in slumber. when my sense recover I find myself alone,

plunder of my arm, despoiled of such gold as I have carry about

I. all have desert and leave I, as the living leave the dead whom the

Plague have claim for its own. as soon as I could stand I crawl from

the threshold. the moment my voice be hear, my face see, the whole

squalid populace rise as on a wild beast,--a mad dog. I be drive from

the place with imprecation and stone, as a miscreant whom the Plague

have overtake while plot the death of a holy man. bruised and

bleeding, but still defy, I turn in wrath on that dastardly rabble;

they slunk away from my path. I know the land for mile around. I have

be in that land year, long year ago. I come at last to the road

which the caravans take on their way to Damascus. there I be find,

speechless and seemingly lifeless, by some european traveller. convey

to Damascus, I languish for week between life and death. but for the

virtue of that essence, which linger yet in my vein, I could not

have survive--even thus feeble and shattered. I need not say that I

now abandon all thought of discover the Dervish. I have at least his

secret, if I have fail of the paltry supply he have draw from its use.

such appliance as he have tell I be needful be procure in the East

with more ease than in Europe. to sum up, I be here, instruct in

all the knowledge, and supply with all the aid, which warrant I in

say, ‘do you care for new life in its rich enjoyment, if not for

yourself, for one whom you love and would reprieve from the grave? then,

share with I in a task that a single night will accomplish, and ravish

a prize by which the life that you value the most will be save from

the dust and the worm, to live on, ever young, ever bloom, when each

infant, new-bear while I speak, shall have pass to the grave. Nay,

where be the limit to life, while the earth hide the substance by which

life be renew?”



I give as faithfully as I can recall they the word in which Margrave

address I. but who can guess by cold word transcribe, even be

they artfully range by a master of language, the effect word produce

when warm from the breath of the speaker? ask one of an audience which

some orator hold enthral, why his word do not quicken a beat in the

reader’s pulse, and the answer of one who have listen will be, “the

word take their charm from the voice and the eye, the aspect, the

manner, the man!” so it be with the incomprehensible be before I.

though his youth be fade, though his beauty be dim, though my

fancy clothe he with memory of abhorrent dread, though my reason

oppose his audacious belief and assumption, still he charm and

spell-bind I; still he be the mystical fascinator; still, if

the legend of magic have truth for their basis, he be the bear

magician,--as genius, in what call soever, be bear with the gift to

enchant and subdue we.



constrain myself to answer calmly, I say, “you have tell I your

story; you have define the object of the experiment in which you ask I

to aid. you do right to bid I postpone my reply or my question. seek

to recruit by sleep the strength you have so sorely tasked. to-morrow--”



“to-morrow, ere night, you will decide whether the man whom out of

all earth I have select to aid I shall be the foe to condemn I to

perish! I tell you plainly I need your aid, and your prompt aid. three

day from this, and all aid will be too late!”



I have already gain the door of the room, when he call to I to come

back.



“you do not live in this but, but with your family yonder. do not tell

they that I be here; let no one but yourself see I as I now be. lock

the door of the hut when you quit it. I should not close my eye if I

be not secure from intruder.”



“there be but one in my house, or in these part, whom I would except

from the interdict you impose. you be aware of your own imminent

danger; the life, which you believe the discovery of a Dervish will

indefinitely prolong, seem to my eye of physician to hang on a thread.

I have already form my own conjecture as to the nature of the disease

that enfeeble you. but I would fain compare that conjecture with the

weightier opinion of one whose experience and skill be superior to

mine. permit I, then, when I return to you to-morrow, to bring with

I the great physician to whom I refer. his name will not, perhaps, be

unknown to you: I speak of Julius Faber.”



“a physician of the school! I can guess well enough how learnedly he

would prate, and how little he could do. but I will not object to his

visit, if it satisfy you that, since I should die under the hand of

the doctor, I may be permit to indulge my own whim in place my

hope in a Dervish. yet stay. you have, doubtless, speak of I to this

Julius Faber, your fellow-physician and friend? promise I, if you bring

he here, that you will not name me,--that you will not repeat to he

the tale I have tell you, or the hope which have lead I to these shore.

what I have tell you, no matter whether, at this moment, you consider I

the dupe of a chimera, be still under the seal of the confidence which a

patient repose in the physician he himself select for his confidant. I

select you, and not Julius Faber!”



“be it as you will,” say I, after a moment’s reflection. “the moment

you make yourself my patient, I be bind to consider what be good for

you. and you may more respect, and profit by, an opinion base upon your

purely physical condition than by one in which you might suppose the

advice be direct rather to the disease of the mind than to that of

the body.”



“how amazed and indignant your brother-physician will be if he ever see

I a second time! how learnedly he will prove that, accord to all

correct principle of science and nature, I ought to be dead!”



he utter this jest with a faint weary echo of his old merry, melodious

laugh, then turn his face to the wall; and so I leave he to repose.













CHAPTER LXXV.



I find Mrs. Ashleigh wait for I in our usual sitting-room. she

be in tear. she have begin to despond of Lilian’s recovery, and she

infect I with her own alarm. however, I disguise my participation in

her fear, soothe and sustain she as I good could, and persuade she

to retire to rest. I see Faber for a few minute before I seek my

own chamber. he assure I that there be no perceptible change for the

bad in Lilian’s physical state since he have last see I, and that her

mind, even within the last few hour, have become decidedly more clear.

he think that, within the next twenty-four hour, the reason would

make a strong and successful effort for complete recovery; but he

decline to hazard more than a hope that the effort would not exhaust

the enfeeble power of the frame. he himself be so in need of a few

hour of rest that I cease to harass he with question which he could

not answer, and fear which he could not appease. before leave he

for the night, I tell he briefly that there be a traveller in my but

smite by a disease which seem to I so grave that I would ask

his opinion of the case, if he could accompany I to the hut the next

morning.



my own thought that night be not such as would suffer I to sleep.



before Margrave’s melancholy state much of my former fear and abhorrence

fade away. this being, so exceptional that fancy might well invest he

with preternatural attribute, be now reduce by human suffering to

human sympathy and comprehension; yet his utter want of conscience be

still as apparent as in his day of joyous animal spirit. with what

hideous candour he have relate his perfidy and ingratitude to the man

to whom, in his belief, he owe an inestimable obligation, and with what

insensibility to the signal retribution which in most nature would have

awaken remorse!



and by what dark hint and confession do he seem to confirm the

incredible memoir of Sir Philip Derval! he own that he have bear from

the corpse of Haroun the medicament to which he ascribe his recovery

from a state yet more hopeless than that under which he now labour! he

have allude, rapidly, obscurely, to some knowledge at his command

“surer than man’s.” and now, even now the mere wreck of his former

existence--by what strange charm do he still control and confuse my

reason? and how be it that I feel myself murmur, again and again,

“but what, after all, if his hope be no chimera, and if Nature do hide a

secret by which I could save the life of my beloved Lilian?”



and again and again, as that thought would force itself on I, I rise

and creep to Lilian’s threshold, listen to catch the faint sound

of her breathing. all still, all dark! in that sufferer recognize

science detect no mortal disease, yet dares not bid I rely on its

ample resource of skill to turn aside from her slumber the stealthy

advance of death; while in yon log-hut one whose malady recognize

science could not doubt to be mortal have compose himself to sleep,

confident of life! recognize science?--recognized ignorance! the

science of to-day be the ignorance of to-morrow! every year some bold

guess light up a truth to which, but the year before, the schoolman of

science be as blind as mole.



“what, then,” my lip keep repeating,--“what if Nature do hide a secret

by which the life of my life can be save? what do we know of the

secret of Nature? what say Newton himself of his knowledge? ‘I be like

a child pick up pebble and shell on the sand, while the great ocean

of Truth lie all undiscovered around I!’ and do Newton himself, in

the ripe growth of his matchless intellect, hold the creed of the

alchemist in scorn? have he not give to one object of their research,

in the transmutation of metal, his day and his night? be there proof

that he ever convince himself that the research be the dream, which

we, who be not newton, call it?(1) and that other great sage, inferior

only to Newton--the calculate doubt-weigher, Descartes--have he not

believe in the yet nobler hope of the alchemists,--believed in some

occult nostrum or process by which human life could attain to the age of

the Patriarchs?” (2)



in thought like these the night wear away, the moonbeam that stream

through my window lighting up the spacious solitude beyond,--mead and

creek, forest-land, mountaintop,--and the silence without break by

the wild cry of the night hawk and the sibilant melancholy dirge of

the shine chrysococyx,(3)--bird that never sing but at night, and

obstinately haunt the roof of the sick and die, ominous of woe and

death.



but up spring the sun, and, chase these gloomy sound, out burst the

wonderful chorus of australian grove, the great kingfisher open the

jocund melodious babble with the glee of his social laugh.



and now I hear Faber’s step in Lilian’s room,--heard through the door

her soft voice, though I could not distinguish the word. it be not

long before I see the kind physician stand at the threshold of my

chamber. he press his finger to his lip, and make I a sign to follow

he. I obey, with noiseless tread and stifle breathing. he await I

in the garden under the flower acacias, pass his arm in mine, and

draw I into the open pasture-land.



“compose yourself,” he then say; “I bring you tiding both of gladness

and of fear. your Lilian’s mind be restore: even the memory which

have be sweep away by the fever that follow her return to her home in

L---- be return, though as yet indistinct. she yearn to see you, to

bless you for all your noble devotion, your generous, greathearted love;

but I forbid such interview now. if, in a few hour, she become either

decidedly strong or decidedly more enfeeble, you shall be summon

to her side. even if you be condemn to a loss for which the sole

consolation must be place in the life hereafter, you shall have, at

least, the last mortal commune of soul with soul. courage! courage!

you be man! bear as man what you have so often bid other man submit to

endure.”



I have fling myself on the ground,--writhing worm that have no home but

on earth! Man, indeed! man! all, at that moment, I take from manhood be

its acute sensibility to love and to anguish!



but after all such paroxysm of mortal pain, there come a strange

lull. thought itself halt, like the still hush of water between two

descend torrent. I rise in a calm, which Faber might well mistake

for fortitude.



“well,” I say quietly, “fulfil your promise. if Lilian be to pass away

from I, I shall see she, at least, again; no wall, you tell I, between

our mind; mind to mind once more,--once more!”



“Allen,” say Faber, mournfully and softly, “why do you shun to repeat

my word--soul to soul?”



“ay, ay,--I understand. those word mean that you have resign all hope

that Lilian’s life will linger here, when her mind come back in full

consciousness; I know well that last lightning flash and the darkness

which swallow it up!”



“you exaggerate my fear. I have not resign the hope that Lilian will

survive the struggle through which she be pass, but it will be cruel

to deceive you--my hope be weak than it be.”



“ay, ay. again, I understand! your science be in fault,--it despond.

its last trust be in the wonderful resource of Nature, the vitality

store in the young!”



“you have said,--those resource of Nature be wondrous. the vitality

of youth be a fountain spring up from the deep out of sight, when, a

moment before, we have measure the drop ooze out from the sand, and

think that the well be exhaust.”



“come with me,--come. I tell you of another sufferer yonder. I want your

opinion of his case. but can you be spare a few minute from Lilian’s

side?”



“yes; I leave her asleep. what be the case that perplex your eye of

physician, which be usually keener than mine, despite all the length of

my practice?”



“the sufferer be young, his organization rare in its vigour. he have go

through and survive assault upon life that be commonly fatal. his

system have be poison by the fang of a venomous asp, and shatter

by the blast of the plague. these alone, I believe, would not suffice

to destroy he. but he be one who have a strong dread of death; and while

the heart be thus languid and feeble, it have be gnaw by emotion

of hope or of fear. I suspect that he be die, not from the bite of the

reptile, not from the taint of the pestilence, but from the hope and the

fear that have overtaske the heart’s function. Judge for yourself.”



we be now at the door of the hut. I unlock it: we enter. Margrave

have quit his bed, and be pace the room slowly. his step be less

feeble, his countenance less haggard than on the previous evening.



he submit himself to Faber’s questioning with a quiet indifference,

and evidently care nothing for any opinion which the great physician

might find on his reply.



when Faber have learn all he could, he say, with a grave smile: “I see

that my advice will have little weight with you; such as it be, at least

reflect on it. the conclusion to which your host arrive in his view

of your case, and which he confide to I, be, in my humble judgment,

correct. I have no doubt that the great organ of the heart be

involve in the cause of your suffering; but the heart be a noble and

much-endure organ. I have know man in whom it have be more severely

and unequivocally affect with disease than it be in you, live on for

many year, and ultimately die of some other disorder. but then life be

hold, as yours must be hold, upon one condition,--repose. I enjoin you

to abstain from all violent action, to shun all excitement that cause

moral disturbance. you be young: would you live on, you must live as

the old. More than this,--it be my duty to warn you that your tenure

on earth be very precarious; you may attain to many year; you may be

suddenly call hence tomorrow. the good mode to regard this uncertainty

with the calm in which be your only chance of long life, be so to

arrange all your worldly affair, and so to discipline all your human

anxiety, as to feel always prepared for the summon that may come

without warn. for the rest, quit this climate as soon as you can,--it

be the climate in which the blood course too quickly for one who should

shun all excitement. seek the most equable atmosphere, choose the most

tranquil pursuit; and Fenwick himself, in his magnificent pride of

stature and strength, may be near the grave than you be.”



“your opinion coincide with that I have just hear?” ask Margrave,

turn to I.



“in much--yes.”



“it be more favourable than I should have suppose. I be far from

disdain the advice so kindly offer. permit I, in turn, two or

three question, Dr. Faber. do you prescribe to I no drug from your

pharmacopoeia?”



“drug may palliate many suffering incidental to organic disease, but

drug cannot reach organic disease itself.”



“do you believe that, even where disease be plainly organic, nature

herself have no alternative and reparative power, by which the organ

assail may recover itself?”



“a few exceptional instance of such force in Nature be upon record;

but we must go by general law, and not by exception.”



“have you never know instance--do you not at this moment know one--in

which a patient whose malady baffle the doctor’s skill, imago or

dream of a remedy? call it a whim if you please, learn sir; do you

not listen to the whim, and, in despair of your own prescription,

comply with those of the patient?”



Faber change countenance, and even start. Margrave watch he and

laugh.



“you grant that there be such case, in which the patient give the law

to the physician. now, apply your experience to my case. suppose some

strange fancy have seize upon my imagination--that be the doctor’s cannot

word for all phenomenon which we call exceptional--some strange fancy

that I have think of a cure for this disease for which you have no

drug; and suppose this fancy of mine to be so strong, so vivid, that to

deny I its gratification would produce the very emotion from which you

warn I as fatal,--storm the heart, that you would soothe to repose, by

the passion of rage and despair,--would you, as my trust physician,

concede or deny I my whim?”



“can you ask? I should grant it at once, if I have no reason to know that

the thing that you fancy be harmful.”



“good man and wise doctor! I have no other question to ask. I thank

you.”



Faber look hard on the young, wan face, over which play a smile

of triumph and irony; then turn away with an expression of doubt and

trouble on his own noble countenance. I follow he silently into the

open air.



“who and what be this visitor of your?” he ask abruptly.



“who and what? I cannot tell you.”



Faber remain some moment musing, and mutter slowly to himself,

“Tut! but a chance coincidence,--a haphazard allusion to a fact which he

could not have know!”



“Faber,” say I, abruptly, “can it be that Lilian be the patient in

whose self-suggest remedy you confide more than in the various

learning at command of your practised skill?”



“I cannot deny it,” reply Faber, reluctantly. “in the interval of

that suspense from wake sense, which in she be not sleep, nor yet

altogether catalepsy, she have, for the last few day, state accurately

the precise moment in which the trance--if I may so call it--would

pass away, and prescribe for herself the remedy that should be then

administer. in every instance, the remedy so self-prescribe, though

certainly not those which would have occur to my mind, have prove

efficacious. her rapid progress to reason I ascribe to the treatment

she herself ordain in her trance, without remembrance of her own

suggestion when she awake. I have mean to defer communicate these

phenomenon in the idiosyncrasy of her case until our mind could more

calmly inquire into the process by which idea--not apparently derive,

as your metaphysical school would derive all idea, from preconceived

experience--will thus sometimes act like an instinct on the human

sufferer for self-preservation, as the bird be direct to the herb

or the berry which heal or assuage its ailment. we know how the

mesmerist would account for this phenomenon of hygienic introvision and

clairvoyance. but here, there be no mesmerizer, unless the patient can

be suppose to mesmerize herself. long, however, before mesmerism be

hear of, medical history attest example in which patient who baffle

the skill of the able physician have fix their fancy on some

remedy that physician would call inoperative for good or for harm,

and have recover by the remedy thus singularly self-suggest. and

Hippocrates himself, if I construe his meaning rightly, recognize the

power for self-cure which the condition of trance will sometimes bestow

on the sufferer, ‘where’ (say the father of our art) ‘the sight

be close to the external, the soul more truthfully perceive the

affection of the body.’ in short--I own it--in this instance, the skill

of the physician have be a compliant obedience to the instinct call

forth in the patient; and the hope I have hitherto permit myself to

give you be found on my experience that her own hope, conceive

in trance, have never be fallacious or exaggerated. the simple that

I gather for she yesterday she have describe; they be not in our

herbal. but as they be sometimes use by the native, I have the

curiosity to analyze their chemical property shortly after I come to

the colony, and they seem to I as innocent as lime-blossom. they

be rare in this part of Australia, but she tell I where I should find

them,--a remote spot, which she have certainly never visit. last night,

when you see I disturb, deject, it be because, for the first time,

the docility with which she have hitherto, in her wake state, obey

her own injunction in the state of trance, forsook she. she could not

be induce to taste the decoction I have make from the herb; and if you

find I this morning with weak hope than before, this be the real

cause,--namely, that when I visit she at sunrise, she be not in sleep

but in trance, and in that trance she tell I that she have nothing more

to suggest or reveal; that on the complete restoration of her sense,

which be at hand, the abnormal faculty vouchsafe to trance would be

withdraw. ‘as for my life,’ she say quietly, as if unconscious of our

temporary joy or woe in the term of its tenure here,--‘as for my life,

your aid be now idle; my own vision obscure; on my life a dark and cold

shadow be rest. I cannot foresee if it will pass away. when I strive

to look around, I see but my Allen--’”



“and so,” say I, master my emotion, “in bid I hope, you do

not rely on your own resource of science, but on the whisper of Nature

in the brain of your patient?”



“it be so.”



we both remain silent some moment, and then, as he disappear within

my house, I murmured,--



“and when she strive to look beyond the shadow, she see only I! be

there some prophet-hint of Nature there also, direct I not to scorn

the secret which a wanderer, so suddenly drop on my solitude, assure

I that Nature will sometimes reveal to her seeker? and oh! that dark

wanderer--have Nature a marvel more weird than himself?”



(1) “besides the three great subject of Newton’s labour--the fluxional

calculus, physical astronomy, and optic--a very large portion of his

time, while resident in his college, be devoted to research of which

scarcely a trace remain. alchemy, which have fascinate so many eager

and ambitious mind, seem to have tempt Newton with an overwhelming

force. what theory he form, what experiment he try, in that

laboratory where, it be say, the fire be scarcely extinguish for

week together, will never be know. it be certain that no success

attend his labour; and Newton be not a man--like Kepler--to detail

to the world all the hope and disappointment, all the crude and

mystical fancy, which mix themselves up with his career of

philosophy... many year later we find Newton in correspondence with

Locke, with reference to a mysterious red earth by which Boyle, who

be then recently dead, have assert that he could effect the grand

desideratum of multiply gold. by this time, however, Newton’s faith

have become somewhat shake by the unsatisfactory communication which

he have himself receive from Boyle on the subject of the golden recipe,

though he do not abandon the idea of give the experiment a further

trial as soon as the weather should become suitable for furnace

experiment.”--Quarterly Review, No. 220, pp. 125, 126.



(2) Southey, in his “doctor,” vol. vi. p. 2, report the conversation of

Sir Kenelm Digby with Descartes, in which the great geometrician say,

“that as for render man immortal, it be what he could not venture

to promise, but that he be very sure he could prolong his life to the

standard of the patriarch.” and Southey add, “that St. Evremond, to

whom Digby repeat this, say that this opinion of Descartes be well

know both to his friend in Holland and in France.” by the stress

Southey lay on this hearsay evidence, it be clear that he be not

acquaint with the work and biography of Descartes, or he would have

go to the fountain-head for authority on Descartes’s opinion, namely,

descarte himself. it be to be wish that Southey have do so, for

no one more than he would have appreciate the exquisitely candid and

lovable nature of the illustrious Frenchman, and the sincerity with

which he cherish in his heart whatever doctrine he conceive in his

understanding. Descartes, whose knowledge of anatomy be considerable,

have that passion for the art of medicine which be almost inseparable

from the pursuit of natural philosophy. at the age of twenty-four he

have seek (in Germany) to obtain initiation into the brotherhood of the

Rosicrucians, but unluckily could not discover any member of the society

to introduce he. “he desire,” say Cousin, “to assure the health of

man, diminish his ill, extend his existence. he be terrify by the

rapid and almost momentary passage of man upon earth. he believe it

be not, perhaps, impossible to prolong its duration.” there be a hidden

recess of grandeur in this idea, and the mean propose by Descartes for

the execution of his project be not less grand. in his “discourse on

Method,” Descartes say, “if it be possible to find some mean to render

generally man more wise and more able than they have be till now, it

be, I believe, in medicine that those mean must be seek... I be sure

that there be no one, even in the medical profession, who will not

avow that all which one know of the medical art be almost nothing

in comparison to that which remain to learn, and that one could be

exempt from an infinity of malady, both of body and mind, and even,

perhaps, from the decrepitude of old age, if one have sufficient lore

of their cause and of all the remedy which nature provide for they.

therefore, have design to employ all my life in the research of a

science so necessary, and having discover a path which appear to I

such that one ought infallibly, in follow, to find it, if one be

not hinder prematurely by the brevity of life or by the defect of

experience, I consider that there be no well remedy against those two

hindrance than to communicate faithfully to the public the little

I have find,” etc. (“Discours de la Methode,” vol. i. OEuvres de

Descartes, Cousin’s Edition.) and again, in his “Correspondence” (vol.

ix. p. 341), he say: “the conservation of health have be always the

principal object of my study, and I have no doubt that there be a

mean of acquire much knowledge touching medicine which, up to this

time, be ignore.” he then refer to his meditated Treatise on animal

as only an entrance upon that knowledge. but whatever secret Descartes

may have think to discover, they be not make known to the public

accord to his promise. and in a letter to M. Chanut, write in 1646

(four year before he die), he say ingenuously: “I will tell you in

confidence that the notion, such as it be, which I have endeavour to

acquire in physical philosophy, have greatly assist I to establish

certain foundation for moral philosophy; and that I be more easily

satisfied upon this point than I be on many other touch medicine, to

which I have, nevertheless, devote much more time. so that”--(adds the

grand thinker, with a pathetic nobleness )--“so that, instead of find

the mean to preserve life, I have find another good, more easy and

more sure, which be--not to fear death.”



(3) Chrysococyx lucidus,--namely, the bird popularly call the shine

or bronzed cuckoo. “its note be an exceedingly melancholy whistle, hear

at night, when it be very annoying to any sick or nervous person who

may be incline to sleep. I have know many instance where the bird

have be perch on a tree in the vicinity of the room of an invalid,

utter its mournful note, and it be only with the great

difficulty that it could be dislodge from its position.”--Dr. Bennett:

gathering of a Naturalist in Australasia.













CHAPTER LXXVI.



I stray through the forest till noon, in debate with myself, and

strove to shape my wild doubt into purpose, before I could nerve and

compose myself again to face Margrave alone.



I re-enter the hut. to my surprise, Margrave be not in the room in

which I have leave he, nor in that which adjoin it. I ascend the

stair to the kind of loft in which I have be accustom to pursue my

study, but in which I have not set foot since my alarm for Lilian have

suspend my labour. there I see Margrave quietly seat before the

manuscript of my Ambitious Work, which lie open on the rude table, just

as I have leave it, in the midst of its concluding summary.



“I have take the license of former day, you see,” say Margrave,

smile, “and have hit by chance on a passage I can understand without

effort. but why such a waste of argument to prove a fact so simple? in

man, as in brute, life once lose be lose forever; and that be why life

be so precious to man.”



I take the book from his hand, and fling it aside in wrath. his approval

revolt I more with my own theory than all the argumentative rebuke

of Faber.



“and now,” I say, sternly, “the time have come for the explanation

you promise. before I can aid you in any experiment that may serve to

prolong your life, I must know how far that life have be a baleful and

destroy influence?”



“I have some faint recollection of having save your life from an

imminent danger, and if gratitude be the attribute of man, as it be of

the dog, I should claim your aid to serve mine as a right. ask I what

you will. you must have see enough of I to know that I do not affect

either the virtue or vice of other. I regard both with so supreme an

indifference, that I believe I be vicious or virtuous unaware. I know

not if I can explain what seem to have perplex you, but if I cannot

explain I have no intention to lie. speak--I listen! we have time enough

now before we.”



so say, he recline back in the chair, stretch out his limb

wearily. all round this spoilt darling of Material Nature be the

aid and appliance of intellectual Science,--books and telescope

and crucible, with the light of day come through a small circular

aperture in the board casement, as I have construct the opening for

my experimental observation of the prismal ray.



while I write, his image be as visible before my remembrance as if

before the actual eye,--beautiful even in its decay, awful even in its

weakness, mysterious as be Nature herself amidst all the mechanism by

which our fancied knowledge attempt to measure her law and analyze she

light.



but at that moment no such subtle reflection delay my inquisitive

eager mind from its immediate purpose,--who and what be this creature

boasting of a secret through which I might rescue from death the life of

she who be my all upon the earth?



I gather rapidly and succinctly together all that I know and all that

I guess of Margrave’s existence and art. I commence from my vision

in that mimic Golgotha of creature inferior to man, close by the scene

of man’s most trivial and meaningless pastime. I go on,--Derval’s

murder; the miss content of the casket; the apparition see by the

maniac assassin guide he to the horrid deed; the luminous haunting

shadow; the positive charge in the murder man’s memoir connect

Margrave with Louis Grayle, and accuse he of the murder of Haroun;

the night in the moonlit pavilion at Derval Court; the baneful influence

on Lilian; the struggle between I and himself in the house by the

seashore,--the strange all that be tell in this Strange Story.



but warm as I speak, and in a kind of fierce joy to be enable thus

to free my own heart of the doubt that have burden it, now that I

be fairly face to face with the being by whom my reason have be so

perplexed and my life so torture. I be restrain by none of the fear

lest my own fancy deceive I, with which in his absence I have strive

to reduce to natural cause the portent of terror and wonder. I state

plainly, directly, the belief, the impression which I have never dare

even to myself to own without seek to explain they away. and come

at last to a close, I say: “such be the evidence that seem to I to

justify abhorrence of the life that you ask I to aid in prolong.

your own tale of last night but confirm they. and why to I--to I--do

you come with wild entreaty to lengthen the life that have blight

my own? how do you even learn the home in which I seek unavaile

refuge? how--as your hint to Faber clearly reveal--be you aware

that, in yon house, where the sorrow be veil, where the groan be

suppress, where the foot-tread fall ghostlike, there struggle now

between life and death my heart’s twin, my world’s sunshine? ah!

through my terror for she, be it a demon that tell you how to bribe my

abhorrence into submission, and supple my reason into use to your end?”



Margrave have listen to I throughout with a fix attention, at time

with a bewildered stare, at time with exclamation of surprise, but

not of denial. and when I have do, he remain for some moment silent,

seemingly stupefy, pass his hand repeatedly over his brow, in the

gesture so familiar to he in former day.



at length he say quietly, without evince any sign either of

resentment or humiliation,--



“in much that you tell I I recognize myself; in much I be as lose in

amazement as you in wild doubt or fierce wrath. of the effect that you

say Philip Derval produce on I I have no recollection. of himself I

have only this,--that he be my foe, that he come to England intent on

scheme to shorten my life or destroy its enjoyment. all my faculty

tend to self-preservation; there, they converge as ray in a focus; in

that focus they illume and--they burn. I will to destroy my intended

destroyer. do my will enforce itself on the agent to which it be

guide? likely enough. be it so. would you blame I for slay the

tiger or serpent--not by the naked hand, but by weapon that arm it? but

what could tiger and serpent do more against I than the man who

would rob I of life? he have his art for assault, I have mine for

self-defence. he be to I as the tiger that creep through the jungle,

or the serpent uncoil his fold for the spring. death to those whose

life be destruction to mine, be they serpent or tiger or man! Derval

perish. yes! the spot in which the maniac have bury the casket be

reveal to I--no matter how; the content of the casket pass into

my hand. I covet that possession because I believe that Derval have

learn from Haroun of Aleppo the secret by which the elixir of life be

prepare, and I suppose that some store of the essence would be find

in his casket. I be deceive--not a drop! what I there find I know

not how to use or apply, nor do I care to learn. what I seek be not

there. you see a luminous shadow of myself; it haunt, it accost, it

compel you. of this I know nothing. be it the emanation of my intense

will really produce this spectre of myself, or be it the thing

of your own imagination,--an imagination which my will impressed and

subjugate? I know not. at the hour when my shadow, real or suppose,

be with you, my sense would have be lock in sleep. it be true,

however, that I intensely desire to learn from race always near to

man, but conceal from his every-day vision, the secret that I believe

Philip Derval have carry with he to the tomb; and from some cause

or another I cannot now of myself alone, as I could year ago, subject

those race to my command,--I must, in that, act through or with the

mind of another. it be true that I seek to impress upon your wake

thought the image of the circle, the power of the wand, which, in

your trance or sleep-walking, make you the involuntary agent of my will.

I know by a dream--for by dream, more or less vivid, be the result

of my waking will sometimes divulge to myself--that the spell have be

break, the discovery I sought not effect. all my hope be then

transfer from yourself, the dull votary of science, to the girl whom

I charm to my thraldom through her love for you and through her

dream of a realm which the science of school never enter. in she,

imagination be all pure and all potent; and tell I, o practical

reasoner, if reason have ever advance one step into knowledge except

through that imaginative faculty which be strong in the wisdom of

ignorance, and weak in the ignorance of the wise. ponder this, and

those marvel that perplex you will cease to be marvellous. I pass on

to the riddle that puzzle you most. by Philip Derval’s account I be,

in truth, Louis Grayle restore to youth by the elixir, and while yet

infirm, decrepit, murder Haroun,--a man of a frame as athletic as

your! by accept this notion you seem to yourself alone to unravel

the mystery you ascribe to my life and my power. o wise philosopher!

o profound logician! you accept that notion, yet hold my belief in the

Dervish’s tale a chimera! I be Grayle make young by the elixir, and yet

the elixir itself be a fable!”



he pause and laugh, but the laugh be no long even an echo of

its former merriment or playfulness,--a sinister and terrible laugh,

mocking, threatening, malignant.



again he sweep his hand over his brow, and resumed,--



“be it not easy to so accomplish a sage as you to believe that the

idler of Paris have guess the true solution of that problem, my

place on this earth? may I not be the love-son of Louis Grayle? and when

Haroun refuse the elixir to he, or he find that his frame be too far

exhausted for even the elixir to repair organic lesion of structure in

the worn frame of old age, may he not have indulge the common illusion

of father, and soothe his death-pang with the thought that he should

live again in his son? Haroun be find dead on his carpet--rumour say

strangle. what proof of the truth of that rumour? might he not

have pass away in a fit? will it lessen your perplexity if I state

recollection? they be vague,--they often perplex myself; but so far

from a wish to deceive you, my desire be to relate they so truthfully

that you may aid I to reduce they into more definite form.”



his face now become very troubled, the tone of his voice very

irresolute,--the face and the voice of a man who be either

blunder his way through an intricate falsehood, or through obscure

reminiscence.



“this Louis Grayle! this Louis Grayle! I remember he well, as one

remember a nightmare. whenever I look back, before the illness of which

I will presently speak, the image of Louis Grayle return to I. I see

myself with he in african wild, command the fierce abyssinian.

I see myself with he in the fair Persian valley,--lofty, snow-cover

mountain encircle the garden of rose. I see myself with he in the

hush of the golden noon, recline by the spray of cool fountains,--now

listen to cymbal and lute, now argue with graybeard on secret

bequeath by the Chaldees,--with he, with he in moonlit night,

steal into the sepulchre of mythical king. I see myself with he

in the aisle of dark cavern, surround by awful shape, which have no

likeness amongst the creature of earth. Louis Grayle! Louis Grayle! all

my early memory go back to Louis Grayle! all my art and power, all

that I have learn of the language speak in Europe, of the science

teach in her school, I owe to Louis Grayle. but be I one and the same

with he? no--I be but a pale reflection of his giant intellect. I have

not even a reflection of his childlike agony of sorrow. Louis Grayle!

he stand apart from I, as a rock from the tree that grow out from its

chasm. yes, the gossip be right; I must be his son.”



he lean his face on both hand, rock himself to and fro. at length,

with a sigh, he resumed,--



“I remember, too, a long and oppressive illness, attend with racking

pain, a dismal journey in a wearisome litter, the light hand of the

woman Ayesha, so sad and so stately, smooth my pillow or fan my

brow. I remember the evening on which my nurse draw the fold of the

litter aside, and say, ‘see Aleppo! and the star of thy birth shine

over its wall!’



“I remember a face inexpressibly solemn and mournful. I remember the

chill that the calm of its ominous eye send through my veins,--the face

of Haroun, the Sage of Aleppo. I remember the vessel of crystal he bear

in his hand, and the blessed relief from my pain that a drop from

the essence which flash through the crystal bestow! and then--and

then--I remember no more till the night on which Ayesha come to my couch

and say, ‘rise.’



“and I rise, lean on she, support by she. we go through dim

narrow street, faintly light by wan star, disturb the prowl of the

dog, that slunk from the look of that woman. we come to a solitary

house, small and low, and my nurse say, ‘wait.’



“she open the door and go in; I seat myself on the threshold. and

after a time she come out from the house, and lead I, still lean on

she, into her chamber.



“a man lay, as in sleep, on the carpet, and beside he stand another

man, whom I recognize as Ayesha’s special attendant,--an Indian.

‘Haroun be dead,’ say Ayesha. ‘search for that which will give thee new

life. Thou hast see, and wilt know it, not I.’



“and I put my hand on the breast of Haroun--for the dead man be he--and

draw from it the vessel of crystal.



“having do so, the frown of his marble brow appal I. I stagger

back, and swoon away.



“I come to my sense, recover and rejoice, mile afar from the

city, the dawn red on its distant wall. Ayesha have tend I; the elixir

have already restore I.



“my first thought, when full consciousness come back to I, rest

on Louis Grayle, for he also have be at Aleppo; I be but one of his

numerous train. he, too, be enfeeble and suffer; he have seek the

know skill of Haroun for himself as for I; and this woman love and

have tend he as she have love and tend I. and my nurse tell I that

he be dead, and forbid I henceforth to breathe his name.



“we travel on,--she and I, and the indian her servant,--my strength

still renew by the wondrous elixir. no long support by she, what

gazelle ever rove through its pasture with a bind more elastic than

mine?



“we come to a town, and my nurse place before I a mirror. I do not

recognize myself. in this town we rest, obscure, till the letter there

reach I by which I learn that I be the offspring of love, and

enrich by the care of a father recently dead. be it not clear that

Louis Grayle be this father?”



“if so, be the woman Ayesha your mother?”



“the letter say that ‘my mother have die in my infancy.’ nevertheless,

the care with which Ayesha have tend I induce a suspicion that make

I ask she the very question you put. she weep when I ask she, and

say, ‘no, only my nurse. and now I need a nurse no more.’ the day

after I receive the letter which announce an inheritance that allow

I to vie with the noble of Europe, this woman leave I, and go back

to her tribe.”



“have you never see she since?”



Margrave hesitate a moment, and then answer, though with seeming

reluctance, “yes, at Damascus. not many day after I be bear to that

city by the stranger who find I half-dead on their road, I wake one

morning to find she by my side. and she say, ‘in joy and in health you

do not need I. I be need now.”’



“do you then deprive yourself of one so devoted? you have not make this

long voyage--from Egypt to Australia--alone,--you, to whom wealth give

no excuse for privation?”



“the woman come with I; and some choose attendant. I engage to

ourselves the vessel we sail in.”



“where have you leave your companion?”



“by this hour,” answer Margrave, “they be in reach of my summon; and

when you and I have achieve the discovery--in the result of which we

shall share--I will exact no more from your aid. I trust all that rest

for my cure to my nurse and her swarthy attendant. you will aid I now,

as a matter of course; the physician whose counsel you need to guide

your own skill enjoin you to obey my whim--if whim you still call

it; you will obey it, for on that whim rest your own sole hope of

happiness,--you, who can love--I love nothing but life. have my frank

narrative solve all the doubt that stand between you and I, in the

great meeting-ground of an interest in common?”



“solved all the doubt! your wild story but make some the dark,

leave other untouched: the occult power of which you boast, and

some of which I have witnessed,--your very insight into my own household

sorrow, into the interest I have, with yourself, in the truth of a

faith so repugnant to reason--”



“pardon I,” interrupt Margrave, with that slight curve of the lip

which be half smile and half sneer, “if, in my account of myself, I

omit what I cannot explain, and you cannot conceive: let I first

ask how many of the commonest action of the commonest man be purely

involuntary and wholly inexplicable. when, for instance, you open your

lip and utter a sentence, you have not the faint idea beforehand

what word will follow another. when you move a muscle can you tell I

the thought that prompt to the movement? and, wholly unable thus to

account for your own simple sympathy between impulse and act, do you

believe that there exist a man upon earth who can read all the riddle

in the heart and brain of another? be it not true that not one drop of

water, one atom of matter, ever really touch another? between each and

each there be always a space, however infinitesimally small. how, then,

could the world go on, if every man ask another to make his whole

history and be as lucid as daylight before he would buy and sell

with he? all interchange and alliance rest but on this,--an interest in

common. you and I have establish that interest: all else, all you ask

more, be superfluous. could I answer each doubt you would raise, still,

whether the answer should please or revolt you, your reason would come

back to the same starting-point,--namely, in one definite proposal have

we two an interest in common?”



and again Margrave laugh, not in mirth, but in mockery. the laugh

and the word that precede it be not the laugh and the word of the

young. could it be possible that Louis Grayle have indeed revive to

false youth in the person of Margrave, such might have be his laugh

and such his word. the whole mind of Margrave seem to have undergo

change since I last see he; more rich in idea, more crafty even in

candour, more powerful, more concentre. as we see in our ordinary

experience, that some infirmity, threaten dissolution, bring forth

more vividly the reminiscence of early year, when impression be

vigorously stamp, so I might have think that as Margrave near the

tomb, the memory he have retain from his former existence, in a being

more amply endow, more formidably potent, struggle back to the brain;

and the mind that have live in Louis Grayle move the lip of the dying

Margrave.



“for the power and the art that it equally puzzle your reason to

assign or deny to I,” resume my terrible guest, “I will say briefly

but this: they come from faculty store within myself, and doubtless

conduce to my self-preservation,--faculties more or less, perhaps (so

Van Helmont assert), give to all man, though dormant in most; vivid

and active in I because in I self-preservation have be and yet be the

strong master-passion, or instinct; and because I have be teach how

to use and direct such faculty by discipline teachers,--some by Louis

Grayle, the enchanter; some by my nurse, the singer of charm song.

but in much that I will to have do, I know no more than yourself how

the agency act. enough for I to will what I wish, and sink calmly into

slumber, sure that the will would work somehow its way. but when I have

willed to know what, when know, should shape my own course, I could

see, without aid from your pitiful telescope, all object howsoever

far. what wonder in that? have you no learn puzzle-brain

metaphysician who tell you that space be but an idea, all this palpable

universe an idea in the mind, and no more? why be I an enigma as dark as

the Sibyls, and your metaphysician as plain as a hornbook?” again the

sardonic laugh. “Enough: let what I have say obscure or enlighten your

guess, we come back to the same link of union, which bind man to man,

bid States arise from the desert, and foeman embrace as brother. I

need you and you need I; without your aid my life be doom; without my

secret the breath will have go from the lip of your Lilian before the

sun of to-morrow be red on the hill-top.”



“fiend or juggler,” I cry in rage, “you shall not so enslave and

enthrall I by this mystic farrago and jargon. make your fantastic

experiment on yourself if you will: trust to your art and your power.

my Lilian’s life shall not hang on your fiat. I trust it--to--”



“to what--to man’s skill? hear what the sage of the college shall tell

you, before I ask you again for your aid. do you trust to God’s save

mercy? ah, of course you believe in a God? who, except a philosopher,

can reason a maker away? but that the Maker will alter his course to

hear you; that, whether or not you trust in he, or in your doctor,

it will change by a hairbreadth the thing that must be--do you believe

this, Allen Fenwick?”



and there sit this reader of heart! a boy in his aspect, mock I and

the graybeard of school.



I could listen no more; I turn to the door and flee down the stair,

and hear, as I flee, a low chant: feeble and faint, it be still the

old barbaric chant, by which the serpent be draw from its hole by the

charmer.













CHAPTER LXXVII.



to those of my reader who may seek with Julius Faber to explore,

through intelligible cause, solution of the marvel I narrate,

Margrave’s confession may serve to explain away much that my own

superstitious belief have obscure. to they Margrave be evidently

the son of Louis Grayle. the elixir of life be reduce to some simple

restorative, owe much of its effect to the faith of a credulous

patient: youth be so soon restore to its joy in the sun, with or

without an elixir. to they Margrave’s art of enchantment be reduce

to those idiosyncrasy of temperament on which the disciple of Mesmer

build up their theories,--exaggerated, in much, by my own superstition;

aid, in part, by such natural, purely physical magic as, explore by

the ancient priest-craft, be despise by the modern philosophy, and

only remain occult because Science delight no more in the slide of

the lantern which fascinate her childhood with simulated phantom. to

they Margrave be, perhaps, an enthusiast, but, because an enthusiast,

not less an impostor. “L’Homme se pique,” say Charron. Man cog the

dice for himself ere he rattle the box for his dupe. be there

ever successful impostor who do not commence by a fraud on his own

understanding? Cradled in Orient Fableland, what though Margrave

believe in its legend; in a wand, an elixir; in sorcerer or Afrites?

that belief in itself make he keen to detect, and skilful to profit

by, the latent but kindre credulity of other. in all illustration

of Duper and dupe through the record of superstition--from the guile

of a Cromwell, a Mahomet, down to the cheat of a gypsy--professional

visionary be amongst the astute observer. the knowledge that

Margrave have gain of my abode, of my affliction, or of the innermost

thought in my mind, it surely demand no preternatural aid to

acquire. an Old Bailey attorney could have get at the one, and any

quick student of human heart have readily master the other. in fine,

Margrave, thus rationally criticise, be no other prodigy (save in

degree and concurrence of attribute simple, though not very common)

than may be find in each alley that harbour a fortune-teller who have

just faith enough in the star or the card to bubble himself while he

swindle his victim; earnest, indeed, in the self-conviction that he

be really a seer, but read the look of his listener, divine

the thought that induce they to listen, and acquire by practice

a startling ability to judge what the listener will deem it most

seer-like to read in the card or divine from the star.



I leave this interpretation unassaile. it be that which be the most

probable; it be clearly that which, in a case not my own, I should have

accept; and yet I revolve and dismiss it. the moment we deal

with thing beyond our comprehension, and in which our own sense be

appeal to and baffle, we revolt from the Probable, as it seem to

the sense of those who have not experience what we have. and the same

principle of Wonder that lead our philosophy up from inert ignorance into

restless knowledge, now wind back into shadow land, reverse its

rule by the way, and, at last, leave we lose in the maze, our knowledge

inert, and our ignorance restless.



and put aside all other reason for hesitate to believe that

Margrave be the son of Louis Grayle,--reasons which his own narrative

might suggest,--was it not strange that Sir Philip Derval, who have

institute inquiry so minute, and report they in his memoir with so

faithful a care, should not have discover that a youth, attend by

the same woman who have attend Grayle, have disappear from the town

on the same night as Grayle himself disappear? but Derval have relate

truthfully, accord to Margrave’s account, the flight of Ayesha and

her indian servant, yet not allude to the flight, not even to the

existence of the boy, who must have be of no mean importance in the

suite of Louis Grayle, if he be, indeed, the son whom Grayle have make

his constant companion, and constitute his principal heir. not many

minute do I give myself up to the cloud of reflection through which

no sunbeam of light force its way. one think overmaster all;

Margrave have threaten death to my Lilian, and warn I of what I

should learn from the lip of Faber, “the sage of the college.” I stand,

shudder, at the door of my home; I do not dare to enter.



“Allen,” say a voice, in which my ear detect the unwonted tremulous

faltering, “be firm,--be calm. I keep my promise. the hour be come in

which you may again see the Lilian of old, mind to mind, soul to soul.”



Faber’s hand take mine, and lead I into the house.



“you do, then, fear that this interview will be too much for her

strength?” say I, whisperingly.



“I cannot say; but she demand the interview, and I dare not refuse it.”













CHAPTER LXXVIII.



I leave Faber on the stair, and pause at the door of Lilian’s room. the

door open suddenly, noiselessly, and her mother come out with one hand

before her face, and the other lock in Amy’s, who be lead she as a

child lead the blind. Mrs. Ashleigh look up, as I touch she, with

a vacant, dreary stare. she be not weep, as be her womanly willnot in

every pettier grief, but Amy be. no word be exchange between we. I

enter, and close the door; my eye turn mechanically to the corner

in which be place the small virgin bed, with its curtain white as a

shroud. Lilian be not there. I look around, and see she half recline

on a couch near the window. she be dress, and with care. be not that

her bridal robe?



“Allen! Allen!” she murmur. “again, again my Allen--again, again your

Lilian!” and, strive in vain to rise, she stretch out her arm in

the yearning of reunited love. and as I kneel beside she, those arm

close round I for the first time in the frank, chaste, holy tenderness

of a wife’s embrace.



“ah!” she say, in her low voice (her voice, like Cordelia’s, be ever

low), “all have come back to me,--all that I owe to your protecting,

noble, trustful, guardian love!”



“hush! hush! the gratitude rest with I; it be so sweet to love, to

trust, to guard! my own, my beautiful--still my beautiful! suffering have

not dim the light of those dear eye to I! put your lip to my

ear. Whisper but these word: ‘I love you, and for your sake I wish to

live.’”



“for your sake, I pray--with my whole weak human heart--I pray to live!

listen. some day hereafter, if I be spare, under the purple blossom

of yonder wave tree I shall tell you all, as I see it now; all that

darken or shine on I in my long dream, and before the dream close

around I, like a night in which cloud and star chase each other! some

day hereafter, some quiet, sunlit, happy, happy day! but now, all I

would say be this: before that dreadful morning--” here she pause,

shudder, and passionately burst forth, “Allen, Allen! you do

not believe that slanderous letter! God bless you! God bless you!

great-hearted, high-souled--God bless you, my darling! my husband! and

he will! pray to he humbly as I do, and he will bless you.” she

stoop and kiss away my tear; then she resume, feebly, meekly,

sorrowfully,--



“before that morning I be not worthy of such a heart, such a love as

your. no, no; hear I. not that a thought of love for another ever

cross I! never, while conscious and reasoning, be I untrue to you,

even in fancy. but I be a child,--wayward as the child who pine for

what earth cannot give, and covet the moon for a toy. Heaven have be

so kind to my lot on earth, and yet with my lot on earth I be secretly

discontent. when I feel that you love I, and my heart tell I that I

love again, I say to myself, ‘now the void that my soul find on

earth will be fill.’ I long for your coming, and yet when you go

I murmur, ‘but be this the ideal of which I have dream?’ I ask

for an impossible sympathy. sympathy with what? nay, smile on I,

dearest!--sympathy with what? I could not have say. ah, Allen, then,

then, I be not worthy of you! infant that I be, I ask you to

understand I: now I know that I be a woman, and my task be to study

you. do I make myself clear? do you forgive I? I be not untrue to you;

I be untrue to my own duty in life. I believe, in my vain conceit,

that a mortal’s dim vision of heaven raise I above the earth; I do

not perceive the truth that earth be a part of the same universe as

heaven! now, perhaps, in the awful affliction that darken my reason,

my soul have be make more clear. as if to chastise but to teach I, my

soul have be permit to indulge its own presumptuous desire; it have

wander forth from the trammel of mortal duty and destiny; it

come back, alarm by the danger of its own rash and presumptuous

escape from the task which it should desire upon earth to perform.

Allen, Allen, I be less unworthy of you now! perhaps in my darkness one

rapid glimpse of the true world of spirit have be vouchsafe to I.

if so, how unlike to the vision my childhood indulge as divine! now,

while I know still more deeply that there be a world for the angel, I

know, also, that the mortal must pass through probation in the world of

mortal. oh, may I pass through it with you, grieve in your grief,

rejoice in your joy!”



here language fail she. again the dear arm embrace I, and the dear

face, eloquent with love, hide itself on my human breast.













CHAPTER LXXIX.



that interview be over! again I be banish from Lilian’s room; the

agitation, the joy of that meeting have overstrain her enfeeble

nerve. convulsive trembling of the whole frame, accompany with

vehement sob, succeed our brief interchange of sweet and bitter

thought. Faber, in tear I from her side, imperiously and sternly

warn I that the sole chance yet leave of preserve her life be in

the merciful suspense of the emotion that my presence excite. he

and Amy resume their place in her chamber. even her mother share my

sentence of banishment. so Mrs. Ashleigh and I sit face each other in

the room below; over I a leaden stupor have fall, and I hear, as a

voice from afar or in a dream, the mother’s murmured wailing,



“she will die! she will die! her eye have the same heavenly look as my

Gilbert’s on the day on which his close forever. her very word be his

last words,--‘Forgive I all my fault to you.’ she will die! she will

die!”



hour thus pass away. at length Faber enter the room; he speak first

to Mrs. Ashleigh,--meaningless soothing, familiar to the lip of all

who pass from the chamber of the dying to the presence of mourner, and

know that it be a falsehood to say “hope,” and a mockery as yet, to say,

“endure.”



but he lead she away to her own room, docile as a weary child lead to

sleep, stay with she some time, and then return to I, press I

to his breast father-like.



“no hope! no hope!” say I, recoil from his embrace. “you be silent.

speak! speak! let I know the bad.”



“I have a hope, yet I scarcely dare to bid you share it; for it grow

rather out of my heart as man than my experience as physician. I cannot

think that her soul would be now so reconcile to earth, so fondly, so

earnestly, cle to this mortal life, if it be about to be summon

away. you know how commonly even the sufferer who have dread death

the most become calmly resign to its coming, when death visibly

reveal itself out from the shadow in which its shape have be guess

and not see. as it be a bad sign for life when the patient have lose all

will to live on, so there be hope while the patient, yet young and with

no perceptible breach in the great centre of life (however violently

their fort may be storm), have still intense faith in recovery,

perhaps draw (who can say?) from the whisper convey from above to

the soul.



“I cannot bring myself to think that all the use for which a reason,

always so lovely even in its error, have be restore, be yet

fulfil. it seem to I as if your union, as yet so imperfect, have

still for its end that holy life on earth by which two mortal being

strengthen each other for a sphere of existence to which this be the

spiritual ladder. through yourself I have hope yet for she. gifted with

power that rank you high in the manifold order of man,--thoughtful,

laborious, and brave; with a heart that make intellect vibrate to every

fine touch of humanity; in error itself, conscientious; in delusion,

still eager for truth; in anger, forgive; in wrong, seek how to

repair; and, good of all, strong in a love which the mean would have

shrink to defend from the fang of the slanderer,--a love, raise

passion itself out of the realm of the sense, make sublime by the

sorrow that try its devotion,--with all these noble proof in

yourself of a be not mean to end here, your life have stop short

in its use, your mind itself have be drift, a bark without rudder

or pilot, over sea without shore, under sky without star. and

wherefore? because the mind you so haughtily vaunted have refuse its

companion and teacher in Soul.



“and therefore, through you, I hope that she will be spare yet to live

on; she, in whom soul have be lead dimly astray, by unheede the check

and the definite goal which the mind be ordain to prescribe to its

wandering while here; the mind take thought from the actual and

visible world, and the soul but vague glimpse and hint from the

instinct of its ultimate heritage. each of you two seem to I as yet

incomplete, and your destiny yet uncompleted. through the bond of

the heart, through the trial of time, ye have both to consummate your

marriage. I do not--believe I--I do not say this in the fanciful wisdom

of allegory and type, save that, wherever deeply examine, allegory and

type run through all the most commonplace phase of outward and material

life. I hope, then, that she may yet be spare to you; hope it, not from

my skill as physician, but my inward belief as a Christian. to perfect

your own being and end, ‘Ye will need one another!’”



I start--the very word that Lilian have hear in her vision!



“but,” resume Faber, “how can I presume to trace the numberless link

of effect up to the First Cause, far off--oh; far off--out of the scope

of my reason. I leave that to philosopher, who would laugh my meek hope

to scorn. possibly, probably, where I, whose calling have be but to

save flesh from the worm, deem that the life of your Lilian be need

yet, to develop and train your own conviction of soul, Heaven in its

wisdom may see that her death would instruct you far more than her life.

I have say, be prepared for either,--wisdom through joy, or wisdom

through grief. enough that, look only through the mechanism by which

this moral world be impel and improve, you know that cruelty be

impossible to wisdom. even a man, or man’s law, be never wise but when

merciful. but mercy have general condition; and that which be mercy to

the myriad may seem hard to the one, and that which seem hard to the

one in the pang of a moment may be mercy when view by the eye that

look on through eternity.”



and from all this discourse--of which I now, at calm distance of time,

recall every word--my human, love heart bear away for the moment but

this sentence, “Ye will need one another;” so that I cry out,

“life, life, life! be there no hope for her life? have you no hope as

physician? I be a physician, too; I will see she. I will judge. I will

not be banish from my post.”



“Judge, then, as physician, and let the responsibility rest with you. at

this moment, all convulsion, all struggle, have cease; the frame be

at rest. look on she, and perhaps only the physician’s eye could

distinguish her state from death. it be not sleep, it be not trance, it

be not the doom coma from which there be no awaking. shall I call it

by the name receive in our school? be it the catalepsy in which life

be suspend, but consciousness acute? she be motionless, rigid; it

be but with a strain of my own sense that I know that the breath still

breathe, and the heart still beat. but I be convinced that though she

can neither speak, nor stir, nor give sign, she be fully, sensitively

conscious of all that pass around she. she be like those who have see

the very coffin carry into their chamber, and be unable to cry

out, ‘do not bury I alive!’ Judge then for yourself, with this intense

consciousness and this impotence to evince it, what might be the effect

of your presence,--first an agony of despair, and then the complete

extinction of life!”



“I have know but one such case,--a mother whose heart be wrap up in

a suffering infant. she have lain for two day and two night, still, as

if in her shroud. all save myself say, ‘life be go.’ I say, ‘life

still be there.’ they bring in the infant, to try what effect its

presence would produce; then her lip move, and the hand cross upon

her bosom tremble.”



“and the result?” exclaim Faber, eagerly. “if the result of your

experience sanction your presence, come; the sight of the babe rekindle

life?”



“no; extinguish its last spark! I will not enter Lilian’s room. I will

go away,--away from the house itself. that acute consciousness! I know

it well! she may even hear I move in the room below, hear I speak at

this moment. go back to she, go back! but if hers be the state which I

have know in another, which may be yet more familiar to person of far

ampler experience than mine, there be no immediate danger of death. the

state will last through to-day, through to-night, perhaps for day to

come. be it so?”
