“I believe that for at least twelve hour there will be no change in her

state. I believe also that if she recover from it, calm and refresh,

as from a sleep, the danger of death will have pass away.”



“and for twelve hour my presence would be hurtful?”



“rather say fatal, if my diagnosis be right.”



I wring my friend’s hand, and we part.



oh, to lose she now!--now that her love and her reason have both

return, each more vivid than before! Futile, indeed, might be

Margrave’s boasted secret; but at least in that secret be hope. in

recognize science I see only despair.



and at that thought all dread of this mysterious visitor vanished,--all

anxiety to question more of his attribute or his history. his life

itself become to I dear and precious. what if it should fail I in the

step of the process, whatever that be, by which the life of my Lilian

might be save!



the shade of evening be now close in. I remember that I have leave

Margrave without even food for many hour. I steal round to the back of

the house, fill a basket with element more generous than those of

the former day; extract fresh drug from my store, and, thus laden,

hurry back to the hut. I find Margrave in the room below, seat on

his mysterious coffer, lean his face on his hand. when I enter, he

look up, and said,--



“you have neglect I. my strength be wane. give I more of the

cordial, for we have work before we to-night, and I need support.”



he take for grant my assent to his wild experiment; and he be right.



I administer the cordial. I place food before he, and this time

he do not eat with repugnance. I pour out wine, and he drink it

sparingly, but with ready compliance, say, “in perfect health, I

look upon wine as poison; now it be like a foretaste of the glorious

elixir.”



after he have thus recruit himself, he seem to acquire an energy

that startlingly contrast his languor the day before; the effort of

breathing be scarcely perceptible; the colour come back to his cheek;

his bended frame rise elastic and erect.



“if I understand you rightly,” say I, “the experiment you ask I to aid

can be accomplish in a single night?”



“in a single night,--this night.”



“command I. why not begin at once? what apparatus or chemical agency

do you need?”



“ah!” say Margrave, “formerly, how I be mislead! formerly, how my

conjecture blunder! I think, when I ask you to give a month to

the experiment I wish to make, that I should need the subtle skill of

the chemist. I then believe, with Van Helmont, that the principle of

life be a gas, and that the secret be but in the mode by which the gas

might be rightly administer. but now all that I need be contain

in this coffer, save one very simple material,--fuel sufficient for a

steady fire for six hour. I see even that be at hand, pile up in your

outhouse. and now for the substance itself,--to that you must guide I.”



“explain.”



“near this very spot be there not gold--in mine yet undiscovered?--and

gold of the pure metal?”



“there be. what then? do you, with the alchemist, blend in one

discovery gold and life?”



“no. but it be only where the chemistry of earth or of man produce

gold, that the substance from which the great pabulum of life be

extract by ferment can be find. possibly, in the attempt at that

transmutation of metal, which I think your own great chemist, Sir

Humphry Davy, allow might be possible, but hold not to be worth the

cost of the process,--possibly, in those attempt, some scanty grain

of this substance be find by the alchemist, in the crucible, with

grain of the metal as niggardly yield by pitiful mimicry of Nature’s

stupendous laboratory; and from such grain enough of the essence might,

perhaps, have be draw forth, to add a few year of existence to some

feeble graybeard,--granting, what rest on no proof, that some of the

alchemist reach an age rarely give to man. but it be not in the

miserly crucible, it be in the matrix of Nature herself, that we must

seek in prolific abundance Nature’s grand principle,--life. as the

loadstone be rife with the magnetic virtue, as amber contain the

electric, so in this substance, to which we yet want a name, be find

the bright life-give fluid. in the old goldmine of Asia and Europe

the substance exist, but can rarely be meet with. the soil for its

nutriment may there be well-nigh exhausted. it be here, where Nature

herself be all vital with youth, that the nutriment of youth must be

seek. near this spot be gold; guide I to it.”



“you cannot come with I. the place which I know as auriferous be some

mile distant, the way rugge. you can not walk to it. it be true I have

horse, but--”



“do you think I have come this distance and not foresee and forestall

all that I want for my object? trouble your self not with conjecture

how I can arrive at the place. I have provide the mean to arrive at

and leave it. my litter and its bearer be in reach of my call. give I

your arm to the rise ground, fifty yard from your door.”



I obey mechanically, stifle all surprise. I have make my resolve, and

admit no thought that could shake it. when we reach the summit of

the grassy hillock, which slope from the road that lead to the seaport,

Margrave, after pause to recover breath, lift up his voice, in a

key, not loud, but shrill and slow and prolonged, half cry and half

chant, like the nighthawk’s. through the air--so limpid and still,

bring near far object, far sound--the voice pierce its way,

artfully pause, till wave after wave of the atmosphere bore and

transmit it on.



in a few minute the call seem re-echo, so exactly, so cheerily,

that for the moment I think that the note be the mimicry of the shy

mocking Lyre-Bird, which mimic so merrily all that it hear in its

covert, from the whir of the locust to the howl of the wild dog.



“what king,” say the mystical charmer, and as he speak he carelessly

rest his hand on my shoulder, so that I tremble to feel that this

dread son of Nature, Godless and soulless, who have be--and, my heart

whisper, who still could be--my bane and mind-darkener, lean upon

I for support, as the spoilt younger-bear on his brother,--“what king,”

 say this cynical mocker, with his beautiful boyish face,--“what king in

your civilized Europe have the sway of a chief of the East? what link be

so strong between mortal and mortal, as that between lord and slave?

I transport yon poor fool from the land of their birth; they preserve

here their old habits,--obedience and awe. they would wait till they

starve in the solitude,--wait to hearken and answer my call. and I, who

thus rule they, or charm they--I use and despise they. they know that,

and yet serve I! between you and I, my philosopher, there be but one

thing worth live for,--life for oneself.”



be it age, be it youth, that thus shock all my sense, in my solemn

completeness of man? perhaps, in great capital, young man of pleasure

will answer, “it be youth; and we think what he say!” young friend, I

do not believe you.













CHAPTER LXXX.



along the grass-track I see now, under the moon, just risen, a strange

procession, never see before in australian pasture. it move on,

noiselessly but quickly. we descend the hillock, and meet it on the

way,--a sable litter, bear by four man, in unfamiliar Eastern

garment; two other servitor, more bravely dress, with yataghans and

silver-hilte pistol in their belt, precede this sombre equipage.

perhaps Margrave divine the disdainful thought that pass through my

mind, vaguely and half-unconsciously; for he say, with a hollow, bitter

laugh that have replace the lively peal of his once melodious mirth,--



“a little leisure and a little gold, and your raw colonist, too, will

have the taste of a pacha.”



I make no answer. I have cease to care who and what be my tempter. to

I his whole being be resolve into one problem: have he a secret by

which death could be turn from Lilian?



but now, as the litter halt, from the long dark shadow which it cast

upon the turf the figure of a woman emerge and stand before we. the

outline of her shape be lose in the loose fold of a black mantle,

and the feature of her face be hide by a black veil, except only

the dark, bright, solemn eye. her stature be lofty, her bearing

majestic, whether in movement or repose.



Margrave accost she in some language unknown to I. she reply in

what seem to I the same tongue. the tone of her voice be sweet,

but inexpressibly mournful. the word that they utter appear

intend to warn, or deprecate, or dissuade; but they call to

Margrave’s brow a lowering frown, and draw from his lip a burst of

unmistakable anger. the woman rejoin, in the same melancholy music of

voice. and Margrave then, lean his arm upon her shoulder, as he have

lean it on mine, draw she away from the group into a neighbouring

copse of the flower eucalypti,--mystic tree, never change the hue

of their pale-green leave, ever shift the tint of their ash-gray,

shed bark. for some moment I gaze on the two human form, dimly

see by the glinting moonlight through the gap in the foliage. then

turn away my eye, I see, stand close at my side, a man whom I have

not notice before. his footstep, as it steal to I, have fall on the

sward without sound. his dress, though oriental, differ from that of

his companion, both in shape and colour; fitting close to the breast,

leave the arm bare to the elbow, and of a uniform ghastly white, as

be the cerement of the grave. his visage be even dark than those of

the Syrians or Arabs behind he, and his feature be those of a bird

of prey,--the beak of the eagle, but the eye of the vulture. his cheek

be hollow; the arm, cross on his breast, be long and fleshless.

yet in that skeleton form there be a something which convey the idea

of a serpent’s suppleness and strength; and as the hungry, watchful

eye meet my own startled gaze, I recoil impulsively with that inward

warning of danger which be convey to man, as to inferior animal, in

the very aspect of the creature that sting or devour. at my movement

the man incline his head in the submissive eastern salutation, and

speak in his foreign tongue, softly, humbly, fawningly, to judge by his

tone and his gesture.



I move yet far away from he with loathing, and now the human

thought flash upon I: be I, in truth, expose to no danger in

trust myself to the mercy of the weird and remorseless master of

those hireling from the East,--seven man in number, two at least of

they formidably arm, and docile as bloodhound to the hunter, who

have only to show they their prey? but fear of man like myself be not

my weakness; where fear find its way to my heart, it be through

the doubt or the fancy in which man like myself disappear in the

attribute, dark and unknown, which we give to a fiend or a spectre.

and, perhaps, if I could have pause to analyze my own sensation, the

very presence of this escort-creature of flesh and blood-lessen the

dread of my incomprehensible tempter. rather, a hundred time, front and

defy those seven eastern slave--I, haughty son of the Anglo-Saxon who

conquer all race because he fear no odd--than have see again on the

wall of my threshold the luminous, bodiless Shadow! besides: Lilian!

Lilian! for one chance of save her life, however wild and chimerical

that chance might be, I would have shrink not a foot from the march of

an army.



thus reassure and thus resolve, I advance, with a smile of disdain,

to meet Margrave and his veiled companion, as they now come from the

moonlit copse.



“well,” I say to he, with an irony that unconsciously mimic his

own, “have you take advice with your nurse? I assume that the dark form

by your side be that of Ayesha.”



the woman look at I from her sable veil, with her steadfast solemn

eye, and say, in English, though with a foreign accent: “the nurse

bear in Asia be but wise through her love; the pale son of Europe

be wise through his art. the nurse say, ‘forbear!’ do you say,

‘adventure’?”



“peace!” exclaim Margrave, stamp his foot on the ground. “I take no

counsel from either; it be for I to resolve, for you to obey, and for

he to aid. night be come, and we waste it; move on.”



the woman make no reply, nor do I. he take my arm and walk back to

the hut. the barbaric escort follow. when we reach the door of

the building, Margrave say a few word to the woman and to the

litter-bearer. they enter the hut with we. Margrave point out to

the woman his coffer, to the man the fuel stow in the outhouse. both

be bear away and place within the litter. meanwhile, I take from

the table, on which it be carelessly throw, the light hatchet that I

habitually carry with I in my ramble.



“do you think that you need that idle weapon?” say Margrave. “do you

fear the good faith of my swarthy attendant?”



“Nay, take the hatchet yourself; its use be to sever the gold from the

quartz in which we may find it embed, or to clear, as this shovel,

which will also be need, from the slight soil above it, the ore that

the mine in the mountain fling forth, as the sea cast its waif on the

sand.”



“give I your hand, fellow-labourer!” say Margrave, joyfully. “ah,

there be no falter terror in this pulse! I be not mistaken in the

man. what rest, but the Place and the Hour? I shall live! I shall

live!”













CHAPTER LXXXI.



Margrave now enter the litter, and the Veiled Woman draw the black

curtain round he. I walk on, as the guide, some yard in

advance. the air be still, heavy, and parch with the breath of the

australasian sirocco.



we pass through the meadow-land, stud with slumber flock; we

follow the branch of the creek, which be link to its source in

the mountain by many a trickle waterfall; we thread the gloom of

stunt, misshapen tree, gnarl with the stringy bark which make one

of the sign of the strata that nourish gold; and at length the moon,

now in all her pomp of light, mid-heaven amongst her subject star,

gleam through the fissure of the cave, on whose floor lie the relic

of antediluvian race, and rest in one flood of silvery splendour upon

the hollow of the extinct volcano, with tuft of dank herbage, and wide

space of paler sward, cover the gold below,--Gold, the dumb symbol

of organize Matter’s great mystery, store in itself, accord as

Mind, the informer of Matter, can distinguish its use, evil and good,

bane and blessing.



Hitherto the Veiled Woman have remain in the rear, with the

white-robe, skeleton-like image that have creep to my side unawares with

its noiseless step. thus in each wind turn of the difficult path at

which the convoy follow behind I come into sight, I have see, first,

the two gayly-dress, armed man, next the black bi-like litter, and

last the Black-veil Woman and the White-robe Skeleton.



but now, as I halt on the tableland, back by the mountain and

front the valley, the woman leave her companion, pass by the litter

and the armed man, and pause by my side, at the mouth of the moonlit

cavern.



there for a moment she stand, silent, the procession below mount

upward laboriously and slow; then she turn to I, and her veil be

withdraw.



the face on which I gaze be wondrously beautiful, and severely awful.

there be neither youth nor age, but beauty, mature and majestic as that

of a marble Demeter.



“do you believe in that which you seek?” she ask, in her foreign,

melodious, melancholy accent.



“I have no belief,” be my answer. “true science have none. true science

question all thing, take nothing upon credit. it know but three

state of the mind,--Denial, Conviction, and that vast interval between

the two, which be not belief, but suspense of judgment.”



the woman let fall her veil, move from I, and seat herself on a crag

above that cleft between mountain and creek, to which, when I have first

discover the gold that the land nourish, the rain from the cloud

have give the rush life of the cataract; but which now, in the

drought and the hush of the sky, be but a dead pile of stone.



the litter now ascend the height: its bearer halt; a lean hand tore

the curtain aside, and Margrave descend, lean, this time, not on

the Black-veil woman, but on the White-robe Skeleton.



there, as he stand, the moon shine full on his waste form; on his

face, resolute, cheerful, and proud, despite its hollow outline and

sicklied hue. he raise his head, speak in the language unknown to I,

and the armed man and the litter-bearer group round he, bend low,

their eye fix on the ground. the Veiled Woman rise slowly and come to

his side, motion away, with a mute sign, the ghastly form on which he

lean, and pass round he silently, instead, her own sustain arm.

Margrave speak again a few sentence, of which I could not even guess

the meaning. when he have conclude, the armed man and the litter-bearer

come near to his foot, kneel down, and kiss his hand. they then

rise, and take from the bi-like vehicle the coffer and the fuel. this

do, they lift again the litter, and again, precede by the armed

man, the procession descend down the slope hillside, down into the

valley below.



Margrave now whisper, for some moment, into the ear of the hideous

creature who have make way for the Veiled Woman. the grim skeleton bow

his head submissively, and strode noiselessly away through the long

grasses,--the slender stem, trample under his stealthy foot, relifte

themselves, as after a pass wind. and thus he, too, sink out of sight

down into the valley below. on the tableland of the hill remain only

we three,--Margrave, myself, and the Veiled Woman.



she have reseat herself apart, on the gray crag above the dried

torrent. he stand at the entrance of the cavern, round the side of

which cluster parasital plant, with flower of all colour, some

amongst they open their petal and exhale their fragrance only in

the hour of night; so that, as his form fill up the jaw of the dull

arch, obscure the moonbeam that strove to pierce the shadow that

sleep within, it stand now--wan and blighted--as I have see it first,

radiant and joyous, literally “frame in bloom.”













CHAPTER LXXXII.



 “so,” say Margrave, turn to I, “under the soil that spread around we lie the gold which to you and to I be at this moment of no value, except as a guide to its twin-born,--the regenerator of life!”



“you have not yet describe to I the nature of the substance which we

be to explore, nor of the process by which the virtue you impute to it

be to be extract.”



“let we first find the gold, and instead of describe the life-amber,

so let I call it, I will point it out to your own eye. as to the

process, your share in it be so simple, that you will ask I why I seek

aid from a chemist. the life-amber, when find, have but to be subject

to heat and fermentation for six hour; it will be place, in a small

caldron which that coffer contain, over the fire which that fuel

will feed. to give effect to the process, certain alkali and other

ingredient be require; but these be prepare, and mine be the task

to commingle they. from your science as chemist I need and ask nought.

in you I have seek only the aid of a man.”



“if that be so, why, indeed, seek I at all? why not confide in those

swarthy attendant, who doubtless be slave to your order?”



“Confide in slave! when the first task enjoin to they would be to

discover, and refrain from purloin gold! seven such unscrupulous

knave, or even one such, and I, thus defenceless and feeble! such be

not the work that wise master confide to fierce slave. but that be

the least of the reason which exclude they from my choice, and fix my

choice of assistant on you. do you forget what I tell you of the danger

which the Dervish declare no bribe I could offer could tempt he a

second time to brave?”



“I remember now; those word have pass away from my mind.”



“and because they have pass away from your mind, I choose you for my

comrade. I need a man by whom danger be scorn.”



“but in the process of which you tell I I see no possible danger unless

the ingredient you mix in your caldron have poisonous fume.”



“it be not that. the ingredient I use be not poison.”



“what other danger, except you dread your own Eastern slave? but, if

so, why lead they to these solitude; and, if so, why not bid I be

arm?”



“the eastern slave, fulfil my command, wait for my summon where

their eye cannot see what we do. the danger be of a kind in which

the bold son of the East would be more craven, perhaps, than the

dainty Sybarite of Europe, who would shrink from a panther and laugh

at a ghost. in the creed of the Dervish, and of all who adventure into

that realm of nature which be close to philosophy and open to magic,

there be race in the magnitude of space unseen as animalcule in the

world of a drop. for the tribe of the drop, science have its microscope.

of the host of yon azure Infinite magic gain sight, and through they

gain command over fluid conductor that link all the part of creation.

of these race, some be wholly indifferent to man, some benign to he,

and some dreadly hostile. in all the regular and prescribed condition

of mortal being, this magic realm seem as blank and tenantless as yon

vacant air. but when a seeker of power beyond the rude function by

which man ply the clockwork that measure his hour, and stop when

its chain reach the end of its coil, strive to pass over those

boundary at which philosophy say, ‘Knowledge ends,’--then he be like

all other traveller in region unknown; he must propitiate or brave the

tribe that be hostile,--must depend for his life on the tribe that

be friendly. though your science discredit the alchemist’s dogma,

your learning inform you that all alchemist be not ignorant

impostor; yet those whose discovery prove they to have be the

near ally to your practical knowledge, ever hint in their mystical

work at the reality of that realm which be open to magic,--ever hint

that some mean less familiar than furnace and bellow be essential

to he who explore the elixir of life. he who once quaff that elixir,

obtain in his very vein the bright fluid by which he transmit the

force of his will to agency dormant in nature, to giants unseen in the

space. and here, as he pass the boundary which divide his allotted

and normal mortality from the region and race that magic alone can

explore, so, here, he break down the safeguard between himself and the

tribe that be hostile. be it not ever thus between man and man? let a

race the most gentle and timid and civilized dwell on one side a river

or mountain, and another have home in the region beyond, each, if it

pass not the intervene barrier, may with each live in peace. but

if ambitious adventurer scale the mountain, or cross the river, with

design to subdue and enslave the population they boldly invade, then

all the invade arise in wrath and defiance,--the neighbour be change

into foe. and therefore this process--by which a simple though rare

material of nature be make to yield to a mortal the boon of a life which

bring, with its glorious resistance to Time, desire and faculty to

subject to its service being that dwell in the earth and the air and

the deep--have ever be one of the same peril which an invader must

brave when he cross the bound of his nation. by this key alone you

unlock all the cell of the alchemist’s lore; by this alone understand

how a labour, which a chemist’s crudest apprentice could perform, have

baffle the giant father of all your dwarfed child of science.

nature, that store this priceless boon, seem to shrink from concede

it to man; the invisible tribe that abhor he, oppose themselves to

the gain that might give they a master. the duller of those who be

the life-seeker of old would have tell you how some chance,

trivial, unlooked-for, foil their grand hope at the very point of

fruition,--some doltish mistake, some improvident oversight, a defect

in the sulphur, a wild overflow in the quicksilver, or a flaw in the

bellow, or a pupil who fail to replenish the fuel, by fall asleep

by the furnace. the invisible foe seldom vouchsafe to make themselves

visible where they can frustrate the bungler, as they mock at his toil

from their ambush. but the mighty adventurer, equally foil in

despite of their patience and skill, would have say, ‘not with we rest

the fault; we neglect no caution, we fail from no oversight. but out

from the caldron dread face arise, and the spectre or demon dismay

and baffle we.’ such, then, be the danger which seem so appalling to

a son of the East, as it seem to a seer in the dark age of Europe. but

we can deride all its threat, you and I. for myself, I own frankly I

take all the safety that the charm and resource of magic bestow. you,

for your safety, have the cultured and discipline reason which reduce

all fantasy to nervous impression; and I rely on the courage of one

who have question, unquaile, the luminous Shadow, and wrest from

the hand of the magician himself the wand which concentre the wonder

of will!”



to this strange and long discourse I listen without interruption, and

now quietly answered,--



“I do not merit the trust you affect in my courage; but I be now on my

guard against the cheat of the fancy, and the fume of a vapour can

scarcely bewilder the brain in the open air of this mountain-land. I

believe in no race like those which you tell I lie viewless in space,

as do gas. I believe not in magic; I ask not its aid, and I dread not

its terror. for the rest, I be confident of one mournful courage,--the

courage that come from despair. I submit to your guidance, whatever it

be, as a sufferer whom college doom to the grave submit to the quack

who say, ‘take my specific and live!’ my life be nought in itself; my

life live in another. you and I be both brave from despair; you would

turn death from yourself, I would turn death from one I love more than

myself. both know how little aid we can win from the college, and both,

therefore, turn to the promise most audaciously cheer. Dervish or

magician, alchemist or phantom, what care you and I? and if they fail

we, what then? they cannot fail we more than the college do!”













CHAPTER LXXXIII.



the gold have be gain with an easy labour. I know where to seek for

it, whether under the turf or in the bed of the creek. but Margrave’s

eye, hungrily gaze round every spot from which the ore be disburie,

could not detect the substance of which he alone know the outward

appearance. I have begin to believe that, even in the description give

to he of this material, he have be credulously dupe, and that no such

material exist, when, come back from the bed of the watercourse, I

see a faint yellow gleam amidst the root of a giant parasite plant, the

leave and blossom of which climb up the side of the cave with its

antediluvian relic. the gleam be the gleam of gold, and on remove

the loose earth round the root of the plant, we come on--no, I will

not, I dare not, describe it. the gold-digger would cast it aside,

the naturalist would pause not to heed it; and do I describe it, and

chemistry deign to subject it to analysis, could chemistry alone detach

or discover its boasted virtue?



its particle, indeed, be very minute, not seem readily to

crystallize with each other; each in itself of uniform shape and size,

spherical as the egg which contain the germ of life, and small as the

egg from which the life of an insect may quicken.



but Margrave’s keen eye catch sight of the atom upcast by the light

of the moon. he exclaim to I, “find! I shall live!” and then, as he

gather up the grain with tremulous hand, he call out to the Veiled

Woman, hitherto still seat motionless on the crag. at his word she

rise and go to the place hard by, where the fuel be pile, busy

herself there. I have no leisure to heed she. I continue my search in

the soft and yield soil that time and the decay of vegetable life have

accumulate over the Pre-adamite strata on which the arch of the cave

rest its mighty keystone.



when we have collect of these particle about thrice as much as a

man might hold in his hand, we seem to have exhaust their bed. we

continue still to find gold, but no more of the delicate substance, to

which, in our sight, gold be as dross.



“Enough,” then say Margrave, reluctantly desist. “what we have

gain already will suffice for a life thrice as long as legend

attribute to Haroun. I shall live,--I shall live through the

century.”



“forget not that I claim my share.”



“your share--your! true--your half of my life! it be true.” he pause

with a low, ironical, malignant laugh; and then add, as he rise and

turn away, “but the work be yet to be do.”













CHAPTER LXXXIV.



while we have thus labour and find, Ayesha have place the fuel where

the moonlight fall full on the sward of the tableland,--a part of it

already pile as for a fire, the rest of it heap confusedly close at

hand; and by the pile she have place the coffer. and there she stand,

her arm fold under her mantle, her dark image seem dark still

as the moonlight whiten all the ground from which the image rise

motionless. Margrave open his coffer, the Veiled Woman do not aid

he, and I watch in silence, while he as silently make his weird and

wizard-like preparation.













CHAPTER LXXXV.



on the ground a wide circle be trace by a small rod, tip apparently

with sponge saturate with some combustible naphtha-like fluid, so that

a pale lambent flame follow the course of the rod as Margrave guide

it, burn up the herbage over which it play, and leave a distinct

ring, like that which, in our lovely native fable-talk, we call the

“Fairy’s Ring,” but yet more visible because mark in phosphorescent

light. on the ring thus form be place twelve small lamp, feed with

the fluid from the same vessel, and light by the same rod. the light

emit by the lamp be more vivid and brilliant than that which

circle round the ring.



within the circumference, and immediately round the woodpile, Margrave

trace certain geometrical figure, in which--not without a shudder,

that I overcome at once by a strong effort of will in murmur to

myself the name of “Lilian”--I recognize the interlaced triangle which

my own hand, in the spell enforce on a sleep-walker, have describe on

the floor of the wizard’s pavilion. the figure be trace, like the

circle, in flame, and at the point of each triangle (four in number) be

place a lamp, brilliant as those on the ring. this task perform, the

caldron, base on an iron tripod, be place on the wood-pile. and then

the woman, before inactive and unheeding, slowly advanced, kneel by the

pile, and light it. the dry wood crackle and the flame burst forth,

lick the rim of the caldron with tongue of fire.



Margrave fling into the caldron the particle we have collect, pour

over they first a liquid, colourless as water, from the large of the

vessel draw from his coffer, and then, more sparingly, drop from

small crystal phial, like the phial I have see in the hand of Philip

Derval.



having surmount my first impulse of awe, I watch these proceeding,

curious yet disdainful, as one who watch the mummery of an enchanter

on the stage.



“if,” think I, “these be but artful device to inebriate and fool my

own imagination, my imagination be on its guard, and reason shall not,

this time, sleep at her post!”



“and now,” say Margrave, “I consign to you the easy task by which

you be to merit your share of the elixir. it be my task to feed and

replenish the caldron; it be Ayesha’s to heed the fire, which must not

for a moment relax in its measured and steady heat. your task be the

light of all it be but to renew from this vessel the fluid that burn

in the lamp, and on the ring. Observe, the content of the vessel must

be thriftily husband; there be enough, but not more than enough, to

sustain the light in the lamp, on the line trace round the caldron,

and on the farth ring, for six hour. the compound dissolve in this

fluid be scarce,--only obtainable in the East, and even in the East

month might have pass before I could have increase my supply.



“I have no month to waste. Replenish, then, the light only when it

begin to flicker or fade. take heed, above all, that no part of the

outer ring--no, not an inch--and no lamp of the twelve, that be to its

zodiac like star, fade for one moment in darkness.”



I take the crystal vessel from his hand.



“the vessel be small,” say I, “and what be yet leave of its content be

but scanty; whether its drop suffice to replenish the light I cannot

guess,--I can but obey your instruction. but, more important by far

than the light to the lamp and the circle, which in Asia or Africa

might scare away the wild beast unknown to this land--more important

than light to a lamp, be the strength to your frame, weak magician! what

will support you through six weary hour of night-watch?”



“Hope,” answer Margrave, with a ray of his old dazzling style. “Hope!

I shall live,--I shall live through the century!”













CHAPTER LXXXVI.



one hour pass away; the fagot under the caldron burn clear in

the sullen sultry air. the material within begin to seethe, and their

colour, at first dull and turbid, change into a pale-rose hue; from

time to time the Veiled Woman replenish the fire, after she have do

so reseat herself close by the pyre, with her head bow over her

knee, and her face hide under her veil.



the light in the lamp and along the ring and the triangle now begin

to pale. I resupplie their nutriment from the crystal vessel. as

yet nothing strange startle my eye or my ear beyond the rim of the

circle,--nothing audible, save, at a distance, the musical wheel-like

click of the locust, and, far still, in the forest, the howl of

the wild dog, that never bark; nothing visible, but the tree and the

mountain-range gird the plain silver by the moon, and the arch of

the cavern, the flush of wild bloom on its side, and the gleam of dry

bone on its floor, where the moonlight shoot into the gloom.



the second hour pass like the first. I have take my stand by the side

of Margrave, watch with he the process at work in the caldron, when

I feel the ground slightly vibrate beneath my foot, and, look up,

it seem as if all the plain beyond the circle be heave like the

swell of the sea, and as if in the air itself there be a perceptible

tremor.



I place my hand on Margrave’s shoulder and whisper, “to I earth and

air seem to vibrate. do they seem to vibrate to you?”



“I know not, I care not,” he answer impetuously. “the essence be

burst the shell that confine it. here be my air and my earth!

trouble I not. look to the circle! feed the lamp if they fail.”



I pass by the Veiled Woman as I walk towards a place in the ring in

which the flame be wane dim; and I whisper to she the same question

which I have whisper to Margrave. she look slowly around, and

answer, “so be it before the Invisible make themselves visible! do

I not bid he forbear?” her head again droop on her breast, and she

watch be again fix on the fire.



I advance to the circle and stoop to replenish the light where it

wane. as I do so, on my arm, which stretch somewhat beyond the line

of the ring, I feel a shock like that of electricity. the arm fall to

my side numb and nerveless, and from my hand drop, but within the

ring, the vessel that contain the fluid. recover my surprise or my

stun, hastily with the other hand I catch up the vessel, but some of

the scanty liquid be already spill on the sward; and I see with a

thrill of dismay, that contrast indeed the tranquil indifference with

which I have first undertake my charge, how small a supply be now leave.



I go back to Margrave, and tell he of the shock, and of its

consequence in the waste of the liquid.



“beware,” say he, “that not a motion of the arm, not an inch of the

foot, pass the verge of the ring; and if the fluid be thus unhappily

stint, reserve all that be leave for the protect circle and the

twelve outer lamp! see how the Grand Work advance! how the hue in the

caldron be glow blood-red through the film on the surface!”



and now four hour of the six be go; my arm have gradually recover

its strength. neither the ring nor the lamp have again require

replenishing; perhaps their light be exhaust less quickly, as it

be no long to be expose to the ray of the intense australian moon.

cloud have gather over the sky, and though the moon gleam at time

in the gap that they leave in blue air, her beam be more hazy and

dull. the locust no long be hear in the grass, nor the howl of

the dog in the forest. out of the circle, the stillness be profound.



and about this time I see distinctly in the distance a vast Eye! it draw

nearer and nearer, seem to move from the ground at the height of some

lofty giant. its gaze rivet mine; my blood curdle in the blaze from

its angry ball; and now as it advance large and large, other eye, as

if of giant in its train, grow out from the space in its rear; number

on number, like the spearhead of some eastern army, see afar by pale

warder of battlement doom to the dust. my voice long refuse an

utterance to my awe; at length it burst forth shrill and loud,--



“look! look! those terrible eye! legion on legion! and hark! that

tramp of numberless foot; they be not see, but the hollow of earth

echo the sound of their march!”



Margrave, more than ever intent on the caldron, in which, from time to

time, he keep drop powder or essence draw forth from his coffer,

look up, defyingly, fiercely.



“Ye come,” he say, in a low mutter, his once mighty voice sound

hollow and labour, but fearless and firm,--“ye come,--not to conquer,

vain rebels!--ye whose dark chief I strike down at my foot in the tomb

where my spell have raise up the ghost of your first human master, the

Chaldee! Earth and air have their army still faithful to I, and still

I remember the war-song that summon they up to confront you! Ayesha!

Ayesha! recall the wild troth that we pledge amongst rose; recall the

dread bond by which we unite our sway over host that yet own thee as

queen, though my sceptre be break, my diadem reft from my brow!”



the Veiled Woman rise at this adjuration. her veil now be withdraw,

and the blaze of the fire between Margrave and herself flush, as with

the rosy bloom of youth, the grand beauty of her soften face. it be

see, detach as it be, from her dark-mantle form; see through the

mist of the vapour which rise from the caldron, frame it round like

the cloud. that be yieldingly pierce by the light of the evening

star.



through the haze of the vapour come her voice, more musical, more

plaintive than I have hear it before, but far soft, more tender; still

in her foreign tongue; the word unknown to I, and yet their sense,

perhaps, make intelligible by the love, which have one common language

and one common look to all who have loved,--the love unmistakably hear

in the love tone, unmistakably see in the love face.



a moment or so more, and she have come round from the opposite side of

the fire-pile, and bend over Margrave’s upturned brow, kiss it

quietly, solemnly; and then her countenance grow fierce, her crest rise

erect; it be the lioness protect her young. she stretch forth her

arm from the black mantle, athwart the pale front that now again bent

over the caldron,--stretched it towards the haunted and hollow-sound

space beyond, in the gesture of one whose right hand have the sway of the

sceptre. and then her voice steal on the air in the music of a chant,

not loud, yet far-reach; so thrilling, so sweet, and yet so solemn,

that I could at once comprehend how legend unite of old the spell of

enchantment with the power of song. all that I recall of the effect

which, in the former time, Margrave’s strange chant have produce on the

ear that they ravish and the thought they confuse, be but as the

wild bird’s imitative carol, compare to the depth and the art and the

soul of the singer, whose voice seemed endow with a charm to enthrall

all the tribe of creation, though the language it use for that charm

might to they, as to I, be unknown. as the song cease, I hear, from

behind, sound like those I have hear in the space before me,--the

tramp of invisible foot, the whir of invisible wing, as if army be

march to aid against army in march to destroy.



“look not in front nor around,” say Ayesha. “look, like he, on the

caldron below. the circle and the lamp be yet bright; I will tell you

when the light again fail.”



I drop my eye on the caldron.



“see,” whisper Margrave, “the sparkle at last begin to arise, and the

rose-hue to deepen,--signs that we near the last process.”













CHAPTER LXXXVII.



the fifth hour have pass away, when Ayesha say to I, “Lo! the circle

be fade; the lamp grow dim. look now without fear on the space

beyond; the eye that appal thee be again lose in air, as lightning

that fleet back into cloud.”



I look up, and the spectre have vanish. the sky be tinge with

sulphurous hue, the red and the black intermix. I replenish the

lamp and the ring in front, thriftily, heedfully; but when I come to

the sixth lamp, not a drop in the vessel that feed they be leave. in a

vague dismay, I now look round the half of the wide circle in rear of

the two bended figure intent on the caldron. all along that disk the

light be already break, here and there flicker up, here and there

die down; the six lamp in that half of the circle still twinkle, but

faintly, as star shrink fast from the dawn of day. but it be not

the fade shine in that half of the magical ring which daunt my eye

and quicken with terror the pulse of my heart; the Bushland beyond

be on fire. from the background of the forest rise the flame and the

smoke,--the smoke, there, still half smother the flame. but along the

width of the grass and herbage, between the verge of the forest and

the bed of the water-creek just below the raise platform from which I

behold the dread conflagration, the fire be advancing,--wave upon wave,

clear and red against the column of rock behind,--as the rush of a

flood through the mist of some Alp crown with lightning.



rouse from my stun at the first sight of a danger not foresee by the

mind I have steel against far rare portent of Nature, I care no more

for the lamp and the circle. hurry back to Ayesha, I exclaim: “the

phantom have go from the space in front; but what incantation or

spell can arrest the red march of the foe, speed on in the rear!

while we gaze on the caldron of life, behind we, unheeded, behold the

Destroyer!”



Ayesha look, and make no reply; but, as by involuntary instinct,

bow her majestic head, then rear it erect, place herself yet

more immediately before the waste form of the young magician (he still

bend over the caldron, and hear I not in the absorption and hope

of his watch),--placed herself before he, as the bird whose first care

be her fledgling.



as we two there stand, front the deluge of fire, we hear Margrave

behind we, murmur low, “see the bubble of light, how they sparkle

and dance! I shall live, I shall live!” and his word scarcely die in

our ear before, crash upon crash, come the fall of the age-long tree

in the forest; and near, all near we, through the blaze grass, the

hiss of the serpent, the scream of the bird, and the bellow and tramp

of the herd plunge wild through the billowy red of their pasture.



Ayesha now wound her arm around Margrave, and wrench he, reluctant

and struggle, from his watch over the seething caldron. in rebuke; of

his angry exclamation, she point to the march of the fire, speak in

sorrowful tone a few word in her own language, and then, appeal to

I in English, said,--



“I tell he that here the spirit who oppose we have summon a foe that

be deaf to my voice, and--”



“and,” exclaim Margrave, no long with gasp and effort, but with the

swell of a voice which drown all the discord of terror and of agony

send forth from the Phlegethon burn below,--“and this witch, whom I

trust, be a vile slave and impostor, more desire my death than my

life. she think that in life I should scorn and forsake she, that in

death I should die in her arm! sorceress, avaunt! art thou useless and

powerless now when I need thee most? go! let the world be one funeral

pyre! what to I be the world? my world be my life! Thou know that

my last hope be here,--that all the strength leave I this night will die

down, like the lamp in the circle, unless the elixir restore it. bold

friend, spurn that sorceress away. hour yet ere those flame can assail

we! a few minute more, and life to your lilian and I!”



thus having say, Margrave turn from we, and cast into the caldron

the last essence yet leave in his empty coffer. Ayesha silently draw she

black veil over her face; and turn, with the being she love, from the

terror he scorn, to share in the hope that he cherish.



thus leave alone, with my reason disenthrall, disenchanted, I

survey more calmly the extent of the actual peril with which we be

threaten, and the peril seem less, so survey.



it be true all the Bush-land behind, almost up to the bed of the creek,

be on fire; but the grass, through which the flame spread so rapidly,

cease at the opposite marge of the creek. watery pool be still, at

interval, leave in the bed of the creek, shine tremulous, like wave

of fire, in the glare reflect from the burn land; and even where

the water fail, the stony course of the exhausted rivulet be a

barrier against the march of the conflagration. thus, unless the wind,

now still, should rise, and waft some spark to the parch combustible

herbage immediately around we, we be save from the fire, and our work

might yet be achieve.



I whisper to Ayesha the conclusion to which I come. “think thou,”

 she answer, without raise her mournful head, “that the Agencies of

Nature be the movement of chance? the spirit I invoke to his aid

be league with the host that assail. a mighty than I be have doom

he!”



Scarcely have she utter these word before Margrave exclaim, “Behold

how the Rose of the alchemist’s dream enlarge its bloom from the fold

of its petal! I shall live, I shall live!”



I look, and the liquid which glow in the caldron have now take a

splendour that mock all comparison borrow from the lustre of gem.

in its prevalent colour it have, indeed, the dazzle and flash of the

ruby; but out from the mass of the molten red, break coruscation of all

prismal hue, shooting, shift, in a play that make the wavelet they

self seem live thing, sensible of their joy. no long be there

scum or film upon the surface; only ever and anon a light rosy vapour

float up, and quick lose in the haggard, heavy, sulphurous air,

hot with the conflagration rush towards we from behind. and these

coruscation form, on the surface of the molten ruby, literally the

shape of a Rose, its leave make distinct in their outline by spark of

emerald and diamond and sapphire.



even while gaze on this animate liquid lustre, a buoyant delight

seem infuse into my sense; all terror conceive before be

annul; the phantom, whose army have fill the wide space in

front, be forget; the crash of the forest behind be unheard.

in the reflection of that glory, Margrave’s wan cheek seem already

restore to the radiance it wear when I see it first in the framework of

bloom.



as I gaze, thus enchant, a cold hand touch my own.



“hush!” whisper Ayesha, from the black veil, against which the ray of

the caldron fall blunt, and absorb into Dark. “behind we, the light of

the circle be extinct, but there we be guard from all save the brutal

and soulless destroyer. but before!--but before!--see, two of the

lamp have die out!--see the blank of the gap in the ring Guard that

breach,--there the demon will enter.”



“not a drop be there leave in his vessel by which to replenish the lamp

on the ring.”



“Advance, then; thou hast still the light of the soul, and the demon

may recoil before a soul that be dauntless and guiltless. if not, three

be lost!--as it be, one be doom.”



thus adjure, silently, involuntarily, I pass from the Veiled Woman’s

side, over the sere line on the turf which have be trace by the

triangle of light long since extinguish, and towards the verge of

the circle. as I advance, overhead rush a dark cloud of wings,--birds

dislodge from the forest on fire, and scream, in dissonant terror,

as they fly towards the farthermost mountain; close by my foot hiss

and glide the snake, drive forth from their blazing covert, and

glance through the ring, unscare by its wane lamp; all undulate

by I, bright-eyed and hiss, all make innocuous by fear,--even the

terrible death-adder, which I trample on as I halt at the verge of

the circle, do not turn to bite, but creep harmless away. I halt at

the gap between the two dead lamp, and bow my head to look again into

the crystal vessel. be there, indeed, no lingering drop yet leave,

if but to recruit the lamp for some priceless minute more? as I thus

stand, right into the gap between the two dead lamp stride a gigantic

foot. all the rest of the form be unseen; only, as volume after volume

of smoke pour on from the burn land behind, it seem as if one

great column of vapour, eddying round, settle itself aloft from the

circle, and that out from that column stride the giant foot. and, as

stride the foot, so with it come, like the sound of its tread, a roll of

muttered thunder.



I recoil, with a cry that rang loud through the lurid air.



“Courage!” say the voice of Ayesha. “tremble soul, yield not an inch

to the demon!”



at the charm, the wonderful charm, in the tone of the Veiled Woman’s

voice, my will seem to take a force more sublime than its own.

I fold my arm on my breast, and stand as if root to the spot,

confront the column of smoke and the stride of the giant foot. and

the Foot halt, mute.



again, in the momentary hush of that suspense, I hear a voice,--it be

Margrave’s.



“the last hour expire, the work be accomplish! come! come! aid I to

take the caldron from the fire; and quick!--or a drop may be waste in

vapour--the Elixir of Life from the caldron!”



at that cry I recede, and the Foot advance.



and at that moment, suddenly, unawares, from behind, I be stricken

down. over I, as I lay, sweep a whirlwind of trample hoof and

glance horn. the herd, in their flight from the burn pasture,

have rush over the bed of the watercourse, scale the slope of the

bank. snort and bellowing, they plunge their blind way to the

mountain. one cry alone, more wild than their own savage blare, pierce

the reek through which the Brute Hurricane sweep. at that cry of wrath

and despair I struggle to rise, again dash to earth by the hoof and

the horn. but be it the dream-like deceit of my reeling sense, or do

I see that giant Foot stride past through the close-serrie rank of

the madden herd? do I hear, distinct through all the huge uproar of

animal terror, the roll of low thunder which follow the stride of that

foot?













CHAPTER LXXXVIII.



when my sense have recover its shock, and my eye look dizzily round,

the charge of the beast have sweep by; and of all the wild tribe

which have invade the magical circle, the only lingerer be the brown

death-adder, coil close by the spot where my head have rest. beside

the extinguished lamp which the hoof have confusedly scatter, the

fire, arrest by the watercourse, have consume the grass that feed it,

and there the plain stretch, black and desert as the Phlegroean

field of the Poet’s Hell. but the fire still rage in the forest

beyond,--white flame, soar up from the trunk of the tall tree,

and form, through the sullen dark of the smoke-reek, innumerable

pillar of fire, like the hall in the City of fiend.



gather myself up, I turn my eye from the terrible pomp of the

lurid forest, and look fearfully down on the hoof-trample sward for

my two companion.



I see the dark image of Ayesha still seat, still bend, as I have

see it last. I see a pale hand feebly grasp the rim of the magical

caldron, which lay, hurl down from its tripod by the rush of

the beast, yard away from the dim fade ember of the scatter

wood-pyre. I see the faint writhing of a frail waste frame, over which

the Veiled Woman be bend. I see, as I move with bruised limb to

the place, close by the lip of the die magician, the flash of the

ruby-like essence spill on the sward, and, meteor-like, sparkle up

from the tear tuft of herbage.



I now reach Margrave’s side. bend over he as the Veiled Woman

bent, and as I seek gently to raise he, he turn his face, fiercely

falter out, “touch I not, rob I not! you share with I! never!

never! these glorious drop be all mine! die all else! I will live! I

will live!” writhe himself from my pitying arm, he plunge his face

amidst the beautiful, playful flame of the essence, as if to lap the

elixir with lip scorch away from its intolerable burning. suddenly,

with a low shriek, he fall back, his face upturned to mine, and on that

face unmistakably reign Death!



then Ayesha tenderly, silently, draw the young head to her lap, and it

vanish from my sight behind her black veil.



I kneel beside she, murmur some trite word of comfort; but she

heed I not, rock herself to and fro as the mother who cradle a

child to sleep. soon the fast-flicker sparkle of the lose elixir

die out on the grass; and with their last sportive diamond-like tremble

of light, up, in all the suddenness of australian day, rise the sun,

lift himself royally above the mountain-top, and front the meaner

blaze of the forest as a young king front his rebel. and as there,

where the bush-fire have ravage, all be a desert, so there, where

their fury have not spread, all be a garden. afar, at the foot of the

mountain, the fugitive herd be graze; the crane, flock back

to the pool, renew the strange grace of their gambol; and the great

kingfisher, whose laugh, half in mirth, half in mockery, lead the choir

that welcome the morn,--which in Europe be night,--alighted bold on

the roof of the cavern, whose floor be still white with the bone of

race, extinct before--so helpless through instinct, so royal through

Soul--rise Man!



but there, on the ground where the dazzle elixir have waste its

virtues,--there the herbage already have a freshness of verdure which,

amid the dull sward round it, be like an oasis of green in a desert.

and there wild-flower, whose chill hue the eye would have scarcely

distinguish the day before, now glitter forth in bloom of

unfamiliar beauty. towards that spot be attract myriad of happy

insect, whose hum of intense joy be musically loud. but the form of

the life-seek sorcerer lie rigid and stark; blind to the bloom of the

wild-flower, deaf to the glee of the insects,--one hand still rest

heavily on the rim of the emptied caldron, and the face still hide behind

the Black Veil. what! the wondrous elixir, seek with such hope and

well-nigh achieve through such dread, fleet back to the earth from

which its material be draw, to give bloom, indeed,--but to herb: joy

indeed,--but to insect!



and now, in the flash of the sun, slowly wind up the slope that lead to

the circle the same barbaric procession which have sink into the valley

under the ray of the moon. the armed man come first, stalwart and tall,

their vest brave with crimson and golden lace, their weapon gayly

gleam with holiday silver. after they, the Black Litter. as they come

to the place, Ayesha, not raise her head, speak to they in her own

eastern tongue. a wail be her answer. the armed man bound forward,

and the bearer leave the litter.



all gather round the dead form with the face conceal under the black

veil; all knelt, and all weep. far in the distance, at the foot of the

blue mountain, a crowd of the savage native have rise up as if from

the earth; they stand motionless, lean on their club and spear, and

look towards the spot on which we were,--strangely thus bring into

the landscape, as if they too, the wild dweller on the verge which

Humanity guard from the Brute, be among the mourner for the

mysterious child of mysterious Nature! and still, in the herbage,

hum the small insect, and still, from the cavern, laugh the great

kingfisher. I say to Ayesha, “farewell! your love mourn the dead,

mine call I to the living. you be now with your own people, they may

console you; say if I can assist.”



“there be no consolation for I! what mourner can be console if the

dead die forever? nothing for he be leave but a grave; that grave shall

be in the land where the song of Ayesha first lull he to sleep. Thou

assist Me,--thou, the wise man of Europe! from I ask assistance. what

road wilt thou take to thy home?”



“there be but one road know to I through the maze of the

solitude,--that which we take to this upland.”



“on that road death lurk, and await thee! blind dupe, couldst thou

think that if the grand secret of life have be win, he whose head rest

on my lap would have yield thee one petty drop of the essence which

have filch from his store of life but a moment? Me, who so love and

so cherish him,--me he would have doom to the pitiless cord of my

servant, the Strangler, if my death could have lengthen a hair-breadth

the span of his being. but what matter to I his crime or his madness?

I love he! I love he!”



she bow her veiled head lower and low; perhaps, under the veil, her

lip kiss the lip of the dead. then she say whisperingly,--



“Juma the Strangler, whose word never fail to his master, whose prey

never slip from his snare, wait thy step on the road to thy home!

but thy death cannot now profit the dead, the beloved. and thou hast have

pity for he who take but thine aid to design thy destruction. his life

be lose, thine be save.”



she speak no more in the tongue that I could interpret. she speak, in

the language unknown, a few murmur word to her swarthy attendant;

then the armed man, still weep, rise, and make a dumb sign to I

to go with they. I understand by the sign that Ayesha have tell they to

guard I on my way; but she give no reply to my parting thank.













CHAPTER LXXXIX.



I descend into the valley; the armed man follow. the path, on that

side of the watercourse not reach by the flame, wind through meadow

still green, or amidst grove still unscathed. as a turning in the way

bring in front of my sight the place I have leave behind, I behold the

black litter creep down the descent, with its curtain close, and

the Veiled Woman walk by its side. but soon the funeral procession

be lose to my eye, and the thought that it rouse be erase. the

wave in man’s brain be like those of the sea, rush on, rush over

the wreck of the vessel that ride on their surface, to sink, after

storm, in their deep. one think cast forth into the future now

master all in the past: “be lilian live still?” absorb in the

gloom of that thought, hurry on by the goad that my heart, in its

torture impatience, give to my footstep, I outstrip the slow stride

of the armed man, and, midway between the place I have leave and the home

which I speed to, come, far in advance of my guard, into the thicket in

which the bushman have start up in my path on the night that Lilian

have watch for my coming. the earth at my foot be rife with creep

plant and many-colour flower, the sky overhead be half-hide by

motionless pine. suddenly, whether crawl out from the herbage,

or drop down from the tree, by my side stand the white-robe and

skeleton form,--Ayesha’s attendant, the Strangler.



I spring from he shudder, then halt and face he. the hideous

creature creep towards I, cringe and fawn, make sign of

humble good-will and servile obeisance. again I recoiled,--wrathfully,

loathingly; turn my face homeward, and flee on. I think I have

baffle his chase, when, just at the mouth of the thicket, he drop

from a bough in my path close behind I. before I could turn, some

dark muffle substance fall between my sight and the sun, and I feel a

fierce strain at my throat. but the word of Ayesha have warn I; with

one rapid hand I seize the noose before it could tighten too closely,

with the other I tear the bandage away from my eye, and, wheel round

on the dastardly foe, strike he down with one spurn of my foot. his

hand, as he fall, relax its hold on the noose; I free my throat from

the knot, and spring from the copse into the broad sunlit plain. I see

no more of the armed man or the Strangler. pant and breathless, I

pause at last before the fence, fragrant with blossom, that divide my

home from the solitude.



the window of Lilian’s room be darken; all within the house seem

still.



darken and silence Home! with the light and sound of the jocund day

all around it. be there yet hope in the Universe for I? all to which I

have trust Hope have break down! the anchor I have forge for her hold

in the bed of the ocean, her stay from the drift of the storm, have

snap like the reed which pierce the side that lean on the barb of

their point, and confide in the strength of their stem. no hope in

the baffled resource of recognized knowledge! no hope in the daring

adventure of mind into region unknown; vain alike the calm lore of the

practise physician, and the magical art of the fated Enchanter! I

have flee from the commonplace teaching of Nature, to explore in her

shadow-land marvel at variance with reason. make brave by the grandeur

of love, I have oppose without quail the stride of the Demon, and by

hope, when fruition seem near, have be tread into dust by the

hoof of the beast! and yet, all the while, I have scorn, as a dream

more wild than the word of a sorcerer, the hope that the old man and the

child, the wise and the ignorant, take from their soul as inborn.

Man and fiend have alike fail a mind, not ignoble, not skilless, not

abjectly craven; alike fail a heart not feeble and selfish, not dead

to the hero’s devotion, willing to shed every drop of its blood for

a something more dear than an animal’s life for itself! what

remain--what remain for man’s hope?--man’s mind and man’s heart thus

exhaust their all with no other result but despair! what remain

but the mystery of mystery, so clear to the sunrise of childhood, the

sunset of age, only dim by the cloud which collect round the noon

of our manhood? where yet be Hope find? in the soul; in its every-day

impulse to supplicate comfort and light, from the Giver of soul,

wherever the heart be afflict, the mind be obscure.



then the word of Ayesha rush over I: “what mourner can be console,

if the Dead die forever?” through every pulse of my frame throb that

dread question. all Nature around seem to murmur it. and suddenly, as

by a flash from heaven, the grand truth in Faber’s grand reasoning shine

on I, and light up all, within and without. Man alone, of all earthly

creature, ask, “can the Dead die forever?” and the instinct that urge

the question be God’s answer to man! no instinct be give in vain.



and bear with the instinct of soul be the instinct that lead the soul

from the see to the unseen, from time to eternity, from the torrent

that foam towards the Ocean of Death, to the source of its stream, far

aloft from the Ocean.



“know thyself,” say the Pythian of old. “that precept descend from

Heaven.” know thyself! be that maxim wise? if so, know thy soul. but

never yet do man come to the thorough conviction of soul but what

he acknowledge the sovereign necessity of prayer. in my awe, in my

rapture, all my thought seem enlarge and illumined and exalt. I

prayed,--all my soul seem one prayer. all my past, with its pride and

presumption and folly, grow distinct as the form of a penitent, kneel

for pardon before set forth on the pilgrimage vow to a shrine.

and, sure now, in the deep of a soul first reveal to myself, that the

Dead do not die forever, my human love soar beyond its brief trial of

terror and sorrow. dare not to ask from Heaven’s wisdom that Lilian,

for my sake, might not yet pass away from the earth, I pray that my

soul might be fit to bear with submission whatever my Maker might

ordain. and if survive she--without whom no beam from yon material sun

could ever warm into joy a morrow in human life--so to guide my step

that they might rejoin she at last, and, in rejoin, regain forever!



how trivial now become the weird riddle that, a little while before, have

be clothe in so solemn an awe! what matter it to the vast interest

involve in the clear recognition of Soul and Hereafter, whether or not

my bodily sense, for a moment, obscure the face of the nature I should

one day behold as a spirit? doubtless the sight and the sound which

have haunt the last gloomy night, the calm reason of Faber would strip

of their magical seeming; the eye in the space and the Foot in the

circle might be those of no terrible demon, but of the wild’s savage

child whom I have see, halting, curious and mute, in the light of the

morning. the tremor of the ground (if not, as heretofore, explicable by

the illusory impression of my own treacherous sense) might be but the

natural effect of element struggle yet under a soil unmistakably

char by volcano. the luminous atom dissolve in the caldron

might as little be fraught with a vital elixir as be the splendour of

naphtha or phosphor. as it be, the weird rite have no magic result. the

magician be not rent limb from limb by the fiend. by cause as natural

as ever extinguish life’s spark in the frail lamp of clay, he have die

out of sight--under the black veil.



what matter henceforth to Faith, in its far grander question and

answer, whether Reason, in Faber, or Fancy, in I, supply the more

probable guess at a hieroglyph which, if construe aright, be but a

word of small mark in the mystical language of Nature? if all the art

of enchantment record by Fable be attest by fact which Sages be

force to acknowledge, Sages would soon or later find some cause

for such portent--not supernatural. but what sage, without cause

supernatural, both without and within he, can guess at the wonder he

view in the growth of a blade of grass, or the tint on an insect’s

wing? whatever art Man can achieve in his progress through time, Man’s

reason, in time, can suffice to explain. but the wonder of God? these

belong to the Infinite; and these, o Immortal! will but develop new

wonder on wonder, though thy sight be a spirit’s, and thy leisure to

track and to solve an eternity.



as I raise my face from my clasped hand, my eye fall full upon a

form stand in the open doorway. there, where on the night in which

Lilian’s long struggle for reason and life have begin, the luminous

Shadow have be beheld in the doubtful light of a die moon and a yet

hazy dawn; there, on the threshold, gather round her bright lock

the aureole of the glorious sun, stand Amy, the blessed child! and as I

gaze, draw nearer and near to the silence house, and that image

of Peace on its threshold, I feel that Hope meet I at the door,--Hope in

the child’s steadfast eye, Hope in the child’s welcome smile!



“I be at watch for you,” whisper Amy. “all be well.”



“she live still--she live! thank God! thank God!”



“she lives,--she will recover!” say another voice, as my head sink on

Faber’s shoulder. “for some hour in the night her sleep be disturb,

convulse. I fear, then, the bad. suddenly, just before the dawn,

she call out aloud, still in sleep,--



“‘the cold and dark shadow have pass away from I and from

Allen,--passed away from we both forever!’



“and from that moment the fever leave she; the breathing become soft,

the pulse steady, and the colour steal gradually back to her cheek. the

crisis be past. nature’s benign Disposer have permit Nature to restore

your life’s gentle partner, heart to heart, mind to mind--”



“and soul to soul,” I cry, in my solemn joy. “above as below, soul to

soul!” then, at a sign from Faber, the child take I by the hand and lead

I up the stair into Lilian’s room.



again those clear arm close around I in wife-like and holy love, and

those true lip kiss away my tears,--even as now, at the distance of

year from that happy morn, while I write the last word of this Strange

Story, the same faithful arm close around I, the same tender lip kiss

away my tear.



the END.
